"MY NOVEL . " 

 by Edward Bulwer - Lytton 




 BOOK first . 




 initial CHAPTER 

  - show how MY novel come to be WRITTEN . 


 Scene , the hall in UNCLE ROLAND 'S tower ; time , night ; season , winter . 


 MR . CAXTON be seat before a great geographical globe , which he be 
 turn round leisurely , and " for his own recreation , " as , accord to 
 Sir Thomas Browne , a philosopher should turn round the orb of which that 
 globe profess to be the representation and effigy . my mother have 
 just adorn a very small frock with a very smart braid , be hold 
 it out at arm 's length , the more to admire the effect . Blanche , though 
 lean both hand on my mother 's shoulder , be not regard the frock , 
 but glance towards PISISTRATUS , who , seat near the fire , lean back 
 in the chair , and his head bend over his breast , seem in a very bad 
 humour . Uncle Roland , who have become a great novel - reader , be deep in 
 the mystery of some fascinating Third volume . Mr. Squills have bring 
 the " Times " in his pocket for his own special profit and delectation , 
 and be now bend his brow over " the state of the money market , " in 
 great doubt whether railway share can possibly fall low , - for Mr. 
 Squills , happy man ! have large saving , and do not know what to do with 
 his money , or , to use his own phrase , " how to buy in at the cheap in 
 order to sell out at the dearest . " 

 MR . CAXTON   musingly . - " it must have be a monstrous long journey . it 
 would be somewhere hereabouts , I take it , that they would split off . " 

 MY mother   mechanically , and in order to show Austin that she pay he 
 the compliment of attend to his remark . - " who split off , my dear ? " 

 " bless I , Kitty , " say my father , in great admiration , " you ask 
 just the question which it be most difficult to answer . an ingenious 
 speculator on race contend that the Danes , whose descendant make the 
 chief part of our northern population   and indeed , if his hypothesis 
 could be correct , we must suppose all the ancient worshipper of Odin , 
 be of the same origin as the Etrurians . and why , Kitty , - I just ask 
 you , why ? " 

 my mother shake her head thoughtfully , and turn the frock to the other 
 side of the light . 

 " because , forsooth , " cry my father , explode , - " because the Etrurians 
 call their god the ' AEsar , ' and the Scandinavians call theirs the 
 ' AEsir , ' or ' Aser ' ! and where do you think this adventurous scholar put 
 their cradle ? " 

 " Cradle ! " say my mother , dreamily , " it must be in the nursery . " 

 MR . CAXTON . - " exactly , - in the nursery of the human race , just here , " 
 and my father point to the globe ; " bound , you see , by the river 
 Halys , and in that region which , take its name from Ees , or as   a 
 word designate light or fire , have be immemorially call Asia . now , 
 Kitty , from Ees , or as , our ethnological speculator would derive not 
 only Asia , the land , but AEsar , or Aser , its primitive inhabitant . 
 hence he suppose the origin of the Etrurians and the Scandinavians . but 
 if we give he so much , we must give he more , and deduce from the same 
 origin the Es of the Celt and the Ized of the Persian , and - what will 
 be of more use to he , I dare say , poor man , than all the rest put 
 together - the aes of the Romans , - that is , the God of copper - money - a 
 very powerful household god he be to this day ! " 

 my mother look musingly at her frock , as if she be take my 
 father 's proposition into serious consideration . 

 " so perhaps , " resume my father , " and not unconformably with sacred 
 record , from one great parent horde come all those various tribe , 
 carry with they the name of their beloved Asia ; and whether they 
 wander north , south , or west , exalt their own emphatic designation 
 of ' child of the Land of Light ' into the title of god . and to think " 
  add Mr. Caxton pathetically , gaze upon that speck on the globe on 
 which his forefinger rest , - " to think how little they change for 
 the well when they get to the Don , or entangle their raft amidst the 
 iceberg of the Baltic , - so comfortably off as they be here , if they 
 could but have stay quiet . " 

 " and why the deuce could not they ? " ask Mr. Squills . " pressure of 
 population , and not enough to live upon , I suppose , " say my father . 

 pisistratu   sulkily . - " More probably they do away with the Corn Laws , 
 sir . " 

 " Papae ! " quoth my father , " that throw a new light on the subject . " 

 PISISTRATUS   full of his grievance , and not care three straw about 
 the origin of the Scandinavians . - " I know that if we be to lose l500 
 every year on a farm which we hold rent - free , and which the good judge 
 allow to be a perfect model for the whole country , we have well make 
 haste and turn AEsir , or Aser , or whatever you call they , and fix a 
 settlement on the property of other nation , otherwise , I suspect , our 
 probable settlement will be on the parish . " 

 MR . SQUILLS   who , it must be remember , be an enthusiastic 
 free - trader . " you have only get to put more capital on the land . " 

 PISISTRATUS . - " well , Mr. Squills , as you think so well of that 
 investment , put your capital on it . I promise that you shall have every 
 shilling of profit . " 

 MR . SQUILLS   hastily retreat behind the " Times "   - " I do not   think 
 the great Western can fall any lower , though it be hazardous ; I can but 
 venture a few hundred - " 

 PISISTRATUS . - " on our land , Squills ? - thank you . " 

 MR . SQUILLS . - " no , no , - anything but that ; on the great Western . " 

 Pisistratus relax into gloom . Blanche steal up coaxingly , and get 
 snub for her pain . 

 a pause . 

 MR . CAXTON . - " there be two golden rule of life ; one relate to the 
 mind , and the other to the pocket . the first be , if our thought get 
 into a low , nervous , aguish condition , we should make they change the 
 air ; the second be comprise in the proverb , ' it be good to have two 
 string to one 's bow . ' therefore , Pisistratus , I tell you what you must 
 do , - write a book ! " 

 PISISTRATUS . - " write a book ! against the abolition of the Corn Laws ? 
 Faith , sir , the mischief 's do ! it take a much well pen than mine to 
 write down an act of parliament . " 

 MR . CAXTON . - " I only say , ' write a book . ' all the rest be the addition 
 of your own headlong imagination . " 

 PISISTRATUS   with the recollection of the Great Book rise before 
 he . - " indeed , sir , I should think that that would just finish we ! " 

 MR . CAXTON   not seem to heed the interruption . - " a book that will 
 sell ; a book that will prop up the fall of price ; a book that will 
 distract your mind from its dismal apprehension , and restore your 
 affection to your specie and your hope in the ultimate triumph of 
 sound principle - by the sight of a favourable balance at the end of 
 the yearly account . it be astonish what a difference that little 
 circumstance make in our view of thing in general . I remember 
 when the bank in which Squills have incautiously leave L1000 break , one 
 remarkably healthy year , that he become a great alarmist , and say that 
 the country be on the verge of ruin ; whereas you see now , when , thank 
 to a long succession of sickly season , he have a surplus capital to risk 
 in the great Western , he be firmly persuade that England be never in 
 so prosperous a condition . " 

 MR . SQUILLS   rather sullenly . - " Pooh , pooh . " 

 MR . CAXTON . - " write a book , my son , - write a book . need I tell you that 
 Money or Moneta , accord to Hyginus , be the mother of the Muses ? 
 write a book . " 

 BLANCHE and my mother   in full chorus . - " o yes , Sisty , a book ! a book ! 
 you must write a book . " 

 " I be sure , " quoth my Uncle Roland , slam down the volume he have just 
 conclude , " he could write a devilish deal well book than this ; and 
 how I come to read such trash night after night be more than I could 
 possibly explain to the satisfaction of any intelligent jury , if I be 
 put into a witness - box , and examine in the mild manner by my own 
 counsel . " 

 MR . CAXTON . - " you see that Roland tell we exactly what sort of a book 
 it shall be . " 

 PISISTRATUS . - " Trash , sir ? " 

 MR . CAXTON . - " no , - that be , not necessarily trash ; but a book of that 
 class which , whether trash or not , people can not   help read . novel 
 have become a necessity of the age . you must write a novel . " 

 PISISTRATUS   flatter , but dubious .-"a novel ! but every subject on 
 which novel can be write be preoccupy . there be novel of low 
 life , novel of high life , military novel , naval novel , novel 
 philosophical , novel religious , novel historical , novel descriptive 
 of India , the Colonies , Ancient Rome , and the egyptian pyramid . from 
 what bird , wild eagle , or barn - door fowl , can I 

  " ' pluck one unwearied plume from Fancy 's wing ? ' " 

 MR . CAXTON   after a little thought . - " you remember the story which 
 trevanion   I beg his pardon , Lord Ulswater   tell we the other night ? 
 that give you something of the romance of real life for your plot , put 
 you chiefly among scene with which you be familiar , and furnish you 
 with character which have be very sparingly deal with since the time 
 of Fielding . you can give we the country Squire , as you remember he 
 in your youth ; it be a speciman of a race worth preserve , the old 
 idiosyncrasy of which be rapidly die off , as the railway bring 
 Norfolk and Yorkshire within easy reach of the manner of London . you 
 can give we the old - fashioned Parson , as in all essential he may yet be 
 find - but before you have to drag he out of the great tractarian bog ; 
 and , for the rest , I really think that while , as I be tell , many popular 
 writer be do their good , especially in France , and perhaps a little 
 in England , to set class against class , and pick up every stone in the 
 kennel to shy at a gentleman with a good coat on his back , something 
 useful might be do by a few good - humour sketch of those innocent 
 criminal a little well off than their neighbour , whom , however we 
 dislike they , I take it for grant we shall have to endure , in one 
 shape or another , as long as civilization exist ; and they seem , on the 
 whole , as good in their present shape as we be likely to get , shake the 
 dice - box of society how we will . " 

 PISISTRATUS . - " very well say , sir ; but this rural country gentleman 
 life be not so new as you think . there be Washington Irving - " 

 MR . CAXTON . - " Charming ; but rather the manner of the last century than 
 this . you may as well cite Addison and Sir Roger de Coverley . " 

 PISISTRATUS . - " ' tremaine ' and ' De Vere . ' " 

 MR . CAXTON . - " nothing can be more graceful , nor more unlike what I mean . 
 the Pales and Terminus I wish you to put up in the field be familiar 
 image , that you may cut out of an oak tree , - not beautiful marble 
 statue , on porphyry pedestal , twenty foot high . " 

 PISISTRATUS . - " Miss Austen ; Mrs. Gore , in her masterpiece of ' Mrs. 
 Armytage ; ' Mrs. Marsh , too ; and then   for scottish manner   Miss 
 Ferrier ! " 

 MR . CAXTON   grow cross . - " oh , if you can not treat on bucolic but 
 what you must hear some Virgil or other cry ' stop thief , ' you deserve 
 to be toss by one of your own ' short - horn . ' "   still more 
 contemptuously   - " I be sure I do not   know why we spend so much money 
 on send our son to school to learn Latin , when that Anachronism 
 of your , Mrs. Caxton , can not   even construe a line and a half of 
 Phaedrus , - Phaedrus , Mrs. Caxton , a book which be in Latin what Goody 
 two - shoe be in the vernacular ! " 

 MRS . CAXTON   alarmed and indignant . - " Fie ! Austin I I be sure you can 
 construe Phaedrus , dear ! " 

 Pisistratus prudently preserve silence . 

 MR . CAXTON . - " I will try he - 

  " ' Sua cuique quum sit animi cogitatio 
  Colurque proprius . ' 

 " what do that mean ? " 

 pisistratits   smile   - " that every man have some colour matter within 
 he , to give his own tinge to - " 

 " his own novel , " interrupt my father . " Contentus peragis ! " 

 during the latter part of this dialogue , Blanche have sew together three 
 quire of the good Bath paper , and she now place they on a little table 
 before I , with her own inkstand and steel pen . 

 my mother put her finger to her lip , and say , " hush ! " my father 
 return to the cradle of the AEsas ; Captain Roland lean his cheek on 
 his hand , and gaze abstractedly on the fire ; Mr. Squills fall into a 
 placid doze ; and , after three sigh that would have melt a heart of 
 stone , I rush into - MY novel . 




 CHAPTER II . 

 " there have never be occasion to use they since I have   be in the 
 parish , " say Parson Dale . 

 " what do that prove ? " quoth the squire , sharply , and look the 
 parson full in the face . 

 " prove ! " repeat Mr. Dale , with a smile of benign , yet too conscious 
 superiority , " what do experience prove ? " 

 " that your forefather be great blockhead , and that their descendant 
 be not a whit the wiser . " 

 " squire , " reply the parson , " although that be a melancholy conclusion , 
 yet if you mean it to apply universally , and not to the family of the 
 Dales in particular ; it be not one which my candour as a reasoner , and 
 my humility as a mortal , will permit I to challenge . " 

 " I defy you , " say Mr. Hazeldean , triumphantly . " but to stick to the 
 subject   which it be monstrous hard to do when one talk with a parson , 
 I only just ask you to look yonder , and tell I on your conscience - I 
 do not   even say as a parson , but as a parishioner - whether you ever see a 
 more disreputable spectacle ? " 

 while he speak , the squire , lean heavily on the parson 's left 
 shoulder , extend his cane in a line parallel with the right eye of 
 that disputatious ecclesiastic , so that he might guide the organ of 
 sight to the object he have thus unflatteringly describe . 

 " I confess , " say the parson , " that , regard by the eye of the sens , 
 it be a thing that in its good day have small pretension to beauty , and 
 be not elevate into the picturesque even by neglect and decay . but , my 
 friend , regard by the eye of the inner man , - of the rural philosopher 
 and parochial legislator , - I say it be by neglect and decay that it 
 be render a very pleasing feature in what I may call ' the moral 
 topography of a parish . ' " 

 the squire look at the parson as if he could have beat he ; and , 
 indeed , regard the object in dispute not only with the eye of the 
 outer man , but the eye of law and order , the eye of a country gentleman 
 and a justice of the peace , the spectacle be scandalously disreputable . 
 it be moss - grown ; it be worm - eat ; it be break right in the middle ; 
 through its four socketless eye , neighbour by the nettle , peer 
 the thistle , - the thistle ! a forest of thistle ! - and , to complete the 
 degradation of the whole , those thistle have attract the donkey of 
 an itinerant tinker ; and the irreverent animal be in the very act of 
 take his luncheon out of the eye and jaw of - THE PARISH stock . 

 the squire look as if he could have beat the parson ; but as he be 
 not without some slight command of temper , and a substitute be luckily 
 at hand , he gulp down his resentment , and make a rush - at the donkey ! 

 now the donkey be hamper by a rope to its fore - foot , to the which be 
 attach a billet of wood , call technically " a clog , " so that it have 
 no fair chance of escape from the assault its sacrilegious luncheon have 
 justly provoke . but the ass turn round with unusual nimbleness at 
 the first stroke of the cane , the squire catch his foot in the rope , 
 and go head over heel among the thistle . the donkey gravely bend 
 down , and thrice smell or sniff its prostrate foe ; then , have 
 convince itself that it have nothing furth to apprehend for the 
 present , and very willing to make the good of the reprieve , accord 
 to the poetical admonition , " gather your rosebud while you may , " it 
 crop a thistle in full bloom , close to the ear of the squire , - so 
 close , indeed , that the parson think the ear be go ; and with the 
 more probability , inasmuch as the squire , feel the warm breath of the 
 creature , bellow out with all the force of lung accustom to give a 
 View - hallo ! 

 " bless I , be it go ? " say the parson , thrust his person between 
 the ass and the squire . 

 " Zounds and the devil ! " cry the squire , rub himself , as he rise to 
 his foot . 

 " hush ! " say the parson , gently . " what a horrible oath ! " 

 " horrible oath ! if you have my nankeen on , " say the squire , still 
 rub himself , " and have fall into a thicket of thistle , with a 
 donkey 's tooth within an inch of your ear - " 

 " it be not go , then ? " interrupt the parson . 

 " no , - that be , I think not , " say the squire , dubiously ; and he clap 
 his hand to the organ in question . " no ! it be not go ! " 

 " thank Heaven ! " say the good clergyman , kindly . " Hum , " growl the 
 squire , who be now once more engage in rub himself . " thank Heaven 
 indeed , when I be as full of thorn as a porcupine ! I should just like 
 to know what use thistle be in the world . " 

 " for donkey to eat , if you will let they , squire , " answer the parson . 

 " Ugh , you beast ! " cry Mr. Hazeldean , all his wrath reawaken , whether 
 by the reference to the donkey specie , or his inability to reply to 
 the parson , or perhaps by some sudden prick too sharp for 
 humanity - especially humanity in nankeen - to endure without kick . 
 " Ugh , you beast ! " he exclaim , shake his cane at the donkey , which , 
 at the interposition of the parson , have respectfully recoil a few 
 pace , and now stand switch its thin tail , and try vainly to lift 
 one of its fore - leg - for the fly tease it . 

 " poor thing ! " say the parson , pityingly . " see , it have a raw place on 
 the shoulder , and the fly have find out the sore . " 

 " I be devilish glad to hear it , " say the squire , vindictively . 

 " Fie , fie ! " 

 " it be very well to say ' Fie , fie . ' it be not you who fall among the 
 thistle . what be the man about now , I wonder ? " 

 the parson have walk towards a chestnut - tree that stand on the village 
 green ; he break off a bough , return to the donkey , whisk away the 
 fly , and then tenderly place the broad leaf over the sore , as a 
 protection from the swarm . the donkey turn round its head , and look 
 at he with mild wonder . 

 " I would bet a shilling , " say the parson , softly , " that this be the 
 first act of kindness thou hast meet with this many a day . and slight 
 enough it be , Heaven know . " 

 with that the parson put his hand into his pocket , and draw out an 
 apple . it be a fine large rose - cheek apple , one of the last winter 's 
 store from the celebrated tree in the parsonage garden , and he be 
 take it as a present to a little boy in the village who have notably 
 distinguish himself in the Sunday - school . " Nay , in common justice , 
 Lenny Fairfield should have the preference , " mutter the parson . the 
 ass prick up one of its ear , and advance its head timidly . " but 
 Lenny Fairfield would be as much pleased with twopence ; and what could 
 twopence do to thee ? " the ass 's nose now touch the apple . " take it , 
 in the name of Charity , " quoth the parson ; " justice be accustomed to be 
 serve last ; " and the ass take the apple . " how have you the heart ! " say 
 the parson , point to the squire 's cane . 

 the ass stop munch , and look askant at the squire . " Pooh ! eat 
 on ; he will not beat thee now . " 

 " no , " say the squire , apologetically . " but after all , he be not an 
 ass of the parish ; he be a vagrant , and he ought to be pound . but the 
 pound be in as bad a state as the stock , thank to your new - fashion 
 doctrine . " 

 " New - fashioned ! " cry the parson , almost indignantly , for he have a 
 great disdain of new fashion . " they be as old as Christianity ; nay , 
 as old as Paradise , which you will observe be derive from a Greek , 
 or rather a persian word , and mean something more than ' garden , ' 
 correspond "   pursue the parson , rather pedantically   " with the 
 latin - vivarium , - namely , grove or park full of innocent dumb creature . 
 depend on it , donkey be allow to eat thistle there . " 

 " very possibly , " say the squire , dryly . " but Hazeldeau , though a 
 very pretty village , be not Paradise . the stock shall be mend 
 to - morrow , - ay , and the pound too , and the next donkey find trespassing 
 shall go into it , as sure as my name 's Hazeldean . " 

 " then , " say the parson , gravely , " I can only hope that the next parish 
 may not follow your example ; or that you and I may never be catch 
 stray . " 




 CHAPTER III . 

 Parson Dale and Squire Hazeldean part company ; the latter to inspect 
 his sheep , the former to visit some of his parishioner , include Lenny 
 Fairfield , whom the donkey have defraud of his apple . 

 Lenny Fairfield be sure to be in the way , for his mother rent a 
 few acre of grass - land from the squire , and it be now hay - time . and 
 Leonard , commonly call Lenny , be an only son , and his mother a widow . 
 the cottage stand apart , and somewhat remote , in one of the many nook 
 of the long , green village lane . and a thoroughly english cottage it 
 be , three century old at least ; with wall of rubble let into oak 
 frame , and duly whitewash every summer , a thatched roof , small pane 
 of glass , an old doorway raise from the ground by two step . there be 
 about this little dwelling all the homely rustic elegance which 
 peasant life admit of ; a honeysuckle be train over the door ; a few 
 flower - pot be place on the window - sill ; the small plot of ground 
 in front of the house be keep with great neatness , and even taste ; some 
 large rough stone on either side the little path have be form 
 into a sort of rockwork , with creeper that be now in flower ; and the 
 potato - ground be screen from the eye by sweet pea and lupine . simple 
 elegance , all this , it be true ; but how well it speak for peasant and 
 landlord , when you see that the peasant be fond of his home , and have 
 some spare time and heart to bestow upon mere embellishment ! such 
 a peasant be sure to be a bad customer to the alehouse , and a safe 
 neighbour to the squire 's preserve . all honour and praise to he , 
 except a small tax upon both , which be due to the landlord ! 

 such sight be as pleasant to the parson as the most beautiful 
 landscape of Italy can be to the dilettante . he pause a moment at the 
 wicket to look around he , and distend his nostril voluptuously to 
 inhale the smell of the sweet pea , mix with that of the new - mow hay 
 in the field behind , which a slight breeze bore to he . he then 
 move on , carefully scrap his shoe , clean and well - polished as they 
 be , - for Mr. Dale be rather a beau in his own clerical way , - on the 
 scraper without the door , and lift the latch . 

 your virtuoso look with artistical delight on the figure of some nymph 
 paint on an etruscan vase , engage in pour out the juice of the 
 grape from her classic urn . and the parson feel as harmless , if not as 
 elegant a pleasure , in contemplate Widow Fairfield brim high a 
 glitter can , which she design for the refreshment of the thirsty 
 haymaker . 

 Mrs. Fairfield be a middle - aged , tidy woman , with that alert precision 
 of movement which seem to come from an active , orderly mind ; and as she 
 now turn her head briskly at the sound of the parson 's footstep , she 
 show a countenance prepossessing though not handsome , - a countenance 
 from which a pleasant , hearty smile , break forth at that moment , 
 efface some line that , in repose , speak " of sorrow , but of sorrow 
 past ; " and her cheek , pale than be common to the complexion even of 
 the fair sex , when bear and breed amidst a rural population , might have 
 favour the guess that the early part of her life have be spend in 
 the languid air and " within - door " occupation of a town . 

 " never mind I , " say the parson , as Mrs. Fairfield drop her quick 
 courtesy , and smooth her apron ; " if you be go into the hayfield , I 
 will go with you ; I have something to say to Lenny , - an excellent boy . " 

 WIDOW . - " well , sir , and you be kind to say it , - but so he be . " 

 parson . - " he read uncommonly well , he write tolerably ; he be the good 
 lad in the whole school at his Catechism and in the Bible lesson ; and I 
 assure you , when I see his face at church , look up so attentively , I 
 fancy that I shall read my sermon all the well for such a listener ! " 

 widow   wipe her eye with the corner of her apron . - " ' Deed , sir , when 
 my poor Mark die , I never think I could have live on as I have do . 
 but that boy be so kind and good , that when I look at he sit there 
 in dear Mark 's chair , and remember how Mark love he , and all he use 
 to say to I about he , I feel somehow or other as if my good man smile 
 on I , and would rather I be not with he yet , till the lad have grow 
 up , and do not want I any more . " 

 parson   look away , and after a pause . - " you never hear anything of 
 the old folk at Lansmere ? " 

 " ' Deed , sir , sin ' poor Mark die , they han't   notice I nor the boy ; 
 but , " add the widow , with all a peasant 's pride , " it be not   that I 
 want their money ; only it be hard to feel strange like to one 's own 
 father and mother ! " 

 parson . - " you must excuse they . your father , Mr. Avenel , be never quite 
 the same man after that sad event which - but you be weep , my friend , 
 pardon I ; your mother be a little proud ; but so be you , though in 
 another way . " 

 WIDOW . - " I proud ! Lord love ye , sir , I have not a bit o ' pride in I ! 
 and that be the reason they always look down on I . " 

 parson . - " your parent must be well off ; and I shall apply to they in a 
 year or two on behalf of Lenny , for they promise I to provide for he 
 when he grow up , as they ought . " 

 WIDOW   with flash eye . - " I be sure , sir , I hope you will do no such 
 thing ; for I would not have Lenny beholden to they as have never give 
 he a kind word sin ' he be bear ! " 

 the parson smile gravely , and shake his head at poor Mrs. Fairfield 's 
 hasty confutation of her own self - acquittal from the charge of pride ; 
 but he see that it be not the time or moment for effectual peace - making 
 in the most irritable of all rancour , - namely , that nourish against 
 one 's near relation . he therefore drop the subject , and say , 
 " well , time enough to think of Lenny 's future prospect ; meanwhile we 
 be forget the haymaker . come . " 

 the widow open the back door , which lead across a little apple orchard 
 into the field . 

 parson . - " you have a pleasant place here ; and I see that my friend Lenny 
 should be in no want of apple . I have bring he one , but I have give 
 it away on the road . " 

 WIDOW . - " oh , sir , it be not the deed , - it be the will ; as I feel 
 when the squire , God bless he ! take two pound off the rent the year 
 he - that be , Mark - die . " 

 parson . - " if Lenny continue to be such a help to you , it will not be 
 long before the squire may put the two pound on again . " 

 " yes , sir , " say the widow , simply ; " I hope he will . " 

 " silly woman ! " mutter the parson . " that be not exactly what the 
 schoolmistress would have say . you do not   read nor write , Mrs. 
 Fairfield ; yet you express yourself with great propriety . " 

 " you know Mark be a schollard , sir , like my poor , poor sister ; and 
 though I be a sad stupid girl afore I marry , I try to take after 
 he when we come together . " 




 CHAPTER IV . 

 they be now in the hayfield , and a boy of about sixteen , but , like 
 most country lad , to appearance much young than he be , look up 
 from his rake , with lively blue eye beam forth under a profusion of 
 brown curly hair . 

 Leonard Fairfield be indeed a very handsome boy , - not so stout nor so 
 ruddy as one would choose for the ideal of rustic beauty , nor yet so 
 delicate in limb and keen in expression as be those child of city , 
 in whom the mind be cultivate at the expense of the body ; but still 
 he have the health of the country in his cheek , and be not without the 
 grace of the city in his compact figure and easy movement . there be 
 in his physiognomy something interesting from its peculiar character of 
 innocence and simplicity . you could see that he have be bring up by 
 a woman , and much apart from familiar contact with other child ; and 
 such intelligence as be yet develop in he be not ripen by the 
 joke and cuff of his coeval , but foster by decorous lecturing from 
 his elder , and good - little - boy maxim in good - little - boy book . 

 parson . - " come hither , Lenny . you know the benefit of school , I see : it 
 can teach you nothing well than to be a support to your mother . " 

 LENNY   look down sheepishly , and with a heighten glow over his 
 face . - " please , sir , that may come one of these day . " 

 parson . - " that be right , Lenny . let I see ! why , you must be nearly a 
 man . how old be you ? " 

 Lenny look up inquiringly at his mother . 

 parson . - " you ought to know , Lenny : speak for yourself . hold your 
 tongue , Mrs. Fairfield . " 

 LENNY   twirl his hat , and in great perplexity . - " well , and there be 
 Flop , neighbour Dutton 's old sheep - dog . he be very old now . " 

 parson . - " I be not ask Flop 's age , but your own . " 

 LENNY . - " ' Deed , sir , I have hear say as how Flop and I be pup 
 together . that is , I - I - " 

 for the parson be laugh , and so be Mrs. Fairfield ; and the haymaker , 
 who have stand still to listen , be laugh too . and poor Lenny have 
 quite lose his head , and look as if he would like to cry . 

 parson   pat the curly lock , encouragingly . - " never mind ; it be not 
 so badly answer , after all . and how old be Flop ? " 

 LENNY . - " why , he must be fifteen year and more .. " 

 parson . - " how old , then , be you ? " 

 LENNY   look up , with a beam of intelligence . - " fifteen year and 
 more . " 

 Widow sigh and nod her head . 

 " that be what we call put two and two together , " say the parson . 
 " or , in other word , " and here he raise his eye majestically towards 
 the haymaker - " in other word , thank to his love for his book , 
 simple as he stand here , Lenny Fairfield have show himself capable of 
 INDUCTIVE RATIOCINATION . " 

 at those word , deliver ore rotundo , the haymaker cease laugh ; 
 for even in lay matter they hold the parson to be an oracle , and word 
 so long must have a great deal in they . Lenny draw up his head proudly . 

 " you be very fond of Flop , I suppose ? " 

 " ' deed he be , " say the widow , " and of all poor dumb creature . " 

 " very good . suppose , my lad , that you have a fine apple , and that you meet 
 a friend who want it more than you , what would you do with it ? " 

 " please you , sir , I would give he half of it . " 

 the parson 's face fall . " not the whole , Lenny ? " 

 Lenny consider . " if he be a friend , sir , he would not like I to give 
 he all . " 

 " upon my word , Master Leonard , you speak so well that I must e'en 
 tell the truth . I bring you an apple , as a prize for good conduct in 
 school . but I meet by the way a poor donkey , and some one beat he for 
 eat a thistle , so I think I would make it up by give he the 
 apple . ought I only to have give he the half ? " 

 Lenny 's innocent face become all smile ; his interest be arouse . " and 
 do the donkey like the apple ? " 

 " very much , " say the parson , fumble in his pocket ; but think of 
 Leonard Fairfield 's year and understanding , and moreover observe , in 
 the pride of his heart , that there be many spectator to his deed , 
 he think the meditate twopence not sufficient , and he generously 
 produce a silver sixpence . 

 " there , my man , that will pay for the half apple which you would have 
 keep for yourself . " the parson again pat the curly lock , and after 
 a hearty word or two with the other haymaker , and a friendly " good - day " 
 to Mrs. Fairfield , strike into a path that lead towards his own glebe . 

 he have just cross the stile , when he hear hasty but timorous foot 
 behind he . he turn , and see his friend Lenny . 

 LENNY   half - cry , and hold out the sixpence . - " indeed , sir , I 
 would rather not . I would have give all to the Neddy . " 

 parson . - " why , then , my man , you have a still great right to the 
 sixpence . " 

 LENNY . - " no , sir ; 'cause you only give it to make up for the half apple . 
 and if I have give the whole , as I ought to have do , why , I should 
 have have no right to the sixpence . please , sir , do not   be offend ; do 
 take it back , will you ? " 

 the parson hesitate . and the boy thrust the sixpence into his hand , as 
 the ass have poke its nose there before in quest of the apple . 

 " I see , " say Parson Dale , soliloquize , " that if one do not   give 
 Justice the first place at the table , all the other Virtues eat up her 
 share . " 

 indeed , the case be perplexing . charity , like a forward , impudent 
 baggage as she be , always thrust herself in the way , and take other 
 people 's apple to make her own little pie , have defraud Lenny of his 
 due ; and now Susceptibility , who look like a shy , blush - face , awkward 
 Virtue in her teen - but who , nevertheless , be always engage in 
 pick the pocket of her sister - try to filch from he his lawful 
 recompense . the case be perplexing ; for the parson hold susceptibility

 give she a slap in the face , which might frighten she away forever . so 
 Mr. Dale stand irresolute , glance from the sixpence to Lenny , and from 
 Lenny to the sixpence . 

 " Buon giorno , good - day to you , " say a voice behind , in an accent 
 slightly but unmistakably foreign , and a strange - look figure 
 present itself at the stile . 

 imagine a tall and exceedingly meagre man , dress in a rusty suit of 
 black , - the pantaloon tight at the calf and ankle , and there form a 
 loose gaiter over thick shoe , buckle high at the instep ; an old cloak , 
 line with red , be throw over one shoulder , though the day be sultry ; 
 a quaint , red , outlandish umbrella , with a carve brass handle , be 
 thrust under one arm , though the sky be cloudless : a profusion of raven 
 hair , in wave curl that seem as fine as silk , escape from the 
 side of a straw hat of prodigious brim ; a complexion sallow and 
 swarthy , and feature which , though not without considerable beauty 
 to the eye of the artist , be not only unlike what we fair , well - feed , 
 neat - face Englishmen be will not to consider comely , but exceedingly like 
 what we be disposed to regard as awful and satanic , - to wit , a long 
 hooked nose , sunken cheek , black eye , whose pierce brilliancy take 
 something wizard - like and mystical from the large spectacle through 
 which they shine ; a mouth round which play an ironical smile , and in 
 which a physiognomist would have remark singular shrewdness , and 
 some closeness , complete the picture . imagine this figure , grotesque , 
 peregrinate , and to the eye of a peasant certainly diabolical ; then 
 perch it on the stile in the midst of those green english field , and 
 in sight of that primitive english village ; there let it sit straddle , 
 its long leg dangle down , a short german pipe emit cloud from

 spectacle full upon the parson , yet askant upon Lenny Fairfield . Lenny 
 Fairfield look exceedingly frightened . 

 " upon my word , Dr. Riccabocca , " say Mr. Dale , smile , " you come in 
 good time to solve a very nice question in casuistry ; " and herewith the 
 parson explain the case , and put the question , " Ought Lenny Fairfield 
 to have the sixpence , or ought he not ? " 

 " Cospetto ! " say the doctor , " if the hen would but hold her tongue , 
 nobody would know that she have lay an egg . " 




 CHAPTER V. 

 " grant , " say the parson ; " but what follow ? the saying be good , but I 
 do not   see the application . " 

 " a thousand pardon ! " reply Dr. Riccabocca , with all the urbanity of 
 an italian ; " but it seem to I that if you have give the sixpence to 
 the fanciullo , that is , to this good little boy , without tell he the 
 story about the donkey , you would never have put he and yourself into 
 this awkward dilemma . " 

 " but , my dear sir , " whisper the parson , mildly , as he incline his 
 lip to the doctor 's ear , " I should then have lose the opportunity of 
 inculcate a moral lesson - you understand ? " 

 Dr. Riccabocca shrug his shoulder , restore his pipe to his mouth , 
 and take a long whiff . it be a whiff eloquent , though cynical , - a whiff 
 peculiar to your philosophical smoker , a whiff that imply the most 
 absolute but the most placid incredulity as to the effect of the 
 parson 's moral lesson . 

 " still you have not give we your decision , " say the parson , after a 
 pause . 

 the doctor withdraw the pipe . " Cospetto ! " say he , - " he who scrub the 
 head of an ass waste his soap . " 

 " if you scrub mine fifty time over with those enigmatical proverb 
 of your , " say the parson , testily , " you would not make it any the 
 wiser . "

 " I never presume to say that there be more ass than one in the 
 story ; but I think that I could not well explain my meaning , which 
 be simply this , - you scrub the ass 's head , and therefore you must 
 lose the soap . let the fanciullo have the sixpence ; and a great sum it 
 be , too , for a little boy , who may spend it all as pocketmoney ! " 

 " there , Lenny , you hear ? " say the parson , stretch out the sixpence . 
 but Lenny retreat , and cast on the umpire a look of great aversion and 
 disgust . 

 " please , Master Dale , " say he , obstinately , " I would rather not . 

 " it be a matter of feeling , you see , " say the parson , turn to the 
 umpire ; " and I believe the boy be right . " 

 " if it be a matter of feeling , " reply Dr. Riccabocca , " there be no 
 more to be say on it . when feel come in at the door , Reason have 
 nothing to do but to jump out of the window . " 

 " go , my good boy , " say the parson , pocket the coin ; " but , stop ! give 
 I your hand first . there - I understand you ; - good - by ! " 

 Lenny 's eye glisten as the parson shake he by the hand , and , not 
 trust himself to speak , he walk off sturdily . the parson wipe his 
 forehead , and sit himself down on the stile beside the italian . the view 
 before they be lovely , and both enjoy it   though not equally   enough 
 to be silent for some moment . on the other side the lane , see between 
 gap in the old oak and chestnut that hang over the mossgrown pale of 
 Hazeldean Park , rise gentle , verdant slope , dot with sheep and herd 
 of deer . a stately avenue stretch far away to the left , and end at 
 the right hand within a few yard of a ha - ha that divide the park from 
 a level sward of tableland , gay with shrub and flower - pot , relieve by 
 the shade of two mighty cedar . and on this platform , only see in part , 
 stand the squire 's old - fashioned house , red - brick , with stone mullion , 
 gable - end , and quaint chimney - pot . on this side the road , immediately 
 face the two gentleman , cottage after cottage whitely emerge from 
 the curve in the lane , while , beyond , the ground decline give an 
 extensive prospect of wood and cornfield , spire and farm . behind , 
 from a belt of lilac and evergreen , you catch a peep of the 
 parsonage - house , back by woodland , and a little noisy rill run in 
 front . the bird be still in the hedgerow , - only   as if from the very 
 heart of the most distant wood , there come now and then the mellow 
 note of the cuckoo . 

 " verily , " say Mr. Dale , softly , " my lot have fall on a goodly 
 heritage . " 

 the italian twitch his cloak over he , and sigh almost inaudibly . 
 perhaps he think of his own Summer Land , and feel that , amidst all 
 that fresh verdure of the North , there be no heritage for the stranger . 

 however , before the parson could notice the sigh or conjecture the 
 cause , Dr. Riccabocca 's thin lip take an expression almost malignant . 

 " per Bacco ! " say he ; " in every country I observe that the rook settle 
 where the tree be the fine . I be sure that , when Noah first land

 the parson fix his meek eye on the philosopher , and there be in they
