 CHAPTER VI . 

 the tinker be a stout , swarthy fellow , jovial and musical withal , for 
 he be sing a stave as he flourish his staff , and at the end of 
 each refrain down come the staff on the quarter of the donkey . the 
 tinker go behind and sing , the donkey go before and be thwack . 

 " yours be a droll country , " quoth Dr. Riccabocca ; " in mine , it be not 
 the ass that walk first in the procession that get the blow . " 

 the parson jump from the stile , and look over the hedge that 
 divide the field from the road - " gently , gently , " say he ; " the sound 
 of the stick spoil the singing ! oh , Mr. Sprott , Mr. Sprott ! a good man 
 be merciful to his beast . " 

 the donkey seem to recognize the voice of its friend , for it stop 
 short , prick one ear wistfully , and look up . the tinker touch his 
 hat , and look up too . " Lord bless your reverence ! he do not mind 
 it , - he like it . I vould not hurt thee ; would I , Neddy ? " 

 the donkey shake his head and shiver ; perhaps a fly have settle on the 
 sore , which the chestnut leave no long protect . 

 " I be sure you do not mean to hurt he , Sprott , " say the parson , 
 more politely I fear than honestly , - for he have see enough of that 
 cross - grained thing call the human heart , even in the little world of 
 a country parish , to know that it require management and coax 
 and flattering , to interfere successfully between a man and his own 
 donkey , - " I be sure you do not mean to hurt he ; but he have already get 
 a sore on his shoulder as big as my hand , poor thing ! " 

 " Lord love ' un ! yes ; that be do a playing with the manger the day I 
 give ' un oats ! " say the tinker . 

 Dr. Riccabocca adjust his spectacle , and survey the ass . the ass 
 prick up his other ear , and survey Dr. Riccabocca . in that mutual 
 survey of physical qualification , each be regard accord to the 
 average symmetry of its specie , it may be doubt whether the advantage 
 be on the side of the philosopher . 

 the parson have a great notion of the wisdom of his friend in all matter 
 not purely ecclesiastical . 

 " say a good word for the donkey ! " whisper he . 

 " Sir , " say the doctor , address Mr. Sprott , with a respectful 
 salutation , " there be a great kettle at my house - the Casino - which want 
 soldering : can you recommend I a tinker ? " 

 " why , that be all in my line , " say Sprott ; " and there ben't   a tinker in 
 the county that I vould recommend like myself , tho'f I say it . " 

 " you jest , good sir , " say the doctor , smile pleasantly . " a man who 
 can not   mend a hole in his own donkey can never demean himself by patch 
 up my great kettle . " 

 " Lord , sir ! " say the tinker , archly , " if I have know that poor Neddy 
 have have two sitch friend in court , I would have see he vas a gintleman , 
 and treat he as sitch . " 

 " Corpo di Bacco ! " quoth the doctor , " though that jest 's not new , I think 
 the tinker come very well out of it . " 

 " true ; but the donkey ! " say the parson ; " I have   a great mind to buy it . " 

 " permit I to tell you an anecdote in point , " say Dr. Riccabocca . 

 " well ? " say the parson , interrogatively . 

 " once on a time , " pursue Riccabocca , " the Emperor Adrian , go to the 
 public bath , see an old soldier , who have serve under he , rub his 
 back against the marble wall . the emperor , who be a wise , and therefore 
 a curious , inquisitive man , send for the soldier , and ask he why he 
 resort to that sort of friction . ' because , ' answer the veteran , ' I 
 be too poor to have slave to rub I down . ' the emperor be touch , and 
 give he slave and money . the next day , when Adrian go to the bath , 
 all the old man in the city be to be see rub themselves against 
 the marble as hard as they could . the emperor send for they , and ask 
 they the same question which he have put to the soldier ; the cunning old 
 rogue , of course , make the same answer . ' friend , ' say Adrian , ' since 
 there be so many of you , you will just rub one another ! ' Mr. Dale , if 
 you do not   want to have all the donkey in the county with hole in their 
 shoulder , you have well not buy the tinker 's ! " 

 " it be the hard thing in the world to do the least bit of good , " 
 groan the parson , as he break a twig off the hedge nervously , snap 
 it in two , and fling away the fragment : one of they hit the donkey on 
 the nose . if the ass could have speak Latin he would have say , " Et tu , 
 Brute ! " as it be , he hang down his ear , and walk on . 

 " Gee hup , " say the tinker , and he follow the ass . then stop , he 
 look over his shoulder , and see that the parson 's eye be gaze 
 mournfully on his protege , " never fear , your reverence , " cry the 
 tinker , kindly , " I will not spite ' un . " 




 CHAPTER VII . 

 " Four , o'clock , " cry the parson , look at his watch ; " half an hour 
 after dinner - time , and Mrs. Dale particularly beg I to be punctual , 
 because of the fine trout the squire send we . will you venture on what 
 our homely language call ' pot - luck , ' Doctor ? " 

 now Riccabocca be a professed philosopher , and value himself on his 
 penetration into the motif of human conduct . and when the parson thus 
 invite he to pot - luck , he smile with a kind of lofty complacency ; for 
 Mrs. Dale enjoy the reputation of have what her friend style 
 " her little temper . " and , as well - breed lady rarely indulge " little 
 temper " in the presence of a third person not of the family , so Dr. 
 Riccabocca instantly conclude that he be invite to stand between the 
 pot and the luck ! nevertheless - as he be fond of trout , and a much 
 more good - natured man than he ought to have be accord to his 
 principle - he accept the hospitality ; but he do so with a sly look 
 from over his spectacle , which bring a blush into the guilty cheek 
 of the parson . certainly Riccabocca have for once guess right in his 
 estimate of human motif . 

 the two walk on , cross a little bridge that span the rill , and 
 enter the parsonage lawn . two dog , that seem to have sit on watch 
 for their master , spring towards he , barking ; and the sound draw the 
 notice of Mrs. Dale , who , with parasol in hand , sally out from the 
 sash window which open on the lawn . now , o reader ! I know that , in 
 thy secret heart , thou art chuckle over the want of knowledge in the 
 sacred arcana of the domestic hearth betray by the author ; thou art 
 say to thyself , " a pretty way to conciliate ' little temper ' indeed , 
 to add to the offence of spoil the fish the crime of bring an 
 unexpected friend to eat it . Pot - luck , quotha , when the pot be boil 
 over this half hour ! " 

 but , to thy utter shame and confusion , o reader ! learn that both the 
 author and Parson Dale know very well what they be about . 

 Dr. Riccabocca be the special favourite of Mrs. Dale , and the only 
 person in the whole county who never put she out , by drop in . in 
 fact , strange though it may seem at first glance , Dr. Riccabocca have 
 that mysterious something about he , which we of his own sex can so 
 little comprehend , but which always propitiate the other . he owe this , 
 in part , to his own profound but hypocritical policy ; for he look upon 
 woman as the natural enemy to man , against whom it be necessary to be 
 always on the guard ; whom it be prudent to disarm by every specie of 
 fawn servility and abject complaisance . he owe it also , in part , to 
 the compassionate and heavenly nature of the angel whom his thought 
 thus villanously traduce - for woman like one whom they can pity without 
 despise ; and there be something in Signor Riccabocca 's poverty , 
 in his loneliness , in his exile , whether voluntary or compel , that 
 excited pity ; while , despite his threadbare coat , the red umbrella , and 
 the wild hair , he have , especially when address lady , that air 
 of gentleman and cavalier , which be or be more innate in an educate 
 Italian , of whatever rank , than perhaps in the high aristocracy of 
 any other country in Europe . for , though I grant that nothing be more 
 exquisite than the politeness of your french marquis of the old regime , 
 nothing more frankly gracious than the cordial address of a high - breed 
 english gentleman , nothing more kindly prepossess than the genial 
 good - nature of some patriarchal German , who will condescend to forget 
 his sixteen quartering in the pleasure of do you a favour , - yet 
 these specimen of the suavity of their several nation be rare ; 
 whereas blandness and polish be common attribute with your italian . 
 they seem to have be immemorially hand down to he , from ancestor 
 emulate the urbanity of Caesar , and refine by the grace of Horace . 

 " Dr. Riccabocca consent to dine with we , " cry the parson , hastily . 

 " if Madame permit ? " say the Italian , bow over the hand extend to 
 he , which , however , he forbear to take , see it be already full of 
 the watch . 

 " I be only sorry that the trout must be quite spoil , " begin Mrs. Dale , 
 plaintively . 

 " it be not the trout one think of when one dine with Mrs. Dale , " say 
 the infamous dissimulator . 

 " but I see James come to say that dinner be ready , " observe the 
 parson . 

 " he say that three - quarter of an hour ago , Charles dear , " retort 
 Mrs. Dale , take the arm of Dr. Riccabocca . 




 CHAPTER VIII . 

 while the parson and his wife be entertain their guest , I propose to 
 regale the reader with a small treatise a propos of that " Charles dear , " 
 murmur by Mrs. Dale , - a treatise expressly write for the benefit of 
 the Domestic Circle . 

 it be an old jest that there be not a word in the language that convey 
 so little endearment as the word " dear . " but though the saying itself , 
 like most truth , be trite and hackneye , no little novelty remain 
 to the search of the inquirer into the variety of inimical import 
 comprehend in that malign monosyllable . for instance , I submit to 
 the experienced that the degree of hostility it betray be in much 
 proportion to its collocation in the sentence . when , gliding 
 indirectly through the rest of the period , it take its stand at the 
 close , as in that " Charles dear " of Mrs. Dale , it have spill so much of 
 its natural bitterness by the way that it assume even a smile , " amara 
 lento temperet risu . " sometimes the smile be plaintive , sometimes arch . 
 for example : - 

  Plaintive .   " I know very well that whatever I do be wrong , Charles 
 dear . " 

 " Nay , I be very glad you amuse yourself so much without I , Charles 
 dear . " 

 " not quite so loud ! if you have but my poor head , Charles dear , " etc . 

  Arch .   " if you could spill the ink anywhere but on the good tablecloth , 
 Charles dear ! " 

 " but though you must always have your own way , you be not quite 
 faultless , own , Charles dear , " etc . 

 when the enemy stop in the middle of the sentence , its venom be 
 naturally less exhausted . for example : - 

 " really , I must say , Charles dear , that you be the most fidgety 
 person , " etc . 

 " and if the house bill be so high last week , Charles dear , I should 
 just like to know whose fault it be - that be all . " 

 " but you know , Charles dear , that you care no more for I and the 
 child than - " etc . 

 but if the fatal word spring up , in its primitive freshness , at the head 
 of the sentence , bow your head to the storm . it then assume the majesty 
 of " my " before it ; it be generally more than simple objurgation , - it 
 preface a sermon . my candour oblige I to confess that this be the 
 mode in which the hateful monosyllable be more usually employ by the 
 marital part of the one flesh ; and have something about it of the 
 odious assumption of the petruchian paterfamilia - the head of the 
 family - bode , not perhaps " peace and love , and quiet life , " but 
 certainly " awful rule and right supremacy . " for example : - 

 " my dear Jane , I wish you would just put by that everlasting crochet , 
 and listen to I for a few moment , " etc . " my dear Jane , I wish you 
 would understand I for once ; do not   think I be angry , - no , but I be 
 hurt ! you must consider , " etc . 

 " my dear Jane , I do not   know if it be your intention to ruin I ; but I 
 only wish you would do as all other woman do who care three straw for 
 their husband 's property , " etc . 

 " my dear Jane , I wish you to understand that I be the last person in 
 the world to be jealous ; but I will be d - -d if that puppy , Captain 
 Prettyman , " etc . 

 now , few so carefully cultivate the connubial garden , as to feel much 
 surprise at the occasional sting of a homely nettle or two ; but who 
 ever expect , before enter that garden , to find himself prick and 
 lacerate by an insidious exotical " dear , " which he have be teach to 
 believe only live in a hothouse , along with myrtle and other tender 
 and sensitive shrub which poet appropriate to Venus ? nevertheless 
 Parson Dale , be a patient man , and a pattern to all husband , would 
 have find no fault with his garden , though there have not be a single 
 speciman of " dear , " - whether the dear humili or the dear superba ; the 
 dear pallida , rubra , or nigra ; the dear suavis or the dear horrida , - no , 
 not a single " dear " in the whole horticulture of matrimony , which Mrs. 
 Dale have not bring to perfection . but this be far from be the 
 case ; Mrs. Dale , live much in retirement , be unaware of the modern 
 improvement , in variety of colour and sharpness of prickle , which have 
 reward the persevere skill of our female florist . 




 CHAPTER IX . 

 in the cool of the evening Dr. Riccabocca walk home across the field . 
 Mr. and Mrs. Dale have accompany he half - way , and as they now turn 
 back to the parsonage , they look behind to catch a glimpse of the 
 tall , outlandish figure , wind slowly through the path amidst the 
 wave of the green corn . 

 " poor man ! " say Mrs. Dale , feelingly ; " and the button be off his 
 wristband ! what a pity he have nobody to take care of he ! he seem very 
 domestic . do not   you think , Charles , it would be a great blessing if we 
 could get he a good wife ? " 

 " um , " say the parson ; " I doubt if he value the married state as he 
 ought . " 

 " what do you mean , Charles ? I never see a man more polite to lady in 
 my life . " 

 " yes , but - " 

 " but what ? you be always so mysterious , Charles dear . " 

 " mysterious ! no , Carry ; but if you could hear what the doctor say of 
 the lady sometimes . " 

 " ay , when you man get together , my dear . I know what that mean - pretty 
 thing you say of we ! but you be all alike ; you know you be , love ! " 

 " I be sure , " say the parson , simply , " that I have good cause to speak 
 well of the sex - when I think of you and my poor mother . " 

 Mrs. Dale , who , with all her " temper , " be an excellent woman , and 
 love her husband with the whole of her quick little heart , be touch . 
 she press his hand , and do not call he dear all the way home . 

 meanwhile the Italian pass the field , and come upon the high road 
 about two mile from Hazeldean . on one side stand an old - fashioned 
 solitary inn , such as english inn use to be before they become 
 railway hotel , - square , solid , old - fashioned , look so hospitable 
 and comfortable , with their great sign swing from some elm - tree in 
 front , and the long row of stable stand a little back , with a chaise 
 or two in the yard , and the jolly landlord talk of the crop to some 
 stout farmer , whose rough pony halt of itself at the well - know door . 
 opposite this inn , on the other side of the road , stand the habitation 
 of Dr. Riecabocca . 

 a few year before the date of these annal , the stage - coach on its way 
 to London from a seaport town stop at the inn , as be its wo not , for a 
 good hour , that its passenger might dine like Christian Englishmen - not 
 gulp down a basin of scald soup , like everlaste heathen Yankees , 
 with that cursed railway - whistle shrieking like a fiend in their ear ! 
 it be the good dining - place on the whole road , for the trout in the 
 neighbouring rill be famous , and so be the mutton which come from 
 Hazeldean Park . 

 from the outside of the coach have descend two passenger , who , alone 
 insensible to the attraction of mutton and trout , refuse to dine , - two 
 melancholy - look foreigner , of whom one be Signor Riccabocca , 
 much the same as we see he now , only that the black suit be less 
 threadbare , the tall form less meagre , and he do not then wear 
 spectacle ; and the other be his servant . " they would walk about 
 while the coach stop . " now the Italian 's eye have be catch by 
 a mouldering , dismantled house on the other side the road , which 
 nevertheless be well situate ; half - way up a green hill , with its 
 aspect due south , a little cascade fall down artificial rockwork , a 
 terrace with a balustrade , and a few broken urn and statue before 
 its ionic portico , while on the roadside stand a board , with 
 character already half effaced , imply that the house be " to be let 
 unfurnished , with or without land . " 

 the abode that look so cheerless , and which have so evidently hang long 
 on hand , be the property of Squire Hazeldean . it have be build by his 
 grandfather on the female side , - a country gentleman who have actually 
 be in Italy   a journey rare enough to boast of in those day , and 
 who , on his return home , have attempt a miniature imitation of an 
 italian villa . he leave an only daughter and sole heiress , who marry 
 Squire Hazeldean 's father ; and since that time , the house , abandon 
 by its proprietor for the large residence of the Hazeldeans , have 
 be uninhabited and neglect . several tenant , indeed , have offer 
 themselves ; but your true country squire be slow in admit upon his 
 own property a rival neighbour . some want shooting . " that , " say the 
 Hazeldeans , who be great sportsman and strict preserver , " be quite 
 out of the question . " other be fine folk from London . " London 
 servant , " say the Hazeldeans , who be moral and prudent people , 
 " would corrupt their own , and bring London price . " other , again , 
 be retire manufacturer , at whom the Hazeldeans turn up their 
 agricultural nose . in short , some be too grand , and other too 
 vulgar . some be refuse because they be know so well : " friend be 
 good at a distance , " say the Hazeldeans ; other because they be not 
 know at all : " no good come of stranger , " say the Hazeldeans . and 
 finally , as the house fall more and more into decay , no one would 
 take it unless it be put into thorough repair : " as if one be make of 
 money ! " say the Hazeldeans . in short , there stand the house unoccupied 
 and ruinous ; and there , on its terrace , stand the two forlorn Italians , 
 survey it with a smile at each other , as for the first time since 
 they set foot in England , they recognize , in dilapidated pilaster and 
 broken statue , in a weed - grow terrace and the remain of an orangery , 
 something that remind they of the land they have leave behind . 

 on return to the inn , Dr. Riccabocca take the occasion to learn from 
 the innkeeper   who be indeed a tenant of the squire   such particular 
 as he could collect ; and a few day afterwards Mr. Hazeldean receive 
 a letter from a solicitor of repute in London , state that a very 
 respectable foreign gentleman have commission he to treat for Clump 
 Lodge , otherwise call the " Casino ; " that the say gentleman do not 
 shoot , live in great seclusion , and , have no family , do not 
 care about the repair of the place , provide only it be make 
 weather - proof , - if the omission of more expensive reparation could 
 render the rent suitable to his finance , which be very limited . 
 the offer come at a fortunate moment , when the steward have just be 
 represent to the squire the necessity of do something to keep the 
 Casino from fall into positive ruin , and the squire be curse the 
 fate which have put the casino into an entail - so that he could not pull 
 it down for the building material . Mr. Hazeldean therefore catch at 
 the proposal even as a fair lady , who have refuse the good offer in the 
 kingdom , catch , at last , at some batter old captain on half - pay , 
 and reply that , as for rent , if the solicitor 's client be a quiet , 
 respectable man , he do not care for that , but that the gentleman might 
 have it for the first year rent - free , on condition of pay the taxis , 
 and put the place a little in order . if they suit each other , they 
 could then come to term . ten day subsequently to this gracious reply , 
 Signor Riccabocca and his servant arrive ; and , before the year 's end , 
 the squire be so content with his tenant that he give he a running 
 lease of seven , fourteen , or twenty - one year , at a rent merely nominal , 
 on condition that Signor Riccabocca would put and maintain the place in 
 repair , bar the roof and fence , which the squire generously renew 
 at his own expense . it be astonishing , by little and little , what a 
 pretty place the Italian have make of it , and , what be more astonishing , 
 how little it have cost he . he have , indeed , paint the wall of the 
 hall , staircase , and the room appropriate to himself , with his own 
 hand . his servant have do the great part of the upholstery . the two 
 between they have get the garden into order . 

 the Italians seem to have take a joint love to the place , and to deck 
 it as they would have do some favourite chapel to their Madonna . 

 it be long before the native reconcile themselves to the odd way 
 of the foreign settler . the first thing that offend they be the 
 exceed smallness of the household bill . three day out of the seven , 
 indeed , both man and master din on nothing else but the vegetable in 
 the garden , and the fish in the neighbouring rill ; when no trout 
 could be catch they fry the minnow   and certainly , even in the good 
 stream , minnow be more frequently catch than trout . the next thing 
 which anger the native quite as much , especially the female part of 
 the neighbourhood , be the very sparing employment the two he creature 
 give to the sex usually deem so indispensable in household matter . at 
 first , indeed , they have no woman - servant at all . but this create 
 such horror that Parson Dale venture a hint upon the matter , which 
 Riccabocca take in very good part ; and an old woman be forthwith 
 engage after some bargaining - at three shilling a week - to wash and 
 scrub as much as she like during the daytime . she always return 
 to her own cottage to sleep . the man - servant , who be style in the 
 neighbourhood " Jackeymo , " do all else for his master , - smooth his 
 room , dust his paper , prepare his coffee , cook his dinner , brush 
 his clothe , and clean his pipe , of which Riccabocca have a large 
 collection . but however close a man 's character , it generally creep out 
 in driblet ; and on many little occasion the Italian have show act of 
 kindness , and , on some more rare occasion , even of generosity , 
 which have serve to silence his calumniator , and by degree he have 
 establish a very fair reputation , - suspect , it be true , of be a 
 little inclined to the Black Art , and of a strange inclination to starve 
 Jackeymo and himself , in other respect harmless enough . 

 Signor Riccabocca have become very intimate , as we have see , at the 
 Parsonage . but not so at the Hall . for though the squire be incline 
 to be very friendly to all his neighbour , he be , like most country 
 gentleman , rather easily huff . Riccabocca have , with great politeness , 
 still with great obstinacy , refuse Mr. Hazeldean 's early invitation 
 to dinner ; and when the squire find that the Italian rarely decline 
 to dine at the Parsonage , he be offend in one of his weak 
 point , - namely , his pride in the hospitality of Hazeldean Hall , - and he 
 cease altogether invitation so churlishly reject . nevertheless , as 
 it be impossible for the squire , however huffed , to bear malice , he now 
 and then remind Riccabocca of his existence by present of game , and 
 would have call on he more often than he do , but that Riccabocca 
 receive he with such excessive politeness that the blunt country 
 gentleman feel shy and put out , and use to say that " to call on 
 Rickeybockey be as bad as go to Court . " 

 but we have leave Dr. Riccabocca on the high road . by this time he have 
 ascend a narrow path that wind by the side of the cascade , he have 
 pass a trelli - work cover with vine , from which Jackeymo have 
 positively succeed in make what he call wine , - a liquid , indeed , 
 that if the cholera have be popularly know in those day , would have 
 sour the mild member of the Board of Health ; for Squire Hazeldean , 
 though a robust man who daily carry off his bottle of port with 
 impunity , have once rashly taste it , do not recover the effect till 
 he have have a bill from the apothecary as long as his own arm . pass 
 this trelli , Dr. Riccabocca enter upon the terrace , with its stone 
 pavement as smooth and trim as hand could make it . here , on neat 
 stand , all his favourite flower be arrange ; here four orange tree 
 be in full blossom ; here a kind of summer - house , or belvidere , build 
 by Jackeymo and himself , make his choose morning room from May till 
 October ; and from this belvidere there be as beautiful an expanse of 
 prospect as if our English Nature have hospitably spread on her green 
 board all that she have to offer as a banquet to the exile . 

 a man without his coat , which be throw over the balustrade , be 
 employ in water the flower , - a man with movement so mechanical , 
 with a face so rigidly grave in its tawny hue , that he seem like an 
 automaton make out of mahogany . 

 " Giacomo , " say Dr. Riccabocca , softly . 

 the automaton stop its hand , and turn its head . 

 " Put by the watering - pot , and come hither , " continue Riccabocca , in 
 italian ; and , move towards the balustrade , he lean over it . Mr. 
 Mitford , the historian , call Jean Jacques " John James . " follow that 
 illustrious example , Giacomo shall be anglify into Jackeymo . Jackeymo 
 come to the balustrade also , and stand a little behind his master . 
 " Friend , " say Riccabocca , " enterprise have not always succeed with 
 we . do not   you think , after all , it be tempt our evil star to rent 
 those field from the landlord ? " Jackeymo cross himself , and make some 
 strange movement with a little coral charm which he wear set in a ring 
 on his finger . 

 " if the Madonna send we luck , and we could hire a lad cheap ? " say 
 Jackeymo , doubtfully . 

 " Piu vale un presente che dui futuri , " -   " a bird in the hand be worth 
 two in the bush . "   - say Riccabocca . 

 " Chi non fa quando pub , non pub , fare quando vuole , " -   " he who will not 
 when he may , when he will it shall have nay . "   - answer Jackeymo , as 
 sententiously as his master . " and the Padrone should think in time that 
 he must lay by for the dower of the poor signorina . " 

 Riccabocca sigh , and make no reply . 

 " she must be that high now ! " say Jackeymo , put his hand on some 
 imaginary line a little above the balustrade . Riccabocca 's eye , raise 
 over the spectacle , follow the hand . 

 " if the Padrone could but see she here - " 

 " I think I do , " mutter the Italian . 

 " he would never let she go from his side till she go to a husband 's , " 
 continue Jackeymo . 

 " but this climate , - she could never stand it , " say Riccabocca , draw 
 his cloak round he , as a north wind take he in the rear . 

 " the orange tree blossom even here with care , " say Jackeymo , turn 
 back to draw down an awning where the orange tree face the north . 
 " see ! " he add , as he return with a sprig in full bud . 

 Dr. Riccabocca bend over the blossom , and then place it in his bosom . 

 " the other one should be there too , " say Jackeymo . 

 " to die - as this do already ! " answer Riccabocca . " say no more . " 

 Jackeymo shrug his shoulder ; and then , glance at his master , draw 
 his hand over his eye . 

 there be a pause . Jackeymo be the first to break it . " but , whether 
 here or there , beauty without money be the orange tree without shelter . 
 if a lad could be get cheap , I would hire the land , and trust for the 
 crop to the Madonna . " 

 " I think I know of such a lad , " say Riccabocca , recover himself , 
 and with his sardonic smile once more lurk about the corner of his 
 mouth , - " a lad make for we . " 

 " Diavolo ! " 

 " no , not the Diavolo ! friend , I have this day see a boy who - refuse 
 sixpence ! " 

 " Cosa stupenda ! " exclaim Jackeymo , open his eye , and let fall 
 the watering - pot . 

 " it be true , my friend . " 

 " take he , Padrone , in Heaven 's name , and the field will grow gold . " 

 " I will think of it , for it must require management to catch such a 
 boy , " say Riccabocca . " meanwhile , light a candle in the parlour , and 
 bring from my bedroom that great folio of Machiavelli . " 




 CHAPTER x. 

 in my next chapter I shall present Squire Hazeldean in patriarchal 
 state , - not exactly under the fig - tree he have plant , but before the 
 stock he have reconstruct , - Squire Hazeldean and his family on the 
 village green ! the canvas be all ready for the colour . 

 but in this chapter I must so far afford a glimpse into antecedent as 
 to let the reader know that there be one member of the family whom he 
 be not likely to meet at present , if ever , on the village green at 
 Hazeldean . 

 our squire lose his father two year after his birth ; his mother 
 be very handsome - and so be her jointure ; she marry again at the 
 expiration of her year of mourning ; the object of her second choice be 
 Colonel Egerton . 

 in every generation of Englishmen   at least since the lively reign of 
 Charles II .   there be a few whom some elegant Genius skim off from 
 the milk of human nature , and reserve for the cream of society . Colonel 
 Egerton be one of these terque quaterque beati , and dwelt apart on 
 a top shelf in that delicate porcelain dish - not bestow upon vulgar 
 buttermilk - which person of fashion call the great World . Mighty be 
 the marvel of Pall Mall , and profound be the pity of Park Lane , 
 when this supereminent personage condescend to lower himself into a 
 husband . but Colonel Egerton be not a mere gaudy butterfly ; he have the 
 provident instinct ascribe to the bee . Youth have pass from he , and 
 carry off much solid property in its flight ; he see that a time be 
 fast come when a home , with a partner who could help to maintain it , 
 would be conducive to his comfort , and an occasional hum - drum evening 
 by the fireside beneficial to his health . in the midst of one season at 
 Brighton , to which gay place he have accompany the Prince of Wales , 
 he see a widow , who , though in the weed of mourning , do not appear 
 inconsolable . her person please his taste ; the account of her jointure 
 satisfy his understanding ; he contrive an introduction , and bring 
 a brief wooing to a happy close . the late Mr. Hazeldean have so far 
 anticipate the chance of the young widow 's second espousal , that , in 
 case of that event , he transfer , by his testamentary disposition , 
 the guardianship of his infant heir from the mother to two squire whom 
 he have name his executor . this circumstance combine with her new tie 
 somewhat to alienate Mrs. Hazeldean from the pledge of her former love ; 
 and when she have bear a son to Colonel Egerton , it be upon that child 
 that her maternal affection gradually concentrate . 

 William Hazeldean be send by his guardian to a large provincial 
 academy , at which his forefather have receive their education time out 
 of mind . at first he spend his holiday with Mrs. Egerton ; but as she 
 now reside either in London , or follow her lord to Brighton , to 
 partake of the gayetie at the Pavilion , so as he grow old , William , 
 who have a hearty affection for country life , and of whose bluff manner 
 and rural breed Mrs. Egerton   have grow exceedingly refined   be 
 openly ashamed , ask and obtain permission to spend his vacation 
 either with his guardian or at the old Hall . he go late to a small 
 college at Cambridge , endow in the fifteenth century by some ancestral 
 Hazeldean ; and leave it , on come of age , without take a degree . a 
 few year afterwards he marry a young lady , country bear and breed like 
 himself . 

 meanwhile his half - brother , Audley Egerton , may be say to have begin 
 his initiation into the beau monde before he have well cast aside his 
 coral and bell ; he have be fondle in the lap of duchess , and 
 have gallop across the room astride on the cane of ambassador and 
 prince . for Colonel Egerton be not only very highly connected , not 
 only one of the Dii majore of fashion , but he have the still rare good 
 fortune to be an exceedingly popular man with all who know he , - so 
 popular , that even the fine lady whom he have adore and abandon 
 forgave he for marry out of " the set , " and continue to be as 
 friendly as if he have not marry at all . People who be commonly 
 call heartless be never weary of do kind thing to the Egertons . 
 when the time come for Audley to leave the preparatory school at which 
 his infancy bud forth amongst the stateliest of the little lily 
 of the field , and go to Eton , half the fifth and sixth form have be 
 canvass to be exceedingly civil to young Egerton . the boy soon show 
 that he inherit his father 's talent for acquire popularity , and 
 that to this talent he add those which put popularity to use . without 
 achieve any scholastic distinction , he yet contrive to establish at 
 Eton the most desirable reputation which a boy can obtain , - namely , that 
 among his own contemporary , the reputation of a boy who be sure to 
 do something when he grow to be a man . as a gentleman - commoner at Christ 
 Church , Oxford , he continue to sustain this high expectation , though he 
 win no prize , and take but an ordinary degree ; and at Oxford the future 
 ' something " become more define , - it be ' something in public life " that 
 this young man be to do . 

 while he be yet at the University , both his parent die , within a few 
 month of each other . and when Audley Egerton come of age , he succeed 
 to a paternal property which be suppose to be large , and indeed have 
 once be so ; but Colonel Egerton have be too lavish a man to enrich 
 his heir , and about L1500 a year be all that sale and mortgage leave 
 of an estate that have formerly approach a rental of L10,000 . 

 still , Audley be consider to be opulent ; and he do not dispel that 
 favourable notion by any imprudent exhibition of parsimony . on enter 
 the world of London , the Clubs fly open to receive he , and he wake 
 one morning to find himself , not indeed famous - but the fashion . to this 
 fashion he at once give a certain gravity and value , he associate as 
 much as possible with public man and political lady , he succeed in 
 confirm the notion that he be " bear to ruin or to rule the state . " 

 the dearest and most intimate friend of Audley Egerton be Lord 
 L'Estrange , from whom he have be inseparable at Eton , and who now , if 
 Audley Egerton be the fashion , be absolutely the rage in London . 

 Harley , Lord L'Estrange , be the only son of the Earl of Lansmere , a 
 nobleman of considerable wealth , and ally , by intermarriage , to 
 the lofty and most powerful family in England . Lord Lansmere , 
 nevertheless , be but little know in the circle of London . he live 
 chiefly on his estate , occupy himself with the various duty of a 
 great proprietor , and when he come to the metropolis , it be rather to 
 save than to spend ; so that he could afford to give his son a very ample 
 allowance , when Harley , at the age of sixteen   have already attain 
 to the sixth form at Eton , leave school for one of the regiment of the 
 Guards . 

 few know what to make of Harley L'Estrange , - and that be , perhaps , 
 the reason why he be so much thought of . he have be by far the 
 most brilliant boy of his time at Eton , - not only the boast of the 
 cricket - ground , but the marvel of the schoolroom ; yet so full of whim 
 and oddity , and seem to achieve his triumph with so little aid 
 from steadfast application , that he have not leave behind he the same 
 expectation of solid eminence which his friend and senior , Audley 
 Egerton , have excite . his eccentricity , his quaint saying , and 
 out - of - the - way action , become as notable in the great world as they have 
 be in the small one of a public school . that he be very clever there 
 be no doubt , and that the cleverness be of a high order might be 
 surmise , not only from the originality but the independence of his 
 character . he dazzle the world , without seem to care for its praise 
 or its censure , - dazzle it , as it be , because he could not help 
 shin . he have some strange notion , whether political or social , which 
 rather frighten his father . accord to Southey , " a man should be 
 no more ashamed of have be a republican than of have be young . " 
 Youth and extravagant opinion naturally go together . I do not   know 
 whether Harley L'Estrange be a republican at the age of eighteen ; but 
 there be no young man in London who seem to care less for be heir 
 to an illustrious name and some forty or fifty thousand pound a year . 
 it be a vulgar fashion in that day to play the exclusive , and cut 
 person who wear bad neckcloth , and call themselves Smith or Johnson . 
 Lord L'Estrange never cut any one , and it be quite enough to slight 
 some worthy man because of his neckcloth or his birth to insure to 
 the offender the pointed civility of this eccentric successor to the 
 Belforts and the Wildairs . 

 it be the wish of his father that Harley , as soon as he come of age , 
 should represent the borough of Lansmere   which say borough be the 
 single plague of the earl 's life . but this wish be never realize . 
 suddenly , when the young idol of London still want some two or three 
 year of his majority , a new whim appear to seize he . he 
 withdraw entirely from society ; he leave unanswere the most pressing 
 three - cornered note of inquiry and invitation that ever strew the 
 table of a young guardsman ; he be rarely see anywhere in his former 
 haunt , - when see , be either alone or with Egerton ; and his gay 
 spirit seem wholly to have leave he . a profound melancholy be 
 write in his countenance , and breathe in the listless tone of 
 his voice . about this time a vacancy happen to occur for the 
 representation of Lansmere , Harley make it his special request to his 
 father that the family interest might be give to Audley Egerton , - a 
 request which be back by all the influence of his lady mother , 
 who share in the esteem which her son feel for his friend . the earl 
 yield ; and Egerton , accompany by Harley , go down to Lansmere Park , 
 which adjoin the borough , in order to be introduce to the elector . 
 this visit make a notable epoch in the history of many personage who 
 figure in my narrative ; but at present I content myself with say 
 that circumstance arise which , just as the canvass for the new election 
 commence , cause both L'Estrange and Audley to absent themselves from 
 the scene of action , and that the last even write to Lord Lansmere 
 express his intention of decline to contest the borough . 

 fortunately for the parliamentary career of Audley Egerton , the election 
 have become to Lord Lansmere not only a matter of public importance , but 
 of personal feeling . he resolve that the battle should be fight out , 
 even in the absence of the candidate , and at his own expense . Hitherto 
 the contest for this distinguished borough have be , to use the language 
 of Lord Lansmere , " conduct in the spirit of gentleman , " - that be to 
 say , the only opponent to the Lansmere interest have be find in one 
 or the other of the two rival family in the same county ; and as the 
 earl be a hospitable , courteous man , much respect and like by the 
 neighbouring gentry , so the hostile candidate have always interlard his 
 speech with profuse compliment to his Lordship 's high character , 
 and civil expression as to his Lordship 's candidate . but , thank to 
 successive election , one of these two family have come to an end , 
 and its actual representative be now reside within the Rules of the 
 Bench ; the head of the other family be the sit member , and , by an 
 amicable agreement with the lansinere interest , he remain as neutral 
 as it be in the power of any sit member to be amidst the passion 
 of an intractable committee . accordingly it have be hop that Egerton 
 would come in without opposition , when , the very day on which he have 
 abruptly leave the place , a handbill , sign " Haverill Dashmore , Captain 
 R. N. , Baker Street , Portman Square , " announce , in very spirited 
 language , the intention of that gentleman " to emancipate the borough 
 from the unconstitutional domination of an oligarchical faction , not 
 with a view to his own political aggrandizement , - indeed at great 
 personal inconvenience , - but actuate solely by abhorrence to tyranny , 
 and patriotic passion for the purity of election . " 

 this announcement be follow , within two hour , by the arrival of 
 Captain Dashmore himself , in a carriage and four , cover with yellow 
 favour , and fill , inside and out , with harumscarum - look friend , 
 who have come down with he to share the canvass and partake the fun . 

 Captain Dashmore be a thorough sailor , who have , however , conceive a 
 disgust to the profession from the date in which a minister 's nephew have 
 be appoint to the command of a ship to which the captain consider 
 himself unquestionably entitle . it be just to the minister to add that 
 Captain Dashmore have show as little regard for order from a distance 
 as have immortalize Nelson himself ; but then the disobedience have not 
 achieve the same redeem success as that of Nelson , and Captain 
 Dashmore ought to have think himself fortunate in escape a severer 
 treatment than the loss of promotion . but no man know when he be 
 well off ; and retire on half pay , just as he come into unexpected 
 possession of some forty or fifty thousand pound , bequeath by a 
 distant relation , Captain Dashmore be seize with a vindictive 
 desire to enter parliament , and inflict oratorical chastisement on the 
 Administration . 

 a very few hour suffice to show the sea - captain to be a most capital 
 electioneerer for a popular but not enlighten constituency . it be true 
 that he talk the sad nonsense ever hear from an open window ; but 
 then his joke be so broad , his manner so hearty , his voice so big , 
 that in those dark day , before the schoolmaster be abroad , he would 
 have beat your philosophical Radical and moralizing Democrat hollow . 
 moreover , he kiss all the woman , old and young , with the zest of a 
 sailor who have know what it be to be three year at sea without 
 sight of a beardless lip ; he throw open all the public - house , ask a 
 numerous committee every day to dinner , and , chuck his purse up in 
 the air , declare " he would stick to his gun while there be a shot in 
 the locker . " till then , there have be but little political difference 
 between the candidate support by Lord Lansmere 's interest and the 
 oppose party ; for country gentleman , in those day , be pretty 
 much of the same way of thinking , and the question have be really 
 local , - namely , whether the Lansmere interest should or should not 
 prevail over that of the two squire - archical family who have alone , 
 hitherto , venture to oppose it . but though Captain Dashmore be really 
 a very loyal man , and much too old a sailor to think that the State 
  which , accord to establish metaphor , be a vessel par excellence 
 should admit Jack upon quarterdeck , yet , what with talk against lord 
 and aristocracy , job and abuse , and search through no very refined 
 vocabulary for the strong epithet to apply to those irritate 
 noun - substantive , his bile have get the well of his understanding , 
 and he become fuddle , as it be , by his own eloquence . thus , though 
 as innocent of jacobinical design as he be incapable of set the 
 Thames on fire , you would have guess he , by his speech , to be one 
 of the most determined incendiary that ever apply a match to the 
 combustible material of a contested election ; while , be by no mean 
 accustom to respect his adversary , he could not have treat 
 the Earl of Lansmere with less ceremony if his Lordship have be a 
 Frenchman . he usually designate that respectable nobleman , who be 
 still in the prime of life , by the title of " Old Pompous ; " and the 
 mayor , who be never see abroad but in top - boot , and the solicitor , 
 who be of a large build , receive from his irreverent wit the joint 
 sobriquet of " Tops and bottom " ! hence the election have now become , as I 
 say before , a personal matter with my Lord , and , indeed , with the great 
 head of the Lansmere interest . the earl seem to consider his very 
 coronet at stake in the question . " the man from Baker Street , " with his 
 preternatural audacity , appear to he a be ominous and awful - not 
 so much to be regard with resentment as with superstitious terror . he 
 feel as feel the dignified Montezuma , when that ruffianly Cortez , with 
 his handful of spanish rapscallion , beard he in his own capital , 
 and in the midst of his mexican splendour . the god be menace if 
 man could be so insolent ! wherefore , say my Lord tremulously , 
 " the Constitution be go if the Man from Baker Street come in for 
 Lansmere ! " 

 but in the absence of Audley Egerton , the election look extremely 
 ugly , and Captain Dashmore gain ground hourly , when the Lansmere 
 solicitor happily bethink he of a notable proxy for the missing 
 candidate . the squire of Hazeldean , with his young wife , have be 
 invite by the earl in honour of Audley ; and in the squire the solicitor 
 behold the only mortal who could cope with the sea - captain , - a man with 
 a voice as burly and a face as bold ; a man who , if permit for the 
 nonce by Mrs. Hazeldean , would kiss all the woman no less heartily than 
 the captain kiss they ; and who be , moreover , a tall and a handsomer 
 and a young man , - all three great recommendation in the kissing 
 department of a contested election . yes , to canvass the borough , and 
 to speak from the window , Squire Hazeldean would be even more popularly 
 presentable than the London - breed and accomplish Audley Egerton 
 himself . 

 the squire , apply to and urge on all side , at first say bluntly 
 that he would do anything in reason to serve his brother , but that he 
 do not like , for his own part , appear , even in proxy , as a lord 's 
 nominee ; and moreover , if he be to be sponsor for his brother , why , 
 he must promise and vow , in his name , to be stanch and true to the land 
 they live by ! and how could he tell that Audley , when once he get into 
 the House , would not forget the land , and then he , William Hazeldean , 
 would be make a liar , and look like a turncoat ! 

 but these scruple be overrule by the argument of the gentleman 
 and the entreaty of the lady , who take in the election that intense 
 interest which those gentle creature usually do take in all matter of 
 strife and contest , the squire at length consent to confront the man 
 from Baker Street , and go accordingly into the thing with that good 
 heart and old english spirit with which he go into everything whereon 
 he have once make up his mind . 

 the expectation form of the squire 's capacity for popular 
 electioneering be fully realize . he talk quite as much nonsense as 
 Captain Dashmore on every subject except the land interest ; there he 
 be great , for he know the subject well , - know it by the instinct that 
 come with practice , and compare to which all your showy theory be 
 mere cobwebs and moonshine . 

 the agricultural outvoter - many of whom , not live under Lord 
 Lansmere , but be small yeoman , have hitherto pride themselves on 
 their independence , and go against my Lord - could not in their heart 
 go against one who be every inch the farmer 's friend . they begin to 
 share in the earl 's personal interest against the Man from Baker Street ; 
 and big fellow , with leg big round than Captain Dashmore 's tight 
 little body , and huge whip in their hand , be soon see enter 
 the shop , " intimidate the elector , " as Captain Dashmore indignantly 
 declare . 

 these new recruit make a great difference in the musterroll of the 
 Lansmere book ; and when the day for polling arrive , the result be a 
 fair question for even bet . at the last hour , after a neck - and - neck 
 contest , Mr. Audley Egerton beat the captain by two vote ; and the name 
 of these voter be John Avenel , resident freeman , and his son - in - law , 
 Mark Fairfield , an outvoter , who , though a Lansmere freeman , have settle 
 in Hazeldean , where he have obtain the situation of head carpenter on 
 the squire 's estate . 

 these vote be unexpected ; for though Mark Fairfield have come to 
 Lansmere on purpose to support the squire 's brother , and though the 
 Avenels have be always stanch supporter of the Lansmere blue interest , 
 yet a severe affliction   as to the nature of which , not desire to 
 sadden the opening of my story , I be considerately silent   have befall 
 both these person , and they have leave the town on the very day after 
 Lord L'Estrange and Mr. Egerton have quit Lansmere Park . 

 whatever might have be the gratification of the squire , as a canvasser 
 and a brother , at Mr. Egerton 's triumph , it be much damp when , on 
 leave the dinner give in honour of the victory at the Lansmere Arms , 
 and about , with no steady step , to enter a carriage which be to convey 
 he to his Lordship 's house , a letter be put into his hand by one of 
 the gentleman who have accompany the captain to the scene of action ; 
 and the perusal of that letter , and a few whisper word from the 
 bearer thereof , send the squire back to Mrs. Hazeldean a much soberer 
 man than she have venture to hope for . the fact be , that on the day of 
 nomination , the captain have honour Mr. Hazeldean with many poetical 
 and figurative appellation , - such as " Prize Ox , " " Tony Lumpkin , " 
 " blood - suck vampire , " and " Brotherly Warming - Pan , " - the squire have 
 retort by a joke about " Saltwater Jack ; " and the captain , who like all 
 satirist be extremely susceptible and thin - skinned , could not consent 
 to be call " salt - water Jack " by a " Prize Ox " and a " Bloodsucking 
 Vampire . " 

 the letter , therefore , now convey to Mr. Hazeldean by a gentleman , 
 who , be from the Sister Country , be deem the most fitting 
 accomplice in the honourable destruction of a brother mortal , contain 
 nothing more nor less than an invitation to single combat ; and the 
 bearer thereof , with the suave politeness enjoin by etiquette on such 
 well - breed homicidal occasion , suggest the expediency of appoint 
 the place of meeting in the neighbourhood of London , in order to prevent 
 interference from the suspicious authority of Lansmere . 

 the native of some country - the warlike French in particular - think 
 little of that formal operation which go by the name of DUELLING . 
 indeed , they seem rather to like it than otherwise . but there be nothing 
 your thorough - pace Englishman - a Hazeldean of Hazeldean - consider with 
 more repugnance and aversion than that same cold - blooded ceremonial . it 
 be not within the range of an Englishman 's ordinary habit of thinking . 
 he prefer go to law , - a much more destructive proceeding of the two . 
 nevertheless , if an Englishman must fight , why , he will fight . he say 
 " it be very foolish ; " he be sure " it be most unchristianlike ; " he agree 
 with all that Philosophy , Preacher , and Press have lay down on the 
 subject ; but he make his will , say his prayer , and go out - like a 
 heathen . 

 it never , therefore , occur to the squire to show the white feather 
 upon this unpleasant occasion . the next day , feign excuse to attend 
 the sale of a hunting stud at Tattersall 's , he ruefully go up to 
 London , after take a peculiarly affectionate leave of his wife . 
 indeed , the squire feel convinced that he should never return home 
 except in a coffin . " it stand to reason , " say he to himself , " that 
 a man who have be actually pay by the King 's Government for shoot 
 people ever since he be a little boy in a midshipman 's jacket , must 
 be a dead hand at the job . I should not mind if it be with 
 double - barrel Mantons and small shot ; but ball and pistol , they be 
 not   human nor sportsmanlike ! " however , the squire , after settle his 
 worldly affair , and hunt up an old college friend who undertake to 
 be his second , proceed to a sequester corner of Wimbledon Common , 
 and plant himself , not sideways , as one ought to do in such encounter 
  the which posture the squire swear be an unmanly way of shirk , 
 but full front to the mouth of his adversary 's pistol , with such sturdy 
 composure that Captain Dashmore , who , though an excellent shot , be at 
 bottom as good - natured a fellow as ever live , testify his admiration 
 by let off his gallant opponent with a ball in the fleshy part of 
 the shoulder , after which he declare himself perfectly satisfied . 
 the party then shake hand , mutual apology be exchange , and 
 the squire , much to his astonishment to find himself still alive , 
 be convey to Limmer 's Hotel , where , after a considerable amount of 
 anguish , the ball be extract and the wound heal . now it be all 
 over , the squire feel very much raise in his own conceit ; and when he 
 be in a humour more than ordinarily fierce , that perilous event become 
 a favourite allusion with he . 

 he consider , moreover , that his brother have incur at his hand the 
 most lasting obligation ; and that , have procure Audley 's return to 
 parliament , and defend his interest at risk of his own life , he have 
 an absolute right to dictate to that gentleman how to vote , - upon all 
 matter , at least , connect with the land interest . and when , not 
 very long after Audley take his seat in parliament   which he do not 
 do for some month , he think proper both to vote and to speak in a 
 manner wholly belie the promise the squire have make on his behalf , 
 Mr. Hazeldean write he such a trimmer that it could not but produce 
 an unconciliatory reply . shortly afterwards the squire 's exasperation 
 reach the culminate point ; for , have to pass through Lansmere on 
 a market - day , he be hoot by the very farmer whom he have induce to 
 vote for his brother ; and , justly impute the disgrace to Audley , he 
 never hear the name of that traitor to the land mention without a 
 heighten colour and an indignant expletive . M. de Ruqueville - who be 
 the great wit of his day - have , like the squire , a half - brother , with 
 whom he be not on the good of term , and of whom he always speak as 
 his " frere de loin ! " Audley Egerton be thus Squire Hazeldean 's 
 " distant - brother " ! 

 enough of these explanatory antecedent , - let we return to the stock . 




 CHAPTER XI . 

 the squire 's carpenter be take from the park pale and set to 
 work at the parish stock . then come the painter and colour they 
 a beautiful dark blue , with white border - and a white rim round the 
 hole - with an ornamental flourish in the middle . it be the gay 
 public edifice in the whole village , though the village possess 
 no less than three other monument of the vitruvian genius of the 
 Hazeldeans , - to wit , the almshouse , the school , and the parish pump . 

 a more elegant , enticing , coquettish pair of stock never gladden the 
 eye of a justice of the peace . 

 and Squire Hazeldean 's eye be gladden . in the pride of his heart he 
 bring all the family down to look at the stock . the squire 's family 
  omit the frere de loin   consist of Mrs. Hazeldean , his wife ; 
 next , of Miss Jemima Hazeldean , his first cousin ; thirdly , of Mr. 
 Francis Hazeldean , his only son ; and fourthly , of Captain Barnabas 
 Higginbotham , a distant relation , - who , indeed , strictly speak , 
 be not of the family , but only a visitor ten month in the year . Mrs. 
 Hazeldean be every inch the lady , - the lady of the parish . in she 
 comely , florid , and somewhat sunburned countenance , there be an equal 
 expression of majesty and benevolence ; she have a blue eye that invite 
 liking , and an aquiline nose that command respect . Mrs. Hazeldean have 
 no affectation of fine air , no wish to be great and handsomer and 
 cleverer than she be . she know herself , and her station , and thank 
 Heaven for it . there be about her speech and manner something of the 
 shortness and bluntness which often characterize royalty ; and if the 
 lady of a parish be not a queen in her own circle , it be never the fault 
 of a parish . Mrs. Hazeldean dress her part to perfection . she wear 
 silk that seem heirloom , - so thick be they , so substantial and 
 impose ; and over these , when she be in her own domain , the whitest 
 of apron ; while at her waist be see no fiddle - faddle chatelaine , with 
 breloque and trumpery , but a good honest gold watch to mark the 
 time , and a long pair of scissor to cut off the dead leaf from her 
 flower , - for she be a great horticulturalist . when occasion need , 
 Mrs. Hazeldean could , however , lie by her more sumptuous and imperial 
 raiment for a stout riding - habit , of blue Saxony , and canter by her 
 husband 's side to see the hound throw off . Nay , on the day on which 
 Mr. Hazeldean drive his famous fast - trotting cob to the market town , it 
 be rarely that you do not see his wife on the left side of the gig . 
 she care as little as her lord do for wind and weather , and in the 
 midst of some pelting shower her pleasant face peep over the collar 
 and cape of a stout dreadnought , expand into smile and bloom as 
 some frank rose , that open from its petal , and rejoice in the dew . 
 it be easy to see that the worthy couple have marry for love ; they 
 be as little apart as they could help it . and still , on the first 
 of September , if the house be not full of company which demand she 
 care , Mrs. Hazeldean ' step out " over the stubble by her husband 's 
 side , with as light a tread and as blithe an eye as when , in the first 
 bridal year , she have enchant the squire by her genial sympathy with 
 his sport . 

 so there now stand Harriet Hazeldean , one hand lean on the squire 's 
 broad shoulder , the other thrust into her apron , and try she good to 
 share her husband 's enthusiasm for his own public - spirited patriotism , 
 in the renovation of the parish stock . a little behind , with two 
 finger rest on the thin arm of Captain Barnabas , stand Miss Jemima , 
 the orphan daughter of the squire 's uncle , by a runaway imprudent 
 marriage with a young lady who belong to a family which have be at 
 war with the Hazeldeans since the reign of Charles the First respect 
 a right of way to a small wood   or rather spring   of about an acre , 
 through a piece of furze land , which be let to a brickmaker at twelve 
 shilling a year . the wood belong to the Hazeldeans , the furze land 
 to the Sticktorights   an old Saxon family , if ever there be one . every 
 twelfth year , when the fagot and timber be fell , this feud break 
 out afresh ; for the Sticktorights refuse to the Hazeldeans the right to 
 cart off the say fagot and timber through the only way by which a cart 
 could possibly pass . it be just to the Hazeldeans to say that they have 
 offer to buy the land at ten time its value . but the Sticktorights , 
 with equal magnanimity , have declare that they would not " alienate the 
 family property for the convenience of the good squire that ever stand 
 upon shoe leather . " therefore , every twelfth year , there be always 
 a great breach of the peace on the part of both Hazeldeans and 
 Sticktorights , magistrate and deputy - lieutenant though they be . 
 the question be fairly fight out by their respective dependant , 
 and follow by various action for assault and trespass . as the legal 
 question of right be extremely obscure , it never have be properly 
 decide ; and , indeed , neither party wish it to be decide , each at 
 heart have some doubt of the propriety of its own claim . a marriage 
 between a young son of the Hazeldeans and a young daughter of the 
 Sticktorights be view with equal indignation by both family ; 
 and the consequence have be that the runaway couple , unblessed and 
 unforgiven , have scramble through life as they could , upon the scanty 
 pay of the husband , who be in a march regiment , and the interest 
 of L1000 , which be the wife 's fortune independent of her parent . they 
 die and leave an only daughter   upon whom the maternal L1000 have be 
 settle , about the time that the squire come of age and into possession 
 of his estate . and though he inherit all the ancestral hostility 
 towards the Sticktorights , it be not in his nature to be unkind to a 
 poor orphan , who be , after all , the child of a Hazeldean . therefore he 
 have educate and foster Jemima with as much tenderness as if she have 
 be his sister ; put out her L1000 at nurse , and devote , from the ready 
 money which have accrue from the rent during his minority , as much as 
 make her fortune   with her own accumulate at compound interest   no less 
 than L4000 , the ordinary marriage portion of the daughter of Hazeldean . 
 on her come of age , he transfer this sum to her absolute disposal , 
 in order that she might feel herself independent , see a little more of 
 the world than she could at Hazeldean , have candidate to choose from 
 if she deign to marry ; or enough to live upon , if she choose to remain 
 single . Miss Jemima have somewhat avail herself of this liberty , by 
 occasional visit to Cheltenham and other watering - place . but her 
 grateful affection to the squire be such that she could never bear to 
 be long away from the Hall . and this be the more praise to her heart , 
 inasmuch as she be far from take kindly to the prospect of be 
 an old maid ; and there be so few bachelor in the neighbourhood of 
 Hazeldean , that she could not but have that prospect before her eye 
 whenever she look out of the Hall window . Miss Jemima be indeed 
 one of the most kindly and affectionate of being feminine ; and if she 
 dislike the thought of single blessedness , it really be from those 
 innocent and womanly instinct towards the tender charity of hearth 
 and home , without which a lady , however otherwise estimable , be little 
 well than a Minerva in bronze . but , whether or not , despite her 
 fortune and her face , which last , though not strictly handsome , be 
 pleasing , and would have be positively pretty if she have laugh more 
 often   for when she laugh , there appear three charming dimple , 
 invisible when she be grave , - whether or not , I say , it be the fault 
 of our insensibility or her own fastidiousness , Miss Jemima approach 
 her thirtieth year , and be still Miss Jemima . now , therefore , that 
 beautify laugh of hers be very rarely hear , and she have of late 
 become confirm in two opinion , not at all conducive to laughter . one 
 be a conviction of the general and progressive wickedness of the male 
 sex , and the other be a decided and lugubrious belief that the world 
 be come to an end . Miss Jemima be now accompany by a small canine

 a slim and elegant figure ; the less say about the face the well , a

 come to an end , only he think it would last his time . 

 quite apart from all the rest , with the nonchalant survey of virgin

 the squire be a stanch politician of the old school , and he do

 Master Stirn be a formidable personage , - more formidable than the 
 squire himself , - as , indeed , a squire 's right hand be generally more 
 formidable than the head can pretend to be . he inspire the great awe , 
 because , like the stock of which he be depute guardian , his power 
 be undefined and obscure , and he have no particular place in the 
 out - of - door establishment . he be not the steward , yet he do much of

 for the squire call himself his own farm - bailiff ; nevertheless , Mr. 
 Hazeldean sow and plough , crop and stock , buy and sell , very 
 much as Mr. Stirn condescend to advise . he be not the park - keeper , 
 for he neither shoot the deer nor superintend the preserve ; but it be 
 he who always find out who have break a park pale or snare a rabbit . 
 in short , what may be call all the harsh duty of a large landed 
 proprietor devolve , by custom and choice , upon Mr. Stirn . if a labourer 
 be to be discharge or a rent enforce , and the squire know that he 
 should be talk over , and that the steward would be as soft as himself , 
 Mr. Stirn be sure to be the avenge messenger , to pronounce the word 
 of fate ; so that he appear to the inhabitant of Hazeldean like the 
 poet 's Saeva Necessitas , a vague incarnation of remorseless power , arm 
 with whip , nail , and wedge . the very brute creation stand in awe of 
 Mr. Stirn . the calf know that it be he who single out which should 
 be sell to the butcher , and huddle up into a corner with beat 
 heart at his grim footstep ; the sow grunt , the duck quack , the hen 
 bristle her feather and call to her chick when Mr. Stirn draw near . 
 Nature have set she stamp upon he . indeed , it may be question whether 
 the great M. de Chambray himself , surname the brave , have an aspect so 
 awe - inspiring as that of Mr. Stirn ; albeit the face of that hero be so 
 terrible , that a man who have be his lackey , see his portrait after 
 he have be dead twenty year , fall a trembling all over like a leaf ! 

 " and what the plague be you do here ? " say Mr. Stirn , as he wave

 a hullabaloo , you woman , you ! that I suspect the squire will be send 
 out to know if the village be on fire . go home , will ye ? high time 
 indeed to have the stock ready , when you get squall and conspire 
 under the very nose of a justice of the peace , just as the french 
 revolutioner do afore they cut off their king 's head ! my hair stand 
 on end to look at ye . " but already , before half this address be 
 deliver , the crowd have disperse in all direction , - the woman still 
 keep together , and the man sneak off towards the ale - house . such 
 be the beneficent effect of the fatal stock on the first day of their 
 resuscitation . however , in the break up of every crowd there must always 
 be one who get off the last ; and it so happen that our friend Lenny 
 Fairfield , who have mechanically approach close to the stock , the 
 well to hear the oracular opinion of Gaffer Solomons , have no less

 stock ; and there now , as if fascinate , he still cower , not dare

 " Hallo , sir - what the deuce , lay a mine to blow up the stock ! just 
 like Guy Fox and the Gunpowder Plot , I declare ! what ha ' you get in 
 your willanous little fist there ? " 

 " nothing , sir , " say Lenny , open his palm . " nothing - um ! " say Mr. 
 Stirn , much dissatisfied ; and then , as he gaze more deliberately ,
