 over his brow ; for Mr. Stirn , who value himself much on his learning , 
 and who , indeed , by dint of more knowledge as well as more wit than 
 his neighbour , have attain his present eminent station of life , be 
 extremely anxious that his only son should also be a scholar . that 
 wish - 

  " the god disperse in empty air . " 

 Master Stirn be a notable dunce at the parson 's school , while Lenny 
 Fairfield be the pride and boast of it ; therefore Mr. Stirn be 
 naturally , and almost justifiably , ill - dispose towards Lenny Fairfield , 
 who have appropriate to himself the praise which Mr. Stirn have design 
 for his son . 

 " um ! " say the right - hand man , glower on Lenny malignantly , " you 
 be the pattern boy of the village , be you ? very well , sir ! then I put 
 these here stock under your care , and you will keep off the other boy 
 from sit on them , and pick off the paint , and play three - hole 
 and chuck - farthing , as I declare they have   be a doing , just in front of 
 the elewation . now , you know your ' sponsibilitie , little boy , - and a 
 great honour they be too , for the like o ' you . 

 " if any damage be do , it be to you I shall look ; d ' ye 
 understand ? - and that be what the squire say to I . so you see what it 
 be to be a pattern boy , Master Lenny ! " with that Mr. Stirn give a loud 
 crack of the cart - whip , by way of military honour , over the head of 
 the vicegerent he have thus create , and stride off to pay a visit to two 
 young unsuspecting pup , whose ear and tail he have graciously promise 
 their proprietor to crop that evening . nor , albeit few charge could 
 be more obnoxious than that of deputy - governor or charge - d'affaires 
 extraordinaire to the parish stock , nor one more likely to render 
 Lenny Fairfield odious to his contemporary , ought he to have be 
 insensible to the signal advantage of his condition over that of the two 
 sufferer , against whose ear and tail Mr. Stirn have no special motif 
 of resentment . to every bad there be a bad ; and fortunately for 
 little boy , and even for grown man , whom the Stirns of the world regard 
 malignly , the majesty and law protect their ear , and the merciful 
 forethought of nature deprive their remote ancestor of the privilege 
 of entail tail upon they . have it be otherwise - consider what 
 handle tail would have give to the oppressor , how many trap envy 
 would have lay for they , how often they must have be scratch and 
 mutilate by the briar of life , how many good excuse would have be 
 find for lop , docking , and trim they - I fear that only the 
 lap - dog of Fortune would have go to the grave tail - whole . 




 CHAPTER XII . 

 the card - table be set out in the drawing - room at Hazeldean Hall ; though 
 the little party be still linger in the deep recess of the large 
 bay window , which   in itself of dimension that would have swallow up 
 a moderate - sized London parlour   hold the great round tea - table , with 
 all appliance and mean to boot , - for the beautiful summer moon shed on 
 the sward so silvery a lustre , and the tree cast so quiet a shadow , 
 and the flower and new - mow hay send up so grateful a perfume , that 
 to close the window , draw the curtain , and call for other light than 
 those of heaven would have be an abuse of the prose of life which even 
 Captain Barnabas , who regard whist as the business of town and the 
 holiday of the country , shrink from suggest . without , the scene , 
 beheld by the clear moonlight , have the beauty peculiar to the 
 garden - ground round those old - fashioned country residence which , though 
 a little modernized , still preserve their original character , - the 
 velvet lawn , stud with large plot of flower , shaded and scented , 
 here to the left by lilacs , laburnum , and rich syringas ; there , to the 
 right , give glimpse , over low clip yew , of a green bowling - alley , 
 with the white column of a summer - house build after the dutch taste , 
 in the reign of William III . ; and in front steal away under covert 
 of those still cedar , into the wilder landscape of the well - woode 
 undulating park . within , view by the placid glimmer of the moon , the 
 scene be no less characteristic of the abode of that race which have no 
 parallel in other land , and which , alas ! be somewhat lose its native 
 idiosyncrasy in this , - the stout country gentleman , not the fine 
 gentleman of the country ; the country gentleman somewhat softened and 
 civilized from the mere sportsman or farmer , but still plain and homely ; 
 relinquish the old hall for the drawing - room , and with book not 
 three month old on his table , instead of Fox 's " Martyrs " and Baker 's 
 " Chronicle , " yet still retain many a sacred old prejudice , that , like 
 the knot in his native oak , rather add to the ornament of the grain 
 than take from the strength of the tree . opposite to the window , the 
 high chimneypiece rise to the heavy cornice of the ceiling , with dark 
 panel glisten against the moonlight . the broad and rather clumsy 
 chintz sofas and settee of the reign of George III . contrast at 
 interval with the tall - back chair of a far more distant generation , 
 when lady in fardingale and gentleman in trunk - hose seem never to 
 have indulge in horizontal position . the wall , of shin wainscot , 
 be thickly cover , chiefly with family picture ; though now and then 
 some dutch fair or battle - piece show that a former proprietor have be 
 less exclusive in his taste for the art . the pianoforte stand open 
 near the fireplace ; a long dwarf bookcase at the far end add its sober 
 smile to the room . that bookcase contain what be call " the Lady 's 
 Library , " - a collection commence by the squire 's grandmother , of pious 
 memory , and complete by his mother , who have more taste for the light 
 letter , with but little addition from the bibliomaniac tendency 
 of the present Mrs. Hazeldean , who , be no great reader , content 
 herself with subscribing to the Book Club . in this feminine Bodleian , 
 the sermon collect by Mrs. Hazeldean , the grandmother , stand 
 cheek - by - jowl beside the novel purchase by Mrs. Hazeldean , the 
 mother , - 

  " mixtaque ridenti colocasia fundet acantho ! " 

 but , to be sure , the novel , in spite of very inflammatory title , such 
 as " Fatal sensibility , " " error of the heart , " etc . , be so harmless 
 that I doubt if the sermon could have have much to say against their 
 next - door neighbour , - and that be all that can be expect by the good 
 of we . 

 a parrot dozing on his perch ; some goldfish fast asleep in their glass 
 bowl ; two or three dog on the rug , and Flimsey , Miss Jemima 's spaniel , 
 curl into a ball on the soft sofa ; Mrs. Hazeldean 's work - table 
 rather in disorder , as if it have be lately use ; the " St. James 's 
 Chronicle " dangle down from a little tripod near the squire 's 
 armchair ; a high screen of gilt and stamp leather fence off the 
 card - table , - all these , disperse about a room large enough to hold they 
 all and not seem crowd , offer many a pleasant resting - place for the 
 eye , when it turn from the world of nature to the home of man . 

 but see , Captain Barnabas , fortify by his fourth cup of tea , have at 
 length summon courage to whisper to Mrs. Hazeldean , " do not   you think 
 the parson will be impatient for his rubber ? " Mrs. Hazeldean glance at 
 the parson and smile ; but she give the signal to the captain , and the 
 bell be rung , light be bring in , the curtain let down ; in a few 
 moment more , the group have collect round the cardtable . the good of 
 we be but human - that be not a new truth , I confess , but yet people 
 forget it every day of their life - and I dare say there be many who 
 be charitably think at this very moment that my parson ought not to 
 be play at whist . all I can say to those rigid disciplinarian be , 
 " every man have his favourite sin : whist be Parson Dale 's ! - lady and 
 gentleman , what be yours ? " in truth , I must not set up my poor parson , 
 nowadays , as a pattern parson , - it be enough to have one pattern in a 
 village no big than Hazeldean , and we all know that Lenny Fairfield 
 have bespeak that place , and get the patronage of the stock for his 
 emolument ! Parson Dale be ordain , not indeed so very long ago , but 
 still at a time when Churchmen take it a great deal more easily than 
 they do now . the elderly parson of that day play his rubber as a 
 matter of course , the middle - aged parson be sometimes see rid to 
 cover   I know a schoolmaster , a doctor of divinity , and an excellent 
 man , whose pupil be chiefly take from the high family in 
 England , who hunt regularly three time a week during the season , 
 and the young parson would often sing a capital song - not compose by 
 David - and join in those rotatory dance , which certainly David never 
 dance before the ark . 

 do it need so long an exordium to excuse thee , poor Parson Dale , for 
 turn up that ace of spade with so triumphant a smile at thy partner ? 
 I must own that nothing which could well add to the parson 's offence be 
 want . in the first place , he do not play charitably , and merely to 
 oblige other people . he delight in the game , he rejoice in the game , 
 his whole heart be in the game , - neither be he indifferent to the 
 mammon of the thing , as a christian pastor ought to have be . he look 
 very sad when he take his shilling out of his purse , and exceedingly 
 please when he put the shilling that have just before belong to 
 other people into it . finally , by one of those arrangement common with 
 married people who play at the same table , ' Mr. and - Mrs. Hazeldean be 
 invariably partner , and no two people could play bad ; while Captain 
 Barnabas , who have play at Graham 's with honour and profit , necessarily 
 become partner to Parson Dale , who himself play a good steady parsonic 
 game . so that , in strict truth , it be hardly fair play ; it be almost 
 swindle , - the combination of these two great don against that 
 innocent married couple ! Mr. Dale , it be true , be aware of this 
 disproportion of force , and have often propose either to change partner 
 or to give odd , - proposition always scornfully scout by the squire 
 and his lady , so that the parson be oblige to pocket his conscience , 
 together with the ten point which make his average winning . 

 the strange thing in the world be the different way in which whist 
 affect the temper . it be no test of temper , as some pretend , - not at 
 all ! the well - temper people in the world grow snappish at whist ; and 
 I have see the most testy and peevish in the ordinary affair of life 
 bear their loss with the stoicism of Epictetus . this be notably 
 manifest in the contrast between the present adversary of the Hall 
 and the Rectory . the squire , who be esteem as choleric a gentleman as 
 most in the county , be the well - humour fellow you could imagine when 
 you set he down to whist opposite the sunny face of his wife . you never 
 hear one of those incorrigible blunderer scold each other ; on the 
 contrary , they only laugh when they throw away the game , with four 
 by honour in their hand . the utmost that be ever say be a " well , 
 Harry , that be the oddest trump of your . Ho , ho , ho ! " or a " bless I , 
 Hazeldean - why , they make three trick in club , and you have the ace in 
 your hand all the time ! ha , ha , ha ! " 

 upon which occasion Captain Barnabas , with great goodhumour , always 
 echo both the squire 's Ho , ho , ho ! and Mrs. Hazeldean 's Ha , ha , ha ! 

 not so the parson . he have so keen and sportsmanlike an interest in the 
 game , that even his adversary ' mistake ruffle he . and you would 
 hear he , with elevated voice and agitated gesture , lay down 
 the law , quote Hoyle , appeal to all the power of memory and 
 common - sense against the very delinquency by which he be enrich , - a 
 waste of eloquence that always heighten the hilarity of Mr. and Mrs. 
 Hazeldean . while these four be thus engage , Mrs. Dale , who have come 
 with her husband despite her headache , sit on the sofa beside Miss 
 Jemima , or rather beside Miss Jemima 's Flimsey , which have already 
 secure the centre of the sofa , and snarl at the very idea of be 
 disturb . and Master Frank - at a table by himself - be employ

 Dale , in her heart , like Miss Jemima well than Mrs. Hazeldean , of

 those tender diminutive belong to the " dear " genus , and be rarely 
 employ by the lady , except at time when , have they be little girl 
 still , and the governess out of the way , they would have slap and 
 pinch each other . Mrs. Dale be still a very pretty woman , as 
 Mrs. Hazeldean be still a very fine woman . Mrs. Dale paint in 
 water - colour , and sing , and make card - rack and penholder , and be 
 call an " elegant , accomplished woman ; " Mrs. Hazeldean cast up the 
 squire 's account , write the good part of his letter , keep a large

 woman . " Mrs. Dale have headache and nerve ; Mrs. Hazeldean have neither 
 nerve nor headache . Mrs. Dale say , " Harry have no real harm in she , 
 but be certainly very masculine ; " Mrs. Hazeldean say , " Carry would 
 be a good creature but for her air and grace . " Mrs. Dale say Mrs. 
 Hazeldean be " just make to be a country squire 's lady ; " Mrs. Hazeldean

 be a parson 's wife . " Carry , when she speak of Harry to a third person ,

 Hazeldean call Mrs. Dale " poor , " - at least as well as I do . for , after

 be call " obscure significant , " resemble the Konx Ompax , which hath 
 so puzzle the inquirer into the Eleusinian Mysteries : the application

 the squire , who be fourth player , drop the king to Captain

  to the left of the village of Dunder , and pleasantly situate in a

 boy be a relation of her . I may say , " add the squire , " that he be a

 child of his own , take up young Randal   when his wife die , poor

 as his heir . quite welcome . Frank and I want nothing from Mr. Audley 
 Egerton , thank Heaven ! " 

 " I can well believe in your brother 's generosity to his wife 's kindre , " 
 say the parson , sturdily , " for I be sure Mr. Egerton be a man of strong 
 feeling . " 

 " what the deuce do you know about Mr. Egerton ? I do not   suppose you could 
 ever have even speak to he . "

 " oh , one of your parishioner at Lansmere , - one of the constituent Mr. 
 Audley Egerton throw over , after all the pain I have take to get he

 " my dear sir , " say the parson , sink his voice , and in a mild tone of 
 conciliatory expostulation , " you be so irritable whenever Mr. Egerton 's

 " irritable ! " exclaim the squire , whose wrath have be long simmer ,

 agricultural distress in a year which break three of the good farmer I

 be compliment by Ricardo , a Jew ! good heaven ! a pretty parson you

 the parson skilfully and remorselessly pursue the advantage he have

 Mrs. Dale open her eye very wide , and star in astonishment .

 CHAPTER xiii . 

   suppose to be a letter from Mrs. Hazeldean to A. Riccabocca , Esq . , 
  the Casino ; but , edit , and indeed compose , by Miss Jemima 
  Hazeldean . 
 HAZELDEAN HALL . 

 DEAR SIR , - to a feeling heart it must always be painful to give pain 
 to another , and   though I be sure unconsciously   you have give the 
 great pain to poor Mr. Hazeldean and myself , indeed to all our little 
 circle , in so cruelly refuse our attempt to become well acquaint 
 with a gentleman we so highly ESTEEM . do , pray , dear sir , make we the 
 amende honorable , and give we the pleasure of your company for a few 
 day at the Hall . may we expect you Saturday next ? - our dinner hour be 
 six o'clock . 

 with the good compliment of Mr. and Miss Jemima Hazeldean , believe I , 
 my dear sir , 

 your truly , H. H. 

 Miss Jemima have carefully seal this note , which Mrs. Hazeldean 
 have very willingly depute she to write , take it herself into the 
 stable - yard , in order to give the groom proper instruction to wait for 
 an answer . but while she be speak to the man , Frank , equip for 
 riding , with more than his usual dandyism , come into the yard , call 
 for his pony in a loud voice ; and single out the very groom whom Miss 
 Jemima be address - for , indeed , he be the smart of all in the 
 squire 's stable - tell he to saddle the gray pad and accompany the 
 pony . 

 " no , Frank , " say Miss Jemima , " you can not   have George ; your father want 
 he to go on a message , - you can take Mat . " 

 " Mat , indeed ! " say Frank , grumble with some reason ; for Mat be a 
 surly old fellow , who tie a most indefensible neckcloth , and always 
 contrive to have a great patch on his boot , - besides , he call Frank 
 " Master , " and obstinately refuse to trot down hill , - " Mat , indeed ! let 
 Mat take the message , and George go with I . " 

 but Miss Jemima have also her reason for reject Mat . Mat 's foible 
 be not servility , and he always show true english independence in all 
 house where he be not invite to take his ale in the servant ' 
 hall . mat might offend Signor Riccabocca , and spoil all . an animate 
 altercation ensue , in the midst of which the squire and his wife 
 enter the yard , with the intention of drive in the conjugal gig to 
 the market town . the matter be refer to the natural umpire by both 
 the contend party . 

 the squire look with great contempt on his son . " and what do you want 
 a groom at all for ? be you afraid of tumble off the pony ? " 

 FRANK . - " no , Sir ; but I like to go as a gentleman , when I pay a visit to 
 a gentleman ! " 

 SQUIRE   in high wrath . - " you precious puppy ! I think I be as good a 
 gentleman as you any day , and I should like to know when you ever see 
 I ride to call on a neighbour with a fellow jingling at my heel , like 
 that upstart Ned Spankie , whose father keep a cotton mill . first time I 
 ever hear of a Hazeldean thinking a livery coat be necessary to prove 
 his gentility ! " 

 MRS . HAZELDEAN   observe Frank colouring , and about to reply . - " hush , 
 Frank , never answer your father , - and you be go to call on Mr. 
 Leslie ? " 

 " yes , ma'am , and I be very much oblige to my father for let I , " 
 say Frank , take the squire 's hand . 

 " well , but , Frank , " continue Mrs. Hazeldean , " I think you hear that 
 the Leslies be very poor . " 

 FRANK . - " Eh , Mother ? " 

 MRS . HAZELDEAN . - " and would you run the chance of wound the pride 
 of a gentleman as well bear as yourself by affect any show of be 
 rich than he be ? " 

 SQUIRE   with great admiration . - " Harry , I would give L10 to have say 
 that ! " 

 FRANK   leave the squire 's hand to take his mother 's . - " you be quite 
 right , Mother ; nothing could be more snobbish ! " 

 SQUIRE . " give we your fist , too , sir ; you will be a chip of the old block , 
 after all . " 

 Frank smile , and walk off to his pony . 

 MRS . HAZELDEAN   to Miss Jemima . - " be that the note you be to write 
 for I ? " 

 MISS jemima . - " yes ; I suppose you do not care about see it , so I 
 have seal it , and give it to George . " 

 MRS . HAZELDEAN . - " but Frank will pass close by the Casino on his way to 
 the Leslies ' . it may be more civil if he leave the note himself . " 

 MISS jemima   hesitatingly . - " do you think so ? " 

 MRS . HAZELDEAN . - " yes , certainly . Frank , Frank , as you pass by the 
 Casino , call on Mr. Riccabocca , give this note , and say we shall be 
 heartily glad if he will come . " Frank nod . 

 " stop a bit , " cry the squire . " if Rickeybockey be at home , ' t be ten 
 to one if he do not   ask you to take a glass of wine ! if he do , mind , 
 ' t be bad than ask you to take a turn on the rack . Faugh ! you 
 remember , Harry ? - I think it be all up with I . " 

 " yes , " cry Mrs. Hazeldean ; " for Heaven 's sake not a drop . wine , 
 indeed ! " 

 " do not   talk of it , " cry the squire , make a wry face .

 and do not leave off her admonition to be extremely polite to the

 BOOK SECOND . 




 INITIAL chapter . 

 inform the reader how this work come to have INITIAL chapter . 

 " there can not   be a doubt , " say my father , " that to each of the main 
 division of your work - whether you call they Books or Parts - you should 
 prefix an initial or Introductory chapter . " 

 PISISTRATUS . - " can not   be a doubt , sir ? why so ? " 

 MR . CAXTON . - " field lay it down as an indispensable rule , which he 
 support by his example ; and Fielding be an artistical writer , and know 
 what he be about . " 

 PISISTRATUS . - " do you remember any of his reason , sir ? " 

 MR . CAXTON . - " why , indeed , Fielding say , very justly , that he be not 
 bind to assign any reason ; but he do assign a good many , here and 
 there , - to find which I refer you to ' Tom Jones . ' I will only observe , 
 that one of his reason , which be unanswerable , run to the effect that 
 thus , in every Part or Book , the reader have the advantage of begin 
 at the fourth or fifth page instead of the first , - ' a matter by no mean 
 of trivial consequence , ' saith Fielding , ' to person who read book with 
 no other view than to say they have read they , - a more general motive to 
 reading than be commonly imagine ; and from which not only law book and 
 good book , but the page of Homer and Virgil , Swift and Cervantes , have 
 be often turn over . ' there , " cry my father , triumphantly , " I will 
 lay a shilling to twopence that I have quote the very word . " 

 MRS . CANTON . - " dear I , that only mean skip ; I do not   see any great 
 advantage in write a chapter , merely for people to skip it . " 

 PISISTRATUS . - " neither do I ! " 

 MR . CANTON   dogmatically . - " it be the repose in the picture , - fielding 
 call it ' contrast . ' -   still more dogmatically .   - I say there can not   be a 
 doubt about it . besides " add my father after a pause , - " besides , this 
 usage give you opportunity to explain what have go before , or to 
 prepare for what be come ; or , since Fielding contend , with great 
 truth , that some learning be necessary for this kind of historical 
 composition , it allow you , naturally and easily , the introduction 
 of light and pleasant ornament of that nature . at each flight in the 
 terrace you may give the eye the relief of an urn or a statue . moreover , 
 when so inclined , you create proper pausing - place for reflection ; and 
 complete by a separate , yet harmonious ethical department , the design of 
 a work , which be but a mere Mother Goose 's tale if it do not embrace a 
 general view of the thought and action of mankind . " 

 PISISTRATUS . - " but then , in these initial chapter , the author thrust 
 himself forward ; and just when you want to get on with the dramatis 
 personae , you find yourself face to face with the poet himself . " 

 MR . CANTON . - " Pooh ! you can contrive to prevent that ! imitate the chorus 
 of the greek stage , who fill up the interval between the action by 
 say what the author would otherwise say in his own person . " 

 PISISTRATUS   slyly . - " that be a good idea , sir , - and I have a chorus , 
 and a choregus too , already in my eye . " 

 MR . CANTON   unsuspectingly . - " aha ! you be not so dull a fellow as you 
 would make yourself out to be ; and , even if an author do thrust himself 
 forward , what objection be there to that ? it be a mere affectation to 
 suppose that a book can come into the world without an author . every 
 child have a father , - one father at least , - as the great Conde say very 
 well in his poem . " 

 PISISTRATUS . - " the great Conde a poet ! I never hear that before . " 

 MR . CANTON . - " I do not   say he be a poet , but he send a poem to Madame de 
 Montansier . envious critic think that he must have pay somebody else 
 to write it ; but there be no reason why a great captain should not write 
 a poem , - I do not   say a good poem , but a poem . I wonder , Roland , if the 
 duke ever try his hand at ' Stanzas to Mary , ' or ' line to a Sleeping 
 Babe . ' " 

 CAPTAIN ROLAND . - " Austin , I be ashamed of you . of course the duke could 
 write poetry if he please , - something , I dare say , in the way of the 
 great Conde ; that is , something warlike and heroic , I will be bind . let us 
 hear ! " 

 MR . CAXTON   reciting . - 

  " Telle est du Ciel la loi severe 
  Qu'il faut qu'un enfant ait un pere ; 
  on dit meme quelquefois 
  Tel enfant en a jusqu'a trois . " 

   " that each child have a father 
  be Nature 's decree ; 
  but , to judge by a rumour , 
  some child have three . " 

 CAPTAIN ROLAND   greatly disgusted . - " Conde write such stuff ! - I do not 
 believe it . " 

 PISISTRATUS . - " I do , and accept the quotation ; you and Roland shall be 
 joint father to my child as well as myself . 

  " ' tel enfant en a jusqu'a trois . ' " 

 MR . CAXTON   solemnly . - " I refuse the proffered paternity ; but so far 
 as administer a little wholesome castigation now and then , I have no 
 objection to join in the discharge of a father 's duty . " 

 PISISTRATUS . - " agree . have you anything to say against the infant 
 hitherto ? " 

 MR . CAXTON . - " he be in long clothe at present ; let we wait till he can 
 walk . " 

 BLANCHE . - " but pray whom do you mean for a hero ? and be Miss Jemima your 
 heroine ? " 

 CAPTAIN ROLAND . - " there be some mystery about the - " 

 PISISTRATUS   hastily .-"Hush , Uncle : no let the cat out of the 
 bag yet . listen , all of you ! I leave Frank Hazeldean on his way to the 
 Casino . " 




 CHAPTER II . 

 " it be a sweet pretty place , " think Frank , as he open the gate which 
 lead across the field to the Casino , that smile down upon he with 
 its plaster pilaster . " I wonder , though , that my father , who be so 
 particular in general , suffer the carriage - road to be so full of hole 
 and weed . Mounseer do not receive many visit , I take it . " 

 but when Frank get into the ground immediately before the house , he see 
 no cause of complaint as to want of order and repair . nothing could be 
 keep more neatly . Frank be ashamed of the dint make by the pony 's hoof 
 on the smooth gravel : he dismount , tie the animal to the wicket , and 
 go on foot towards the glass door in front . 

 he ring the bell once , twice , but nobody come , for the old 
 woman - servant , who be hard of hearing , be far away in the yard , 
 search for any egg which the hen might have scandalously hide for 
 culinary purpose ; and Jackeymo be fish for the stickleback and 
 minnow which be , when catch , to assist the egg , when find , in 
 keep together the body and soul of himself and his master . the old 
 woman have be lately put upon board wage . lucky old woman ! Frank ring 
 a third time , and with the impetuosity of his age . a face peep from 
 the belvidere on the terrace . " Diavolo ! " say Dr. Riccabocca to himself . 
 " Young cock crow hard on their own dunghill ; it must be a cock of a 
 high race to crow so loud at another 's . " 

 Therewith he shamble out of the summer - house , and appear suddenly 
 before Frank , in a very wizard - like dressing - robe of black serge , a red 
 cap on his head , and a cloud of smoke come rapidly from his lip , as a 
 final consolatory whiff , before he remove the pipe from they . Frank have 
 indeed see the doctor before , but never in so scholastic a costume , and 
 he be a little startled by the apparition at his elbow , as he turn 
 round . 

 " Signorino , " say the Italian , take off his cap with his usual 
 urbanity , " pardon the negligence of my people ; I be too happy to receive 
 your command in person . " 

 " Dr. Rickeybockey ? " stammer Frank , much confused by this polite 
 address , and the low , yet stately , bow with which it be accompany . 
 " I - I have a note from the Hall . Mamma - that be , my mother - and aunt 
 Jemima beg their good compliment , and hope you will come , sir . " 

 the doctor take the note with another bow , and , open the glass door , 
 invite Frank to enter . 

 the young gentleman , with a schoolboy 's usual bluntness , be about to 
 say that he be in a hurry , and have rather not ; but Dr. Riccabocca 's 
 grand manner awe he , while a glimpse of the hall excite his 
 curiosity , so he silently obey the invitation . 

 the hall , which be of an octagon shape , have be originally panel 
 off into compartment , and in these the Italian have paint landscape , 
 rich with the warm sunny light of his native climate . Frank be no 
 judge of the art display ; but he be greatly strike with the scene 
 depict : they be all view of some lake , real or imaginary ; in all , 
 dark - blue shin water reflect dark - blue placid sky . in one , a 
 flight of step ascend to the lake , and a gay group be see feast 
 on the margin ; in another , sunset throw its rose - hue over a vast villa 
 or palace , back by Alpine hill , and flank by long arcade of vine , 
 while pleasure - boat skim over the wave below . in short , throughout 
 all the eight compartment , the scene , though it differ in detail , 
 preserve the same general character , as if illustrate some favourite 
 locality . the Italian do not , however , evince any desire to do the 
 honour of his own art , but , precede Frank across the hall , open the 
 door of his usual sitting - room , and request he to enter . Frank do so 
 rather reluctantly , and seat himself with unwonted bashfulness on the 
 edge of a chair . but here new specimen of the doctor 's handicraft soon 
 rivet attention . the room have be originally paper , but Riccabocca 
 have stretch canvas over the wall , and paint thereon sundry 
 satirical device , each separate from the other by scroll - work of 
 fantastic arabesque . here a Cupid be trundle a wheelbarrow full of 
 heart , which he appear to be sell to an ugly old fellow , with a 
 money - bag in his hand - probably Plutus . there diogene might be see 
 walk through a market - place , with his lantern in his hand , in search 
 of an honest man , whilst the child jeered at he , and the cur 
 snap at his heel . in another place a lion be see half dress in a 
 fox 's hide , while a wolf in a sheep 's mask be converse very amicably 
 with a young lamb . here again might be see the geese stretch out 
 their neck from the Roman Capitol in full cackle , while the stout 
 invader be beheld in the distance , run off as hard as they 
 could . in short , in all these quaint entablature some pithy sarcasm be 
 symbolically convey ; only over the mantel piece be the design graver 
 and more touching . it be the figure of a man in a pilgrim 's garb , 
 chain to the earth by small but innumerable ligament , while a phantom 
 likeness of himself , his shadow , be see hasten down what seem an 
 interminable vista ; and underneath be write the pathetic word of 
 Horace - 

  " Patriae quis exul 
  Se quoque fugit ? " 

   " what exile from his country can also fly from himself ? " 

 the furniture of the room be extremely simple , and somewhat scanty ; 
 yet it be arrange so as to impart an air of taste and elegance to the 
 room . even a few plaster bust and statue , though buy but of some 
 humble itinerant , have their classical effect , glisten from out 
 stand of flower that be group around they , or back by 
 graceful screen - work form from twisted osier , which , by the simple 
 contrivance of tray at the bottom fill with earth , serve for live 
 parasitical plant , with gay flower contrast thick ivy leaf , 
 and give to the whole room the aspect of a bower . " may I ask your 
 permission ? " say the Italian , with his finger on the seal of the 
 letter . 

 " oh , yes , " say Frank , with naivete . 

 Riccabocca break the seal , and a slight smile steal over his 
 countenance . then he turn a little aside from Frank , shade his face 
 with his hand , and seem to muse . " Mrs. Hazeldean , " say he , at last , 
 " do I very great honour . I hardly recognize her handwriting , or I 
 should have be more impatient to open the letter . " the dark eye be 
 lift over the spectacle and go right into Frank 's unprotected 
 and undiplomatic heart . the doctor raise the note , and point to the 
 character with his forefinger . 

 " Cousin Jemima 's hand , " say Frank , as directly as if the question have 
 be put to he . 

 the Italian smile . " Mr. Hazeldean have company stay with he ? " 

 " No ; that be , only Barney , - the captain . there be seldom much company 
 before the shooting season , " add Frank , with a slight sigh ; " and then , 
 you know , the holiday be over . for my part , I think we ought to break 
 up a month later . " 

 the doctor seem reassure by the first sentence in Frank 's reply , 
 and , seat himself at the table , write his answer , - not hastily , as we 
 english write , but with care and precision , like one accustom to 
 weigh the nature of word , - in that stiff italian hand , which allow 
 the writer so much time to think while he form his letter . he do not , 
 therefore , reply at once to Frank 's remark about the holiday , but be 
 silent till he have conclude his note , read it three time over , seal 
 it by the taper he slowly light , and then , give it to Frank , he 
 say , 

 " for your sake , young gentleman , I regret that your holiday be so 
 early ; for mine , I must rejoice , since I accept the kind invitation you 
 have render doubly gratifying by bring it yourself . " 

 " deuce take the fellow and his fine speech ! one do not   know which way 
 to look , " think English Frank . 

 the Italian smile again , as if this time he have read the boy 's heart , 
 without need of those pierce black eye , and say , less ceremoniously 
 than before , " you do not   care much for compliment , young gentleman ? " 

 " no , I do not   indeed , " say Frank , heartily . 

 " so much the well for you , since your way in the world be make : it 
 would be so much the bad if you have to make it ! " 

 Frank look puzzle : the thought be too deep for he , so he turn to 
 the picture . 

 " those be very funny , " say he ; " they seem capitally do . who do 
 them ? " 

 " Signoriuo Hazeldean , you be give I what you refuse yourself . " 

 " Eh ? " say Frank , inquiringly . 

 " compliment ! " 

 " oh - I - no ; but they be well do : be not   they , sir ? " - 

 " not particularly : you speak to the artist . " 

 " what ! you paint they ? " 

 " yes . " 

 " and the picture in the hall ? " 

 " those too . " 

 " take from nature , eh ? " 

 " nature , " say the italian , sententiously , perhaps evasively , " let 
 nothing be take from she . " 

 " oh ! " say Frank , puzzle again . " well , I must wish you good morning , 
 sir ; I be very glad you be come . " 

 " without compliment ? " 

 " without compliment . " 

 " a rivedersi - good - by for the present , my young signorino . this way , " 
 observe Frank make a bolt towards the wrong door . " can I offer you a 
 glass of wine ? - it be pure , of our own making . " 

 " no , thank you , indeed , sir , " cry Frank , suddenly recollect his 
 father 's admonition . " good - by , do not   trouble yourself , sir ; I know any 
 way now . " 

 but the bland Italian follow his guest to the wicket , where Frank 
 have leave the pony . the young gentleman , afraid lest so courteous a host 
 should hold the stirrup for he , twitch off the bridle , and mount in 
 haste , not even stay to ask if the Italian could put he in the way 
 to Rood Hall , of which way he be profoundly ignorant . the Italian 's eye 
 follow the boy as he ride up the ascent in the lane , and the doctor 
 sigh heavily . " the wiser we grow , " say he to himself , " the more we 
 regret the age of our folly : it be well to gallop with a light heart 
 up the stony hill than sit in the summer - house and cry ' how true ! ' to 
 the stony truth of Machiavelli ! " 

 with that he turn back into the belvidere ; but he could not resume 
 his study . he remain some minute gaze on the prospect , till 
 the prospect remind he of the field which Jackeymo be bent on his 
 hiring , and the field remind he of Lenny Fairfield . he return to 
 the house , and in a few moment re - emerge in his out - of - door trim , with 
 cloak and umbrella , re - light his pipe , and stroll towards hazeldean 
 village . 

 meanwhile Frank , after canter on for some distance , stop at a 
 cottage , and there learn that there be a short cut across the field 
 to Rood Hall , by which he could save nearly three mile . Frank , 
 however , miss the short cut , and come out into the high road ; a 
 turnpike - keeper , after first take his toll , put he back again into 
 the short cut ; and finally , he get into some green lane , where a 
 dilapidate finger - post direct he to Rood . late at noon , have 
 ride fifteen mile in the desire to reduce ten to seven , he come 
 suddenly upon a wild and primitive piece of ground , that seem half 
 chase , half common , with crazy tumbledown cottage of villanous aspect 
 scatter about in odd nook and corner . idle , dirty child be 
 make mud - pie on the road ; slovenly - look woman be plait straw 
 at the threshold ; a large but forlorn and decayed church , that seem 
 to say that the generation which see it build be more pious than the 
 generation which now resort to it , stand boldly and nakedly out by the 
 roadside . 

 " be this the village of Rood ? " ask Frank of a stout young man break 
 stone on the road - sad sign that no well labour could be find for 
 he ! 

 the man sullenly nod , and continue his work . " and where be the 
 Hall - Mr. Leslie 's ? " 

 the man look up in stolid surprise , and this time touch his hat . 

 " be you go there ? " 

 " yes , if I can find out where it be . " 

 " I will show your honour , " say the boor , alertly . 

 Frank rein in the pony , and the man walk by his side . Frank be 
 much of his father 's son , despite the difference of age , and that more 
 fastidious change of manner which characterize each succeed race 
 in the progress of civilization . despite all his Eton finery , he be 
 familiar with peasant , and have the quick eye of one country - bear as to 
 country matter . 

 " you do not   seem very well off in this village , my man ? " say he , 
 knowingly . 

 " Noa ; there be a deal of distress here in the winter time , and summer 
 too , for that matter ; and the parish ben't   much help to a single man . " 

 " but surely the farmer want work here as well as elsewhere ? " 

 " ' Deed , and there ben't   much farming work here , - most o ' the parish be 
 all wild ground loike . " 

 " the poor have a right of common , I suppose , " say Frank , survey a 
 large assortment of vagabond bird and quadruped . 

 " yes ; neighbour Timmins keep his geese on the common , and some have 
 a cow , and they be neighbour Jowlas 's pig . I do not   know if there be a 
 right , loike ; but the folk at the Hall do all they can to help we , 
 and that ben't   much : they ben't   as rich as some folk ; but , " add the 
 peasant , proudly , " they be as good blood as any in the shire . " 

 " I ' m glad to see you like they , at all event . " 

 " oh , yes , I like they well eno ' ; mayhap you be at school with the 
 young gentleman ? " 

 " yes , " say Frank . 

 " ah , I hear the clergyman say as how Master Randal be a mighty clever 
 lad , and would get rich some day . I ' se sure I wish he would , for a poor 
 squire make a poor parish . there be the Hall , sir . " 




 CHAPTER III . 

 Frank look right ahead , and see a square house that , in spite of 
 modern sash window , be evidently of remote antiquity . a high conical 
 roof ; a stack of tall quaint chimney - pot of red - baked clay   like 
 those at Sutton Place in Surrey   dominating over isolate vulgar 
 smoke - conductor , of the ignoble fashion of present time ; a dilapidated 
 groin - work , encase within a Tudor arch a door of the comfortable date 
 of George III . , and the peculiarly dingy and weather - stain appearance 
 of the small finely - finish brick , of which the habitation be 
 build , - all show the abode of former generation adapt with 
 tasteless irreverence to the habit of descendant unenlightene by 
 Pugin , or indifferent to the poetry of the past . the house have emerge 
 suddenly upon Frank out of the gloomy waste land , for it be place in 
 a hollow , and shelter from sight by a disorderly group of ragged , 
 dismal , valetudinarian fir - tree , until an abrupt turn of the 
 road clear that screen , and leave the desolate abode bare to the 
 discontent eye . Frank dismount ; the man hold his pony ; and after 
 smooth his cravat , the smart Etonian saunter up to the door , and

 brass knocker , - a knock which instantly bring forth an astonished

 inquisitive family , stroll up to the gate of the fence , and , lean

 much curiosity and some suspicion . 

 while Frank be still without , impatiently swinge his white trouser

 member of the family within . Mr. Leslie , the paterfamilia , be in a

 Mr. Leslie pass those hour no one ever form a conjecture . at the

 and scrap of newspaper ; and the bureau be open , and reveal a great 
 number of pigeonhole and division , fill with various odd and end ,

 of letter , very yellow , and tie in packet with faded tape ; in

 be neatly label , " find in Hollow Lane , May 21st , 1804 , by Maunder 
 Slugge Leslie , Esq . " the next division hold several bit of iron in

 have also meet with in his ramble , and , accord to a harmless popular 
 superstition , deem it highly unlucky not to pick up , and , once pick 
 up , no less unlucky to throw away . item , in the adjoining pigeon - hole , a 
 goodly collection of pebble with hole in they , preserve for the same 
 reason , in company with a crooked sixpence ; item , neatly arrange in 
 fanciful mosaic , several periwinkle , Blackamoor 's tooth   I mean the 
 shell so call , and other specimen of the conchiferous ingenuity of 
 nature , partly inherit from some ancestral spinster , partly amass

 the farm - bailiff 's account , several file of bill , an old stirrup , 
 three set of knee and shoe buckle which have belong to Mr. Leslie 's 
 father , a few seal tie together by a shoe - string , a shagreen toothpick 
 case , a tortoise shell magnifying - glass to read with , his eld son 's 
 first copybook , his second son 's ditto , his daughter 's ditto , and a 
 lock of his wife 's hair arrange in a true lover 's knot , frame and 
 glaze . there be also a small mousetrap ; a patent corkscrew too good 
 to be use in common ; fragment of a silver teaspoon , that have , by 
 natural decay , arrive at a dissolution of its part ; a small brown 
 holland bag , contain halfpence of various date , as far back as Queen 
 Anne , accompany by two french sous and a german silber gros , - the 
 which miscellany Mr. Leslie magniloquently call " his coin , " and have 
 leave in his will as a family heirloom . there be many other curiosity 
 of congenial nature and equal value - quae nunc describere longum est . 
 Mr. Leslie be engage at this time in what be term " put thing 
 to right , " - an occupation he perform with exemplary care once a week . 
 this be his day ; and he have just count his coin , and be slowly 
 tie they up again in the brown holland bag , when Frank 's knock reach 
 his ear . 

 Mr. Maunder Slugge Leslie pause , shake his head as if incredulously , 
 and be about to resume his occupation , when he be seize with a fit of 
 yawning which prevent the bag be tie for full two minute . 

 while such the employment of the study , let we turn to the recreation 
 in the drawing - room , or rather parlour . a drawing - room there be on the 
 first floor , with a charming look - out , not on the dreary fir - tree , but 
 on the romantic undulating forest - land ; but the drawing - room have not

 too good to sit in , except when there be company : there never be

 close the view . near the fir - tree window sit Mrs. Leslie ; before she ,

 a former generation , under the name of " Mrs. Bridget Blue Mantle . " she 
 have a small needle in her left hand , and a very thick piece of thread

 would never suit ; " and to wonder " why Mr. Leslie would not see that the 
 work - table be mend . " Mrs. Leslie have be rather a pretty woman . in 
 spite of a dress at once slatternly and economical , she have still the 
 air of a lady , - rather too much so , the hard duty of her situation 
 consider . she be proud of the antiquity of her family on both side ; 
 her mother be of the venerable stock of the daudler of Daudle Place , a 
 race that exist before the Conquest . indeed , one have only to read 
 our early chronicle , and to glance over some of those long - wind 
 moralizing poem which delight the thane and ealderman of old , in 
 order to see that the Daudles must have be a very influential family 
 before William the First turn the country topsy - turvy . while the 
 mother 's race be thus indubitably Saxon , the father 's have not only 
 the name but the peculiar idiosyncrasy of the Normans , and go far to 
 establish that crotchet of the brilliant author of " Sybil ; or , the two 
 nation , " as to the continue distinction between the conquering 
 and conquered population . Mrs. Leslie 's father boast the name of 
 Montfichet , - doubtless of the same kith and kin as those great baron of 
 Alontfichet , who once own such broad land and such turbulent castle . 
 a high - nosed , thin , nervous , excitable progeny , those same montfydget , 
 as the most troublesome Norman could pretend to be . this fusion of race 
 be notable to the most ordinary physiognomist in the physique and 
 in the morale of Mrs. Leslie . she have the speculative blue eye of the 
 Saxon , and the passionate high nose of the Norman ; she have the musing 
 do - nothingness of the daudler , and the reckless have - at - every - thingness 
 of the Montfydgets . at Mrs. Leslie 's foot , a little girl with her hair 
 about her ear   and beautiful hair it be too   be amusing herself with 
 a break - nosed doll . at the far end of the room , before a high desk , 
 sit Frank 's Eton schoolfellow , the eld son . a minute or two before 
 Frank 's alarum have disturb the tranquillity of the household , he have 
 raise his eye from the book on the desk to glance at a very tattered 
 copy of the Greek Testament , in which his brother Oliver have find a 
 difficulty that he come to Randal to solve . as the young Etonian 's face 
 be turn to the light , your first impression on see it would have 
 be melancholy , but respectful , interest , - for the face have already 
 lose the joyous character of youth ; there be a wrinkle between the 
 brow ; and the line that speak of fatigue be already visible under 
 the eye and about the mouth ; the complexion be sallow , the lip be 
 pale . year of study have already sow in the delicate organization the 
 seed of many an infirmity and many a pain ; but if your look have rest 
 long on that countenance , gradually your compassion might have give 
 place to some feeling uneasy and sinister , - a feeling akin to fear . 
 there be in the whole expression so much of cold calm force , that it 
 belie the debility of the frame . you see there the evidence of a mind 
 that be cultivate , and you feel that in that cultivation there 
 be something formidable . a notable contrast to this countenance , 
 prematurely wear and eminently intelligent , be the round healthy face 
 of Oliver , with slow blue eye fix hard on the penetrate orb of his 
 brother , as if try with might and main to catch from they a gleam of 
 that knowledge with which they shine clear and frigid as a star . 

 at Frank 's knock , Oliver 's slow blue eye sparkle into animation , and 
 he spring from his brother 's side . the little girl fling back the hair 
 from her face , and star at her mother with a look which speak wonder 
 and fright . 

 the young student knit his brow , and then turn wearily back to the 
 book on his desk . 

 " dear I , " cry Mrs. Leslie , " who can that possibly be ? oliver , come 
 from the window , sir , this instant : you will be see ! Juliet , run , ring 
 the bell ; no , go to the head of the kitchen stair , and call out to 
 Jenny ' not at home . ' not at home , on any account , " repeat Mrs. Leslie , 
 nervously , for the Montfydget blood be now in full flow . 

 in another minute or so , Frank 's loud boyish voice be distinctly hear 
 at the outer door . 

 randal slightly start . 

 " Frank Hazeldean 's voice , " say he ; " I should like to see he , Mother . " 

 " see he , " repeat Mrs. Leslie , in amaze ; ' see he ! and the room in 
 this state ! " 

 randal might have reply that the room be in no bad state than 
 usual ; but he say nothing . a slight flush come and go over his pale

 the outer door close with a sullen , inhospitable jar , and a slip - shod

 " oh , look , randal , look up , " cry Oliver , who have again rush to the

 Randal do look up ; nay , he go deliberately to the window , and gaze a
