 that moment change pass over Randal 's countenance more rapidly than 
 cloud over the sky in a gusty day . now envy and discontent , with the 
 curl lip and the gloomy scowl ; now hope and proud self - esteem , with 
 the clearing brow and the lofty smile ; and then again all become 
 cold , firm , and close , as he walk back to his book , seat himself 
 resolutely , and say , half aloud , - " well , KNOWLEDGE be power ! " 




 CHAPTER IV . 

 Mrs. Leslie come up in fidget and in fuss ; she lean over Randal 's 
 shoulder and read the card . write in pen and ink , with an attempt at 
 imitation of print roman character , there appear first " MR . FRANK 
 HAZELDEAN ; " but just over these letter , and scribble hastily and less 
 legibly in pencil , be , - 

 " dear leslie , - sorry you be out ; come and see we , - do ! " 

 " you will go , randal ? " say Mrs. Leslie , after a pause . 

 " I be not sure . " 

 " yes , you can go ; you have clothe like a gentleman ; you can go 
 anywhere , not like those child ; " and Mrs. Leslie glance almost 
 spitefully at poor Oliver 's coarse threadbare jacket , and little 
 Juliet 's tear frock . 

 " what I have I owe at present to Mr. Egerton , and I should consult his 
 wish ; he be not on good term with these Hazeldeans . " then turn 
 towards his brother , who look mortified , he add , with a strange sort 
 of haughty kindness , " what I may have hereafter , Oliver , I shall owe to 
 myself ; and then if I rise , I will raise my family . " 

 " Dear Randal , " say Mrs. Leslie , fondly kiss he on the forehead , 
 " what a good heart you have ! " 

 " no , Mother ; my book do not   tell I that it be a good heart that get 
 on in the world : it be a hard head , " reply Randal , with a rude and 
 scornful candour . " but I can read no more just now : come out , Oliver . " 

 so say , he slide from his mother 's hand and leave the room . when Oliver 
 join he , Randal be already on the common ; and , without seem to 
 notice his brother , he continue to walk quickly , and with long stride , 
 in profound silence . at length he pause under the shade of an old oak , 
 that , too old to be of value save for firewood , have escape the axe . 
 the tree stand on a knoll , and the spot command a view of the decayed 
 house , the dilapidated church , the dreary village . 

 " Oliver , " say Randal , between his tooth , so that his voice have the 
 sound of a hiss , " it be under this tree that I first resolve to - " 

 he pause . 

 " what , randal ? " 

 " read hard : knowledge be power ! " 

 " but you be so fond of reading . " 

 " I ! " cry Randal . " do you think , when Wolsey and Thomas - a - becket become 
 priest , they be fond of tell their bead and pattering Aves ? I 
 fond of reading ! " 

 Oliver star ; the historical allusion be beyond his comprehension . 

 " you know , " continue Randal , " that we leslie be not always the 
 beggarly poor gentleman we be now . you know that there be a man who 
 live in Grosvenor Square , and be very rich , - very . his rich come to 
 he from a Leslie ; that man be my patron , Oliver , and he - be very good 
 to I . " 

 Randal 's smile be wither as he speak . " come on , " he say , after 
 a pause , - " come on . " again the walk be quick , and the brother be 
 silent . 

 they come at length to a little shallow brook , across which some large 
 stone have be place at short interval , so that the boy walk over 
 the ford dryshod . " will you pull down that bough , Oliver ? " say Randal , 
 abruptly , point to a tree . Oliver obey mechanically ; and randal , 
 strip the leaf and snap off the twig , leave a fork at the end ; 
 with this he begin to remove the stepping - stone . 

 " what be you about , randal ? " ask Oliver , wonderingly . 

 " we be on the other side of the brook now , and we shall not come back 
 this way . we do not   want the step - stone any more ! - away with they ! " 




 CHAPTER V. 

 the morning after this visit of Frank Hazeldean 's to Rood Hall , the 
 Right Honourable Audley Egerton , member of parliament , privy councillor , 
 and minister of a high department in the State , - just below the rank of 
 the cabinet , - be seat in his library , await the delivery of the 
 post , before he walk down to his office . in the mean while he 
 sip his tea , and glance over the newspaper with that quick and 
 half - disdainful eye with which your practical man in public life be will not 
 to regard the abuse or the eulogium of the Fourth Estate . 

 there be very little likeness between Mr. Egerton and his half - brother ; 
 none , indeed , except that they be both of tall stature , and strong , 
 sinewy , English build . but even in this last they do not resemble each 
 other ; for the squire 's athletic shape be already begin to expand 
 into that portly embonpoint which seem the natural development of 
 content man as they approach middle life . Audley , on the contrary , be 
 inclined to be spare ; and his figure , though the muscle be as firm 
 as iron , have enough of the slender to satisfy metropolitan idea of 
 elegance . his dress , his look , his tout ensemble , be those of the 
 London man . in the first , there be more attention to fashion than be 
 usual amongst the busy member of the House of Commons ; but then Audley 
 Egerton have always be something more than a mere busy member of 
 the House of Commons . he have always be a person of mark in the 
 good society ; and one secret of his success in life have be his high 
 reputation as " a gentleman . " 

 as he now bend over the journal , there be an air of distinction in the 
 turn of the well - shape head , with the dark brown hair , - dark in spite 
 of a reddish tinge , - cut close behind , and wear away a little towards 
 the crown , so as to give an additional height to a commanding forehead . 
 his profile be very handsome , and of that kind of beauty which impose 
 on man if it please woman ; and be , therefore , unlike that of your mere 
 pretty fellow , a positive advantage in public life . it be a profile 
 with large feature clearly cut , masculine , and somewhat severe . the 
 expression of his face be not open , like the squire 's , nor have it the 
 cold closeness which accompany the intellectual character of 
 young Leslie 's ; but it be reserve and dignified , and significant of 
 self - control , as should be the physiognomy of a man accustomed to think 
 before he speak . when you look at he , you be not surprised to learn 
 that he be not a florid orator nor a smart debater , - he be a " weighty 
 speaker . " he be fairly read , but without any great range either of 
 ornamental scholarship or constitutional lore . he have not much humour ; 
 but he have that kind of wit which be essential to grave and serious 
 irony . he have not much imagination , nor remarkable subtlety in 
 reasoning ; but if he do not dazzle he do not bore , - he be too much 
 of the man of the world for that . he be consider to have sound sense 
 and accurate judgment . Withal , as he now lay aside the journal , and 
 his face relax its austerer line , you will not be astonish to hear 
 that he be a man who be say to have be greatly belove by woman , 
 and still to exercise much influence in drawing - room and boudoir . at 
 least , no one be surprised when the great heiress , Clementina Leslie , 
 kinswoman and ward to Lord Lansmere , - a young lady who have refuse three 
 earl and the heir apparent to a dukedom , - be declare by her dear 
 friend to be die of love for Audley Egerton . it have be the natural 
 wish of the Lansmeres that this lady should marry their son , Lord 
 L'Estrange . but that young gentleman , whose opinion on matrimony 
 partook of the eccentricity of his general character , could never be 
 induce to propose , and have , accord to the on - dit of town , be the 
 principal party to make up the match between Clementina and his friend 
 Audley ; for the match require making - up , despite the predilection of 
 the young heiress . Mr. Egerton have have scruple of delicacy . he avow , 
 for the first time , that his fortune be much less than have be 
 generally suppose , and he do not like the idea of owe all to a wife , 
 however highly be might esteem and admire she . now , Lord L'Estrange   not 
 long after the election at Lansmere , which have give to Audley his first 
 seat in parliament   have suddenly exchange from the battalion of the 
 Guards to which he belong , and which be detain at home , into a 
 cavalry regiment on active service in the Peninsula . nevertheless , even 
 abroad , and amidst the distraction of war , his interest in all that 
 could forward Egerton 's career be unabated ; and by letter to his 
 father and to his cousin Clementina , he assist in the negotiation for 
 the marriage between Miss Leslie and his friend ; and before the year in 
 which Audley be return for Lansmere have expire , the young senator 
 receive the hand of the great heiress . the settlement of her fortune , 
 which be chiefly in the Funds , have be unusually advantageous to the 
 husband ; for though the capital be tie up so long as both survive , 
 for the benefit of any child they might have , yet in the event of 
 one of the party die without issue by the marriage , the whole 
 pass without limitation to the survivor . Miss Leslie , in spite of all 
 remonstrance from her own legal adviser , have settle this clause with 
 Egerton 's confidential solicitor , one Mr. Levy , of whom we shall see 
 more hereafter ; and Egerton be to be keep in ignorance of it till after 
 the marriage . if in this Miss Leslie show a generous trust in Mr. 
 Egerton , she still inflict no positive wrong on her relation , for 
 she have none sufficiently near to she to warrant their claim to the 
 succession . her near kinsman , and therefore her natural heir , be 
 Harley L'Estrange ; and if he be content , no one have a right to 
 complain . the tie of blood between herself and the Leslies of Rood Hall 
 be , as we shall see presently , extremely distant . 

 it be not till after his marriage that Mr. Egerton take an active 
 part in the business of the House of Commons . he be then at the most 
 advantageous starting - point for the career of ambition . his word on the 
 state of the country take importance from his stake in it . his talent 
 find accessory in the opulence of Grosvenor Square , the dignity of 
 a princely establishment , the respectability of one firmly settle in 
 life , the reputation of a fortune in reality very large , and which 
 be magnify by popular report into the revenue of a Croesus . Audley 
 Egerton succeed in parliament beyond the early expectation form 
 of he . he take , from the first , that station in the House which it 
 require tact to establish , and great knowledge of the world to free 
 from the charge of impracticability and crotchet , but which , once 
 establish , be peculiarly impose from the rarity of its independence ; 
 that be to say , the station of the moderate man who belong sufficiently 
 to a party to obtain its support , but be yet sufficiently disengage 
 from a party to make his vote and word , on certain question , matter of 
 anxiety and speculation . 

 Professing Toryism   the word Conservative , which would have suit 
 he well , be not then know , he separate himself from the country 
 party , and always avow great respect for the opinion of the 
 large town . the epithet give to the view of Audley Egerton be 
 " enlighten . " never too much in advance of the passion of the day , yet 
 never behind its movement , he have that shrewd calculation of odd 
 which a consummate mastery of the world sometimes bestow upon 
 politician , - perceive the chance for and against a certain question 
 be carry within a certain time , and nick the question between 
 wind and water . he be so good a barometer of that changeful weather 
 call Public Opinion , that he might have have a hand in the " Times " 
 newspaper . he soon quarrel , and purposely , with his Lansmere 
 constituent ; nor have he ever revisit that borough , - perhaps because 
 it be associate with unpleasant reminiscence in the shape of the 
 squire 's epistolary trimmer , and in that of his own effigy which 
 his agricultural constituent have burn in the corn - market . but the 
 speech that produce such indignation at Lansmere have delight one of 
 the great of our commercial town , which at the next general election 
 honour he with its representation . in those day , before the Reform 
 Bill , great commercial town choose man of high mark for their member ; 
 and a proud station it be for he who be delegate to speak the voice 
 of the princely merchant of England . 

 Mrs. Egerton survive her marriage but a few year . she leave no 
 child ; two have be bear , but die in their first infancy . the 
 property of the wife , therefore , pass without control or limit to the 
 husband . 

 whatever might have be the grief of the widower , he disdain to 
 betray it to the world . indeed , Audley Egerton be a man who have early 
 teach himself to conceal emotion . he bury himself in the country , 
 none know where , for some month . when he return , there be a deep 
 wrinkle on his brow , - but no change in his habit and avocation , except 
 that , shortly afterwards , he accept office , and thus become more busy 
 than ever . 

 Mr. Egerton have always be lavish and magnificent in money spatter . 
 a rich man in public life have many claim on his fortune , and no one 
 yield to those claim with in air so regal as Audley Egerton . but 
 amongst his many liberal action , there be none which seem more 
 worthy of panegyric than the generous favour he extend to the son of 
 his wife 's poor and distant kinsfolk , the Leslies of Rood Hall . 

 some four generation back , there have live a certain squire Leslie , a 
 man of large acre and active mind . he have cause to be displeased with 
 his eld son , and though he do not disinherit he , he leave half his 
 property to a young . 

 the young have capacity and spirit , which justify the parental 
 provision . he increase his fortune ; lift himself into notice and 
 consideration by public service and a noble alliance . his descendant 
 follow his example , and take rank among the first commoner 
 in England , till the last male , dying , leave his sole heiress and 
 representative in one daughter , Clementina , afterwards marry to Mr. 
 Egerton . 

 meanwhile the eld son of the fore - mention squire have muddle and 
 sotte away much of his share in the Leslie property ; and , by low habit 
 and mean society , lower in repute his representation of the name . 

 his successor imitate he , till nothing be leave to Randal 's father , 
 Mr. Maunder Slugge Leslie , but the decayed house , which be what the 
 Germans call the stamm schloss , or ' stem hall , " of the race , and the 
 wretched land immediately around it . 

 still , though all intercourse between the two branch of the family have 
 cease , the young have always feel a respect for the elder , as the head 
 of the House . and it be suppose that , on her death - bed , Mrs. Egerton 
 have recommend her impoverished namesake and kindre to the care of 
 her husband ; for when he return to town , after Mrs. Egerton 's death , 
 Audley have send to Mr. Maunder Slugge Leslie the sum of L5000 , which 
 he say his wife , leave no write will , have orally bequeath as a 
 legacy to that gentleman ; and he request permission to charge himself 
 with the education of the eld son . 

 Mr. Maunder Slugge Leslie might have do great thing for his little 
 property with those L5000 , or even keep in the three - per - cent the 
 interest would have afford a material addition to his comfort . but 
 a neighbouring solicitor , have catch scent of the legacy , hunt 
 it down into his own hand , on pretence of have find a capital 
 investment in a canal ; and when the solicitor have get possession of the 
 L5000 , he go off with they to America . 

 meanwhile Randal , place by Mr. Egerton at an excellent preparatory 
 school , at first give no sign of industry or talent ; but just before 
 he leave it , there come to the school , as classical tutor , an ambitious 
 young Oxford man ; and his zeal - for he be a capital teacher - produce 
 a great effect generally on the pupil , and especially on Randal Leslie . 
 he talk to they much in private on the advantage of learning , and 
 shortly afterwards he exhibit those advantage in his own person ; for , 
 have edit a greek play with much subtle scholarship , his college , 
 which some slight irregularity of his have displease , recall he to 
 its venerable bosom by the presentation of a fellowship . after this he 
 take order , become a college tutor , distinguish himself yet more by 
 a treatise on the Greek accent , get a capital living , and be consider 
 on the high road to a bishopric . this young man , then , communicate to 
 randal the thirst for knowledge ; and when the boy go afterwards to 
 Eton , he apply with such earnestness and resolve that his fame soon 
 reach the ear of Audley ; and that person , who have the sympathy for 
 talent , and yet more for purpose , which often characterize ambitious 
 man , go to Eton to see he . from that time Audley evince great and 
 almost fatherly interest in the brilliant Etonian ; and randal always 
 spend with he some day in each vacation . 

 I have say that Egerton 's conduct with respect to this boy be more 
 praiseworthy than most of those generous action for which he be 
 renowne , since to this the world give no applause . what a man do 
 within the range of his family connection do not carry with it that 
 eclat which invest a munificence exhibit on public occasion . either 
 people care nothing about it , or tacitly suppose it to be but his duty . 
 it be true , too , as the squire have observe , that Randal Leslie be 
 even less distantly related to the Hazeldeans than to Mrs. Egerton , 
 since Randal 's grandfather have actually marry a Miss Hazeldean   the 
 highest worldly connection that branch of the family have form since 
 the great split I have commemorate . but Audley Egerton never appear 
 aware of that fact . as he be not himself descend from the Hazeldeans , 
 he do not trouble himself about their genealogy ; and he take care to 
 impress it upon the Leslies that his generosity on their behalf be 
 solely to be ascribe to his respect for his wife 's memory and kindre . 
 still the squire have feel as if his " distant brother " imply a rebuke 
 on his own neglect of these poor leslie , by the liberality Audley 
 evince towards they ; and this have make he doubly sore when the name of 
 Randal Leslie be mention . but the fact really be , that the leslie 
 of Rood have so shrink out of all notice that the squire have actually 
 forget their existence , until Randal become thus indebted to his 
 brother ; and then he feel a pang of remorse that any one save himself , 
 the head of the Hazeldeans , should lend a help hand to the grandson 
 of a Hazeldean . 

 but have thus , somewhat too tediously , explain the position of 
 Audley Egerton , whether in the world or in relation to his young 
 protege , I may now permit he to receive and to read his letter . 




 CHAPTER VI . 

 Mr. Egerton glance over the pile of letter place beside he , 
 and first he tear up some , scarcely read , and throw they into the 
 waste - basket . public man have such odd , out - of - the - way letter , that 
 their waste - basket be never empty , - letter from amateur financier 
 propose new way to pay off the National Debt ; letter from America 
  never free !   ask for autograph ; letter from fond mother in 
 country village , recommend some miracle of a son for a place in 
 the king 's service ; letter from free - thinker in reproof of bigotry ; 
 letter from bigot in reproof of free - thinking ; letter sign Brutus 
 Redivivus , contain the agreeable information that the writer have a 
 dagger for tyrant , if the danish claim be not forthwith adjust ; 
 letter sign Matilda or Caroline , state that Caroline or Matilda 
 have see the public man 's portrait at the exhibition , and that a heart 
 sensible to its attraction may be find at No . - Piccadilly ; letter 
 from beggar , impostor , monomaniac , speculator , jobber , - all food 
 for the waste - basket . 

 from the correspondence thus winnow , Mr. Egerton first select those 
 on business , which he put methodically together in one division of 
 his pocket - book ; and secondly , those of a private nature , which he as 
 carefully put into another . of these last there be but three , - one 
 from his steward , one from Harley L'Estrange , one from Randal Leslie . 
 it be his custom to answer his correspondence at his office ; and to 
 his office , a few minute afterwards , he slowly take his way . many a 
 passenger turn back to look again at the firm figure , which , despite 
 the hot summer day , be button up to the throat ; and the black 
 frock - coat thus wear well become the erect air and the deep , full chest 
 of the handsome senator . when he enter Parliament Street , Audley 
 Egerton be join by one of his colleague , also on his way to the 
 care of office . 

 after a few observation on the last debate this gentleman say , - 

 " by the way , can you dine with I next Saturday , to meet Lansmere ? he 
 come up to town to vote for we on Monday . " 

 " I have ask some people to dine with I , " answer Egerton , " but I will 
 put they off . I see Lord Lansmere too seldom to miss any occasion to 
 meet a man whom I respect so much . " 

 " so seldom ! true , he be very little in town ; but why do not   you go and 
 see he in the country ? good shooting , - pleasant , old - fashioned house . " 

 " my dear Westbourne , his house be ' nimium vicina Cremonae , ' close to a 
 borough in which I have be burn in effigy . " 

 " ha ! ha ! yes , I remember you first come into parliament for that snug 
 little place ; but Lansmere himself never find fault with your vote , 
 do he ? " 

 " he behave very handsomely , and say he have not presume to consider I 
 his mouthpiece ; and then , too , I be so intimate with L'Estrange . " 

 " be that queer fellow ever come back to England ? " 

 " he come , generally , every year , for a few day , just to see his father 
 and mother , and then return to the continent . " 

 " I never meet he . " 

 " he come in September or October , when you , of course , be not in town , 
 and it be in town that the Lansmeres meet he . " 

 " why do he not go to they ? " 

 " a man in England but once a year , and for a few day , have so much to do 
 in London , I suppose . " 

 " be he as amusing as ever ? " Egerton nod . 

 " so distinguished as he might be ! " remark Lord Westbourne . 

 " so distinguished as he be ! " say Egerton , formally ; " an officer 
 select for praise , even in such field as Quatre Bras and Waterloo ; 
 a scholar , too , of the fine taste ; and as an accomplished gentleman 
 matchless ! " 

 " I like to hear one man praise another so warmly in these ill - natured 
 day , " answer Lord Westbourne . " but still , though L'Estrange be 
 doubtless all you say , do not   you think he rather waste his life live 
 abroad ? " 

 " and try to be happy , Westbourne ? be you sure it be not we who waste 
 our life ? but I can not   stay to hear your answer . here we be at the door 
 of my prison . " 

 " on Saturday , then ? " 

 " on Saturday . good day . " 

 for the next hour or more , Mr. Egerton be engage on the affair of the 
 State . he then snatch an interval of leisure   while await a report , 
 which he have instruct a clerk to make he , in order to reply to his 
 letter . those on public business be soon despatch ; and throw 
 his reply aside to be seal by a subordinate hand , he draw out the 
 letter which he have put apart as private . 

 he attend first to that of his steward : the steward 's letter be long , 
 the reply be contain in three line . Pitt himself be scarcely more 
 negligent of his private interest and concern than Audley Egerton ; 
 yet , withal , Audley Egerton be say by his enemy to be an egotist . 

 the next letter he write be to Randal , and that , though long , be far 
 from prolix : it run thus : - 

  DEAR MR . LESLIE , - I appreciate your delicacy in consult I 
  whether you should accept Frank Hazeldean 's invitation to call at 
  the Hall . since you be ask , I can see no objection to it . I 
  should be sorry if you appear to force yourself there ; and for the 
  rest , as a general rule , I think a young man who have his own way to 
  make in life have well avoid all intimacy with those of his own age 
  who have no kindre object nor congenial pursuit . 

  as soon as this visit be pay , I wish you to come to London . the 
  report I receive of your progress at Eton render it unnecessary , in 
  my judgment , that you should return there . if your father have no 
  objection , I propose that you should go to Oxford at the ensue 
  term . meanwhile , I have engage a gentleman , who be a fellow of 
  Balliol , to read with you . he be of opinion , judge only by your 
  high repute at Eton , that you may at once obtain a scholarship in 
  that college . if you do so , I shall look upon your career in life 
  as assure . 

  your affectionate friend , and sincere well - wisher , A. E. 

 the reader will remark that in this letter there be a certain tone of 
 formality . Mr. Egerton do not call his protege " Dear Randal , " as would 
 seem natural , but coldly and stiffly , " dear Mr. Leslie . " he hint , also , 
 that the boy have his own way to make in life . be this mean to guard 
 against too sanguine notion of inheritance , which his generosity may 
 have excite ? the letter to Lord L'Estrange be of a very different kind 
 from the other . it be long , and full of such little scrap of news and 
 gossip as may interest friend in a foreign land ; it be write gayly , 
 and as with a wish to cheer his friend ; you could see that it be a 
 reply to a melancholy letter ; and in the whole tone and spirit there be 
 an affection , even to tenderness , of which those who most like Audley 
 Egerton would have scarcely suppose he capable . yet , notwithstanding , 
 there be a kind of constraint in the letter , which perhaps only the 
 fine tact of a woman would detect . it have not that abandon , that hearty 
 self - outpouring , which you might expect would characterize the letter 
 of two such friend , who have be boy at school together , and which 
 do breathe indeed in all the abrupt ramble sentence of his 
 correspondent . but where be the evidence of the constraint ? Egerton be 
 off - hand enough where his pen run glibly through paragraph that relate 
 to other ; it be simply that he say nothing about himself , - that he 
 avoid all reference to the inner world of sentiment and feeling ! but 
 perhaps , after all , the man have no sentiment and feeling ! how can you 
 expect that a steady personage in practical life , whose morning be 
 spend in Downing Street , and whose night be consume in watch 
 Government bill through a committee , can write in the same style as an 
 idle dreamer amidst the pine of Ravenna , or on the bank of Como ? 

 Audley have just finish this epistle , such as it be , when the 
 attendant in waiting announce the arrival of a deputation from 
 a provincial trading town , the member of which deputation he have 
 appoint to meet at two o'clock . there be no office in London at which 
 deputation be keep wait less than at that over which Mr. Egerton 
 preside . 

 the deputation enter , - some score or so of middle - age , 
 comfortable - look person , who , nevertheless , have their grievance , and 
 consider their own interest , and those of the country , menace by a 
 certain clause in a bill bring in by Mr. Egerton . 

 the mayor of the town be the chief spokesman , and he speak well , - but 
 in a style to which the dignified official be not accustom . it be a 
 slap - dash style , - unceremonious , free and easy , - an american style . and , 
 indeed , there be something altogether in the appearance and bearing of 
 the mayor which savour of residence in the Great Republic . he be a 
 very handsome man , but with a look sharp and domineering , - the look of 
 a man who do not care a straw for president or monarch , and who enjoy 
 the liberty to speak his mind and " wallop his own nigger ! " 

 his fellow - burgher evidently regard he with great respect ; and Mr. 
 Egerton have penetration enough to perceive that Mr. Mayor must be a rich 
 man , as well as an eloquent one , to have overcome those impression 
 of soreness or jealousy which his tone be calculate to create in the 
 self - love of his equal . 

 Mr. Egerton be far too wise to be easily offend by mere manner ; 
 and though he star somewhat haughtily when he find his observation 
 actually pooh - poohe , he be not above be convince . there be much 
 sense and much justice in Mr. Mayor 's argument , and the statesman 
 civilly promise to take they into full consideration . 

 he then bow out the deputation ; but scarcely have the door close 
 before it open again , and Mr. Mayor present himself alone , say 
 aloud to his companion in the passage , " I forgot something I have to say 
 to Mr. Egerton ; wait below for I . " 

 " well , Mr. Mayor , " say Audley , point to a seat , " what else would you 
 suggest ? " 

 the mayor look round to see that the door be close ; and then , 
 draw his chair close to Mr. Egerton 's , lay his forefinger on that 
 gentleman 's arm , and say , " I think I speak to a man of the world , sir ? " 

 Mr. Egerton bow , and make no reply by word , but he gently remove his 
 arm from the touch of the forefinger . 

 MR . MAYOR . - " you observe , sir , that I do not ask the member whom we 
 return to parliament to accompany we . do well without them . you know 
 they be both in Opposition , - out - and - outer . " 

 MR . EGERTON . - " it be a misfortune which the Government can not remember 
 when the question be whether the trade of the town itself be to be 
 serve or injure . " 

 MR . MAYOR . - " well , I guess you speak handsome , sir . but you would be glad to 
 have two member to support minister after the next election . " 

 MR . EGERTON   smile . - " unquestionably , Mr. Mayor . " 

 MR . MAYOR . - " and I can do it , Mr. Egerton . I may say I have the town in 
 my pocket ; so I ought , - I spend a great deal of money in it . now , 
 you see , Mr. Egerton , I have pass a part of my life in a land of 
 liberty - the United States - and I come to the point when I speak to a 
 man of the world . I be a man of the world myself , sir . and so , if the 
 Government will do something for I , why , I will do something for the 
 Government . two vote for a free and independent town like ours , - that be 
 something , be not   it ? " 

 MR . EGERTON   take by surprise . - " really , I - " 

 MR . MAYOR   advance his chair still near , and interrupt the 
 official . - " no nonsense , you see , on one side or the other . the fact 
 be , that I have   take it into my head that I should like to be knight . 
 you may well look surprised , Mr. Egerton , - trumpery thing enough , I 
 dare say ; still , every man have his weakness , and I should like to be 
 Sir Richard . well , if you can get I make Sir Richard , you may just name 
 your two member for the next election , - that be , if they belong to 
 your own set , enlighten man , up to the time . that be speak fair and 
 manful , be not   it ? " 

 MR . EGERTON   draw himself up . - " I be at a loss to guess why you 
 should select I , sir , for this very extraordinary proposition . " 

 MR . MAYOR   nod good - humouredly . - " why , you see , I do not   go along 
 with the government ; you be the good of the bunch . and may be you would 
 like to strengthen your own party . this be quite between you and I , you 
 understand ; honour be a jewel ! " 

 MR . EGERTON   with great gravity . - " Sir , I be oblige by your good 
 opinion ; but I agree with my colleague in all the great question that 
 affect the government of the country , and - " 

 MR . MAYOR   interrupt he . - " ah , of course , you must say so ; very 
 right . but I guess thing would go differently if you be Prime 
 Minister . however , I have another reason for speak to you about my 
 little job . you see you be member for Lansmere once , and I think you 
 only come in by a majority of two , eh ? " 

 MR . EGERTON . - " I know nothing of the particular of that election ; I be 
 not present . " 

 MR . MAYOR . - " no ; but luckily for you , two relation of mine be , and 
 they vote for you . two vote , and you come in by two . since then , you 
 have get into very snug quarter here , and I think we have a claim on 
 you - " 

 MR . EGERTON . - " Sir , I acknowledge no such claim ; I be and be a stranger 
 to Lansmere ; and if the elector do I the honour to return I to 
 parliament , it be in compliment rather to - " 

 MR . MAYOR   again interrupt the official . - " rather to Lord Lansmere , 
 you be go to say ; unconstitutional doctrine that , I fancy . peer of 
 the realm . but never mind , I know the world ; and I would ask Lord Lansmere 
 to do my affair for I , only he be a pompous sort of man ; might be 
 qualmish : antiquated notion . not up to snuff like you and I . " 

 MR . EGERTON   in great disgust , and settle his paper before 
 he . - " Sir , it be not in my department to recommend to his Majesty 
 candidate for the honour of knighthood , and it be still less in my 
 department to make bargain for seat in parliament . " 

 MR . MAYOR . - " oh , if that be the case , you will excuse I ; I do not   know much 
 of the etiquette in these matter . but I think that if I put two 
 seat in your hand for your own friend , you might contrive to take 
 the affair into your department , whatever it be . but since you say you 
 agree with your colleague , perhaps it come to the same thing . now , you 
 must not suppose I want to sell the town , and that I can change and chop 
 my politic for my own purpose . no such thing ! I do not   like the sit 
 member ; I be all for progressing , but they go too much ahead for I ; 
 and since the Government be disposed to move a little , why , I would as 
 lief support they as not . but , in common gratitude , you see , " add the 
 mayor , coaxingly , " I ought to be knight ! I can keep up the dignity , 
 and do credit to his Majesty . " 

 MR . EGERTON   without look up from his paper . - " I can only refer 
 you , sir , to the proper quarter . " 

 MR . MAYOR   impatiently . - " proper quarter ! well , since there be so much 
 humbug in this old country of ours , that one must go through all the 
 form and get at the job regularly , just tell I whom I ought to go to . " 

 MR . EGERTON   begin to be amuse as well as indignant . - " if you want 
 a knighthood , Mr. Mayor , you must ask the Prime Minister ; if you want 
 to give the Government information relative to seat in parliament , you 
 must introduce yourself to Mr. - - - , the Secretary of the Treasury . " 

 MR . MAYOR . - " and if I go to the last chap , what do you think he will say ? " 

 MR . EGERTON   the amusement preponderate over the indignation . - " he 
 will say , I suppose , that you must not put the thing in the light in 
 which you have put it to I ; that the Government will be very proud to 
 have the confidence of yourself and your brother elector ; and that a 
 gentleman like you , in the proud position of mayor , may well hope to be 
 knight on some fitting occasion ; but that you must not talk about the 
 knighthood just at present , and must confine yourself to convert the 
 unfortunate political opinion of the town . " 

 MR . MAYOR . - " well , I guess that chap there would want to do I ! not 
 quite so green , Mr. Egerton . perhaps I would well go at once to the 
 fountain - head . how d ' ye think the Premier would take it ? " 

 MR . EGERTON   the indignation preponderate over the 
 amusement . - " probably just as I be about to do . " 

 Mr. Egerton ring the bell ; the attendant appear . " show Mr. Mayor the 
 way out , " say the minister . 

 the mayor turn round sharply , and his face be purple . he walk 
 straight to the door ; but suffer the attendant to precede he along 
 the corridor , he come back with a rapid stride , and clench his hand , 
 and with a voice thick with passion , cry , " some day or other I will 
 make you smart for this , as sure as my name 's Dick Avenel ! " 

 " Avenel ! " repeat Egerton , recoiling , - " Avenel ! " but the mayor be 
 go . 

 Audley fall into a deep and musing revery , which seem gloomy , and 
 last till the attendant announce that the horse be at the door . 

 he then look up , still abstractedly , and see his letter to Harley 
 L'Estrange open on the table . he draw it towards he , and write , " a man 
 have just leave I , who call himself Aven - " in the middle of the name 
 his pen stop . " no , no , " mutter the writer , " what folly to reopen 
 the old wound there ! " and he carefully erase the word . 

 Audley Egerton do not ride in the Park that day , as be his will not , but 
 dismiss his groom ; and , turn his horse 's head towards Westminster 
 Bridge , take his solitary way into the country . he ride at first slowly , 
 as if in thought ; then fast , as if try to escape from thought . he 
 be later than usual at the House that evening , and he look pale and 
 fatigue . but he have to speak , and he speak well . 




 CHAPTER VII . 

 in spite of all his machiavellian wisdom , Dr. Riccabocca have be foil 
 in his attempt to seduce Leonard Fairfield into his service , even though 
 he succeed in partially win over the widow to his view . for to 
 she he represent the worldly advantage of the thing . Lenny would 
 learn to be fit for more than a day - labourer ; he would learn gardening , 
 in all its branch , - rise some day to be a head gardener . " and , " 
 say Riccabocca , " I will take care of his book - learning , and teach he 
 whatever he have a head for . " 

 " he have a head for everything , " say the widow . 

 " then , " say the wise man , " everything shall go into it . " the widow be 
 certainly dazzle ; for , as we have see , she highly prize scholarly 
 distinction , and she know that the parson look upon Riccabocca as a 
 wondrous learn man . but still Riccabocca be say to be a Papist , 
 and suspect to be a conjuror . her scruple on both these point , the 
 Italian , who be an adept in the art of talk over the fair sex , would 
 no doubt have dissipate , if there have be any use in it ; but Lenny 
 put a dead stop to all negotiation . he have take a mortal dislike to 
 Riccabocca : he be very much frighten by he , - and the spectacle , 
 the pipe , the cloak , the long hair , and the red umbrella ; and say so 
 sturdily , in reply to every overture , " please , sir , I would rather not ; I would 
 rather stay along with Mother , " that Riccabocca be force to suspend 
 all further experiment in his machiavellian diplomacy . he be not at 
 all cast down , however , by his first failure ; on the contrary , he be 
 one of those man whom opposition stimulate ; and what before have be 
 but a suggestion of prudence , become an object of desire . plenty 
 of other lad might no doubt be have on as reasonable term as Lenny 
 Fairfield ; but the moment Lenny presume to baffle the Italian 's 
 design upon he , the special acquisition , of Lenny become of paramount 
 importance in the eye of Signor Riccabocca . 

 Jackeymo , however , lose all his interest in the trap , snare , and gin 
 which his master propose to lay for Leonard Fairfield , in the more 
 immediate surprise that await he on learn that Dr. Riccabocca have 
 accept an invitation to pass a few day at the Hall . 

 " there will be no one there but the family , " say Riccabocca . " poor 
 Giacomo , a little chat in the servant ' hall will do you good ; and the 
 squire 's beef be more nourishing , after all , than the stickleback and 
 minnow . it will lengthen your life . " 

 " the padrone jest , " say Jackeymo , statelily ; " as if any one could 
 starve in his service . " 

 " um , " say Riccabocca . " at least , faithful friend , you have try that 
 experiment as far as human nature will permit ; " and he extend his hand 
 to his fellow - exile with that familiarity which exist between servant 
 and master in the usage of the continent . Jackeymo bend low , and a tear 
 fall upon the hand he kiss . 

 " Cospetto ! " say Dr. Riccabocca , " a thousand mock pearl do not make up 
 the cost of a single true one ! the tear of woman - we know their worth ; 
 but the tear of an honest man - Fie , Giacomo ! - at least I can never 
 repay you this ! go and see to our wardrobe . " 

 so far as his master 's wardrobe be concern , that order be pleasing 
 to Jackeymo ; for the doctor have in his drawer suit which Jackeymo 
 pronounce to be as good as new , though many a long year have pass 
 since they leave the tailor 's hand . but when Jackeymo come to examine 
 the state of his own clothing department , his face grow considerably 
 long . it be not that he be without other clothe than those on his 
 back , - quantity be there , but the quality ! mournfully he gaze on two 
 suit , complete in three separate member of which man 's raiment be 
 compose : the one suit extend at length upon his bed , like a veteran 
 stretch by pious hand after death ; the other bring piecemeal to the 
 invidious light , - the torso place upon a chair , the limb dangle down 
 from Jackeymo 's melancholy arm . no body long expose at the Morgue 
 could evince less sign of resuscitation than those respectable defunct ! 
 for , indeed , Jackeymo have be less thrifty of his apparel , more 
 profusus sui , than his master . in the early day of their exile , he 
 preserve the decorous habit of dress for dinner , - it be a respect 
 due to the padrone , - and that habit have last till the two habit on 
 which it necessarily depend have evince the first symptom of decay ; 
 then the evening clothe have be take into morning wear , in which hard 
 service they have breathe their last . 

 the doctor , notwithstanding his general philosophical abstraction from 
 such household detail , have more than once say , rather in pity to 
 Jackeymo than with an eye to that respectability which the costume 
 of the servant reflect on the dignity of the master , " Giacomo , thou 
 want clothe ; fit thyself out of mine ! " 

 and Jackeymo have bow his gratitude , as if the donation have be 
 accept ; but the fact be that that same fitting out be easy say 
 than do . for though - thank to an existence mainly upon stickleback 
 and minnow - both Jackeymo and Riccabocca have arrive at that state 
 which the longevity of miser prove to be most healthful to the human 
 frame , - namely , skin and bone , - yet the bone contain in the skin of 
 Riccabocca all take longitudinal direction ; while those in the skin of 
 Jackeymo spread out latitudinally . and you might as well have make 
 the bark of a lombardy poplar serve for the trunk of some dwarfed and 
 pollard oak - in whose hollow the Babes of the Wood could have sleep 
 at their ease - as have fit out Jackeymo from the garb of Riccabocca . 
 moreover , if the skill of the tailor could have accomplish that 
 undertaking , the faithful Jackeymo would never have have the heart 
 to avail himself of the generosity of his master . he have a sort of 
 religious sentiment , too , about those vestment of the padrone . the 
 ancient , we know , when escape from shipwreck , suspend in the 
 votive temple the garment in which they have struggle through the wave . 
 Jackeymo look on those relic of the past with a kindre superstition . 
 " this coat the padrone wear on such an occasion . I remember the 
 very evening the padrone last put on those pantaloon ! " and coat and 
 pantaloon be tenderly dust , and carefully restore to their sacred 
 rest . 

 but now , after all , what be to be do ? Jackeymo be much too proud 
 to exhibit his person to the eye of the squire 's butler in habiliment 
 discreditable to himself and the padrone . in the midst of his perplexity 
 the bell ring , and he go down into the parlour . 

 Riccabocca be stand on the hearth under his symbolical 
 representation of the " Patriae Exul . " 

 " Giacomo , " quoth he , " I have be think that thou hast never do 
 what I tell thee , and fit thyself out from my superfluity . but we 
 be go now into the great world : visit once begin , Heaven know 
 where it may stop . go to the near town and get thyself clothe . 
 thing be dear in England . will this suffice ? " and Riccabocca extend 
 a five - pound note . 

 Jackeymo , we have see , be more familiar with his master than we formal 
 English permit our domestic to be with we ; but in his familiarity he 
 be usually respectful . this time , however , respect desert he . 

 " the padrone be mad ! " he exclaim ; " he would fling away his whole 
 fortune if I would let he . five pound English , or a hundred and 
 twenty - six pound Milanese ! Santa Maria ! unnatural father ! and what be 
 to become of the poor signorina ? be this the way you be to marry she in 
 the foreign land ? " 

 " Giacomo , " say Riccabocca , bow his head to the storm , " the 
 signorina to - morrow ; to - day the honour of the House . thy small - clothe , 
 Giacomo , - miserable man , thy small - clothe ! " 

 " it be just , " say Jackeymo , recover himself , and with humility ; " and 
 the padrone do right to blame I , but not in so cruel a way . it be 
 just , - the padrone lodge and board I , and give I handsome wage , 
 and he have a right to expect that I should not go in this figure . " 

 " for the board and the lodgment , good , " say Riccabocca . " for the 
 handsome wage , they be the vision of thy fancy ! " 

 " they be no such thing , " say Jackeymo , " they be only in arrear . as if 
 the padrone could not pay they some day or other ; as if I be demean 
 myself by serve a master who do not intend to pay his servant ! and 
 can not   I wait ? have I not my saving too ? but be cheer , be cheer ; 
 you shall be content with I . I have two beautiful suit still . I be 
 arrange they when you rang for I . you shall see , you shall see . " 

 and Jackeymo hurry from the room , hurry back into his own chamber , 
 unlock a little trunk which he keep at his bed - head , toss out 
 a variety of small article , and from the deep depth extract a 
 leathern purse . he empty the content on the bed . they be chiefly 
 italian coin , some five - franc piece , a silver medallion inclose 
 a little image of his patron saint , - San Giacomo , - one solid English 
 guinea , and somewhat more than a pound 's worth in english silver . 
 Jackeymo put back the foreign coin , say prudently , " one will lose on 
 they here ; " he seize the english coin , and count they out . " but be 
 you enough , you rascal ? " quoth he , angrily , give they a good shake . 
 his eye catch sight of the medallion , - he pause ; and after eye the 
 tiny representation of the saint with great deliberation , he add , in 
 a sentence which he must have pick up from the proverbial aphorism of 
 his master , - 

 " what be the difference between the enemy who do not hurt I , and the 
 friend who do not serve I ? Monsignore San Giacomo , my patron saint , 
 you be of very little use to I in the leathern bag ; but if you help I 
 to get into a new pair of small - clothe on this important occasion , 
 you will be a friend indeed . alla bisogna , Monsignore . " then , gravely 
 kiss the medallion , he thrust it into one pocket , the coin into 
 the other , make up a bundle of the two defunct suit , and mutter 
 to himself , " Beast , miser , that I be , to disgrace the padrone with all 
 these saving in his service ! " run downstairs into his pantry , catch 
 up his hat and stick , and in a few moment more be see trudge off to 
 the neighbour town of L - - - - . 

 apparently the poor Italian succeed , for he come back that evening in 
 time to prepare the thin gruel which make his master 's supper , with a 
 suit of black , - a little threadbare , but still highly respectable , - two 
 shirt front , and two white cravat . but out of all this finery , 
 Jackeymo hold the small - clothe in especial veneration ; for as they have 
 cost exactly what the medallion have sell for , so it seem to he that 
 San Giacomo have hear his prayer in that quarter to which he have more 
 exclusively direct the saint 's direction . the other habiliment come 
 to he in the merely human process of sale and barter ; the small - clothe 
 be the personal gratuity of San Giacomo ! 




 CHAPTER VIII . 

 Life have be subject to many ingenious comparison ; and if we do 
 not understand it any well , it be not for want of what be call 
 " reasoning by illustration . " amongst other resemblance , there be 
 moment when , to a quiet contemplator , it suggest the image of one of 
 those rotatory entertainment commonly see in fair , and know by the 
 name of " whirligig , " or " roundabout , " in which each participator of 
 the pastime , seat on his hobby , be always apparently in the act of 
 pursue some one before he , while he be pursue by some one behind . 
 Man , and woman too , be naturally animal of chase ; the great still 
 find something to follow , and there be no one too humble not to be an 
 object of prey to another . thus , confine our view to the village of 
 Hazeldean , we behold in this whirligig Dr. Riccabocca spur his hobby 
 after Lenny Fairfield ; and Miss Jemima , on her decorous side - saddle , 
 whip after Dr. Riccabocca . why , with so long and intimate a 
 conviction of the villany of our sex , Miss Jemima should resolve upon 
 give the male animal one more chance of redeem itself in her eye , 
 I leave to the explanation of those gentleman who profess to find 
 " their only book in woman 's look . " perhaps it might be from the 
 over - tenderness and clemency of Miss Jemima 's nature ; perhaps it might 
 be that as yet she have only experience the villany of man bear and 
 rear in these cold northern climate , and in the land of Petrarch and 
 Romeo , of the citron and myrtle , there be reason to expect that 
 the native monster would be more amenable to gentle influence , less 
 obstinately harden in his iniquity . without enter far into 
 these hypothesis , it be sufficient to say that , on Signor Riccabocca 's 
 appearance in the drawing - room at Hazeldean , Miss Jemima feel more than 
 ever rejoice that she have relax in his favour her general hostility 
 to man . in truth , though Frank see something quizzical in the 
 old - fashioned and outlandish cut of the Italian 's sober dress ; in his 
 long hair , and the chapeau bras , over which he bow so gracefully , and 
 then press it , as if to his heart , before tuck it under his arm , 
 after the fashion in which the gizzard repose under the wing of a 
 roasted pullet , - yet it be impossible that even Frank could deny 
 to Riccabocca that praise which be due to the air and manner of an 
 unmistakable gentleman . and certainly as , after dinner , conversation 
 grow more familiar , and the parson and Mrs. Dale , who have be invite 
 to meet their friend , do their good to draw he out , his talk , though 
 sometimes a little too wise for his listener , become eminently animate 
 and agreeable . it be the conversation of a man who , besides the 
 knowledge which be acquire from book and life , have study the art 
 which become a gentleman , - that of please in polite society . 

 the result be that all be charm with he ; and that even Captain 
 Barnabas postpone the whist - table for a full hour after the usual time . 
 the doctor do not play ; he thus become the property of the two lady , 
 Miss Jemima and Mrs. Dale . 

 seat between the two , in the place rightfully appertain to Flimsey , 
 who this time be fairly dislodge , to her great wonder and discontent , 
 the doctor be the emblem of true Domestic Felicity , place between 
 Friendship and Love . 

 friendship , as become she , work quietly at the embroider 
 pocket - handkerchief and leave Love to more animate operation . 

 " you must be very lonely at the Casino , " say love , in a sympathizing 
 tone . 

 " Madam , " reply Riccabocca , gallantly , " I shall think so when I leave 
 you . " 

 friendship cast a sly glance at love ; Love blush , or look down 
 on the carpet , - which come to the same thing . " yet , " begin love 
 again , - " yet solitude to a feeling heart - " 

 Riccabocca think of the note of invitation , and involuntarily button 
 his coat , as if to protect the individual organ thus alarmingly refer 
 to . 

 " solitude to a feeling heart have its charm . it be so hard even for we 
 poor ignorant woman to find a congenial companion - but for you ! " love 
 stop short , as if it have say too much , and smell confusedly at its 
 bouquet . 

 Dr. Riccabocca cautiously lower his spectacle , and dart one glance 
 which , with the rapidity and comprehensiveness of lightning , seem to 
 envelop and take in , as it be , the whole inventory of Miss Jemima 's 
 personal attraction . now Miss Jemima , as I have before observe , have 
 a mild and pensive expression of countenance ; and she would have be 
 positively pretty have the mildness look a little more alert , and the 
 pensiveness somewhat less lackadaisical . in fact , though Miss Jemima be 
 constitutionally mild , she be not de natura pensive ; she have too much 
 of the hazeldean blood in her vein for that sullen and viscid humour 
 call melancholy , and therefore this assumption of pensiveness really 
 spoil her character of feature , which only want to be light up 
 by a cheerful smile to be extremely prepossess . the same remark might 
 apply to the figure , which - thank to the same pensiveness - lose all 
 the undulating grace which movement and animation bestow on the fluent 
 curve of the feminine form . the figure be a good figure , examine in 
 detail , - a little thin , perhaps , but by no mean emaciate , with just 
 and elegant proportion , and naturally light and flexible . but the same 
 unfortunate pensiveness give to the whole a character of inertness and 
 languor ; and when Miss Jemima recline on the sofa , so complete seem 
 the relaxation of nerve and muscle that you would have think she have 
 lose the use of her limb . over her face and form , thus defraud of 
 the charm Providence have bestow on they , Dr. Riccabocca 's eye glance 
 rapidly ; and then move near to Mrs. Dale - " defend I "   he stop 
 a moment , and add   " from the charge of not be able to appreciate 
 congenial companionship . " 

 " oh , I do not say that ! " cry Miss Jemima . 

 " pardon I , " say the Italian , " if I be so dull as to misunderstand 
 you . one may well lose one 's head , at least , in such a neighbourhood as 
 this . " he rise as he speak , and bend over Frank 's shoulder to examine 
 some view of Italy , which Miss Jemima   with what , if wholly unselfish , 
 would have be an attention truly delicate   have extract from the 
 library in order to gratify the guest . 

 " most interesting creature , indeed , " sigh Miss Jemima , " but too - too 
 flattering . " 

 " tell I , " say Mrs. Dale , gravely , " do you think , love , that you could 
 put off the end of the world a little long , or must we make haste in 
 order to be in time ? " 

 " how wicked you be ! " say Miss Jemima , turn aside . some few minute 
 afterwards , Mrs. Dale contrive it so that Dr. Riccabocca and herself 
 be in a farth corner of the room , look at a picture say to be by 
 Wouvermans . 

 MRS . DALE . - " she be very amiable , Jemima , be she not ? " 

 RICCABOCCA . - " exceedingly so . very fine battle - piece ! " 

 MRS . DALE . - " so kind - hearted . " 

 RICCABOCCA . - " all lady be . how naturally that warrior make his 
 desperate cut at the runaway ! " 

 MRS . DALE . - " she be not what be call regularly handsome , but she have 
 something very win . " 

 riccabocca   with a smile . - " so win , that it be strange she be not 
 win . that gray mare in the foreground stand out very boldly ! " 

 MRS . DALE   distrust the smile of Riccabocca , and throw in a more 
 effective grape - charge . - " not win yet ; and it be strange ! she will have 
 a very pretty fortune . " 

 RICCABOCCA . - " ah ! " 

 MRS . DALE . " six thousand pound , I dare say , - certainly four . " 

 riccabocca   suppress a sigh , and with his wonte address . - " if Mrs. 
 Dale be still single , she would never need a friend to say what her
