 not Miss Jemima 's fault that she be still - Miss Jemima ! " 

 the foreigner slip away as he speak , and sit himself down beside the 
 whist - player . 

 Mrs. Dale be disappoint , but certainly not offend . " it would be 
 such a good thing for both , " mutter she , almost inaudibly . 

 " Giacomo , " say Riccabocca , as he be undressing that night in the 
 large , comfortable , well - carpet english bedroom , with that great 
 English four - post bed in the recess which seem make to shame folk 
 out of single blessedness , " Giacomo , I have have this evening the offer 
 of probably L6000 , certainly of four thousand . " 

 " Cosa meravigliosa ! " -   " miraculous thing . "   - exclaim Jackeymo , and he 
 cross himself with great fervour . " six thousand pound English ! 
 why , that must be a hundred thousand - blockhead that I be ! - more than 
 L150,000 Milanese ! " and Jackeymo , who be considerably enliven by the 
 squire 's ale , commence a series of gesticulation and caper , in the 
 midst of which he stop and cry , " but not for nothing ? " 

 " nothing ! no ! " 

 " these mercenary English ! the Government want to bribe you ? " 

 " that be not it . " 

 " the priest want you to turn heretic ? " 

 " bad than that ! " say the philosopher . 

 " bad than that ! o padrone ! for shame ! " 

 " do not   be a fool , but pull off my pantaloon - they want I never to wear 
 THESE again ! " 

 " never to wear what ? " exclaim Jackeymo , star outright at his 
 master 's long leg in their linen drawer , - " never to wear - " 

 " the breech , " say Riccabocca , laconically . 

 " the barbarian ! " falter Jackeymo . 

 " my nightcap ! and never to have any comfort in this , " say Riccabocca , 
 draw on the cotton head - gear ; " and never to have any sound sleep 
 in that , " point to the four - post bed ; " and to be a bondsman and 
 a slave , " continue Riccabocca , wax wroth ; " and to be wheedle and 
 purr at , and paw and claw , and scold and fondle , and blind 
 and deafen , and bridle and saddle - bedevil and - married ! " 

 " married ! " say Jackeymo , more dispassionately - " that be very bad , 
 certainly ; but more than a hundred and fifty thousand lira , and perhaps 
 a pretty young lady , and - " 

 " pretty young lady ! " growl Riccabocca , jump into bed and draw 
 the clothe fiercely over he . " put out the candle , and get along with 
 you , - do , you villanous old incendiary ! " 




 CHAPTER IX . 

 it be not many day since the resurrection of those ill - omen stock , 
 and it be evident already , to an ordinary observer , that something 
 wrong have get into the village . the peasant wear a sullen expression of 
 countenance ; when the squire pass , they take off their hat with more 
 than ordinary formality , but they do not return the same broad smile to 
 his quick , hearty " good - day , my man . " the woman peer at he from the 
 threshold or the casement , but do not , as be their will not   as least the 
 will not of the prettiest , take occasion to come out to catch his passing 
 compliment on their own good look , or their tidy cottage . and the 
 child , who use to play after work on the site of the old stock , now 
 shun the place , and , indeed , seem to cease play altogether . 

 on the other hand , no man like to build , or rebuild , a great public 
 work for nothing . now that the squire have resuscitate the stock , and 
 make they so exceedingly handsome , it be natural that he should wish 
 to put somebody into they . moreover , his pride and self - esteem have be 
 wound by the parson 's opposition ; and it would be a justification to 
 his own forethought , and a triumph over the parson 's understanding , 
 if he could satisfactorily and practically establish a proof that the 
 stock have not be repair before they be want . 

 therefore , unconsciously to himself , there be something about the 
 squire more burly and authoritative and menacing than heretofore . Old 
 Gaffer Solomons observe , " that they have well moind well what they 
 be about , for that the squire have a wicked look in the tail of his 
 eye , - just as the dun bull have afore it toss neighbour Barnes 's little 
 boy . " 

 for two or three day these mute sign of something brew in the 
 atmosphere have be rather noticeable than notice , without any positive 
 overt act of tyranny on the one hand or rebellion on the other . but on 
 the very Saturday night in which Dr. Riccabocca be instal in 
 the four - post bed in the chintz chamber , the threaten revolution 
 commence . in the dead of that night personal outrage be commit on 
 the stock . and on the Sunday morning , Mr. Stirn , who be the early 
 riser in the parish , perceive , in go to the farmyard , that the knob 
 of the column that flank the board have be feloniously break off ; 
 that the four hole be bung up with mud ; and that some jacobinical 
 villain have carve , on the very centre of the flourish or scroll - work , 
 " dam the stock ! " Mr. Stirn be much too vigilant a right - hand man , much 
 too zealous a friend of law and order , not to regard such proceeding 
 with horror and alarm . and when the squire come into his dressing - room 
 at half - past seven , his butler   who fulfil also the duty of valet 
 inform he , with a mysterious air , that Mr. Stirn have something " very 
 partikler to communicate about a most howdacious midnight ' spiracy and 
 ' sault . " 

 the squire star , and bade Mr. Stirn be admit . 

 " well ? " cry the squire , suspend the operation of strop his 
 razor . 

 Mr. Stirn groan . 

 " well , man , what now ? " 

 " I never know such a thing in this here parish afore , " begin Mr. 
 Stirn ; " and I can only ' count for it by s'pose that they foreign 
 Papishers have be semminate - " 

 " been what ? " 

 " semminating - " 

 " disseminate , you blockhead , - disseminate what ? " 

 " Damn the stock , " begin Mr. Stirn , plunge right in media re , and by 
 a fine use of one of the noble figure in rhetoric . 

 " Mr. Stirn ! " cry the squire , redden , " do you say , ' damn the 
 stock ' ? - damn my new handsome pair of stock ! " 

 " Lord forbid , sir ; that be what they say : that be what they have digge on 
 it with knife and dagger , and they have stuff mud in its four hole , 
 and break the capital of the elewation . " 

 the squire take the napkin off his shoulder , lay down strop and razor ; 
 he seat himself in his armchair majestically , cross his leg , and , 
 in a voice that affect tranquillity , say , - 

 " compose yourself , Stirn ; you have a deposition to make , touch 
 an assault upon - can I trust my sens ? - upon my new stock . compose 
 yourself ; be calm . now ! what the devil be come to the parish ? " 

 " ah , sir , what indeed ? " reply Mr. Stirn : and then lay the 
 forefinger of the right hand on the palm of the left he narrate the 
 case . 

 " and whom do you suspect ? be calm now ; do not   speak in a passion . you be 
 a witness , sir , - a dispassionate , unprejudiced witness . zound and 
 fury ! this be the most insolent , unprovoked , diabolical - but whom do you 
 suspect , I say ? " Stirn twirl his hat , elevate his eyebrow , jerk 
 his thumb over his shoulder , and whisper , " I hear as how the two 
 Papishers sleep at your honour 's last night . " 

 " what , dolt ! do you suppose Dr. Rickeybockey get out of his warm bed to 
 bung up the hole in my new stock ? " 

 " Noa ; he be too cunning to do it himself , but he may have be 
 semminate . he be mighty thick with Parson Dale , and your honour know 
 as how the parson set his face agin the stock . wait a bit , sir , - do not 
 fly at I yet . there be a boy in this here parish - " 

 " a boy ! ah , fool , now you be near the mark . the parson write ' damn 
 the stock , ' indeed ! what boy do you mean ? " 

 " and that boy be cocker up much by Mr. Dale ; and the Papisher go and 
 sit with he and his mother a whole hour t ' other day ; and that boy be 
 as deep as a well ; and I seed he lurk about the place , and hiding 
 hisself under the tree the day the stock be put up , - and that ' ere boy 
 be Lenny Fairfield . " 

 " whew , " say the squire , whistle , " you have not your usual sens 
 about you to - day , man . Lenny Fairfield , - pattern boy of the village . 
 hold your tongue . I dare say it be not do by any one in the parish , 
 after all : some good - for - nothing vagrant - that cursed tinker , who 
 go about with a very vicious donkey , - a donkey that I catch pick 
 thistle out of the very eye of the old stock ! show how the tinker 
 bring up his donkey ! well , keep a sharp look - out . to - day be Sunday ; 
 bad day of the week , I be sorry and ashamed to say , for row and 
 depredation . between the service , and after evening church , there be 
 always idle fellow from all the neighbour country about , as you know 
 too well . depend on it , the real culprit will be find gather round 
 the stock , and will betray themselves ; have your eye , ear , and wit 
 about you , and I have   no doubt we shall come to the right of the matter 
 before the day 's out . and if we do , " add the squire , " we will make an 
 example of the ruffian ! " 

 " in course , " say Stirn : " and if we do not   find he we must make an 
 example all the same . that be what it be , sir . that be why the stock 
 ben't   respect ; they have not have an example yet , - we want an example . " 

 " on my word I believe that be very true ; and we will clap in the first idle 
 fellow you catch in anything wrong , and keep he there for two hour at 
 least . " 

 " with the big pleasure , your honour , - that be what it be . " 

 and Mr. Stirn have now get what he consider a complete and 
 unconditional authority over all the leg and wrist of Hazeldean 
 parish , quoad the stock , take his departure . 




 CHAPTER X. 

 " randal , " say Mrs. Leslie on this memorable Sunday , - " Randal , do you 
 think of go to Mr. Hazeldean 's ? " 

 " yes , ma'am , " answer Randal . " Mr. Egerton do not object to it ; and 
 as I do not return to Eton , I may have no other opportunity of see 
 Frank for some time . I ought not to fail in respect to Mr. Egerton 's 
 natural heir . " 

 " gracious I ! " cry Mrs. Leslie , who , like many woman of her cast and 
 kind , have a sort of worldliness in her notion , which she never evince 
 in her conduct , - " gracious I ! natural heir to the old Leslie property ! " 

 " he be Mr. Egerton 's nephew , and , " add Randal , ingenuously let out 
 his thought , " I be no relation to Mr. Egerton at all . " 

 " but , " say poor Mrs. Leslie , with tear in her eye , " it would be a 
 shame in the man , after pay your schooling and send you to Oxford , 
 and have you to stay with he in the holiday , if he do not mean 
 anything by it . " 

 " Anything , Mother , yes , - but not the thing you suppose . no matter . it 
 be enough that he have arm I for life , and I shall use the weapon as 
 seem to I well . " 

 here the dialogue be suspend by the entrance of the other member of 
 the family , dress for church . 

 " it can not   be time for church ! no , it can not , " exclaim Mrs. Leslie . she 
 be never in time for anything , 

 " last bell ring , " say Mr. Leslie , who , though a slow man , be 
 methodical and punctual . Mrs. Leslie make a frantic rush at the door , 
 the Montfydget blood be now in a blaze , dash up the stair , burst 
 into her room , tear her good bonnet from the peg , snatch her new 
 shawl from the drawer , crush the bonnet on her head , fling the shawl 
 on her shoulder , thrust a desperate pin into its fold , in order to 
 conceal a buttonless yawn in the body of her gown , and then fly back 
 like a whirlwind . meanwhile the family be already out of door , in 
 wait ; and just as the bell cease , the procession move from the 
 shabby house to the dilapidated church . 

 the church be a large one , but the congregation be small , and so be 
 the income of the parson . it be a lay rectory , and the great tithe 
 have belong to the Leslies , but they have be long since sell . the 
 vicarage , still in their gift , might be worth a little more than L100 a 
 year . the present incumbent have nothing else to live upon . he be a good 
 man , and not originally a stupid one ; but penury and the anxious 
 care for wife and family , combine with what may be call solitary 
 confinement for the cultivate mind , when , amidst the two - legged 
 creature round , it see no other cultivate mind with which it can 
 exchange one extra - parochial thought , have lull he into a lazy 
 mournfulness , which at time be very like imbecility . his income 
 allow he to do no good to the parish , whether in work , trade , or 
 charity ; and thus he have no moral weight with the parishioner beyond 
 the example of his sinless life , and such negative effect as might be 
 produce by his slumberous exhortation . therefore his parishioner 
 trouble he very little ; and but for the influence which , in hour 
 of Montfydget activity , Mrs. Leslie exercise over the most 
 tractable , - that is , the child and the aged , - not half - a - dozen 
 person would have know or care whether he shut up his church or not . 

 but our family be seat in state in their old seignorial pew , and Mr. 
 Dumdrum , with a nasal twang , go lugubriously through the prayer ; and 
 the old people who could sin no more , and the child who have not yet 
 learn to sin , croak forth response that might have come from the 
 choral frog in Aristophanes ; and there be a long sermon a propos to 
 nothing which could possibly interest the congregation , - be , in fact , 
 some controversial homily which Mr. Dumdrum have compose and preach 
 year before . and when this discourse be over , there be a loud 
 universal grunt , as if of relief and thanksgiving , and a great clatter 
 of shoe , and the old hobbled , and the young scramble , to the church 
 door . 

 immediately after church , the Leslie family din ; and as soon as dinner 
 be over , Randal set out on his foot journey to Hazeldean Hall . 

 delicate and even feeble though his frame , he have the energy and 
 quickness of movement which belong to nervous temperament ; and he 
 task the slow stride of a peasant , whom he take to serve he as 
 a guide for the first two or three mile . though Randal have not the 
 gracious open manner with the poor which Frank inherit from his 
 father , he be still   despite many a secret hypocritical vice at war 
 with the character of a gentleman   gentleman enough to have no churlish 
 pride to his inferior . he talk little , but he suffer his guide to 
 talk ; and the boor , who be the same whom Frank have accost , indulge 
 in eulogistic comment on that young gentleman 's pony , from which he 
 diverge into some compliment on the young gentleman himself . randal 
 draw his hat over his brow . there be a wonderful tact and fine breeding 
 in your agricultural peasant ; and though Tom Stowell be but a brutish 
 speciman of the class , he suddenly perceive that he be give pain . 
 he pause , scratch his head , and , glance affectionately towards his 
 companion , exclaim , - 

 " but I shall live to see you on a handsomer beastis than that little 
 pony , Master Randal ; and sure I ought , for you be as good a gentleman as 
 any in the land . " 

 " thank you , " say randal . " but I like walk well than rid , - I be 
 more used to it . " 

 " well , and you walk bra'ly , - there ben't   a well walker in the county . 
 and very pleasant it be walk ; and ' t be a pretty country afore you , 
 all the way to the Hall . " 

 randal strode on , as if impatient of these attempt to flatter or to 
 soothe ; and come at length into a broad lane , say , " I think I can 
 find my way now . many thank to you , Tom ; " and he force a shilling into 
 Tom 's horny palm . the man take it reluctantly , and a tear start to 
 his eye . he feel more grateful for that shilling than he have for Frank 's 
 liberal half - crown ; and he think of the poor fallen family , and forget 
 his own dire wrestle with the wolf at his door . 

 he stay linger in the lane till the figure of Randal be out of 
 sight , and then return slowly . Young Leslie continue to walk on at 
 a quick pace . with all his intellectual culture and his restless 
 aspiration , his breast afford he no thought so generous , no 
 sentiment so poetic , as those with which the unlettered clown creep 
 slouchingly homeward . 

 as Randal gain a point where several lane meet on a broad piece of 
 waste land , he begin to feel tired , and his step slacken . just then 
 a gig emerge from one of these byroad , and take the same direction as 
 the pedestrian . the road be rough and hilly , and the driver proceed 
 at a foot 's pace ; so that the gig and the pedestrian go pretty well 
 abreast . 

 " you seem tired , sir , " say the driver , a stout young farmer of the 
 high class of tenant , and he look down compassionately on the boy 's 
 pale countenance and weary stride . " perhaps we be go the same way , 
 and I can give you a lift ? " 

 it be Randal 's habitual policy to make use of every advantage proffer 
 to he , and he accept the proposal frankly enough to please the honest 
 farmer . 

 " a nice day , sir , " say the latter , as Randal sit by his side . " have you 
 come far ? " 

 " from Rood Hall . " 

 " oh , you be young squire Leslie , " say the farmer , more respectfully , 
 and lift his hat . 

 " yes , my name be Leslie . you know Rood , then ? " 

 " I be bring up on your father 's land , sir . you may have hear of 
 Farmer Bruce ? " 

 RANDAL . - " I remember , when I be a little boy , a Mr. Bruce who rent , I 
 believe , the good part of our land , and who use to bring we cake when 
 he call to see my father . he be a relation of your ? " 

 FARMER BRUCE . - " he be my uncle . he be dead now , poor man . " 

 RANDAL.-"Dead ! I be grieve to hear it . he be very kind to us child . 
 but it be long since he leave my father 's farm . " 

 FARMER BRUCE   apologetically . - " I be sure he be very sorry to go . but , 
 you see , he have an unexpected legacy - " 

 RANDAL . - " and retire from business ? " 

 FARMER BRUCE . - " no . but , have capital , he could afford to pay a good 
 rent for a real good farm . " 

 RANDAL   bitterly . - " all capital seem to fly from the land of Rood . 
 and whose farm do he take ? " 

 FARMER BRUCE . - " he take Hawleigh , under Squire Hazeldean . I rent it now . 
 we have   lay out a power o ' money on it . but I do not   complain . it pay 
 well . " 

 RANDAL . - " would the money have pay as well sink on my father 's land ? " 

 FARMER BRUCE . - " perhaps it might , in the long run . but then , sir , we 
 want new premise , - barn and cattleshed , and a deal more , - which 
 the landlord should do ; but it be not every landlord as can afford that . 
 Squire Hazeldean 's a rich man . " 

 RANDAL . - " ay ! " 

 the road now become pretty good , and the farmer put his horse into a 
 brisk trot . 

 " but which way be you go , sir ? I do not   care for a few mile more or 
 less , if I can be of service . " 

 " I be go to Hazeldean , " say Randal , rouse himself from a revery . 
 " do not   let I take you out of your way . " 

 " O , Hawleigh Farm be on the other side of the village , so it be quite my 
 way , sir . " 

 the farmer , then , who be really a smart young fellow , - one of that race 
 which the application of capital to land have produce , and which , 
 in point of education and refinement , be at least on a par with the 
 squire of a former generation , - begin to talk about his handsome horse , 
 about horse in general , about hunt and coursing : he handle all 
 these subject with spirit , yet with modesty . Randal pull his hat 
 still low down over his brow , and do not interrupt he till they 
 pass the Casino , when , strike by the classic air of the place , and 
 catch a scent from the orange - tree , the boy ask abruptly , " whose 
 house be that ? " 

 " oh , it belong to Squire Hazeldean , but it be let or lend to a foreign 
 mounseer . they say he be quite the gentleman , but uncommonly poor . " 

 " poor , " say Randal , turn back to gaze on the trim garden , the neat 
 terrace , the pretty belvidere , and   the door of the house be open 
 catch a glimpse of the paint hall within , - " poor ? the place seem 
 well keep . what do you call poor , Mr. Bruce ? " 

 the farmer laugh . " well , that be a home question , sir . but I believe 
 the mounseer be as poor as a man can be who make no debt and do not 
 actually starve . " 

 " as poor as my father ? " ask Randal , openly and abruptly . 

 " Lord , sir ! your father be a very rich man compare to he . " randal 
 continue to gaze , and his mind 's eye conjure up the contrast of his 
 slovenly shabby home , with all its neglect appurtenance . no trim 
 garden at Rood Hall , no scent from odorous orange blossom . here poverty 
 at least be elegant , - there , how squalid ! he do not comprehend at 
 how cheap a rate the luxury of the beautiful can be effect . they now 
 approach the extremity of the squire 's park pale ; and Randal , see 
 a little gate , bid the farmer stop his gig , and descend . the boy 
 plunge amidst the thick oak grove ; the farmer go his way blithely , 
 and his mellow merry whistle come to Randal 's moody ear as he glide 
 quick under the shadow of the tree . 

 he arrive at the Hall to find that all the family be at church ; and , 
 accord to the patriarchal custom , the churchgoe family embrace 
 nearly all the servant . it be therefore an old invalid housemaid who 
 open the door to he . she be rather deaf , and seem so stupid 
 that Randal do not ask leave to enter and wait for Frank 's return . he 
 therefore say briefly that he would just stroll on the lawn , and call 
 again when church be over . 

 the old woman star , and strove to hear he ; meanwhile Randal turn 
 round abruptly , and saunter towards the garden side of the handsome 
 old house . 

 there be enough to attract any eye in the smooth greensward of the 
 spacious lawn , in the numerous parterre of variegate flower , in the 
 venerable grandeur of the two mighty cedar , which throw their still 
 shadow over the grass , and in the picturesque building , with its 
 project mullion and heavy gable ; yet I fear that it be with no 
 poet 's nor painter 's eye that this young old man gaze on the scene 
 before he . 

 he behold the evidence of wealth - and the envy of wealth jaundice his 
 soul . 

 fold his arm on his breast , he stand a while , look all around 
 he , with closed lip and lower brow ; then he walk slowly on , his 
 eye fix on the ground , and mutter to himself , - 

 " the heir to this property be little well than a dunce ; and they tell 
 I I have talent and learning , and I have take to my heart the maxim , 
 ' Knowledge be power . ' and yet , with all my struggle , will knowledge 
 ever place I on the same level as that on which this dunce be bear ? I 
 do not   wonder that the poor should hate the rich . but of all the poor , 
 who should hate the rich like the pauper gentleman ? I suppose Audley 
 Egerton mean I to come into parliament , and be a Tory like himself ? 
 what ! keep thing as they be ! no ; for I not even Democracy , unless 
 there first come Revolution . I understand the cry of a Marat , - ' More 
 blood ! ' Marat have live as a poor man , and cultivate science - in the 
 sight of a prince 's palace . " 

 he turn sharply round , and glare vindictively on the poor old Hall , 
 which , though a very comfortable habitation , be certainly no palace ; 
 and , with his arm still fold on his breast , he walk backward , as if 
 not to lose the view , nor the chain of idea it conjure up . 

 " but , " he continue to soliloquize , - " but of revolution there be no 
 chance . yet the same wit and will that would thrive in revolution 
 should thrive in this commonplace life . Knowledge be power . well , then , 
 shall I have no power to oust this blockhead ? oust he - what from ? his 
 father 's hall ? well , but if he be dead , who would be the heir of 
 Hazeldean ? have I not hear my mother say that I be as near in blood to 
 this squire as any one , if he have no child ? oh , but the boy 's life be 
 worth ten of mine ! oust he from what ? at least from the thought of his 
 Uncle Egerton , - an uncle who have never even see he ! that , at least , 
 be more feasible . ' make my way in life , ' sayest thou , Audley Egerton ? 
 ay , - and to the fortune thou hast rob from my ancestor . Simulation ! 
 simulation ! Lord Bacon allow simulation . Lord Bacon practise it , 
 and - " 

 here the soliloquy come to a sudden end ; for as , rapt in his thought , 
 the boy have continue to walk backwards , he have come to the verge where 
 the lawn slide off into the ditch of the ha - ha ; and just as he be 
 fortify himself by the precept and practice of my Lord Bacon , the 
 ground go from under he , and - slap into the ditch go Randal Leslie ! 

 it so happen that the squire , whose active genius be always at some 
 repair or improvement , have be but a few day before widen and 
 slop off the ditch just in that part , so that the earth be fresh 
 and damp , and not yet either turf or flatten down . thus when randal , 
 recover his first surprise and shock , rise to his foot , he find his 
 clothe cover with mud ; while the rudeness of the fall be evince by 
 the fantastic and extraordinary appearance of his hat , which , hollow 
 here , bulge there , and crush out of all recognition generally , 
 be as little like the hat of a decorous , hard - read young 
 gentleman - protege of the dignified Mr. Audley Egerton - as any hat 
 pick out of a kennel after some drunken brawl possibly could be . 

 Randal be dizzy and stunned and bruise , and it be some moment before 
 he take heed of his raiment . when he do so his spleen be greatly 
 aggravate . he be still boy enough not to like the idea of present 
 himself to the unknown squire and the dandy Frank in such a trim : he 
 resolve incontinently to regain the lane and return home , without 
 accomplish the object of his journey ; and see the footpath right 
 before he , which lead to a gate that he conceive would admit he into 
 the highway soon than the path by which he have come , he take it at 
 once . 

 it be surprising how little we human creature hee the warning of our 
 good genius . I have no doubt that some benignant power have precipitate 
 randal Leslie into the ditch , as a significant hint of the fate of all 
 who choose what be , nowadays ; by no mean an uncommon step in the march 
 of intellect , - namely , the walking backwards , in order to gratify a 
 vindictive view of one 's neighbour 's property ! I suspect that , before 
 this century be out , many a fine fellow will thus have find his ha - ha , 
 and scramble out of the ditch with a much shabby coat than he have 
 on when he fall into it . but Randal do not thank his good genius for 
 give he a premonitory tumble , - and I never yet know a man who do ! 




 CHAPTER , XI . 

 the squire be greatly ruffle at breakfast that morning . he be too 
 much of an Englishman to bear insult patiently , and he consider that 
 he have be personally insult in the outrage offer to his recent 
 donation to the parish . his feeling , too , be hurt as well as his 
 pride . there be something so ungrateful in the whole thing , just 
 after he have take so much pain , not only in the resuscitation but the 
 embellishment of the stock . it be not , however , so rare an occurrence 
 for the squire to be ruffle as to create any remark . Riccabocca , 
 indeed , as a stranger , and Mrs. Hazeldean , as a wife , have the quick tact 
 to perceive that the host be glum and the husband snappish ; but the 
 one be too discreet , and the other too sensible , to chafe the new sore , 
 whatever it might be , and shortly after breakfast the squire retire 
 into his study , and absent himself from morning service . in his 
 delightful " life of Oliver Goldsmith , " Mr. Forster take care to 
 touch our heart by introduce his hero 's excuse for not enter 
 the priesthood . " he do not feel himself good enough . " Thy Vicar of 
 Wakefield , poor Goldsmith , be an excellent substitute for thee ; and 
 Dr. Primrose , at least , will be good enough for the world until Miss 
 Jemima 's fear be realize . now , Squire Hazeldean have a tenderness 
 of conscience much less reasonable than Goldsmith 's . there be 
 occasionally day in which he do not feel good enough - I do not   say for 
 a priest , but even for one of the congregation , - " day in which , " say 
 the squire in his own blunt way , " as I have never in my life meet a bad 
 devil than a devil of a temper , I will not carry mine into the family 
 pew . he sha'n't   be growl out hypocritical response from my poor 
 grandmother 's prayer - book . " so the squire and his demon stay at home . 
 but the demon be generally cast out before the day be over : and on 
 this occasion , when the bell ring for afternoon service , it may be 
 presume that the squire have reason or fret himself into a proper 
 state of mind ; for he be then see sallye forth from the porch of his 
 hall , arm - in - arm with his wife , and at the head of his household . the 
 second service be   as be commonly the case in rural district   more 
 numerously attend than the first one ; and it be our parson 's will not to 
 devote to this service his most effective discourse . 

 Parson Dale , though a very fair scholar , have neither the deep theology 
 nor the archaeological learning that distinguish the rise generation 
 of the clergy . I much doubt if he could have pass what would now be 
 call a creditable examination in the Fathers ; and as for all the nice 
 formality in the rubric , he would never have be the man to divide a 
 congregation or puzzle a bishop . neither be Parson Dale very erudite 
 in ecclesiastical architecture . he do not much care whether all the 
 detail in the church be purely Gothic or not ; crocket and finial , 
 round arch and pointed arch , be matter , I fear , on which he have never 
 trouble his head . 

 but one secret Parson Dale do possess , which be perhaps of equal 
 importance with those subtler mystery , - he know how to fill his 
 church ! even at morning service no pew be empty , and at evening 
 service the church overflow . 

 Parson Dale , too , may be consider nowadays to hold but a mean idea 
 of the spiritual authority of the Church . he have never be know 
 to dispute on its exact bearing with the State , - whether it be 
 incorporate with the State or above the State , whether it be 
 antecedent to the Papacy or form from the Papacy , etc . accord to 
 his favourite maxim , " Quieta non movere , " -   " not to disturb thing that 
 be quiet . "   - I have no doubt that he would have think that the less 
 discussion be provoke upon such matter the well for both Church and 
 laity . nor have he ever be know to regret the disuse of the ancient 
 custom of excommunication , nor any other diminution of the power of the 
 priesthood , whether minatory or militant ; yet for all this , Parson 
 Dale have a great notion of the sacred privilege of a minister of the 
 gospel , - to advise , to deter , to persuade , to reprove . and it be for 
 the evening service that he prepare those sermon which may be call 
 ' sermon that preach at you . " he prefer the evening for that salutary 
 discipline , not only because the congregation be more numerous , but 
 also because , be a shrewd man in his own innocent way , he know that 
 people bear well to be preach at after dinner than before ; that you 
 arrive more insinuatingly at the heart when the stomach be at peace . 
 there be a genial kindness in Parson Dale 's way of preach at you . 
 it be do in so imperceptible , fatherly , a manner that you never feel 
 offend . he do it , too , with so much art that nobody but your own 
 guilty self know that you be the sinner he be exhort . yet he do 
 not spare rich nor poor : he preach at the squire , and that great 
 fat farmer , Mr. Bullock , the churchwarden , as boldly as at Hodge the 
 ploughman and Scrub the hedger . as for Mr. Stirn , he have preach at he 
 more often than at any one in the parish ; but Stirn , though he have the 
 sense to know it , never have the grace to reform . there be , too , in 
 Parson Dale 's sermon something of that boldness of illustration which 
 would have be scholarly if he have not make it familiar , and which 
 be find in the discourse of our eld divine . like they , he do not 
 scruple now and then to introduce an anecdote from history , or borrow 
 an allusion from some non - scriptural author , in order to enliven the 
 attention of his audience , or render an argument more plain . and the 
 good man have an object in this , a little distinct from , though wholly 
 subordinate to , the main purpose of his discourse . he be a friend to 
 knowledge , - but to knowledge accompany by religion ; and sometimes 
 his reference to source not within the ordinary reading of his 
 congregation would spirit up some farmer 's son , with an evening 's 
 leisure on his hand , to ask the parson for further explanation , and so 
 to be lure on to a little solid or graceful instruction , under a safe 
 guide . 

 now , on the present occasion , the parson , who have always his eye and 
 heart on his flock , and who have see with great grief the realization of 
 his fear at the revival of the stock ; see that a spirit of discontent 
 be already at work amongst the peasant , and that magisterial and 
 inquisitorial design be darken the natural benevolence of the 
 squire , - see , in short , the sign of a breach between class , and the 
 precursor of the ever inflammable feud between the rich and the poor , 
 meditate nothing less than a great Political Sermon , - a sermon that 
 should extract from the root of social truth a heal virtue for 
 the wound that lie sore , but latent , in the breast of his parish of 
 Hazeldean . 

 and thus run - 

 the POLITICAL SERMON of parson DALE . 




 CHAPTER XII . 

  for every man shall bear his own burden . - Gal . vi . 5 . 

 " BRETHREN ! every man have his burden . if God design our life to end at 
 the grave , may we not believe that he would have free an existence so 
 brief from the care and sorrow to which , since the beginning of the 
 world , mankind have be subject ? suppose that I be a kind father , and 
 have a child whom I dearly love , but I know by a divine revelation 
 that he will die at the age of eight year , surely I should not vex his 
 infancy by needless preparation for the duty of life ? if I be a rich 
 man , I should not send he from the caress of his mother to the stern 
 discipline of school . if I be a poor man , I should not take he with I 
 to hedge and dig , to scorch in the sun , to freeze in the winter 's cold : 
 why inflict hardship on his childhood for the purpose of fit he 
 for manhood , when I know that he be doom not to grow into man ? but 
 if , on the other hand , I believe my child be reserve for a more durable 
 existence , then should I not , out of the very love I bear to he , 
 prepare his childhood for the struggle of life , accord to that 
 station in which he be bear , give many a toil , many a pain , to the 
 infant , in order to rear and strengthen he for his duty as man ? so it 
 be with our Father that be in heaven . view this life as our infancy 
 and the next as our spiritual maturity , where ' in the age to come he 
 may show the exceed rich of his grace , ' it be in his tenderness , 
 as in his wisdom ; to permit the toil and the pain which , in task 
 the power and develop the virtue of the soul , prepare it for ' the 
 earnest of our inheritance . ' hence it be that every man have his burden . 
 Brethren , if you believe that God be good , yea , but as tender as a human 
 father , you will know that your trouble in life be a proof that you 
 be rear for an eternity . but each man think his own burden the 
 hard to bear : the poor - man groan under his poverty , the rich man 
 under the care that multiply with wealth . for so far from wealth 
 free we from trouble , all the wise man who have write in all age 
 have repeat , with one voice , the word of the wise , ' when good 
 increase , they be increase that eat they : and what good be there to 
 the owner thereof , save the beholding of they with their eye ? ' and 
 this be literally true , my brother : for , let a man be as rich as be 
 the great King Solomon himself , unless he lock up all his gold in a 
 chest , it must go abroad to be divide amongst other ; yea , though , 
 like Solomon , he make he great work , - though he build house and plant 
 vineyard , and make he garden and orchard , - still the gold that he 
 spend feed but the mouth he employ ; and Solomon himself could not 
 eat with a well relish than the poor mason who build the house , 
 or the humblest labourer who plant the vineyard . therefore ' when good 
 increase , they be increase that eat they . ' and this , my brother , may 
 teach we toleration and compassion for the rich . we share their rich , 
 whether they will or not ; we do not share their care . the profane 
 history of our own country tell we that a princess , destine to be 
 the great queen that ever sit on this throne , envy the milk - maid 
 singing ; and a profane poet , whose wisdom be only less than that of the 
 inspire writer , represent the man who , by force - and wit , have rise 
 to be a king sigh for the sleep vouchsafe to the mean of his 
 subject , - all bear out the word of the son of David , ' the sleep 
 of the labour man be sweet , whether he eat little or much ; but the 
 abundance of the rich will not suffer he to sleep . ' 

 " amongst my brother now present there be , doubtless , some one who have 
 be poor , and by honest industry have make himself comparatively rich . 
 let his heart answer I while I speak : be not the chief care that now 
 disturb he to be find in the good he hath acquire ? have he not both 
 vexation to his spirit and trial to his virtue , which he know not when 
 he go forth to his labour , and take no heed of the morrow ? but it be 
 right , my brother , that to every station there should be its care , 
 to every man his burden ; for if the poor do not sometimes so far feel 
 poverty to be a burden as to desire to better their condition , and 
  to use the language of the world   ' seek to rise in life , ' their most 
 valuable energy would never be arouse ; and we should not witness 
 that spectacle , which be so common in the land we live in , - namely , the 
 successful struggle of manly labour against adverse fortune , - a struggle 
 in which the triumph of one give hope to thousand . it be say that 
 necessity be the mother of invention ; and the social blessing which be 
 now as common to we as air and sunshine have come from that law of our 
 nature which make we aspire towards indefinite improvement , enrich 
 each successive generation by the labour of the last , and in free 
 country often lift the child of the labourer to a place amongst 
 the ruler of the land . Nay , if necessity be the mother of invention , 
 poverty be the creator of the art . if there have be no poverty , and no 
 sense of poverty , where would have be that which we call the wealth of 
 a country ? subtract from civilization all that have be produce by 
 the poor , and what remain ? - the state of the savage . where you now see 
 labourer and prince , you would see equality indeed , - the equality of 
 wild man . no ; not even equality there ! for there brute force become 
 lordship , and woe to the weak ! where you now see some in frieze , some in 
 purple , you would see nakedness in all . where stand the palace and 
 the cot , you would behold but mud hut and cave . as far as the peasant 
 excel the king among savage , so far do the society exalt and 
 enrich by the struggle of labour excel the state in which poverty 
 feel no disparity , and Toil sigh for no ease . on the other hand , if 
 the rich be perfectly content with their wealth , their heart 
 would become harden in the sensual enjoyment it procure . it be that 
 feeling , by Divine Wisdom implant in the soul , that there be vanity 
 and vexation of spirit in the thing of Mammon , which still leave the 
 rich man sensitive to the instinct of Heaven , and teach he to seek 
 for happiness in those beneficent virtue which distribute his wealth to 
 the profit of other . if you could exclude the air from the ray of the 
 fire , the fire itself would soon languish and die in the midst of its 
 fuel ; and so a man 's joy in his wealth be keep alive by the air which it 
 warm ; and if pen within itself , be extinguish . 

 " and this , my brother , lead I to another view of the vast subject 
 open to we by the word of the apostle , ' every man shall bear his 
 own burden . ' the worldly condition of life be unequal . why be they 
 unequal ? o my brother , do you not perceive ? think you that , if it have 
 be well for our spiritual probation that there should be neither 
 great nor lowly , rich nor poor , Providence would not so have order 
 the dispensation of the world , and so , by its mysterious but merciful 
 agency , have influence the framework and foundation of society ? but 
 if from the remotest period of human annal , and in all the numberless 
 experiment of government which the wit of man have devise , still this 
 inequality be ever find to exist , may we not suspect that there be 
 something in the very principle of our nature to which that inequality 
 be necessary and essential ? ask why this inequality ? why ? - as well ask 
 why life be the sphere of duty and the nursery of virtue ! for if all 
 man be equal , if there be no suffering and no ease , no poverty and 
 no wealth , would you not sweep with one blow the half , at least , of 
 human virtue from the world ? if there be no penury and no pain , what 
 would become of fortitude ; what of patience ; what of resignation ? if 
 there be no greatness and no wealth , what would become of benevolence , 
 of charity , of the bless human pity , of temperance in the midst of 
 luxury , of justice in the exercise of power ? carry the question far ; 
 grant all condition the same , - no reverse , no rise , and no fall , 
 nothing to hope for , nothing to fear , - what a moral death you would at 
 once inflict upon all the energy of the soul , and what a link between 
 the Heart of Man and the Providence of God would be snap asunder ! 
 if we could annihilate evil , we should annihilate hope ; and hope , my 
 brother , be the avenue to faith . if there be ' a time to weep and a time 
 to laugh , ' it be that he who mourn may turn to eternity for comfort , 
 and he who rejoice may bless God for the happy hour . ah , my brother , 
 be it possible to annihilate the inequality of human life , it would 
 be the banishment of our worthy virtue , the torpor of our spiritual 
 nature , the palsy of our mental faculty . the moral world , like the 
 world without we , derive its health and its beauty from diversity and 
 contrast . 

 " ' every man shall bear his own burden . ' true ; but now turn to an early 
 verse in the same chapter , - ' Bear ye one another 's burden , and so 
 fulfil the law of Christ . ' yes , while Heaven ordain to each his 
 peculiar suffering , it connect the family of man into one household , by 
 that feel which , more perhaps than any other , distinguish we from 
 the brute creation , - I mean the feeling to which we give the name of 
 sympathy , - the feeling for each other ! the herd of deer shun the stag 
 that be mark by the gunner ; the flock heedeth not the sheep that 
 creep into the shade to die ; but man have sorrow and joy not in himself 
 alone , but in the joy and sorrow of those around he . he who feel only 
 for himself abjure his very nature as man ; for do we not say of one who 
 have no tenderness for mankind that he be inhuman ; and do we not call he 
 who sorrow with the sorrowful humane ? 

 " now , brother , that which especially mark the divine mission of our 
 Lord be the direct appeal to this sympathy which distinguish we from 
 the brute . he seize , not upon some faculty of genius give but to few , 
 but upon that ready impulse of heart which be give to we all ; and in 
 say , ' love one another , ' ' Bear ye one another 's burden , ' he elevate 
 the most delightful of our emotion into the most sacred of his law . 
 the lawyer ask our Lord , ' who be my neighbour ? ' our Lord reply by the 
 parable of the good Samaritan . the priest and the Levite see the wound 
 man that fall among the thief and pass by on the other side . that 
 priest might have be austere in his doctrine , that Levite might have 
 be learn in the law ; but neither to the learning of the Levite nor 
 to the doctrine of the priest do our saviour even deign to allude . he 
 cite but the action of the Samaritan , and saith to the lawyer , ' which 
 now of these three , think thou , be neighbour unto he that fall 
 among the thief ? and he say , he that show mercy unto he . then say 
 Jesus unto he , go , and do thou likewise . ' 

 " o shallowness of human judgment ! it be enough to be bear a Samaritan 
 in order to be reject by the priest , and despise by the Levite . yet 
 now , what to we the priest and the Levite , of God 's choose race though 
 they be ? they pass from the heart of man when they pass the 
 sufferer by the wayside ; while this loathe Samaritan , half thrust from 
 the pale of the Hebrew , become of our family , of our kindre ; a brother 
 amongst the brotherhood of Love , so long as Mercy and Affliction shall 
 meet in the common thoroughfare of life ! 

 " ' Bear ye one another 's burden and so fulfil the law of Christ . ' think 
 not , o my brother , that this apply only to almsgiving , to that relief 
 of distress which be commonly call charity , to the obvious duty of 
 devote from our superfluity something that we scarcely miss to the 
 want of a starve brother . no . I appeal to the poor amongst ye , 
 if the bad burden be those of the body , - if the kind word and the 
 tender thought have not often lighten your heart more than bread 
 bestow with a grudge , and charity that humble you by a frown . 
 Sympathy be a beneficence at the command of we all , - yea , of the 
 pauper as of the king ; and sympathy be Christ 's wealth . Sympathy be 
 brotherhood . the rich be tell to have charity for the poor , and the 
 poor be enjoin to respect their superior . good : I say not to the 
 contrary . but I say also to the poor , ' in your turn have charity for the 
 rich ; ' and I say to the rich , ' in your turn respect the poor . ' 

 " ' Bear ye one another 's burden , and so fulfil the law of Christ . ' 
 Thou , o poor man , envy not nor grudge thy brother his large portion 
 of worldly good . believe that he hath his sorrow and cross like 
 thyself , and perhaps , as more delicately nurture , he feel they more ; 
 nay , hath he not temptation so great that our Lord hath exclaim , ' how 
 hardly shall they that have rich enter into the kingdom of heaven ' ? 
 and what be temptation but trial ; what be trial but peril and 
 sorrow ? think not that you can bestow no charity on the rich man , even 
 while you take your sustenance from his hand . a heathen writer , 
 often cite by the early preacher of the gospel , hath truly say , 
 ' wherever there be room for a man there be place for a benefit . ' 

 " and I ask any rich brother amongst you , when he hath go forth 
 to survey his barn and his granary , his garden and orchard , if 
 suddenly in the vain pride of his heart , he see the scowl on the brow 
 of the labourer , - if he deem himself hat in the midst of his wealth , 
 if he feel that his least fault be treasure up against he with 
 the hardness of malice , and his plainest benefit receive with the 
 ingratitude of envy , - I ask , I say , any rich man , whether straightway 
 all pleasure in his worldly possession do not fade from his heart , 
 and whether he do not feel what a wealth of gladness it be in the 
 power of the poor man to bestow ! for all these thing of Mammon pass 
 away ; but there be in the smile of he whom we have serve a something 
 that we may take with we into heaven . if , then , ye bear one another 's 
 burden , they who be poor will have mercy on the error and compassion 
 for the grief of the rich . to all man it be say - yes , to Lazarus as 
 to Dives - ' Judge not , that ye be not judge . ' but think not , o rich man , 
 that we preach only to the poor . if it be their duty not to grudge thee 
 thy substance , it be thine to do all that may sweeten their labour . 
 remember that when our Lord say , ' how hardly shall they that have 
 rich enter into the kingdom of heaven , ' he reply also to they who 
 ask , ' who then can be save ? ' ' the thing which be impossible with 
 man be possible with God , ' that is , man leave to his own temptation 
 would fail ; but , strengthen by God , he shall be save . if thy rich 
 be the test of thy trial , so may they also be the instrument of thy 
 virtue . prove by thy rich that thou art compassionate and tender , 
 temperate and benign , and thy rich themselves may become the evidence 
 at once of thy faith and of thy work . 

 " we have constantly on our lip the simple precept , ' do unto other as 
 you would be do by . ' why do we fail so often in the practice ? because 
 we neglect to cultivate that sympathy which nature implant as an 
 instinct , and the Saviour exalt as a command . if thou wouldst do unto 
 thy neighbour as thou wouldst be do by , ponder well how thy neighbour 
 will regard the action thou art about to do to he . put thyself into his 
 place . if thou art strong and he be weak , descend from thy strength and 
 enter into his weakness ; lie aside thy burden for the while , and buckle 
 on his own ; let thy sight see as through his eye , thy heart beat as 
 in his bosom . do this , and thou wilt often confess that what have seem 
 just to thy power will seem harsh to his weakness . for ' as a zealous man 
 hath not do his duty when he call his brother drunkard and beast , ' 
 even so an administrator of the law mistake his object if he write 
 on the grand column of society only warning that irritate the bold and 
 terrify the timid ; and a man will be no more in love with law than with 
 virtue , ' if he be force to it with rudeness and incivility . ' if , 
 then , ye would bear the burden of the lowly , o ye great , feel not only 
 for they , but with ! watch that your pride do not chafe they , your 
 power do not wantonly gall . your worldly inferior be of the class 
 from which the Apostles be choose , amidst which the Lord of Creation 
 descend from a throne above the seraph . " 

 the parson here pause a moment , and his eye glance towards the pew 
 near the pulpit , where sit the magnate of Hazeldean . the squire be 
 lean his chin thoughtfully on his hand , his brow incline downward , 
 and the natural glow of his complexion much heighten . 

 " but , " resume the parson , softly , without turn to his book , and 
 rather as if prompt by the suggestion of the moment - " but he who have 
 cultivate sympathy commit not these error , or , if commit they , 
 hasten to retract . so natural be sympathy to the good man that he 
 obey it mechanically when he suffer his heart to be the monitor of his 
 conscience . in this sympathy , behold the bond between rich and poor ! by 
 this sympathy , whatever our vary worldly lot , they become what they 
 be mean to be , - exercise for the virtue more peculiar to each ; and 
 thus , if in the body each man bear his own burden , yet in the fellowship 
 of the soul all have common relief in bear the burden of each other . 
 this be the law of Christ , - fulfil it , o my flock ! " 

 here the parson close his sermon , and the congregation bow their 
 head . 




 BOOK THIRD . 




 INITIAL chapter . 

 show how MY novel come to be call " MY NOVEL . " 

 " I be not displeased with your novel , so far as it have go , " say my 
 father , graciously ; " though as for the Sermon - " here I tremble ; but 
 the lady , Heaven bless they ! have take Parson Dale under their special

 of caviller to discover the place at which the author of " Human Error " 
 direct his great gun . 

 " but , " say the captain , " you be a lad of too much spirit , Pisistratus ,

 pisistratu   magisterially , for he have be somewhat nettle by Mr.

 lengthen perspective of the variety of english life - " 

 MR . CAXTON . - " Hum ! " 

 blanche   put her hand on my father 's lip . - " we shall know well 
 the design , perhaps , when we know the title . pray , Mr. Author , what be 
 the title ? " 

 MY mother   with more animation than usual . - " ay , Sisty , the title ! " 

 PISISTRATUS   startle . - " the title ! by the soul of Cervantes ! I have 
 never yet think of a title ! " 

 CAPTAIN ROLAND   solemnly . - " there be a great deal in a good title . as a 
 novel reader , I know that by experience . " 

 MR . SQUILLS . - " certainly ; there be not a catchpenny in the world but 
 what go down , if the title be apt and seductive . Witness ' Old 
 Parr 's Life Pills . ' sell by the thousand , Sir , when my ' Pills for Weak 
 Stomachs , ' which I believe to be just the same compound , never pay for

 MR . CAXTON . - " Parr 's Life Pills ! a fine stroke of genius . it be not

 but who would not swallow a pill to live to a hundred and fifty - two ? " 

 PISISTRATUS   stir the fire in great excitement . - " my title ! my 
 title ! - what shall be my title ? " 

 MR . CAXTON   thrust his hand into his waistcoat , and in his most 
 didactic of tone . - " from a remote period , the choice of a title have 
 perplex the scribble portion of mankind . we may guess how their

 to begin with the Hebrews . ' the Lips of the sleeping '   Labia

 ' nocte ' with a list of the title in fashion in his day . for instance ,

 Meadow . ' some title , indeed , be more truculent , and promise food to 
 those who love to sup upon horror , - such as ' the torch , ' ' the Poniard , ' 
 ' the Stiletto ' - " 

 PISISTRATUS   impatiently . - " yes , sir , but to come to my novel . " 

 MR . CAXTON   unheede the interruption . - " you see you have a fine 
 choice here , and of a nature pleasing , and not unfamiliar , to a 
 classical reader ; or you may borrow a hint from the early dramatic 
 writer . " 

 pisistratu   more hopefully . - " ay , there be something in the Drama akin 
 to the Novel . now , perhaps , I may catch an idea . " 

 MR . CAXTON . - " for instance , the author of the ' curiosity of 
 Literature '   from whom , by the way , I be plagiarize much of the

 write a Comedy , by which he intend to serve what he take for Moral 
 Philosophy . " 

 PISISTRATUS   eagerly . - " well , sir ? " 

 MR . CAXTON . - " and call it ' the Pain of the Sleep of the World . ' "

 MR . CAXTON . - " grave thing be then call Comedies , as old thing be 
 now call novel . then there be all the title of early Romance itself 
 at your disposal , - ' Theagenes and Chariclea ' or ' the Ass ' of Longus , or 
 ' the Golden Ass ' of Apuleius , or the title of Gothic Romance , such as 
 ' the most elegant , delicious , mellifluous , and delightful history of 
 Perceforest , King of Great Britain . ' " and therewith my father run over a

 girl   for I have not read many since , I be ashamed to say   - "

 Austin . "

 my uncle hesitate , when Mr. Caxton exclaim imperiously . - " the thing be
