 CHAPTER II . 

 it be greatly to be regret that Mr. Stirn be not present at the 
 parson 's discourse ; but that valuable functionary be far otherwise 
 engage , - indeed , during the summer month he be rarely see at the 
 afternoon service . not that he care for be preach at , - not he ; Mr. 
 Stirn would have snap his finger at the thunder of the Vatican . 
 but the fact be , that Mr. Stirn choose to do a great deal of gratuitous 
 business upon the day of rest . the squire allow all person who choose 
 to walk about the park on a Sunday ; and many come from a distance to 
 stroll by the lake , or recline under the elm . these visitor be 
 object of great suspicion , nay , of positive annoyance , to Mr. 
 Stirn - and , indeed , not altogether without reason , for we English have 
 a natural love of liberty , which we be even more apt to display in 
 the ground of other people than in those which we cultivate ourselves . 
 sometimes , to his inexpressible and fierce satisfaction , Mr. Stirn 
 fall upon a knot of boy pelt the swan ; sometimes he miss a 
 young sapling , and find it in felonious hand , convert into a 
 walking - stick ; sometimes he catch a hulking fellow scramble up the 
 ha - ha to gather a nosegay for his sweetheart from one of poor Mrs. 
 Hazeldean 's pet parterre ; not infrequently , indeed , when all the family 
 be fairly at church , some curious impertinent force or sneak their 
 way into the garden , in order to peep in at the window . for these , 
 and various other offence of like magnitude , Mr. Stirn have long , 
 but vainly , seek to induce the squire to withdraw a permission so 
 villanously abuse . but though there be time when Mr. Hazeldean 
 grunt and growl , and swear " that he would shut up the park , and 
 fill it   illegally   with mantrap and spring - gun , " his anger always 
 evaporate in word . the park be still open to all the world on a 
 Sunday ; and that blessed day be therefore convert into a day of 
 travail and wrath to Mr. Stirn . but it be from the last chime of the 
 afternoon - service bell until dusk that the spirit of this vigilant 
 functionary be most perturb ; for , amidst the flock that gather 
 from the little hamlet round to the voice of the pastor , there be 
 always some stray sheep , or rather climb , desultory , vagabond goat , 
 who strike off in all perverse direction , as if for the special purpose 
 of distract the energetic watchfulness of Mr. Stirn . as soon as 
 church be over , if the day be fine , the whole park become a scene 
 animate with red cloak or lively shawl , Sunday waistcoat and hat 
 stick full of wildflower - which last Mr. Stirn often stoutly 
 maintain to be Mrs. Hazeldean 's new geranium . now , on this Sunday , 
 especially , there be an imperative call upon an extra exertion of 
 vigilance on the part of the superintendent , - he have not only to detect 
 ordinary depredator and trespasser ; but , first , to discover the 
 author of the conspiracy against the stock ; and , secondly , to " make an 
 example . " 

 he have begin his round , therefore , from the early morning ; and just 
 as the afternoon bell be sound its final peal , he emerge upon the 
 village green from a hedgerow , behind which he have be at watch to 
 observe who have the most suspiciously gather round the stock . at that 
 moment the place be desert . at a distance , the superintendent see 
 the fast disappear form of some belate group hasten towards the 
 church ; in front , the stock stand star at he mournfully from its 
 four great eye , which have be cleanse from the mud , but still look 
 bleared and stain with the inark of the recent outrage . here Mr. 
 Stirn pause , take off his hat , and wipe his brow . 

 " if I have sum ' un to watch here , " think he , " while I take a turn by 
 the water - side , p'r'aps summat might come out ; p'r'aps they as do it 
 ben't   go to church , but will come sneak round to look on their 
 willany ! as they say murderer be always lead back to the place where 
 they ha ' leave the body . but in this here willage there ben't   a man , 
 woman , or child as have any consarn for squire or parish , bar 
 myself . " it be just as he arrive at that misanthropical conclusion 
 that Mr. Stirn behold Leonard Fairfield walk very fast from his own 
 home . the superintendent clap on his hat , and stick his right arm 
 akimbo . " hollo , you , sir , " say he , as Lenny now come in hearing , " where 
 be you go at that rate ? " 

 " please , sir , I be go to church . " 

 " stop , sir , - stop , Master Lenny . go to church ! - why , the bell 's 
 do ; and you know the parson be very angry at they as come in late , 
 disturb the congregation . you can not   go to church now ! " 

 " please , sir - " 

 " I say you can not   go to church now . you must learn to think a little 
 of other , lad . you see how I sweat to serve the squire ! and you must 
 serve he too . why , your mother 's get the house and premishe almost 
 rent - free ; you ought to have a grateful heart , Leonard Fairfield , and 
 feel for his honour ! poor man ! his heart be well - nigh bruk , I be sure , 
 with the going on . " 

 Leonard open his innocent blue eye , while Mr. Stirn dolorously wipe 
 his own . 

 " look at that ' ere dumb cretur , " say Stirn , suddenly , point to the 
 stock , - " look at it . if it could speak , what would it say , Leonard 
 Fairfield ? answer I that ! ' damn the stock , ' indeed ! " 

 " it be very bad in they to write such naughty word , " say Lenny , 
 gravely . " Mother be quite shocked when she hear of it this morning . " 

 MR . STIRN . - " I dare say she be , consider what she pay for the 
 premishe ; "   insinuatingly   " you do not know who do it , - eh , Lenny ? " 

 LENNY . - " no , sir ; indeed I do not ! " 

 MR . STIRN . - " well , you see , you can not   go to church , - prayer half 
 over by this time . you recollex that I put they stock under your 
 ' sponsibility , ' and see the way you be do your duty by them ! I have   half a 
 mind to - " 

 Mr. Stirn cast his eye on the eye of the stock . " please , sir , " begin 
 Lenny again , rather frightened . 

 " no , I will not   please ; it ben't   pleasing at all . but I forgive you this 
 time , only keep a sharp lookout , lad , in future . now you must stay 
 here - no , there - under the hedge , and you watch if any person come 
 to loiter about , or look at the stock , or laugh to hisself , while I 
 go my round . I shall be back either afore church be over or just arter ; 
 so you stay till I come , and give I your report . be sharp , boy , or it 
 will be bad for you and your mother ; I can let the premishe for L4 a 
 year more to - morrow . " 

 conclude with that somewhat menacing and very significant remark , and 
 not stay for an answer , Mr. Stirn wave his hand and walk off . 

 Poor Lenny remain by the stock , very much deject , and greatly 
 dislike the neighbourhood to which the be consign . at length he 
 slowly creep off to the hedge , and sit himself down in the place of 
 espionage point out to he . now , philosopher tell we that what be 
 call the point of honour be a barbarous feudal prejudice . amongst 
 the high class , wherein those feudal prejudice may be suppose to 
 prevail , Lenny Fairfield 's occupation would not have be consider 
 peculiarly honourable ; neither would it have seem so to the more 
 turbulent spirit among the humbler order , who have a point of honour 
 of their own , which consist in the adherence to each other in defiance 
 of all lawful authority . but to Lenny Fairfield , bring up much apart 
 from other boy , and with a profound and grateful reverence for the 
 squire instill into all his habit of thought , notion of honour 
 bound themselves to simple honesty and straightforward truth ; and as 
 he cherish an unquestione awe of order and constitutional authority , 
 so it do not appear to he that there be anything derogatory and 
 debasing in be thus set to watch for an offender . on the contrary , as 
 he begin to reconcile himself to the loss of the church service , and 
 to enjoy the cool of the summer shade and the occasional chirp of the 
 bird , he get to look on the bright side of the commission to which he 
 be depute . in youth , at least , everything have its bright side , - even 
 the appointment of Protector to the Parish stock . for the stock itself 
 Leonard have no affection , it be true ; but he have no sympathy with its 
 aggressor , and he could well conceive that the squire would be very 
 much hurt at the revolutionary event of the night . " so , " think poor 
 Leonard in his simple heart , - ' so , if I can serve his honour , by keep 
 off mischievous boy , or let he know who do the thing , I be sure 
 it would be a proud day for Mother . " then he begin to consider that , 
 however ungraciously Mr. Stirn have bestow on he the appointment , 
 still it be a compliment to he , - show trust and confidence in he , 
 pick he out from his contemporary as the sober , moral , pattern boy ; 
 and Lenny have a great deal of pride in he , especially in matter of 
 repute and character . 

 all these thing consider , I say , Leonard Fairfield recline on his 
 lurking - place , if not with positive delight and intoxicate rapture , at 
 least with tolerable content and some complacency . 

 Mr. Stirn might have be go a quarter of an hour , when a boy come 
 through a little gate in the park , just opposite to Lenny 's retreat in 
 the hedge , and , as if fatigue with walking , or oppress by the heat of 
 the day , pause on the green for a moment or so , and then advance under 
 the shade of the great tree which overhang the stock . 

 Lenny prick up his ear , and peep out jealously . 

 he have never see the boy before : it be a strange face to he . 

 Leonard Fairfield be not fond of stranger ; moreover , he have a vague 
 belief that stranger be at the bottom of that desecration of the 
 stock . the boy , then , be a strange ; but what be his rank ? be he 
 of that grade in society in which the natural offence be or be not 
 consonant to , or harmonious with , outrage upon stock ? on that Lenny 
 Fairfield do not feel quite assured . accord to all the experience of 
 the villager , the boy be not dress like a young gentleman . Leonard 's 
 notion of such aristocratic costume be naturally fashion upon the 
 model of Frank Hazeldean . they represent to he a dazzle vision of 
 snow - white trouser and beautiful blue coat and incomparable cravat . 
 now the dress of this stranger , though not that of a peasant or of a 
 farmer , do not in any way correspond with Lenny 's notion of the costume 
 of a young gentleman . it look to he highly disreputable : the coat 
 be cover with mud , and the hat be all manner of shape , with a gap 
 between the side and crown . 

 Lenny be puzzle , till it suddenly occur to he that the gate 
 through which the boy have pass be in the direct path across the park 
 from a small town , the inhabitant of which be in very bad odour at 
 the Hall , - they have immemorially furnish the most daring poacher to 
 the preserve , the most troublesome trespasser on the park , the most 
 unprincipled orchard robber , and the most disputatious asserter of 
 various problematical right of way , which , accord to the Town , be 
 public , and , accord to the Hall , have be private since the Conquest . 
 it be true that the same path lead also directly from the squire 's 
 house , but it be not probable that the wearer of attire so equivocal 
 have be visit there . all thing consider , Lenny have no doubt in 
 his mind but that the stranger be a shop - boy or ' prentice from the town 
 of Thorndyke ; and the notorious repute of that town , couple with this 
 presumption , make it probable that Lenny now see before he one of the 
 midnight desecrator of the stock . as if to confirm the 
 suspicion , which pass through Lenny 's mind with a rapidity wholly 
 disproportionate to the number of line it cost I to convey it , the 
 boy , now stand right before the stock , bend down and read that pithy 
 anathema with which it be deface . and have read it , he repeat it 
 aloud , and Lenny actually see he smile , - such a smile ! so disagreeable 
 and sinister ! Lenny have never before see the smile sardonic . 

 but what be Lenny 's pious horror and dismay when this ominous stranger 
 fairly seat himself on the stock , rest his heel profanely on 
 the lid of two of the four round eye , and take out a pencil and a 
 pocket - book , begin to write . 

 be this audacious Unknown take an inventory of the church and the 
 Hall for the purpose of conflagration ? he look at one and at the 
 other , with a strange fix stare as he write , - not keep his eye 
 on the paper , as Lenny have be teach to do when he sit down to his 
 copy - book . the fact be , that Randal Leslie be tired and faint , and he 
 feel the shock of his fall the more , after the few pace he have walk , 
 so that he be glad to rest himself a few moment ; and he take that 
 opportunity to write a line to Frank , to excuse himself for not 
 call again , intend to tear the leaf on which he write out of 
 his pocket - book and leave it at the first cottage he pass , with 
 instruction to take it to the Hall . 

 while Randal be thus innocently engage , Lenny come up to he , with the 
 firm and measure pace of one who have resolve , cost what it may , to do 
 his duty . and as Lenny , though brave , be not ferocious , so the anger he 
 feel and the suspicion he entertain only exhibit themselves in the 
 follow solemn appeal to the offender 's sense of propriety , - " Ben't 
 you ashamed of yourself ? sit on the squire 's new stock ! do get up , 
 and go along with you ! " 

 Randal turn round sharply ; and though , at any other moment , he would 
 have have sense enough to extricate himself very easily from his false 
 position , yet Nemo mortalium , etc . no one be always wise . and Randal be 
 in an exceedingly bad humour . the affability towards his inferior , 
 for which I lately praise he , be entirely lose in the contempt for 
 impertinent snob natural to an insulted Etonian . 

 therefore , eye Lenny with great disdain , Randal answer briefly , - 

 " you be an insolent young blackguard . " 

 so curt a rejoinder make Lenny 's blood fly to his face . persuade before 
 that the intruder be some lawless apprentice or shop - lad , he be now 
 more confirm in that judgment , not only by language so uncivil , but by 
 the truculent glance which accompany it , and which certainly do 
 not derive any imposing dignity from the mutilated , rakish , hang - dog , 
 ruinous hat , under which it shoot its sullen and menacing fire . 

 of all the various article of which our male attire be compose , there 
 be perhaps not one which have so much character and expression as the top 
 covering . a neat , well - brush , short - nap , gentlemanlike hat , put on 
 with a certain air , give a distinction and respectability to the whole 
 exterior ; whereas , a break , squashed , higgledy - piggledy sort of a hat , 
 such as Randal Leslie have on , would go far towards transform the 
 stateliest gentleman who ever walk down St. James 's Street into the 
 ideal of a ruffianly scamp . 

 now , it be well know that there be nothing more antipathetic to your 
 peasant - boy than a shop - boy . even on grand political occasion , the 
 rural working - class can rarely be coax into sympathy with the trading 
 town class . your true english peasant be always an aristocrat . moreover , 
 and irrespectively of this immemorial grudge of class , there be 
 something peculiarly hostile in the relationship between boy and boy 
 when their back be once up , and they be alone on a quiet bit of 
 green , - something of the game - cock feeling ; something that tend to 
 keep alive , in the population of this island   otherwise so lamblike and 
 peaceful , the martial propensity to double the thumb tightly over the 
 four finger , and make what be call " a fist of it . " dangerous symptom 
 of these mingled and aggressive sentiment be visible in Lenny 
 Fairfield at the word and the look of the unprepossesse stranger . and 
 the stranger seem aware of they ; for his pale face grow more pale , and 
 his sullen eye more fixed and more vigilant . 

 " you get off they stock , " say Lenny , disdain to reply to the coarse 
 expression bestow on he ; and , suit the action to the word , he 
 give the intruder what he mean for a shove , but what randal take for 
 a blow . the Etonian spring up , and the quickness of his movement , aid 
 but by a slight touch of his hand , make Lenny lose his balance , and send 
 he neck - and - crop over the stock . burn with rage , the young villager 
 rise alertly , and , fly at Randal , strike out right and left . 




 CHAPTER III . 

 aid I , o ye Nine ! whom the incomparable Persius satirize his 
 contemporary for invoke , and then , all of a sudden , invoke on his 
 own behalf , - aid I to describe that famous battle by the stock , and 
 in defence of the stock , which be wag by the two representative 
 of Saxon and Norman England . here , sober support of law and duty 
 and delegate trust , - pro aris et focis ; there , haughty invasion and 
 bellicose spirit of knighthood and that respect for name and person 
 which we call " honour . " here , too , hardy physical force , - there , skilful 
 discipline . here - the Nine be as deaf as a post , and as cold as a 
 stone ! plague take the jade ! I can do well without they . 

 Randal be a year or two old than Lenny , but he be not so tall nor so 
 strong , nor even so active ; and after the first blind rush , when the two 
 boy pause , and draw back to breathe , Lenny , eye the slight form and 
 hueless cheek of his opponent , and see blood trickle from Randal 's 
 lip , be seize with an instantaneous and generous remorse . " it be 
 not fair , " he think , " to fight one whom he could beat so easily . " so , 
 retreat still far , and let his arm fall to his side , he say 
 mildly , " there , let us have no more of it ; but go home and be good . " 

 Randal Leslie have no remarkable degree of that constitutional quality 
 call physical courage ; but he have some of those moral quality 
 which supply its place . he be proud , he be vindictive , he have high 
 self - esteem , he have the destructive organ more than the combative , - what 
 have once provoke his wrath it become his instinct to sweep away . 
 therefore , though all his nerve be quiver , and hot tear be in 
 his eye , he approach Lenny with the sternness of a gladiator , and 
 say between his tooth , which he set hard , choke back the sob of rage 
 and pain , - 

 " you have strike I - and you shall not stir from this ground till I have 
 make you repent it . put up your hand , - defend yourself . " 

 Lenny mechanically obey ; and he have good need of the admonition ; for 
 if before he have have the advantage , now that Randal have recover the 
 surprise to his nerve , the battle be not to the strong . 

 though Leslie have not be a fighting boy at Eton , still his temper have 
 involve he in some conflict when he be in the low form , and 
 he have learn something of the art as well as the practice in 
 pugilism , - an excellent thing too , I be barbarous enough to believe , and 
 which I hope will never quite die out of our public school . ah , many a 
 young duke have be a well fellow for life from a fair set - to with a 
 trader 's son ; and many a trader 's son have learn to look a lord more 
 manfully in the face on the husting , from the recollection of the sound 
 thrashing he once give to some little Lord Leopold Dawdle . 

 so randal now bring his experience and art to bear ; put aside 
 those heavy roundabout blow , and dart in his own , quick and sharp , 
 supply to the natural feebleness of his arm the due momentum of 
 pugilistic mechanic . ay , and the arm , too , be no long so feeble ; for 
 strange be the strength that come from passion and pluck ! 

 Poor Lenny , who have never fight before , be bewilder ; his sensation 
 grow so entangled that he could never recall they distinctly ; he have a 
 dim reminiscence of some breathless impotent rush , of a sudden blindness 
 follow by quick flash of intolerable light , of a deadly faintness , 
 from which he be rouse by sharp pang - here - there - everywhere ; and 
 then all he could remember be , that he be lie on the ground , 
 huddle up and pant hard , while his adversary bent over he with a 
 countenance as dark and livid as Lara himself might have bend over the 
 fall Otho . for Randal Leslie be not one who , by impulse and nature , 
 subscribe to the noble English maxim , " never hit a foe when he be 
 down ; " and it cost he a strong , if brief , self - struggle not to set 
 his heel on that prostrate form . it be the mind , not the heart , 
 that subdue the savage within he , as mutter something 
 inwardly - certainly not christian forgiveness - the victor turn 
 gloomily away . 




 CHAPTER IV . 

 just at that precise moment , who should appear but Mr. Stirn ! for , in 
 fact , be extremely anxious to get Lenny into disgrace , he have hop 
 that he should have find the young villager have shirk the commission 
 intrust to he ; and the right - hand man have slily come back to see if 
 that amiable expectation be realize . he now behold Lenny rise with 
 some difficulty , still pant hard , and with hysterical sound akin 
 to what be vulgarly call blubber , his fine new waistcoat sprinkle 
 with his own blood , which flow from his nose , - nose that seem 
 to Lenny Fairfield 's feeling to be a nose no more , but a swollen , 
 gigantic , mountainous slawkenbergian excrescence ; in fact , he feel all 
 nose ! turn aghast from this spectacle , Mr. Stirn survey , with no 
 more respect than Lenny have manifest , the strange boy , who have again 
 seat himself on the stock   whether to recover his breath , or whether 
 to show that his victory be consummate , and that he be in his right 
 of possession . " hollo , " say Mr. Stirn , " what be all this ? what be the 
 matter , Lenny , you blockhead ? " 

 " he will sit there , " answer Lenny , in broken gasp , " and he have beat 
 I because I would not let he ; but I do not   mind that , " add the 
 villager , try hard to suppress his tear , " and I be ready again for 
 he - that I be . " 

 " and what do you do lollopope there on they bless stock ? " 

 " look at the landscape ; out of my light , man ! " 

 this tone instantly inspire Mr. Stirn with misgiving : it be a tone 
 so disrespectful to he that he be seize with involuntary respect ; who 
 but a gentleman could speak so to Mr. Stirn ? 

 " and may I ask who you be ? " say Stirn , falteringly , and half incline 
 to touch his hat . " what be your name , pray ? what be your bizness ? " 

 " my name be Randal Leslie , and my business be to visit your master 's 
 family , - that be , if you be , as I guess from your manner , Mr. 
 Hazeldean 's ploughman ! " 

 so say , randal rise ; and move on a few pace , turn , and throw 
 half - a - crown on the road , say to Lenny , " let that pay you for your 
 bruise , and remember another time how you speak to a gentleman . as for 
 you , fellow , " - and he point his scornful hand towards Mr. Stirn , who , 
 with his mouth open , and his hat now fairly off , stand bow to the 
 earth , - " as for you , give my compliment to Mr. Hazeldean , and say that 
 when he do we the honour to visit we at Rood Hall , I trust that the 
 manner of our villager will make he ashamed of Hazeldean . " 

 oh , my poor squire ! Rood Hall ashame of Hazeldean ! if that message have 
 be deliver to you , you would never have look up again ! 

 with those bitter word , randal swing himself over the stile that lead 
 into the parson 's glebe , and leave Lenny Fairfield still feel his 
 nose , and Mr. Stirn still bow to the earth . 




 CHAPTER V. 

 Randal Leslie have a very long walk home ; he be bruise and sore from 
 head to foot , and his mind be still more sore and more bruised than his 
 body . but if Randal Leslie have rest himself in the squire 's garden , 
 without walk backwards and indulge in speculation suggest by 
 Marat , and warrant by my Lord Bacon , he would have pass a most 
 agreeable evening , and really avail himself of the squire 's wealth 
 by go home in the squire 's carriage . but because he choose to take 
 so intellectual a view of property , he tumble into a ditch ; because 
 he tumble into a ditch , he spoil his clothe ; because he spoil his 
 clothe , he give up his visit ; because he give up his visit , he get into 
 the village green , and sit on the stock with a hat that give he the 
 air of a fugitive from the treadmill ; because he sit on the stock - with 
 that hat , and a cross face under it - he have be force into the most 
 discreditable squabble with a clodhopper , and be now limp home , 
 at war with god and man ; ergo   this be a moral that will bear 
 repetition , - ergo , when you walk in a rich man 's ground , be content 
 to enjoy what be your , namely , the prospect , - I dare say you will enjoy 
 it more than he do ! 




 CHAPTER VI . 

 if , in the simplicity of his heart and the crudity of his experience , 
 Lenny Fairfield have conceive it probable that Mr. Stirn would address 
 to he some word in approbation of his gallantry and in sympathy for 
 his bruise , he soon find himself wofully mistaken . that truly 
 great man , worthy prime minister of Hazeldean , might perhaps pardon a 
 dereliction from his order , if such dereliction prove advantageous to 
 the interest of the service , or redound to the credit of the 
 chief ; but he be inexorable to that bad of diplomatic offence , - an 
 ill - time , stupid , over - zealous obedience to order , which , if it 
 establish the devotion of the employee , get the employer into what 
 be popularly call a scrape ! and though , by those unverse in the 
 intricacy of the human heart , and unacquainte with the especial 
 heart of prime minister and right - hand man , it might have seem 
 natural that Mr. Stirn , as he stand still , hat in hand , in the middle 
 of the road , stung , humble , and exasperate by the mortification he have 
 receive from the lip of Randal Leslie , would have feel that that young 
 gentleman be the proper object of his resentment , yet such a breach of 
 all the etiquette of diplomatic life as resentment towards a superior 
 power be the last idea that would have suggest itself to the profound 
 intellect of the premier of Hazeldean . still , as rage , like steam , must 
 escape somewhere , Mr. Stirn , on feeling - as he afterwards express it 
 to his wife - that his " buzzom be a burstin ' , " turn with the natural 
 instinct of self - preservation to the safety - valve provide for the 
 explosion ; and the vapour within he rush into vent upon Lenny 
 Fairfield . he clap his hat on his head fiercely , and thus relieve 
 his " buzzom . " 

 " you young willain ! you howdaeious wiper ! and so all this bless 
 Sabbath afternoon , when you ought to have be in church on your 
 marrow - bone , a praying for your better , you have be a fitting with a 
 young gentleman , and a wisiter to your master , on the wery place of the 
 parridge hinstitution that you be to guard and pertect ; and a bloodying 
 it all over , I declare , with your blaggard little nose ! " thus say , 
 and as if to mend the matter , Mr. Stirn aim an additional stroke at 
 the offend member ; but Lenny mechanically put up both arm to 
 defend his face , Mr. Stirn strike his knuckle against the large brass 
 button that adorn the cuff of the boy 's coat - sleeve , - an incident 
 which considerably aggravate his indignation . and Lenny , whose spirit 
 be fairly rouse at what the narrowness of his education conceive to 
 be a signal injustice , place the trunk of the tree between Mr. Stirn 
 and himself , begin that task of self - justification which it be equally 
 impolitic to conceive and imprudent to execute , since , in such a case , 
 to justify be to recriminate . 

 " I wonder at you , Master Stirn , - if Mother could hear you ! you know it 
 be you who would not let I go to church ; it be you who tell I to - " 

 " fit a young gentleman , and break the Sabbath , " say Mr. Stirn , 
 interrupt he with a wither sneer . " oh , yes ! I tell you to 
 disgrace his honour the squire , and I , and the parridge , and bring 
 we all into trouble . but the squire tell I to make an example , and I 
 will ! " with those word , quick as lightning flash upon Mr. Stirn 's 
 mind the luminous idea of set Lenny in the very stock which he have 
 too faithfully guard . Eureka ! the " example " be before he ! here he 
 could gratify his long grudge against the pattern boy ; here , by such 
 a selection of the very good lad in the parish , he could strike terror 
 into the bad ; here he could appease the offended dignity of randal 
 Leslie ; here be a practical apology to the squire for the affront put 
 upon his young visitor ; here , too , there be prompt obedience to the 
 squire 's own wish that the stock should be provide as soon as possible 
 with a tenant . suit the action to the thought , Mr. Stirn make a rapid 
 plunge at his victim , catch he by the skirt of his jacket ; and in a 
 few second more , the jaw of the stock have open , and Lenny Fairfield 
 be thrust therein , - a sad spectacle of the revers of fortune . 
 this do , and while the boy be too astounded , too stupefy , by the 
 suddenness of the calamity , for the resistance he might otherwise have 
 make , - nay , for more than a few inaudible word , - Mr. Stirn hurry from 
 the spot , but not without first pick up and pocket the half - crown 
 design for Lenny , and which , so great have be his first emotion , 
 he have hitherto even almost forgotten . he then make his way towards the 
 church , with the intention to place himself close by the door , catch 
 the squire as he come out , whisper to he what have pass , and lead he , 
 with the whole congregation at his heel , to gaze upon the sacrifice 
 offer up to the joint power of Nemesis and Themis . 




 CHAPTER VII . 

 unaffectedly I say it - upon the honour of a gentleman , and the 
 reputation of an author , - unaffectedly I say it , no word of mine can 
 do justice to the sensation experience by Lenny Fairfield , as he sit 
 alone in that place of penance . he feel no more the physical pain of 
 his bruise ; the anguish of his mind stifle and overbore all corporeal 
 suffering , - an anguish as great as the childish breast be capable of 
 hold . 

 for first and deepest of all , and early feel , be the burn sense 
 of injustice . he have , it might be with err judgment , but with all 
 honesty , earnestness , and zeal , execute the commission entrust to 
 he ; he have stand forth manfully in discharge of his duty ; he have 
 fight for it , suffer for it , bleed for it . this be his reward ! now 
 in Lenny 's mind there be pre - eminently that quality which distinguish 
 the Anglo Saxon race , - the sense of justice . it be perhaps the 
 strong principle in his moral constitution ; and the principle have 
 never lose its virgin bloom and freshness by any of the minor act of 
 oppression and iniquity which boy of high birth often suffer from 
 harsh parent , or in tyrannical school . so that it be for the 
 first time that that iron enter into his soul , and with it come its 
 attendant feeling , - the wrathful , gall sense of impotence . he have 
 be wrong , and he have no means to right himself . then come another 
 sensation , if not so deep , yet more smarting and envenom for the 
 time , - shame ! he , the good boy of all good boy ; he , the pattern of the 
 school , and the pride of the parson ; he , whom the squire , in sight of 
 all his contemporary , have often single out to slap on the back , and 
 the grand squire 's lady to pat on the head , with a smile gratulation 
 on his young and fair repute ; he , who have already learn so dearly to 
 prize the sweet of an honourable name , - he to be make , as it be , in 
 the twinkling of an eye , a mark for opprobrium , a butt of scorn , a jeer , 
 and a byword ! the stream of his life be poison at the fountain . and 
 then come a tenderer thought of his mother ! of the shock this would be 
 to she , - she who have already begin to look up to he as her stay and 
 support ; he bow his head , and the tear , long suppress , roll down . 

 then he wrestle and struggle , and strove to wrench his limb from 
 that hateful bondage , - for he hear step approach . and he begin to 
 picture to himself the arrival of all the villager from church , the 
 sad gaze of the parson , the bent brow of the squire , the idle , 
 ill - suppress titter of all the boy , jealous of his unspotted 
 character , - character of which the original whiteness could never , never 
 be restore ! 

 he would always be the boy who have sit in the stock ! and the word 
 utter by the squire come back on his soul , like the voice of 
 conscience in the ear of some doom Macbeth : " a sad disgrace , 
 Lenny , - you will never be in such a quandary . " " Quandary " - the word be 
 unfamiliar to he ; it must mean something awfully discreditable . the 
 poor boy could have pray for the earth to swallow he . 




 CHAPTER VIII . 

 " Kettles and frying - pan ! what have we here ? " cry the tinker . 

 this time Mr. Sprott be without his donkey ; for it be Sunday , it 
 be presume that the donkey be enjoy his Sabbath on the common . 
 the tinker be in his Sunday 's good , clean and smart , about to take his 
 lounge in the park . 

 Lenny Fairfield make no answer to the appeal . 

 " you in the wood , my baby ! well , that be the last sight I should 
 ha ' thought to see . but we all live to larn , " add the tinker , 
 sententiously . " who give you they leggin ? can not   you speak , lad ? " 

 " Nick Stirn . " 

 " Nick Stirn ! ay , I would ha ' ta'en my davy on that : and cos vy ? " 

 " 'cause I do as he tell I , and fight a boy as be trespass on 
 these very stock ; and he beat I - but I do not   care for that ; and that 
 boy be a young gentleman , and go to visit the squire ; and so Nick 
 Stirn - " Lenny stop short , choke by rage and humiliation . 

 " Augh , " say the tinker , start , " you fit with a young gentleman , do

 you ha ' get off so cheap . ' t be salt and battery to fit with your 
 better , and a Lunnon justice o ' peace would have give you two month 
 o ' the treadmill . 

 " but vy should you fit cos he trespass on the stock ? it ben't   your 
 natural side for fitting , I take it . " 

 Lenny murmur something not very distinguishable about serve the 
 squire , and do as he be bid . 

 " oh , I see , Lenny , " interrupt the tinker , in a tone of great 
 contempt , " you be one of those who would rayther ' unt with the ' ound 
 than run with the ' be ! you be 's the good pattern boy , and would peach 
 agin your own border to curry favour with the grand folk . Fie , lad ! you 
 be sarve right ; stick by your border , then you will be ' specte when you 
 get into trouble , and not be ' varsally ' spise , - as you will be arter 
 church - time ! Vell , I can not   be see ' sort with you , now you be in 
 this d'rogotary fix ; it might hurt my c'r'acter , both with they as build 
 the stock and they as want to pull them down . old kettle to mend ! Vy ,

 out of it ; ' speck to your mother , and say we can deal for the pan and

 the tinker go his way . Lenny 's eye follow he with the sullenness 
 of despair . the tinker , like all the tribe of human comforter , have only 
 water the bramble to invigorate the prick of the horn . yes , if Lenny

 he ; but to be incarcerate for defend they ! you might as well have

 machine . and even the tinker , itinerant , ragamuffin vagabond as he be ,

 thus pass , when the unhappy prisoner become aware of the presence of 
 another spectator to his shame ; he hear no step , but he see a shadow 
 throw over the sward . he hold his breath , and would not look up , with 
 some vague idea that if he refuse to see he might escape be see . 




 CHAPTER IX . 

 " per Bacco ! " say Dr. Riccabocca , put his hand on Lenny 's shoulder , 
 and bend down to look into his face , - " per Bacco ! my young friend , do 
 you sit here from choice or necessity ? " 

 Lenny slightly shudder , and wince under the touch of one whom he have 
 hitherto regard with a sort of superstitious abhorrence . 

 " I fear , " resume Riccabocca , after wait in vain for an answer to his 
 question , " that though the situation be charming , you do not select it 
 yourself . what be this ? " - and the irony of the tone vanish - " what be 
 this , my poor boy ? you have be bleed , and I see that those tear 
 which you try to check come from a deep well . tell I , povero fanciullo 
 mio "   the sweet italian vowel , though Lenny do not understand they , 
 sound softly and soothingly , - " tell I , my child , how all this 
 happen . perhaps I can help you ; we have all err , - we should all help 
 each other . " 

 Lenny 's heart , that just before have seem bind in brass , find itself 
 a way as the Italian speak thus kindly , and the tear rush down ; but 
 he again stop they , and gulp out sturdily , - 

 " I have not do no wrong ; it ben't   my fault , - and ' t be that which 
 kill I ! " conclude Lenny , with a burst of energy . 

 " you have not do wrong ? then , " say the philosopher , draw out 
 his pocket - handkerchief with great composure , and spread it on the 
 ground , - " then I may sit beside you . I could only stoop pityingly over 
 sin , but I can lie down on equal term with misfortune . " 

 Lenny Fairfield do not quite comprehend the word , but enough of their 
 general meaning be apparent to make he cast a grateful glance on the 
 Italian . Riccabocca resume , as he adjust the pocket - handkerchief , " I 
 have a right to your confidence , my child , for I have be afflict 
 in my day ; yet I too say with thee , ' I have not do wrong . ' Cospetto ! " 
  and here the doctor seat himself deliberately , rest one arm on 
 the side column of the stock , in familiar contact with the 
 captive 's shoulder , while his eye wander over the lovely scene 
 around   - " Cospetto ! my prison , if they have catch I , would not have have 
 so fair a look - out as this . but , to be sure , it be all one ; there be no 
 ugly love , and no handsome prison . " 

 with that sententious maxim , which , indeed , he utter in his native 
 Italian , Riccabocca turn round and renew his soothe invitation to 
 confidence . a friend in need be a friend indeed , even if he come in 
 the guise of a Papist and wizard . all Lenny 's ancient dislike to the 
 foreigner have go , and he tell he his little tale . 

 Dr. Riccabocca be much too shrewd a man not to see exactly the motif 
 which have induce Mr. Stirn to incarcerate his agent   bar only that 
 of personal grudge , to which Lenny 's account give he no clew . that a 
 man high in office should make a scapegoat of his own watch - dog for an 
 unlucky snap , or even an indiscreet bark , be nothing strange to the 
 wisdom of the student of Machiavelli . however , he set himself to the 
 task of consolation with equal philosophy and tenderness . he begin by 
 reminding , or rather inform , Leonard Fairfield of all the instance 
 of illustrious man afflict by the injustice of other that occur to 
 his own excellent memory . he tell he how the great Epictetus , when in 
 slavery , have a master whose favourite amusement be pinch his leg , 
 which , as the amusement end in break that limb , be bad than the 
 stock . he also tell he the anecdote of Lenny 's own gallant countryman , 
 Admiral Byng , whose execution give rise to Voltaire 's celebrated 
 witticism , " En Angleterre on tue un admiral pour encourager les autre . " 

   " in England they execute one admiral in order to encourage the 
  other . " 

 many other illustration , still more pertinent to the case in point , his 
 erudition supply from the store of history . but on see that 
 Lenny do not seem in the slight degree console by these memorable 
 example , he shift his ground , and reduce his logic to the strict 
 argumentum ad rem , begin to prove , first , that there be no disgrace 
 at all in Lenny 's present position , that every equitable person 
 would recognize the tyranny of Stirn and the innocence of its victim ; 
 secondly , that if even here he be mistaken , for public opinion be 
 not always righteous , what be public opinion after all ? - " a breath , a 
 puff , " cry Dr. Riccabocca , " a thing without matter , - without length , 
 breadth , or substance , - a shadow , a goblin of our own creating . a man 's 
 own conscience be his sole tribunal , and he should care no more for that 
 phantom ' opinion ' than he should fear meet a ghost if he cross the 
 churchyard at dark . " 

 now , as Lenny do very much fear meet a ghost if he cross the 
 churchyard at dark , the simile spoil the argument , and he shake his 
 head very mournfully . Dr. Riccabocca , be about to enter into a third 
 course of reasoning , which , have it come to an end , would doubtless have 
 settle the matter , and reconcile Lenny to sit in the stock till 
 doomsday , when the captive , with the quick ear and eye of terror and 
 calamity , become conscious that church be over , that the congregation 
 in a few second more would be flock thitherward . he see visionary 
 hat and bonnet through the tree , which Riccabocca see not , despite 
 all the excellence of his spectacle ; hear phantasmal rustling and 
 murmuring which Riccabocca hear not , despite all that theoretical 
 experience in plot , stratagem , and treason , which should have make 
 the Italian 's ear as fine as a conspirator 's or a mole 's . and with 
 another violent but vain effort at escape , the prisoner exclaim , - 

 " oh , if I could but get out before they come ! let I out , let I out ! 
 oh , kind sir , have pity , - let I out ! " 

 " Diavolo ! " say the philosopher , startle , " I wonder that I never 
 think of that before . after all , I believe he have hit the right nail 
 on the head , " and , look close , he perceive that though the partition 
 of wood have hitch firmly into a sort of spring - clasp , which defy 
 Lenny 's unaided struggle , still it be not lock   for , indeed , the 
 padlock and key be snug in the justice - room of the squire , who never 
 dream that his order would be execute so literally and summarily 
 as to dispense with all formal appeal to himself . as soon as Dr. 
 Riccabocca make that discovery , it occur to he that all the wisdom 
 of all the school that ever exist can not   reconcile man or boy to a bad 
 position - the moment there be a fair opportunity of let he out of 
 it . accordingly , without more ado , he lift up the creak board , and 
 Lenny Fairfield dart forth like a bird from a cage , halt a moment as 
 if for breath , or in joy ; and then , take at once to his heel , flee , 
 as a hare to its form , fast to his mother 's home . 

 Dr. Riccabocca drop the yawn wood into its place , pick up 
 his handkerchief and restore it to his pocket ; and then , with some 
 curiosity , begin to examine the nature of that place of duress which 
 have cause so much painful emotion to its rescue victim . " Man be a 
 very irrational animal at good , " quoth the sage , soliloquizing , " and be 
 frighten by strange buggaboo ! ' t be but a piece of wood ! how little 
 it really injure ! and , after all , the hole be but rest to the leg , 
 and keep the foot out of the dirt . and this green bank to sit upon , 
 under the shade of the elm - tree - verily the position must be more 
 pleasant than otherwise ! I have   a great mind - " here the doctor look 
 around , and see the coast still clear , the oddest notion imaginable 
 take possession of he ; yet , not indeed a notion so odd , consider 
 philosophically , - for all philosophy be base on practical 
 experiment , - and Dr. Riccabocca feel an irresistible desire practically 
 to experience what manner of thing that punishment of the stock really 
 be . " I can but try ! only for a moment , " say he apologetically to his 
 own expostulate sense of dignity . " I have time to do it , before any 
 one come . " he lift up the partition again : but stock be build 
 on the true principle of english law , and do not   easily allow a man to 
 criminate himself , - it be hard to get into they without the help of 
 a friend . however , as we before notice , obstacle only whet Dr. 
 Riccabocca 's invention . he look round , and see a withered bit of stick 
 under the tree ; this he insert in the division of the stock , somewhat 
 in the manner in which boy place a stick under a sieve for the purpose 
 of ensnare sparrow ; the fatal wood thus prop , Dr. Riceabocca sit 
 gravely down on the bank , and thrust his foot through the aperture . 

 " nothing in it ! " cry he , triumphantly , after a moment 's deliberation . 
 " the evil be only in idea . such be the boasted reason of mortal ! " with 
 that reflection , nevertheless , he be about to withdraw his foot from 
 their voluntary dilemma , when the crazy stick suddenly give way and 
 the partition fall back into its clasp . Dr. Riceabocca be fairly 
 catch , - " Facilis descensus - se revocare gradum ! " true , his hand be 
 at liberty , but his leg be so long that , be thus fix , they keep 
 the hand from the rescue ; and as Dr. Riccabocca 's form be by no mean 
 supple , and the twin part of the wood stick together with that firmness 
 of adhesion which thing newly paint possess , so , after some vain 
 twist and contortion , in which he succeed at length   not without a 
 stretch of the sinew that make they crack again   in find the clasp 
 and break his nail thereon , the victim of his own rash experiment 
 resign himself to his fate . Dr. Riceabocca be one of those man who 
 never do thing by half . when I say he resign himself , I mean not 
 only christian but philosophical resignation . the position be not quite 
 so pleasant as , theoretically , he have deem it ; but he resolve to 
 make himself as comfortable as he could . at first , as be natural in all 
 trouble to man who have grow familiar with that odoriferous comforter 
 which Sir Walter Raleigh be say first to have bestow upon the 
 caucasian race , the doctor make use of his hand to extract from his 
 pocket his pipe , match - box , and tobacco - pouch . after a few whiff he 
 would have be quite reconcile to his situation , but for the discovery 
 that the sun have shift its place in the heaven , and be no long 
 shade from his face by the elm - tree . the doctor again look round , and 
 perceive that his red silk umbrella , which he have lay aside when he 
 have seat himself by Lenny , be within arm 's reach . possess himself 
 of this treasure , he soon expand its friendly fold . and thus , doubly 
 fortify within and without , under shade of the umbrella , and his 
 pipe composedly between his lip , Dr. Riceabocca gaze on his own 
 incarcerate leg , even with complacency . 

 " ' he who can despise all thing , ' " say he , in one of his native 
 proverb , " ' possess all thing ! ' - if one despise freedom , one be 
 free ! this seat be as soft as a sofa ! I be not sure , " he resume , 
 soliloquize , after a pause , - " I be not sure that there be not 
 something more witty than manly and philosophical in that national 
 proverb of mine which I quote to the fanciullo , ' that there be 
 no handsome prison ' ! do not the son of that celebrated Frenchman , 
 surname Bras de Fer , write a book not only to prove that adversity 
 be more necessary than prosperity , but that among all adversity a 
 prison be the most pleasant and profitable ? but be not this condition of 
 mine , voluntarily and experimentally incur , a type of my life ? be 
 it the first time that I have thrust myself into a hobble ? and if in a 
 hobble of mine own choosing , why should I blame the god ? " 

 upon this , Dr. Riceabocca fall into a train of musing so remote from 
 time and place , that in a few minute he no more remember that he be 
 in the parish stock than a lover remember that flesh be grass , a miser 
 that mammon be perishable , a philosopher that wisdom be vanity . Dr. 
 Riccabocca be in the cloud . 




 CHAPTER X. 

 the dull dog that ever write a novel   and , entre nous , reader   - but 
 let it go no far , - we have a good many dog among the fraternity 
 that be not Munitos might have see with half an eye that the parson 's 
 discourse have produce a very genial and humanizing effect upon his 
 audience . 

   Munito be the name of a dog famous for his learning   a Porson of a 
  dog   at the date of my childhood . there be no such dog nowadays . 

 when all be over , and the congregation stand up to let Mr. Hazeldean 
 and his family walk first down the aisle   for that be the custom at 
 Hazeldean , moistened eye glance at the squire 's sun - burn manly

 roguery in his bright hazel eye , that contrast his assumed stateliness 
 of mien , - without his portion of the silent blessing . not that he have 
 do anything yet to deserve it ; but we all give youth so large a credit 
 in the future . as for Miss Jemima , her trifle foible only rise from 
 too soft and feminine a susceptibility , too ivy - like a yearning for some 
 masculine oak whereon to entwine she tendril ; and so little confine to 
 self be the natural lovingness of her disposition , that she have help 
 many a village lass to find a husband , by the bribe of a marriage gift 
 from her own privy purse ; notwithstanding the assurance with which she 
 accompany the marriage gift , - namely , that " the bridegroom would turn 
 out like the rest of his ungrateful sex ; but that it be a comfort to

 the fat footman who come last , with the family Prayer - book , have his due 
 share in the general association of neighbourly kindness between hall 
 and hamlet . few be there present to whom he have not extend the 
 right - hand of fellowship with a full horn of October in the clasp of 
 it ; and he be a hazeldean man , too , bear and breed , as two - third of

 under the gallery   be . 

 on his part , too , you could see that the squire " be move withal , " and

 Scarcely have Mr. Hazeldean get clear of the churchyard , ere Mr. Stirn 
 be whisper in his ear . as Stirn whisper , the squire 's face grow

 the squire elevate the cane , and his eye shoot fire . Mr. Stirn do not

 Frank hear , and reply readily , - " give them some beer , sir . "

 CHAPTER XI . 

 Dr. Riccabocca , awaken out of his revery by the sound of footstep , 
 be still so little sensible of the indignity of his position , that he 
 enjoy exceedingly , and with all the malice of his natural humour , 
 the astonishment and stupor manifest by Stirn , when that functionary 
 behold the extraordinary substitute which fate and philosophy have find 
 for Lenny Fairfield . instead of the weeping , crush , break - hearte 
 captive whom he have reluctantly come to deliver , he star speechless 
 and aghast upon the grotesque but tranquil figure of the doctor enjoy 
 his pipe , and cool himself under his umbrella , with a sangfroid 
 that be truly appalling and diabolical . indeed , consider that Stirn 
 always suspect the Papisher of have have a hand in the whole of that 
 black and midnight business , in which the stock have be break , bung 
 up , and consign to perdition , and that the Papisher have the evil 
 reputation of dabble in the Black Art , the hocus - pocus way in which 
 the Lenny he have incarcerate be transform into the doctor he find , 
 conjoin with the peculiarly strange eldrich and mephistophelean 
 physiognomy and person of Riccabocca , could not but strike a thrill of 
 superstitious dismay into the breast of the parochial tyrant ; while 
 to his first confused and stammer exclamation and interrogatory , 
 Riccabocca reply with so tragic an air , such ominous shake of the 
 head , such mysterious equivocating , long - word sentence , that Stirn 
 every moment feel more and more convinced that the boy have sell himself 
 to the Powers of Darkness , and that he himself , prematurely and in the 
 flesh , stand face to face with the Arch - Enemy . 

 Mr. Stirn have not yet recover his wonted intelligence , which , to do 
 him justice , be usually prompt enough , when the squire , follow hard 
 by the parson , arrive at the spot . indeed , Mrs. Hazeldean 's report of 
 the squire 's urgent message , disturb manner , and most unparalleled 
 invitation to the parishioner , have give wing to Parson Dale 's 
 ordinarily slow and sedate movement . and while the squire , share 
 Stirn 's amazement , beheld indeed a great pair of foot project from 
 the stock , and see behind they the grave face of Dr. Riccabocca under 
 the majestic shade of the umbrella , but not a vestige of the only 
 be his mind could identify with the tenancy of the stock , Mr. Dale , 
 catch he by the arm , and pant hard , exclaim with a petulance he 
 have never before be know to display , - except at the whist - table , - 

 " Mr. Hazeldean , Mr. Hazeldean , I be scandalize , - I be shock at you . 
 I can bear a great deal from you , sir , as I ought to do ; but to ask my 
 whole congregation , the moment after divine service , to go up and guzzle 
 ale at the Hall , and drink my health , as if a clergyman 's sermon have 
 be a speech at a cattle - fair ! I be ashamed of you , and of the parish ! 
 what on earth have come to you all ? " 

 " that be the very question I wish to Heaven I could answer , " groan the 
 squire , quite mildly and pathetically , - " what on earth have come to we 
 all ? ask Stirn : "   then burst out   " Stirn , you infernal rascal , do not 
 you hear ? what on earth have come to we all ? " 

 " the Papisher be at the bottom of it , sir , " say Stirn , provoke out of 
 all temper . " I do my duty , but I be but a mortal man , arter all . " 

 " a mortal fiddlestick ! where 's Leonard Fairfield , I say ? " 

 " he know well , " answer Stirn , retreat mechanically for safety 's 
 sake behind the parson , and point to Dr. Riccabocca . Hitherto , though 
 both the squire and parson have indeed recognize the italian , they have 
 merely suppose he to be seat on the bank . it never enter into 
 their head that so respectable and dignify a man could by any 
 possibility be an inmate , compel or voluntary , of the parish stock . 
 no , not even though , as I before say , the squire have see , just under 
 his nose , a very long pair of sol insert in the aperture , that 
 sight have only confused and bewilder he , unaccompanie , as it ought 
 to have be , with the trunk and face of Lenny Fairfield . those sol 
 seem to he optical delusion , phantom of the overheat brain ; but 
 now , catch hold of Stirn , while the parson in equal astonishment 
 catch hold of he , the squire falter out , " well , this beat 
 cock - fighting ! the man 's as mad as a March hare , and have take Dr. 
 Rickeybockey for Little Lenny ! " 

 " perhaps , " say the doctor , break silence with a bland smile , and 
 attempt an inclination of the head as courteous as his position would 
 permit , - " perhaps , if it be quite the same to you , before you proceed to 
 explanation , you will just help I out of the stock . " 

 the parson , despite his perplexity and anger , could not repress a smile , 
 as he approach his learn friend , and bend down for the purpose of 
 extricate he . 

 " Lord love your reverence , you would well not ! " cry Mr. Stirn . " do not   be 
 tempt , - he only want to get you into be claw . I would not go a near 
 he for all the - " 

 the speech be interrupt by Dr. Riccabocca himself , who now , thank to 
 the parson , have rise into his full height , and half a head tall than 
 all present - even than the tall squire - approach Mr. Stirn , with 
 a gracious wave of the hand . Mr. Stirn retreat rapidly towards the 
 hedge , amidst the bramble of which he plunge himself incontinently . 

 " I guess whom you take I for , Mr. Stirn , " say the Italian , lift his 
 hat with his characteristic politeness . " it be certainly a great honour ; 
 but you will know well one of these day , when the gentleman in 
 question admit you to a personal interview in another - and a hotter 
 world . " 




 CHAPTER XII . 

 " but how on earth do you get into my new stock ? " ask the squire , 
 scratch his head . 

 " my dear sir , Pliny the elder get into the crater of Mount Etna . " 

 " do he , and what for ? " 

 " to try what it be like , I suppose , " answer Riccabocca . the squire 
 burst out a laughing . 

 " and so you get into the stock to try what it be like . well , I can not 
 wonder , - it be a very handsome pair of stock , " continue the squire , 
 with a love look at the object of his praise . " nobody need be ashamed 
 of be see in those stock , - i'should not mind it myself . "

 whole village here presently , gaze on the lord of the manor in the

 into disgrace ? " 

 " yes , he have though , " cry the squire . " Stirn , I say , stirn ! " but Stirn 
 have force his way through the hedge and vanish . thus leave to his own

 the parson , mollify towards the rude and hasty invention of the

 only some ebullition of your heart that could stifle your sense of

 good friend in the world . I can not   think what it be , " continue Mr.

 there be I , son of his own mother , - who might have be shoot through 
 the lung , only the ball lodge in the shoulder ! and now his wife 's 
 kinsman - my kinsman , too - grandmother a Hazeldean , - a hard - reading ,

 ever be see make a rush at he like a mad bull . it be fatality ! "

 " ancient legend record similar instance of fatality in certain

 polynice and Eteocles , the son of OEdipus . "

 " do ? " say the squire ; " why , reparation must be make to young 
 Leslie . and though I wish to spare Lenny , the young ruffian , a public

 can in private - " 

 " stop , sir ! " say Riccabocca , mildly , " and hear I . " the Italian then , 
 with much feeling and considerable tact , plead the cause of his poor 
 protege , and explain how Lenny 's error arise only from mistaken zeal 
 for the squire 's service , and in the execution of the order receive 
 from Mr. Stirn . 

 " that alter the matter , " say the squire , soften ; " and all that be 
 necessary now will be for he to make a proper apology to my kinsman . " 

 " yes , that be just , " rejoin the parson ; " but I still do not   learn how 
 he get out of the stock . " 

 Riccabocca then resume his tale ; and , after confess his own 
 principal share in Lenny 's escape , draw a moving picture of the boy 's 
 shame and honest mortification . " let we march against Philip ! " cry the 
 Athenians when they hear Demosthenes - 

 " let we go at once and comfort the child ! " cry the parson , before 
 Riccabocca could finish . 

 with that benevolent intention all three quicken their pace , and soon 
 arrive at the widow 's cottage . but Lenny have catch sight of their 
 approach through the window ; and not doubt that , in spite of 
 Riccabocca 's intercession , the parson be come to upbraid and the squire 
 to re - imprison , he dart out by the back way , get amongst the wood , 
 and lie there perdu all the evening . Nay , it be not till after dark 
 that his mother - who sit wring her hand in the little kitchen , and 
 try in vain to listen to the parson and Mrs. Dale , who   after send 
 in search of the fugitive   have kindly come to console the mother - hear 
 a timid knock at the door and a nervous fumble at the latch . she start 
 up , open the door , and Lenny spring to her bosom , and there bury his 
 face , sob aloud . 

 " no harm , my boy , " say the parson , tenderly ; " you have nothing to 
 fear , - all be explain and forgive . " 

 Lenny look up , and the vein on his forehead be much swollen . " Sir , " 
 say he , sturdily , " I do not   want to be forgive , - I be not   do no wrong . 
 and - I have   be disgrace - and I will not   go to school , never no more . " 

 " Hush , Carry ! " say the parson to his wife , who with the usual 
 liveliness of her little temper , be about to expostulate . " good - night , 
 Mrs. Fairfield . I shall come and talk to you to - morrow , Lenny ; by that 
 time you will think well of it . " 

 the parson then conduct his wife home , and go up to the Hall to 
 report Lenny 's safe return ; for the squire be very uneasy about he , 
 and have even in person share the search . as soon as he hear Lenny 
 be safe - " well , " say the squire , " let he go the first thing in the 
 morning to Rood Hall , to ask Master Leslie 's pardon , and all will be 
 right and smooth again . " 

 " a young villain ! " cry Frank , with his cheek the colour of scarlet ; 
 " to strike a gentleman and an Etonian , who have just be to call on I ! 
 but I wonder Randal let he off so well , - any other boy in the sixth 
 form would have kill he ! " 

 " Frank , " say the parson , sternly , " if we all have our desert , what 
 should be do to he who not only let the sun go down on his own 
 wrath , but strive with uncharitable breath to fan the die ember of 
 another 's ? " 

 the clergyman here turn away from Frank , who bite his lip , and seem 
 abash , while even his mother say not a word in his exculpation ; for 
 when the parson do reprove in that stern tone , the majesty of the 
 Hall stand awe before the rebuke of the Church . Catching Riccabocca 's 
 inquisitive eye , Mr. Dale draw aside the philosopher , and whisper to 
 he his fear that it would be a very hard matter to induce Lenny to beg 
 Randal Leslie 's pardon , and that the proud stomach of the pattern - boy 
 would not digest the stock with as much ease as a long regimen of 
 philosophy have enable the sage to do . this conference Miss Jemima soon 
 interrupt by a direct appeal to the doctor respect the number of 
 year   even without any previous and more violent incident   that the 
 world could possibly withstand its own wear and tear . 

 " Ma'am , " say the doctor , reluctantly summon away to look at a passage 
 in some prophetic periodical upon that interesting subject , - " ma'am , 
 it be very hard that you should make one remember the end of the world , 
 since , in converse with you , one 's natural temptation be to forget its 
 existence . " 

 Miss Jemima 's cheek be suffuse with a deep scarlet than Frank 's 
 have be a few minute before . certainly that deceitful , heartless 
 compliment justify all her contempt for the male sex ; and yet - such be 
 human blindness - it go far to redeem all mankind in her credulous and 
 too confide soul . 

 " he be about to propose , " sigh Miss Jemima . 

 " Giacomo , " say Riccabocca , as he draw on his nightcap , and step 
 majestically into the four - post bed , " I think we shall get that boy 
 for the garden now ! " 

 thus each spur his hobby , or drive her car , round the Hazeldean 
 whirligig . 




 CHAPTER XIII . 

 whatever , may be the ultimate success of Miss Jemima Hazeldean 's design 
 upon Dr. Riccabocca , the machiavellian sagacity with which the Italian 
 have count upon secure the service of Lenny Fairfield be speedily 
 and triumphantly establish by the result . no voice of the parson 's , 
 charm he ever so wisely , could persuade the peasant - boy to go and ask 
 pardon of the young gentleman , to whom , because he have do as he be 
 bid , he owe an agonizing defeat and a shameful incarceration ; and , 
 to Mrs. Dale 's vexation , the widow take the boy 's part . she be deeply 
 offend at the unjust disgrace Lenny have undergo in be put in the 
 stock ; she share his pride , and openly approve his spirit . nor be 
 it without great difficulty that Lenny could be induce to resume his 
 lesson at school , - nay , even to set foot beyond the precinct of his 
 mother 's holding . the point of the school at last he yield , though 
 sullenly ; and the parson think it well to temporize as to the more 
 unpalatable demand . unluckily , Lenny 's apprehension of the mockery that 
 await he in the merciless world of his village be realize . though 
 Stirn at first keep his own counsel the tinker blab the whole affair . 
 and after the search institute for Lenny on the fatal night , all 
 attempt to hush up what have pass would have be impossible . so then 
 Stirn tell his story , as the tinker have tell his own ; both tale be 
 very unfavourable to Leonard Fairfield . the pattern - boy have break the 
 Sabbath , fight with his better , and be well maul into the bargain ; 
 the village lad have side with Stirn and the authority in spy out 
 the misdemeanour of his equal therefore Leonard Fairfield , in both 
 capacity of degraded pattern - boy and baffled spy , could expect no 
 mercy , - he be ridicule in the one , and hat in the other . 

 it be true that , in the presence of the schoolmaster and under the eye 
 of Mr. Dale , no one openly give vent to malignant feeling ; but the 
 moment those check be remove , popular persecution begin . 

 some point and mow at he , some curse he for a sneak , and all 
 shun his society ; voice be hear in the hedgerow , as he pass 
 through the village at dusk , " who be put into the stock ? - baa ! " " who 
 get a bloody nob for play spy to Nick Stirn ? - baa ! " to resist this 
 specie of aggression would have be a vain attempt for a wise head 
 and a cold temper than our poor pattern - boy 's . he take his resolution 
 at once , and his mother approve it ; and the second or third day after 
 Dr. Riccabocca 's return to the Casino , Lenny Fairfield present himself 
 on the terrace with a little bundle in his hand . " please , sir , " say he 
 to the doctor , who be sit cross - legged on the balustrade , with his 
 red silk umbrella over his head , - " please , sir , if you will be good enough 
 to take I now , and give I any hole to sleep in , I will work for 
 your honour night and day ; and as for wage , Mother say , ' just suit 
 yourself , sir . ' " 

 " my child , " say the doctor , take Lenny by the hand , and look at 
 he with the sagacious eye of a wizard , " I know you would come ! and 
 Giacomo be already prepare for you ! as to wage , we will talk of they by 
 and by . " 

 Lenny be thus settle , his mother look for some evening on the 
 vacant chair , where he have so long sit in the place of her beloved 
 Mark ; and the chair seem so comfortless and desolate , thus leave all to 
 itself , that she could bear it no long . 

 indeed the village have grow as distasteful to she as to Lenny , - perhaps 
 more so ; and one morning she hail the steward as he be trot his 
 hog - maue cob beside the door , and bid he tell the squire that ' she 
 would take it very kind if he would let she off the six month ' notice 
 for the land and premise she hold ; there be plenty to step into the 
 place at a much well rent . " 

 " you be a fool , " say the good - natured steward ; " and I be very glad you 
 do not speak to that fellow Stirn instead of to I . you have   be do 
 extremely well here , and have the place , I may say , for nothing . " 

 " nothing as to rent , sir , but a great deal as to feelin ' , " say the 
 widow . " and now Lenny have go to work with the foreign gentleman , I 
 should like to go and live near he . " 

 " ah , yes , I hear Lenny have take himself off to the Casino , more fool 
 he ; but , bless your heart , ' t be no distance , - two mile or so . can not   he 
 come home every night after work ? " 

 " no , sir , " exclaim the widow , almost fiercely ; " he sha'n't   come home 
 here , to be call bad name and jeer at ! - he whom my dead good man 
 be so fond and proud of . no , sir ; we poor folk have our feeling , as 
 I say to Mrs. Dale , and as I will say to the squire hisself . not that 
 I do not   thank he for all favour , - he be a good gentleman if let alone ; 
 but he say he will not   come near we till Lenny go and axis pardin . 
 pardin for what , I should like to know ? poor lamb ! I wish you could ha ' 
 see his nose , sir , - as big as your two fist . ax pardin ! if the squire 
 have have such a nose as that , I do not   think it be pardin he have be ha ' 
 ax . but I let the passion get the well of I , - I humbly beg you will 
 excuse it , sir . I be no schollard , as poor Mark be , and Lenny would have 
 be , if the Lord have not visit us otherway . therefore just get the 
 squire to let I go as soon as may be ; and as for the bit o ' hay and 
 what be on the ground and orchard , the new comer will no doubt settle 
 that . " 

 the steward , find no eloquence of his could induce the widow 
 to relinquish her resolution , take her message to the squire . Mr. 
 Hazeldean , who be indeed really offend at the boy 's obstinate refusal 
 to make the amende honorable to randal Leslie , at first only bestow a 
 hearty curse or two on the pride and ingratitude both of mother and son . 
 it may be suppose , however , that his second thought be more gentle , 
 since that evening , though he do not go himself to the widow , he send 
 his " Harry . " now , though Harry be sometimes austere and brusque 
 enough on her own account , and in such business as might especially be 
 transact between herself and the cottager , yet she never appear as 
 the delegate of her lord except in the capacity of a herald of peace and 
 mediating angel . it be with good heart , too , that she undertake 
 this mission , since , as we have see , both mother and son be great 
 favourite of her . she enter the cottage with the friendly beam 
 in her bright blue eye , and it be with the soft tone of her 
 frank cordial voice that she accost the widow . but she be no more 
 successful than the steward have be . the truth be , that I do not   believe 
 the haughty duke in the three kingdom be really so proud as your 
 plain english rural peasant , nor half so hard to propitiate and deal 
 with when his sense of dignity be ruffle . nor be there many of my own 
 literary brother   thin - skinned creature though we be   so sensitively 
 alive to the Public Opinion , wisely despise by Dr. Riccabocca , as that 
 same peasant . he can endure a good deal of contumely sometimes , it be 
 true , from his superior   though , thank Heaven ! that he rarely meet 
 with unjustly ; but to be look down upon and mock and point at by 
 his own equal - his own little world - cut he to the soul . and if you 
 can succeed in break this pride and destroy this sensitiveness , 
 then he be a lost being . he can never recover his self - esteem , and 
 you have chuck he half - way - a stolid , inert , sullen victim - to the 
 perdition of the prison or the convict - ship . 

 of this stuff be the nature both of the widow and her son . have the 
 honey of Plato flow from the tongue of Mrs. Hazeldean , it could not 
 have turn into sweetness the bitter spirit upon which it descend . 
 but Mrs. Hazeldean , though an excellent woman , be rather a bluff , 
 plain - speak one ; and after all she have some little feeling for the son 
 of a gentleman , and a decayed , fallen gentleman , who , even by Lenny 's 
 account , have be assail without any intelligible provocation ; nor 
 could she , with her strong common - sense , attach all the importance which 
 Mrs. Fairfield do to the unmannerly impertinence of a few young cub , 
 which she say truly , " would soon die away if no notice be take of 
 it . " the widow 's mind be make up , and Mrs. Hazeldean depart , - with 
 much chagrin and some displeasure . 

 Mrs. Fairfield , however , tacitly understand that the request she have 
 make be grant , and early one morning her door be find lock , the 
 key left at a neighbour 's to be give to the steward ; and , on further 
 inquiry , it be ascertain that her furniture and effect have be 
 remove by the errand cart in the dead of the night . Lenny have succeed 
 in find a cottage on the road - side , not far from the Casino ; and 
 there , with a joyous face , he wait to welcome his mother to breakfast , 
 and show how he have spend the night in arrange her furniture . 

 " Parson ! " cry the squire , when all this news come upon he , as he be 
 walk arm in arm with Mr. Dale to inspect some propose improvement in 
 the Almshouse , " this be all your fault . why do you not go and talk to 
 that brute of a boy and that dolt of a woman ? you have   get ' soft sawder 
 enough , ' as Frank call it in his new - fashioned slang . " 

 " as if I have not talk myself hoarse to both ! " say the parson , in a 
 tone of reproachful surprise at the accusation . " but it be in vain ! 
 o squire , if you have take my advice about the stock , - ' quieta non 
 movere ' ! " 

 " Bother ! " say the squire . " I suppose I be to be hold up as a tyrant , 
 a Nero , a Richard the Third , or a Grand Inquisitor , merely for have 
 thing smart and tidy ! stock indeed ! your friend Rickeybockey say he 
 be never more comfortable in his life , - quite enjoy sit there . 
 and what do not hurt Rickeybockey 's dignity   a very gentlemanlike 
 man he be , when he please   ought to be no such great matter to Master 
 Leonard Fairfield . but ' t be no use talk ! what be to be do now ? the 
 woman must not starve ; and I be sure she can not   live out of Rickeybockey 's 
 wage to Lenny , - by the way , I hope he do not   board the boy upon his 
 and Jackeymo 's leaving : I hear they dine upon newts and stickleback , 
 faugh ! I will tell you what , Parson , now I think of it , at the back of the 
 cottage which she have take there be some field of capital land just 
 vacant . rickeybockey want to have them , and sound I as to the rent 
 when he be at the Hall . I only half promise he the refusal . and he 
 must give up four or five acre of the good land round the cottage to 
 the widow - just enough for she to manage - and she can keep a dairy . 
 if she want capital , I will lend she some in your name , - only do not   tell 
 Stirn ; and as for the rent - we will talk of that when we see how she get 
 on , thankless , obstinate jade that she be ! you see , " add the squire , 
 as if he feel there be some apology due for this generosity to an

 out of countenance , but go down to the woman at once , or Stirn will

 CHAPTER XIV . 

 of all the ware and commodity in exchange and barter , wherein so 
 mainly consist the civilization of our modern world , there be not one 
 which be so carefully weigh , so accurately measure , so plumb and 
 gauge , so dole and scrap , so pour out in minima and balance with 
 scruple , - as that necessary of social commerce call " an apology " ! if 
 the chemist be half so careful in vend their poison , there would 
 be a notable diminution in the yearly average of victim to arsenic and 
 oxalic acid . but , alas ! in the matter of apology , it be not from the 
 excess of the dose , but the timid , niggardly , miserly manner in which 
 it be dispense , that poor Humanity be hurry off to the Styx ! how many 
 time do a life depend on the exact proportion of an apology ! be it 
 a hairbreadth too short to cover the scratch for which you want it ? make 
 your will , - you be a dead man ! a life do I say ? - a hecatomb of life ! 
 how many war would have be prevent , how many throne would be 
 stand , dynasty flourish , commonwealth brawl round a bema , 
 or fit out galley for corn and cotton , if an inch or two more 
 of apology have be add to the proffer ell ! but then that plaguy , 
 jealous , suspicious , old vinegar - face Honour , and her partner Pride - as 
 penny - wise and pound - foolish a she - skinflint as herself - have the 
 monopoly of the article . and what with the time they lose in adjust 
 their spectacle , hunt in the precise shelf for the precise quality 
 demand , then   quality find   the haggling as to quantum , - consider 
 whether it should be Apothecary 's weight or Avoirdupois , or English 
 measure or flemish , - and , finally , the hullabuloo they make if the 
 customer be not perfectly satisfied with the monstrous little he get 
 for his money , I do not   wonder , for my part , how one lose temper 
 and patience , and send Pride , Honour , and Apology all to the devil . 
 aristophane , in his comedy of " Peace , " insinuate a beautiful allegory 
 by only suffer that goddess , though in fact she be his heroine , to 
 appear as a mute . she take care never to open her lip . the shrewd 
 Greek know very well that she would cease to be Peace , if she once begin 
 to chatter . wherefore , o reader , if ever you find your pump under the 
 iron heel of another man 's boot , Heaven grant that you may hold your 
 tongue , and not make thing past all endurance and forgiveness by 
 bawl out for an apology ! 




 CHAPTER XV . 

 but the squire and his son , Frank , be large - hearted generous creature 
 in the article of apology , as in all thing less skimpingly deal out . 
 and see that Leonard Fairfield would offer no plaster to randal 
 Leslie , they make amend for his stinginess by their own prodigality . 
 the squire accompany his son to Rood Hall , and none of the family 
 choose to be at home , the squire in his own hand , and from his own 
 head , indite and compose an epistle which might have satisfy all the 
 wound which the dignity of the Leslies have ever receive . 

 this letter of apology end with a hearty request that Randal would 
 come and spend a few day with his son . Frank 's epistle be to the same 
 purport , only more etonian and less legible . 

 it be some day before Randal 's reply to these epistle be 
 receive . the reply bear the address of a village near London ; and 
 state that the writer be now read with a tutor preparatory to 
 entrance to Oxford , and could not , therefore , accept the invitation 
 extend to he . 

 for the rest , Randal express himself with good sense , though not with 
 much generosity . he excuse his participation in the vulgarity of such a 
 conflict by a bitter , but short allusion to the obstinacy and ignorance 
 of the village boor ; and do not do what you , my kind reader , certainly 
 would have do under similar circumstance , - namely , intercede in 
 behalf of a brave and unfortunate antagonist . Most of we like a foe 
 well after we have fight he , - that be , if we be the conquering
