 Etonian be concern , the matter rest . and the squire , irritate that 
 he could not repair whatever wrong that young gentleman have sustain , 
 no long feel a pang of regret as he pass by Mrs. Fairfield 's 
 deserted cottage . 




 CHAPTER XVI . 

 Lenny Fairfield continue to give great satisfaction to his new 
 employer , and to profit in many respect by the familiar kindness with 
 which he be treat . Riccabocca , who value himself on penetrate into 
 character , have from the first see that much stuff of no common quality 
 and texture be to be find in the disposition and mind of the english 
 village boy . on further acquaintance , he perceive that , under a child 's 
 innocent simplicity , there be the working of an acuteness that 
 required but development and direction . he ascertain that the 
 pattern - boy 's progress at the village school proceed from something 
 more than mechanical docility and readiness of comprehension . Lenny have 
 a keen thirst for knowledge , and through all the disadvantage of birth 
 and circumstance , there be the indication of that natural genius 
 which convert disadvantage themselves into stimulant . still , with 
 the germ of good quality lie the embryo of those which , difficult to 
 separate , and hard to destroy , often mar the produce of the soil . with 
 a remarkable and generous pride in self - repute , there be some 
 stubbornness ; with great sensibility to kindness , there be also strong 
 reluctance to forgive affront . 

 this mixed nature in an uncultivated peasant 's breast interested 
 Riccabocca , who , though long seclude from the commerce of mankind , 
 still look upon man as the most various and entertaining volume which 
 philosophical research can explore . he soon accustom the boy to the 
 tone of a conversation generally subtle and suggestive ; and Lenny 's 
 language and idea become insensibly less rustic and more refined . 
 then Riccabocca select from his library , small as it be , book that , 
 though elementary , be of a high cast than Lenny could have find 
 within his reach at Hazeldean . Riccabocca know the English language 
 well , - well in grammar , construction , and genius than many a not 
 ill - educate Englishman ; for he have study it with the minuteness with 
 which a scholar study a dead language , and amidst his collection he 
 have many of the book which have formerly serve he for that purpose . 
 these be the first work he lend to Lenny . meanwhile Jackeymo impart 
 to the boy many secret in practical gardening and minute husbandry , 
 for at that day farming in England   some favour county and 
 estate except   be far below the nicety to which the art have be 
 immemorially carry in the north of Italy , - where , indeed , you may 
 travel for mile and mile as through a series of market - garden ; so 
 that , all these thing consider , Leonard Fairfield might be say to 
 have make a change for the well . yet , in truth , and look below the 
 surface , that might be fair matter of doubt . for the same reason which 
 have induce the boy to fly his native village , he no long repair to 
 the church of Hazeldean . the old intimate intercourse between he and 
 the parson become necessarily suspend , or bound to an occasional 
 kindly visit from the latter , - visit which grow more rare and less 
 familiar , as he find his former pupil in no want of his service , and 
 wholly deaf to his mild entreaty to forget and forgive the past , and 
 come at least to his old seat in the parish church . Lenny still go to 
 church , - a church a long way off in another parish , - but the sermon do 
 not do he the same good as Parson Dale 's have do ; and the clergyman , 
 who have his own flock to attend to , do not condescend , as Parson Dale 
 would have do , to explain what seem obscure , and enforce what be 
 profitable , in private talk , with that stray lamb from another 's fold . 

 now I question much if all Dr. Riccabocca 's maxim , though they be 
 often very moral and generally very wise , serve to expand the peasant 
 boy 's native good quality , and correct his bad , half so well as the 
 few simple word , not at all indebted to Machiavelli , which Leonard have 
 once reverently listen to when he stand by Mark 's elbow - chair , yield 
 up for the moment to the good parson , worthy to sit in it ; for Mr. Dale 
 have a heart in which all the fatherless of the parish find their place . 
 nor be this loss of tender , intimate , spiritual lore so counterbalance 
 by the great facility for purely intellectual instruction as modern 
 enlightenment might presume . for , without dispute the advantage of 
 knowledge in a general way , knowledge , in itself , be not friendly 
 to content . its tendency , of course , be to increase the desire , to 
 dissatisfy we with what be , in order to urge progress to what may be ; 
 and in that progress , what unnoticed martyr among the many must fall 
 baffle and crush by the way ! to how large a number will be give 
 desire they will never realize , dissatisfaction of the lot from which 
 they will never rise ! allon ! one be view the dark side of the 
 question . it be all the fault of that confound Riccabocca , who have 
 already cause Lenny Fairfield to lean gloomily on his spade , and , after 
 look round and see no one near he , groan out querulously , - " and 
 be I bear to dig a potato ground ? " 

 Pardieu , my friend Lenny , if you live to be seventy , and ride in your 
 carriage , and by the help of a dinner - pill digest a spoonful of curry , 
 you may sigh to think what a relish there be in potato , roast in 
 ash after you have digge they out of that ground with your own stout 
 young hand . dig on , Lenny Fairfield , dig on ! Dr. Riccabocca will 
 tell you that there be once an illustrious personage -   the Emperor 
 Diocletian   - who make experience of two very different occupation , - one 
 be rule man , the other be plant cabbage ; he think plant 
 cabbage much the pleasanter of the two ! 




 CHAPTER XVIL 

 Dr. Riccabocca have secure Lenny Fairfield , and might therefore 
 be consider to have ride his hobby in the great whirligig with 
 adroitness and success . but Miss Jemima be still drive round in her 
 car , handle the rein , and flourish the whip , without apparently 
 have get an inch near to the fly form of Dr. Riccabocca . 

 indeed , that excellent and only too susceptible spinster , with all her 
 experience of the villany of man , have never conceive the wretch to be 
 so thoroughly beyond the reach of redemption as when Dr. Riccabocca take 
 his leave , and once more inter himself amidst the solitude of the 
 Casino , and without have make any formal renunciation of his criminal 
 celibacy . for some day she shut herself up in her own chamber , and 
 brood with more than her usual gloomy satisfaction on the certainty 
 of the approach crash . indeed , many sign of that universal calamity , 
 which , while the visit of Riccabocca last , she have permit herself 
 to consider ambiguous , now become luminously apparent . even the 
 newspaper , which during that credulous and happy period have give half a 
 column to Births and Marriages , now bear an ominously long catalogue of 
 death ; so that it seem as if the whole population have lose heart , and 
 have no chance of repair its daily loss . the lead article speak , 
 with the obscurity of a Pythian , of an impending crisis . monstrous 
 turnip sprout out from the paragraph devote to General News . cow 
 bear calf with two head , whale be strand in the Humber , shower 
 of frog descend in the High Street of Cheltenham . 

 all these symptom of the world 's decrepitude and consummation , which by 
 the side of the fascinating Riccabocca might admit of some doubt as to 
 their origin and cause , now , conjoin with the bad of all , namely , 
 the frightfully progressive wickedness of man , - leave to Miss Jemima 
 no ray of hope save that afford by the reflection that she could 
 contemplate the wreck of matter without a single sentiment of regret . 

 Mrs. Dale , however , by no mean share the despondency of her fair 
 friend , and have gain access to Miss Jemima 's chamber , succeed , 
 though not without difficulty , in her kindly attempt to cheer the 
 droop spirit of that female misanthropist . nor , in her benevolent 
 desire to speed the car of Miss Jemima to its hymeneal goal , be Mrs. 
 Dale so cruel towards her male friend , Dr. Riccabocca , as she seem to 
 her husband . for Mrs. Dale be a woman of shrewdness and penetration , as 
 most quick - temper woman be ; and she know that Miss Jemima be one 
 of those excellent young lady who be likely to value a husband in 
 proportion to the difficulty of obtain he . in fact , my reader of 
 both sex must often have meet , in the course of their experience , with 
 that peculiar sort of feminine disposition , which require the warmth of 
 the conjugal hearth to develop all its native good quality ; nor be 
 it to be blame overmuch if , innocently aware of this tendency in 
 its nature , it turn towards what be well fit for its growth and 
 improvement , by law akin to those which make the sunflower turn to 
 the sun , or the willow to the stream . lady of this disposition , 
 permanently thwart in their affectionate bias , gradually languish 
 away into intellectual inanition , or sprout out into those abnormal 
 eccentricity which be class under the general name of " oddity " or 
 " character . " but once admit to their proper soil , it be astonishing 
 what healthful improvement take place , - how the poor heart , before 
 starve and stint of nourishment , throw out its sucker , and burst 
 into bloom and fruit . and thus many a belle from whom the beaux have 
 stand aloof , only because the puppy think she could be have for the 
 asking , they see afterwards settle down into true wife and fond mother , 
 with amaze at their former disparagement , and a sigh at their blind 
 hardness of heart . 

 in all probability Mrs. Dale take this view of the subject ; and 
 certainly , in addition to all the hitherto dormant virtue which would 
 be awaken in Miss Jemima when fairly Mrs. Riccabocca , she count 
 somewhat upon the mere worldly advantage which such a match would bestow 
 upon the exile . so respectable a connection with one of the old , 
 wealthy , and most popular family in the shire would in itself give 
 he a position not to be despise by a poor stranger in the land ; and 
 though the interest of Miss Jemima 's dowry might not be much , regard 
 in the light of english pound   not milanese lira , still it would 
 suffice to prevent that gradual process of dematerialization which the 
 lengthen diet upon minnow and stickleback have already make apparent 
 in the fine and slow - evanishe form of the philosopher . 

 like all person convince of the expediency of a thing , Mrs. Dale see 
 nothing want but opportunity to insure its success . and that these 
 might be forthcoming she not only renew with great frequency , and 
 more urgent instance than ever , her friendly invitation to Riccabocca 
 to drink tea and spend the evening , but she so artfully chafe the 
 squire on his sore point of hospitality , that the doctor receive weekly 
 a press solicitation to dine and sleep at the Hall . 

 at first the italian pishe and grunt , and say Cospetto , and 
 per Bacco , and Diavolo , and try to creep out of so much proffered 
 courtesy . but like all single gentleman , he be a little under the 
 tyrannical influence of his faithful servant ; and Jackeymo , though he 
 could bear starve as well as his master when necessary , still , when 
 he have the option , preferred roast beef and plum - pudding . moreover , that 
 vain and incautious confidence of Riccabocca touch the vast sum at 
 his command , and with no heavy drawback than that of so amiable a 
 lady as Miss Jemima - who have already show he   Jackeymo   many little 
 delicate attention - have greatly whet the cupidity which be in 
 the servant 's italian nature , - a cupidity the more keen because , long 
 debar its legitimate exercise on his own mercenary interest , he 
 carry it all to the account of his master 's ! 

 thus tempt by his enemy and betray by his servant , the unfortunate 
 Riccabocca fall , though with eye not unblinde , into the hospitable 
 snare extend for the destruction of his - celibacy ! he go often 
 to the Parsonage , often to the Hall , and by degree the sweet of 
 the social domestic life , long deny he , begin to exercise their 
 enervate charm upon the stoicism of our poor exile . Frank have now 
 return to Eton . an unexpected invitation have carry off Captain 
 Higginbotham to pass a few week at Bath with a distant relation , who 
 have lately return from India , and who , as rich as Creesus , feel 
 so estranged and solitary in his native isle that , when the captain 
 " claim kindre there , " to his own amaze " he have his claim allow ; " 
 while a very protracted sitting of parliament still delay in London 
 the squire 's habitual visitor during the later summer ; so that - a 
 chasm thus make in his society - Mr. Hazeldean welcome with no hollow 
 cordiality the diversion or distraction he find in the foreigner 's 
 companionship . thus , with pleasure to all party , and strong hope to 
 the two female conspirator , the intimacy between the Casino and Hall 
 rapidly thicken ; but still not a word resemble a distinct proposal 
 do Dr. Riccabocca breathe . and still , if such an idea obtrude itself 
 on his mind , it be chase therefrom with so determined a Diavolo that 
 perhaps , if not the end of the world , at least the end of Miss Jemima 's 
 tenure in it , might have approach and see she still Miss Jemima , but 
 for a certain letter with a foreign postmark that reach the doctor one 
 Tuesday morning . 




 CHAPTER XVIII . 

 the servant see that something have go wrong , and , under pretence of 
 syringe the orange - tree , he linger near his master , and peer 
 through the sunny leaf upon Riccabocca 's melancholy brow . 

 the doctor sigh heavily . nor do he , as be his will not after some such 
 sigh , mechanically take up that dear comforter the pipe . but though 
 the tobacco - pouch lay by his side on the balustrade , and the pipe stand 
 against the wall between his knee , childlike lift up its lip to the 
 customary caress , he heed neither the one nor the other , but lay the 
 letter silently on his lap , and fix his eye upon the ground . 

 " it must be bad news indeed ! " think Jackeymo , and desist from his 
 work . approach his master , he take up the pipe and the tobacco - pouch , 
 and fill the bowl slowly , glance all the while towards that dark 
 musing face on which , when abandon by the expression of intellectual 
 vivacity or the exquisite smile of italian courtesy , the deep downward 
 line reveal the character of sorrow . Jackeymo do not venture to 
 speak ; but the continued silence of his master disturb he much . he 
 lay that peculiar tinder which your smoker use upon the steel , and 
 strike the spark , - still not a word , nor do Riccabocca stretch forth 
 his hand . 

 " I never know he in this taking before , " think Jackeymo ; and 
 delicately he insinuate the neck of the pipe into the nerveless finger 
 of the band that lie supine on those quiet knee . the pipe fall to the 
 ground . 

 Jackeymo cross himself , and begin pray to his sainted namesake with 
 great fervour . 

 the doctor rise slowly , and as if with effort ; he walk once or twice 
 to and fro the terrace ; and then he halt abruptly and say , - 

 " Friend ! " 

 " bless Monsignore San Giacomo , I know thou wouldst hear I ! " cry 
 the servant ; and he raise his master 's hand to his lip , then abruptly 
 turn away and wipe his eye . 

 " friend , " repeat Riccabocca , and this time with a tremulous emphasis , 
 and in the soft tone of a voice never wholly without the music of the 
 sweet South , " I would talk to thee of my child . " 




 CHAPTER XIX . 

 " the letter , then , relate to the signorina . she be well ? " 

 " yes , she be well now . she be in our native Italy . " Jackeymo raise 
 his eye involuntarily towards the orange - tree , and the morning breeze 
 sweep by and bore to he the odour of their blossom . 

 " those be sweet even here , with care , " say he , point to the tree . 
 " I think I have say that before to the padrone . " 

 but Riccabocca be now look again at the letter , and do not notice 
 either the gesture or the remark of his servant . " my aunt be no more ! " 
 say he , after a pause . 

 " we will pray for her soul ! " answer Jackeymo , solemnly . " but she be 
 very old , and have be a long time ail . let it not grieve the padrone 
 too keenly : at that age , and with those infirmity , death come as a 
 friend . " 

 " peace be to her dust ! " return the Italian . " if she have her fault , 
 be they now forget forever ; and in the hour of my danger and distress 
 she shelter my infant ! that shelter be destroy . this letter be from 
 the priest , her confessor . and the home of which my child be bereave 
 fall to the inheritance of my enemy . " 

 " traitor ! " mutter Jackeymo ; and his right hand seem to feel for 
 the weapon which the Italians of low rank often openly wear in their 
 girdle . 

 " the priest , " resume Riccabocca , calmly , " have rightly judge in 
 remove my child as a guest from the house in which that traitor enter 
 as lord . " 

 " and where be the signorina ? " 

 " with the poor priest . see , Giacomo , here , here - this be her handwriting 
 at the end of the letter , - the first line she ever yet trace to I . " 

 Jackeymo take off his hat , and look reverently on the large character 
 of a child 's writing . but large as they be , they seem indistinct , 
 for the paper be blister with the child 's tear ; and on the place 
 where they have not fall , there be a round fresh moist stain of the 
 tear that have drop from the lid of the father . Riccabocca renew , 
 " the priest recommend a convent . " 

 " to the devil with the priest ! " cry the servant ; then cross 
 himself rapidly , he add , " I do not mean that , Monsignore San 
 Giacomo , - forgive I ! but your Excellency do not think of make a nun 
 of his only child ! " 

   the title of Excellency do not , in italian , necessarily express 
  any exalt rank , but be often give by servant to their master . 

 " and yet why not ? " say Riccabocca , mournfully ; " what can I give she in 
 the world ? be the land of the stranger a well refuge than the home of 
 peace in her native clime ? " 

 " in the land of the stranger beat her father 's heart ! " 

 " and if that beat be still , what then ? Ill fare the life that a 
 single death can bereave of all . in a convent at least   and the priest 's 
 influence can obtain she that asylum amongst her equal and amidst her 
 sex   she be safe from trial and from penury - to her grave ! " 

 " Penury ! just see how rich we shall be when we take those field at 
 Michaelmas . " 

 " Pazzie ! " -   Follies   - say Riccabocca , listlessly . " be these sun more 
 serene than ours , or the soil more fertile ? yet in our own Italy , saith 
 the proverb , ' he who sow land reap more care than corn . ' it be 
 different , " continue the father , after a pause , and in a more resolute 
 tone , " if I have some independence , however small , to count on , - nay , 
 if among all my tribe of dainty relative there be but one female who 
 would accompany Violante to the exile 's hearth , - Ishmael have his Hagar . 
 but how can we two rough - bearded man provide for all the nameless 
 want and care of a frail female child ? and she have be so delicately 
 rear , - the woman - child need the foster hand and tender eye of a 
 woman . " 

 " and with a word , " say Jackeymo , resolutely , " the padrone might secure 
 to his child all that he need to save she from the sepulchre of a 
 convent ; and ere the autumn leave fall , she might be sit on his 
 knee . padrone , do not think that you can conceal from I the truth , that 
 you love your child well than all thing in the world , - now the Patria 
 be as dead to you as the dust of your father , - and your heart - string 
 would crack with the effort to tear she from they , and consign she to a 
 convent . padrone , never again to hear her voice , never again to see she 
 face ! those little arm that twin round your neck that dark night , 
 when we flee fast for life and freedom , and you say , as you feel their 
 clasp , ' friend , all be not yet lose . ' " 

 " Giacomo ! " exclaim the father , reproachfully , and his voice seem to 
 choke he . Riccabocca turn away , and walk restlessly to and fro 
 the terrace ; then , lift his arm with a wild gesture , as he still 
 continue his long irregular stride , he mutter , " yes , Heaven be my 
 witness that I could have bear reverse and banishment without a murmur , 
 have I permit myself that young partner in exile and privation . Heaven 
 be my witness that , if I hesitate now , it be because I would not listen 
 to my own selfish heart . yet never , never to see she again , - my child ! 
 and it be but as the infant that I behold she ! o friend , friend ! "   and , 
 stop short with a burst of uncontrollable emotion , he bow his 
 head upon his servant 's shoulder , " thou know what I have endure 
 and suffer at my hearth , as in my country ; the wrong , the perfidy , 
 the - the - " his voice again fail he ; he cling to his servant 's 
 breast , and his whole frame shake . 

 " but your child , the innocent one - think now only of she ! " falter 
 Giacomo , struggle with his own sob . " true , only of she , " reply 
 the exile , raise his face , " only of she . put aside thy thought for 
 thyself , friend , - counsel I . if I be to send for Violante , and if , 
 transplant to these keen air , she droop and die - look , look , 
 the priest say that she need such tender care ; or if I myself be 
 summon from the world , to leave she in it alone , friendless , homeless , 
 breadless perhaps , at the age of woman 's sharp trial against 
 temptation , would she not live to mourn the cruel egotism that close on 
 her infant innocence the gate of the House of God ? " 

 Jackeymo be appal by this appeal ; and indeed Riccabocca have 
 never before thus reverently speak of the cloister . in his hour of 
 philosophy , he be will not to sneer at monk and nun , priesthood and 
 superstition . but now , in that hour of emotion , the Old Religion 
 reclaim her empire ; and the sceptical world - wise man , think only of 
 his child , speak and feel with a child 's simple faith . 




 CHAPTER XX . 

 " but again I say , " murmur Jackeymo , scarce audibly , and after a long 
 silence , " if the padrone would make up his mind - to marry ! " 

 he expect that his master would start up in his customary indignation 
 at such a suggestion , - nay , he might not have be sorry so to have 
 change the current of feeling ; but the poor Italian only wince 
 slightly , and mildly withdraw himself from his servant 's support 
 arm , again pace the terrace , but this time quietly and in silence . a 
 quarter of an hour thus pass . " give I the pipe , " say Dr. Riccabocca , 
 pass into the belvidere . 

 Jackeymo again strike the spark , and , wonderfully relieve at the 
 padrone 's return to the habitual adviser , mentally besought his sainted 
 namesake to bestow a double portion of soothe wisdom on the benignant 
 influence of the weed . 




 CHAPTER XXI . 

 Dr. Riccabocca have be some little time in the solitude of the 
 belvidere , when Lenny Fairfield , not know that his employer be 
 therein , enter to lay down a book which the doctor have lend he , with 
 injunction to leave it on a certain table when do with . Riccabocca 
 look up at the sound of the young peasant 's step . 

 " I beg your honour 's pardon , I do not know - " 

 " never mind : lie the book there . I wish to speak with you . you look 
 well , my child : this air agree with you as well as that of Hazeldean ? " 

 " oh , yes , Sir ! " 

 " yet it be high ground , - more expose ? " 

 " that can hardly be , sir , " say Lenny ; " there be many plant grow here 
 which do not   flourish at the squire 's . the hill yonder keep off the east 
 wind , and the place lay to the south . " 

 " lie , not lay , Lenny . what be the principal complaint in these 
 part ? " 

 " eh , sir ? " 

 " I mean what malady , what disease ? " 

 " I never hear tell of any , sir , except the rheumatism . " 

 " no low fever , no consumption ? " 

 " never hear of they , sir . " 

 Riccabocca draw a long breath , as if relieve . " that seem a very kind 
 family at the Hall . " 

 " I have nothing to say against it , " answer Lenny , bluntly . " I have not 
 be treat justly . but as that book say , sir , ' it be not every one 
 who come into the world with a silver spoon in his mouth . ' " 

 Little think the doctor that those wise maxim may leave sore thought 
 behind they ! he be too occupy with the subject most at his own heart 
 to think then of what be in Lenny Fairfield 's . 

 " yes ; a kind , english domestic family . do you see much of Miss 
 Hazeldean ? " 

 " not so much as of the Lady . " 

 " be she like in the village , think you ? " 

 " Miss Jemima ? yes . she never do harm . her little dog bit I once , - she 
 do not ask I to beg its pardon , she ask mine ! she be a very nice 
 young lady ; the girl say she be very affable ; and , " add Lenny , with a 
 smile , " there be always more wedding go on when she be down at the 
 Hall . " 

 " oh ! " say Riccabocca . then , after a long whiff , " do you ever see she 
 play with the little child ? be she fond of child , do you think ? " 

 " Lord , sir , you guess everything ! she be never so pleased as when she be 
 play with the baby . " 

 " Humph ! " grunt Riccabocca . " baby ! well , that be woman - like . I do not 
 mean exactly baby , but when they be old , - little girl ? " 

 " indeed , Sir , I dare say ; but , " say Lenny , primly , " I never as yet keep 
 company with the little girl . " 

 " quite right , Lenny ; be equally discreet all your life . Mrs. Dale be 
 very intimate with Miss Hazeldean , - more than with the squire 's lady . 
 why be that , think you ? " 

 " well , sir , " say Leonard , shrewdly , " Mrs. Dale have her little temper , 
 though she be a very good lady ; and Madame Hazeldean be rather high , and 
 have a spirit . but Miss Jemima be so soft : any one could live with Miss 
 Jemima , as Joe and the servant say at the Hall . " 

 " indeed ! get my hat out of the parlour , and - just bring a clothe - brush , 
 Lenny . a fine sunny day for a walk . " 

 after this most mean and dishonourable inquisition into the character 
 and popular repute of Miss Hazeldean , Signor Riccabocca seem as much 
 cheer up and elated as if he have commit some very noble action ; 
 and he walk forth in the direction of the Hall with a far light and 
 lively step than that with which he have pace the terrace . 

 " Monsignore San Giacomo , by thy help and the pipe 's , the padrone shall 
 have his child ! " mutter the servant , look up from the garden . 




 CHAPTER XXII . 

 yet Dr. Riccabocca be not rash . the man who want his wedding - garment 
 to fit he must allow plenty of time for the measure . but from that day , 
 the Italian notably change his manner towards Miss Hazeldean . he cease 
 that profusion of compliment in which he have hitherto carry off 
 in safety all serious meaning . for indeed the doctor consider that 
 compliment to a single gentleman be what the inky liquid it dispense 
 be to the cuttle - fish , that by obscure the water sail away from its 
 enemy . neither do he , as before , avoid prolonged conversation with 
 the young lady , and contrive to escape from all solitary ramble by 
 her side . on the contrary , he now seek every occasion to be in her 
 society ; and entirely drop the language of gallantry , he assume 
 something of the earnest tone of friendship . he bend down his intellect 
 to examine and plumb her own . to use a very homely simile , he blow away 
 that froth which there be on the surface of mere acquaintanceship , 
 especially with the opposite sex ; and which , while it last , scarce 
 allow you to distinguish between small beer and double x. apparently 
 Dr. Riccabocca be satisfied with his scrutiny , - at all event under 
 that froth there be no taste of bitter . the italian might not find 
 any great strength of intellect in Miss Jemima , but he find that , 
 disentangle from many little whim and foible , - which he have himself 
 the sense to perceive be harmless enough if they last , and not so 
 absolutely constitutional but what they might be remove by a tender 
 hand , - Miss Hazeldean have quite enough sense to comprehend the plain 
 duty of married life ; and if the sense could fail , it find a 
 substitute in good old homely english principle , and the instinct of 
 amiable , kindly feeling . 

 I know not how it be , but your very clever man never seem to care so 
 much as your less gifted mortal for cleverness in his helpmate . your 
 scholar and poet and minister of state be more often than not find 
 assort with exceedingly humdrum , good sort of woman , and apparently 
 like they all the well for their deficiency . just see how happily 
 Racine live with his wife , and what an angel he think she , and yet 
 she have never read his play . certainly Goethe never trouble the lady 
 who call he " Mr. Privy Councillor " with whim about " monad , " and 
 speculation on colour , nor those stiff metaphysical problem on which 
 one break one 's shin in the second Past of the " Faust . " probably 
 it may be that such great genius - know that , as compare with 
 themselves , there be little difference between your clever woman and 
 your humdrum woman - merge at once all minor distinction , relinquish 
 all attempt at sympathy in hard intellectual pursuit , and be quite 
 satisfied to establish that tie which , after all , good resist wear 
 and tear , - namely , the tough household bond between one human heart and 
 another . 

 at all event , this , I suspect , be the reasoning of Dr. Riccabocca , 
 when one morning , after a long walk with Miss Hazeldean , he mutter to 
 himself , - 

  " Duro con duro 
  Non fete mai buon muro , " - 

 which may bear the paraphrase , " Bricks without mortar would make a very 
 bad wall . " there be quite enough in Miss Jemima 's disposition to make 
 excellent mortar : the doctor take the brick to himself . 

 when his examination be conclude , our philosopher symbolically evince 
 the result he have arrive at by a very simple proceeding on his 
 part , which would have puzzle you greatly if you have not pause , and 
 meditate thereon , till you see all that it imply . Dr. Riccabocca , 
 take of his spectacle ! he wipe they carefully , put they into their 
 shagreen case , and lock they in his bureau , - that be to say , he leave 
 off wear his spectacle . 

 you will observe that there be a wonderful depth of meaning in that 
 critical symptom , whether it be regard as a sign outward , positive , 
 and explicit , or a sign metaphysical , mystical , and esoteric . for , as 
 to the last , it denote that the task of the spectacle be over ; 
 that , when a philosopher have make up his mind to marry , it be well 
 henceforth to be shortsighte - nay , even somewhat purblind - than to 
 be always scrutinize the domestic felicity , to which he be about to 
 resign himself , through a pair of cold , unillusory barnacle . as for the 
 thing beyond the hearth , if he can not see without spectacle , be he 
 not about to ally to his own defective vision a good sharp pair of eye , 
 never at fault where his interest be concern ? on the other hand , 
 regard positively , categorically , and explicitly , Dr. Roccabocca , by 
 lay aside those spectacle , signify that he be about to commence 
 that happy initiation of courtship when every man , be he ever so much a 
 philosopher , wish to look as young and as handsome as time and nature 
 will allow . vain task to speed the soft language of the eye through the 
 medium of those glassy interpreter ! I remember , for my own part , that 
 once , on a visit to the town of Adelaide , I - Pisistratus Caxton - be in 
 great danger of fall in love , - with a young lady , too , who would have 
 bring I a very good fortune , - when she suddenly produce from her 
 reticule a very neat pair of no . 4 , set in tortoiseshell , and fix 
 upon I their Gorgon gaze , freeze the astonish Cupid into stone ! and 
 I hold it a great proof of the wisdom of Riccabocca , and of his vast 
 experience in mankind , that he be not above the consideration of what 
 your pseudo - sage would have regard as foppish and ridiculous trifle . 
 it argue all the well for that happiness which be our being 's end 
 and aim that in condescend to play the lover , he put those unbecoming 
 petrifier under lock and key . 

 and certainly , now the spectacle be abandon , it be impossible to 
 deny that the Italian have remarkably handsome eye . even through the 
 spectacle , or lift a little above they , they be always bright and 
 expressive ; but without those adjunct , the blaze be soft and more 
 temper : they have that look which the french call veloute , or velvety ; 
 and he appear altogether ten year young . if our Ulysses , thus 
 rejuvenate by his Minerva , have not fully make up his mind to make 
 a Penelope of Miss Jemima , all I can say be , that he be bad than 
 Polyphemus , who be only an Anthropophagos , - 

 he prey upon the weak sex , and be a Gynopophagite ! 




 CHAPTER XXIII . 

 " and you commission I , then , to speak to our dear Jemima ? " say Mrs. 
 Dale , joyfully , and without any bitterness whatever in that " dear . " 

 DR . RICCABOCCA . - " Nay , before speak to Miss Hazeldean , it would 
 surely be proper to know how far my address would be acceptable to the 
 family . " 

 MRS . DALE . - " ah ! " 

 DR . RICCAROCCA . - " the squire be of course the head of the family . " 

 MRS . DALE   absent and distraite . - " the squire - yes , very true - quite 
 proper . "   then , look up , and with naivete   " can you believe I ? I 
 never think of the squire . and he be such an odd man , and have so many 
 english prejudice , that really - dear I , how vexatious that it should 
 never once have occur to I that Mr. Hazeldean have a voice in the 
 matter ! indeed , the relationship be so distant , it be not like be her 
 father ; and Jemima be of age , and can do as she please ; and - but , as 
 you say , it be quite proper that he should be consult as the head of 
 the family . " 

 DR . RICCASOCCA . - " and you think that the squire of Hazeldean might 
 reject my alliance ! Pshaw ! that be a grand word indeed , - I mean , that he 
 might object very reasonably to his cousin 's marriage with a foreigner , 
 of whom he can know nothing , except that which in all country be 
 disreputable , and be say in this to be criminal , - poverty . " 

 MRS . DALE   kindly   - " you misjudge we poor english people , and you wrong 
 the squire , Heaven bless he ! for we be poor enough when he single 
 out my husband from a hundred for the minister of his parish , for his 
 neighbour and his friend . I will speak to he fearlessly - " 

 DR . RICCABOCCA . - " and frankly . and now I have use that word , let I 
 go on with the confession which your kindly readiness , my fair friend , 
 somewhat interrupted . I say that if I might presume to think my 
 address would be acceptable to Miss Hazeldean and her family , I be 
 too sensible of her amiable quality not to - not to - " 

 MRS . DALE   with demure archness . - " not to be the happy of 
 man , - that be the customary english phrase , Doctor . " 

 riccabocca   gallantly . - " there can not be a well . but , " continue he , 
 seriously , " I wish it first to be understand that I have - be marry 
 before ! " 

 MRS . DALE   astonish . - " marry before ! " 

 RICCABOCCA . - " and that I have an only child , dear to I , - inexpressibly 
 dear . that child , a daughter , have hitherto live abroad ; circumstance 
 now render it desirable that she should make her home with I ; and I own 
 fairly that nothing have so attach I to Miss Hazeldean , nor so induce 
 my desire for our matrimonial connection , as my belief that she have the 
 heart and the temper to become a kind mother to my little one . " 

 MRS . DALE   with feeling and warmth . - " you judge she rightly there . " 

 RICCABOCCA . - " now , in pecuniary matter , as you may conjecture from my 
 mode of life , I have nothing to offer to Miss Hazeldean correspondent 
 with her own fortune , whatever that may be ! " 

 MRS . DALE . - " that difficulty be obviate by settle Miss Hazeldean 's 
 fortune on herself , which be customary in such case . " 

 Dr. Riccabocca 's face lengthen . " and my child , then ? " say he , 
 feelingly . there be something in that appeal so alien from all sordid 
 and merely personal mercenary motif , that Mrs. Dale could not have have 
 the heart to make the very rational suggestion , " but that child be not 
 Jemima 's , and you may have child by she . " 

 she be touch , and reply hesitatingly , " but from what you and Jemima 
 may jointly possess you can save something annually , - you can insure 
 your life for your child . we do so when our poor child whom we lose be 
 bear "   the tear rush into Mrs. Dale 's eye ; " and I fear that Charles 
 still insure his life for my sake , though Heaven know that - that - " 

 the tear burst out . that little heart , quick and petulant though 
 it be , have not a fibre of the elastic muscular tissue which be 
 mercifully bestow on the heart of predestine widow . Dr. Riccabocca 
 could not pursue the subject of life insurance far . but the 
 idea - which have never occur to the foreigner before , though 
 so familiar with we english people when only possess of a life 
 income - please he greatly . I will do he the justice to say that he 
 prefer it to the thought of actually appropriate to himself and to 
 his child a portion of Miss Hazeldean 's dower . 

 shortly afterwards he take his leave , and Mrs. Dale hasten to seek 
 her husband in his study , inform he of the success of her matrimonial 
 scheme , and consult he as to the chance of the squire 's acquiescence 
 therein . " you see , " say she , hesitatingly , " though the squire might be 
 glad to see Jemima marry to some Englishman , yet if he ask who and 
 what be this Dr. Riccabocca , how be I to answer he ? " 

 " you should have think of that before , " say Mr. Dale , with unwonted 
 asperity ; " and , indeed , if I have ever believe anything serious could 
 come out of what seem to I so absurd , I should long since have 
 request you not to interfere in such matter . good heaven ! " continue 
 the parson , change colour , " if we should have assist , underhand as 
 it be , to introduce into the family of a man to whom we owe so much 
 a connection that he would dislike , how base we should be , how 
 ungrateful ! " 

 Poor Mrs. Dale be frighten by this speech , and still more by her 
 husband 's consternation and displeasure . to do Mrs. Dale justice , 
 whenever her mild partner be really either grieve or offend , her 
 little temper vanish , - she become as meek as a lamb . as soon as she 
 recover the first shock she experience , she hasten to dissipate the 
 parson 's apprehension . she assure he that she be convince that , if 
 the squire disapprove of Riccabocca 's pretension , the italian would 
 withdraw they at once , and Miss Hazeldean would never know of his 
 proposal . therefore , in that case , no harm would be do . 

 this assurance , coincide with Mr. Dale 's conviction as to 
 Riccabocca 's scruple on the point of honour , tend much to compose 
 the good man ; and if he do not , as my reader of the gentler sex would 
 expect from he , feel alarm lest Miss Jemima 's affection should have 
 be irretrievably engage , and her happiness thus put in jeopardy by 
 the squire 's refusal , it be not that the parson want tenderness of 
 heart , but experience in womankind ; and he believe , very erroneously , 
 that Miss Jemima Hazeldean be not one upon whom a disappointment of 
 that kind would produce a lasting impression . therefore Mr. Dale , after 
 a pause of consideration , say kindly , - 

 " well , do not   vex yourself , - and I be to blame quite as much as you . 
 but , indeed , I should have think it easy for the squire to have 
 transplant one of his tall cedar into his kitchen - garden than for you 
 to inveigle Dr. Riccabocca into matrimonial intention . but a man who 
 could voluntarily put himself into the parish stock for the sake of 
 experiment must be capable of anything ! however , I think it well that 
 I , rather than yourself , should speak to the squire , and I will go at 
 once . " 




 CHAPTER XXIV . 

 the parson put on the shovel - hat , which - conjoin with other detail in 
 his dress peculiarly clerical , and already , even then , begin to be 
 out of fashion with Churchmen - have serve to fix upon he emphatically 
 the dignified but antiquated style and cognoman of " Parson ; " and take 
 his way towards the Home Farm , at which he expect to find the squire . 
 but he have scarcely enter upon the village green when he behold Mr. 
 Hazeldean , lean both hand on his stick , and gaze intently upon 
 the parish stock . now , sorry be I to say that , ever since the Hegira 
 of Lenny and his mother , the Anti - stockian and Revolutionary spirit in 
 Hazeldean , which the memorable homily of our parson have a while avert 
 or suspend , have break forth afresh . for though while Lenny be 
 present to be mow and jeer at , there have be no pity for he , 
 yet no soon be he remove from the scene of trial than a universal 
 compassion for the barbarous usage he have receive produce what be 
 call " the reaction of public opinion . " not that those who have mow 
 and jeer repent they of their mockery , or consider themselves in 
 the slight degree the cause of his expatriation . no ; they , with the 
 rest of the villager , lay all the blame upon the stock . it be not to 
 be expect that a lad of such exemplary character could be thrust into 
 that place of ignominy , and not be sensible to the affront . and who , in 
 the whole village , be safe , if such going - on and putting - in be 
 to be tolerate in silence , and at the expense of the very good and 
 quiet lad the village have ever know ? thus , a few day after 
 the widow 's departure , the stock be again the object of midnight 
 desecration : it be bedaub and bescratche , it be hack and hew , 
 it be scrawl over with pithy lamentation for Lenny , and laconic 
 execration on tyrant . night after night new inscription appear , 
 testify the sarcastic wit and the vindictive sentiment of the parish . 
 and perhaps the stock be only spar from axe and bonfire by the 
 convenience it afford to the malice of the disaffect : it become the 
 Pasquin of Hazeldean . 

 as disaffection naturally produce a correspondent vigour in authority , 
 so affair have be lately administer with great severity than have 
 be hitherto will not in the easy rule of the squire and his predecessor . 
 suspect person be naturally mark out by Mr. Stirn , and report 
 to his employer , who , too proud or too pained to charge they openly with 
 ingratitude , at first only pass they by in his walk with a silent and 
 stiff inclination of his head ; and afterwards , gradually yield to the 
 baleful influence of Stirn , the squire grumble forth " that he do 
 not see why he should be always put himself out of his way to show 
 kindness to those who make such a return . there ought to be a difference 
 between the good and the bad . " encourage by this admission , Stirn have 
 conduct himself towards the suspect party , and their whole kith 
 and kin , with the iron - hand justice that belong to his character . 
 for some , habitual donation of milk from the dairy and vegetable from 
 the garden be surlily suspend ; other be inform that their pig 
 be always trespass on the wood in search of acorn , or that they 
 be violate the Game Laws in keep lurcher . a beer - house , 
 popular in the neighbourhood , but of late resort to over - much by the 
 grievance - monger   and no wonder , since they have become the popular 
 party , be threaten with an application to the magistrate for 
 the withdrawal of its license . sundry old woman , whose grandson be 
 notoriously ill - dispose towards the stock , be interdict from 
 gather dead stick under the avenue , on pretence that they break 
 down the live bough ; and , what be more obnoxious to the young 
 member of the parish than most other retaliatory measure , three 
 chestnut - tree , one walnut , and two cherry - tree , stand at the bottom 
 of the Park , and which have , from time immemorial , be give up to the 
 youth of Hazeldean , be now solemnly place under the general defence 
 of " private property . " and the crier have announce that , henceforth , all 
 depredator on the fruit tree in Copse Hollow would be punish with 
 the utmost rigour of the law . stirn , indeed , recommend much more 
 stringent proceeding than all these indication of a change of policy , 
 which , he aver , would soon bring the parish to its sens , - such as 
 discontinue many little job of unprofitable work that employ the 
 surplus labour of the village . but there the squire , fall into the 
 department and under the benigner influence of his Harry , be as yet not 
 properly harden . when it come to a question that affect the absolute 
 quantity of loaf to be consume by the graceless mouth that feed 
 upon he , the milk of human kindness - with which Providence have so 
 bountifully supply that class of the mammalia call the " Bucolic , " 
 and of which our squire have an extra " yield " - burst forth , and wash 
 away all the indignation of the harsh Adam . 

 still your policy of half - measure , which irritate without crush 
 its victim , which flap an exasperate wasp - nest with a silk 
 pocket - handkerchief , instead of blow it up with a match and train , 
 be rarely successful ; and after three or four other and much guilty 
 victim than Lenny have be incarcerate in the stock , the parish 
 of Hazeldean be ripe for any enormity . Pestilent jacobinical tract , 
 conceive and compose in the sink of manufacturing town , find their 
 way into the popular beer - house , - Heaven know how , though the tinker 
 be suspect of be the disseminator by all but Stirn , who still , in 
 a whisper , accuse the Papishers . and , finally , there appear amongst 
 the other graphic embellishment which the poor stock have receive , 
 the rude gravure of a gentleman in a broad - brim hat and top - boot , 
 suspend from a gibbet , with the inscription beneath , " a warnin to hall 
 tirans - mind your hi ! - sighnde Captin sTraw . " 

 it be upon this significant and emblematic portraiture that the 
 squire be gaze when the parson join he . " well , Parson , " say Mr. 
 Hazeldean , with a smile which he mean to be pleasant and easy , 
 but which be exceedingly bitter and grim , " I wish you joy of your 
 flock , - you see they have just hang I in effigy ! " 

 the parson star , and though greatly shock , smother his emotion ; 
 and attempt , with the wisdom of the serpent and the mildness of the 
 dove , to find another original for the effigy . 

 " it be very bad , " quoth he , " but not so bad as all that , Squire ; that be 
 not the shape of your bat . it be evidently mean for Mr. Stirn . " 

 " do you think so ? " say the squire , soften . " yet the top - boots - Stirn 
 never wear top - boot . " 

 " no more do you , except in the hunting - field . if you look again , those 
 be not top , they be legging , - stirn wear legging . besides , that 
 flourish , which be mean for a nose , be a kind of hook , like Stirn 's ; 
 whereas your nose - though by no mean a snub - rather turn up than not , 
 as the Apollo 's do , accord to the plaster cast in Riccabocca 's 
 parlour . " 

 " Poor Stirn ! " say the squire , in a tone that evince complacency , not 
 unmingled with compassion , " that be what a man get in this world by 
 be a faithful servant , and do his duty with zeal for his employer . 
 but you see thing have come to a strange pass , and the question now 
 be , what course to pursue . the miscreant hitherto have defy all 
 vigilance , and Stirn recommend the employment of a regular nightwatch , 
 with a lanthorn and bludgeon . " 

 " that may protect the stock certainly ; but will it keep those 
 detestable tract out of the beer - house ? " 

 " we shall shut the beer - house up the next session . " 

 " the tract will break out elsewhere , - the humour be in the blood ! " 

 " I have   half a mind to run off to Brighton or Leamingtongood hunt at 
 Leamington - for a year , just to let the rogue see how they can get on 
 without I ! " 

 the squire 's lip tremble . 

 " my dear Mr. Hazeldean , " say the parson , take his friend 's hand , 
 " I do not   want to parade my superior wisdom ; but , if you have take my 
 advice , ' quieta non movere ! ' be there ever a parish so peaceable 
 as this , or a country gentleman so beloved as you be , before you 
 undertake the task which have dethrone king and ruin States , - that 
 of wantonly meddle with antiquity , whether for the purpose of 
 uncalled - for repair , or the revival of obsolete us . " 

 at this rebuke , the squire do not manifest his constitutional 
 tendency to chol ; but he reply almost meekly , " if it be to do 
 again , faith , I would leave the parish to the enjoyment of the shabby 
 pair of stock that ever disgrace a village . certainly I mean it for 
 the good , - an ornament to the green ; however , now the stock be rebuild , 
 the stock must be support . will Hazeldean be not the man to give way 
 to a set of thankless rapscallion . " 

 " I think , " say the parson , " that you will allow that the House 
 of Tudor , whatever its fault , be a determine , resolute dynasty 
 enough , - high - hearted and strong - headed . a Tudor would never have fall 
 into the same calamity as the poor Stuart do ! " 

 " what the plague have the House of Tudor get to do with my stock ? " 

 " a great deal . Henry VIII . find a subsidy so unpopular that he give it 
 up ; and the people , in return , allow he to cut off as many head as 
 he please , besides those in his own family . good Queen Bess , who , I 
 know , be your idol in history - " 

 " to be sure ! - she knight my ancestor at Tilbury Fort . " 

 " Good Queen Bess struggle hard to maintain a certain monopoly ; she see 
 it would not do , and she surrender it with that frank heartiness which 
 become a sovereign , and make surrender a grace . " 

 " ha ! and you would have I give up the stock ? " 

 " I would much rather the stock have remain as it be before 
 you touch it ; but , as it be , if you could find a good plausible 
 pretext - and there be an excellent one at hand , - the stern king open 
 prison , and grant favour , upon joyful occasion . now a marriage in the 
 royal family be of course a joyful occasion ! and so it should be in 
 that of the King of Hazeldean . " Admire that artful turn in the parson 's 
 eloquence ! - it be worthy of Riccabocca himself . indeed , Mr. Dale have 
 profit much by his companionship with that Machiavellian intellect . 

 " a marriage , - yes ; but Frank have only just get into coattail ! " 

 " I do not allude to Frank , but to your cousin Jemima ! " 




 CHAPTER XXV . 

 the squire stagger as if the breath have be knock out of he , and , 
 for want of a well seat , sit down on the stock . all the female head 
 in the neighbouring cottage peer , themselves unseen , through the 
 casement . what could the squire be about ? what new mischief do he 
 meditate ? do he mean to fortify the stock ? Old Gaffer Solomons , who 
 have an indefinite idea of the lawful power of squire , and who have be 
 for the last ten minute at watch on his threshold , shake his head and 
 say , " they as a cut out the mon a hanging , as a put it in the squire 's 
 head ! " 

 " put what ? " ask his grand - daughter . 

 " the gallus ! " answer Solomons , - " he be a go to have it hang from 
 the great elfin - tree . and the parson , good mon , be a quote Scripter 
 agin it ; you see he be a taking off his glove , and a put his two 
 han be together , as he do when he pray for the sick , Jeany . " 

 that description of the parson 's mien and manner , which with his usual 
 niceness of observation , Gaffer Solomons thus sketch off , will convey 
 to you some idea of the earnestness with which the parson plead the 
 cause he have undertake to advocate . he dwell much upon the sense of 
 propriety which the foreigner have evince in request that the squire 
 might be consult before any formal communication to his cousin ; and 
 he repeat Mrs. Dale 's assurance , that such be Riccabocca 's high 
 standard of honour and belief in the sacred right of hospitality , that , 
 if the squire withhold his consent to his proposal , the parson 
 be convince that the Italian would instantly retract they . now , 
 consider that Miss Hazeldean be , to say the least , come to year of 
 discretion , and the squire have long since place her property entirely 
 at her own disposal , Mr. Hazeldean be force to acquiesce in the 
 parson 's corollary remark , " that this be a delicacy which could not be 
 expect from every english pretender to the lady 's hand . " see that 
 he have so far clear the ground , the parson go on to intimate , though 
 with great tact , that since Miss Jemima would probably marry soon or 
 later   and , indeed , that the squire could not wish to prevent she , it 
 might be well for all party concerned that it should be with some 
 one who , though a foreigner , be settle in the neighbourhood , and of 
 whose character what be know be certainly favourable , rather than 
 run the hazard of her be married for her money by some adventurer , or 
 irish fortune - hunter , at the watering - place she yearly visit . then he 
 touch lightly on Riccabocca 's agreeable and companionable quality ; 
 and conclude with a skilful peroration upon the excellent occasion the 
 wedding would afford to reconcile Hall and parish , by make a voluntary 
 holocaust of the stock . 

 as he conclude , the squire 's brow , before thoughtful , though not

 accordingly , when Mr. Dale have come to an end , the squire reply , with

  the squire   have no right to interfere in the matter , far than with

 the parson , rejoice at this easy way of take the matter , hook his

 " Fie , William ! " say Mrs. Hazeldean , coyly , and blush as she see the

 the parson obey . 

 Mrs. Hazeldean , as the reader may suppose , show much less surprise

 choose England as a country in which to save money ! she suppose that

 " my clear madam , " say the parson , " what you say be extremely just . as

 foreigner pretend to be , a marquis or a baron at least . "

 Rickeybockey that he do not   attempt to humbug we by any such foreign

 indeed , Harry ! - pshaw , good english style that ! Doctor ! my aunt marry 
 a Doctor of divinity - excellent man - wear a wig and be make a dean ! so

 " Lord ! Hazeldean , where on earth do you pick up that idea ? " say Harry , 
 laugh . 

 " pick it up ! - why , I see a fellow myself at the cattle fair last

 CHAPTER XXVI . 

 it be with a slight disturbance of his ordinary suave and well - breed 
 equanimity that the Italian receive the information that he need 
 apprehend no obstacle to his suit from the insular prejudice or the 
 worldly view of the lady 's family . not that he be mean and cowardly 
 enough to recoil from the near and unclouded prospect of that felicity 
 which he have leave off his glass to behold with unblinking , naked 
 eye , - no , there his mind be make up ; but he have meet in life with much 
 that incline a man towards misanthropy , and he be touch not only by 
 the interest in his welfare testify by a heretical priest , but by 
 the generosity with which he be admit into a well - bear and wealthy 
 family , despite his notorious poverty and his foreign descent . he 
 concede the propriety of the only stipulation , which be convey 
 to he by the parson with all the delicacy that become one long 
 professionally habituate to deal with the subtler susceptibility of 
 mankind , - namely , that , amongst Riccabocca 's friend or kindre , some 
 person should be find whose report would confirm the persuasion of his 
 respectability entertain by his neighbour , - he assent , I say , 
 to the propriety of this condition ; but it be not with alacrity and 
 eagerness . his brow become clouded . the parson hasten to assure he 
 that the squire be not a man qui stupet in tituli , -   " who be besotte 
 with title . "   - that he neither expect nor desire to find an 
 origin and rank for his brother - in - law above that decent mediocrity 
 of condition to which it be evident from Riccabocca 's breeding and 
 accomplishment he could easily establish his claim . " and though , " say 
 he , smile , " the squire be a warm politician in his own country , and 
 would never see his sister again , I fear , if she marry some convict 
 enemy of our happy constitution , yet for foreign politic he do not

 grant must be insupportable , - he would but consider you as he would 
 a Saxon who flee from the iron hand of William the Conqueror , or a 
 Lancastrian expel by the Yorkists in our war of the Roses . " 

 the Italian smile . " Mr. Hazeldean shall be satisfy , " say he , simply . 
 " I see , by the squire 's newspaper , that an english gentleman who know I 
 in my own country have just arrive in London . I will write to he for 
 a testimonial , at least to my probity and character . probably he may 
 be know to you by name , - nay , he must be , for he be a distinguished 
 officer in the late war . I allude to Lord L'Estrange . " 

 the parson start . 

 " you know Lord L'Estrange ? - profligate , bad man , I fear . " 

 " profligate ! bad ! " exclaim Riccabocca . " well , calumnious as the world 
 be , I should never have think that such expression would be apply 
 to one who , though I know he but little , - know he chiefly by the 
 service he once render to I , - first teach I to love and revere the 
 english name ! " 

 " he may be change since - " the parson pause . 

 " since when ? " ask Riccabocca , with evident curiosity . Mr. Dale seem 
 embarrassed . " excuse I , " say he , " it be many year ago ; and in short 
 the opinion I then form of the nobleman you name be base upon 
 circumstance which I can not communicate . " 

 the punctilious italian bow in silence , but he still look as if he 
 should have like to prosecute inquiry . 

 after a pause he say , " whatever your impression respect Lord 
 L'Estrange , there be nothing , I suppose , which would lead you to doubt 
 his honour , or reject his testimonial in my favour ? " 

 " accord to fashionable morality , " say Mr. Dale , rather precisely , 
 " I know of nothing that could induce I to suppose that Lord L'Estrange 
 would not , in this instance , speak the truth . and he have unquestionably 
 a high reputation as a soldier , and a considerable position in the 
 world . " Therewith the parson take his leave . a few day afterwards , Dr. 
 Riccabocca inclose to the squire , in a blank envelope , a letter he 
 have receive from Harley L'Estrange . it be evidently intend for 
 the squire 's eye , and to serve as a voucher for the Italian 's 
 respectability ; but this object be fulfil , not in the coarse form of 
 a direct testimonial , but with a tact and delicacy which seem to show 
 more than the fine breeding to be expect from one in Lord L'Estrange 's 
 station . it evince that most exquisite of all politeness which come 
 from the heart ; a certain tone of affectionate respect   which even the 
 homely sense of the squire feel , intuitively , prove far more in favour 
 of Riccabocca than the most elaborate certificate of his quality and 
 antecedent   pervade the whole , and would have suffice in itself to 
 remove all scruple from a mind much more suspicious and exact than 
 that of the Squire of Hazeldean . but , to and behold ! an obstacle 
 now occur to the parson , of which he ought to have think long 
 before , - namely , the papistical religion of the Italian . Dr. Riccabocca 
 be professedly a roman Catholic . he so little obtrude that fact - and , 
 indeed , have assent so readily to any animadversion upon the 
 superstition and priestcraft which , accord to Protestants , be the 
 essential characteristic of papistical community - that it be not 
 till the hymeneal torch , which bring all fault to light , be fairly 
 illumined for the altar , that the remembrance of a faith so cast into 
 the shade burst upon the conscience of the parson . the first idea that 
 then occur to he be the proper and professional one , - namely , the 
 conversion of Dr. Riccabocca . he hasten to his study , take down from 
 his shelf long neglect volume of controversial divinity , arm 
 himself with an arsenal of authority , argument , and text ; then , 
 seize the shovel - hat , post off to the Casino . 




 CHAPTER XXVII . 

 the parson burst upon the philosopher like an avalanche ! he be so full 
 of his subject that he could not let it out in prudent driblet . no , he 
 go souse upon the astounded Riccabocca - 

  " Tremendo 
  Jupiter ipse rueus tumultu . " 

 the sage - shrink deeply into his armchair , and draw his 
 dressing - robe more closely round he - suffer the parson to talk for 
 three quarter of an hour , till indeed he have thoroughly prove his 
 case ; and , like Brutus , " pause for a reply . " 

 then say Riccabocca mildly : " in much of what you have urge so ably , 
 and so suddenly , I be inclined to agree . but base be the man who 
 formally forswear the creed he have inherit from his father , and 
 profess since the cradle up to year of maturity , when the change 
 present itself in the guise of a bribe ; when , for such be human nature , 
 he can hardly distinguish or disentangle the appeal to his reason 
 from the lure to his interest , - here a text , and there a dowry ! - here 
 protestantism , there Jemima ! Own , my friend , that the sober casuist 
 would see double under the inebriate effect produce by so mix 
 his polemical liquor . Appeal , my good Mr. Dale , from Philip drunken to

 appeal from Riccabocca the wooer to Riccabocca the spouse . I may be 
 convertible , but conversion be a slow progress ; courtship should be a 
 quick one , - ask Miss Jemima . Finalmente , marry I first , and convert I 
 afterwards ! " 

 " you take this too jestingly , " begin the parson ; " and I do not   see why , 
 with your excellent understanding , truth so plain and obvious should 
 not strike you at once . " 

 " truth , " interrupt Riccabocca , profoundly , " be the slow grow 
 thing in the world ! it take fifteen hundred year from the date of the 
 christian era to produce your own Luther , and then he fling his Bible at 
 Satan   I have see the mark make by the book on the wall of his prison 
 in Germany , besides run off with a nun , which no protestant 
 clergyman would think it proper and right to do nowadays . " then he 
 add , with seriousness , " look you , my dear sir , I should lose my 
 own esteem if I be even to listen to you now with become 
 attention , - now , I say , when you hint that the creed I have profess 
 may be in the way of my advantage . if so , I must keep the creed and 
 resign the advantage . but if , as I trust not only as a Christian but a 
 man of honour , you will defer this discussion , I will promise to listen 
 to you hereafter ; and though , to say truth , I believe that you will not 
 convert I , I will promise you faithfully never to interfere with my 
 wife 's religion . " 

 " and any child you may have ? " 

 " child ! " say Dr. Riccabocca , recoiling ; " you be not content with 
 fire your pocket - pistol right in my face ! you must also pepper I all 
 over with small shot . child ! well , if they be girl , let they follow 
 the faith of their mother ; and if boy , while in childhood , let they be 
 content with learn to be Christians ; and when they grow into man , 
 let they choose for themselves which be the good form for the practice 
 of the great principle which all sect have in common . " 

 " but , " begin Mr. Dale again , pull a large book from his pocket . 

 Dr. Riccabocca fling open the window , and jump out of it . 

 it be the rapidest and most dastardly flight you could possibly 
 conceive ; but it be a great compliment to the argumentative power of 
 the parson , and he feel it as such . nevertheless , Mr. Dale think it 
 right to have a long conversation , both with the squire and Miss 
 Jemima herself , upon the subject which his intended convert have so 
 ignominiously escape . 

 the squire , though a great foe to Popery , politically consider , have 
 also quite as great a hatred to renegade and apostate . and in his 
 heart he would have despise Riccabocca if he could have throw off 
 his religion as easily as he have do his spectacle . therefore he say 
 simply , " well , it be certainly a great pity that Rickeybockey be not of 
 the Church of England ; though , I take it , that would be unreasonable to 
 expect in a man bear and breed under the nose of the inquisition "   the 
 squire firmly believe that the inquisition be in full force in all 
 the italian States , with whip , rack , and thumbscrew ; and , indeed , his 
 chief information of Italy be gather from a perusal he have give 
 in early youth to " the one - handed Monk " ; " but I think he speak very 
 fairly , on the whole , as to his wife and child . and the thing 's 
 go too far now to retract . it be all your fault for not think of 
 it before ; and I have   now just make up my mind as to the course to pursue 
 respect the d - -d stock ! " 

 as for Miss Jemima , the parson leave she with a pious thanksgiving that 
 Riccabocca at least be a Christian , and not a Pagan , Mahometan , or Jew ! 




 CHAPTER XXVIII . 

 there be that in a wedding which appeal to a universal sympathy . no 
 other event in the life of their superior in rank create an equal 
 sensation amongst the humbler class . 

 from the moment the news that Miss Jemima be to be marry have spread

 think of the stock in such a season ? the stock be sweep out of 
 fashion , - hunt from remembrance as completely as the question of

 the squire taste once more the sweet of the only popularity which be

 he feel grateful to Jemima and to Riccabocca as the special agent of

 one may well conceive that such would have be an inauspicious moment

 of Hazeldean . 

 it be the heartiest rural wedding ! village girl strew flower on the

 have be with all boy , - for she be always kind and gentle , and make 
 he many pretty present whenever she come from the watering - place ; 
 and Frank know that he should miss she sadly , and think she have make a 
 very queer choice . 

 Captain Higginbotham have be invite ; but to the astonishment of 
 Jemima , he have reply to the invitation by a letter to herself , mark 
 " private and confidential . " 

 " she must have long know , " say the letter , " of his devoted attachment 
 to she ! motif of delicacy , arise from the narrowness of his income 
 and the magnanimity of his sentiment , have alone prevent his formal 
 proposal ; but now that he be inform   he could scarcely believe his 
 sens or command his passion   that her relation wish to force 
 she into a barbarous marriage with a foreigner of MOST forbid 
 APPEARANCE , and most abject circumstance , he lose not a moment in 
 lay at her foot his own hand and fortune . and he do this the more 
 confidently , inasmuch as he could not but be aware of Miss Jemima 's 
 secret feeling towards he , while he be proud and happy to say , that 
 his dear and distinguished cousin , Mr. Sharpe Currie , have honour

 expectation , - likely to be soon realize , as his eminent relative have 
 contract a very bad liver complaint in the service of his country , and 
 could not last long ! " 

 in all the year they have know each other , Miss Jemima , strange as it 
 may appear , have never once suspect the captain of any other feeling

 learn her mistake would be to say that she be more than woman .

 to let the reader into a secret , never know to Miss Jemima , Captain

 read their account by those corpse - light , or will - o'-the - wisps , call 
 expectation . ever since the squire 's grandfather have leave he - then in

 rather than see so considerable a cipher suddenly sponge out of his

 among " the expectation " which gather round the form of Mr. Sharpe 
 Currie , who be the cross old tyrant imaginable , and never allow 
 at his table any dish not compound with rice , which play Old Nick 
 with the captain 's constitutional function , I return to the wedding 
 at Hazeldean , just in time to see the bridegroom - who look singularly 
 well on the occasion - hand the bride   who , between sunshiny tear and

 it may be think strange by the unreflective that these rural 
 spectator should so have approve and bless the marriage of a 
 Hazeldean of Hazeldean with a poor , outlandish , long - haired foreigner ; 
 but besides that Riccabocca , after all , have become one of the

 generally noticeable that on wedding occasion the bride so monopolize

 lean on his wife 's arm   for it be a habit of the squire to lean on 
 his wife 's arm rather than she on his , when he be specially pleased ;

 frame thus insensibly , in hour of happiness , seek dependence on the 
 frail arm of woman , - lean , I say , on his wife 's arm , the squire , 
 about the hour of sunset , walk down to the booth by the lake . 

 all the parish - young and old , man , woman , and child , be assemble 
 there , and their face seem to bear one family likeness , in the common 
 emotion which animate all , as they turn to his frank , fatherly smile . 
 Squire Hazeldean stand at the head of the long table : he fill a horn 
 with ale from the brim tankard beside he . then he look round ,

 a speech , and the earnestness of the attention be proportion to the 
 rarity of the event ; for   though he be not unpractised in the oratory
