 fairly be call " a speech " to the villager of Hazeldean , - once on a 
 kindre festive occasion , when he have present to they his bride ; 
 once in a contested election for the shire , in which he take more than 
 ordinary interest , and be not quite so sober as he ought to have 
 be ; once in a time of great agricultural distress , when in spite of 
 reduction of rent , the farmer have be compel to discard a large 
 number of their customary labourer , and when the squire have say , " I 
 have give up keep the hound because I want to make a fine piece of 
 water   that be the origin of the lake , and to drain all the low land 
 round the Park . let every man who want work come to I ! " and that sad 
 year the parish rate of Hazeldean be not a penny the heavy . 

 now , for the fourth time , the squire rise , and thus he speak , - at his 
 right hand , Harry ; at his left , Frank ; at the bottom of the table , as 
 vice - president , Parson Dale , his little wife behind he , only obscurely 
 see . she cry readily , and her handkerchief be already before her 
 eye . 




 CHAPTER XXIX . 

 the SQUIRE 's SPEECH . 

 " friend and neighbour , I thank you kindly for come round I this 
 day , and for show so much interest in I and mine . my cousin be not 
 bear amongst you as I be , but you have know she from a child . it be a 
 familiar face , and one that never frown , which you will miss at your 
 cottage door , as I and mine will miss it long in the old Hall - " 

 here there be a sob from some of the woman , and nothing be see of 
 Mrs. Dale but the white handkerchief . the squire himself pause , and 
 brush away a tear with the back of his hand . then he resume , with a 
 sudden change of voice that be electrical , - 

 " for we none of we prize a blessing till we have lose it ! now , friend 
 and neighbour , a little time ago , it seem as if some ill - will have 
 creep into the village , - ill - will between you and I , neighbour ! - why , 
 that be not like Hazeldean ! " 

 the audience hang their head ! you never see people look so thoroughly 
 ashamed of themselves . the squire proceed , - 

 " I do not   say it be all your fault ; perhaps it be mine . " 

 " Noa , noa , noa , " burst forth in a general chorus . 

 " Nay , friend , " continue the squire , humbly , and in one of those 
 illustrative aphorism which , if less subtle than Riccabocca 's , be 
 more within reach of the popular comprehension , - " nay , we be all human , 
 and every man have his hobby ; sometimes he break in the hobby , and 
 sometimes the hobby , if it be very hard in the mouth , break in he . 
 one man 's hobby have an ill habit of always stop at the public house ! 
  Laughter .   another man 's hobby refuse to stir a peg beyond the door 
 where some buxom lass pat its neck the week before , - a hobby I ride 
 pretty often when I go court my good wife here !   much laughter and 
 applause .   other have a lazy hobby that there be no getting on ; other , 
 a runaway hobby that there be no stopping : but to cut the matter short , 
 my favourite hobby , as you well know , be always trot out to any place 
 on my property which seem to want the eye and hand of the master . I 
 hate , " cry the squire , warming , " to see thing neglect and decay , 
 and go to the dog ! this land we live in be a good mother to we , and 
 we can not   do too much for she . it be very true , neighbour , that I owe 
 she a good many acre , and ought to speak well of she ; but what then ? I 
 live amongst you , and what I take from the rent with one hand , I divide 
 amongst you with the other .   low but assent murmur .   now the more I 
 improve my property , the more mouth it feed . my great - grandfather keep 
 a field - book in which be enter not only the name of all the farmer 
 and the quantity of land they hold , but the average number of the 
 labourer each employ . my grandfather and father follow his example : 
 I have do the same . I find , neighbour , that our rent have double 
 since my great - grandfather begin to make the book . ay , - but there be 
 more than four time the number of labourer employ on the estate , and 
 at much well wage too ! well , my man , that say a great deal in favour 
 of improve property , and not let it go to the dog .   applause . 
 and therefore , neighbour , you will kindly excuse my bobby : it carry 
 grist to your mill .   reiterate applause .   well , but you will say , 
 ' what be the squire drive at ? ' why this , my friend : there be only one 
 wear - out , dilapidated , tumble - down thing in the parish of Hazeldean , and 
 it become an eyesore to I ; so I saddle my hobby , and ride at it . oh , 
 ho ! you know what I mean now ! yes , but , neighbour , you need not have 
 take it so to heart . that be a scurvy trick of some of you to hang I 
 in effigy , as they call it . " 

 " it warn't   you , " cry a voice in the crowd , " it war Nick Stirn . " 

 the squire recognize the voice of the tinker ; but though he now guess 
 at the ringleader , on that day of general amnesty he have the prudence 
 and magnanimity not to say , " stand forth , Sprott : thou art the man . " 
 yet his gallant english spirit would not suffer he to come off at the 
 expense of his servant . 

 " if it be Nick Stirn you mean , " say he , gravely , " more shame for you . 
 it show some pluck to hang the master ; but to hang the poor servant , 
 who only think to do his duty , careless of what ill - will it bring 
 upon he , be a shabby trick , - so little like the lad of Hazeldean , 
 that I suspect the man who teach it to they be never bear in the 
 parish . but let bygone be bygone . one thing be clear , - you do not   take 
 kindly to my new pair of stock ! the stock have be a stumble - block 
 and a grievance , and there be no deny that we go on very pleasantly 
 without it . I may also say that , in spite of it , we have be come 
 together again lately . and I can not   tell you what good it do I to see 
 your child play again on the green , and your honest face , in 
 spite of the stock , and those diabolical tract you have   be read 
 lately , light up at the thought that something pleasant be go on 
 at the Hall . do you know , neighbour , you put I in mind of an old story 
 which , besides apply to the parish , all who be marry , and all who 
 intend to marry , will do well to recollect . a worthy couple , name John 
 and Joan , have live happily together many a long year , till one unlucky 
 day they buy a new bolster . Joan say the bolster be too hard , and 
 John that it be too soft . so , of course , they quarrel . after sulk 
 all day , they agree to put the bolster between they at night . "   roar 
 of laughter amongst the man ; the woman do not know which way to look , 
 except , indeed , Mrs. Hazeldean , who , though she be more than usually 
 rosy , maintain her innocent genial smile , as much as to say , " there 
 be no harm in the squire 's jest . "   the orator resume , " after they have 
 thus lain apart for a little time , very silent and sullen , John sneeze . 
 ' God bless you ! ' say Joan , over the bolster . ' do you say God bless 
 I ? ' cry John , ' then here go the bolster ! ' " 

 prolonged laughter and tumultuous applause . 

 " friend and neighbour , " say the squire , when silence be restore , 
 and lift the horn of ale , " I have the pleasure to inform you that I 
 have order the stock to be take down , and make into a bench for 
 the chimney - nook of our old friend Gaffer Solomons yonder . but mind I , 
 lad , if ever you make the parish regret the loss of the stock , and 
 the overseer come to I with long face , and say , ' the stock must 
 be rebuild , ' why - " here from all the youth of the village rise so 
 deprecate a clamour , that the squire would have be the most burgle 
 orator in the world , if he say a word far on the subject . he 
 elevate the horn over his head - " why , that be my old Hazeldean again ! 
 Health and long life to you all ! " 

 the tinker have sneak out of the assembly , and do not show his face in 
 the village for the next six month . and as to those poisonous tract , 
 in spite of their salubrious label , " the Poor Man 's friend , " or " the 
 Rights of Labour , " you could no more have find one of they lurk in 
 the drawer of the kitchen dresser in Hazeldean than you would have 
 find the deadly nightshade on the flower - stand in the drawing - room of 
 the Hall . as for the revolutionary beerhouse , there be no need to apply 
 to the magistrate to shut it up , - it shut itself up before the week be 
 out . 

 o young head of the great House of Hapsburg , what a Hazeldean you might 
 have make of Hungary ! what a " moriamur pro rege nostro ! " would have ring 
 in your infant reign , - if you have make such a speech as the squire 's ! 




 BOOK FOURTH . 




 INITIAL chapter . 

 COMPRISING MR . CAXTON 's opinion on the MATRIMONIAL state , support BY 
 LEARNED AUTHORITIES . 

 " it be no bad idea of your , Pisistratus , " say my father , graciously , 
 " to depict the heightened affection and the serious intention of Signor 
 Riccabocca by a single stroke , - he leave of his spectacle ! good . " 

 " yet , " quoth my uncle , " I think Shakspeare represent a lover as fall 
 into slovenly habit , neglect his person , and suffer his hose to 
 be ungartered , rather than pay that attention to his outer man which 
 induce Signor Riccabocca to leave off his spectacle , and look as 
 handsome as nature will permit he . " 

 " there be different degree and many phase of the passion , " reply 
 my father . " Shakspeare be speak of an ill - treat , pining , woe - begone 
 lover , much aggrieve by the cruelty of his mistress , - a lover who have 
 find it of no avail to smarten himself up , and have fall despondently 
 into the opposite extreme . whereas Signor Riccabocca have nothing to 
 complain of in the barbarity of Miss Jemima . " 

 " indeed he have not ! " cry Blanche , toss her head , - " forward 
 creature ! " 

 " yes , my dear , " say my mother , try she good to look stately , " I be 
 decidedly of opinion that , in that respect , Pisistratus have lower the 
 dignity of the sex . not intentionally , " add my mother , mildly , and 
 afraid she have say something too bitter ; " but it be very hard for a man 
 to describe we woman . " 

 the captain nod approvingly ; Mr. Squills smile ; my father quietly 
 resume the thread of his discourse . 

 " to continue , " quoth he . " Riccabocca have no reason to despair of success 
 in his suit , nor any object in move his mistress to compassion . he 
 may , therefore , very properly tie up his garter and leave off his 
 spectacle . what do you say , Mr. Squills ? - for , after all , since 
 love - making can not fail to be a great constitutional derangement , the 
 experience of a medical man must be the good to consult . " 

 " Mr. Caxton , " reply Squills , obviously flatter , " you be quite 
 right : when a man make love , the organ of self - esteem and desire 
 of applause be greatly stimulate , and therefore , of course , he set 
 himself off to the good advantage . it be only , as you observe , when , 
 like Shakspeare 's lover , he have give up make love as a bad job , and 
 have receive that severe hit on the ganglion which the cruelty of a 
 mistress inflict , that he neglect his personal appearance : he neglect 
 it , not because he be in love , but because his nervous system be 
 depressed . that be the cause , if you remember , with poor Major Prim . 
 he wear his wig all awry when Susan Smart jilt he ; but I set it right 
 for he . " 

 " by sham Miss Smart into repentance , or get he a new 
 sweetheart ? " ask my uncle . 

 " Pooh ! " answer Squills , " by quinine and cold bathing . " 

 " we may therefore grant , " renew my father , " that , as a general rule , 
 the process of courtship tend to the spruceness , and even foppery , of 
 the individual engage in the experiment , as Voltaire have very prettily 
 prove somewhere . Nay , the Mexicans , indeed , be of opinion that the 
 lady at least ought to continue those care of her person even after 
 marriage . there be extant , in Sahagun 's ' history of New Spain , ' the 
 advice of an aztec or mexican mother to her daughter , in which she say , 
 ' that your husband may not take you in dislike , adorn yourself , wash 
 yourself , and let your garment be clean . ' it be true that the good lady 
 add , ' do it in moderation ; since if every day you be wash yourself 
 and your clothe , the world will say that you be over - delicate ; and 
 particular people will call you - TAPETZON TINEMAXOCH ! ' what those word 
 precisely mean , " add my father , modestly , " I can not say , since I 
 never have the opportunity to acquire the ancient aztec language , - but 
 something very opprobrious and horrible , no doubt . " 

 " I dare say a philosopher like Signor Riccabocca , " say my uncle , " be 
 not himself very tapetzon tine - what d ' ye call it ? - and a good healthy 
 english wife , that poor affectionate Jemima , be throw away upon he . " 

 " Roland , " say my father , " you do not   like foreigner ; a respectable 
 prejudice , and quite natural in a man who have be try his good to 
 hew they in piece and blow they up into splinter . but you do not   like 
 philosopher either , - and for that dislike you have no equally good 
 reason . " 

 " I only imply that they be not much addicted to soap and water , " say 
 my uncle . 

 " a notable mistake . many great philosopher have be very great beaux . 
 Aristotle be a notorious fop . Buffon put on his well laced ruffle 
 when he sit down to write , which imply that he wash his hand first . 
 Pythagoras insist greatly on the holiness of frequent ablution ; and 
 Horace - who , in his own way , be as good a philosopher as any the Romans 
 produce - take care to let we know what a neat , well - dress , dapper 
 little gentleman he be . but I do not   think you ever read the ' apology ' 
 of Apuleius ? " 

 " not I ; what be it about ? " ask the captain . 

 " about a great many thing . it be that Sage 's vindication from several 
 malignant charge , - amongst other , and principally indeed , that of 
 be much too refined and effeminate for a philosopher . nothing 
 can exceed the rhetorical skill with which he excuse himself for 
 use - tooth - powder . ' ought a philosopher , ' he exclaim , ' to allow 
 anything unclean about he , especially in the mouth , - the mouth , which 
 be the vestibule of the soul , the gate of discourse , the portico of 
 think ! ah , but AEmilianus   the accuser of Apuleius   never open 
 his mouth but for slander and calumny , - tooth - powder would indeed be 
 unbecoming to he ! or , if he use any , it will not be my good arabian 
 tooth powder , but charcoal and cinder . ay , his tooth should be as foul 
 as his language ! and yet even the crocodile like to have his tooth 
 clean ; insect get into they , and , horrible reptile though he be , 
 he open his jaw inoffensively to a faithful dentistical bird , who 
 volunteer his beak for a toothpick . ' " 

 my father be now warm in the subject he have start , and soar 
 mile away from Riccabocca and " my novel . " " and observe , " he 
 exclaim , - " observe with what gravity this eminent Platonist plead 
 guilty to the charge of have a mirror . ' why , what , ' he exclaim , ' more 
 worthy of the regard of a human creature than his own image ' nihil 
 respectabilius homini quam formam suam ! be not that one of our child 
 the most dear to we who be call ' the picture of his father ' ? but take 
 what pain you will with a picture , it can never be so like you as 
 the face in your mirror ! think it discreditable to look with proper 
 attention on one 's self in the glass ! do not Socrates recommend such 
 attention to his disciple , - do he not make a great moral agent of 
 the speculum ? the handsome , in admire their beauty therein , be 
 admonish that handsome be who handsome do ; and the more the ugly 
 star at themselves , the more they become naturally anxious to hide the 
 disgrace of their feature in the loveliness of their merit . be not 
 demosthene always at his speculum ? do he not rehearse his cause 
 before it as before a master in the art ? he learn his eloquence from 
 Plato , his dialectics from eubulide ; but as for his delivery - there , he 
 come to the mirror ! 

 " therefore , " conclude Mr. Caxton , return unexpectedly to the 
 subject , - " therefore , it be no reason to suppose that Dr. Riccabocca 
 be averse to cleanliness and decent care of the person because he be a 
 philosopher ; and , all thing consider , he never show himself more a 
 philosopher than when he leave off his spectacle and look his good . " 

 " well , " say my mother , kindly , " I only hope it may turn out happily . 
 but I should have be well pleased if Pisistratus have not make Dr. 
 Riccabocca so reluctant a wooer . " 

 " very true , " say the captain ; " the Italian do not shine as a lover . 
 throw a little more fire into he , Pisistratus , - something gallant and 
 chivalrous . " 

 " Fire ! gallantry ! chivalry ! " cry my father , who have take Riccabocca 
 under his special protection ; " why , do not   you see that the man 
 be describe as a philosopher ? - and I should like to know when a 
 philosopher ever plunge into matrimony without considerable misgiving 
 and cold shiver ! indeed , it seem that - perhaps before he be a 
 philosopher - Riccabocca have try the experiment , and know what it 
 be . why , even that plain - speak , sensible , practical man , Metellus 
 Numidicus , who be not even a philosopher , but only a roman censor , 
 thus express himself in an exhortation to the people to perpetrate 
 matrimony : ' if , o Quirites , we could do without wife , we should all 
 dispense with that subject of care ea molestia careremus ; but since 
 nature have so manage it that we can not live with woman comfortably , nor 
 without they at all , let we rather provide for the human race than our 
 own temporary felicity . ' " 

 here the lady set up such a cry of indignation , that both Roland and 
 myself endeavour to appease their wrath by hasty assurance that we 
 utterly repudiate the damnable doctrine of Metellus Numidicus . 

 my father , wholly unmove , as soon as a sullen silence be establish , 
 recommence . " do not think , lady , " say he , " that you be without 
 advocate at that day : there be many Romans gallant enough to blame 
 the censor for a mode of express himself which they hold to be 
 equally impolite and injudicious . ' surely , ' say they , with some 
 plausibility , if Numidicus wish man to marry , he need not have 
 refer so peremptorily to the disquietude of the connection , and thus 
 have make they more inclined to turn away from matrimony than give they 
 a relish for it . ' but against these critic one honest man   whose name 
 of Titus Castricius should not be forget by posterity   maintain 
 that Metellus Numidicus could not have speak more properly ; ' for 
 remark , ' say he , ' that Metellus be a censor , not a rhetorician . it 
 become rhetoricians to adorn and disguise and make the good of thing ; 
 but Metellus , sanctus vir , - a holy and blameless man , grave and sincere 
 to wit , and address the roman people in the solemn capacity of 
 Censor , - be bind to speak the plain truth , especially as he be 
 treat of a subject on which the observation of every day , and the 
 experience of every life , could not leave the least doubt upon the mind 
 of his audience . ' still , Riccabocca , have decide to marry , have no 
 doubt prepare himself to bear all the concomitant evil - as become a 
 profess sage ; and I own I admire the art with which Pisistratus have 
 draw the kind of woman most likely to suit a philosopher - " 

 Pisistratus bow , and look round complacently ; but recoil from two 
 very peevish and discontent face feminine . 

 MR . CAXTON   complete his sentence . - " not only as regard mildness 
 of temper and other household qualification , but as regard the 
 very person of the object of his choice . for you evidently remember , 
 Pisistratus , the reply of Bias , when ask his opinion on marriage : 
  long sentence in Greek " 

 Pisistratus try to look as if he have the opinion of Bias by heart , and 
 nod acquiescingly . 

 MR . CAXTON . - " that be , my dear , ' the woman you would marry be either 
 handsome or ugly : if handsome , she be koine , - namely , you do not   have 
 she to yourself ; if ugly , she be poine , - that is , a fury . ' but , as it 
 be observe in Aulus Gellius   whence I borrow this citation , there be a 
 wide interval between handsome and ugly . and thus Ennius , in his tragedy 
 of ' Menalippus , ' use an admirable expression to designate woman of the 
 proper degree of matrimonial comeliness , such as a philosopher would 
 select . he call this degree stata forma , - a rational , mediocre sort of 
 beauty , which be not liable to be either koine or poine . and Favorinus , 
 who be a remarkably sensible man , and come from Provence - the male 
 inhabitant of which district have always value themselves on their 
 knowledge of love and lady - call this say stata forma the beauty of 
 wife , - the uxorial beauty . Ennius say that woman of a stata forma be 
 almost always safe and modest . now , Jemima , you observe , be describe as 
 possess this stata forma ; and it be the nicety of your observation in 
 this respect , which I like the most in the whole of your description of 
 a philosopher 's matrimonial courtship , Pisistratus   except only the 
 stroke of the spectacle , for it show that you have properly consider 
 the opinion of Bias , and master all the counter logic suggest in 
 Book v. , chapter xi . , of Aulus Gellius . " 

 " for all that , " say Blanche , half archly , half demurely , with a smile 
 in the eye and a pout of the lip , " I do not   remember that Pisistratus , in 
 the day when he wish to be most complimentary , ever assure I that I 
 have a stata forma , - a rational , mediocre sort of beauty . " 

 " and I think , " observe my uncle , " that when he come to his real 
 heroine , whoever she may be , he will not trouble his head much about 
 either Bias or Aulus Gellius . " 




 CHAPTER II . 

 Matrimony be certainly a great change in life . one be astonish not to 
 find a notable alteration in one 's friend , even if he or she have be 
 only wed a week . in the instance of Dr. and Mrs. Riccabocca the 
 change be peculiarly visible . to speak first of the lady , as in 
 chivalry bind , Mrs. Riccabocca have entirely renounce that melancholy 
 which have characterize Miss Jemima ; she become even sprightly and gay , 
 and look all the well and pretty for the alteration . she do not 
 scruple to confess honestly to Mrs. Dale that she be now of opinion 
 that the world be very far from approach its end . but , in the 
 meanwhile , she do not neglect the duty which the belief she have 
 abandon serf to inculcate , - " she set her house in order . " the cold 
 and penurious elegance that have characterize the casino disappear 
 like enchantment , - that is , the elegance remain , but the cold and 
 penury flee before the smile of woman . like Puss - in - Boots , after 
 the nuptial of his master , Jackeymo only now catch minnow and 
 stickleback for his own amusement . Jackeymo look much plumper , and 
 so do Riccabocca . in a word , the fair Jemima become an excellent wife . 
 Riccabocca secretly think her extravagant , but , like a wise man , 
 decline to look at the house bill , and eat his joint in unreproachful 
 silence . 

 indeed there be so much unaffected kindness in the nature of Mrs. 
 Riccabocca - beneath the quiet of her manner there beat so genially 
 the heart of the Hazeldeans - that she fairly justify the favourable 
 anticipation of Mrs. Dale . and though the doctor do not noisily 
 boast of his felicity , nor , as some new married folk do , thrust it 
 insultingly under the nimis unctis naribus , - the turn - up nose of 
 your surly old married folk , - nor force it gaudily and glaringly on 
 the envious eye of the single , you might still see that he be a more 
 cheerful and light - hearted man than before . his smile be less 
 ironical , his politeness less distant . he do not study Machiavelli so 
 intensely , - and he do not return to the spectacle ; which last be an 
 excellent sign . moreover , the humanizing influence of the tidy English 
 wife might be see in the improvement of his outward or artificial man . 
 his clothe seem to fit he well ; indeed , the clothe be new . Mrs. 
 Dale no long remark that the button be off the wristband , which 
 be a great satisfaction to she . but the sage still remain faithful to 
 the pipe , the cloak , and the red silk umbrella . Mrs. Riccabocca have   to 
 her credit be it speak   use all become and wife - like art against 
 these three remnant of the old bachelor , Adam , but in vain . " Anima 
 mia , "   soul of mine   - say the doctor , tenderly , " I hold the cloak , the 
 umbrella , and the pipe as the sole relic that remain to I of my native 
 country . respect and spare they . " 

 Mrs. Riccabocca be touch , and have the good sense to perceive that 
 man , let he be ever so much married , retain certain sign of his 
 ancient independence , - certain token of his old identity , which a wife , 
 the most despotic , will do well to concede . she concede the cloak , 
 she submit to the umbrella , she overcome her abhorrence of the pipe . 
 after all , consider the natural villany of our sex , she confess to 
 herself that she might have be bad off . but through all the calm 
 and cheerfulness of Riccabocca , a nervous perturbation be sufficiently 
 perceptible ; it commence after the second week of marriage ; it go on 
 increase , till one bright sunny afternoon , as he be stand on his 
 terrace , gaze down upon the road , at which Jackeymo be place , lo , a 
 stage - coach stop ! the doctor make a bound , and put both hand to his 
 heart as if he have be shoot ; he then leap over the balustrade , and 
 his wife from her window behold he fly down the hill , with his long 
 hair stream in the wind , till the tree hide he from her sight . 

 " ah , " think she , with a natural pang of conjugal jealousy , " henceforth 
 I be only second in his home . he have go to welcome his child ! " and at 
 that reflection Mrs. Riccabocca shed tear . 

 but so naturally amiable be she , that she hasten to curb her emotion , 
 and efface as well as she could the trace of a stepmother 's grief . when 
 this be do , and a silent , self - rebuke prayer murmur over , the 
 good woman descend the stair with alacrity , and summon up her good 
 smile , emerge on the terrace . 

 she be repay ; for scarcely have she come into the open air , when two 
 little arm be throw around she , and the sweetest voice that ever 
 come from a child 's lip sigh out in break English , " good mamma , love 
 I a little . " 

 " love you ? with my whole heart ! " cry the stepmother , with all a 
 mother 's honest passion . and she clasp the child to her breast . 

 " God bless you , my wife ! " say Riccabocca , in a husky tone . 

 " please take this too , " add Jackeymo , in Italian , as well as his sob 
 would let he , and he break off a great bough full of blossom from his 
 favourite orange - tree , and thrust it into his mistress 's hand . she have 
 not the slight notion what he mean by it ! 




 CHAPTER III . 

 Violante be indeed a bewitching child , - a child to whom I defy Mrs. 
 Caudle herself   immortal Mrs. Caudle !   to have be a harsh stepmother . 

 look at she now , as release from those kindly arm , she stand , still 
 cling with one hand to her new mamma , and hold out the other to 
 Riccabocca , with those large dark eye swim in happy tear . what a 
 lovely smile ! what an ingenuous , candid brow ! she look delicate , she 
 evidently require care , she want the mother . and rare be the woman 
 who would not love she the well for that ! still , what an innocent , 
 infantine bloom in those clear , smooth cheek ! and in that slight frame , 
 what exquisite natural grace ! 

 " and this , I suppose , be your nurse , darling ? " say Mrs. Riccabocca , 
 observe a dark , foreign - look woman , dress very strangely , 
 without cap or bonnet , but a great silver arrow stick in her hair , and a 
 filigree chain or necklace rest upon her kerchief . 

 " ah , good Annetta , " say Violante , in Italian . " Papa , she say she be to 
 go back ; but she be not to go back , be she ? " 

 Riccabocca , who have scarcely before notice the woman , start at that 
 question , exchange a rapid glance with Jackeymo , and then , mutter 
 some inaudible excuse , approach the nurse , and , beckon she to 
 follow he , go away into the ground . he do not return for more than 
 an hour , nor do the woman then accompany he home . he say briefly to 
 his wife that the nurse be oblige to return at once to Italy , and that 
 she would stay in the village to catch the mail ; that indeed she would 
 be of no use in their establishment , as she could not speak a word 
 of English ; that he be sadly afraid Violante would pine for she . and 
 Violante do pine at first . but still , to a child it be so great a thing 
 to find a parent , to be at home , that , tender and grateful as Violante 
 be , she could not be inconsolable while her father be there to 
 comfort . 

 for the first few day , Riccabocca scarcely permit any one to be with 
 his daughter but himself . he would not even leave she alone with 
 his Jemima . they walk out together , - sit together for hour in the 
 belvidere . then by degree he begin to resign she more and more to 
 Jemima 's care and tuition , especially in English , of which language at 
 present she speak only a few sentence   previously , perhaps , learn by 
 heart   so as to be clearly intelligible . 




 CHAPTER IV . 

 there be one person in the establishment of Dr. Riccabocca who be 
 satisfied neither with the marriage of his master nor the arrival of 
 Violante , - and that be our friend Lenny Fairfield . previous to the 
 all - absorb duty of courtship , the young peasant have secure a very 
 large share of Riccabocca 's attention . the sage have feel interest in the 
 growth of this rude intelligence struggle up to light . but what with 
 the wooing and what with the wedding , Lenny Fairfield have sink very 
 much out of his artificial position as pupil into his natural station 
 of under - gardener . and on the arrival of Violante , he see , with natural 
 bitterness , that he be clean forget , not only by Riccabocca , but 
 almost by Jackeymo . it be true that the master still lend he book , 
 and the servant still give he lecture on horticulture . but Riccabocca 
 have no time nor inclination now to amuse himself with enlighten that 
 tumult of conjecture which the book create . and if Jackeymo have be 
 covetous of those mine of gold bury beneath the acre now fairly 
 take from the squire   and good - naturedly add rent - free , as an aid to 
 Jemima 's dower , before the advent of the young lady whose future dowry 
 the produce be to swell , now that she be actually under the eye of 
 the faithful servant , such a stimulus be give to his industry that he 
 could think of nothing else but the land , and the revolution he design 
 to effect in its natural english crop . the garden , save only the 
 orangetree , be abandon entirely to Lenny , and additional labourer 
 be call in for the field work . Jackeymo have discover that one part 
 of the soil be suit to lavender , that another would grow camomile . he 
 have in his heart apportion a beautiful field of rich loam to flax ; 
 but against the growth of flax the squire set his face obstinately . 
 that most lucrative , perhaps , of all crop when soil and skill suit , be 
 formerly attempt in England much more commonly than it be now , since 
 you will find few old lease do not contain a clause prohibitory of 
 flax as an impoverishment of the land . and though Jackeymo learnedly 
 endeavour to prove to the squire that the flax itself contain 
 particle which , if return to the soil , repay all that the crop take 
 away , Mr. Hazeldean have his old - fashioned prejudice on the matter , 
 which be insuperable . " my forefather , " quoth he , " do not put that 
 clause in their lease without good cause ; and as the casino land be 
 entail on Frank , I have no right to gratify your foreign whim at his 
 expense . " 

 to make up for the loss of the flax , Jackeymo resolve to convert a very 
 nice bit of pasture into orchard ground , which he calculate would bring 
 in L10 net per acre by the time Miss Violante be marriageable . at this 
 the squire pishe a little ; but as it be quite clear that the land 
 would be all the more valuable hereafter for the fruit - tree , he 
 consent to permit the " grass - land " to be thus partially break up . 

 all these change leave poor Lenny Fairfield very much to himself , - at 
 a time when the new and strange device which the initiation into 
 book knowledge create make it most desirable that he should have the 
 constant guidance of a superior mind . 

 one evening after his work , as Lenny be return to his mother 's 
 cottage , very sullen and very moody , he suddenly come in contact with 
 Sprott the tinker . 




 CHAPTER V. 

 the tinker be seat under a hedge , hammer away at an old kettle , 
 with a little fire burn in front of he , and the donkey hard by , 
 indulge in a placid doze . Mr. Sprott look up as Lenny pass , nod 
 kindly , and say , - 

 " Good evenin ' , Lenny : glad to hear you be so ' spectably sitivate with 
 Mounseer . "

 CHAPTER VI . 

 as Violante become more familiar with her new home , and those around 
 she become more familiar with Violante , she be remark for a certain 
 stateliness of manner and bearing , which , have it be less evidently 
 natural and inborn , would have seem misplace in the daughter of

 CHAPTER VII . 

 now from that day the humble Lenny and the regal Violante become great 
 friend . with what pride he teach she to distinguish between celery and 
 weed , - and how proud too be she when she learn that she be useful ! 
 there be not a great pleasure you can give child , especially female 
 child , than to make they feel they be already of value in the world , 
 and serviceable as well as protect . week and month roll away , and 
 Lenny still read , not only the book lend he by the doctor , but those 
 he buy of Mr. Sprott . as for the bomb and shell against religion 
 which the tinker carry in his bag , Lenny be not induce to blow 
 himself up with they . he have be rear from his cradle in simple love 
 and reverence for the Divine Father , and the tender Saviour , whose life 
 beyond all record of human goodness , whose death beyond all epic of 
 mortal heroism , no be whose infancy have be teach to supplicate 
 the merciful and adore the Holy , yea , even though his later life may be 
 entangle amidst the thorn of some desolate pyrrhonism , can ever hear 
 revile and scoff without a shock to the conscience and a revolt of 
 the heart . as the deer recoil by instinct from the tiger , as the very 
 look of the scorpion deter you from handle it , though you never see 
 a scorpion before , so the very first line in some ribald profanity on 
 which the tinker put his black finger make Lenny 's blood run cold . safe , 
 too , be the peasant boy from any temptation in work of a gross and 
 licentious nature , not only because of the happy ignorance of his rural 
 life , but because of a more enduring safeguard , - genius ! Genius , 
 that , manly , robust , healthful as it be , be long before it lose its 
 instinctive dorian modesty ; shameface , because so susceptible to 
 glory , - genius , that love indeed to dream , but on the violet bank , not 
 the dunghill . wherefore , even in the error of the sens , it seek to 
 escape from the sensual into world of fancy , subtle and refine . but 
 apart from the passion , true genius be the most practical of all human 
 gift . like the Apollo , whom the Greek worship as its type , even 
 Arcady be its exile , not its home . soon weary of the dalliance of Tempe , 
 it ascend to its mission , - the Archer of the silver bow , the guide of 
 the car of light . speak more plainly , genius be the enthusiasm for 
 self - improvement ; it cease or sleep the moment it desist from seek 
 some object which it believe of value , and by that object it insensibly 
 connect its self - improvement with the positive advance of the world . 
 at present Lenny 's genius have no bias that be not to the Positive 
 and useful . it take the direction natural to its sphere , and the want 
 therein , - namely , to the art which we call mechanical . he want to 
 know about steam - engine and artesian well ; and to know about they it 
 be necessary to know something of mechanic and hydrostatic ; so he 
 buy popular elementary work on those mystic science , and set all 
 the power of his mind at work on experiment . 

 noble and generous spirit be ye , who , with small care for fame , and 
 little reward from pelf , have open to the intellect of the poor the 
 portal of wisdom ! I honour and revere ye ; only do not think ye have 
 do all that be needful . consider , I pray ye , whether so good a choice 
 from the tinker 's bag would have be make by a boy whom religion 
 have not scar from the Pestilent , and genius have not lead to the 
 self - improve . and Lenny do not wholly escape from the mephitic 
 portion of the motley element from which his awakening mind draw its 
 nurture . think not it be all pure oxygen that the pant lip draw in . 
 no ; there be still those inflammatory tract . political I do not like 
 to call they , for politic mean the art of government , and the tract I 
 speak of assailed all government which mankind have hitherto recognize . 
 sad rubbish , perhaps , be such tract to you , o sound thinker , in your 
 easy - chair ! or to you , practise statesman , at your post on the Treasury 
 Bench ; to you , calm dignitary of a learn Church ; or to you , my lord 
 judge , who may often have send from your bar to the dire Orcus 
 of Norfolk 's Isle the ghost of man whom that rubbish , fall 
 simultaneously on the bump of acquisitiveness and combativeness , hath 
 untimely slain ! sad rubbish to you ! but seem it such rubbish to the 
 poor man , to whom it promise a paradise on the easy term of upset 
 a world ? for , ye see , those " Appeals to Operatives " represent that 
 same world - upsetting as the simple thing imaginable , - a sort of 
 two - and - two - make - four proposition . the poor have only get to set 
 their strong hand to the axle , and heave - a - boy ! and hurrah for 
 the topsy - turvy ! then just to put a little wholesome rage into the 
 heave - a - hoy ! it be so facile to accompany the eloquence of " Appeals " 
 with a kind of stir - the - bile - up statistic , - " abuse of the 
 aristocracy , " " job of the priesthood , " " expense of the Army keep up 
 for Peers ' young son , " " war contract for the villanous purpose of 
 raise the rent of the landowner , " - all arithmetically dish up , 
 and season with tale of every gentleman who have commit a misdeed , 
 every clergyman who have dishonour his cloth ; as if such instance be 
 fair specimen of average gentleman and minister of religion ! all this , 
 passionately advanced   and , observe , never answer , for that literature 
 admit no controversialist , and the writer have it all his own way , 
 may be rubbish ; but it be out of such rubbish that operative build 
 barricade for attack , and legislator prison for defence . 

 our poor friend Lenny draw plenty of this stuff from the tinker 's 
 bag . he think it very clever and very eloquent ; and he suppose the 
 statistic be as true as mathematical demonstration . 

 a famous knowledge - diffuser be look over my shoulder , and tell I , 
 " increase education , and cheapen good book , and all this rubbish will 
 disappear ! " Sir , I do not   believe a word of it . if you print Ricardo 
 and Adam Smith at a farthe a volume , I still believe that they would 
 be as little read by the operative as they be nowadays by a very large 
 proportion of highly - cultivate man . I still believe that , while the 
 press work , attack on the rich and proposition for heave - a - hoy will 
 always form a popular portion of the Literature of Labour . there 's Lenny 
 Fairfield read a treatise on hydraulic , and construct a model for 
 a fountain into the bargain ; but that do not prevent his acquiescence

 certainly never agree to pay , and which he be tell make sugar and tea 
 so shamefully dear . no . I tell you what do a little counteract those 
 eloquent incentive to break his own head against the strong wall of

 be not always employ in reading . and have be tell in print that 
 master be tyrant , parson hypocrite or drone in the hive , and 
 landowner vampire and bloodsucker , he look out into the little world 
 around he , and , first , he be compel to acknowledge that his master 
 be not a tyrant   perhaps because he be a foreigner and a philosopher , 
 and , for what I and Lenny know , a republican . but then Parson Dale , 
 though High Church to the marrow , be neither hypocrite nor drone . he 
 have a very good living , it be true , - much well than he ought to have , 
 accord to the " political " opinion of those tract ! but Lenny be 
 oblige to confess that if Parson Dale be a penny the poor , he would 
 do a pennyworth 's less good ; and compare one parish with another , such 
 as Rood Hall and Hazeldean , he be dimly aware that there be no great 
 CIVILIZER than a parson tolerably well off . then , too , Squire Hazeldean , 
 though as arrant a tory as ever stand upon shoe - leather , be certainly 
 not a vampire nor blood sucker . he do not feed on the public ; a 
 great many of the public feed upon he : and , therefore , his practical 
 experience a little stagger and perplex Lenny Fairfield as to

 landowner ! have , at the risk of all popularity , just give a coup de 
 patte to certain sage extremely the fashion at present , I be not go 
 to let you off without an admonitory flea in the ear . do not   suppose that 
 any mere scribbling and typework will suffice to answer the scribble 
 and typework set at work to demolish you , - write down that rubbish you 
 can not ; live it down you may . if you be rich , like Squire Hazeldean , do 
 good with your money ; if you be poor , like Signor Riccabocca , do good 
 with your kindness . 

 see ! there be Lenny now receive his week 's wage ; and though Lenny 
 know that he can get high wage in the very next parish , his blue

 Lenny will see the tinker as he go home , and he will buy a most

 very little , will serve to neutralize the effect of that " appeal " much
