 CHAPTER VIII . 

 Spring have come again ; and one beautiful May day , Leonard Fairfield sit 
 beside the little fountain which he have now actually construct in the 
 garden . the butterfly be hover over the belt of flower which 
 he have place around his fountain , and the bird be sing overhead . 
 Leonard Fairfield be rest from his day 's work , to enjoy his 
 abstemious dinner , beside the cool play of the sparkling water , and , 
 with the yet keener appetite of knowledge , he devour his book as he 
 munch his crust . 

 a penny tract be the shoeing - horn of literature ! it draw on a great 
 many book , and some too tight to be very useful in walk . the penny 
 tract quote a celebrate writer - you long to read he ; it prop a 
 startling assertion by a grave authority - you long to refer to it . 
 during the night of the past winter , Leonard 's intelligence have 
 make vast progress ; he have teach himself more than the element of 
 mechanic , and put to practice the principle he have acquire not only 
 in the hydraulical achievement of the fountain , nor in the still 
 more notable application of science , commence on the stream in which 
 Jackeymo have fish for minnow , and which Lenny have divert to the 
 purpose of irrigate two field , but in various ingenious contrivance 
 for the facilitation or abridgment of labour , which have excite great 
 wonder and praise in the neighbourhood . on the other hand , those rabid 
 little tract , which deal so summarily with the destiny of the 
 human race , even when his grow reason and the perusal of work 
 more classical or more logical have lead he to perceive that they be 
 illiterate , and to suspect that they jump from premise to conclusion 
 with a celerity very different from the careful ratiocination of 
 mechanical science , have still , in the citation and reference wherewith 
 they abound , lure he on to philosopher more specious and more 
 perilous . out of the tinker 's bag he have draw a translation of 
 Condorcet 's " Progress of Man " and another of Rousseau 's " Social 
 Contract . " work so eloquent have induce he to select from the tract 
 in the tinker 's miscellany those which abound most in profession of 
 philanthropy , and prediction of some come Golden Age , to which old 
 Saturn 's be a joke , - tract so mild and mother - like in their language , 
 that it require a much more practical experience than Lenny 's to 
 perceive that you would have to pass a river of blood before you have 
 the slight chance of set foot on the flowery bank on which they 
 invite you to repose ; tract which rouge poor Christianity on the 
 cheek , clap a crown of innocent daffodillie on her head , and 
 set she to dance a pas de zephyr in the pastoral ballet in which 
 Saint - Simon pipe to the flock he shear ; or have first lay it down 
 as a preliminary axiom that - 

  " the cloud - cap tower , the gorgeous palace , 
  the solemn temple , the great globe itself , - 
  Yea , all which it inherit , shall dissolve , " 

 substitute in place thereof M. Fourier 's symmetrical phalanstere , or 
 Mr. Owen 's architectural parallelogram . it be with some such tract 
 that Lenny be season his crust and his radish , when Riccabocca , 
 bend his long dark face over the student 's shoulder , say abruptly , - 

 " Diavolo , my friend ! what on earth have you get there ? just let I look 
 at it , will you ? " 

 Leonard rise respectfully , and colour deeply as he surrender the 
 tract to Riccabocca . 

 the wise man read the first page attentively , the second more cursorily , 
 and only run his eye over the rest . he have go through too vast a 
 range of problem political , not to have pass over that venerable 
 Pons Asinorum of Socialism , on which Fouriers and Saint - Simons sit 
 straddling , and cry aloud that they have arrive at the last boundary of 
 knowledge ! 

 " all this be as old as the hill , " quoth Riccabocca , irreverently ; " but 
 the hill stand still , and this - there it go ! " and the sage point to 
 a cloud emit from his pipe . " do you ever read Sir David Brewster on 
 Optical Delusions ? no ! well , I will lend it to you . you will find therein 
 a story of a lady who always see a black cat on her hearth - rug . the 
 black cat exist only in her fancy , but the hallucination be natural 
 and reasonable , - eh , what do you think ? " 

 " why , sir , " say Leonard , not catch the Italian 's meaning , " I do not 
 exactly see that it be natural and reasonable . " 

 " foolish boy , yes ! because black cat be thing possible and know . 
 but who ever see upon earth a community of man such as sit on the 
 hearth - rug of Messrs. Owen and Fourier ? if the lady 's hallucination be 
 not reasonable , what be his who believe in such vision as these ? " 

 Leonard bite his lip . 

 " my dear boy , " cry Riccabocca , kindly , " the only thing sure and 
 tangible to which these writer would lead you lie at the first step , 
 and that be what be commonly call a revolution . now , I know what that 
 be . I have go , not indeed through a revolution , but an attempt at 
 one . " 

 Leonard raise his eye towards his master with a look of profound 
 respect and great curiosity . 

 " yes , " add Riccabocca , and the face on which the boy gaze exchange 
 its usual grotesque and sardonic expression for one animate , noble , and 
 heroic . " yes , not a revolution for chimeras , but for that cause which 
 the cold allow to be good , and which , when successful , all time 
 approve as divine , - the redemption of our native soil from the rule 
 of the foreigner ! I have share in such an attempt . and , " continue the 
 Italian , mournfully , " recall now all the evil passion it arouse , 
 all the tie it dissolve , all the blood that it command to flow , all 
 the healthful industry it arrest , all the madman that it arm , all the 
 victim that it dupe , I question whether one man really honest , pure , 
 and humane , who have once go through such an ordeal , would ever hazard 
 it again , unless he be assure that the victory be certain , - ay , and 
 the object for which he fight not to be wrest from his hand amidst 
 the uproar of the element that the battle have release . " 

 the Italian pause , shade his brow with his hand , and remain long 
 silent . then , gradually resume his ordinary tone , he continue , - 

 " revolution that have no definite object make clear by the positive 
 experience of history ; revolution , in a word , that aim less at 
 substitute one law or one dynasty for another , than at change the 
 whole scheme of society , have be little attempt by real statesman . 
 even Lycurgus be prove to be a myth who never exist . such organic 
 change be but in the day - dream of philosopher who live apart from 
 the actual world , and whose opinion   though generally they be very 
 benevolent , good sort of man , and write in an elegant poetical style 
 one would no more take on a plain matter of life , than one would look 
 upon Virgil 's Eclogues as a faithful picture of the ordinary pain and 
 pleasure of the peasant who tend our sheep . read they as you would 
 read poet , and they be delightful . but attempt to shape the world 
 accord to the poetry , and fit yourself for a madhouse . the far 
 off the age be from the realization of such project , the more these 
 poor philosopher have indulge they . thus , it be amidst the sad 
 corruption of court manner that it become the fashion in Paris to sit 
 for one 's picture with a crook in one 's hand , as Alexis or Daphne . just 
 as liberty be fast die out of Greece , and the successor of Alexander 
 be found their monarchy , and Rome be grow up to crush in its 
 iron grasp all state save its own , Plato withdraw his eye from the 
 world , to open they in his dreamy " Atlantis . " just in the grim 
 period of english history , with the axe hang over his head , Sir 
 Thomas more give you his " Utopia . " just when the world be to be the 
 theatre of a new Sesostris , the sage of France tell you that the age be 
 too enlightened for war , that man be henceforth to be govern by pure 
 reason , and live in a paradise . very pretty read all this to a man 
 like I , Lenny , who can admire and smile at it . but to you , to the man 
 who have to work for his living , to the man who think it would be so 
 much more pleasant to live at his ease in a phalanstere than to work 
 eight or ten hour a day ; to the man of talent and action and industry , 
 whose future be invest in that tranquillity and order of a state 
 in which talent and action and industry be a certain capital , - why , 
 Messrs. Coutts , the great banker , have well encourage a theory to 
 upset the system of banking ! whatever disturb society , yea , even by a 
 causeless panic , much more by an actual struggle , fall first upon the 
 market of labour , and thence affect prejudicially every department 
 of intelligence . in such time the art be arrest ; literature be 
 neglect ; people be too busy to read anything save appeal to their 
 passion . and capital , shake in its sense of security , no long 
 venture boldly through the land , call forth all the energy of toil 
 and enterprise , and extend to every workman his reward . now , Lenny , 
 take this piece of advice . you be young , clever , and aspiring : man 
 rarely succeed in change the world ; but a man seldom fail of success 
 if he let the world alone , and resolve to make the good of it . you 
 be in the midst of the great crisis of your life ; it be the struggle 
 between the new desire knowledge excite , and that sense of poverty 
 which those desire convert either into hope and emulation , or into envy 
 and despair . I grant that it be an up - hill work that lie before you ; 
 but do not   you think it be always easy to climb a mountain than it be 
 to level it ? these book call on you to level the mountain ; and that 
 mountain be the property of other people , subdivide amongst a great 
 many proprietor , and protect by law . at the first stroke of the 
 pickaxe , it be ten to one but what you be take up for a trespass . but 
 the path up the mountain be a right of way uncontested . you may be safe 
 at the summit , before   even if the owner be fool enough to let you 
 you could have level a yard . cospetto ! " quoth the doctor , " it be more 
 than two thousand year ago since poor Plato begin to level it , and the 
 mountain be as high as ever ! " 

 thus say , Riccabocca come to the end of his pipe , and stalk 
 thoughtfully away , he leave Leonard Fairfield try to extract light 
 from the smoke . 




 CHAPTER IX . 

 shortly after this discourse of Riccabocca 's , an incident occur to 
 Leonard that serve to carry his mind into new direction . one evening , 
 when his mother be out , he be at work on a new mechanical contrivance , 
 and have the misfortune to break one of the instrument which he 
 employ . now it will be remember that his father have be the 
 squire 's head carpenter : the widow have carefully hoard the tool 
 of his craft , which have belong to her poor Mark ; and though she 
 occasionally lend they to Leonard , she would not give they up to his 
 service . amongst these Leonard know that he should find the one that he 
 want ; and be much interested in his contrivance , he could not wait 
 till his mother 's return . the tool , with other little relie of the 
 lose , be keep in a large trunk in Mrs. Fairfield 's sleepingroom ; 
 the trunk be not lock , and Leonard go to it with out ceremony or 
 scruple . in rummage for the instrument his eye fall upon a bundle of 
 manuscript ; and he suddenly recollect that when he be a mere child , 
 and before he much know the difference between verse and prose , his 
 mother have point to these manuscript , and say , " one day or other , 
 when you can read nicely , I will let you look at these , Lenny . my poor 
 Mark write such verse - ah , he be a schollard ! " Leonard , reasonably 
 enough , think that the time have now arrive when he be worthy the 
 privilege of read the paternal effusion , and he take forth the 
 manuscript with a keen but melancholy interest . he recognize his 
 father 's handwriting , which he have often see before in account - book 
 and memorandum , and read eagerly some trifling poem , which do not show 
 much genius , nor much mastery of language and rhythm , - such poem , in 
 short , as a self - educate man , with poetic taste and feel rather than 
 poetic inspiration or artistic culture , might compose with credit , but 
 not for fame . but suddenly , as he turn over these " occasional 
 Pieces , " Leonard come to other in a different handwriting , - a woman 's 
 handwriting , small and fine and exquisitely form . he have scarcely read 
 six line of these last , before his attention be irresistibly chain . 
 they be of a different order of merit from poor Mark 's ; they bear the 
 unmistakable stamp of genius . like the poetry of woman in general , they 
 be devoted to personal feeling , - they be not the mirror of a world , 
 but reflection of a solitary heart . yet this be the kind of poetry most 
 pleasing to the young . and the verse in question have another attraction 
 for Leonard : they seem to express some struggle akin to his own , - some 
 complaint against the actual condition of the writer 's life , some sweet 
 melodious murmur at fortune . for the rest , they be characterize by a 
 vein of sentiment so elevated , that , if write by a man , it would have 
 run into exaggeration ; write by a woman , the romance be carry off 
 by so many genuine revelation of sincere , deep , pathetic feeling , that 
 it be always natural , though true to a nature for which you would not 
 augur happiness . 

 Leonard be still absorb in the perusal of these poem when Mrs. 
 Fairfield enter the room . 

 " what have you be about , Lenny , - search in my box ? " 

 " I come to look for my father 's bag of tool , Mother , and I find these 
 paper , which you say I might read some day . " 

 " I do not   wonder you do not hear I when I come in , " say the widow , 
 sigh . " I use to sit still for the hour together , when my poor Mark 
 read his poem to I . there be such a pretty one about the ' Peasant 's 
 Fireside , ' Lenny , - have you get hold of that ? " 

 " yes , dear mother ; and I remark the allusion to you : it bring tear 
 to my eye . but these verse be not my father 's ; whose be they ? they 
 seem in a woman 's handwriting . " 

 Mrs. Fairfield look , change colour , grow faint and seat herself . 

 " poor , poor Nora ! " say she , falteringly . " I do not know as they be 
 there ; Mark kep ' them ; they get among his - " 

 LEONARD . - " who be Nora ? " 

 MRS . FAIRFIELD . - " who ? - child - who ? Nora be - be my own - own sister . " 

 LEONARD   in great amaze , contrast his ideal of the writer of these 
 musical line , in that graceful hand , with his homely uneducated mother , 
 who could neither read nor write . - " your sister ! be it possible ! my 
 aunt , then . how come it you never speak of she before ? oh , you should 
 be so proud of she , Mother ! " 

 MRS . FAIRFIELD   clasp her hand . - " we be proud of she , all of 
 we , - father , mother , all ! she be so beautiful and so good , and not 
 proud she ! though she look like the first lady in the land . oh , Nora , 
 Nora ! " 

 LEONARD   after a pause . - " but she must have be highly educate ? " 

 MRS . FAIRFIELD . - " ' Deed she be ! " 

 LEONARD . - " how be that ? " 

 MRS . FAIRFIELD   rock herself to and fro in her chair . - " oh , my Lady 
 be her godmother , - Lady Lansmere I mean , - and take a fancy to she when 
 she be that high , and have she to stay at the Park , and wait on her 
 ladyship ; and then she put she to school , and Nora be so clever that 
 nothing would do but she must go to London as a governess . but do not 
 talk of it , boy ! do not   talk of it ! " 

 LEONARD . - " why not , Mother ? what have become of she ; where be she ? " 

 MRS . FAIRFIELD   burst into a paroxysm of tear . - " in her grave , - in 
 her cold grave ! dead , dead ! " 

 Leonard be inexpressibly grieve and shock . it be the attribute of 
 the poet to seem always live , always a friend . Leonard feel as if some 
 one very dear have be suddenly tear from his heart . he try to console 
 his mother ; but her emotion be contagious , and he weep with she . 

 " and how long have she be dead ? " he ask at last , in mournful accent . 

 " Many be the long year , many ; but , " add Mrs. Fairfield , rise , and 
 put her tremulous hand on Leonard 's shoulder , " you will just never 
 talk to I about she ; I can not   bear it , it break my heart . I can bear 
 well to talk of Mark ; come downstairs , - come . " 

 " may I not keep these verse , Mother ? do let I . " 

 " well , well , those bit o ' paper be all she leave behind she , - yes , keep 
 they , but put back Mark 's . be they all here , - sure ? " and the widow , 
 though she could not read her husband 's verse , look jealously at the 
 manuscript write in his irregular , large scrawl , and , smooth they 
 carefully , replace they in the trunk , and resettle over they some 
 sprig of lavender , which Leonard have unwittingly disturb . 

 " but , " say Leonard , as his eye again rest on the beautiful 
 handwriting of his lost aunt , - " but you call she Nora - I see she sign 
 herself L. " 

 " Leonora be her name . I say she be my Lady 's god - child . we call she 
 Nora for short - " 

 " Leonora - and I be Leonard - be that how I come by the name ? " 

 " yes , yes ; do hold your tongue , boy , " sob poor Mrs. Fairfield ; 
 and she could not be soothe nor coax into continue or renew a 
 subject which be evidently associate with insupportable pain . 




 CHAPTER X. 

 it be difficult to exaggerate the effect that this discovery produce 
 on Leonard 's train of thought . some one belong to his own humble race 
 have , then , precede he in his struggle flight towards the lofty 
 region of Intelligence and Desire . it be like the mariner amidst 
 unknown sea , who find carve upon some desert isle a familiar 
 household name . 

 and this creature of genius and of sorrow - whose existence he have only 
 learn by her song , and whose death create , in the simple heart of 
 her sister , so passionate a grief , after the lapse of so many 
 year - supply to the romance awake in his young heart the ideal 
 which it unconsciously seek . he be pleased to hear that she have be 
 beautiful and good . he pause from his book to muse on she , and picture 
 her image to his fancy . that there be some mystery in her fate be 
 evident to he ; and while that conviction deepen his interest , the 
 mystery itself by degree take a charm which he be not anxious to 
 dispel . he resign himself to Mrs. Fairfield 's obstinate silence . he 
 be content to rank the dead amongst those holy and ineffable image 
 which we do not seek to unveil . Youth and Fancy have many secret hoard 
 of idea which they do not desire to impart , even to those most in their 
 confidence . I doubt the depth of feeling in any man who have not certain 
 recess in his soul into which none may enter . 

 Hitherto , as I have say , the talent of Leonard Fairfield have be 
 more turn to thing positive than to the ideal , - to science and 
 investigation of fact than to poetry , and that airier truth in which 
 poetry have its element . he have read our great poet , indeed , but 
 without thought of imitating ; and rather from the general curiosity 
 to inspect all celebrate monument of the human mind than from that 
 especial predilection for verse which be too common in childhood and 
 youth to be any sure sign of a poet . but now these melody , unknown to 
 all the world beside , ring in his ear , mingle with his thought , - set , 
 as it be , his whole life to music . he read poetry with a different 
 sentiment , - it seem to he that he have discover its secret . and so 
 reading , the passion seize he , and " the number come . " 

 to many mind , at the commencement of our grave and earnest pilgrimage , 
 I be vandal enough to think that the indulgence of poetic taste and 
 revery do great and lasting harm ; that it serve to enervate the 
 character , give false idea of life , impart the semblance of drudgery 
 to the noble toil and duty of the active man . all poetry would not do 
 this , - not , for instance , the Classical , in its diviner master ; not the 
 poetry of Homer , of Virgil , of Sophocles ; not , perhaps , even that of 
 the indolent horace . but the poetry which youth usually love and 
 appreciate the good - the poetry of mere sentiment - do so in mind 
 already over - predispose to the sentimental , and which require brace 
 to grow into healthful manhood . 

 on the other hand , even this latter kind of poetry , which be peculiarly 
 modern , do suit many mind of another mould , - mind which our modern 
 life , with its hard positive form , tend to produce . and as in certain 
 climate plant and herb , peculiarly adapt as antidote to those 
 disease most prevalent in the atmosphere , be profusely sown , as it 
 be , by the benignant providence of Nature , so it may be that the 
 soft and more romantic specie of poetry , which come forth in 
 harsh , money - making , unromantic time , be intend as curative and 
 counter - poison . the world be so much with we , nowadays , that we need 
 have something that prate to we , albeit even in too fine a euphuism , of 
 the moon and star . 

 Certes , to Leonard Fairfield , at that period of his intellectual life , 
 the softness of our Helicon descend as healing dew . in his turbulent 
 and unsettled ambition , in his vague grapple with the giant form of 
 political truth , in his bias towards the application of science to 
 immediate practical purpose , this lovely vision of the Muse come in the 
 white robe of the Peacemaker ; and with upraised hand pointing to serene 
 sky , she open to he fair glimpse of the Beautiful , which be give 
 to Peasant as to Prince , - show to he that on the surface of earth 
 there be something noble than fortune , that he who can view the world 
 as a poet be always at soul a king ; while to practical purpose itself , 
 that large and more profound invention , which poetry stimulate , 
 supply the grand design and the subtle view , - lead he beyond the 
 mere ingenuity of the mechanic , and habituate he to regard the inert 
 force of the matter at his command with the ambition of the Discoverer . 
 but , above all , the discontent that be within he find a vent , not 
 in deliberate war upon this actual world , but through the purifying 
 channel of song , in the vent itself it evaporate , it be lose . by 
 accustom ourselves to survey all thing with the spirit that retain 
 and reproduce they only in their lovelier or grander aspect , a vast 
 philosophy of toleration for what we before gaze on with scorn or 
 hate insensibly grow upon we . Leonard look into his heart after the 
 Enchantress have breathe upon it ; and through the mist of the fleeting 
 and tender melancholy which betray where she have be , he behold a new 
 sun of delight and joy dawn over the landscape of human life . 

 thus , though she be dead and go from his actual knowledge , this 
 mysterious kinswoman - " a voice , and nothing more " - have speak to he , 
 soothe , elevated , cheer , attune each discord into harmony ; and if 
 now permit from some serener sphere to behold the life that her soul 
 thus strangely influence , verily with yet holier joy the saving and 
 lovely spirit might have glide onward in the Eternal Progress . 

 we call the large majority of human life obscure . presumptuous that we 
 be ! how know we what live a single thought retain from the dust of 
 nameless graf may have light to renown ? 




 CHAPTER XI . 

 it be about a year after Leonard 's discovery of the family manuscript 
 that Parson Dale borrow the quiet pad - mare in the squire 's stable , 
 and set out on an equestrian excursion . he say that he be bind on 
 business connect with his old parishioner of Lansmere ; for , as it have 
 be incidentally imply in a previous chapter , he have be connect 
 with that borough town   and , I may here add , in the capacity of curate 
 before he have be induct into the living of Hazeldean . 

 it be so rarely that the parson stir from home , that this journey 
 to a town more than twenty mile off be regard as a most daring 
 adventure , both at the Hall and at the Parsonage . Mrs. Dale could not 
 sleep the whole previous night with thinking of it ; and though she have 
 naturally one of her bad nervous headache on the eventful morn , 
 she yet suffer no hand less thoughtful than her own to pack up the 
 saddle - bag which the parson have borrow along with the pad . Nay , so 
 distrustful be she of the possibility of the good man 's exert the 
 slight common - sense in her absence , that she keep he close at 
 her side while she be engage in that same operation of 
 packing - up , - show he the exact spot in which the clean shirt be 
 put ; and how nicely the old slipper be pack up in one of his 
 own sermon . she implore he not to mistake the sandwich for his 
 shaving - soap , and make he observe how carefully she have provide 
 against such confusion , by place they as far apart from each other as 
 the nature of saddle - bag will admit . the poor parson - who be really 
 by no mean an absent man , but as little likely to shave himself with 
 sandwich and lunch upon soap as the most commonplace mortal may 
 be - listen with conjugal patience , and think that man never have such 
 a wife before ; nor be it without tear in his own eye that he tear 
 himself from the farewell embrace of his weeping Carry . 

 I confess , however , that it be with some apprehension that he set 
 his foot in the stirrup , and trust his person to the mercy of 
 an unfamiliar animal . for , whatever might be Mr. Dale 's minor 
 accomplishment as man and parson , horsemanship be not his forte . 
 indeed , I doubt if he have take the rein in his hand more than twice 
 since he have be marry . 

 the squire 's surly old groom , Mat , be in attendance with the pad ; and , 
 to the parson 's gentle inquiry whether Mat be quite sure that the pad 
 be quite safe , reply laconically , " Oi , oi ; give she her head . " 

 " give she her head ! " repeat Mr. Dale , rather amazed , for he have not 
 the slight intention of take away that part of the beast 's frame , 
 so essential to its vital economy , - " give she her head ! " 

 " Oi , oi ; and do not   jerk she up like that , or she will fall a doince on 
 her hind - leg . " 

 the parson instantly slacken the rein ; and Mrs. Dale - who have tarry 
 behind to control her tear - now run to the door for " more last 
 word , " he wave his hand with courageous amenity , and amble forth into 
 the lane . 

 our equestrian be absorb at first in study the idiosyncrasy of 
 the pad - mare , and try thereby to arrive at some notion of her general 
 character : guess , for instance , why she raise one ear and lay down 
 the other ; why she keep bear so close to the left that she brush 
 his leg against the hedge ; and why , when she arrive at a little 
 side - gate in the field , which lead towards the home - farm , she come to a 
 full stop , and fall to rub her nose against the rail , - an occupation 
 from which the parson , find all civil remonstrance in vain , at 
 length divert she by a timorous application of the whip . 

 this crisis on the road fairly pass , the pad seem to comprehend that 
 she have a journey before she , and give a petulant whisk of her tail , 
 quicken her amble into a short trot , which soon bring the parson 
 into the high road , and nearly opposite the Casino . 

 here , sit on the gate which lead to his abode , and shade by his 
 umbrella , he behold Dr. Riccabocca . 

 the Italian lift his eye from the book he be read , and star 
 hard at the parson ; and he - not venture to withdraw his whole 
 attention from the pad   who , indeed , set up both her ear at the 
 apparition of Riccabocca , and evince symptom of that surprise and 
 superstitious repugnance at unknown object which go by the name of 
 ' shy "   - look askance at Riccabocca . 

 " do not   stir , please , " say the parson , " or I fear you will alarm the 
 creature ; it seem a nervous , timid thing ; - soho , gently , gently . " 

 and he fall to pat the mare with great unction . 

 the pad , thus encouraged , overcome her first natural astonishment at the 
 sight of Riccabocca and the red umbrella ; and have before be at the 
 Casino on sundry occasion , and sagaciously prefer place within the 
 range of her experience to bourn neither cognate nor conjecturable , she 
 move gravely up towards the gate on which the italian sit ; and , 
 after eye he a moment , - as much as to say , " I wish you would get 
 off , " - come to a deadlock . 

 " well , " say Riccabocca , ' since your horse seem more disposed to be 
 polite to I than yourself , Mr. Dale , I take the opportunity of your 
 present involuntary pause to congratulate you on your elevation in life , 
 and to breathe a friendly prayer that pride may not have a fall ! " 

 " tut , " say the parson , affect an easy air , though still 
 contemplate the pad , who appear to have fall into a quiet doze , 
 " it be true that I have not ride much of late year , and the squire 's 
 horse be very high - fed and spirit ; but there be no more harm in they 
 than their master when one once know their way . " 

  " ' chi va piano va sano , 
  E chi va sano va lontano , ' " 

 say Riccabocca , point to the saddle - bag . " you go slowly , therefore 
 safely ; and he who go safely may go far . you seem prepared for a 
 journey ? " 

 " I be , " say the parson ; " and on a matter that concern you a little . " 

 " I ! " exclaim Riccabocca , - " concern I ! " 

 " yes , so far as the chance of deprive you of a servant whom you like 
 and esteem affect you . " 

 " oh , " say Riccabocca , " I understand : you have hint to I very often 
 that I or Knowledge , or both together , have unfit Leonard Fairfield 
 for service . " 

 " I do not say that exactly ; I say that you have fit he for 
 something high than service . but do not repeat this to he . and I 
 can not yet say more to you , for I be very doubtful as to the success 
 of my mission ; and it will not do to unsettle poor Leonard until we be 
 sure that we can improve his condition . " 

 " of that you can never be sure , " quoth the wise man , shake his head ; 
 " and I can not   say that I be unselfish enough not to bear you a grudge for 
 seek to decoy away from I an invaluable servant , - faithful , steady , 
 intelligent , and "   add Riccabocca , warming as he approach the 
 climacteric adjective   " exceedingly cheap ! nevertheless go , and Heaven 
 speed you . I be not an Alexander , to stand between man and the sun . " 

 " you be a noble , great - hearted creature , Signor Riccabocca , in spite of 
 your cold - blooded proverb and villanous book . " the parson , as he say 
 this , bring down the whiphand with so indiscreet an enthusiasm on the 
 pad 's shoulder , that the poor beast , startle out of her innocent doze , 
 make a bolt forward , which nearly precipitate Riccabocca from his seat 
 on the stile , and then turn round - as the parson tug desperately 
 at the rein - catch the bit between her tooth , and set off at a canter . 
 the parson lose both his stirrup ; and when he regain they   as the 
 pad slacken her pace , and have time to breathe and look about he , 
 Riccabocca and the Casino be both out of sight . 

 " certainly , " quoth Parson Dale , as he resettle himself with great 
 complacency , and a conscious triumph that he be still on the pad 's 
 back , - " certainly it be true ' that the noble conquest ever make by 
 man be that of the horse : ' a fine creature it be , - a very fine 
 creature , - and uncommonly difficult to sit on , especially without 
 stirrup . " firmly in his stirrup the parson plant his foot ; and the 
 heart within he be very proud . 




 CHAPTER XII . 

 the borough town of Lansmere be situate in the county adjoining 
 that which contain the village of Hazeldean . late at noon the parson 
 cross the little stream which divide the two shire , and come to an 
 inn , which be place at an angle , where the great main road branch 
 off into two direction , the one lead towards Lansmere , the other 
 go more direct to London . at this inn the pad stop , and put down 
 both ear with the air of a pad who have make up her mind to bait . and 
 the parson himself , feel very warm and somewhat sore , say to the 
 pad , benignly , " it be just , - thou shalt have corn and water ! " 

 Dismounting , therefore , and find himself very stiff as soon as he 
 reach terra firma , the parson consign the pad to the hostler , and 
 walk into the sanded parlour of the inn , to repose himself on a very 
 hard Windsor chair . 

 he have be alone rather more than half - an - hour , read a county 
 newspaper which smell much of tobacco , and try to keep off the 
 fly that gather round he in swarm , as if they have never before 
 see a parson , and be anxious to ascertain how the flesh of he 
 taste , - when a stagecoach stop at the inn . a traveller get out with 
 his carpetbag in his hand , and be show into the sanded parlour . 

 the parson rise politely , and make a bow . 

 the traveller touch his hat , without take it off , look at Mr. 
 Dale from top to toe , then walk to the window , and whistle a lively , 
 impatient tune , then stride towards the fireplace and ring the bell ; 
 then star again at the parson ; and that gentleman have courteously 
 lay down the newspaper , the traveller seize it , throw himself into a 
 chair , fling one of his leg over the table , toss the other up on the 
 mantelpiece , and begin read the paper , while he tilt the chair on 
 its hind - leg with so dare a disregard to the ordinary position of 
 chair and their occupant , that the shudder parson expect every 
 moment to see he come down on the back of his skull . 

 move , therefore , to compassion , Mr. Dale say mildly , - " those chair 
 be very treacherous , sir . I be afraid you will be down . " 

 " eh , " say the traveller , look up much astonish . " eh , down ? - oh , 
 you be satirical , sir . " 

 " satirical , sir ? upon my word , no ! " exclaim the parson , earnestly . 

 " I think every freeborn man have a right to sit as he please in his 
 own house , " resume the traveller , with warmth ; " and an inn be his own 
 house , I guess , so long as he pay his score . Betty , my dear . " 

 for the chambermaid have now reply to the bell . " I han't   Betty , sir ; do 
 you want she ? " 

 " no , Sally ; cold brandy and water - and a biscuit . " 

 " I han't   Sally , either , " mutter the chambermaid ; but the traveller , 
 turn round , show so smart a neckcloth and so comely a face , that 
 she smile , colour , and go her way . 

 the traveller now rise , and fling down the paper . he take out a 
 penknife , and begin par his nail . suddenly desist from this 
 elegant occupation , his eye catch sight of the parson 's shovel - hat , 
 which lie on a chair in the corner . 

 " you be a clergyman , I reckon , sir , " say the traveller , with a slight 
 sneer . 

 again Mr. Dale bow , - bow in part deprecatingly , in part with 
 dignity . it be a bow that say , " no offence , sir , but I be a clergyman , 
 and I be not ashamed of it . " 

 " go far ? " ask the traveller . 

 parson . - " not very . " 

 TRAVELLER . - " in a chaise or fly ? if so , and we be go the same way , 
 half . " 

 parson . - " Halves ? " 

 TRAVELLER . - " yes , I will pay half the damage , pike inclusive . " 

 parson . - " you be very good , sir . but "   speak with pride   " I be on 
 horseback . " 

 TRAVELLER . - " on horseback ! well , I should not have guess that ! you 
 do not   look like it . where do you say you be go ? " 

 " I do not say where I be go , sir , " say the parson , dryly , for he 
 be much offend at that vague and ungrammatical remark applicable to 
 his horsemanship , that " he do not look like it . " 

 " close ! " say the traveller , laughing ; " an old traveller , I reckon . " 

 the parson make no reply , but he take up his shovel - hat , and , with a bow 
 more majestic than the previous one , walk out to see if his pad have 
 finish her corn . 

 the animal have indeed finish all the corn afford to she , which be 
 not much , and in a few minute more Mr. Dale resume his journey . he have 
 perform about three mile , when the sound of wheel behind he make 
 he turn his head ; and he perceive a chaise drive very fast , while out 
 of the window thereof dangle strangely a pair of human leg . the pad 
 begin to curvet as the post - horse rattle behind , and the parson have 
 only an indistinct vision of a human face supplant those human leg . 
 the traveller peer out at he as he whirl by , - see Mr. Dale toss 
 up and down on the saddle , and cry out , " how be the leather ? " 

 " leather ! " soliloquize the parson , as the pad recompose herself , " what 
 do he mean by that ? leather ! a very vulgar man . but I get rid of he 
 cleverly . " 

 Mr. Dale arrive without further adventure at Lansmere . he put up at 
 the principal inn , refresh himself by a general ablution , and sit down 
 with good appetite to his beefsteak and pint of port . 

 the parson be a well judge of the physiognomy of man than that of the 
 horse ; and after a satisfactory glance at the civil smirk landlord , 
 who remove the cover and set on the wine , he venture on an attempt at 
 conversation . " be my Lord at the Park ? " 

 LANDLORD   still more civilly than before . - " no , sir , his Lordship and 
 my Lady have go to town to meet Lord L'Estrange ! " 

 " Lord L'Estrange ! he be in England , then ? " 

 " why , so I hear , " reply the landlord , " but we never see he here now . 
 I remember he a very pretty young man . every one be fond of he and 
 proud of he . but what prank be do play when he be a lad ! we hop 
 he would come in for our boro ' some of these day , but he have take to 
 foren part , - more be the pity . I be a reg'lar Blue , sir , as I ought to 
 be . the blue candidate always do I the honour to come to the Lansmere 
 arm . ' t be only the low party put up with the Boar , " add the 
 landlord , with a look of ineffable disgust . " I hope you like the wine , 
 sir ? " 

 " very good , and seem old . " 

 " bottled these eighteen year , sir . I have in the cask for the great 
 election of Dashmore and Egerton . I have little left of it , and I never 
 give it but to old friend like , - for , I think , Sir , though you be grow 
 stout , and look more grand , I may say that I have   have the pleasure of 
 see you before . " 

 " that be true , I dare say , though I fear I be never a very good 
 customer . " 

 " ah , it be Mr. Dale , then ! I think so when you come into the hall . I 
 hope your lady be quite well , and the squire too ; fine pleasant - speak 
 gentleman ; no fault of his if Mr. Egerton go wrong . well , we have 
 never see he - I mean Mr. Egerton - since that time . I do not   wonder he 
 stay away ; but my Lord 's son , who be bring up here , it an't   nat'ral 
 like that he should turn his back on we ! " 

 Mr. Dale make no reply , and the landlord be about to retire , when the 
 parson , pour out another glass of the port , say , " there must be 
 great change in the parish . be Mr. Morgan , the medical man , still 
 here ? " 

 " no , indeed ! he take out his ' ploma after you leave , and become a real 
 doctor ; and a pretty practice he have too , when he take , all of a 
 sudden , to some new - fangled way of physicking , - I think they call it 
 homy - something . " 

 " Homoeopathy ? " 

 " that be it ; something against all reason : and so he lose his practice 
 here and go up to Lunnun . I have   not hear of he since . " 

 " do the Avenels still reside in their old house ? " 

 " oh , yes ! - and be pretty well off , I hear say . John be always poorly , 
 though he still go now and then to the Odd Fellows , and take his 
 glass ; but his wife come and fetch he away before he can do himself 
 any harm . " 

 " Mrs. Avenel be the same as ever ? " 

 " she hold her head higher , I think , " say the landlord , smile . " she 
 be always - not exactly proud like , but what I call bumptious . " 

 " I never hear that word before , " say the parson , lay down his 
 knife and fork . " bumptious indeed , though I believe it be not in the 
 dictionary , have creep into familiar parlance , especially amongst young 
 folk at school and college . " 

 " bumptious be bumptious , and gumptious be bumptious , " say the landlord , 
 delight to puzzle a parson . " now the town beadle be bumptious , and 
 Mrs. Avenel be bumptious . " 

 " she be a very respectable woman , " say Mr. Dale , somewhat rebukingly . 

 " in course , sir , all gumptious folk be ; they value themselves on their 
 respectability , and look down on their neighbour . " 

 parson   still philologically occupy . - " gumptious - gumptious . I think 
 I remember the substantive at school , - not that my master teach it to 
 I . ' gumption ' - it mean cleverness . "

 know ; but when I say that sum ' un be gumptious , I mean - though that be

 take I , sir ? " 

 " I think I do , " say the parson , half smile . " I believe the Avenels

 " thank you , I have   din . " 

 the parson put on his hat and sally forth into the street . he eye

 see they ; and take in this card . "

 " missis say it be late , sir ; but walk in . "

 " of which I apprise Mr. Avenel by letter . "

 " a poor creature ! " say John , feebly , and as if in compassion of

 Mr. Dale be much touch . he have remember John Avenel the comely ,

 that " now , sir , " have in its sound something sinister and warlike . this

 CHAPTER xiii . 

 Mr. Dale have be more than a quarter of an hour converse with Mrs. 
 Avenel , and have seemingly make little progress in the object of his 
 diplomatic mission , for now , slowly draw on his glove , he say , - 

 " I grieve to think , Mrs. Avenel , that you should have so harden 
 your heart - yes , you must pardon I , - it be my vocation to speak stern 
 truth . you can not say that I have not keep faith with you , but I must 
 now invite you to remember that I specially reserved to myself the 
 right of exercise a discretion to act as I judge well for the child 's 
 interest on any future occasion ; and it be upon this understanding that 
 you give I the promise , which you would now evade , of provide for he 
 when he come to manhood . " 

 " I say I will provide for he . I say that you may ' prentice he in any 
 distant town , and by and by we will stock a shop for he . what would

 be not   reasonable what you ask , sir . "

 grandson should succeed in life , and do you credit . now , I doubt very

 " heaven forbid ! some of the first man in England have be the son of 
 small shopkeeper . but be it a crime in they , or in their parent ,

 " good ! " say , or rather grunt , an approve voice , but neither Mrs. 
 Avenel nor the parson hear it .

 to the University - where be the money to come from ? "

 be great at a small college at Cambridge ; and if you will pay half the

 can afford it . "

 yet still not graciously . " but the money be not the only point . "

 income on which he could maintain himself until he make his way in life . 
 come , Mrs. Avenel , you be well off ; you have no relation near to you

 " Sir , " say - Mrs. Avenel , interrupt the parson , " it be not because

 " why ? I do not   see that . " 

 " why ! " exclaim Mrs. Avenel , fiercely , - " why ! you , know why . no , I

 during the last two or three minute , and just before that approve

 CHAPTER XIV . 

 the parson join Mr. Richard Avenel on the road . it be a fine night , 
 and the moon clear and shin . 

 " so , then , " say Mr. Richard , thoughtfully , " poor Jane , who be always 
 the drudge of the family , have contrive to bring up her son well ; and 
 the boy be really what you say , eh , - could make a figure at college ? " 

 " I be sure of it , " say the parson , hook himself on to the arm which 
 Mr. Avenel proffer . 

 " I should like to see he , " say Richard . " have he any manner ? be he 
 genteel , or a mere country lout ? " 

 " indeed , he speak with so much propriety , and have so much modest 
 dignity about he , that there be many a rich gentleman who would be proud 
 of such a son . " 

 " it be odd , " observe Richard , " what a difference there be in family . 
 there be Jane , now , who can not   read nor write , and be just fit to be a 
 workman 's wife , have not a thought above her station ; and when I think of 
 my poor sister Nora - you would not believe it , sir , but she be the 
 most elegant creature in the world , - yes , even as a child   she be but 
 a child when I go off to America . and often , as I be get on in

 after all . ' poor thing - but she die young . " Richard 's voice grow husky . 

 the parson kindly press the arm on which he lean , and say , after a 
 pause , - 

 " nothing refine we like education , sir . I believe your sister Nora have 
 receive much instruction , and have the talent to profit by it : it be 
 the same with your nephew . " 

 " I will see he , " say Richard , stamp his foot firmly on the ground ,

 Mr. - what be your name , sir ? " 

 " Dale . " 

 " Mr. Dale , look you , I be a single man . perhaps I may marry some day ; 
 perhaps I sha ' not . I be not go to throw myself away . if I can get 
 a lady of quality , why - but that be neither here nor there ; meanwhile I 
 should be glad of a nephew whom I need not be ashamed of . you see , sir , 
 I be a new man , the builder of my own fortune ; and though I have pick 
 up a little education - I do not   well know how , - as I scramble on still , 
 now I come back to the old country , I be well aware that I ' m not 
 exactly a match for those d - -d aristocrat ; do not   show so well in a 
 drawing - room as I could wish . I could be a parliament man if I like , 
 but I might make a goose of myself ; so , all thing consider , if I can 
 get a sort of junior partner to do the polite work , and show off 
 the good , I think the house of Avenel    Co. might become a pretty 
 considerable honour to the Britishers . you understand I , sir ? " 

 " oh , very well , " answer Mr. Dale , smile , though rather gravely . 

 " now , " continue the New Man , " I be not ashamed to have rise in life by 
 my own merit ; and I do not   disguise what I have   be . and , when I be in my 
 own grand house , I be fond of say , ' I land at New York with L10 in 
 my purse , and here I be ! ' but it would not do to have the old folk with 
 I . People take you with all your fault if you be rich ; but they will not 
 swallow your family into the bargain . so if I do not   have at my house 
 my own father and mother , whom I love dearly , and should like to see 
 sit at table , with my servant behind their chair , I could still 
 less have sister Jane . I recollect she very well , but she can not   have get 
 genteeler as she be grow old . therefore I beg you will not set she on 
 come after I ! it would not do by any manner of mean . do not   say a 
 word about I to she . but send the boy down here to his grandfather , and 
 I will see he quietly , you understand . " 

 " yes , but it will be hard to separate she from the boy . " 

 " Stuff ! all boy be separate from their parent when they go into the 
 world . so that be settle . now , just tell I . I know the old folk always 
 snub Jane , - that be , Mother do . my poor dear father never snub 
 any of we . perhaps Mother have not behave altogether well to Jane . but 
 we must not blame she for that ; you see this be how it happen . there 
 be a good many of we , while Father and Mother keep shop in the High 
 Street , so we be all to be provide for anyhow ; and Jane , be very 
 useful and handy at work , get a place when she be a little girl , and 
 have no time for learn . afterwards my father make a lucky hit , in 
 get my Lord Lansmere 's custom after an election , in which he do 
 a great deal for the Blues   for he be a famous electioneerer , my poor 
 father . my Lady stand godmother to Nora ; and then all my brother , and 
 two of my sister , die off , and Father retire from business ; and when 
 he take Jane from service , she be so common - like that Mother could not 
 help contrast she with Nora . you see Jane be their child when they 
 be poor little shop - people , with their head scarce above water ; 
 and Nora be their child when they be well off , and have retire from 
 trade , and live genteel : so that make a great difference . and mother 
 do not quite look on she as on her own child . but it be Jane 's own 
 fault : for Mother would have make it up with she if she have marry the 
 son of our neighbour the great linen - draper , as she might have do ; 
 but she would take Mark Fairfield , a common carpenter . parent like good 
 those of their child who succeed well in life . natural . why , they do 
 not care for I till I come back the man I be . but to return to Jane : 
 I be afraid they have   neglect she . how be she off ? " 

 " she earn her livelihood , and be poor , but content . " 

 " ah , just be good enough to give she this "   and Richard take a bank - note 
 of L50 from his pocket - book . 

 " you can say the old folk send it to she ; or that it be a present from 
 Dick , without tell she he have come back from America . " 

 " my dear sir , " say the parson , " I be more and more thankful to have 
 make your acquaintance . this be a very liberal gift of your ; but your 
 good plan will be to send it through your mother . for , though I do not 
 want to betray any confidence you place in I , I should not know what to 
 answer if Mrs. Fairfield begin to question I about her brother . I never 
 have but one secret to keep , and I hope I shall never have another . a 
 secret be very like a lie ! " 

 " you have a secret then ? " say Richard , as he take back the bank - note . he 
 have learn , perhaps in America , to be a very inquisitive man . he add 
 point - blank , " pray , what be it ? " 

 " why , what it would not be if I tell you , " say the parson , with a 
 force laugh , - " a secret ! " 

 " well , I guess we be in a land of liberty . do as you like . now , I dare 
 say you think I a very odd fellow to come out of my shell to you in 
 this off - hand way ; but I like the look of you , even when we be at the 
 inn together . and just now I be uncommonly pleased to find that , 
 though you be a parson , you do not   want to keep a man 's nose down to 
 a shopboard , if he have anything in he . you be not one of the 
 aristocrat - " 

 " indeed , " say the parson , with imprudent warmth , " it be not the 
 character of the aristocracy of this country to keep people down . they 
 make way amongst themselves for any man , whatever his birth , who have the 
 talent and energy to aspire to their level . that be the especial boast of 
 the british constitution , sir ! " 

 " oh , you think so , do you ? " say Mr. Richard , look sourly at the 
 parson . " I dare say those be the opinion in which you have bring 
 up the lad . just keep he yourself and let the aristocracy provide for 
 he ! " 

 the parson 's generous and patriotic warmth evaporate at once , at this 
 sudden inlet of cold air into the conversation . he perceive that he have 
 make a terrible blunder ; and as it be not his business at that moment 
 to vindicate the british constitution , but to serve Leonard Fairfield , 
 he abandon the cause of the aristocracy with the most poltroon 
 and scandalous abruptness . catch at the arm which Mr. Avenel have 
 withdraw from he , he exclaim , - 

 " indeed , sir , you be mistaken ; I have never attempt to influence your 
 nephew 's political opinion . on the contrary , if , at his age , he can be 
 say to have form any opinion , I be greatly afraid - that be , I think 
 his opinion be by no means sound - that be , constitutional . I mean , 
 I mean - " and the poor parson , anxious to select a word that would not 
 offend his listener , stop short in lamentable confusion of idea . 

 Mr. Avenel enjoy his distress for a moment , with a saturnine smile , 
 and then say , - 

 " well , I calculate he be a Radical . natural enough , if he have not get a 
 sixpence to lose - all come right by and by . I be not a Radical , - at least 
 not a Destructive - much too clever a man for that , I hope . but I wish 
 to see thing very different from what they be . do not   fancy that I want 
 the common people , who have   get nothing , to pretend to dictate to their 
 better , because I hate to see a parcel of fellow who be call lord 
 and squire try to rule the roast . I think , sir , that it be man like 
 I who ought to be at the top of the tree ! and that be the long and the 
 short of it . what do you say ? " 

 " I have   not the least objection , " say the crestfallen parson , basely . 
 but , to do he justice , I must add that he do not the least know what 
 he be say ! 




 CHAPTER XV . 

 Unconscious of the change in his fate which the diplomacy of the parson 
 seek to effect , Leonard Fairfield be enjoy the first virgin 
 sweetness of fame ; for the principal town in his neighbourhood have 
 follow the then grow fashion of the age , and set up a Mechanics ' 
 Institute , and some worthy person interested in the formation of that 
 provincial Athenaeum have offer a prize for the good essay on the 
 Diffusion of Knowledge , - a very trite subject , on which person seem to 
 think they can never say too much , and on which there be , nevertheless , 
 a great deal yet to be say . this prize Leonard Fairfield have recently 
 win . his Essay have be publicly compliment by a full meeting of the 
 Institute ; it have be print at the expense of the Society , and have 
 be reward by a silver medal , - delineative of Apollo crown Merit 
  poor Merit have not a rag to his back ; but Merit , leave only to the care 
 of Apollo , never be too good a customer to the tailor !   and the County 
 Gazette have declare that Britain have produce another prodigy in the 
 person of Dr. Riccabocca 's self - educate gardener . 

 attention be now direct to Leonard 's mechanical contrivance . the 
 squire , ever eagerly bent on improvement , have bring an engineer 
 to inspect the lad 's system of irrigation , and the engineer have 
 be greatly strike by the simple mean by which a very considerable 
 technical difficulty have be overcome . the neighbour farmer now 
 call Leonard " Mr. Fairfield , " and invite he on equal term to their 
 house . Mr. Stirn have meet he on the high road , touch his hat , and 
 hop that " he bear no malice . " all this , I say , be the first sweetness 
 of fame ; and if Leonard Fairfield come to be a great man , he will 
 never find such sweet in the after fruit . it be this success which have 
 determine the parson on the step which he have just take , and which he 
 have long before anxiously meditate . for , during the last year or so , 
 he have renew his old intimacy with the widow and the boy ; and he 
 have notice , with great hope and great fear , the rapid growth of 
 an intellect , which now stand out from the lowly circumstance that 
 surround it in bold and unharmonize relief . 

 it be the evening after his return home that the parson stroll up to 
 the Casino . he put Leonard Fairfield 's Prize Essay in his pocket ; for he 
 feel that he could not let the young man go forth into the world without 
 a preparatory lecture , and he intend to scourge poor Merit with the 
 very laurel wreath which it have receive from Apollo . but in this he 
 want Riccabocca 's assistance ; or rather he fear that , if he do not 
 get the philosopher on his side , the philosopher might undo all the work 
 of the parson . 




 CHAPTER XVI . 

 a sweet sound come through the orange bough , and float to the ear 
 of the parson , as he wind slowly up the gentle ascent , - so sweet , 
 so silvery , he pause in delight - unaware , wretched man ! that he be 
 thereby connive at papistical error . Soft it come and sweet ; soft 
 and sweet , - " Ave Maria ! " Violante be chant the evening hymn to the 
 Virgin Mother . the parson at last distinguish the sense of the word , 
 and shake his head with the pious shake of an orthodox protestant . 
 he break from the spell resolutely , and walk on with a sturdy 
 step . gain the terrace , he find the little family seat under an 
 awning , - Mrs. Riccabocca knitting ; the signor with his arm fold on

 on the ground , and his dark eye be soft and dreamy . Violante have 
 finish her hymn , and seat herself on the ground between the two , 
 pillow her head on her stepmother 's lap , but with her hand rest on 
 her father 's knee , and her gaze fix fondly on his face . 

 " good - evening , " say Mr. Dale . Violante steal up to he , and , pull 
 he so as to bring his ear near to her lip , whisper , " talk to papa , 
 do , - and cheerfully ; he be sad . " 

 she escape from he as she say this , and appear to busy herself with 
 water the flower arrange on stand round the awning . but she keep 
 her swim lustrous eye wistfully on her father . 

 " how fare it with you , my dear friend ? " say the parson , kindly , as he 
 rest his hand on the Italian 's shoulder . " you must not let he get out 
 of spirit , Mrs. Riccabocca . " 

 " I be very ungrateful to she if I ever be so , " say the poor Italian , 
 with all his natural gallantry . many a good wife , who think it be a
