 turn peevishly from that speech , more elegant than sincere , and so 
 have make bad bad ; but Mrs. Riccabocca take her husband 's proffered 
 hand affectionately , and say with great naivete , - 

 " you see I be so stupid , Mr. Dale ; I never know I be so stupid till I 
 marry . but I be very glad you be come . you can get on some learn 
 subject together , and then he will not miss so much his - " 

 " his what ? " ask Riccabocca , inquisitively . 

 " his country . do you think that I can not sometimes read your thought ? " 

 " very often . but you do not read they just then . the tongue touch 
 where the tooth ache , but the good dentist can not guess at the tooth 
 unless one open one 's mouth . - Basta ! can we offer you some wine of our 
 own making , Mr. Dale ? - it be pure . " 

 " I would rather have some tea , " quoth the parson , hastily . Mrs. Riccabocca , 
 too pleased to be in her natural element of domestic use , hurry into 
 the house to prepare our national beverage . and the parson , slide into 
 her chair , say , - 

 " but you be deject then ? Fie ! if there be a virtue in the world at 
 which we should always aim , it be cheerfulness . " 

 " I do not   dispute it , " say Riccabocca , with a heavy sigh . " but though it 
 be say by some Greek , who , I think , be quote by your favourite seneca , 
 that a wise man carry his country with he at the sol of his foot , 
 he can not   carry also the sunshine over his head . " 

 " I tell you what it be , " say the parson , bluntly ; " you would have 
 a much keener sense of happiness if you have much less esteem for 
 philosophy . " 

 " Cospetto ! " say the doctor , rouse himself . " just explain , will you ? " 

 " do not the search after wisdom induce desire not satisfied in this 
 small circle to which your life be confine ? it be not so much your 
 country for which you yearn , as it be for space to your intellect , 
 employment for your thought , career for your aspiration . " 

 " you have guess at the tooth which ache , " say Riccabocca , with 
 admiration . 

 " easy to do that , " answer the parson . " our wisdom tooth come last and 
 give we the most pain ; and if you would just starve the mind a little , 
 and nourish the heart more , you would be less of a philosopher and more 
 of a - " the parson have the word " Christian " at the tip of his tongue ; 
 he suppress a word that , so spoken , would have be exceedingly 
 irritate , and substitute , with elegant antithesis , " and more of a 
 happy man ! " 

 " I do all I can with my heart , " quoth the doctor . 

 " not you ! for a man with such a heart as yours should never feel the 
 want of the sunshine . my friend , we live in an age of over mental 
 cultivation . we neglect too much the simple healthful outer life , in 
 which there be so much positive joy . in turn to the world within we , 
 we grow blind to this beautiful world without ; in study ourselves 
 as man , we almost forget to look up to heaven , and warm to the smile of 
 God . " 

 the philosopher mechanically shrug his shoulder , as he always do 
 when another man moralize , - especially if the moralizer be a priest ; 
 but there be no irony in his smile , as he answer thoughtfully , - 

 " there be some truth in what you say . I own that we live too much as if 
 we be all brain . Knowledge have its penalty and pain , as well as its 
 prize . " 

 " that be just what I want you to say to Leonard . " 

 " how have you settle the object of your journey ? " 

 " I will tell you as we walk down to he after tea . at present , I be 
 rather too much occupy with you . " 

 " I ? the tree be form - try only to bend the young twig ! " 

 " tree be tree , and twig twig , " say the parson , dogmatically ; " but 
 man be always grow till he fall into the grave . I think I have hear 
 you say that you once have a narrow escape of a prison ? " 

 " very narrow . " 

 " just suppose that you be now in that prison , and that a fairy 
 conjure up the prospect of this quiet home in a safe land ; that you 
 see the orange - tree in flower , feel the evening breeze on your cheek ; 
 behold your child gay or sad , as you smile or knit your brow ; that 
 within this phantom home be a woman , not , indeed , all your young 
 romance might have dream of , but faithful and true , every beat of her 
 heart all your own , - would you not cry from the depth of your dungeon , 
 ' o fairy ! such a change be a paradise ! ' ungrateful man ! you want 
 interchange for your mind , and your heart should suffice for all ! " 

 Riccabocca be touch and silent . 

 " come hither , my child , " say Mr. Dale , turn round to Violante , who 
 stand still among the flower , out of hearing , but with watchful eye . 
 " come hither , " he say , open his arm . 

 Violante bound forward , and nestle to the good man 's heart . 

 " tell I , Violante , when you be alone in the field or the garden , and 
 have leave your father look pleased and serene , so that you have 
 no care for he at your heart , - tell I , Violante , though you be all 
 alone , with the flower below , and the bird sing overhead , do you 
 feel that life itself be happiness or sorrow ? " 

 " happiness ! " answer Violante , half shut her eye , and in a 
 measure voice . 

 " can you explain what kind of happiness it be ? " 

 " oh , no , impossible ! and it be never the same . sometimes it be so 
 still - so still , and sometimes so joyous , that I long for wing to fly 
 up to God , and thank he ! " 

 " o friend , " say the parson , " this be the true sympathy between life and 
 nature , and thus we should feel ever , do we take more care to preserve 
 the health and innocence of a child . we be tell that we must become as 
 child to enter into the kingdom of Heaven ; methink we should also 
 become as child to know what delight there be in our heritage of 
 earth ! " 




 CHAPTER XVII . 

 the maid - servant   for Jackeymo be in the field   bring the table 
 under the awning , and with the english luxury of tea , there be other 
 drink as cheap and as grateful on summer evening , - drink 
 which Jackeymo have retain and teach from the custom of the 
 South , - unebriate liquor , press from cool fruit , sweeten with 
 honey , and deliciously ice : ice should cost nothing in a country in 
 which one be freeze up half the year ! and Jackeymo , too , have add to 
 our good , solid , heavy english bread preparation of wheat much light , 
 and more propitious to digestion , - with those crisp grissin , which seem 
 to enjoy be eat , they make so pleasant a noise between one 's tooth . 

 the parson esteem it a little treat to drink tea with the Riccaboccas . 
 there be something of elegance and grace in that homely meal at the 
 poor exile 's table , which please the eye as well as taste . and the very 
 utensil , plain Wedgwood though they be , have a classical simplicity , 
 which make Mrs. Hazeldean 's old India delf , and Mrs. Dale 's good 
 Worcester china , look tawdry and barbarous in comparison . for it be 
 Flaxman who give design to Wedgwood , and the most truly refined of all 
 our manufacture in porcelain   if we do not look to the mere material 
 be in the reach of the most thrifty . 

 the little banquet be at first rather a silent one ; but Riccabocca 
 throw off his gloom , and become gay and animate . then poor Mrs. 
 Riccabocca smile , and press the grissin ; and Violante , forget 
 all her stateliness , laugh and play trick on the parson , steal 
 away his cup of warm tea when his head be turn , and substitute 
 ice cherry - juice . then the parson get up and run after Violante , make 
 angry face , and Violante dodge beautifully , till the parson , 
 fairly tired out , be too glad to cry " peace , " and come back to the 
 cherry - juice . thus time roll on , till they hear afar the stroke of 
 the distant church - clock , and Mr. Dale start up and cry , " but we 
 shall be too late for Leonard . come , naughty little girl , get your 
 father his hat . " 

 " and umbrella ! " say Riccabocca , look up at the cloudless , moonlit 
 sky . 

 " Umbrella against the star ? " ask the parson , laugh . " the star 
 be no friend of mine , " say Riccabocca , " and one never know what may 
 happen ! " 

 the philosopher and the parson walk on amicably . 

 " you have do I good , " say Riccabocca , " but I hope I be not always 
 so unreasonably melancholic as you seem to suspect . the evening will 
 sometimes appear long , and dull too , to a man whose thought on the past 
 be almost his sole companion . " 

 " Sole companion ? - your child ? " 

 " she be so young . " 

 " your wife ? " 

 " she be so - " the bland Italian appear to check some disparaging 
 adjective , and mildly add , ' so good , I allow ; but you must own that 
 she and I can not have much in common . " 

 " I own nothing of the sort . you have your house and your interest , your 
 happiness and your life , in common . we man be so exact , we expect 
 to find ideal nymph and goddess when we condescend to marry a mortal ; 
 and if we do , our chicken would be boil to rag , and our mutton come 
 up as cold as a stone . " 

 " per Bacco , you be an oracle , " say Riccabocca , laugh . " but I be 
 not so sceptical as you be . I honour the fair sex too much . there be 
 a great many woman who realize the ideal of man , to be find in - the 
 poet ! " 

 " there be my dear Mrs. Dale , " resume the parson , not heed the 
 sarcastic compliment to the sex , but sink his voice into a whisper , 
 and look round cautiously , - " there be my dear Mrs. Dale , the good 
 woman in the world , - an angel I would say , if the word be not profane ; 
 but - " 

 " what be the but ? " ask the doctor , demurely . 

 " but I too might say that ' she and I have not much in common , ' if I be 
 only to compare mind to mind , and when my poor Carry say something less 
 profound than Madame de Stael might have say , smile on she in contempt 
 from the elevation of logic and Latin . yet when I remember all the 
 little sorrow and joy that we have share together , and feel how 
 solitary I should have be without she - oh , then , I be instantly aware 
 that there be between we in common something infinitely close and 
 well than if the same course of study have give we the same equality 
 of idea ; and I be force to brace myself for a combat of intellect , as 
 I be when I fall in with a tiresome sage like yourself . I do not   pretend 
 to say that Mrs. Riccabocca be a Mrs. Dale , " add the parson , with 
 lofty candour , - " there be but one Mrs. Dale in the world ; but still , you 
 have draw a prize in the wheel matrimonial ! think of Socrates , and yet 
 he be content even with his - Xantippe ! " 

 Dr. Riccabocca call to mind Mrs. Dale 's " little temper , " and inly 
 rejoice that no second Mrs. Dale have exist to fall to his own lot . 
 his placid Jemima gain by the contrast . nevertheless he have the ill 
 grace to reply , " Socrates be a man beyond all imitation ! - yet I believe 
 that even he spend very few of his evening at home . but revenon a nos 
 mouton , we be nearly at Mrs. Fairfield 's cottage , and you have not yet 
 tell I what you have settle as to Leonard . " 

 the parson halt , take Riccabocca by the button , and inform he , in 
 very few word , that Leonard be to go to Lansmere to see some relation 
 there , who have the fortune , if they have the will , to give full career to 
 his ability . 

 " the great thing , in the mean while , " say the parson , " would be to 
 enlighten he a little as to what he call - enlightenment . " 

 " ah ! " say Riccabocca , divert , and rub his hand , " I shall listen 
 with interest to what you say on that subject . " 

 " and must aid I : for the first step in this modern march of 
 enlightenment be to leave the poor parson behind ; and if one call out 
 ' hold ! and look at the sign - post , ' the traveller hurry on the fast , 
 say to himself , ' Pooh , pooh ! - that be only the cry of the parson ! ' 
 but my gentleman , when he doubt I , will listen to you , - you be a 
 philosopher ! " 

 " we philosopher be of some use now and then , even to parson ! " 

 " if you be not so conceited a set of delude poor creature already , 
 I would say ' yes , ' " reply the parson , generously ; and , take hold of 
 Riccabocca 's umbrella , he apply the brass handle thereof , by way of a 
 knocker , to the cottage door . 




 CHAPTER XVIII . 

 certainly it be a glorious fever , - that desire to know ! and there be 
 few sight in the moral world more sublime than that which many a garret 
 might afford , if Asmodeus would bare the roof to our survey , - namely , a 
 brave , patient , earnest human be toil his own arduous way , athwart 
 the iron wall of penury , into the magnificent Infinite , which be 
 luminous with starry soul . 

 so there sit Leonard the self - teach in the little cottage alone : for , 
 though scarcely past the hour in which great folk dine , it be the hour 
 in which small folk go to bed , and Mrs. Fairfield have retire to rest , 
 while Leonard have settle to his book . 

 he have place his table under the lattice , and from time to time he 
 look up and enjoy the stillness of the moon . well for he that , in 
 reparation for those hour steal from night , the hardy physical labour 
 commence with dawn . student would not be the sad dyspeptic they be , 
 if they work as many hour in the open air as my scholar - peasant . but 
 even in he you could see that the mind have begin a little to affect the 
 frame . they who task the intellect must pay the penalty with the body . 
 Ill , believe I , would this work - day world get on if all within it be 
 hard - reading , studious animal , play the deuce with the ganglionic 
 apparatus . 

 Leonard start as he hear the knock at the door ; the parson 's 
 well - know voice reassure he . in some surprise he admit his 
 visitor . 

 " we be come to talk to you , Leonard , " say Mr. Dale ; " but I fear we 
 shall disturb Mrs. Fairfield . " 

 " oh , no , sir ! the door to the staircase be shut , and she sleep 
 soundly . " 

 " why , this be a french book ! do you read French , Leonard ? " ask 
 Riccabocca . 

 " I have not find french difficult , sir . once over the grammar , and the 
 language be so clear ; it seem the very language for reasoning . " 

 " true . Voltaire say justly , ' whatever be obscure be not french , ' " 
 observe Riccabocca . 

 " I wish I could say the same of English , " mutter the parson . 

 " but what be this , - Latin too ? - Virgil ? " 

 " yes , sir . but I find I make little way there without a master . I fear I 
 must give it up "   and Leonard sigh . 

 the two gentleman exchange look , and seat themselves . the young 
 peasant remain stand modestly , and in his air and mien there be 
 something that touch the heart while it please the eye . he be no 
 long the timid boy who have shrink from the frown of Mr. Stirn , 
 nor that rude personation of simple physical strength , rouse to 
 undisciplined bravery , which have receive its downfall on the village 
 green of Hazeldean . the power of thought be on his brow , - somewhat 
 unquiet still , but mild and earnest . the feature have attain that 
 refinement which be often attribute to race , but come , in truth , from 
 elegance of idea , whether catch from our parent or learn from book . 
 in his rich brown hair , throw carelessly from his temple , and curl 
 almost to the shoulder ; in his large blue eye , which be deepen to 
 the hue of the violet by the long dark lash ; in that firmness of lip , 
 which come from the grapple with difficulty , there be considerable 
 beauty , but no long the beauty of the mere peasant . and yet there be 
 still about the whole countenance that expression of goodness and purity 
 which a painter would give to his ideal of the peasant lover , - such as 
 Tasso would have place in the " Aminta , " or Fletcher have admit to 
 the side of the Faithful Shepherdess . 

 " you must draw a chair here , and sit down between we , Leonard , " say the 
 parson . 

 " if any one , " say Riccabocca , " have a right to sit , it be the one who be 
 to hear the sermon ; and if any one ought to stand , it be the one who be 
 about to preach it . " 

 " do not   be frightened , Leonard , " say the parson , graciously ; " it be only 
 a criticism , not a sermon ; " and he pull out Leonard 's Prize Essay . 




 CHAPTER XIX . 

 parson . - " you take for your motto this aphorism , ' Knowledge be 
 Power . ' - BACON . " 

 RICCABOCCA . - " Bacon make such an aphorism ! the last man in the world to 
 have say anything so pert and so shallow ! " 

 LEONARD   astonish . - " do you mean to say , sir , that that aphorism be 
 not in Lord Bacon ? why , I have see it quote as his in almost every 
 newspaper , and in almost every speech in favour of popular education . " 

 RICCABOCCA . - " then that should be a warning to you never again to fall 
 into the error of the would - be scholar , - 

   this aphorism have be probably assign to Lord Bacon upon the 
  mere authority of the index to his work . it be the aphorism of the 
  index - maker , certainly not of the great master of inductive 
  philosophy . Bacon have , it be true , repeatedly dwelt on the power of 
  knowledge , but with so many explanation and distinction that 
  nothing could be more unjust to his general meaning than the attempt 
  to cramp into a sentence what it cost he a volume to define . 
  thus , if on one page he appear to confound knowledge with power , in 
  another he set they in the strong antithesis to each other ; as 
  follow " Adeo signanter Deus opera potentix et sapientive 
  discriminavit . " but it would be as unfair to Bacon to convert into 
  an aphorism the sentence that discriminate between knowledge and 
  power as it be to convert into an aphorism any sentence that 
  confound they . 

 namely , quote second - hand . Lord Bacon write a great book to show in what 
 knowledge be power , how that power should be define , in what it might 
 be mistake . and , pray , do you think so sensible a man ever would have 
 take the trouble to write a great book upon the subject , if he could 
 have pack up all he have to say into the portable dogma , ' Knowledge be 
 power ' ? Pooh ! no such aphorism be to be find in Bacon from the first 
 page of his writing to the last . " 

 parson   candidly . - " well , I suppose it be Lord Bacon 's , and I be very 
 glad to hear that the aphorism have not the sanction of his authority . " 

 LEONARD   recover his surprise . - " but why so ? " 

 parson . - " because it either say a great deal too much , or just - nothing 
 at all . " 

 LEONARD . - " at least , sir , it seem to I undeniable . " 

 parson . - " well , grant that it be undeniable . do it prove much in 
 favour of knowledge ? pray , be not ignorance power too ? " 

 RICCABOCCA . - " and a power that have have much the good end of the 
 quarter - staff . " 

 parson . - " all evil be power , and do its power make it anything the 
 well ? " 

 RICCABOCCA . - " Fanaticism be power , - and a power that have often sweep 
 away knowledge like a whirlwind . the Mussulman burn the library of a 
 world , and force the Koran and the sword from the school of Byzantium 
 to the college of Hindostan . " 

 parson   bear on with a new column of illustration . - " hunger be 
 power . the barbarian , starve out of their forest by their own 
 swarm population , sweep into Italy and annihilated letter . the 
 Romans , however degrade , have more knowledge at least than the Gaul and 
 the Visigoth . " 

 riccabocca   bring up the reserve . - " and even in Greece , when Greek 
 meet Greek , the Athenians - our master in all knowledge - be beat by the 
 Spartans , who hold learn in contempt . " 

 parson . - " wherefore you see , Leonard , that though knowledge be power , it 
 be only one of the power of the world ; that there be other as strong , 
 and often much strong ; and the assertion either mean but a barren 
 truism , not worth so frequent a repetition , or it mean something that 
 you would find it very difficult to prove . " 

 LEONARD . - " one nation may be beat by another that have more physical 
 strength and more military discipline ; which last , permit I to say , 
 sir , be a species of knowledge - " 

 RICCABOCCA . - " yes ; but your knowledge - monger at present call upon we to 
 discard military discipline , and the quality that produce it , from 
 the list of the useful art . and in your own essay , you insist upon 
 knowledge as the great disbander of army , and the foe of all military 
 discipline ! " 

 parson . - " let the young man proceed . nation , you say , may be beat by 
 other nation less learn and civilized ? " 

 LEONARD . - " but knowledge elevate a class . I invite the member of my 
 own humble order to knowledge , because knowledge will lift they into 
 power . " 

 RICCABOCCA . - " what do you say to that , Mr. Dale ? " 

 parson . - " in the first place , be it true that the class which have the 
 most knowledge get the most power ? I suppose philosopher , like my 
 friend Dr. Riccabocca , think they have the most knowledge . and pray , 
 in what age have philosopher govern the world ? be they not always 
 grumble that nobody attend to they ? " 

 RICCABOCCA . - " per Bacco , if people have attend to we , it would have 
 be a droll sort of world by this time ! " 

 parson . - " very likely . but , as a general rule , those have the most 
 knowledge who give themselves up to it the most . let we put out of the 
 question philosopher   who be often but ingenious lunatic , and 
 speak only of erudite scholar , man of letter and practical science , 
 professor , tutor , and fellow of college . I fancy any member of 
 parliament would tell we that there be no class of man which have less 
 actual influence on public affair . these scholar have more knowledge 
 than manufacturer and shipowner , squire and farmer ; but do you find 
 that they have more power over the Government and the vote of the House 
 of Parliament ? " 

 " they ought to have , " say Leonard . 

 " ought they ? " say the parson ; " we will consider that later . meanwhile , 
 you must not escape from your own proposition , which be , that knowledge 
 be power , - not that it ought to be . now , even grant your corollary , 
 that the power of a class be therefore proportion to its knowledge , 
 pray , do you suppose that while your order , the operative , be 
 instruct themselves , all the rest of the community be to be at 
 a standstill ? diffuse knowledge as you may , you will never produce 
 equality of knowledge . those who have most leisure , application , and 
 aptitude for learning will still know the most . Nay , by a very natural 
 law , the more general the appetite for knowledge , the more the increase 
 competition will favour those most adapt to excel by circumstance and 
 nature . at this day , there be a vast increase of knowledge spread over 
 all society , compare with that in the Middle Ages ; but be there not a 
 still great distinction between the highly educate gentleman and the 
 intelligent mechanic , than there be then between the baron who could 
 not sign his name and the churl at the plough ; between the accomplished 
 statesman , verse in all historical lore , and the voter whose politic 
 be form by his newspaper , than there be between the legislator who 
 pass law against witch and the burgher who defend his guild from 
 some feudal aggression ; between the enlightened scholar and the dunce of 
 to - day , than there be between the monkish alchemist and the blockhead 
 of yesterday ? peasant , voter , and dunce of this century be no doubt 
 wise than the churl , burgher , and blockhead of the twelfth . but the 
 gentleman , statesman , and scholar of the present age be at least quite 
 as favourable a contrast to the alchemist , witch - burner , and baron of 
 old . as the progress of enlightenment have do hitherto , so will it ever 
 do . 

 " Knowledge be like capital : the more there be in a country , the great 
 the disparity in wealth between one man and another . therefore , if the 
 work class increase in knowledge , so do the other class ; and if the 
 work class rise peaceably and legitimately into power , it be not 
 in proportion to their own knowledge alone , but rather accord as it 
 seem to the knowledge of the other order of the community , that such 
 augmentation of proportional power be just and safe and wise . " 

 place between the parson and the philosopher , Leonard feel that his 
 position be not favourable to the display of his force . insensibly he 
 edge his chair somewhat away , and say mournfully , - 

 " then , accord to you , the reign of knowledge would be no great 
 advance in the aggregate freedom and welfare of man ? " 

 parson . - " let we define . by knowledge , do you mean intellectual 
 cultivation ; by the reign of knowledge , the ascendency of the most 
 cultivate mind ? " 

 LEONARD   after a pause . - " yes . " 

 RICCABOCCA . - " oh , indiscreet young man ! that be an unfortunate 
 concession of your ; for the ascendency of the most cultivate mind 
 would be a terrible oligarchy ! " 

 parson . - " perfectly true ; and we now reply to your assertion that man 
 who , by profession , have most learning , ought to have more influence 
 than squire and merchant , farmer and mechanic . Observe , all the 
 knowledge that we mortal can acquire be not knowledge positive and 
 perfect , but knowledge comparative , and subject to the error and 
 passion of humanity . and suppose that you could establish , as the sole 
 regulator of affair , those who have the most mental cultivation , do you 
 think they would not like that power well enough to take all mean which 
 their superior intelligence could devise to keep it to themselves ? the 
 experiment be try of old by the priest of Egypt ; and in the empire 
 of China , at this day , the aristocracy be elect from those who have 
 most distinguish themselves in learn college . if I may call myself 
 a member of that body , ' the people , ' I would rather be an Englishman , 
 however much displeased with dull minister and blundering parliament , 
 than I would be a Chinese under the rule of the pick sage of the 
 Celestial Empire . happily , therefore , my dear Leonard , nation be 
 govern by many thing besides what be commonly call knowledge ; and 
 the great practical minister , who , like Themistocles , have make 
 small States great , and the most dominant race , who , like the Romans , 
 have stretch their rule from a village half over the universe , have 
 be distinguish by various quality which a philosopher would sneer 
 at , and a knowledge - monger would call ' sad prejudice ' and ' lamentable 
 error of reason . ' " 

 LEONARD   bitterly . - " Sir , you make use of knowledge itself to argue 
 against knowledge . " 

 parson . - " I make use of the little I know to prove the foolishness 
 of idolatry . I do not argue against knowledge ; I argue against 
 knowledge - worship . for here , I see in your essay , that you be not 
 content with raise human knowledge into something like divine 
 omnipotence , - you must also confound she with virtue . accord to you , 
 it be but to diffuse the intelligence of the few among the many , and all 
 at which we preacher aim be accomplish . Nay , more ; for , whereas we 
 humble preacher have never presume to say , with the heathen Stoic , 
 that even virtue be sure of happiness below   though it be the good road 
 to it , you tell we plainly that this knowledge of yours give not only 
 the virtue of a saint , but bestow the bliss of a god . before the step 
 of your idol , the evil of life disappear . to hear you , one have but ' to 
 know , ' in order to be exempt from the sin and sorrow of the ignorant . 
 have it ever be so ? grant that you diffuse amongst the many all the 
 knowledge ever attain by the few . have the wise few be so unerring 
 and so happy ? you suppose that your motto be accurately cite from 
 Bacon . what be Bacon himself ? the poet tell you 

  " ' the wise , bright , mean of mankind ! ' 

 " can you hope to bestow upon the vast mass of your order the luminous 
 intelligence of this ' Lord Chancellor of Nature ' ? Grant that you do so , 
 and what guarantee have you for the virtue and the happiness which you 
 assume as the concomitant of the gift ? see Bacon himself : what black 
 ingratitude ! what miserable self - seeking ! what truckle servility ! what 
 abject and pitiful spirit ! so far from intellectual knowledge , in its 
 high form and type , insure virtue and bliss , it be by no mean 
 uncommon to find great mental cultivation combine with great moral 
 corruption . "   aside to Riccabocca . - " push on , will you ? " 

 RICCASOCCA . - " a combination remarkable in era as in individual . 
 Petronius show we a state of moral at which a commonplace devil would 
 blush , in the midst of a society more intellectually cultivate than 
 certainly be that which produce Regulus or the Horatii . and the most 
 learn era in modern Italy be precisely those which bring the 
 vice into the most ghastly refinement . " 

 LEONARD   rise in great agitation , and clasp his hand . - " I can not 
 contend with you , who produce against information so slender and crude 
 as mine the store which have be lock from my reach ; but I feel that 
 there must be another side to this shield , - a shield that you will not 
 even allow to be silver . and , oh , if you thus speak of knowledge , why 
 have you encourage I to know ? " 




 CHAPTER XX . 

 " ah , my son ! " say the parson , " if I wish to prove the value of 
 religion , would you think I serve it much if I take as my motto , 
 ' religion be power ' ? would not that be a base and sordid view of its 
 advantage ? and would you not say , he who regard religion as a power 
 intend to abuse it as a priestcraft ? " 

 " well put ! " say Riccabocca . 

 " wait a moment - let I think ! ah , I see , Sir ! " say Leonard . 

 parson . - " if the cause be holy , do not weigh it in the scale of the 
 market ; if its object be peaceful , do not seek to arm it with the 
 weapon of strife ; if it be to be the cement of society , do not vaunt it 
 as the triumph of class against class . " 

 LEONARD   ingenuously . - " you correct I nobly , sir . Knowledge be power , 
 but not in the sense in which I have interpret the saying . " 

 parson . - " Knowledge be one of the power in the moral world , but 
 one that , in its immediate result , be not always of the most worldly 
 advantage to the possessor . it be one of the slow , because one of the 
 most durable , of agency . it may take a thousand year for a thought 
 to come into power ; and the thinker who originate it might have die in 
 rag or in chain . " 

 RICCABOCCA . - " our italian proverb saith that ' the teacher be like the 
 candle , which light other in consume itself . ' " 

 parson . - " therefore he who have the true ambition of knowledge should 
 entertain it for the power of his idea , not for the power it may 
 bestow on himself : it should be lodge in the conscience , and , like the 
 conscience , look for no certain reward on this side the grave . and since 
 knowledge be compatible with good and with evil , would not it be well 
 to say , ' Knowledge be a trust ' ? " 

 " you be right , sir , " say Leonard , cheerfully ; " pray proceed . " 

 parson . - " you ask I why we encourage you to know . first , because   as 
 you say yourself in your Essay   knowledge , irrespective of gain , be in 
 itself a delight , and ought to be something far more . like liberty , like 
 religion , it may be abuse ; but I have no more right to say that the 
 poor shall be ignorant than I have to say that the rich only shall be 
 free , and that the clergy alone shall learn the truth of redemption . 
 you truly observe in your treatise that knowledge open to we other 
 excitement than those of the sens , and another life than that of the 
 moment . the difference between we be this , - that you forget that the 
 same refinement which bring we new pleasure expose we to new pain ; 
 the horny hand of the peasant feel not the nettle which ste the fine 
 skin of the scholar . you forget also , that whatever widen the sphere 
 of the desire open to they also new temptation . Vanity , the desire of 
 applause , pride , the sense of superiority , gnawing discontent where that 
 superiority be not recognize , morbid susceptibility , which come with 
 all new feeling , the underrating of simple pleasure apart from the 
 intellectual , the chase of the imagination , often unduly stimulate , 
 for thing unattainable below , - all these be surely amongst the first 
 temptation that beset the entrance into knowledge . " Leonard shade his 
 face with his hand . 

 " hence , " continue the parson , benignantly , - " hence , so far from 
 consider that we do all that be needful to accomplish ourselves as 
 man , when we cultivate only the intellect , we should remember that we 
 thereby continually increase the range of our desire , and therefore of 
 our temptation ; and we should endeavour , simultaneously , to cultivate 
 both those affection of the heart which prove the ignorant to be God 's 
 child no less than the wise , and those moral quality which have 
 make man great and good when read and writing be scarcely know : 
 to wit , - patience and fortitude under poverty and distress ; humility and 
 beneficence amidst grandeur and wealth , and , in counteraction to that 
 egotism which all superiority , mental or worldly , be apt to inspire , 
 Justice , the father of all the more solid virtue , soften by Charity , 
 which be their love mother . thus accompany , knowledge indeed become 
 the magnificent crown of humanity , - not the imperious despot , but the 
 check and temper sovereign of the soul . " 

 the parson pause , and Leonard , come near he , timidly take his hand , 
 with a child 's affectionate and grateful impulse . 

 riccarocca . - " and if , Leonard , you be not satisfied with our parson 's 
 excellent definition , you have only to read what Lord Bacon himself have 
 say upon the true end of knowledge to comprehend at once how angry the 
 poor great man , whom Mr. Dale treat so harshly , would have be with 
 those who have stint his elaborate distinction and provident caution 
 into that coxcombical little aphorism , and then misconstrue all 
 he design to prove in favour of the commandment , and authority of 
 learn . for , " add the sage , look up as a man do when he be 
 task his memory , " I think it be thus that after say the great 
 error of all be the mistaking or misplace the end of knowledge , and 
 denounce the various object for which it be vulgarly seek , - I think 
 it be thus that Lord Bacon proceed : ' Knowledge be not a shop for profit 
 or sale , but a rich storehouse for the glory of the Creator , and the 
 relief of man 's estate . ' " 

   " but the great error of all the rest be the mistaking or 
  misplacing of the last or farth end of knowledge : for man have 
  enter into a desire of learning and knowledge , sometimes upon a 
  natural curiosity and inquisitive appetite ; sometimes to entertain 
  their mind with variety and delight ; sometimes for ornament and 
  reputation ; and sometimes to enable they to victory of wit and 
  contradiction ; and most time for lucre and profession " -   that be , 
  for most of those object which be mean by the ordinary titer of 
  the saying , " Knowledge be power "   - " and seldom sincerely to give a 
  true account of these gift of reason to the benefit and use of man , 
  as if there be seek in knowledge a couch whereupon to rest a 
  searching and restless spirit ; or a terrace for a wandering and 
  variable mind to walk up and down , with a fair prospect ; or a tower 
  of state for a proud mind to raise itself upon ; or a fort or 
  commanding ground for strife and contention ; or a shop for profit or 
  sale , - and not a rich storehouse for the glory of the Creator , and 
  the relief of man 's estate . " - Advancement of Learning , Book I. 

 parson   remorsefully . - " be those Lord Bacon 's word ? I be very sorry 
 I speak so uncharitably of his life . I must examine it again . I may find 
 excuse for it now that I could not when I first form my judgment . 
 I be then a raw lad at Oxford . but I see , Leonard , there be still 
 something on your mind . " 

 LEONARD . - " it be true , sir : I would but ask whether it be not by 
 knowledge that we arrive at the quality and virtue you so well 
 describe , but which you seem to consider as come to we through 
 channel apart from knowledge ? " 

 parson . - " if you mean by the word ' knowledge ' something very different 
 from what you express in your Essay - and which those contend for 
 mental instruction , irrespective of religion and ethic , appear also to 
 convey by the word - you be right ; but , remember , we have already agree 
 that by the word ' knowledge ' we mean culture purely intellectual . " 

 LEONARD . - " that be true , - we so understand it . " 

 parson . - " thus , when this great Lord Bacon err , you may say that 
 he err from want of knowledge , - the knowledge which moralist and 
 preacher would convey . but Lord Bacon have read all that moralist and 
 preacher could say on such matter ; and he certainly do not err from 
 want of intellectual cultivation . let I here , my child , invite you 
 to observe , that he who know most of our human heart and our immortal 
 destiny do not insist on this intellectual culture as essential to 
 the virtue that form our well - being here , and conduce to our salvation 
 hereafter . have it be essential , the all - wise one would not have 
 select humble fisherman for the teacher of his doctrine , instead of 
 cull his disciple from roman portico or athenian academe . and this , 
 which distinguish so remarkably the Gospel from the ethic of heathen 
 philosophy , wherein knowledge be declare to be necessary to virtue , 
 be a proof how slight be the heathen sage 's insight into the nature of 
 mankind , when compare with the Saviour 's ; for hard indeed would it be 
 to man , whether high or low , rich or poor , if science and learning , or 
 contemplative philosophy , be the sole avenue to peace and redemption ; 
 since , in this state of ordeal require active duty , very few in 
 any age , whether they be high or low , rich or poor , ever be or can be 
 devote to pursuit merely mental . Christ do not represent Heaven as a 
 college for the learn . therefore the rule of the Celestial Legislator 
 be render clear to the simple understanding as to the deep . " 

 RICCABOCCA . - " and that which Plato and Zeno , Pythagoras and Socrates 
 could not do , be do by man whose ignorance would have be a by - word 
 in the school of the Greek . the god of the vulgar be dethrone ; the 
 face of the world be change ! this thought may make we allow , indeed , 
 that there be agency more powerful than mere knowledge , and ask , 
 after all , what be the mission which knowledge should achieve ? " 

 parson . - " the Sacred Book tell we even that ; for after establish the 
 truth that , for the multitude , knowledge be not essential to happiness 
 and good , it accord still to knowledge its sublime part in the 
 revelation prepare and announce . when an instrument of more than 
 ordinary intelligence be require for a purpose divine ; when the 
 Gospel , record by the simple , be to be explain by the acute , 
 enforce by the energetic , carry home to the doubt of the Gentile , 
 the Supreme will join to the zeal of the early apostle the learning 
 and genius of Saint Paul , - not holy than the other , call himself 
 the least , yet labour more abundantly than they all , make himself 
 all thing unto all man , so that some might be save . the ignorant may 
 be save no less surely than the wise ; but here come the wise man who 
 help to save . and how the fulness and animation of this grand Presence , 
 of this indomitable Energy , seem to vivify the toil , and to speed the 
 work ! ' in journeying often , in peril of water , in peril of robber , 
 in peril of mine own countryman , in peril by the heathen , in peril in 
 the city , in peril in the wilderness , in peril in the sea , in peril 
 amongst false brother . ' Behold , my son ! do not Heaven here seem to 
 reveal the true type of Knowledge , - a sleepless activity , a pervading 
 agency , a dauntless heroism , an all - support faith ? - a power , a power 
 indeed ; a power apart from the aggrandizement of self ; a power that 
 bring to he who own and transmit it but ' weariness and painfulness ; 
 in watching often , in hunger and thirst , in fasting often , in cold and 
 nakedness , ' - but a power distinct from the mere circumstance of the 
 man , rush from he as ray from the sun ; bear through the air , and 
 clothing it with light , pierce under earth , and call forth the 
 harvest . worship not knowledge , worship not the sun , o my child ! let 
 the sun but proclaim the Creator ; let the knowledge but illumine the 
 worship ! " 

 the good man , overcome by his own earnestness , pause ; his head droop 
 on the young student 's breast , and all three be long silent . 




 CHAPTER XXI . 

 whatever ridicule may be throw upon Mr. Dale 's dissertation by the wit 
 of the enlighten , they have a considerable , and I think a beneficial , 
 effect upon Leonard Fairfield , - an effect which may perhaps create less 
 surprise , when the reader remember that Leonard be unaccustomed to 
 argument , and still retain many of the prejudice natural to his 
 rustic breeding . Nay , he actually think it possible that , as both 
 Riccabocca and Mr. Dale be more than double his age , and have have 
 opportunity not only of read twice as many book , but of gather 
 up experience in wide range of life , - he actually , I say , think it 
 possible that they might be well acquaint with the property and 
 distinction of knowledge than himself . at all event , the parson 's 
 word be so far well - time , that they produce in Leonard very much 
 of that state of mind which Mr. Dale desire to effect , before 
 communicate to he the startling intelligence that he be to visit 
 relation whom he have never see , of whom he have hear but little , and 
 that it be at least possible that the result of that visit might be to 
 open to he great facility for instruction , and a high degree in 
 life . 

 without some such preparation , I fear that Leonard would have go forth 
 into the world with an exaggerated notion of his own acquirement , and 
 with a notion yet more exaggerated as to the kind of power that such 
 knowledge as he possess would obtain for itself . as it be , when 
 Mr. Dale break to he the news of the experimental journey before 
 he , caution he against be over sanguine , Leonard receive the 
 intelligence with a serious meekness , and thought that be nobly 
 solemn . 

 when the door close on his visitor , he remain for some moment 
 motionless , and in deep meditation ; then he unclose the door and stole 
 forth . the night be already far advanced , the heaven be luminous 
 with all the host of star . " I think , " say the student , refer , in 
 later life , to that crisis in his destiny , - " I think it be then , as I 
 stand alone , yet surround by world so numberless , that I first feel 
 the distinction between mind and soul . " 

 " tell I , " say Riccabocca , as he part company with Mr. Dale , " whether 
 you would have give to Frank Hazeldean , on enter life , the same 
 lecture on the limit and end of knowledge which you have bestow on 
 Leonard Fairfield ? " 

 " my friend , " quoth the parson , with a touch of human conceit , " I have 
 ride on horseback , and I know that some horse should be guide by the 
 bridle , and some should be urge by the spur . " 

 " Cospetto ! " say Riccabocca , " you contrive to put every experience of 
 your to some use , - even your journey on Mr. Hazeldean 's pad . and I 
 now see why , in this little world of a village , you have pick up so 
 general an acquaintance with life . " 

 " do you ever read White 's ' natural history of Selborne ' ? " 

 " no . " 

 " do so , and you will find that you need not go far to learn the habit 
 of bird , and know the difference between a swallow and a swift . learn 
 the difference in a village , and you know the difference wherever 
 swallow and swift skim the air . " 

 " swallow and swift ! - true ; but man - " 

 " be with we all the year round , - which be more than we can say of 
 swallow and swift . " 

 " Mr. Dale , " say Riccabocca , take off his hat with great formality , 
 " if ever again I find myself in a dilemma , I will come to you instead of 
 to Machiavelli . " 

 " ah ! " cry the parson , " if I could but have a calm hour 's talk with you 
 on the error of the Papal relig - " 

 Riccabocca be off like a shot . 




 CHAPTER XXII . 

 the next day Mr. Dale have a long conversation with Mrs. Fairfield . at 
 first he find some difficulty in get over her pride , and induce 
 she to accept overture from parent who have so long slight both 
 Leonard and herself . and it would have be in vain to have put before 
 the good woman the worldly advantage which such overture imply . but 
 when Mr. Dale say , almost sternly , " your parent be old , your father 
 infirm ; their least wish should be as bind to you as their command , " 
 the widow bow her head , and say , - 

 " God bless they , sir , I be very sinful ' honour your father and mother . ' 
 I be no schollard , but I know the Commandments . let Lenny go . but he will 
 soon forget I , and mayhap he will learn to be ashamed of I . " 

 " there I will trust he , " say the parson ; and he contrive easily to 
 reassure and soothe she . 

 it be not till all this be settle that Mr. Dale draw forth an 
 unseal letter , which Mr. Richard Avenel , take his hint , have give to 
 he , as from Leonard 's grandparent , and say , " this be for you , and it 
 contain an inclosure of some value . " 

 " will you read it , sir ? as I say before , I be no schollard . " 

 " but Leonard be , and he will read it to you . " 

 when Leonard return home that evening , Mrs. Fairfield show he the 
 letter . it run thus : - 

  DEAR JANE , - Mr. Dale will tell you that we wish Leonard to come to 
  we . we be glad to hear you be well . we forward , by Mr. Dale , a 
  bank - note for L50 , which come from Richard , your brother . so no 
  more at present from your affectionate parent , 

  JOHN and MARGARET AVENEL . 

 the letter be in a stiff female scrawl , and Leonard observe that two 
 or three mistake in spelling have be correct , either in another pen 
 or in a different hand . 

 " dear brother Dick , how good in he ! " cry the widow . " when I see 
 there be money , I think it must be he . how I should like to see Dick 
 again ! but I s'pose he be still in Amerikay . well , well , this will buy 
 clothe for you . " 

 " no ; you must keep it all , Mother , and put it in the Savings Bank . " 

 " I ' m not quite so silly as that , " cry Mrs. Fairfield , with contempt ; 
 and she put the L50 into a crack teapot . 

 " it must not stay there when I ' m go . you may be rob , Mother . " 

 " dear I , dear I , that be true . what shall I do with it ? what do I want 
 with it , too ? dear I ! I wish they have not   send it . I sha ' not   sleep in 
 peace . you must e'en put it in your own pouch , and button it up tight , 
 boy . " 

 Lenny smile , and take the note ; but he take it to Mr. Dale , and beg 
 he to put it into the Savings Bank for his mother . 

 the day follow he go to take leave of his master , of Jackeymo , of 
 the fountain , the garden . but after he have go through the first of 
 these adieus with Jackeymo - who , poor man , indulge in all the lively 
 gesticulation of grief which make half the eloquence of his countryman , 
 and then , absolutely blubber , hurry away - Leonard himself be 
 so affected that he could not proceed at once to the house , but stand 
 beside the fountain , try hard to keep back his tear . 

 " you , Leonard - and you be go ! " say a soft voice ; and the tear fall 
 fast than ever , for he recognize the voice of Violante . 

 " do not cry , " continue the child , with a kind of tender gravity . " you 
 be go , but Papa say it would be selfish in we to grieve , for it be 
 for your good ; and we should be glad . but I be selfish , Leonard , and I 
 do grieve . I shall miss you sadly . " 

 " you , young lady , - you miss I ? " 

 " yes ; but I do not cry , Leonard , for I envy you , and I wish I be a 
 boy : I wish I could do as you . " 

 the girl clasp her hand , and rear her slight form , with a kind of 
 passionate dignity . 

 " do as I , and part from all those you love ! " 

 " but to serve those you love . one day you will come back to your 
 mother 's cottage , and say , ' I have conquer fortune . ' oh that I could 
 go forth and return , as you will ! but my father have no country , and his 
 only child be a useless girl . " 

 as Violante speak , Leonard have dry his tear : her emotion distract 
 he from his own . 

 " oh , " continue Violante , again raise her head loftily , " what it be to 
 be a man ! a woman sigh , ' I wish , ' but a man should say , ' I will . ' " 

 occasionally before Leonard have note fitful flash of a nature grand 
 and heroic in the italian child , especially of late , - flash the more 
 remarkable from the contrast to a form most exquisitely feminine , and 
 to a sweetness of temper which make even her pride gentle . but now it 
 seem as if the child speak with the command of a queen , - almost with 
 the inspiration of a Muse . a strange and new sense of courage enter 
 within he . 

 " may I remember these word ! " he murmur , half audibly . 

 the girl turn and survey he with eye bright for their moisture . 
 she then extend her hand to he , with a quick movement , and as he bend 
 over it , with a grace teach to he by genuine emotion , she say , " and 
 if you do , then , girl and child as I be , I shall think I have aid a 
 brave heart in the great strife for honour ! " 

 she linger a moment , smile as if to herself , and then , glide away , 
 be lose amongst the tree . 

 after a long pause , in which Leonard recover slowly from the surprise 
 and agitation into which Violante have throw his spirit - previously 
 excited as they be - he go , murmur to himself , towards the 
 house . but Riccabocca be from home . Leonard turn mechanically to 
 the terrace , and busy himself with the flower ; but the dark eye of 
 Violante shine on his thought , and her voice ring in his ear . 

 at length Riccabocca appear on the road , attend by a labourer , who 
 carry something indistinct under his arm . the Italian beckon to 
 Leonard to follow he into the parlour , and after converse with he 
 kindly , and at some length , and pack up , as it be , a considerable 
 provision of wisdom in the portable shape of aphorism and proverb , the 
 sage leave he alone for a few moment . Riccabocca then return with his 
 wife , and bear a small knapsack : - 

 " it be not much we can do for you , Leonard , and money be the bad 
 gift in the world for a keepsake ; but my wife and I have put our head 
 together to furnish you with a little outfit . Giacomo , who be in our 
 secret , assure we that the clothe will fit ; and stole , I fancy , a coat 
 of your , to have the right measure . put they on when you go to your 
 relation : it be astonish what a difference it make in the idea 
 people form of we , accord as our coat be cut one way or another . I 
 should not be presentable in London thus ; and nothing be more true than 
 that a tailor be often the making of a man . " 

 " the shirt , too , be very good holland , " say Mrs. Riccabocca , about to 
 open the knapsack . 

 " never mind detail , my dear , " cry the wise man ; ' shirt be 
 comprehend in the general principle of clothe . and , Leonard , as a 
 remembrance somewhat more personal , accept this , which I have wear many 
 a year when time be a thing of importance to I , and nobler fate than 
 mine hang on a moment . we miss the moment , or abuse it ; and here I be 
 a waif on a foreign shore . methink I have do with Time . " 

 the exile , as he thus speak , place in Leonard 's reluctant hand a watch 
 that would have delight an antiquary , and shock a dandy . it be 
 exceedingly thick , have an outer case of enamel and an inner one of 
 gold . the hand and the figure of the hour have originally be form 
 of brilliant ; but the brilliant have long since vanish . still , even 
 thus bereft , the watch be much more in character with the giver than 
 the receiver , and be as little suited to Leonard as would have be the 
 red silk umbrella . 

 " it be old - fashioned , " say Mrs. Riccabocca ; " but it go well than 
 any clock in the county . I really think it will last to the end of the 
 world . " 

 " Carissima mia ! " cry the doctor , " I think I have convince you that 
 the world be by no mean come to its last leg . " 

 " oh , I do not mean anything , Alphonso , " say Mrs. Riccabocca , 
 colour . 

 " and that be all we do mean when we talk about that of which we can know 
 nothing , " say the doctor , less gallantly than usual , for he resent 
 that epithet of " old - fashioned , " as apply to the watch . 

 Leonard , we see , have be silent all this time ; he could not 
 speak , - literally and truly , he could not speak . how he get out of his 
 embarrassment and how he get out of the room , he never explain to my 
 satisfaction . but a few minute afterwards , he be see hurry down 
 the road very briskly . 

 Riccabocca and his wife stand at the window gaze after he . 

 " there be a depth in that boy 's heart , " say the sage , " which might 
 float an argosy . " 

 " poor dear boy ! I think we have put everything into the knapsack that he 
 can possibly want , " say good Mrs. Riccabocca , musingly . 

 the doctor   continue his soliloquy . - " they be strong , but they be 
 not immediately apparent . " 

 MRS . RICCABOCCA   resume hers . - " they be at the bottom of the 
 knapsack . " 

 the DOCTOR . - " they will stand long wear and tear . " 

 MRS . RICCABOCCA . - " a year , at least , with proper care at the wash . " 

 the doctor   startle . - " care at the wash ! what on earth be you talk 
 of , ma'am ? " 

 MRS . riccabocca   mildly . - " the shirt , to be sure , my love ! and you ? " 

 the doctor   with a heavy sigh . - " the feeling , ma'am ! " then , after a 
 pause , take his wife 's hand affectionately , " but you do quite right 
 to think of the shirt : Mr. Dale say very truly - " 

 MRS . RICCABOCCA . - " what ? " 

 the DOCTOR . - " that there be a great deal in common between we - even 
 when I think of feeling , and you but of - shirt ! " 




 CHAPTER XXIII . 

 Mr. and Mrs. Avenel sit within the parlour , Mr. Richard stand on the 
 hearthrug , whistle " Yankee Doodle . " " the parson write word that 
 the lad will come to - day , " say Richard , suddenly ; " let I see the 
 letter , - ay , to - day . if he take the coach as far as - - - - , he might 
 walk the rest of the way in two or three hour . he should be pretty 
 nearly here . I have a great mind to go and meet he : it will save his 
 ask question , and hear about I . I can clear the town by the back 
 way , and get out at the high road . " 

 " you will not know he from any one else , " say Mrs. Avenel . 

 " well , that be a good one ! not know an avenel ! we have   all the same cut of 
 the jib , - have we not , Father ? " 

 Poor John laugh heartily , till the tear roll down his cheek . 

 " we be always a well - favour fam'ly , " say John , recompose himself . 
 " there be Luke , but he be go ; and Harry , but he be dead too ; and Dick , 
 but he be in Amerikay - no , he be here ; and my darling Nora , but - " 

 " hush ! " interrupt Mrs. Avenel ; " hush , John ! " 

 the old man star at she , and then put his tremulous hand to his brow . 
 " and Nora 's go too ! " say he , in a voice of profound woe . both hand 
 then fall on his knee , and his head droop on his breast . 

 Mrs. Avenel rise , kiss her husband on the forehead , and walk away to 
 the window . Richard take up his hat and brush the nap carefully with 
 his handkerchief ; but his lip quiver . 

 " I ' m go , " say he , abruptly . " now mind , Mother , not a word about 
 uncle Richard yet ; we must first see how we like each other , and -   in a 
 whisper   you will try and get that into my poor father 's head ? " 

 " ay , Richard , " say Mrs. Avenel , quietly . Richard put on his hat and 
 go out by the back way . he steal along the field that skirt the 
 town , and have only once to cross the street before he get into the high 
 road . 

 he walk on till he come to the first milestone . there he seat 
 himself , light his cigar , and await his nephew . it be now nearly 
 the hour of sunset , and the road before he lie westward . Richard , from 
 time to time , look along the road , shade his eye with his hand ; and 
 at length , just as the disk of the sun have half sink down the horizon , 
 a solitary figure come up the way . it emerge suddenly from the turn 
 in the road ; the redden beam colour all the atmosphere around it . 
 Solitary and silent it come as from a Land of Light . 




 CHAPTER XXIV . 

 " you have be walk far , young man ? " say Richard Avenel . 

 " no , sir , not very . that be Lansmere before I , be it not ? " 

 " yes , it be Lansmere ; you stop there , I guess ? " 

 Leonard make a sign in the affirmative , and walk on a few pace ; then , 
 see the stranger who have accost he still by his side , he say , - 

 " if you know the town , sir , perhaps you will have the goodness to tell 
 I whereabout Mr. Avenel live ? " 

 " I can put you into a straight cut across the field , that will bring 
 you just behind the house . " 

 " you be very kind , but it will take you out of your way . " 

 " no , it be in my way . so you be go to Mr. Avenel 's ? - a good old 
 gentleman . " 

 " I have   always hear so ; and Mrs. Avenel - " 

 " a particular superior woman , " say Richard . " any one else to ask 
 after ? - I know the family well . " 

 " no , thank you , sir . " 

 " they have a son , I believe ; but he be in America , be he not ? " 

 " I believe he be , sir . " 

 " I see the parson have keep faith with I mutter Richard . " 

 " if you can tell I anything about HIM , " say Leonard , " I should be very 
 glad . " 

 " why so , young man ? perhaps he be hang by this time . " 

 " hang ! " 

 " he be a sad dog , I be tell . " 

 " then you have be tell very falsely , " say Leonard , colour . 

 " a sad wild dog ; his parent be so glad when he cut and run , - go 
 off to the States . they say he make money ; but , if so , he neglect his 
 relation shamefully . " 

 " Sir , " say Leonard , " you be wholly misinformed . he have be most 
 generous to a relation who have little claim on he : and I never hear 
 his name mention but with love and praise . " 

 Richard instantly fall to whistle " Yankee Doodle , " and walk on 
 several pace without say a word . he then make a slight apology for 
 his impertinence , hop no offence , and , with his usual bold but astute 
 style of talk , contrive to bring out something of his companion 's mind . 
 he be evidently strike with the clearness and propriety with which 
 Leonard express himself , raise his eyebrow in surprise more than 
 once , and look he full in the face with an attentive and pleased 
 survey . Leonard have put on the new clothe with which Riccabocca and his 
 wife have provide he . they be those appropriate to a young country 
 tradesman in good circumstance ; but as Leonard do not think about the 
 clothe , so he have unconsciously something of the ease of the gentleman . 

 they now come into the field . Leonard pause before a slip of ground 
 sown with rye . 

 " I should have think grass - land would have answer well so near a 
 town , " say he . 

 " no doubt it would , " answer Richard ; " but they be sadly behind - hand 
 in these part . you see the great park yonder , on the other side of the 
 road ? that would answer well for rye than grass ; but then , what would 
 become of my Lord 's deer ? the aristocracy eat we up , young man . " 

 " but the aristocracy do not sow this piece with rye , I suppose ? " say 
 Leonard , smile . 

 " and what do you conclude from that ? " 

 " let every man look to his own ground , " say Leonard , with a cleverness 
 of repartee catch from Dr. Riccabocca . 

 " ' cute lad you be , " say Richard ; " and we will talk more of these matter 
 another time . " 

 they now come within sight of Mr. Avenel 's house . 

 " you can get through the gap in the hedge , by the old pollard - oak , " 
 say Richard ; " and come round by the front of the house . why , you be not 
 afraid , be you ? " 

 " I be a stranger . " 

 " shall I introduce you ? I tell you that I know the old couple . " 

 " oh , no , sir ! I would rather meet they alone . " 

 " go ; and - wait a bit - hark ye , young man , Mrs. Avenel be a cold - mannered 
 woman ; but do not   be abash by that . " Leonard thank the good - natured 
 stranger , cross the field , pass the gap , and pause a moment 
 under the stint shade of the old hollow - hearted oak . the raven be 
 return to their nest . at the sight of a human form under the tree 
 they wheel round and watch he afar . from the thick of the bough , 
 the young raven send their hoarse low cry . 




 CHAPTER XXV . 

 the young man enter the neat , prim , formal parlour . " you be welcome ! " 
 say Mrs. Avenel , in a firm voice . " the gentleman be heartily welcome , " 
 cry poor John . 

 " it be your grandson , Leonard Fairfield , " say Mrs. Avenel . but John , 
 who have rise with knock knee , gaze hard at Leonard , and then fall 
 on his breast , sob aloud , " Nora 's eye ! - he have a blink in his eye 
 like Nora 's . " 

 Mrs. Avenel approach with a steady step , and draw away the old man 
 tenderly . 

 " he be a poor creature , " she whisper to Leonard ; " you excite he . come 
 away , I will show you your room . " Leonard follow she up the stair , 
 and come into a room neatly and even prettily furnish . the carpet and 
 curtain be fade by the sun , and of old - fashioned pattern ; there be 
 a look about the room as if it have be long disuse . Mrs. Avenel sink 
 down on the first chair on enter . Leonard draw his arm round her 
 waist affectionately : " I fear that I have put you out sadly , my 
 dear grandmother . " Mrs. Avenel glide hastily from his arm , and her 
 countenance work much , every nerve in it twitch , as it be ; then , 
 place her hand on his lock , she say with passion , " God bless you , my 
 grandson , " and leave the room . 

 Leonard drop his knapsack on the floor , and look around he 
 wistfully . the room seem as if it have once be occupy by a female . 
 there be a work - box on the chest of drawer , and over it hang 
 shelf for book , suspend by ribbon that have once be blue , with 
 silk and fringe append to each shelf , and knot and tassel here and 
 there , - the taste of a woman , or rather of a girl , who seek to give a 
 grace to the commonest thing around she . with the mechanical habit of 
 a student , Leonard take down one or two of the volume still leave on the 
 shelf . he find Spenser 's " Faerie Queene , " Racine in French , Tasso 
 in Italian ; and on the fly - leaf of each volume , in the exquisite 
 handwriting familiar to his memory , the name " Leonora . " he kiss the 
 book , and replace they with a feeling akin both to tenderness and awe . 

 he have not be alone in his room more than a quarter of an hour before 
 the maid - servant knock at his door and summon he to tea . 

 Poor John have recover his spirit , and his wife sit by his side , 
 hold his hand in her . poor John be even gay . he ask many 
 question about his daughter Jane , and do not wait for the answer . 
 then he speak about the squire , whom he confound with Audley Egerton , 
 and talk of election and the blue party , and hop Leonard would 
 always be a good blue ; and then he fall to his tea and toast , and say 
 no more . 

 Mrs. Avenel speak little , but she eye Leonard askant , as it be , from 
 time to time ; and , after each glance , the nerve of the poor severe face 
 twitch again . 

 a little after nine o'clock , Mrs. Avenel light a candle , and place 
 it in Leonard 's hand , say , " you must be tired , - you know your own room 
 now . good - night . " 

 Leonard take the light , and , as be his will not with his mother , kiss 
 Mrs. Avenel on the cheek . then he take John 's hand and kiss he too . 
 the old man be half asleep , and murmur dreamily , " that be Nora . " 

 Leonard have retire to his room about half an hour , when Richard Avenel 
 enter the house softly , and join his parent . 

 " well , Mother ? " say he . 

 " well , Richard , you have see he ? " 

 " and like he . do you know he have a great look of poor Nora ? - more like 
 she than Jane . " 

 " yes ; he be handsomer than Jane ever be , but more like your father than 
 any one . John be so comely . you take to the boy , then ? " 

 " ay , that I do . just tell he in the morning that he be to go with a 
 gentleman who will be his friend , and do not   say more . the chaise shall 
 be at the door after breakfast . let he get into it : I shall wait for 
 he out of the town . what be the room you give he ? " 

 " the room you would not take . " 

 " the room in which Nora sleep ? oh , no ! I could not have sleep a wink 
 there . what a charm there be in that girl ! how we all love she ! but 
 she be too beautiful and good for we , - too good to live ! " 

 " none of we be too good , " say Mrs. Avenel , with great austerity , " and 
 I beg you will not talk in that way . Goodnight , - I must get your poor 
 father to bed . " 

 when Leonard open his eye the next morning , they rest on the face 
 of Mrs. Avenel , which be bend over his pillow . but it be long 
 before he could recognize that countenance , so changed be its 
 expression , - so tender , so mother - like . Nay , the face of his own mother 
 have never seem to he so soft with a mother 's passion . 

 " ah ! " he murmur , half rise , and fling his young arm round her 
 neck . Mrs. Avenel , this time take by surprise , warmly return the 
 embrace ; she clasp he to her breast , she kiss he again and again . 
 at length , with a quick start , she escape , and walk up and down the 
 room , press her hand tightly together . when she halt , her face have 
 recover its usual severity and cold precision . 

 " it be time for you to rise , Leonard , " say she . " you will leave we 
 to - day . a gentleman have promise to take charge of you , and do for you 
 more than we can . a chaise will be at the door soon , - make haste . " 

 John be absent from the breakfast - table . his wife say that he never 
 rise till late , and must not be disturb . 

 the meal be scarcely over before a chaise and pair come to the door . 

 " you must not keep the chaise waiting , - the gentleman be very punctual . " 

 " but he be not come . " 

 " no ; he have walk on before , and will get in after you be out of the 
 town . " 

 " what be his name , and why should he care for I , Grandmother ? " 

 " he will tell you himself . be quick . " 

 " but you will bless I again , Grandmother ? I love you already . " 

 " I do bless you , " say Mrs. Avenel , firmly . " be honest and good , and 
 beware of the first false step . " she press his hand with a convulsive 
 grasp , and lead he to the outer door . 

 the postboy clank his whip , the chaise rattle off . Leonard put his 
 head out of the window to catch a last glimpse of the old woman ; but the 
 bough of the pollard - oak , and its gnarled decay trunk , hide she from 
 his eye , and look as he would , till the road turn , he see but the 
 melancholy tree . 




 BOOK FIFTH . 




 INITIAL chapter . 

 contain MR . CAXTON 's UNAVAILING caution not to be DULL . 

 " I hope , Pisistratus , " say my father , " that you do not intend to be 
 dull ? " 

 " heaven forbid , sir ! what could make you ask such a question ? intend ! 
 no ! if I be dull it be from innocence . " 

 " a very long discourse upon knowledge ! " say my father ; " very long ! I 
 should cut it out . " 

 I look upon my father as a byzantian sage might have look on a 
 Vandal . " cut it out ! " 

 " stop the action , sir ! " say my father , dogmatically . 

 " Action ! but a novel be not a drama . " 

 " no ; it be a great deal long , - twenty time as long , I dare say , " 
 reply Mr. Caxton , with a sigh . 

 " well , sir , well ! I think my Discourse upon Knowledge have much to do 
 with the subject , be vitally essential to the subject ; do not stop the 
 action , - only explain and elucidate the action . and I be astonish , 
 sir , that you , a scholar , and a cultivator of knowledge - " 

 " there , there ! " cry my father , deprecatingly . " I yield , I yield ! what 
 well could I expect when I set up for a critic ? what author ever live 
 that do not fly into a passion , even with his own father , if his father 
 presume to say , ' cut out ' ! " 

 MRS . CAXTON . - " my dear Austin , I be sure Pisistratus do not mean to 
 offend you , and I have no doubt he will take your - " 

 PISISTRATUS   hastily . - " advice for the future , certainly . I will 
 quicken the action , and - " 

 " go on with the novel , " whisper Roland , look up from his eternal 
 account - book . " we have lose l200 by our barley ! " 

 Therewith I plunge my pen into the ink , and my thought into the " Fair 
 Shadowland . " 




 CHAPTER II . 

 " HALT , cry a voice ; and not a little surprised be Leonard when the 
 stranger who have accost he the precede evening get into the chaise . 

 " well , " say Richard , " I be not the sort of man you expect , eh ? take 
 time to recover yourself . " and with these word Richard draw forth a 
 book from his pocket , throw himself back , and begin to read . Leonard 
 steal many a glance at the acute , hardy , handsome face of his companion , 
 and gradually recognize a family likeness to poor John , in whom , 
 despite age and infirmity , the trace of no common share of physical 
 beauty be still evident . and , with that quick link in idea which 
 mathematical aptitude bestow , the young student at once conjecture 
 that he see before he his uncle Richard . he have the discretion , 
 however , to leave that gentleman free to choose his own time for 
 introduce himself , and silently revolve the new thought produce

 quickness , - sometimes cut the leaf of the book with his penknife , 
 sometimes tear they open with his forefinger , sometimes skip 
 whole page altogether . thus he gallop to the end of the volume , flung 
 it aside , light his cigar , and begin to talk . he put many question to 
 Leonard relative to his rearing , and especially to the mode by which he 
 have acquire his education ; and Leonard , confirm in the idea that he 
 be reply to a kinsman , answer frankly . 

 Richard do not think it strange that Leonard should have acquire so 
 much instruction with so little direct tuition . Richard Avenel himself 
 have be tutor to himself . he have live too long with our go - ahead 
 brother who stride the world on the other side the Atlantic with

 glorious fever for reading . but it be for a reading wholly different 
 from that which be familiar to Leonard . the book he read must be new ;

 he fancy that new book necessarily contain new idea , - a common 
 mistake , - and our lucky adventurer be the man of his day . 

 tired with talk , he at length chuck the book he have run through to

 absorb train of thought , part pecuniary , part ambitious . 

 Leonard find the book interesting : it be one of the numerous work , 
 half - statistic , half - declamatory , relate to the condition of the 
 working class , which peculiarly distinguish our century , and ought to 
 bind together rich and poor , by prove the grave attention which modern 
 society bestow upon all that can affect the welfare of the last . 

 " dull stuff ! theory ! claptrap ! " say Richard , rouse himself from his 
 revery at last ; " it can not   interest you . "

 for it relate to the work class , and I be one of they . " 

 " you be yesterday , but you may not   be to - morrow , " answer Richard , 
 good - humouredly , and pat he on the shoulder . " you see , my lad , that 
 it be the middle class which ought to govern the country . what the book 
 say about the ignorance of country magistrate be very good ; but the 
 man write pretty considerable trash when he want to regulate the 
 number of hour a free - bear boy should work at a factory , - only ten 
 hour a day - pooh ! and so lose two hour to the nation ! labour be 
 wealth ; and if we could get man to work twenty - four hour a day , 
 we should be just twice as rich . if the march of civilization be to 
 proceed , " continue Richard , loftily , " man , and boy too , must not lie 
 a bed do nothing , all night , sir . " then , with a complacent tone , " we 
 shall get to the twenty - four hour at last ; and , by gad , we must , or we 
 sha'n't   flog the Europeans as we do now . " 

 on arrive at the inn at which Richard have first make acquaintance with 
 Mr. Dale , the coach by which he have intend to perform the rest of the 
 journey be find to be full . Richard continue to perform the journey 
 in postchaise , not without some grumbling at the expense , and incessant 
 order to the post - boy to make the good of the way . " slow country this 
 in spite of all its brag , " say he , - " very slow . time be money - they 
 know that in the States ; for why ? they be all man of business there . 
 always slow in a country where a parcel of lazy , idle lord and duke 
 and baronet seem to think ' time be pleasure . ' " 

 towards evening the chaise approach the confine of a very large town , 
 and Richard begin to grow fidgety . his easy , cavalier air be abandon . 
 he withdraw his leg from the window , out of which they have be 
 luxuriously dangle , pull down his waistcoat , buckle more tightly 
 his stock ; it be clear that he be resume the decorous dignity that 
 belong to state . he be like a monarch who , after travel happy and 
 incognito , return to his capital . Leonard divine at once that they 
 be near their journey 's end . 

 humble foot - passenger now look at the chaise , and touch their hat . 
 Richard return the salutation with a nod , - a nod less gracious than 
 condescend . the chaise turn rapidly to the left , and stop before 
 a small lodge , very new , very white , adorn with two doric column 
 in stucco , and flank by a large pair of gate . " Hollo ! " cry the 
 post - boy , and crack his whip . 

 two child be play before the lodge , and some clothe be 
 hang out to dry on the shrub and pale round the neat little 
 building . 

 " hang those brat ! they be actually play , " growl Dick . " as I live , 
 the jade have be wash again ! stop , boy ! " during this soliloquy , a 
 good - look young woman have rush from the door , slap the child 
 as , catch sight of the chaise , they run towards the house , open the 
 gate , and drop a courtesy to the ground , seem to wish that she 
 could drop into it altogether ; so frightened and so trembling seem 
 she to shrink from the wrathful face which the master now put out of the 
 window . 

 " do I tell you , or do I not , " say Dick , " that I would not have those 
 horrid , disreputable cub of yours play just before my lodge gate ? " 

 " please , sir - " 

 " do not   answer I . and do I tell you , or do I not , that the next time I 
 see you make a drying - ground of my lilac , you should go out , neck and 
 crop - " 

 " oh , please , sir - " 

 " you leave my lodge next Saturday ! drive on , boy . the ingratitude and 
 insolence of those common people be disgraceful to human nature , " 
 mutter Richard , with an accent of the bitter misanthropy . 

 the chaise wheel along the smooth and fresh of gravel road , and 
 through field of the fine land , in the high state of cultivation . 
 Rapid as be Leonard 's survey , his rural eye detect the sign of a 
 master in the art agronomial . Hitherto he have consider the squire 's 
 model farm as the near approach to good husbandry he have see ; for 
 Jackeymo 's fine skill be develop rather on the minute scale of 
 market - gardening than what can fairly be call husbandry . but 
 the squire 's farm be degrade by many old - fashioned notion , and 
 concession to the whim of the eye , which would not be find in model 
 farm nowadays , - large tangled hedgerow , which , though they constitute 
 one of the beauty most picturesque in old England , make sad deduction 
 from produce ; great tree , overshadow the corn and harbour the 
 bird ; little patch of rough sward leave to waste ; and angle of 
 woodland run into field , expose they to rabbit and block out 
 the sun . these and such like blot on a gentleman - farmer 's agriculture , 
 common - sense and Giacomo have make clear to the acute comprehension of 
 Leonard . no such fault be perceptible in Richard Avenel 's domain . the 
 field lie in broad division , the hedge be clip and narrow 
 into their proper destination of mere boundary . not a blade of wheat 
 wither under the cold shade of a tree ; not a yard of land lie waste ; 
 not a weed be to be see , not a thistle to waft its baleful seed 
 through the air : some young plantation be place , not where the

 kind , - beauty at once recognizable to the initiate , beauty of use 
 and profit , beauty that could bear a monstrous high rent . and Leonard 
 utter a cry of admiration which thrill through the heart of Richard 
 Avenel . 

 " this be farm ! " say the villager . 

 " well , I guess it be , " answer Richard , all his ill - humour vanishing . 
 " you should have see the land when I buy it . but we new man , as they 
 call we   damn their impertinence !   be the new blood of this country . " 

 Richard Avenel never say anything more true . long may the new blood 
 circulate through the vein of the mighty giantess ; but let the grand 
 heart be the same as it have beat for proud age . 

 the chaise now pass through a pretty shrubbery , and the house come 
 into gradual view , - a house with a portico , all the office carefully 
 thrust out of sight . 

 the postboy dismount and ring the bell . 

 " I almost think they be go to keep I wait , " say Mr. Richard , 
 well - nigh in the very word of Louis XIV . but the fear be not
