 " where 's George ? why do he not come to the door ? " ask Richard ; 
 descend from the chaise slowly , and lean on the servant 's 
 outstretche arm with as much precaution as if he have have the gout . 

 fortunately , George here come into sight , settle himself hastily into 
 his livery coat . 

 " see to the thing , both of you , " say Richard , as he pay the postboy . 

 Leonard stand on the gravel sweep , gaze at the square white house . 

 " handsome elevation - classical , I take it , eh ? " say Richard , join 
 he . " but you should see the office . " he then , with familiar kindness , 
 take Leonard by the arm , and draw he within . he show he the hall , 
 with a carve mahogany stand for hat ; he show he the drawing - room , 
 and point out all its beauty ; though it be summer , the drawing - room 
 look cold , as will look room newly furnish , with wall newly 
 paper , in house newly build . the furniture be handsome , and suit 
 to the rank of a rich trader . there be no pretence about it , and 
 therefore no vulgarity , which be more than can be say for the house 
 of many an honourable Mrs. Somebody in Mayfair , with room twelve foot 
 square , ebokeful of buhl , that would have have its proper place in

 book - case , and plate glass , and the fashionable author handsomely 
 bind . your new man be much well friend to live author than 
 your old family who live in the country , and at most subscribe to 
 a book - club . then Richard take he up - stair , and lead he through 
 the bedroom , - all very clean and comfortable , and with every modern 
 convenience ; and pause in a very pretty single gentleman 's chamber , 
 say , " this be your den . and now , can you guess who I be ? " 

 " no one but my uncle Richard could be so kind , " answer Leonard . 

 but the compliment do not flatter Richard . he be extremely 
 disconcert and disappointed . he have hop that he should be take for 
 a lord at least , forgetful of all that he have say in disparagement of 
 lord . 

 " Fish ! " say he at last , bite his lip , ' so you do not   think that I look 
 like a gentleman ? Come , now , speak honestly . " 

 Leonard , wonderingly , see he have give pain , and with the good breeding

 your heart , sir , and your likeness to my grandfather , - otherwise I 
 should never have presume to fancy we could be relation . " 

 " Hum ! " answer Richard . " you can just wash your hand , and then come

 colour up to the temple . 

 " Jarvis , " say he , mildly , " Jarvis , put I in mind to have these 
 inexpressible alter . " 




 CHAPTER III . 

 a propos of the inexpressible , Mr. Richard do not forget to provide 
 his nephew with a much large wardrobe than could have be thrust into 
 Dr. Riccabocca 's knapsack . there be a very good tailor in the town , and 
 the clothe be very well make . and , but for an air more ingenuous , 
 and a cheek that , despite study and night vigil , retain much of the 
 sunburned bloom of the rustic , Leonard Fairfield might now have almost 
 pass , without disparage comment , by the bow - window at White 's . 
 Richard burst into an immoderate fit of laughter when he first see the 
 watch which the poor Italian have bestow upon Leonard ; but to atone 
 for the laughter , he make he a present of a very pretty substitute , and 
 bid he " lock up his turnip . " Leonard be more hurt by the jeer at his 
 old patron 's gift than pleased by his uncle 's . but Richard Avenel have 
 no conception of sentiment . it be not for many day that Leonard could 
 reconcile himself to his uncle 's manner . not that the peasant could 
 pretend to judge of its mere conventional defect ; but there be an ill 
 breeding to which , whatever our rank and nurture , we be almost equally 
 sensitive , - the ill breeding that come from want of consideration for 
 other . now , the squire be as homely in his way as Richard Avenel , but 
 the squire 's bluntness rarely hurt the feeling ; and when it do so , the 
 squire perceive and hasten to repair his blunder . but Mr. Richard , 
 whether kind or cross , be always wound you in some little delicate 
 fibre , - not from malice , but from the absence of any little delicate 
 fibre of his own . he be really , in many respect , a most excellent 
 man , and certainly a very valuable citizen - ; but his merit want the 
 fine tint and fluent curve that constitute beauty of character . he be 
 honest , but sharp in his practice , and with a keen eye to his interest . 
 he be just , but as a matter of business . he make no allowance , and do 
 not leave to his justice the large margin of tenderness and mercy . he 
 be generous , but rather from an idea of what be due to himself 
 than with much thought of the pleasure he give to other ; and he even 
 regard generosity as a capital put out to interest . he expect 
 a great deal of gratitude in return , and , when he oblige a man , 
 consider that he have buy a slave . every needy voter know where to 
 come , if he want relief or a loan ; but woe to he if he have venture 
 to express hesitation when Mr. Avenel tell he how he must vote . 

 in this town Richard have settle after his return from America , in which 
 country he have enrich himself , - first , by spirit and industry , 
 lastly , by bold speculation and good luck . he invest his fortune in 
 business , - become a partner in a large brewery , soon buy out his 
 associate , and then take a principal share in a flourish corn - mill . 
 he prosper rapidly , - buy a property of some two or three hundred 
 acre , build a house , and resolve to enjoy himself , and make a figure . 
 he have now become the lead man of the town , and the boast to Audley 
 Egerton that he could return one of the member , perhaps both , be by 
 no mean an exaggerated estimate of his power . nor be his proposition , 
 accord to his own view , so unprincipled as it appear to the 
 statesman . he have take a great dislike to both the sit member , - a 
 dislike natural to a sensible man of moderate politic , who have 
 something to lose . for Mr. Slappe , the active member , who be 
 head - over - ear in debt , be one of the furious democrats - rare before 
 the Reform Bill , - and whose opinion be hold dangerous even by the 
 mass of a liberal constituency ; while Mr. Sleekie , the gentleman member 
 who lay by L5000 every year from his dividend in the Funds , be one of 
 those man whom Richard justly pronounce to be " humbug , " - man who curry 
 favour with the extreme party by vote for measure sure not to 
 be carry ; while if there be the least probability of come to a 
 decision that would lower the money market . Mr. Sleekie be seize with 
 a well - time influenza . those politician be common enough now . Propose 
 to march to the Millennium , and they be your man . ask they to march a 
 quarter of a mile , and they fall to feel their pocket , and tremble 
 for fear of the footpad . they be never so joyful as when there be no 
 chance of a victory . do they beat the minister , they would be carry 
 out of the House in a fit . 

 Richard Avenel - despise both these gentleman , and not take kindly 
 to the whig since the great Whig leader be lord - have look with 
 a friendly eye to the government as it then exist , and especially 
 to Audley Egerton , the enlighten representative of commerce . but in 
 give Audley and his colleague the benefit of his influence , through 
 conscience , he think it all fair and right to have a quid pro quo , 
 and , as he have so frankly confess , it be his whim to rise up " Sir 
 Richard . " for this worthy citizen abuse the aristocracy much on the 
 same principle as the fair Olivia depreciated squire Thornhill , - he have 
 a sneak affection for what he abuse . the society of Screwstown be , 
 like most provincial capital , compose of two class , - the commercial 
 and the exclusive . these last dwelt chiefly apart , around the ruin of 
 an old abbey ; they affect its antiquity in their pedigree , and 
 have much of its ruin in their finance . widow of rural thane in the 
 neighbourhood , genteel spinster , officer retire on half - pay , young 
 son of rich squire , who have now become old bachelor , - in short , 
 a very respectable , proud , aristocratic set , who think more of 
 themselves than do all the Gowers and howard , Courtenays and Seymours , 
 put together . it have early be the ambition of Richard Avenel to 
 be admit into this sublime coterie ; and , strange to say , he have 
 partially succeed . he be never more happy than when he be ask to 
 their card - party , and never more unhappy than when he be actually 
 there . various circumstance combine to raise Mr. Avenel into this 
 elevated society . first , he be unmarried , still very handsome , and in 
 that society there be a large proportion of unwedded female . secondly , 
 he be the only rich trader in Screwstown who keep a good cook , and 
 profess to give dinner , and the half - pay captain and colonel 
 swallow the host for the sake of the venison . thirdly , and 
 principally , all these exclusive abhor the two sit member , and 
 " idem nolle idem velle de republica , ea firma amicitia est ; " that be , 
 congeniality in politic piece porcelain and crockery together well 
 than the good diamond cement . the sturdy Richard Avenel , who value 
 himself on american independence , hold these lady and gentleman in 
 an awe that be truly brahminical . whether it be that , in England , 
 all notion , even of liberty , be mix up historically , traditionally , 
 socially , with that fine and subtle element of aristocracy which , like 
 the press , be the air we breathe ; or whether Richard imagine that 
 he really become magnetically imbue with the virtue of these silver 
 penny and gold seven - shill piece , distinct from the vulgar coinage 
 in popular use , it be hard to say . but the truth must be tell , - Richard 
 Avenel be a notable tuft - hunter . he have a great longing to marry out of 
 this society ; but he have not yet see any one sufficiently high - bear and 
 high - breed to satisfy his aspiration . in the meanwhile , he have convince 
 himself that his way would be smooth could he offer to make his ultimate 
 choice " my Lady ; " and he feel that it would be a proud hour in his life 
 when he could walk before stiff Colonel Pompley to the sound of " Sir 
 Richard . " still , however disappointed at the ill - success of his 
 bluff diplomacy with Mr. Egerton , and however yet cherish the most 
 vindictive resentment against that individual , he do not , as many would 
 have do , throw up his political conviction out of personal spite . 
 he reserve his private grudge for some special occasion , and continue 
 still to support the Administration , and to hate one of the minister . 

 but , duly to appreciate the value of Richard Avenel , and in just 
 counterpoise to all his foible , one ought to have see what he have 
 effect for the town . well might he boast of " new blood ; " he have do 
 as much for the town as he have for his field . his energy , his quick 
 comprehension of public utility , back by his wealth and bold , 
 bullying , imperious character , have speed the work of civilization as if 
 with the celerity and force of a steam - engine . 

 if the town be so well paved and so well light , if half - a - dozen 
 squalid lane have be transform into a stately street , if half the 
 town no long depend on tank for their water , if the poor - rate be 
 reduce one - third , praise to the brisk new blood which Richard Avenel 
 have infuse into vestry and corporation . and his example itself be so 
 contagious ! 

 " there be not a plate - glass window in the town when I come into it , " 
 say Richard Avenel ; " and now look down the High Street ! " he take the 
 credit to himself , and justly ; for though his own business do not 
 require window of plate - glass , he have awaken the spirit of enterprise 
 which adorn a whole city . 

 Mr. Avenel do not present Leonard to his friend for more than a 
 fortnight . he allow he to wear off his rust . he then give a grand 
 dinner , at which his nephew be formally introduce , and , to his great 
 wrath and disappointment , never open his lip . how could he , poor 
 youth , when Miss Clarina Mowbray only talk upon high life , till 
 proud Colonel Pompley go in state through the history of the Siege of 
 Seringapatam ? 




 CHAPTER IV . 

 while Leonard accustom himself gradually to the splendour that 
 surround he , and often turn with a sigh to the remembrance of his 
 mother 's cottage and the sparkle fount in the Italian 's flowery 
 garden , we will make with thee , o reader , a rapid flight to the 
 metropolis , and drop ourselves amidst the gay group that loiter along 
 the dusty ground or loll over the roadside paling of Hyde Park . the 
 season be still at its height ; but the short day of fashionable London 
 life , which commence two hour after noon , be in its decline . 

 the crowd in Rotten row begin to thin . near the statue of Achilles , and 
 apart from all other lounger , a gentleman , with one hand thrust into 
 his waistcoat , and the other rest on his cane , gaze listlessly on 
 the horseman and carriage in the brilliant ring . he be still in the 
 prime of life , at the age when man be usually the most social , - when the 
 acquaintance of youth have ripen into friendship , and a personage of 
 some rank and fortune have become a well - know feature in the mobile 
 face of society . but though , when his contemporary be boy scarce 
 at college , this gentleman have blaze foremost amongst the prince of 
 fashion , and though he have all the quality of nature and circumstance 
 which either retain fashion to the last , or exchange its false celebrity 
 for a graver repute , he stand as a stranger in that throng of his 
 countryman . Beauties whirl by to the toilet , statesman pass on to 
 the senate , dandy take flight to the club ; and neither nod , nor 
 beck , nor wreathe smile say to the solitary spectator , " follow 
 we , - thou art one of our set . " now and then some middle - aged beau , 
 near the post of the loiterer , turn round to look again ; but the 
 second glance seem to dissipate the recognition of the first , and the 
 beau silently continue his way . 

 " by the tomb of my father ! " say the solitary to himself , " I know now 
 what a dead man might feel if he come to life again , and take a peep at 
 the living . " 

 Time pass on , - the evening shade descend fast . our stranger in 
 London have well - nigh the Park to himself . he seem to breathe more 
 freely as he see that the space be so clear . 

 " there be oxygen in the atmosphere now , " say he , half aloud ; " and I can 
 walk without breathe in the gaseous fume of the multitude . oh , those 
 chemist - what dolt they be ! they tell we that crowd taint the air , 
 but they never guess why ! Pah , it be not the lung that poison the 
 element , - it be the reek of bad heart . when a periwigpated fellow 
 breathe on I , I swallow a mouthful of care . allon ! my friend Nero ; 
 now for a stroll . " he touch with his cane a large Newfoundland dog , 
 who lie stretch near his foot , and dog and man go slow through the 
 grow twilight , and over the brown dry turf . at length our solitary 
 pause , and throw himself on a bench under a tree . " half - past eight ! " 
 say he , look at his watch , " one may smoke one 's cigar without 
 shock the world . " 

 he take out his cigar - case , strike a light , and in another moment 
 recline at length on the bench , seem absorb in regard the smoke , 
 that scarce colour ere it vanish into air . 

 " it be the most bareface lie in the world , my Nero , " say he , 
 address his dog , " this boasted liberty of man ! now , here be I , a 
 free - bear Englishman , a citizen of the world , care - I often say to 
 myself - care not a jot for Kaiser or Mob ; and yet I no more dare smoke 
 this cigar in the Park at half - past six , when all the world be abroad , 
 than I dare pick my Lord Chancellor 's pocket , or hit the archbishop 
 of Canterbury a thump on the nose . yet no law in England forbid I my 
 cigar , Nero ! what be law at half - past eight be not crime at six and 
 a half ! Britannia say , ' Man , thou art free , and she lie like a 
 commonplace woman . o Nero , Nero ! you enviable dog ! you serve but from 
 like . no thought of the world cost you one wag of the tail . your big 
 heart and true instinct suffice you for reason and law . you would want 
 nothing to your felicity , if in these moment of ennui you would but 
 smoke a cigar . try it , Nero ! - try it ! " and , rise from his incumbent 
 posture , he seek to force the end of the weed between the tooth of the 
 dog . 

 while thus gravely engage , two figure have approach the place . 
 the one be a man who seem weak and sickly . his threadbare coat be 
 button to the chin , but hang large on his shrunken breast . the other 
 be a girl , who might be from twelve to fourteen , on whose arm he lean 
 heavily . her cheek be wan , and there be a patient , sad look on her 
 face , which seem so settle that you would think she could never have 
 know the mirthfulness of childhood . 

 " pray rest here , Papa , " say the child , softly ; and she point to 
 the bench , without take heed of its pre - occupant , who now , indeed , 
 confine to one corner of the seat , be almost hide by the shadow of 
 the tree . 

 the man sit down , with a feeble sigh , and then , observe the stranger , 
 raise his hat , and say , in that tone of voice which betray the usage 
 of polished society , " forgive I if I intrude on you , sir . " 

 the stranger look up from his dog , and see that the girl be 
 stand , rise at once , as if to make room for she on the bench . 

 but still the girl do not heed he . she hang over her father , and wipe 
 his brow tenderly with a little kerchief which she take from her own 
 neck for the purpose . 

 Nero , delight to escape the cigar , have take to some unwieldy curvet 
 and gambol , to vent the excitement into which he have be throw ; and 
 now return , approach the bench with a low growl of surprise , and 
 sniff at the intruder of his master 's privacy . 

 " come here , sir , " say the master . " you need not fear he , " he add , 
 address himself to the girl . 

 but the girl , without turn round to he , cry in a voice rather of 
 anguish than alarm , " he have faint ! Father ! Father ! " 

 the stranger kick aside his dog , which be in the way , and loosen 
 the poor man 's stiff military stock . while thus charitably engage , the 
 moon break out , and the light fall full on the pale , careworn face of 
 the unconscious sufferer . 

 " this face seem not unfamiliar to I , though sadly change , " say the 
 stranger to himself ; and bend towards the girl , who have sink on her 
 knee , and be chafe her father 's hand , he ask , " my child , what be 
 your father 's name ? " 

 the child continue her task , too absorb to answer . 

 the stranger put his hand on her shoulder , and repeat the question . 

 " Digby , " answer the child , almost unconsciously ; and as she speak the 
 man 's sens begin to return . in a few minute more he have sufficiently 
 recover to falter forth his thank to the stranger . but the last take 
 his hand , and say , in a voice at once tremulous and soothing , " be it 
 possible that I see once more an old brother in arm ? Algernon Digby , I 
 do not forget you ; but it seem England have forget . " 

 a hectic flush spread over the soldier 's face , and he look away from 
 the speaker as he answer , - 

 " my name be Digby , it be true , sir ; but I do not think we have meet 
 before . Come , Helen , I be well now , - we will go home . " 

 " try and play with that great dog , my child , " say the strange , - " I 
 want to talk with your father . " 

 the child bow her submissive head , and move away ; but she do not 
 play with the dog . 

 " I must reintroduce myself formally , I see , " quoth the stranger . " you 
 be in the same regiment with myself , and my name be L'Estrange . " 

 " my Lord , " say the soldier , rise , " forgive I that - " 

 " I do not   think that it be the fashion to call I ' my lord ' at the 
 mess - table . come , what have happen to you ? - on half - pay ? " 

 Mr. Digby shake his head mournfully . 

 " Digby , old fellow , can you lend I L100 ? " say Lord L'Estrange , 
 clap his ci - devant brother - officer on the shoulder , and in a tone of 
 voice that seem like a boy 's , so impudent be it , and devil - I - garish . 
 " no ! well , that be lucky , for I can lend it to you . " Mr. Digby burst into 
 tear . 

 Lord L'Estrange do not seem to observe the emotion , but go on 
 carelessly , - 

 " perhaps you do not   know that , besides be heir to a father who be not 
 only very rich , but very liberal , I inherit , on come of age , from a 
 maternal relation , a fortune so large that it would bore I to death 
 if I be oblige to live up to it . but in the day of our old 
 acquaintance , I fear we be both sad extravagant fellow , and I dare 
 say I borrow of you pretty freely . " 

 " I ! oh , Lord L'Estrange ! " 

 " you have marry since then , and reform , I suppose . tell I , old 
 friend , all about it . " 

 Mr. Digby , who by this time have succeed in restore some calm to his 
 shattered nerve , now rise , and say in brief sentence , but clear , firm 
 tone , - 

 " my Lord , it be idle to talk of I , - useless to help I . I be fast 
 die . but my child there , my only child "   he pause for an instant , and 
 go on rapidly . " I have relation in a distant county , if I could but 
 get to they ; I think they would , at least , provide for she . this have 
 be for week my hope , my dream , my prayer . I can not afford the journey 
 except by your help . I have beg without shame for myself ; shall I be 
 ashamed , then , to beg for she ? " 

 " Digby , " say L'Estrange , with some grave alteration of manner , " talk 
 neither of die nor beg . you be nearer death when the ball 
 whistle round you at Waterloo . if soldier meet soldier and say 
 ' Friend , thy purse , ' it be not beg , but brotherhood . Ashamed ! by the 
 soul of Belisarius ! if I need money , I would stand at a crossing with 
 my Waterloo medal over my breast , and say to each sleek citizen I have 
 help to save from the sword of the Frenchman , ' it be your shame if I 
 starve . ' now , lean upon I ; I see you should be at home : which way ? " 

 the poor soldier point his hand towards Oxford Street , and reluctantly 
 accept the proffered arm . 

 " and when you return from your relation , you will call on I ? 
 what - hesitate ? come , promise . " 

 " I will . " 

 " on your honour . " 

 " if I live , on my honour . " 

 " I be stay at present at Knightsbridge , with my father ; but you will 
 always hear of my address at No . - , Grosvenor Square , Mr. Egerton 's . so 
 you have a long journey before you ? " 

 " very long . " 

 " do not fatigue yourself , - travel slowly . Ho , you foolish child ! I see 
 you be jealous of I . your father have another arm to spare you . " 

 thus talk , and get but short answer , Lord L'Estrange continue 
 to exhibit those whimsical peculiarity of character , which have 
 obtain for he the repute of heartlessness in the world . perhaps the 
 reader may think the world be not in the right ; but if ever the world 
 do judge rightly of the character of a man who do not live for 
 the world nor talk of the world nor feel with the world , it will be 
 century after the soul of Harley L'Estrange have do with this planet . 




 CHAPTER V. 

 Lord L'Estrange part company with Mr. Digby at the entrance of Oxford 
 Street . the father and child there take a cabriolet . Mr. Digby direct 
 the driver to go down the Edgware Road . he refuse to tell L'Estrange 
 his address , and this with such evident pain , from the sore of pride , 
 that L'Estrange could not press the point . remind the soldier of his 
 promise to call , Harley thrust a pocket - book into his hand , and walk 
 off hastily towards Grosvenor Square . 

 he reach Audley Egerton 's door just as that gentleman be get out 
 of his carriage ; and the two friend enter the house together . 

 " do the nation take a nap to - night ? " ask L'Estrange . " poor old 
 lady ! she hear so much of her affair , that she may well boast of her 
 constitution : it must be of iron . " 

 " the House be still sit , " answer Audley , seriously , and with small 
 heed of his friend 's witticism . " but it be not a Government motion , and 
 the division will be late , so I come home ; and if I have not find you 
 here , I should have go into the Park to look for you . " 

 " yes ; one always know where to find I at this hour , nine o'clock 
 P.M. , cigar , Hyde Park . there be not a man in England so regular in his 
 habit . " 

 here the friend reach a drawing - room in which the member of 
 parliament seldom sit , for his private apartment be all on the 
 ground - floor . 

 " but it be the strange whim of your , Harley , " say he . 

 " what ? " 

 " to affect detestation of ground - floor . " 

 " affect ! o sophisticated man , of the earth , earthy ! affect ! - nothing 
 less natural to the human soul than a ground - floor . we be quite far 
 enough from Heaven , mount as many stair as we will , without grovel 
 by preference . " 

 " accord to that symbolical view of the case , " say Audley , " you 
 should lodge in an attic . " 

 " so I would , but that I abhor new slipper . as for hairbrush , I be 
 indifferent . " 

 " what have slipper and hair - brush to do with attic ? " 

 " try ! make your bed in an attic , and the next morning you will have 
 neither slipper nor hair - brush ! " 

 " what shall I have do with they ? " 

 " shy they at the cat ! " 

 " what odd thing you say , Harley ! " 

 " Odd ! by Apollo and his nine spinster ! there be no human being who have 
 so little imagination as a distinguished member of parliament . answer I 
 this , thou solemn right Honourable , - Hast thou climb to the height of 
 august contemplation ? Hast thou gaze on the star with the rapt eye 
 of song ? Hast thou dream of a love know to the angel , or seek to 
 seize in the Infinite the mystery of life ? " 

 " not I indeed , my poor Harley . " 

 " then no wonder , poor Audley , that you can not conjecture why he who 
 make his bed in an attic , disturb by base catterwaul , shy his 
 slipper at cat . bring a chair into the balcony . Nero spoil my cigar 
 to - night . I be go to smoke now . you never smoke . you can look on the 
 shrub in the square . " 

 Audley slightly shrug his shoulder , but he follow his friend 's 
 counsel and example , and bring his chair into the balcony . Nero come 
 too , but at sight and smell of the cigar prudently retreat , and take 
 refuge under the table . 

 " Audley Egerton , I want something from Government . " 

 " I be delighted to hear it . " 

 " there be a cornet in my regiment , who would have do well not to 
 have come into it . we be , for the most part of we , puppy and fop . " 

 " you all fight well , however . " 

 " puppy and fop do fight well . vanity and valour generally go 
 together . CAesar , who scratch his head with due care of his scanty 
 curl , and even in die thought of the fold in his toga ; Walter 
 Raleigh , who could not walk twenty yard because of the gem in his 
 shoe ; Alcibiades , who lounge into the Agora with dove in his bosom , 
 and an apple in his hand ; Murat , bedizen in gold lace and fur ; and 
 Demetrius , the City - Taker , who make himself up like a french marquise , 
 be all pretty good fellow at fight . a slovenly hero like Cromwell 
 be a paradox in nature , and a marvel in history . but to return to my 
 cornet . we be rich ; he be poor . when the pot of clay swim down the 
 stream with the brass - pot , it be sure of a smash . man say Digby be 
 stingy ; I see he be extravagant . but every one , I fear , would be rather 
 thought stingy than poor . Bref - I leave the army , and see he no more 
 till to - night . there be never shabby poor gentleman on the stage more 
 awfully shabby , more pathetically gentleman . but , look ye , this man have 
 fight for England . it be no child 's play at Waterloo , let I tell you , 
 Mr. Egerton ; and , but for such man , you would be at good a sous prefet , 
 and your parliament a Provincial Assembly . you must do something for 
 Digby . what shall it be ? " 

 " why , really , my dear Harley , this man be no great friend of your , 
 eh ? " 

 " if he be , he would not want the Government to help he , - he would not 
 be ashamed of take money from I . " 

 " that be all very fine , Harley ; but there be so many poor officer , 
 and so little to give . it be the most difficult thing in the world 
 that which you ask I . indeed , I know nothing can be do : he have his 
 half - pay ? " 

 " I think not ; or , if he have it , no doubt it all go on his debt . 
 that be nothing to we : the man and his child be starve . " 

 " but if it be his own fault , - if he have be imprudent ? " 

 " ah , well , well ; where the devil be Nero ? " 

 " I be so sorry I can not   oblige you . if it be anything else - " 

 " there be something else . my valet - I can not   turn he adrift - excellent 
 fellow , but get drunk now and then . will you find he a place in the 
 Stamp Office ? " 

 " with pleasure . " 

 " no , now I think of it , the man know my way : I must keep he . but my 
 old wine - merchant - civil man , never dun - be a bankrupt . I be under 
 great obligation to he , and he have a very pretty daughter . do you 
 think you could thrust he into some small place in the Colonies , or 
 make he a King 's Messenger , or something of the sort ? " 

 " if you very much wish it , no doubt I can . " 

 " my dear Audley , I be but feel my way : the fact be , I want something 
 for myself . " 

 " ah , that indeed give I pleasure ! " cry Egerton , with animation . 

 " the mission to Florence will soon be vacant , - I know it privately . the 
 place would quite suit I . pleasant city ; the good fig in Italy ; very 
 little to do . you could sound Lord on the subject . " 

 " I will answer beforehand . Lord - would be enchant to secure to the 
 public service a man so accomplished as yourself , and the son of a peer 
 like Lord Lansmere . " 

 Harley L'Estrange spring to his foot , and fling his cigar in the face of 
 a stately policeman who be look up at the balcony . 

 " infamous and bloodless official ! " cry Harley L'Estrange ; ' so you 
 could provide for a pimple - nosed lackey , for a wine - merchant who have 
 be poison the king 's subject with white lead , - or sloe - juice , - for 
 an idle sybarite , who would complain of a crumpled rose - leaf ; and 
 nothing , in all the vast patronage of England , for a break - down 
 soldier , whose dauntless breast be her rampart ? " 

 " Harley , " say the member of parliament , with his calm , sensible smile , 
 " this would be a very good claptrap at a small theatre ; but there be 
 nothing in which parliament demand such rigid economy as the military 
 branch of the public service ; and no man for whom it be so hard to 
 effect what we must plainly call a job as a subaltern officer who have 
 do nothing more than his duty , - and all military man do that . still , 
 as you take it so earnestly , I will use what interest I can at the War 
 Office , and get he , perhaps , the mastership of a barrack . " 

 " you have well ; for , if you do not , I swear I will turn Radical , and 
 come down to your own city to oppose you , with Hunt and Cobbett to 
 canvass for I . " 

 " I should be very glad to see you come into parliament , even as a 
 Radical , and at my expense , " say Audley , with great kindness ; " but the 
 air be grow cold , and you be not accustomed to our climate . Nay , if 
 you be too poetic for catarrhs and rheum , I be not , - come in . " 




 CHAPTER VI . 

 Lord L'Estrange throw himself on a sofa , and lean his cheek on his 
 hand thoughtfully . Audley Egerton sit near he , with his arm fold , 
 and gaze on his friend 's face with a soft expression of aspect , which 
 be very unusual to the firm outline of his handsome feature . the two 
 man be as dissimilar in person as the reader will have divine that 
 they be in character . all about Egerton be so rigid , all about 
 L'Estrange so easy . in every posture of Harley 's there be the 
 unconscious grace of a child . the very fashion of his garment show 
 his abhorrence of restraint . his clothe be wide and loose ; his 
 neckcloth , tie carelessly , leave his throat half bare . you could see 
 that he have live much in warm and southern land , and contract a 
 contempt for conventionality ; there be as little in his dress as 
 in his talk of the formal precision of the North . he be three or four 
 year young than Audley , but he look at least twelve year young . 
 in fact , he be one of those man to whom old age seem impossible ; 
 voice , look , figure , have all the charm of youth : and perhaps it be from 
 this gracious youthfulness - at all event , it be characteristic of the 
 kind of love he inspire - that neither his parent , nor the few 
 friend admit into his intimacy , ever call he , in their habitual 
 intercourse , by the name of his title . he be not L'Estrange with they , 
 he be Harley ; and by that familiar baptismal I will usually designate 
 he . he be not one of those man whom author or reader wish to view at a 
 distance , and remember as " my Lord " - it be so rarely that he remember 
 it himself . for the rest , it have be say of he by a shrewd wit , " he 
 be so natural that every one call he affect . " Harley L'Estrange be 
 not so critically handsome as Audley Egerton ; to a commonplace observer , 
 he be only rather good - look than otherwise . but woman say that 
 he have " a beautiful countenance , " and they be not wrong . he wear 
 his hair , which be of a fair chestnut , long , and in loose curl ; 
 and instead of the Englishman 's whisker , indulge in the foreigner 's 
 mustache . his complexion be delicate , though not effeminate : it be 
 rather the delicacy of a student than of a woman . but in his clear 
 gray eye there be a wonderful vigour of life . a skilful physiologist , 
 look only into that eye , would have recognize rare stamina of 
 constitution , - a nature so rich that , while easily disturb , it would 
 require all the effect of time , or all the fell combination of passion 
 and grief , to exhaust it . even now , though so thoughtful , and even so 
 sad , the ray of that eye be as concentrated and steadfast as the 
 light of the diamond . 

 " you be only , then , in jest , " say Audley , after a long silence , 
 " when you speak of this mission to Florence . you have still no idea of 
 enter into public life ? " 

 " none . " 

 " I have hop well thing when I get your promise to pass one season in 
 London ; but , indeed , you have keep your promise to the ear to break it 
 to the spirit . I could not presuppose that you would shun all society , 
 and be as much of a hermit here as under the vine of Como . " 

 " I have sit in the Strangers ' Gallery , and hear your great speaker ; 
 I have be in the pit of the opera , and see your fine lady ; I have 
 walk your street ; I have lounge in your park , and I say that 
 I can not   fall in love with a fade dowager , because she fill up her 
 wrinkle with rouge . " 

 " of what dowager do you speak ? " ask the matter - of - fact Audley . 

 " she have a great many title . some people call she fashion , you busy 
 man , politic : it be all one , - trick out and artificial . I mean London 
 Life . no , I can not   fall in love with she , fawn old harridan ! " 

 " I wish you could fall in love with something . " 

 " I wish I could , with all my heart . " 

 " but you be so blaze . " 

 " on the contrary , I be so fresh . look out of the window - what do you 
 see ? " 

 " nothing ! " 

 " nothing ? " 

 " nothing but house and dusty lilac , my coachman dozing on his box , and 
 two woman in patten cross the kennel . " 

 " I see not those where I lie on the sofa . I see but the star . and I 
 feel for they as I do when I be a schoolboy at Eton . it be you who 
 be blaze , not I. enough of this . you do not forget my commission with 
 respect to the exile who have marry into your brother 's family ? " 

 " no ; but here you set I a task more difficult than that of saddle 
 your cornet on the War Office . " 

 " I know it be difficult , for the counter influence be vigilant and 
 strong ; but , on the other hand , the enemy be so damnable a traitor that 
 one must have the Fates and the household god on one 's side . " 

 " nevertheless , " say the practical Audley , bend over a book on the 
 table ; " I think that the good plan would be to attempt a compromise with 
 the traitor . " 

 " to judge of other by myself , " answer Harley , with spirit , " it 
 be less bitter to put up with wrong than to palter with it for 
 compensation . and such wrong ! compromise with the open foe - that maybe 
 do with honour ; but with the perjure friend - that be to forgive the 
 perjury ! " 

 " you be too vindictive , " say Egerton ; " there may be excuse for the 
 friend , which palliate even - " 

 " Hush ! Audley , hush ! or I shall think the world have indeed corrupt 
 you . excuse for the friend who deceive , who betray ! no , such be the 
 true outlaw of Humanity ; and the fury surround he even while he 
 sleep in the temple . " 

 the man of the world lift his eye slowly on the animate face of one 
 still natural enough for the passion . he then once more return to his 
 book , and say , after a pause , " it be time you should marry , Harley . " 

 " no , " answer L'Estrange , with a smile at this sudden turn in the 
 conversation , " not time yet ; for my chief objection to that change in 
 life be , that the woman nowadays be too old for I , or I be too young 
 for they . a few , indeed , be so infantine that one be ashamed to be 
 their toy ; but most be so know that one be afraid to be their dupe . 
 the first , if they condescend to love you , love you as the big doll 
 they have yet dandle , and for a doll 's good quality , - your pretty 
 blue eye and your exquisite millinery . the last , if they prudently 
 accept you , do so on algebraical principle ; you be but the x or the 
 Y that represent a certain aggregate of good matrimonial , - pedigree , 
 title , rent - roll , diamond , pin - money , opera - box . they cast you up with 
 the help of mamma , and you wake some morning to find that plus wife 
 minus affection equal - the Devil ! " 

 " nonsense , " say Audley , with his quiet , grave laugh . " I grant that it 
 be often the misfortune of a man in your station to be married rather 
 for what he have than for what he be ; but you be tolerably penetrate , 
 and not likely to be deceive in the character of the woman you court . " 

 " of the woman I court ? - no ! but of the woman I marry , very likely 
 indeed ! woman be a changeable thing , as our Virgil inform we at 
 school ; but her change par excellence be from the fairy you woo to the 
 brownie you we d. it be not that she have be a hypocrite , - it be that 
 she be a transmigration . you marry a girl for her accomplishment . 
 she paint charmingly , or play like Saint Cecilia . clap a ring on her 
 finger , and she never draw again , - except perhaps your caricature on 
 the back of a letter , - and never open a piano after the honeymoon . 
 you marry she for her sweet temper ; and next year , her nerve be so 
 shatter that you can not   contradict she but you be whirl into a storm 
 of hysteric . you marry she because she declare she hat ball 
 and like quiet ; and ten to one but what she become a patroness at 
 Almack 's , or a lady - in - waiting . " 

 " yet most man marry , and most man survive the operation . " 

 " if it be only necessary to live , that would be a consolatory and 
 encourage reflection . but to live with peace , to live with dignity , to 
 live with freedom , to live in harmony with your thought , your habit , 
 your aspiration - and this in the perpetual companionship of a person 
 to whom you have give the power to wound your peace , to assail your 
 dignity , to cripple your freedom , to jar on each thought and each habit , 
 and bring you down to the mean detail of earth , when you invite she , 
 poor soul , to soar to the sphere - that make the to be or not to be , 
 which be the question . " 

 " if I be you , Harley , I would do as I have hear the author of 
 ' Sandford and Merton ' do , - choose out a child and educate she yourself , 
 after your own heart . " 

 " you have hit it , " answer Harley , seriously . " that have long be my 
 idea , - a very vague one , I confess . but I fear I shall be an old man 
 before I find even the child . " 

 " ah ! " he continue , yet more earnestly , while the whole character of his 
 vary countenance change again , - " ah , if indeed I could discover what 
 I seek , - one who , with the heart of a child , have the mind of a woman ; 
 one who behold in nature the variety , the charm , the never feverish , 
 ever healthful excitement that other vainly seek in the bastard 
 sentimentality of a life false with artificial form ; one who can 
 comprehend , as by intuition , the rich poetry with which creation be 
 clothe , - poetry so clear to the child when enrapture with the flower , 
 or when wonder at the star ! if on I such exquisite companionship 
 be bestow - why , then - " he pause , sigh deeply , and , cover his 
 face with his hand , resume , in falter accent , - 

 " but once - but once only , do such vision of the Beautiful make human 
 rise before I , - rise amidst ' golden exhalation of the dawn . ' it 
 beggar my life in vanish . you know only - you only - how - how - " 

 he bow his head , and the tear force themselves through his clench 
 finger . 

 " so long ago ! " say Audley , share his friend 's emotion . " year so long 
 and so weary , yet still thus tenacious of a mere boyish memory ! " 

 " away with it , then ! " cry Harley , spring to his foot , and with 
 a laugh of strange merriment . " your carriage still wait : set I home 
 before you go to the House . " 

 then lay his hand lightly on his friend 's shoulder , he say , " be it 
 for you , Audley Egerton , to speak sneeringly of boyish memory ? what 
 else be it that bind we together ? what else warm my heart when I meet 
 you ? what else draw your thought from blue - book and beer - bill to 
 waste they on a vagrant like I ? shake hand . oh , friend of my boyhood ! 
 recollect the oar that we ply and the bat that we wield in the old 
 time , or the murmur talk on the moss - grow bank , as we sit together , 
 building in the summer air castle mightier than Windsor . ah , they be 
 strong tie , those boyish memory believe I ! I remember , as if it 
 be yesterday , my translation of that lovely passage in Persius , 
 beginning - let I see - ah ! 

  " ' Quum primum pavido custos mihi purpura cernet , ' - 

 that passage on friendship which gush out so livingly from the stern 
 heart of the satirist . and when old - compliment I on my verse , my 
 eye seek your . verily , I now say as then , - 

  " ' Nescio quod , certe est quod I tibi temperet astrum . ' " 

   " what be the star I know not , but certainly some star 
  it be that attune I unto thee . " 

 Audley turn away his head as he return the grasp of his friend 's 
 hand ; and while Harley , with his light elastic footstep , descend the 
 stair , Egerton linger behind , and there be no trace of the worldly 
 man upon his countenance when he take his place in the carriage by his 
 companion 's side . 

 two hour afterwards , weary cry of " question , question ! " " Divide , 
 divide ! " sink into reluctant silence as Audley Egerton rise to conclude 
 the debate , - the man of man to speak late at night , and to impatient 
 bench : a man who would be hear ; whom a Bedlam break loose would not 
 have roar down ; with a voice clear and sound as a bell , and a form as 
 firmly set on the ground as a church - tower . and while , on the dull 
 of dull question , Audley Egerton thus , not too lively himself , enforce 
 attention , where be Harley L'Estrange ? stand alone by the river 
 at Richmond , and murmur low fantastic thought as he gaze on the 
 moonlit tide . 

 when Audley leave he at home he have join his parent , make they gay 
 with his careless gayety , see the old - fashioned folk retire to 
 rest , and then - while they , perhaps , deem he once more the hero of 
 ball - room and the cynosure of club - he drive slowly through the soft 
 summer night , amidst the perfume of many a garden and many a gleam 
 chestnut grove , with no other aim before he than to reach the lovely 
 margin of England 's lovely river , at the hour when the moon be 
 full and the song of the nightingale most sweet . and so eccentric a 
 humourist be this man , that I believe , as he there loiter , - no one 
 near to cry " how affected ! " or " how romantic ! " - he enjoy himself 
 more than if he have be exchange the politest " how - d'ye - dos " in the 
 hottest of London drawing - room , or bet his hundred on the odd 
 trick , with Lord de R - - - for his partner . 




 CHAPTER VII . 

 Leonard have be about six week with his uncle , and those week 
 be well spend . Mr. Richard have take he to his counting - house , and 
 initiate he into business and the mystery of double entry ; and in 
 return for the young man 's readiness and zeal in matter which the 
 acute trader instinctively feel be not exactly to his taste , Richard 
 engage the good master the town afford to read with his nephew in the 
 evening . this gentleman be the head usher of a large school , who have 
 his hour to himself after eight o'clock , and be pleased to vary the 
 dull routine of enforce lesson by instruction to a pupil who take 
 delightedly even to the Latin grammar . Leonard make rapid stride , and 
 learn more in those six week than many a cleverish boy do in twice 
 as many month . these hour which Leonard devote to study Richard 
 usually spend from home , - sometimes at the house of his grand 
 acquaintance in the Abbey Gardens , sometimes in the Reading - Room 
 appropriate to those aristocrat . if he stay at home , it be in 
 company with his head clerk , and for the purpose of check his 
 account - book , or look over the name of doubtful elector . 

 Leonard have naturally wish to communicate his altered prospect to his 
 old friend , that they , in turn , might rejoice his mother with such good 
 tiding . but he have not be two day in the house before Richard have 
 strictly forbid all such correspondence . 

 " look you , " say he , " at present we be on an experiment , - we must 
 see if we like each other . suppose we do not , you will only have raise 
 expectation in your mother which must end in bitter disappointment ; and 
 suppose we do , it will be time enough to write when something definite 
 be settle . " 

 " but my mother will be so anxious - " 

 " make your mind easy on that score . I will write regularly to Mr. Dale , 
 and he can tell she that you be well and thriving . no more word , my 
 man , - when I say a thing , I say it . " then , observe that Leonard look 
 blank and dissatisfied , Richard add , with a good - humour smile , " I 
 have my reason for all this - you shall know they later . and I tell you 
 what : if you do as I bid you , it be my intention to settle something 
 handsome on your mother ; but if you do not , devil a penny she will get from 
 I . " 

 with that Richard turn on his heel , and in a few moment his voice be 
 hear loud in objurgation with some of his people . 

 about the fourth week of Leonard 's residence at Mr. Avenel 's , his host 
 begin to evince a certain change of manner . he be no long quite so 
 cordial with Leonard , nor do he take the same interest in his progress . 
 about the same period he be frequently catch by the London butler 
 before the look - glass . he have always be a smart man in his dress , 
 but he be now more particular . he would spoil three white cravat when 
 he go out of an evening , before he could satisfy himself as to the 
 tie . he also buy a ' peerage , ' and it become his favourite study at 
 odd quarter of an hour . all these symptom proceed from a cause , and 
 that cause be - woman . 




 CHAPTER VIII . 

 the first people at Screwstown be indisputably the Pompleys . Colonel 
 Pompley be grand , but Mrs. Pompley be grander . the colonel be stately 
 in right of his military rank and his service in India ; Mrs. Pompley 
 be majestic in right of her connection . indeed , Colonel Pompley

 aristocratic , but for the well - sound name of his relation , " the 
 Digbies . " perhaps on the principle that obscurity increase the natural

 amongst the temptation of Screwstown . suddenly , to the astonishment of 
 the Abbey Gardens , Mrs. M'Catchley prove her identity , and arrive at

 adjoin his garden , and he no soon see Richard Avenel enter than he

 till his progress be arrest by a sofa - table at which sit Mrs. 
 M'Catchley herself , with Mrs. Pompley by her side . for on this great

 and , whether to show her respect to Mrs. M'Catchley , or to show Mrs. 
 M'Catchley her well - breed contempt for the people of Screwstown , remain 
 in state by her friend , honour only the elite of the town with 
 introduction to the illustrious visitor . 

 Mrs. M'Catchley be a very fine woman , - a woman who justify Mrs. 
 Pompley 's pride in she . her cheek - bone be rather high , it be true but 
 that prove the purity of her caledonian descent ; for the rest , she have

 have be different . 

 look round she with a glass , which Mrs. Pompley be in the habit of

 be , she be nevertheless deceive into whispering to Mrs. Pompley ,

 " oh , " say Mrs. Pompley , in unaffected surprise , " that be the nephew of 
 the rich Vulgarian I be tell you of this morning . "

 " Avenel - not Arundel - my sweet friend . " 

 " Avenel be not a bad name , " say Mrs. M'Catchley . " but be the uncle 
 really so rich ? " 

 " the colonel be try this very day to guess what he be worth ; but he 
 say it be impossible to guess it . " 

 " and the young man be his heir ? "

 clever . "

 about ten minute afterwards , Richard Avenel have effect his escape

  " Rank be but the guinea 's stamp , 
  the man be the gowd for a ' that . "

 " a very fine young man your nephew , sir , " resume Mrs. M ' Catchley . 

 Richard grunt . 

 " and seem full of talent . not yet at the University ? will he go to 
 Oxford or Cambridge ? " 

 " I have not make up my mind yet if I shall send he to the University at 
 all . " 

 " a young man of his expectation ! " exclaim Mrs. M'Catchley , artfully . 

 " expectation ! " repeat Richard , fire up . " have he be talk to you 
 of his expectation ? " 

 " no , indeed , sir . but the nephew of the rich Mr. Avenel ! ah , one hear 
 a great deal , you know , of rich people ; it be the penalty of wealth , Mr. 
 Avenel ! " 

 Richard be very much flattered . his crest rise .

 slowly , as she adjust her blonde scarf , " that Mr. Avenel have resolve

 " the devil they do , ma'am ! " bolt out Richard , gruffly ; and then , 
 ashamed of his lapsus linguae , screw up his lip firmly , and glare on

 Mrs. M'Catchley observe he over her fan . Richard turn abruptly , and

 five minute afterwards , the widow and the bachelor seem so much at

 now , it be from that evening that Mr. Richard Avenel exhibit

 CHAPTER IX . 

 some day after this memorable soiree , Colonel Pompley sit alone in his 
 study   which open pleasantly on an old - fashioned garden , absorb in 
 the house bill . for Colonel Pompley do not leave that domestic care 
 to his lady , - perhaps she be too grand for it . Colonel Pompley with 
 his own sonorous voice order the joint , and with his own heroic hand 
 dispense the store . in justice to the colonel , I must add - at 
 whatever risk of offence to the fair sex - that there be not a house at 
 Screwstown so well manage as the Pompleys ' ; none which so successfully 
 achieve the difficult art of unite economy with show . I should 
 despair of conveying to you an idea of the extent to which Colonel 
 Pompley make his income go . it be but seven hundred a year ; and many 
 a family contrive to do less upon three thousand . to be sure , the 
 Pompleys have no child to sponge upon they . what they have they spend 
 all on themselves . neither , if the pompley never exceed their income , 
 do they pretend to live much within it . the two end of the year meet at 
 Christmas , - just meet , and no more . 

 Colonel Pompley sit at his desk . he be in his well - brush blue coat , 
 button across his breast , his gray trouser fit tight to his limb , 
 and fasten under his boot with a link chain . he save a great deal 
 of money in strap . no one ever see Colonel Pompley in dressing - gown and 
 slipper . he and his house be alike in order - always fit to be see 

  " from morn to noon , from noon to dewy eve . " 

 the colonel be a short compact man , incline to be stout , - with a very 
 red face , that seem not only shave , but rasp . he wear his 
 hair crop close , except just in front , where it form what the 
 hairdresser call a feather , but it seem a feather of iron , so stiff 
 and so strong be it . firmness and precision be emphatically mark on 
 the colonel 's countenance . there be a resolute strain on his feature , 
 as if he be always employ in make the two end meet ! 

 so he sit before his house - book , with his steel - pen in his hand , and 
 make cross here and note of interrogation there . 

 " Mrs. M'Catchley 's maid , " say the colonel to himself , " must be put upon 
 ration . the tea that she drink ! good heavens ! - tea again ! " 

 there be a modest ring at the outer door . " too early for a visitor ! " 
 think the colonel . " perhaps it be the water - rate . " 

 the neat man - servant - never see beyond the office , save in grande 
 tenue , plushed and powder - enter and bow . " a gentleman , sir , wish 
 to see you . " 

 " a gentleman , " repeat the colonel , glance towards the clock . " be 
 you sure it be a gentleman ? " 

 the man hesitate . " why , sir , I ben't   exactly sure ; but he speak like a 
 gentleman . he do say he come from London to see you , sir . " 

 a long and interesting correspondence be then be hold between the 
 colonel and one of his wife 's trustee touch the investment of 
 Mrs. Pompley 's fortune . it might be the trustee , - nay , it must be . the 
 trustee have talk of run down to see he . 

 " let he come in , " say the colonel , " and when I ring - sandwich and 
 sherry . " 

 " Beef , sir ? " 

 " Ham . " 

 the colonel put aside his house - book , and wipe his pen . in another 
 minute the door open and the servant announce - 

  " MR . DIGBY . " 

 the colonel 's face fall , and he stagger back . 

 the door close , and Mr. Digby stand in the middle of the room , lean 
 on the great writing - table for support . the poor soldier look sickly 
 and shabby , and near the end of all thing in life and fortune , 
 than when Lord L'Estrange have thrust the pocket - book into his hand . 
 but still the servant show knowledge of the world in call he 
 gentleman ; there be no other word to apply to he . 

 " Sir , " begin Colonel Pompley , recover himself , and with great 
 solemnity , " I do not expect this pleasure . " 

 the poor visitor star round he dizzily , and sink into a chair , 
 breathe hard . the colonel look as a man only look upon a poor 
 relation , and button up first one trouser pocket and then the other . 

 " I think you be in Canada , " say the colonel , at last . Mr. Digby 
 have now get breath to speak , and he say meekly , " the climate would have 
 kill my child , and it be two year since I return . " 

 " you ought to have find a very good place in England to make it worth 
 your while to leave Canada . " 

 " she could not have live through another winter in Canada , - the doctor 
 say so . " 

 " Pooh , " quoth the colonel . 

 Mr. Digby draw a long breath . " I would not come to you , Colonel Pompley , 
 while you could think that I come as a beggar for myself . " 

 the colonel 's brow relaxed . " a very honourable sentiment , Mr. Digby . " 

 " no : I have go through a great deal ; but you see , Colonel , " add the 
 poor relation , with a faint smile , " the campaign be well - nigh over , and 
 peace be at hand . " 

 the colonel seem touch . 

 " do not   talk so , Digby , - I do not   like it . you be young than I 
 be - nothing more disagreeable than these gloomy view of thing . you 
 have get enough to live upon , you say , - at least so I understand you . 
 I be very glad to hear it ; and , indeed , I could not assist you - so many 
 claim on I . so it be all very well , Digby . " 

 " oh , Colonel Pompley , " cry the soldier , clasp his hand , and with 
 feverish energy , " I be a suppliant , not for myself , but my child ! I have 
 but one , - only one , a girl . she have be so good to I ! she will cost 
 you little . take she when I die ; promise she a shelter , a home . I ask no 
 more . you be my near relative . I have no other to look to . you have 
 no child of your own . she will be a blessing to you , as she have be 
 all upon earth to I ! " 

 if Colonel Pompley 's face be red in ordinary hour , no epithet 
 sufficiently rubicund or sanguineous can express its colour at this 
 appeal . " the man 's mad , " he say , at last , with a tone of astonishment 
 that almost conceal his wrath , - ' stark mad ! I take his child ! - lodge 
 and board a great , positive , hungry child ! why , sir , many and many a 
 time have I say to Mrs. Pompley , " t be a mercy we have no child . we 
 could never live in this style if we have child , - never make both 
 end meet . ' child - the most expensive , ravenous , ruinous thing in the 
 world - a child . " 

 " she have be accustom to starve , " say Mr. Digby , plaintively . " oh , 
 Colonel , let I see your wife . her heart I can touch , - she be a woman . " 

 Unlucky father ! a more untoward , unseasonable request the Fates could 
 not have put into his lip . 

 Mrs. Pompley see the Digbies ! Mrs. Pompley learn the condition of the 
 colonel 's grand connection ! the colonel would never have be his own 
 man again . at the bare idea , he feel as if he could have sink into the 
 earth with shame . in his alarm he make a stride to the door , with the 
 intention of lock it . good heaven , if Mrs. Pompley should come in ! 
 and the man , too , have be announce by name . Mrs. Pompley might 
 have learn already that a Digby be with her husband , - she might be 
 actually dress to receive he worthily ; there be not a moment to 
 lose . 

 the colonel explode . " Sir , I wonder at your impudence . see Mrs. 
 Pompley ! Hush , sir , hush ! - hold your tongue . I have disown your 
 connection . I will not have my wife - a woman , sir , of the first 
 family - disgrace by it . yes ; you need not fire up . John Pompley be not 
 a man to be bully in his own house . I say disgrace . do not you run 
 into debt , and spend your fortune ? do not you marry a low creature , - a 
 vulgarian , a tradesman 's daughter ? - and your poor father such a 
 respectable man , - a benefit clergyman ! do not you sell your 
 commission ? Heaven know what become of the money ! do not you turn   I 
 shudder to say it   a common stage - player , sir ? and then , when you be 
 on your last leg , do I not give you l200 out of my own purse to go 
 to Canada ? and now here you be again , - and ask I , with a coolness 
 that - that take away my breath - take away - my breath , sir - to 
 provide for the child you have think proper to have , - a child 
 whose connection on the mother 's side be of the most abject and 
 discreditable condition . leave my house , leave it ! good heavens , sir , 
 not that way ! - this . " and the colonel open the glass - door that lead 
 into the garden . " I will let you out this way . if Mrs. Pompley should 
 see you ! " and with that think the colonel absolutely hook his arm 
 into his poor relation 's , and hurry he into the garden . 

 Mr. Digby say not a word , but he struggle ineffectually to escape from 
 the colonel 's arm ; and his colour go and come , come and go , with a 
 quickness that show that in those shrink vein there be still some 
 drop of a soldier 's blood . 

 but the colonel have now reach a little postern - door in the 
 garden - wall . he open the latch , and thrust out his poor cousin . then 
 look down the lane , which be long , straight , and narrow , and see 
 it be quite solitary , his eye fall upon the forlorn man , and remorse 
 shoot through his heart . for a moment the hard of all kind of 
 avarice , that of the genteel , relax its gripe . for a moment the 
 most intolerant of all form of pride , that which be base upon false 
 pretence , hush its voice , and the colonel hastily draw out his purse . 
 " there , " say he , " that be all I can do for you . do leave the town as 
 quick as you can , and do not   mention your name to any one . your father 
 be such a respectable man , - benefice clergyman ! " 

 " and pay for your commission , Mr. Pompley . my name ! I be not ashamed of 
 it . but do not fear I shall claim your relationship . no ; I be ashamed of 
 you ! " 

 the poor cousin put aside the purse , still stretch towards he , with 
 a scornful hand , and walk firmly down the lane . Colonel Pompley stand 
 irresolute . at that moment a window in his house be throw open . he 
 hear the noise , turn round , and see his wife look out . 

 Colonel Pompley sneak back through the shrubbery , hide himself 
 amongst the tree . 




 CHAPTER X. 

 " Ill - luck be a betise , " say the great Cardinal Richelieu ; and in the 
 long run , I fear , his Eminence be right . if you could drop Dick Avenel 
 and Mr. Digby in the middle of Oxford Street , - Dick in a fustian jacket , 
 Digby in a suit of superfine ; Dick with five shilling in his pocket , 
 Digby with L1000 , - and if , at the end of ten year , you look up your 
 two man , Dick would be on his road to a fortune , Digby - what we have 
 see he ! yet Digby have no vice ; he do not drink nor gamble . what be 
 he , then ? helpless . he have be an only son , - a spoiled child , bring 
 up as " a gentleman ; " that be , as a man who be not expect to be 
 able to turn his hand to anything . he enter , as we have see , a very 
 expensive regiment , wherein he find himself , at his father 's death , 
 with L4000 and the incapacity to say " no . " not naturally extravagant , 
 but without an idea of the value of money , - the easy , gentle , 
 well - temper man whom example ever lead astray . this part of his career 
 comprise a very common history , - the poor man live on equal term 
 with the rich . debt ; recourse to usurer ; bill sign sometimes for 
 other , renew at twenty per cent ; the L4000 melt like snow ; pathetic 
 appeal to relation ; relation have child of their own ; small help 
 give grudgingly , eke out by much advice , and couple with 
 condition . amongst the condition there be a very proper and prudent 
 one , - exchange into a less expensive regiment . Exchange effect ; peace ; 
 obscure country quarter ; ennui , flute - playing , and idleness . Mr. Digby 
 have no resource on a rainy day - except flute - playing ; pretty girl of 
 inferior rank ; all the officer after she ; Digby smitten ; pretty girl 
 very virtuous ; Digby form honourable intention ; excellent sentiment ; 
 imprudent marriage . Digby fall in life ; colonel 's lady will not 
 associate with Mrs. Digby ; Digby cut by his whole kith and kin ; many 
 disagreeable circumstance in regimental life ; Digby sell out ; love 
 in a cottage ; execution in ditto . Digby have be much applaud as an 
 amateur actor ; think of the stage ; genteel comedy , - a gentlemanlike 
 profession . try in a provincial town , under another name ; unhappily 
 succeed ; life of an actor ; hand - to - mouth life ; illness ; chest affect ; 
 Digby 's voice become hoarse and feeble ; not aware of it ; attribute 
 fail success to ignorant provincial public ; appear in London ; be 
 hiss ; return to the province ; sink into very small part ; prison ; 
 despair ; wife die ; appeal again to relation ; a subscription make 
 to get rid of he ; send he out of the country ; place in 
 Canada , - superintendent to an estate , L150 a year ; pursue by ill - luck ; 
 never before fit for business , not fit now ; honest as the day , but keep 
 slovenly account ; child can not bear the winter of Canada ; Digby wrap 
 up in the child ; return home ; mysterious life for two year ; child 
 patient , thoughtful , loving ; have learn to work ; manage for father ; 
 often support he ; constitution rapidly break ; think of what will 
 become of his child , - bad disease of all . poor Digby ! never do a 
 base , cruel , unkind thing in his life ; and here he be , walk down 
 the lane from Colonel Pompley 's house ! now , if Digby have but learn a 
 little of the world 's cunning , I think he would have succeed even with 
 Colonel Pompley . have he spend the l100 receive from Lord L'Estrange 
 with a view to effect ; have he bestow a fitting wardrobe on himself and 
 his pretty Helen ; have he stop at the last stage , take thence a smart 
 chaise and pair , and present himself at Colonel Pompley 's in a way 
 that would not have discredit the colonel 's connection , and then , 
 instead of pray for home and shelter , ask the colonel to become 
 guardian to his child in case of his death , I have a strong notion that 
 the colonel , in spite of his avarice , would have stretch both end so 
 as to take in Helen Digby . but our poor friend have no such art . indeed , 
 of the l100 he have already very little left , for before leave town 
 he have commit what Sheridan consider the extreme of 
 extravagance , - fritter away his money in pay his debt ; and as for 
 dress up Helen and himself - if that think have ever occur to he , 
 he would have reject it as foolish . he would have think that the 
 more he show his poverty , the more he would be pity , - the bad 
 mistake a poor cousin can commit . accord to Theophrastus , the 
 partridge of Paphlagonia have two heart : so have most man ; it be the 
 common mistake of the unlucky to knock at the wrong one . 




 CHAPTER XI . 

 Mr. Digby enter the room of the inn in which he have leave Helen . 
 she be seat by the window , and look out wistfully on the narrow 
 street , perhaps at the child at play . there have never be a playtime 
 for Helen Digby . 

 she spring forward as her father come in . his come be her holiday . 

 " we must go back to London , " say Mr. Digby , sink helplessly on the 
 chair . then with his sort of sickly smile , - for he be bland even to his 
 child , - " will you kindly inquire when the first coach leave ? " 

 all the active care of their careful life devolve upon that quiet 
 child . she kiss her father , place before he a cough mixture which 
 he have bring from London , and go out silently to make the necessary 
 inquiry , and prepare for the journey back . 

 at eight o'clock the father and child be seat in the night - coach , 
 with one other passenger , - a man muffle up to the chin . after the first 
 mile the man let down one of the window . though it be summer the air 
 be chill and raw . Digby shiver and cough . 

 Helen place her hand on the window , and , lean towards the passenger , 
 whisper softly . 

 " eh ! " say the passenger , " draw up the window ? you have get your own 
 window ; this be mine . Oxygen , young lady , " he add solemnly , " oxygen be 
 the breath of life . Cott , child ! " he continue with suppress choler , 
 and a Welsh pronunciation , " Cott ! let we breathe and live . " 

 Helen be frightened , and recoil . 

 her father , who have not hear , or have not heed , this colloquy , 
 retreat into the corner , put up the collar of his coat , and cough 
 again . 

 " it be cold , my dear , " say he , languidly , to Helen . 

 the passenger catch the word , and reply indignantly , but as if 
 soliloquize , - 

 " Cold - ugh ! I do believe the English be the stuffy people ! look at 
 their four - post bed - all the curtain draw , shutter closed , board 
 before the chimney - not a house with a ventilator ! cold - ugh ! " 

 the window next Mr. Digby do not fit well into its frame . " there be a 
 sad draught , " say the invalid . 

 Helen instantly occupy herself in stop up the chink of the window 
 with her handkerchief . Mr. Digby glance ruefully at the other window . 
 the look , which be very eloquent , arouse yet more the traveller 's 
 spleen . 

 " Pleasant ! " say he . " Cott ! I suppose you will ask I to go outside 
 next ! but people who travel in a coach should know the law of a coach . I 
 do not   interfere with your window ; you have no business to interfere with 
 mine . " 

 " Sir , I do not speak , " say Mr. Digby , meekly . 

 " but Miss here do . " 

 " ah , sir ! " say Helen , plaintively , " if you know how Papa suffer ! " and 
 her hand again move towards the obnoxious window . 

 " no , my dear ; the gentleman be in his right , " say Mr. Digby ; and , 
 bow with his wonted suavity , he add , " excuse she , sir . she think a 
 great deal too much of I . " 

 the passenger say nothing , and Helen nestle close to her father , and 
 strove to screen he from the air . 

 the passenger move uneasily . " well , " say he , with a sort of snort , 
 " air be air , and right be right : but here go - " and he hastily draw up 
 the window . 

 Helen turn her face full towards the passenger with a grateful 
 expression , visible even in the dim light . 

 " you be very kind , sir , " say poor Mr. Digby ; " I be ashamed to - " 
 his cough choke the rest of the sentence . the passenger , who be a 
 plethoric , sanguineous man , feel as if he be stifle . but he take off 
 his wrapper , and resign the oxygen like a hero . 

 presently he draw near to the sufferer , and lay hand on his wrist . 

 " you be feverish , I fear . I be a medical man . St ! - one - two . Cott ! you 
 should not travel ; you be not fit for it ! " 

 Mr. Digby shake his head ; he be too feeble to reply . 

 the passenger thrust his hand into his coat - pocket , and draw out what 
 seem a cigar - case , but what , in fact , be a leathern repertory , 
 contain a variety of minute phial . 

 from one of these phial he extract two tiny globule . " there , " say 
 he , " open your mouth , put those on the tip of your tongue . they will 
 lower the pulse , check the fever . be well presently , but should not 
 travel , want rest ; you should be in bed . Aconite ! henbane ! hum ! your 
 papa be of fair complexion , - a timid character , I should say ; - a horror 
 of work , perhaps . eh , child ? " 

 " Sir ! " falter Helen , astonish and alarmed . be the man a conjuror ? 

 " a case for phosphor ! " cry the passenger : " that fool Browne would have 
 say arsenic . do not   be persuade to take arsenic ! " 

 " Arsenic , sir ! " echo the mild Digby . " no : however unfortunate a man 
 may be , I think , sir , that suicide be - tempting , perhaps , but highly 
 criminal . " 

 " Suicide , " say the passenger , tranquilly , - ' suicide be my hobby ! you 
 have no symptom of that kind , you say ? " 

 " good heaven ! no , sir . " 

 " if ever you feel violently impel to drown yourself , take pulsatilla ; 
 but if you feel a preference towards blow out your brain , 
 accompany with weight in the limb , loss of appetite , dry cough , and 
 bad corn , sulphuret of antimony . do not   forget . " 

 though poor Mr. Digby confusedly think that the gentleman be out of 
 his mind , yet he try politely to say " that he be much oblige , and 
 would be sure to remember ; " but his tongue fail he , and his own idea 
 grow perplexed . his head fall back heavily , and he sink into a silence 
 which seem that of sleep . 

 the traveller look hard at Helen , as she gently draw her father 's head 
 on her shoulder , and there pillow it with a tenderness which be more 
 that of a mother than child . 

 " moral affection , soft , compassionate ! - a good child and would go well 
 with - pulsatilla . " 

 Helen hold up her finger , and glance from her father to the traveller , 
 and then to her father again . 

 " certainly , - pulsatilla ! " mutter the homoeopathist , and ensconce 
 himself in his own corner , he also seek to sleep . but after vain 
 effort , accompany by restless gesture and movement , he suddenly 
 start up , and again extract his phial - book . 

 " what the deuce be they to I ? " he mutter . " morbid sensibility of 
 character - coffee ? no ! - accompany by vivacity and violence - nux ! " he 
 bring his book to the window , contrive to read the label on a pigmy 
 bottle . " Nux ! that be it , " he say , - and he swallow a globule ! 

 " now , " quoth he , after a pause , " I do not   care a straw for the 
 misfortune of other people ; nay , I have half a mind to let down the 
 window . " 

 Helen look up . 

 " but I will not , " he add resolutely ; and this time he fall fairly 
 asleep . 




 CHAPTER XII . 

 the coach stop at eleven o'clock to allow the passenger to sup . the 
 homoeopathist wake up , get out , give himself a shake , and inhale the 
 fresh air into his vigorous lung with an evident sensation of delight . 
 he then turn and look into the coach . 

 " let your father get out , my dear , " say he , with a tone more gentle 
 than usual . " I should like to see he indoor , - perhaps I can do he 
 good . " 

 but what be Helen 's terror when she find that her father do not stir ! 
 he be in a deep swoon , and still quite insensible when they lift he

 the effort bring up blood . 

 it be impossible for he to proceed far . the homoeopathist assist

 near doctor , - for the inn stand by itself in a small hamlet . there 
 be the parish apothecary three mile off . but on hear that the 
 gentlefolk employ Dr. Dosewell , and it be a good seven mile to his 
 house , the homoeopathist fetch a deep breath . the coach only stop a

 " Cott ! " say he , angrily , to himself , " the nux be a failure . my 
 sensibility be chronic . I must go through a long course to get rid of

 and the good man after a very slight supper go upstairs again to the

 " shall I send for Dr. Dosewell , sir ? " ask the landlady , stop he 
 at the door . 

 " Hum ! at what hour to - morrow do the next coach to London pass ? " 

 " not before eight , sir . " 

 " well , send for the doctor to be here at seven . that leave we at least 
 some hour free from allopathy and murder , " grunt the disciple of 
 Hahnemann , as he enter the room . 

 whether it be the globule that the homoeopathist have administer ,

 more than it become one not of the Faculty to opine . but certainly Mr.

 wistful eye be fix on the physician 's with all the earnestness of

 " your father be very ill , " reply the doctor , after a short pause . " he 
 can not move hence for some day at least . I be go to London ; shall I 
 call on your relation , and tell some of they to join you ? " 

 " no , thank you , sir , " answer Helen , colour . " but do not fear ; I 
 can nurse Papa . I think he have be bad before , - that is , he have 
 complain more . " 

 the homeopathist rise , and take two stride across the room ; then he 
 pause by the bed , and listen to the breathing of the sleep man . 

 he steal back to the child , who be still kneel , take she in his arm 
 and kiss she . " tamn it , " say he , angrily , and put she down , " go 
 to bed now , - you be not want any more . " 

 " please , sir , " say Helen , " I can not leave he so . if he wake he would 
 miss I . " 

 the doctor 's hand tremble ; he have recourse to his globule . 

 " anxiety - grief suppress , " mutter he . " do not   you want to cry , my 
 dear ? cry , - do ! " 

 " I can not , " murmur Helen . 

 " Pulsatilla ! " say the doctor , almost with triumph . " I say so from the 
 first . open your mouth - here ! Goodnight . my room be opposite , - no . 6 ; 
 call I if he wake . " 




 CHAPTER xiii .
