 glad - " she stop . 

 " you be not ashamed of I , then , in spite of what have happen ? " 

 " ashamed of you ! why , I should be so proud of you , if I be - " 

 " finish the sentence and say - ' your wife ! ' - there , it be out . my dear 
 madam , I be rich , as you know ; I love you very heartily . with your 
 help , I think I can make a figure in a large world than this : and that , 
 whatever my father , my grandson at least will be - but it be time enough 
 to speak of he . what say you ? - you - turn away . I will not tease you , - it 
 be not my way . I say before , ay or no ; and your kindness so embolden 
 I that I say it again , ay or no ? " 

 " but you take I so unawares - so - so - Lord ! my dear Mr. Avenel ; you 
 be so hasty - I - I - " and the widow actually blush , and be genuinely 
 bashful . 

 " those horrid Pompleys ! " think Richard , as he see the colonel bustle 
 up with Mrs. M'Catchley 's cloak on his arm . " I press for your answer , " 
 continue the suitor , speak very fast . " I shall leave this place 
 to - morrow , if you will not give it . " 

 " leave this place - leave I ? " 

 " then you will be mine ? " 

 " ah , Mr. Avenel ! " say the widow , languidly , and leave her hand in 
 his , " who can resist you ? " 

 up come Colonel Pompley ; Richard take the shawl : " no hurry for that now , 
 Colonel , - Mrs. M'Catchley feel already at home here . " 

 ten minute afterwards , Richard Avenel so contrive that it be know 
 by the whole company that their host be accept by the Honourable Mrs. 
 M'Catchley . and every one say , " he be a very clever man and a very good 
 fellow , " except the Pompleys - and the Pompleys be frantic . Mr. Richard 
 Avenel have force his way into the aristocracy of the country ; the 
 husband of an honourable , connect with peer ! 

 " he will stand for our city - Vulgarian ! " cry the colonel . " and his 
 wife will walk out before I , " cry the colonel 's lady , - " nasty woman ! " 
 and she burst into tear . 

 the guest be go ; and Richard have now leisure to consider what 
 course to pursue with regard to his sister and her son . 

 his victory over his guest have in much soften his heart towards his 
 relation ; but he still feel bitterly aggrieve at Mrs. Fairfield 's 
 unseasonable intrusion , and his pride be greatly chafe by the boldness 
 of Leonard . he have no idea of any man whom he have serve , or mean to 
 serve , have a will of his own , have a single thought in opposition 
 to his pleasure . he begin , too , to feel that word have pass between 
 he and Leonard which could not be well forget by either , and would 
 render their close connection less pleasant than heretofore . he , the 
 great Richard Avenel , beg pardon of Mrs. Fairfield , the washerwoman ! 
 no ; she and Leonard must beg his . " that must be the first step , " say 
 Richard Avenel ; " and I suppose they have come to their sens . " with 
 that expectation , he unlock the door of his parlour , and find himself 
 in complete solitude . the moon , lately risen , shine full into the room , 
 and light up every corner . he star round bewilder , - the bird have 
 fly . " do they go through the keyhole ? " say Air . Avenel . " ha ! I see ! 
 the window be open ! " the window reach to the ground . Mr. Avenel , in 
 his excitement , have forget that easy mode of egress . " well , " say he , 
 throw himself into his easy - chair , " I suppose I shall soon hear from 
 they : they will be want my money fast enough , I fancy . " his eye catch 
 sight of a letter , unseal , lie on the table . he open it , and see 
 bank - note to the amount of L50 , - the widow 's forty - five country note , 
 and a new note , Bank of England , that he have lately give to Leonard . 
 with the money be these line , write in Leonard 's bold , clear 
 writing , though a word or two here and there show that the hand have 
 tremble , - 

  I thank you for all you have do to one whom you regard as the 
  object of charity . my mother and I forgive what have pass . I 
  depart with she . you bade I make my choice , and I have make it . 

  LEONARD FAIRFIELD . 

 the paper drop from Richard 's hand , and he remain mute and 
 remorseful for a moment . he soon feel , however , that he have no help for 
 it but work himself up into a rage . " of all people in the world , " 
 cry Richard , stamp his foot on the floor , " there be none so 
 disagreeable , insolent , and ungrateful as poor relation . I wash my 
 hand of they ! " 




 BOOK SIXTH . 




 INITIAL chapter . 

 WHEREIN MR . CAXTON be PROFOUNDLY METAPHYSICAL . 

 " life , " say my father , in his most dogmatical tone , " be a certain 
 quantity in time , which may be regard in two way , - first , as life 
 integral ; second , as life fractional . life integral be that complete 
 whole expressive of a certain value , large or small , which each man 
 possess in himself . life fractional be that same whole seize upon 
 and invade by other people , and subdivide amongst they . they who get a 
 large slice of it say , ' a very valuable life this ! ' those who get but a 
 small handful say , ' so , so ; nothing very great ! ' those who get none of 
 it in the scramble exclaim , ' good for nothing ! ' " 

 " I do not   understand a word you be say , " growl Captain Roland . 

 my father survey his brother with compassion : " I will make it all 
 clear , even to your understanding . when I sit down by myself in my 
 study , have carefully lock the door on all of you , alone with my 
 book and thought , I be in full possession of my integral life . I be 
 totu , tere , atque rotundus , - a whole human being , equivalent in value , 
 we will say , for the sake of illustration , to a fix round sum , l100 
 for example . but when I go forth into the common apartment , each of 
 those to whom I be of any worth whatsoever put his finger into the bag 
 that contain I , and take out of I what he want . Kitty require I 
 to pay a bill ; Pisistratus to save he the time and trouble of look 
 into a score or two of book ; the child to tell they story , or 
 play at hide - and - seek ; and so on throughout the circle to which I have 
 incautiously give myself up for plunder and subdivision . the l100 which 
 I represent in my study be now parcel out ; I be worth L40 or L50 
 to Kitty , L20 to Pisistratus , and perhaps 30 . to the child . this be 
 life fractional . and I cease to be an integral till once more return 
 to my study , and again close the door on all existence but my own . 
 meanwhile , it be perfectly clear that to those who , whether I be in the 
 study or whether I be in the common sitting - room , get nothing at all 
 out of I , I be not worth a farthing . it must be wholly indifferent to a 
 native of Kamschatka whether Austin Caxton be or be not raze out of the 
 great account - book of human being . 

 " hence , " continue my father , - " hence it follow that the more 
 fractional a life be - that is , the great the number of person among 
 whom it can be subdivide - why , the more there be to say , ' a very 
 valuable life that ! ' thus the leader of a political party , a conqueror , 
 a king , an author , who be amusing hundred or thousand or million , have 
 a great number of person whom his worth interest and affect than a 
 Saint Simeon Stylites could have when he perch himself at the top of 
 a column ; although , regard each in himself , Saint Simeon , in his grand 
 mortification of flesh , in the idea that he thereby please his Divine 
 Benefactor , might represent a large sum of moral value per se than 
 Bonaparte or Voltaire . " 

 PISISTRATUS . - " perfectly clear , sir ; but I do not   see what it have to do 
 with ' my novel . ' " 

 MR . CAXTON . - " everything . your novel , if it be to be a full and 
 comprehensive survey of the ' Quicquid agunt homine '   which it ought to 
 be , consider the length and breadth to which I foresee , from the slow 
 development of your story , you meditate extend and expand 
 it , will embrace the two view of existence , - the integral and the 
 fractional . you have show we the former in Leonard , when he be sit 
 in his mother 's cottage , or rest from his work by the little fount in 
 Riccabocca 's garden . and in harmony with that view of his life , you have 
 surround he with comparative integral , only subdivide by the tender 
 hand of their immediate family and neighbour , - your squire and 
 parson , your italian exile and his Jemima . with all these , life be , 
 more or less , the life natural , and this be always , more or less , the 
 life integral . then come the life artificial , which be always , more or 
 less , the life fractional . in the life natural , wherein we be sway 
 but by our own native impulse and desire , subservient only to the 
 great silent law of Virtue   which have pervade the universe since 
 it swing out of chaos , a man be of worth from what he be in 
 himself , - Newton be as worthy before the apple fall from the tree as 
 when all Europe applaud the discoverer of the Principle of Gravity . 
 but in the life artificial we be only of worth inasmuch as we affect 
 other ; and , relative to that life , Newton rise in value more than a 
 million per cent when down fall the apple from which ultimately spring 
 up his discovery . in order to keep civilization go and spread over 
 the world the light of human intellect , we have certain desire within 
 we , ever swell beyond the ease and independence which belong to we 
 as integral . cold man as Newton might be   he once take a lady 's hand in 
 his own , Kitty , and use her forefinger for his tobacco - stopper , - great 
 philosopher ! , cold as he might be , he be yet move into give his 
 discovery to the world , and that from motif very little differ 
 in their quality from the motif that make Dr. Squills communicate 
 article to the ' Phrenological Journal ' upon the skull of Bushmen and 
 wombat . for it be the property of light to travel . when a man have 
 light in he , forth it must go . but the first passage of genius from its 
 integral state   in which it have be repose on its own wealth   into 
 the fractional be usually through a hard and vulgar pathway . it leave 
 behind it the revery of solitude , - that self - contemplate rest which 
 may be call the Visionary , - and enter suddenly into the state that 
 may be call the positive and actual . there it see the operation 
 of money on the outer life ; see all the ruder and commoner spring 
 of action ; see ambition without nobleness , love without romance ; be 
 bustle about and order and trample and cow , - in short , it pass 
 an apprenticeship with some Richard Avenel , and do not detect what 
 good and what grandeur , what addition even to the true poetry of the 
 social universe , fractional existence like Richard Avenel 's bestow ; 
 for the pillar that support society be like those of the Court of the 
 Hebrew Tabernacle , - they be of brass , it be true , but they be fillet 
 with silver . from such intermediate state Genius be expel and drive 
 on its way , and would have be so in this case have Mrs. Fairfield   who 
 be but the representative of the homely natural affection , strong 
 ever in true genius , - for light be warm   never crush Mr. Avenel 's moss 
 rise on her sisterly bosom . now , forth from this passage and defile of 
 transition into the large world , must Genius go on , work out its 
 natural destiny amidst thing and form the most artificial . passion 
 that move and influence the world be at work around it . often lose 
 sight of itself , its very absence be a silent contrast to the agency 
 present . Merged and vanish for a while amidst the Practical World , yet 
 we ourselves feel all the while that it be there ; be at work amidst the 
 working around it . this practical world that efface it rise out of 
 some genius that have go before ; and so each man of genius , though we 
 never come across he , as his operation proceed in place remote from 
 our thoroughfare , be yet influence the practical world that ignore 
 he , for ever and ever . that be geniu ! we can not   describe it in book ; 
 we can only hint and suggest it by the accessory which we artfully 
 heap about it . the entrance of a true probationer into the terrible 
 ordeal of Practical Life be like that into the miraculous cavern , by 
 which , legend inform we , Saint Patrick convert Ireland . " 

 BLANCHE . - " what be that legend ? I never hear of it . " 

 MR . CAXTON . - " my dear , you will find it in a thin folio at the right on 
 enter my study , write by Thomas Messingham , and call ' Florilegium 
 Insulae Sanctorum , ' etc . the account therein be confirm by the 
 relation of an honest soldier , one Louis Ennius , who have actually 
 enter the cavern . in short , the truth of the legend be undeniable , 
 unless you mean to say , which I can not   for a moment suppose , that Louis 
 Ennius be a liar . thus it run : Saint Patrick , find that the irish 
 pagan be incredulous as to his pathetic assurance of the pain and 
 torment destine to those who do not expiate their sin in this world , 
 pray for a miracle to convince they . his prayer be hear ; and a 
 certain cavern , so small that a man could not stand up therein at his 
 ease , be suddenly convert into a Purgatory , comprehend torture 
 sufficient to convince the most incredulous . one unacquainted with 
 human nature might conjecture that few would be dispose to venture 
 voluntarily into such a place ; on the contrary , pilgrim come in crowd . 
 now , all who enter from vain curiosity or with soul unprepared 
 perish miserably ; but those who enter with deep and earnest faith , 
 conscious of their fault , and if bold , yet humble , not only come out 
 safe and sound , but purify , as if from the water of a second baptism . 
 see Savage and Johnson at night in Fleet Street , - and who shall doubt 
 the truth of Saint Patrick 's purgatory ! " Therewith my father sigh ; 
 close his Lucian , which have lie open on the table , and would read none 
 but " good book " for the rest of the evening . 




 CHAPTER II . 

 on their escape from the prison to which Mr. Avenel have condemn they , 
 Leonard and his mother find their way to a small public - house that lie 
 at a little distance from the town , and on the outskirt of the high 
 road . with his arm round his mother 's waist , Leonard support she 
 step , and soothe her excitement . in fact , the poor woman 's nerve 
 be greatly shake , and she feel an uneasy remorse at the injury her 
 intrusion have inflict on the young man 's worldly prospect . as the 
 shrewd reader have guess already , that infamous tinker be the prime 
 agent of evil in this critical turn in the affair of his quondam 
 customer ; for , on his return to his haunt around Hazeldean and the 
 Casino , the tinker have hasten to apprise Mrs. Fairfield of his 
 interview with Leonard , and , on find that she be not aware that the 
 boy be under the roof of his uncle , the pestilent vagabond   perhaps 
 from spite against Mr. Avenel , or perhaps from that pure love of 
 mischief by which metaphysical critic explain the character of Iago , 
 and which certainly form a main element in the idiosyncrasy of Mr. 
 Sprott   have so impressed on the widow 's mind the haughty demeanour of 
 the uncle , and the refined costume of the nephew , that Mrs. Fairfield 
 have be seize with a bitter and insupportable jealousy . there be an 
 intention to rob she of her boy ! - he be to be make too fine for she . 
 his silence be now account for . this sort of jealousy , always more or 
 less a feminine quality , be often very strong amongst the poor ; and it 
 be the more strong in Mrs. Fairfield , because , lone woman that she be , 
 the boy be all in all to she . and though she be reconcile to the loss 
 of his presence , nothing could reconcile she to the thought that his 
 affection should be wean from she . moreover , there be in her mind 
 certain impression , of the justice of which the reader may well 
 judge hereafter , as to the gratitude - more than ordinarily filial - which 
 Leonard owe to she . in short , she do not like , as she phrase it , " to 
 be shake off ; " and after a sleepless night she resolve to judge for 
 herself , much move thereto by the malicious suggestion to that effect 
 make by Mr. Sprott , who mightily enjoy the idea of mortify the 
 gentleman by whom he have be so disrespectfully threaten with 
 the treadmill . the widow feel angry with Parson Dale and with the 
 Riccaboccas : she think they be in the plot against she ; she 
 communicate therefore , her intention to none , and off she set , 
 perform the journey partly on the top of the coach , partly on foot . 
 no wonder that she be dusty , poor woman ! 

 " and , oh , boy ! " say she , half sob , " when I get through the 
 lodge - gate , come on the lawn , and see all that power o ' fine folk , I 
 say to myself , say I - for I feel fritt - I will just have a look at he 
 and go back . but ah , Lenny , when I see thee , look so handsome , and 
 when thee turn and cry ' Mother , ' my heart be just ready to leap out 
 o ' my mouth , and so I could not help hug thee , if I have die for 
 it . and thou wert so kind , that I forget all Mr. Sprott have say about 
 Dick 's pride , or think he have just tell a fib about that , as he have 
 want I to believe a fib about thee . then Dick come up - and I have not 
 see he for so many year - and we come o ' the same father and mother ; 
 and so - and so - " the widow 's sob here fairly choke she . " ah , " she 
 say , after give vent to her passion , and throw her arm round 
 Leonard 's neck , as they sit in the little sand parlour of the 
 public - house , - " ah , and I have   bring thee to this . go back ; go back , 
 boy , and never mind I . " 

 with some difficulty Leonard pacify poor Mrs. Fairfield , and get she 
 to retire to bed ; for she be , indeed , thoroughly exhaust . he then 
 step forth into the road ; musingly . all the star be out ; and 
 Youth , in its trouble , instinctively look up to the star . fold his 
 arm , Leonard gaze on the heaven , and his lip murmur . 

 from this trance , for so it might be call , he be awaken by a 
 voice in a decidedly London accent ; and , turn hastily round , see Mr. 
 Avenel 's very gentlemanlike butler . 

 Leonard 's first idea be that his uncle have repent , and send in search 
 of he . but the butler seem as much surprised at the rencontre as 
 himself : that personage , indeed , the fatigue of the day be over , be 
 accompany one of Mr. Gunter 's waiter to the public - house   at which 
 the latter have secure his lodging , have discover an old friend 
 in the waiter , and propose to regale himself with a cheerful glass , 
 and - that of course - abuse of his present situation . 

 " Mr. Fairfield ! " exclaim the butler , while the waiter walk 
 discreetly on . 

 Leonard look , and say nothing . the butler begin to think that some 
 apology be due for leave his plate and his pantry , and that he might 
 as well secure Leonard 's propitiatory influence with his master . 

 " please , sir , " say he , touch his hat , " I be just a showing Mr. 
 Giles the way to the Blue Bells , where he put up for the night . I hope 
 my master will not be offend . if you be a go back , sir , would you 
 kindly mention it ? " 

 " I be not go back , Jarvis , " answer Leonard , after a pause ; " I be 
 leave Mr. Avenel 's house , to accompany my mother , - rather suddenly . 
 I should be very much oblige to you if you would bring some thing of 
 mine to I at the Blue Bells . I will give you the list , if you will step 
 with I to the inn . " 

 without wait for a reply , Leonard then turn towards the inn , and 
 make his humble inventory : item , the clothe he have bring with he 
 from the Casino ; item , the knapsack that have contain they ; item , a few 
 book , ditto ; item , Dr. Riccabocca 's watch ; item , sundry manuscript , 
 on which the young student now build all his hope of fame and fortune . 
 this list he put into Mr. Jarvis 's hand . 

 " Sir , " say the butler , twirl the paper between his finger and thumb , 
 " you be not a go for long , I hope ? " and he look on the face of 
 the young man , who have always be " civil speak to he , " with as 
 much curiosity and as much compassion as so apathetic and princely 
 a personage could experience in matter affect a family less 
 aristocratic than he have hitherto condescend to serve . 

 " yes , " say Leonard , simply and briefly ; " and your master will no doubt 
 excuse you for render I this service . " Mr. Jarvis postpone for the 
 present his glass and chat with the waiter , and go back at once to Mr. 
 Avenel . that gentleman , still seat in his library , have not be aware 
 of the butler 's absence ; and when Mr. Jarvis enter and tell he that 
 he have meet Mr. Fairfield , and communicate the commission with which 
 he be intrust , ask leave to execute it , Mr. Avenel feel the man 's 
 inquisitive eye be on he , and conceive new wrath against Leonard for 
 a new humiliation to his pride . it be awkward to give no explanation 
 of his nephew 's departure , still more awkward to explain . after a short 
 pause , Mr. Avenel say sullenly , " my nephew be go away on business 
 for some time , - do what he tell you ; " and then turn his back , and 
 light his cigar . 

 " that beast of a boy , " say he , soliloquizing , " either mean this as an 
 affront , or an overture : if an affront , he be , indeed , well get rid 
 of ; if an overture , he will soon make a more respectful and proper 
 one . after all , I can not   have too little of relation till I have fairly 
 secure Mrs. M'Catchley . an Honourable ! I wonder if that make I an 
 honourable too ? this curse Debrett contain no practical information on 
 those point . " 

 the next morning the clothe and the watch with which Mr. Avenel 
 present Leonard be return , with a note mean to express gratitude , 
 but certainly write with very little knowledge of the world ; and so 
 full of that somewhat over - resentful pride which have in early life 
 make Leonard fly from Hazeldean , and refuse all apology to Randal , that 
 it be not to be wonder at that Mr. Avenel 's last remorseful 
 feeling evaporate in ire . " I hope he will starve ! " say the uncle , 
 vindictively . 




 CHAPTER III . 

 " listen to I , my dear mother , " say Leonard the next morning , as , with 
 knapsack on his shoulder and Mrs. Fairfield on his arm , he walk along 
 the high road ; " I do assure you from my heart that I do not regret the 
 loss of favour which I see plainly would have crush out of I the 
 very sense of independence . but do not fear for I ; I have education 
 and energy , - I shall do well for myself , trust I . - no , I can not , it be 
 true , go back to our cottage ; I can not be a gardener again . do not   ask 
 I , - I should be discontent , miserable . but I will go up to London ! 
 that be the place to make a fortune and a name : I will make both . oh , 
 yes , trust I , I will . you shall soon be proud of your Leonard ; and then 
 we will always live together , - always ! do not   cry . " 

 " but what can you do in Lunnon , - such a big place , Lenny ? " 

 " what ! every year do not some lad leave our village , and go and seek 
 his fortune , take with he but health and strong hand ? I have these , 
 and I have more : I have brain and thought and hope , that - again I 
 say , no , no ; never fear for I ! " 

 the boy throw back his head proudly ; there be something sublime in his 
 young trust in the future . 

 " well . but you will write to Mr. Dale or to I ? I will get Mr. Dale 
 or the good mounseer   now I know they be not agin I   to read your 
 letter . " 

 " I will , indeed ! " 

 " and , boy , you have nothing in your pocket . we have pay Dick ; these , 
 at least , be my own , after pay the coach fare . " and she would thrust 
 a sovereign and some shilling into Leonard 's waistcoat pocket . 

 after some resistance , he be force to consent . 

 " and there be a sixpence with a hole in it . do not   part with that , Lenny ; 
 it will bring thee good luck . " 

 thus talk , they gain the inn where the three road meet , and from 
 which a coach go direct to the Casino . and here , without enter the 
 inn , they sit on the greensward by the hedgerow , wait the arrival 
 of the coach - Mrs. Fairfield be much subdue in spirit , and there 
 be evidently on her mind something uneasy , - some struggle with her 
 conscience . she not only upbraid herself for her rash visit , but she 
 keep talk of her dead Mark . and what would he say of she , if he could 
 see she in heaven ? 

 " it be so selfish in I , Lenny . " 

 " Pooh , pooh ! have not a mother a right to her child ? " 

 " ay , ay , ay ! " cry Mrs. Fairfield . " I do love you as a child , - my own 
 child . but if I be not your mother , after all , Lenny , and cost you all 
 this - oh , what would you say of I then ? " 

 " not my own mother ! " say Leonard , laugh as he kiss she . " well , I 
 do not   know what I should say then differently from what I say now , - that 
 you , who bring I up and nurse and cherish I , have a right to my 
 home and my heart , wherever I be . " 

 " bless thee ! " cry Mrs. Fairfield , as she press he to her heart . 
 " but it weigh here , - it weigh , " she say , start up . 

 at that instant the coach appear , and Leonard run forward to inquire 
 if there be an outside place . then there be a short bustle while the 
 horse be be change ; and Mrs. Fairfield be lift up to the roof 
 of the vehicle , so all further private conversation between she and 
 Leonard cease . but as the coach whirl away , and she wave her hand 
 to the boy , who stand on the road - side gaze after she , she still 
 murmur , " it weigh here , - it weigh ! " 




 CHAPTER IV . 

 Leonard walk sturdily on in the high road to the Great City . the day 
 be calm and sunlit , but with a gentle breeze from gray hill at the 
 distance ; and with each mile that he pass , his step seem to grow 
 more firm , and his front more elate . oh , it be such joy in youth to be 
 alone with one 's daydream ! and youth feel so glorious a vigour in the 
 sense of its own strength , though the world be before and - against it ! 
 remove from that chilling counting - house , from the imperious will of 
 a patron and master , all friendless , but all independent , the young 
 adventurer feel a new being , feel his grand nature as Man . and on the 
 Man rush the genius long interdict and thrust aside , - rush back , 
 with the first breath of adversity , to console - no ! the Man need not 
 consolation , - to kindle , to animate , to rejoice ! if there be a being in 
 the world worthy of our envy , after we have grow wise philosopher 
 of the fireside , it be not the palled voluptuary , nor the careworn 
 statesman , nor even the great prince of art and letter , already 
 crown with the laurel , whose leaf be as fit for poison as for 
 garland ; it be the young child of adventure and hope . ay , and the 
 emptier his purse , ten to one but the rich his heart , and the wide 
 the domain which his fancy enjoy as he go on with kingly step to the 
 Future . 

 not till towards the evening do our adventurer slacken his pace and 
 think of rest and refreshment . there , then , lie before he on either 
 side the road those wide patch of uninclosed land which in England 
 often denote the entrance to a village . presently one or two neat 
 cottage come in sight ; then a small farmhouse , with its yard and barn . 
 and some way far yet , he see the sign swing before an inn of some 
 pretension , - the sort of inn often find on a long stage between two 
 great town commonly call " the Halfway House . " but the inn stand back 
 from the road , have its own separate sward in front , whereon be a 
 great beech - tree   from which the sign extend   and a rustic arbour ; so 
 that to gain the inn , the coach that stop there take a sweep from 
 the main thoroughfare . between our pedestrian and the inn there stand , 
 naked and alone , on the common land , a church ; our ancestor never would 
 have choose that site for it ; therefore it be a modern church , - modern 
 Gothic ; handsome to an eye not verse in the attribute of 
 ecclesiastical architecture , very barbarous to an eye that be . somehow 
 or other the church look cold and raw and uninviting . it look a 
 church for show , - much too big for the scattered hamlet , and void of 
 all the venerable association which give their peculiar and unspeakable 
 atmosphere of piety to the church in which succeed generation have 
 kneel and worship . Leonard pause and survey the edifice with 
 an unlearned but poetical gaze ; it dissatisfy he . and he be yet 
 ponder why , when a young girl pass slowly before he , her 
 eye fix on the ground , open the little gate that lead into the 
 churchyard , and vanish . he do not see the child 's face ; but there be 
 something in her movement so utterly listless , forlorn , and sad that 
 his heart be touch . what do she there ? he approach the low wall 
 with a noiseless step , and look over it wistfully . 

 there by a grave , evidently quite recent , with no wooden tomb nor 
 tombstone like the rest , the little girl have throw herself , and she be 
 sob loud and passionately . Leonard open the gate , and approach 
 she with a soft step . mingle with her sob , he hear broken sentence , 
 wild and vain , as all human sorrowing over graf must be . 

 " Father ! oh , Father , do you not really hear I ? I be so lone , so lone ! 
 take I to you , - take I ! " and she bury her face in the deep grass . 

 " poor child ! " say Leonard , in a half whisper , - " he be not there . look 
 above ! " 

 the girl do not heed he ; he put his arm round her waist gently ; she 
 make a gesture of impatience and anger , but she would not turn her face , 
 and she cling to the grave with her hand . 

 after clear , sunny day the dew fall more heavily ; and now , as the sun 
 set , the herbage be bath in a vaporous haze , - a dim mist rise around . 
 the young man seat himself beside she , and try to draw the child to 
 his breast . then she turn eagerly , indignantly , and push he aside 
 with jealous arm . he profane the grave ! he understand she with his 
 deep poet - heart , and rise . there be a pause . Leonard be the first to 
 break it . 

 " come to your home with I , my child , and we will talk of he by the 
 way . " 

 " Him ! who be you ? you do not know he ! " say the girl , still with 
 anger . " go away ! why do you disturb I ? I do no one harm . go ! go ! " 

 " you do yourself harm , and that will grieve he if he see you yonder ! 
 come ! " 

 the child look at he through her blind tear , and his face 
 soften and soothe she . 

 " go ! " she say , very plaintively , and in subdued accent . " I will but 
 stay a minute more . I - I have so much to say yet . " 

 Leonard leave the churchyard , and wait without ; and in a short time 
 the child come forth , waive he aside as he approach she , and hurry 
 away . he follow she at a distance , and see she disappear within the 
 inn . 




 CHAPTER V. 

 " hip - Hip - Hurrah ! " such be the sound that greet our young traveller 
 as he reach the inn door , - a sound joyous in itself , but sadly out of 
 harmony with the feeling which the child sob on the tombless grave 
 have leave at his heart . the sound come from within , and be follow by 
 thump and stamp , and the jingle of glass . a strong odour of 
 tobacco be waft to his olfactory sense . he hesitate a moment at the 
 threshold . 

 before he , on bench under the beech - tree and within the arbour , be 
 group sundry athletic form with " pipe in the liberal air . " 

 the landlady , as she pass across the passage to the taproom , catch 
 sight of his form at the doorway , and come forward . Leonard still stand 
 irresolute . he would have go on his way , but for the child : she have 
 interested he strongly . 

 " you seem full , ma'am , " say he . " can I have accommodation for the 
 night ? " 

 " why , indeed , sir , " say the landlady , civilly , " I can give you a 
 bedroom , but I do not   know where to put you meanwhile . the two parlour 
 and the tap - room and the kitchen be all choke - full . there have be a 
 great cattle - fair in the neighbourhood , and I suppose we have as many as 
 fifty farmer and drover stop here . " 

 " as to that , ma'am , I can sit in the bedroom you be kind enough to give 
 I ; and if it do not cause you much trouble to let I have some 
 tea there , I should be glad ; but I can wait your leisure . do not put 
 yourself out of the way for I . " 

 the landlady be touch by a consideration she be not much habituate 
 to receive from her bluff customer . " you speak very handsome , sir , and 
 we will do our good to serve you , if you will excuse all fault . this 
 way , sir . " Leonard lower his knapsack , step into the passage , 
 with some difficulty force his way through a knot of sturdy giant 
 in top - boot or leathern gaiter , who be swarine in and out the 
 tap - room , and follow his hostess upstairs to a little bedroom at the 
 top of the house . 

 " it be small , sir , and high , " say the hostess , apologetically . " but 
 there be four gentleman farmer that have come a great distance , and all 
 the first floor be engage ; you will be more out of the noise here . " 

 " nothing can suit I well . but , stay , - pardon I ; " and Leonard , 
 glance at the garb of the hostess , observe she be not in mourning . 
 " a little girl whom I see in the churchyard yonder , weep very 
 bitterly - be she a relation of your ? poor child ! she seem to have 
 deep feeling than be common at her age . " 

 " ah , sir , " say the landlady , put the corner of her apron to her 
 eye , " it be a very sad story . I do not   know what to do . her father be 
 take ill on his way to Lunnon , and stop here , and have be bury 
 four day . and the poor little girl seem to have no relation - and 
 where be she to go ? Laryer Jones say we must pass she to Marybone 
 parish , where her father live last ; and what be to become of she then ? 
 my heart bleed to think on it . " 

 here there rise such an uproar from below , that it be evident some 
 quarrel have break out ; and the hostess , recall to her duty , 
 hasten to carry thither her propitiatory influence . 

 Leonard seat himself pensively by the little lattice . here be some 
 one more alone in the world than he ; and she , poor orphan , have no stout 
 man 's heart to grapple with fate , and no golden manuscript that be 
 to be as the " open - Sesame " to the treasure of Aladdin . by and by , 
 the hostess bring he up a tray with tea and other refreshment , and 
 Leonard resume his inquiry . " no relative ? " say he ; ' surely the 
 child must have some kinsfolk in London ? do her father leave no 
 direction , or be he in possession of his faculty ? " 

 " yes , sir ; he be quite reasonable like to the last . and I ask he 
 if he have not anything on his mind , and he say , ' I have . ' and I say , 
 ' your little girl , sir ? ' and he answer I , ' yes , ma'am ; ' and lay 
 his head on his pillow , he weep very quietly . I could not say more 
 myself , for it set I off to see he cry so meekly ; but my husband be 
 hard nor I , and he say , ' cheer up , Mr. Digby ; have not you well 
 write to your friend ? ' 

 " ' friend ! ' say the gentleman , in such a voice ! ' friend I have but 
 one , and I be go to he ! I can not take she there ! ' then he seem 
 suddenly to recollect himself , and call for his clothe , and rummage 
 in the pocket as if look for some address , and could not find it . 
 he seem a forgetful kind of gentleman , and his hand be what I call 
 helpless hand , sir ! and then he gasp out , ' stop , stop ! I never have 
 the address . write to Lord Les - ' , something like Lord Lester , but we 
 could not make out the name . indeed he do not finish it , for there 
 be a rush of blood to his lip ; and though he seem sensible when 
 he recover   and know we and his little girl too , till he go off 
 smile , he never speak word more . " 

 " poor man , " say Leonard , wipe his eye . " but his little girl surely 
 remember the name that he do not finish ? " 

 " no . she say he must have mean a gentleman whom they have meet in 
 the Park not long ago , who be very kind to her father , and be Lord 
 something ; but she do not   remember the name , for she never see he before 
 or since , and her father talk very little about any one lately , but 
 think he should find some kind friend at Screwstown , and travel 
 down there with she from Lunnon . but she suppose he be disappoint , 
 for he go out , come back , and merely tell she to put up the thing , as 
 they must go back to Lunnon . and on his way there he - die . hush , what be 
 that ? I hope she do not overhear we . no , we be talk low . she have 
 the next room to your'n , sir . I think I hear her sobbing . hush ! " 

 " in the next room ? I hear nothing . well , with your leave , I will speak 
 to she before I quit you . and have her father no money with he ? " 

 " yes , a few sovereign , sir ; they pay for his funeral , and there be 
 a little left still , - enough to take she to town ; for my husband say , 
 say he , ' Hannah , the widow give her mite , and we must not take the 
 orphan 's ; ' and my husband be a hard man , too , sir - bless he ! " 

 " let I take your hand , ma'am . God reward you both . " 

 " La , sir ! why , even Dr. Dosewell say , rather grumpily though , ' never 
 mind my bill ; but do not   call I up at six o'clock in the morning again , 
 without know a little more about people . ' and I never afore know Dr. 
 Dosewell go without his bill be pay . he say it be a trick o ' the 
 other doctor to spite he . " 

 " what other doctor ? " 

 " oh , a very good gentleman , who get out with Mr. Digby when he be take 
 ill , and stay till the next morning ; and our doctor say his name be 
 Morgan , and he live in Lunnou , and be a homy - something . " 

 " Homicide , " suggest Leonard , ignorantly . 

 " ah , homicide ; something like that , only a deal long and bad . but 
 he leave some of the tiny little ball you ever see , sir , to give the 
 child ; but , bless you , they do she no good , - how should they ? " 

 " tiny ball , oh - homoeopathist - I understand . and the doctor be kind to 
 she ; perhaps he may help she . have you write to he ? " 

 " but we do not   know his address , and Lunnon be a vast place , sir . " 

 " I be go to London and will find it out . " 

 " ah , sir , you seem very kind ; and sin ' she must go to Lunnon   for what 
 can we do with she here ? - she be too genteel for service , I wish she be 
 go with you . " 

 " with I ! " say Leonard , startle , - " with I ! well , why not ? " 

 " I be sure she come of good blood , sir . you would have know her father 
 be quite the gentleman , only to see he die , sir . he go off so kind 
 and civil like , as if he be ashamed to give so much trouble , - quite a 
 gentleman , if ever there be one . and so be you , sir , I be sure , " say 
 the land lady , courtesye ; " I know what gentlefolk be . I have   be a 
 housekeeper in the first of family in this very shire , sir , though I 
 can not   say I have   serve in Lunnon ; and so , as gentlefolk know each other , 
 I ' ve   no doubt you could find out her relation . dear , dear ! come , 
 come ! " 

 here there be loud cry for the hostess , and she hurry away . the 
 farmer and drover be begin to depart , and their bill be to be 
 make out and pay . Leonard see his hostess no more that night . the last 
 hip - hip - hurrah be hear , - some toast , perhaps to the health of the 
 county member , - and the chamber of woe beside Leonard 's rattle with 
 the shout . by and by , silence gradually succeed the various dissonant 
 sound below . the cart and gig roll away ; the clatter of hoof on 
 the road cease ; there be then a dumb dull sound as of lock - up , and 
 low , hum voice below , and footstep mount the stair to bed , 
 with now and then a drunken hiccough or maudlin laugh , as some conquer 
 votary of Bacchus be fairly carry up to his domicile . 

 all , then , at last be silent , just as the clock from the church sound 
 the stroke of eleven . 

 Leonard , meanwhile , have be look over his manuscript . there be 
 first a project for an improvement on the steam - engine , - a project that 
 have long lain in his mind , begin with the first knowledge of mechanic 
 that he have glean from his purchase of the tinker . he put that 
 aside now , - it require too great an effort of the reasoning faculty to 
 re - examine . 

 he glance less hastily over a collection of essay on various 
 subject , - some that he think indifferent , some that he think good . 
 he then linger over a collection of verse write in his good hand 
 with love care , - verse first inspire by his perusal of Nora 's 
 melancholy memorial . these verse be as a diary of his heart and his 
 fancy , - those deep , unwitnessed struggle which the boyhood of all more 
 thoughtful nature have pass in its bright yet murky storm of the cloud 
 and the lightning - flash , though but few boy pause to record the crisis 
 from which slowly emerge Man . and these first desultory grappling with 
 the fugitive airy image that flit through the dim chamber of the 
 brain have become with each effort more sustained and vigorous , till the 
 phantom be spell , the fly one arrest , the Immaterial seize , 
 and clothe with Form . gaze on his last effort , Leonard feel that 
 there at length speak forth the poet . it be a work which though as yet 
 but half complete , come from a strong hand ; not that shadow tremble 
 on unsteady water , which be but the pale reflex and imitation of 
 some bright mind , sphere out of reach and afar , but an original 
 substance , - a life , a thing of the Creative Faculty , - breathing 
 back already the breath it have receive . this work have pause during 
 Leonard 's residence with Mr. Avenel , or have only now and then , in 
 stealth , and at night , receive a rare touch . now , as with a fresh eye 
 he reperuse it , and with that strange , innocent admiration , not of 
 self - for a man 's work be not , alas ! himself , - it be the beautify 
 and idealize essence , extract he know not how from his own human 
 element of clay ; admiration know but to poet , - their pure delight , 
 often their sole reward . and then with a warm and more earthly beat of 
 his full heart , he rush in fancy to the Great City , where all river 
 of fame meet , but not to be merge and lose , sallye forth again , 
 individualized and separate , to flow through that one vast Thought of 
 God which we call the world . 

 he put up his paper ; and open his window , as be his ordinary custom , 
 before he retire to rest , - for he have many odd habit ; and he love to 
 look out into the night when he pray . his soul seem to escape from 
 the body - to mount on the air , to gain more rapid access to the far 
 Throne in the Infinite - when his breath go forth among the wind , and 
 his eye rest fix on the star of heaven . 

 so the boy pray silently ; and after his prayer he be about , 
 lingeringly , to close the lattice , when he hear distinctly sob close 
 at hand . he pause , and hold his breath , then look gently out ; the 
 casement next his own be also open . someone be also at watch by that 
 casement , - perhaps also pray . he listen yet more intently , and 
 catch , soft and low , the word , " Father , Father , do you hear I now ? " 




 CHAPTER VI . 

 Leonard open his door and steal towards that of the room adjoining ; 
 for his first natural impulse have be to enter and console . but when 
 his touch be on the handle , he draw back . child though the mourner be , 
 her sorrow be render yet more sacred from intrusion by her sex . 
 something , he know not what , in his young ignorance , withhold he 
 from the threshold . to have cross it then would have seem to he 
 profanation . so he return , and for hour yet he occasionally hear the 
 sob , till they die away , and childhood weep itself to sleep . 

 but the next morning , when he hear his neighbour astir , he knock 
 gently at her door : there be no answer . he enter softly , and see 
 she seat very listlessly in the centre of the room , - as if it have no 
 familiar nook or corner as the room of home have , her hand droop on 
 her lap , and her eye gaze desolately on the floor . then he approach 
 and speak to she . 

 Helen be very subdued , and very silent . her tear seem dry up ; 
 and it be long before she give sign or token that she heed he . at 
 length , however , he gradually succeed in rouse her interest ; and 
 the first symptom of his success be in the quiver of her lip , and the 
 overflow of her downcast eye . 

 by little and little he worm himself into her confidence ; and she tell 
 he in broken whisper her simple story . but what move he the most 
 be , that beyond her sense of loneliness she do not seem to feel her 
 own unprotected state . she mourn the object she have nurse and 
 heed and cherish , for she have be rather the protectress than the 
 protect to the helpless dead . he could not gain from she any more 
 satisfactory information than the landlady have already impart , as to 
 her friend and prospect ; but she permit he passively to look among 
 the effect her father have leave , save only that , if his hand touch 
 something that seem to her association especially holy , she wave he 
 back , or draw it quickly away . there be many bill receipt in the 
 name of Captain Digby , old yellow fade music - score for the flute , 
 extract of Parts from Prompt Books , gay part of lively comedy , 
 in which hero have so noble a contempt for money , - fit hero for 
 a Sheridan and a Farquhar ; close by these be several pawnbroker 's 
 ticket ; and , not array smoothly , but crumple up , as if with an 
 indignant nervous clutch of the helpless hand , some two or three 
 letter . he ask Helen 's permission to glance at these , for they might 
 afford a clew to friend . Helen give the permission by a silent bend of 
 the head . the letter , however , be but short and freeze answer from 
 what appear to be distant connection or former friend , or person 
 to whom the deceased have apply for some situation . they be all very 
 disheartening in their tone . Leonard next endeavour to refresh 
 Helen 's memory as to the name of the nobleman which have be last on her 
 father 's lip ; but there he fail wholly . for it may be remember that 
 Lord L'Estrange , when he press his loan on Mr. Digby , and subsequently 
 tell that gentleman to address he at Mr. Egerton 's , have , from a natural 
 delicacy , send the child on , that she might not witness the charity 
 bestow on the father ; and Helen say truly that Mr. Digby have sink 
 latterly into an habitual silence on all his affair . she might have 
 hear her father mention the name , but she have not treasure it up ; all 
 she could say be , that she should know the stranger again if she meet 
 he , and his dog too . see that the child have grow calm , Leonard be 
 then go to leave the room , in order to confer with the hostess , when 
 she rise suddenly , though noiselessly , and put she little hand in his , 
 as if to detain he . she do not say a word ; the action say all , - say , 
 " do not desert I . " and Leonard 's heart rush to his lip , and he 
 answer to the action , as he bend down , and kiss she cheek , " Orphan , 
 will you go with I ? we have one Father yet to both of we , and he will 
 guide we on earth . I be fatherless like you . " she raise her eye to 
 his , look at he long , and then lean her head confidingly on his 
 strong young shoulder . 




 CHAPTER VII . 

 at noon that same day the young man and the child be on their road to 
 London . the host have at first a little demurred at trust Helen to so 
 young a companion ; but Leonard , in his happy ignorance , have talk so 
 sanguinely of find out this lord , or some adequate protector for the 
 child ; and in so grand a strain , though with all sincerity , have speak 
 of his own great prospect in the metropolis   he do not say what they 
 be !   that have he be the craftiest impostor he could not more have 
 take in the rustic host . and while the landlady still cherish the 
 illusive fancy that all gentlefolk must know each other in London , as 
 they do in a county , the landlord believe , at least , that a young man 
 so respectably dressed , although but a foot - traveller , who talk in 
 so confident a tone , and who be so willing to undertake what might 
 be rather a burdensome charge , unless he see how to rid himself of it , 
 would be sure to have friend old and wise than himself , who would 
 judge what could well be do for the orphan . 

 and what be the host to do with she ? well this volunteered escort , 
 at least , than vaguely pass she on from parish to parish , and leave 
 her friendless at last in the street of London . Helen , too , smile 
 for the first time on be ask her wish , and again put her hand in 
 Leonard 's . in short , so it be settle . 

 the little girl make up a bundle of the thing she most prize or 
 need . Leonard do not feel the additional load , as he sling it to his 
 knapsack ; the rest of the luggage be to be send to London as soon as 
 Leonard write   which he promise to do soon   and give an address . 

 Helen pay her last visit to the churchyard ; and she join she 
 companion as he stand on the road , without the solemn precinct . and now 
 they have go on some hour ; and when he ask she if she be tired , 
 she still answer " no . " but Leonard be merciful , and make their day 's 
 journey short ; and it take they some day to reach London . by the long 
 lonely way they grow so intimate , at the end of the second day , they 
 call each other brother and sister ; and Leonard , to his delight , find 
 that as her grief , with the bodily movement and the change of scene , 
 subside from its first intenseness and its insensibility to other 
 impression , she develop a quickness of comprehension far beyond her 
 year . poor child ! that have be force upon she by Necessity . and 
 she understand he in his spiritual consolation , half poetical , half 
 religious ; and she listen to his own tale , and the story of his 
 self - education and solitary struggle , - those , too , she understand . 
 but when he burst out with his enthusiasm , his glorious hope , his 
 confidence in the fate before they , then she would shake her head very 
 quietly and very sadly . do she comprehend they ! alas ! perhaps too well . 
 she know more as to real life than he do . Leonard be at first their 
 joint treasurer ; but before the second day be over , Helen seem to 
 discover that he be too lavish ; and she tell he so , with a prudent 
 grave look , put her hand on his arm as he be about to enter an 
 inn to dine ; and the gravity would have be comic , but that the eye 
 through their moisture be so meek and grateful . she feel he be about 
 to incur that ruinous extravagance on her account . somehow or other , the 
 purse find its way into her keeping , and then she look proud and in 
 her natural element . 

 ah ! what happy meal under her care be provide ; so much more 
 enjoyable than in dull , sand inn - parlour , swarm with fly , and 
 reeking with stale tobacco . she would leave he at the entrance of a 
 village , bind forward , and cater , and return with a little basket and a 
 pretty blue jug - which she have buy on the road , - the last fill with 
 new milk ; the first with new bread , and some special dainty in radish 
 or water - tress . and she have such a talent for find out the 
 prettiest spot whereon to halt and dine : sometimes in the heart of a 
 wood , - so still , it be like a forest in fairy tale , the hare steal 
 through the alley , or the squirrel peep at they from the bough ; 
 sometimes by a little brawl stream , with the fish see under the 
 clear wave , and shoot round the crumb throw to they . they make an 
 Arcadia of the dull road up to their dread Thermopylae , the war against 
 the million that wait they on the other side of their pass through 
 Tempo . 

 " shall we be as happy when we be great ? " say Leonard , in his grand 
 simplicity . 

 Helen sigh , and the wise little head be shake . 




 CHAPTER VIII . 

 at last they come within easy reach of London ; but Leonard have resolve 
 not to enter the metropolis fatigued and exhaust , as a wanderer 
 need refuge , but fresh and elate , as a conqueror come in triumph 
 to take possession of the capital . therefore they halt early in the 
 evening of the day precede this imperial entry , about six mile from 
 the metropolis , in the neighbourhood of Ealing   for by that route lay 
 their way . they be not tired on arrive at their inn . the weather 
 be singularly lovely , with that combination of softness and brilliancy 
 which be only know to the rare true summer day of England ; all below 
 so green , above so blue , - day of which we have about six in the year , 
 and recall vaguely when we read of Robin Hood and Maid Marian , of Damsel 
 and Knight in Spenser 's golden Summer Song , or of Jacques , drop under 
 the oak - tree , watch the deer amidst the dell of Ardennes . so , after 
 a little pause at their inn , they stroll forth , not for travel but 
 pleasure , towards the cool of sunset , pass by the ground that once 
 belong to the Duke of Kent , and catch a glimpse of the shrub 
 and lawn of that beautiful domain through the lodge - gate ; then they 
 cross into some field , and come to a little rivulet call the 
 Brent . Helen have be more sad that day than on any during their 
 journey , - perhaps because , on approach London , the memory of her 
 father become more vivid ; perhaps from her precocious knowledge of life , 
 and her foreboding of what be to befall they , child that they both 
 be . but Leonard be selfish that day ; he could not be influence by 
 his companion 's sorrow ; he be so full of his own sense of be , and 
 he already catch from the atmosphere the fever that belong to anxious 
 capital . 

 " sit here , sister , " say he , imperiously , throw himself under the 
 shade of a pollard - tree that overhang the wind brook , ' sit here and 
 talk . " 

 he fling off his hat , toss back his rich curl , and sprinkle his brow 
 from the stream that eddy round the root of the tree that bulge out , 
 bald and gnarl , from the bank and delve into the wave below . Helen 
 quietly obey he , and nestle close to his side . 

 " and so this London be really very vast , - very ? " he repeat 
 inquisitively . 

 " very , " answer Helen , as , abstractedly , she pluck the cowslip near 
 she , and let they fall into the running water . " see how the flower 
 be carry down the stream ! they be lose now . London be to we what the 
 river be to the flower , very vast , very strong ; " and she add , after a 
 pause , " very cruel ! " 

 " Cruel ! ah , it have be so to you ; but now - now I will take care of 
 you ! " he smile triumphantly ; and his smile be beautiful both in its 
 pride and its kindness . it be astonish how Leonard have alter since 
 he have leave his uncle 's . he be both young and old ; for the sense of 
 genius , when it snap its shackle , make we both old and wise as to 
 the world it soar to , young and blinder as to the world it spring 
 from . 

 " and it be not a very handsome city , either , you say ? " 

 " very ugly indeed , " say Helen , with some fervour ; " at least all I have 
 see of it . " 

 " but there must be part that be pretty than other ? you say there 
 be park : why should not we lodge near they and look upon the green 
 tree ? " 

 " that would be nice , " say Helen , almost joyously ; " but - " and here 
 the head be shake - " there be no lodging for we except in court and 
 alley . " 

 " why ? " 

 " why ? " echo Helen , with a smile , and she hold up the purse . 

 " Pooh ! always that horrid purse ; as if , too , we be not go to fill 
 it ! do not I tell you the story of Fortunio ? well , at all event , we 
 will go first to the neighbourhood where you last live , and learn there 
 all we can ; and then the day after to - morrow I will see this Dr. Morgan , 
 and find out the lord . " 

 the tear start to Helen 's soft eye . " you want to get rid of I soon , 
 brother . " 

 " I ! ah , I feel so happy to have you with I it seem to I as if I have 
 pin for you all my life , and you have come at last ; for I never have 
 brother nor sister nor any one to love , that be not old than myself , 
 except - " 

 " except the young lady you tell I of , " say Helen , turn away her 
 face ; for child be very jealous . 

 " yes , I love she , love she still . but that be different , " say 
 Leonard . " I could never have talk to she as to you : to you I open my 
 whole heart ; you be my little Muse , Helen : I confess to you my wild 
 whim and fancy as frankly as if I be write poetry . " as he say 
 this , a step be hear , and a shadow fall over the stream . a belated 
 angler appear on the margin , draw his line impatiently across the 
 water , as if to worry some doze fish into a bite before it finally 
 settle itself for the night . absorb in his occupation , the angler do 
 not observe the young person on the sward under the tree , and he halt 
 there , close upon they . 

 " curse that perch ! " say he , aloud . 

 " take care , sir , " cry Leonard ; for the man , in step back , nearly 
 trod upon Helen . 

 the angler turn . " what be the matter ? Hist ! you have frighten my 
 perch . keep still , can not   you ? " 

 Helen draw herself out of the way , and Leonard remain motionless . he 
 remember Jackeymo , and feel a sympathy for the angler . 

 " it be the most extraordinary perch , that ! " mutter the stranger , 
 soliloquize . " it have the devil 's own luck . it must have be bear 
 with a silver spoon in its mouth , that damned perch ! I shall never 
 catch it , - never ! ha ! no , only a weed . I give it up . " with this , he 
 indignantly jerk his rod from the water and begin to disjoint it . 
 while leisurely engage in this occupation , he turn to Leonard . 

 " Humph ! be you intimately acquaint with this stream , sir ? " 

 " no , " answer Leonard . " I never see it before . " 

 ANGLER ,   solemnly . - " then , young man , take my advice , and do not give 
 way to its fascination . Sir , I be a martyr to this stream ; it have be 
 the Delilah of my existence . " 

 LEONARD   interested , the last sentence seem to he poetical . - " The 
 Delilah ! sir , the Delilah ! " 

 ANGLER . - " the Delilah . young man , listen , and be warn by example . when 
 I be about your age , I first come to this stream to fish . Sir , on that 
 fatal day , about three p.m. , I hook up a fish , - such a big one , it 
 must have weigh a pound and a half . Sir , it be that length ; " and the 
 angler put finger to wrist . " and just when I have get it nearly ashore , 
 by the very place where you be sit , on that shelving bank , young 
 man , the line break , and the perch twist himself among those root , 
 and - cacodaemon that he be - run off , hook and all . well , that fish 
 haunt I ; never before have I see such a fish . minnow I have catch in 
 the Thames and elsewhere , also gudgeon , and occasionally a dace . but 
 a fish like that - a PERCH , all his fin up , like the sail of a 
 man - of - war - a monster perch , - a whale of a perch ! no , never till then 
 have I know what leviathan lie hide within the deep . I could not sleep 
 till I have return ; and again , sir , - I catch that perch . and this time 
 I pull he fairly out of the water . he escape ; and how do he escape ? 
 Sir , he leave his eye behind he on the hook . year , long year , have 
 pass since then ; but never shall I forget the agony of that moment . " 

 LEONARD . - " to the perch , sir ? " 

 ANGLER . - " perch ! agony to he ! he enjoy it . Agony to I ! I gaze on 
 that eye , and the eye look as sly and as wicked as if it be laugh 
 in my face . well , sir , I have hear that there be no well bait for a 
 perch than a perch 's eye . I adjust that eye on the hook , and drop 
 in the line gently . the water be unusually clear ; in two minute I 
 see that perch return . he approach the hook ; he recognize his eye , 
 frisk his tail , make a plunge , and , as I live , carry off the 
 eye , safe and sound ; and I see he digest it by the side of that 
 water - lily . the mocking fiend ! seven time since that day , in the course 
 of a varied and eventful life , have I catch that perch , and seven time 
 have that perch escape . " 

 LEONARD   astonish . - " it can not   be the same perch ; perch be very 
 tender fish . a hook inside of it , and an eye hook out of it - no perch 
 could withstand such havoc in its constitution . " 

 angler   with an appearance of awe . - " it do seem supernatural . but it 
 be that perch ; for hark ye , sir , there be only one perch in the whole 
 brook ! all the year I have fish here , I have never catch another 
 perch ; and this solitary inmate of the watery element I know by sight 
 well than I know my own lose father . for each time that I have raise 
 it out of the water , its profile have be turn to I , and I have see 
 with a shudder that it have have only - one Eye ! it be a most mysterious 
 and a most diabolical phenomenon , that perch ! it have be the ruin of my 
 prospect in life . I be offer a situation in Jamaica : I could not 
 go with that perch leave here in triumph . I might afterwards have have an 
 appointinent in India , but I could not put the ocean between myself 
 and that perch : thus have I fritter away my existence in the fatal 
 metropolis of my native land . and once a week from February to December 
 I come hither . good heaven ! if I should catch the perch at last , the 
 occupation of my existence will be go . " 

 Leonard gaze curiously at the angler , as the last thus mournfully 
 conclude . the ornate turn of his period do not suit with his costume . 
 he look wofully threadbare and shabby , - a genteel sort of shabbiness 
 too , - shabbiness in black . there be humour in the corner of his 
 lip ; and his hand , though they do not seem very clean - indeed his 
 occupation be not friendly to such nicety - be those of a man who 
 have not know manual labour . his face be pale and puff , but the tip 
 of the nose be red . he do not seem as if the watery element be as 
 familiar to himself as to his Delilah , the perch . 

 " such be life ! " recommence the angler , in a moralize tone , as he slide 
 his rod into its canvas case . " if a man know what it be to fish all 
 one 's life in a stream that have only one perch , to catch that one perch 
 nine time in all , and nine time to see it fall back into the water , 
 plump , - if a man know what it be , why , then " - here the angler look 
 over his shoulder full at Leonard - " why then , young sir , he would know 
 what human life be to vain ambition . good - evening . " 

 away he go treading over the daisy and kingcup . Helen 's eye 
 follow he wistfully . 

 " what a strange person ! " say Leonard , laugh . 

 " I think he be a very wise one , " murmur Helen ; and she come close up 
 to Leonard , and take his hand in both her , as if she feel already that 
 he be in need of the Comforter , - the line break , and the perch lose ! 




 CHAPTER IX . 

 at noon the next day , London steal upon they through a gloomy , thick , 
 oppressive atmosphere ; for where be it that we can say London burst on 
 the sight ? it steal on they through one of its fairest and most gracious 
 avenue of approach , - by the stately garden of Kensington , along the 
 side of Hyde Park , and so on towards Cumberland Gate . 

 Leonard be not the least strike . and yet with a very little money , and 
 a very little taste , it would be easy to render this entrance to London 
 as grand and as impose as that to Paris from the Champs Elysees . as 
 they come near the Edgware Road , Helen take her new brother by the 
 hand and guide he ; for she know all that neighbourhood , and she be 
 acquaint with a lodging near that occupy by her father   to that 
 lodge itself she could not have go for the world , where they might 
 be house cheaply . 

 but just then the sky , so dull and overcast since morning , seem one 
 mass of black cloud . there suddenly come on a violent storm of rain . the 
 boy and girl take refuge in a cover mew , in a street run out 
 of the Edgware Road . this shelter soon become crowded ; the two young 
 pilgrim creep close to the wall , apart from the rest , Leonard 's arm 
 round Helen 's waist , shelter she from the rain that the strong 
 wind contend with it beat in through the passage . presently a young 
 gentleman of well mien and dress than the other refugee enter , not 
 hastily , but rather with a slow and proud step , as if , though he deign 
 to take shelter , he scorn to run to it . he glance somewhat haughtily 
 at the assemble group , pass on through the midst of it , come near 
 Leonard , take off his hat , and shake the rain from its brim . his head 
 thus uncover , leave all his feature expose ; and the village youth 
 recognize , at the first glance , his old victorious assailant on the 
 green at Hazeldean . 




 CHAPTER IX . 

 yet Randal Leslie be alter . his dark cheek be as thin as in boyhood , 
 and even yet more waste by intense study and night vigil ; but the 
 expression of his face be at once more refined and manly , and there be 
 a steady concentrated light in his eye , like that of one who have be 
 in the habit of bring all his thought to one point . he look old 
 than he be . he be dress simply in black , a colour which become 
 he ; and altogether his aspect and figure be , not showy indeed , but 
 distinguish . he look to the common eye a gentleman ; and to the more 
 observant a scholar . 

 helter - skelter ! pell - mell ! the group in the passage now press each 
 on each , now scatter on all side , make way , rush down the mew , 
 against the wall , as a fiery horse dart under shelter . the rider , a 
 young man with a very handsome face , and dress with that peculiar care 
 which we commonly call dandyism , cry out , good - humouredly , " do not   be 
 afraid ; the horse sha'n't   hurt any of you . a thousand pardon - so ho ! so 
 ho ! " he pat the horse , and it stand as still as a statue , fill up 
 the centre of the passage . the group resettle ; Randal approach the 
 rider . 

 " Frank Hazeldean ! " 

 " ah , be it indeed randal Leslie ? " 

 Frank be off his horse in a moment , and the bridle be consign to the 
 care of a slim ' prentice - boy hold a bundle . 

 " my dear fellow , how glad I be to see you . how lucky it be that I 
 should turn in here . not like I either , for I do not   much care for a 
 ducking . stay in town , Randal ? " 

 " yes ; at your uncle 's , Mr. Egerton . I have leave Oxford . " 

 " for good ? " 

 " for good . " 

 " but you have not take your degree , I think ? we Etonians all consider 
 you book for a double - first . oh , we have be so proud of your 
 fame , - you carry off all the prize . " 

 " not all ; but some , certainly . Mr. Egerton offer I my choice , - to 
 stay for my degree , or to enter at once into the Foreign Office . I 
 prefer the end to the mean . for , after all , what good be academical 
 honours but as the entrance to life ? to enter now be to save a step in a 
 long way , Frank . " 

 " ah , you be always ambitious , and you will make a great figure , I be 
 sure . " 

 " perhaps so - if I work for it . Knowledge be power . " Leonard start . 

 " and you ! " resume Randal , look with some curious attention at his 
 old schoolfellow . " you never come to Oxford . I do hear you be go 
 into the army . " 

 " I be in the Guards , " say Frank , try hard not to look too conceited 
 as he make that acknowledgment . " the governor pishe a little , and would 
 rather I have come to live with he in the old Hall , and take to farming . 
 Time enough for that , eh ? by Jove , Randal , how pleasant a thing be life 
 in London ! do you go to Almack 's to - night ? " 

 " no ; Wednesday be a holiday in the House . there be a great parliamentary 
 dinner at Mr. Egerton 's . he be in the Cabinet now , you know ; but you 
 do not   see much of your uncle , I think . " 

 " our set be different , " say the young gentleman , in a tone of voice 
 worthy of Brummel . " all those parliamentary fellow be devilish dull . 
 the rain be over . I do not   know whether the governor would like I to call 
 at Grosvenor Square ; but pray come and see I . here be my card to remind 
 you ; you must dine at our mess . such capital fellow ! what day will you 
 fix ? " 

 " I will call and let you know . do not   you find it rather expensive in the 
 Guards ? I remember that you think the governor , as you call he , use 
 to chafe a little when you write for more pocket - money ; and the only 
 time I ever see you with tear in your eye be when Mr. Hazeldean , in 
 send you L5 , remind you that his estate be not entail , - be 
 at his own disposal , and they should never go to an extravagant 
 spendthrift . it be not a pleasant threat that , Frank . " 

 " oh ! " cry the young man , colour deeply . " it be not the threat that 
 pain I ; it be that my father could think so meanly of I as to 
 fancy that - well , well , but those be schoolboy day . and my father be 
 always more generous than I deserve . we must see a great deal of each 
 other , randal . how good - natured you be at Eton , make my long and 
 short for I ; I shall never forget it . do call soon . " 

 Frank swing himself into his saddle , and reward the slim youth with 
 half - a - crown , - a largess four time more ample than his father would 
 have deem sufficient . a jerk of the rein and a touch of the heel , off 
 bound the fiery horse and the gay young rider . randal muse , and as 
 the rain have now cease , the passenger under shelter disperse and go 
 their way . only Randal , Leonard , and Helen remain behind . then , as 
 randal , still muse , lift his eye , they fall full upon Leonard 's 
 face . he start , pass his hand quickly over his brow , look again , 
 hard and piercingly ; and the change in his pale cheek to a shade still 
 paler , a quick compression and nervous gnawing of his lip , show that 
 he too recognize an old foe . then his glance run over Leonard 's dress , 
 which be somewhat dust - stain , but far above the class amongst which 
 the peasant be bear . randal raise his brow in surprise , and with a 
 smile slightly supercilious - the smile sting Leonard - and with a slow 
 step , Randal leave the passage , and take his way towards Grosvenor 
 Square . the Entrance of Ambition be clear to he . 

 then the little girl once more take Leonard by the hand , and lead he 
 through row of humble , obscure , dreary street . it seem almost like 
 an allegory personify , as the sad , silent child lead on the penniless 
 and low - bear adventurer of genius by the squalid shop and through 
 the winding lane , which grow mean and meaner , till both their form 
 vanish from the view . 




 CHAPTER X. 

 " but do come ; change your dress , return and dine with I ; you will have 
 just time , Harley . you will meet the most eminent man of our party ; 
 surely they be worth your study , philosopher that you affect to be . " 

 thus say Audley Egerton to Lord L'Estrange , with whom he have be 
 rid   after the toil of his office . the two gentleman be in 
 Audley 's library , - Mr. Egerton , as usual , button up , seat in his 
 chair , in the erect posture of a man who scorn " inglorious ease ; " 
 Harley , as usual , throw at length on the sofa . , his long hair in 
 careless curl , his neckcloth loose , his habiliment flow simplex 
 mundit be , indeed , his grace all his own ; seemingly negligent , never 
 slovenly ; at ease everywhere and with every one , even with Mr. Audley 
 Egerton , who chill or awe the ease out of most people . 

 " Nay , my dear Audley , forgive I . but your eminent man be all man of 
 one idea , and that not a divert one , politic ! politic ! politic ! 
 the storm in the saucer . " 

 " but what be your life , Harley ? - the saucer without the storm ? " 

 " do you know , that be very well say , Audley ? I do not think you have 
 so much liveliness of repartee . life ! life ! it be insipid , it be 
 shallow , - no launch Argosies in the saucer . Audley , I have the oddest 
 fancy - " 

 " that of course , " say Audley , dryly ; " you never have any other . what be 
 the new one ? " 

 HARLEY   with great gravity . - " do you believe in Mesmerism ? " 

 AUDLEY . - " certainly not . " 

 HARLEY . - " if it be in the power of an animal magnetizer to get I out 
 of my own skin into somebody 's else ! that be my fancy ! I be so tired of 
 myself , - so tired ! I have run through all my idea , - know every one of 
 they by heart . when some pretentious impostor of an idea perk itself up 
 and say , ' look at I , - I ' m a new acquaintance , ' I just give it a nod , 
 and say ' not at all , you have only get a new coat on ; you be the same 
 old wretch that have bore I these last twenty year ; get away . ' but 
 if one could be in a new skin , if I could be for half - an - hour your tall 
 porter , or one of your eminent matter - of - fact man , I should then really 
 travel into a new world . ' every man 's brain must be a world in 
 itself , eh ? if I could but make a parochial settlement even in your , 
 Audley , - run over all your thought and sensation . upon my life , I will 
 go and talk to that french mesmerizer about it . " 

   if , at the date in which Lord L'Estrange hold this conversation 
  with Mr. Egerton , Alfred de Musset have write his comedy , we 
  should suspect that his lordship have plagiarize from one of they 
  the whimsical idea that he here vent upon Audley . in repeat it , 
  the author at least can not escape from the charge of obligation to a 
  writer whose humour be sufficiently opulent to justify the loan . 

 AUDLEY   who do not seem to like the notion of have his thought 
 and sensation rummage , even by his friend , and even in fancy   - " Pooh , 
 pooh , pooh ! do talk like a man of sense . " 

 HARLEY . - " Man of sense ! where shall I find a model ? I do not   know a man 
 of sense ! - never meet such a creature . do not   believe it ever exist . at 
 one time I think Socrates must have be a man of sense : a delusion ; 
 he would stand gaze into the air , and talk to his genius from 
 sunrise to sunset . be that like a man of sense ? poor Audley ! how puzzle 
 he look ! well , I will try and talk sense to oblige you . and first "   here 
 Harley raise himself on his elbow , - " first , be it true , as I have 
 hear vaguely , that you be pay court to the sister of that infamous 
 italian traitor ? " 

 " Madame di Negra ? no : I be not pay court to she , " answer Audley , 
 with a cold smile . " but she be very handsome ; she be very clever ; she be 
 useful to I , - I need not say how or why ; that belong to my metier as a 
 politician . but I think , if you will take my advice , or get your friend 
 to take it , I could obtain from her brother , through my influence with 
 she , some liberal concession to your exile . she be very anxious to know 
 where he be . " 

 " you have not tell she ? " 

 " no ; I promise you I would keep that secret . " 

 " be sure you do ; it be only for some mischief , some snare , that she 
 could desire such information . concession ! pooh ! this be no question of 
 concession , but of right . " 

 " I think you should leave your friend to judge of that . " 

 " well , I will write to he . meanwhile , beware of this woman . I have 
 hear much of she abroad , and she have the character of her brother for 
 duplicity and - " 

 " beauty , " interrupt Audley , turn the conversation with practise 
 adroitness . " I be tell that the count be one of the handsome man in 
 Europe , much handsomer than his sister still , though nearly twice her 
 age . tut , tut , Harley ; fear not for I . I be proof against all feminine 
 attraction . this heart be dead . " 

 " Nay , nay ; it be not for you to speak thus , - leave that to I . but even 
 I will not say it . the heart never die . and you ; what have you lose ? - a 
 wife ; true : an excellent , noble - hearted woman . but be it love that you 
 feel for she ? enviable man , have you ever love ? " 

 " perhaps not , Harley , " say Audley , with a sombre aspect and in deject 
 accent ; " very few man ever have love , at least as you mean by the 
 word . but there be other passion than love that kill the heart , and 
 reduce we to mechanism . " 

 while Egerton speak , Harley turn aside , and his breast heave . there 
 be a short silence ; Audley be the first to break it . 

 " speak of my lost wife , I be sorry that you do not approve what I 
 have do for her young kinsman , Randal Leslie . " 

 HARLEY   recover himself with an effort . - " be it true kindness to 
 bid he exchange manly independence for the protection of an official 
 patron ? " 

 AUDLEV . - " I do not bid he . I give he his choice . at his age , I should 
 have choose as he have do . " 

 HARLEY . - " I trust not ; I think well of you . but answer I one question 
 frankly , and then I will ask another . do you mean to make this young man 
 your heir ? " 

 AUDLEY   with a slight embarrassment . - " Heir , pooh ! I be young still . I 
 may live as long as he - time enough to think of that . " 

 HARLEY . - " then now to my second question . have you tell this youth 
 plainly that he may look to you for influence , but not for wealth ? " 

 AUDLEY   firmly . - " I think I have ; but I shall repeat it more 
 emphatically . " 

 HARLEY . - " then I be satisfied as to your conduct , but not as to his . 
 for he have too acute an intellect not to know what it be to forfeit 
 independence ; and , depend on it , he have make his calculation , and would 
 throw you into the bargain in any balance that he could strike in his 
 favour . you go by your experience in judge man ; I by my instinct . 
 Nature warn we as it do the inferior animal , - only we be too 
 conceited , we bipeds , to heed she . my instinct of soldier and gentleman 
 recoil from that old young man . he have the soul of the Jesuit . I see 
 it in his eye , I hear it in the tread of his foot ; volto sciolto he have 
 not ; I pensieri stretti he have . Hist ! I hear now his step in the hall . 
 I should know it from a thousand . that be his very touch on the handle of 
 the door . " 

 Randal Leslie enter . Harley - who , despite his disregard for form , and 
 his dislike to Randal , be too high - breed not to be polite to his junior 
 in age or inferior in rank - rose and bow . but his bright pierce eye 
 do not soften as they catch and bear down the deep and more latent
