 RANDAL . - " I have fulfil your commission , Mr. Egerton . I go first 
 to Maida Hill , and see Mr. Burley . I give he the check , but he say it 
 be too much , and he should return half to the banker ; he will write the 
 article as you suggest . I then - " 

 AUDLEY . - " enough , Randal ! we will not fatigue Lord L'Estrange with these 
 little detail of a life that displease he , - the life political . " 

 HARLEY . - " but these detail do not displease I ; they reconcile I to my 
 own life . go on , pray , Mr. Leslie . " 

 Randal have too much tact to need the caution glance of Mr. Egerton . 
 he do not continue , but say with a soft voice , " do you think , Lord 
 L'Estrange , that the contemplation of the mode of life pursue by other 
 can reconcile a man to his own , if he have before think it need a 
 reconciler ? " Harley look pleased , for the question be ironical ; and 
 if there be a thing in the world be abhor , it be flattery . 

 " recollect your Lucretius , Mr. Leslie , the Suave mare , etc . , ' pleasant 
 from the cliff to see the mariner toss on the ocean . ' faith , I think 
 that sight reconcile one to the cliff , though , before , one might have 
 be tease by the splash from the spray , and deafen by the scream

 more of my soldier ! remember I have your promise when I come to claim 
 it . good - by , Mr. Leslie , I hope that Burley 's article will be worth the 
 check . " 

 Lord L'Estrange mount his horse , which be still at the door , and ride 
 through the Park . but he be no long now unknown by sight . bow and 
 nod salute he on every side . 

 " alas , I be find out , then , " say he to himself . " that terrible duchess 
 of Knaresborough , too - I must fly my country . " he push his horse into 
 a canter , and be soon out of the Park . as he dismount at his 
 father 's sequester house , you would have hardly suppose he the same 
 whimsical , fantastic , but deep and subtle humourist that delight in 
 perplex the material Audley , for his expressive face be unutterably 
 serious . but the moment he come into the presence of his parent , the 
 countenance be again light and cheerful . it brighten the whole room

 CHAPTER XI . 

 " Mr. Leslie , " say Egerton , when Harley have leave the library , " you do 
 not act with your usual discretion in touch upon matter connect 
 with politic in the presence of a third party . " 

 " I feel that already , sir ; my excuse be , that I hold Lord L'Estrange to 
 be your most intimate friend . " 

 " a public man , Mr. Leslie , would ill serve his country if he be not 
 especially reserve towards his private friend - when they do not belong 
 to his party . " 

 " but pardon I my ignorance . Lord Lansmere be so well known to be one of 
 your supporter , that I fancy his son must share his sentiment , and 
 be in your confidence . " 

 Egerton 's brow slightly contract , and give a stern expression to a 
 countenance always firm and decide . he however answer in a mild tone , 

 " at the entrance into political life , Mr. Leslie , there be nothing 
 in which a young man of your talent should be more on his guard than

 that be one reason why young man of talent disappoint their friend , and 
 remain so long out of office . " 

 a haughty flush pass over Randal 's brow , and fade away quickly ; he 
 bow in silence . 

 Egerton resume , as if in explanation , and even in kindly apology , 

 " look at Lord L'Estrange himself . what young man could come into life 
 with bright auspex ? rank , wealth , high animal spirit   a great

 share for practical existence . " 

 " you have a keen eye , " say Audley , with some admiration , - " keen for one

 " once for all , then , distinctly and emphatically , I say , never count

 he . well , and what say Frank ? "

 " because they be idle , and idleness be contagious . right , - well not

 Mr. Grinders be steward to Audley Egerton . 

 Mr. Levy be a handsome man , who wear a camellia in his button - hole ;

 CHAPTER XII . 

 as the company assemble in the drawing - room , Mr. Egerton introduce 
 Randal Leslie to his eminent friend in a way that greatly contrast

 Leslie be receive with all that charming courtesy which be the to

 " it be your first ball at Almack 's then , Mr. Leslie ? " 

 " my first . " 

 " and you have not secure a partner ? shall I find you one ? what do you 
 think of that pretty girl in pink ? " 

 " I see she - but I can not think of she . " 

 " you be rather , perhaps , like a diplomatist in a new court , and your 
 first object be to know who be who . " 

 " I confess that on begin to study the history of my own day I should 
 like to distinguish the portrait that illustrate the memoir . "

 see the different notabilite enter one by one , and observe without 
 be observe . this be the least I can do for a friend of Mr. 
 Egerton 's . " 

 " Mr. Egerton , then , " say Randal , - as they thread their way through 
 the space without the rope that protect the dancer , - " Mr. Egerton have 
 have the good fortune to win your esteem even for his friend , however

 perhaps as the Creator look down on this world , whose wondrous beauty

 CHAPTER XIV . 

 Leonard go out the next day with his precious manuscript . he have 
 read sufficient of modern literature to know the name of the principal 
 London publisher ; and to these he take his way with a bold step , though 
 a beat heart . 

 that day he be out long than the last ; and when he return , and come

 he , - there be on his face so deep , so silent , and so concentrate a 
 despondency . he sit down listlessly , and do not kiss she this time , as

 he take charge of another life ! he ! 

 she coax he at last into communicate his day 's chronicle . the 
 reader beforehand know too well what it must be to need detailed 
 repetition . Most of the publisher have absolutely refuse to look at 
 his manuscript ; one or two have good - naturedly glanced over and return 
 they at once with a civil word or two of flat rejection . one publisher 
 alone - himself a man of letter , and who in youth have go through 
 the same bitter process of disillusion that now await the village 
 genius - volunteer some kindly though stern explanation and counsel to

 poem with attention , and even with frank admiration . he could appreciate

 history , and even with his hope ; and then he say , in bid he

 " if I publish this poem for you , speak as a trader , I shall be a 
 considerable loser . do I publish all I admire , out of sympathy with 
 the author , I should be a ruin man . but suppose that , impress as 
 I really be with the evidence of no common poetic gift in this 
 manuscript , I publish it , not as a trader , but a lover of literature , 
 I shall in reality , I fear , render you a great disservice , and perhaps 
 unfit your whole life for the exertion on which you must rely for 
 independence . " 

 " how , sir ? " cry Leonard . " not that I would ask you to injure yourself 
 for I , " he add , with proud tear in his eye . 

 " how , my young friend ? I will explain . there be enough talent in 
 these verse to induce very flattering review in some of the literary 
 journal . you will read these , find yourself proclaim a poet , will cry 
 ' I be on the road to fame . ' you will come to I , ' and my poem , how do 
 it sell ? ' I shall point to some groan shelf , and say , ' not twenty 
 copy ! the journal may praise , but the public will not buy it . ' 
 ' but you will have get a name , ' you say . yes , a name as a poet just 
 sufficiently know to make every man in practical business disincline 
 to give fair trial to your talent in a single department of positive 
 life ; none like to employ poet ; - a name that will not put a penny in 
 your purse , - bad still , that will operate as a barrier against every 
 escape into the way whereby man get to fortune . but have once taste 
 praise , you will continue to sigh for it : you will perhaps never again 
 get a publisher to bring forth a poem , but you will hanker round the 
 purlieus of the Muses , scribble for periodical , fall at last into a 
 bookseller 's drudge . profit will be so precarious and uncertain , that 
 to avoid debt may be impossible ; then , you who now seem so ingenuous and 
 so proud , will sink deeply still into the literary mendicant , begging , 
 borrowing - " 

 " never ! never ! never ! " cry Leonard , veil his face with his hand . 

 " such would have be my career , " continue the publisher ; " but I 
 luckily have a rich relative , a trader , whose call I despise as a 
 boy , who kindly forgive my folly , bind I as an apprentice , and here I 
 be ; and now I can afford to write book as well as sell they . 

 " young man , you must have respectable relation , - go by their advice and 
 counsel ; cle fast to some positive calling . be anything in this city 
 rather than poet by profession . " 

 " and how , sir , have there ever be poet ? have they other calling ? " 

 " read their biography , and then - envy they ! " 

 Leonard be silent a moment ; but lift his head , answer loud 
 and quickly , " I have read their biography . true , their lot be 
 poverty , - perhaps hunger . Sir , i - envy they ! " 

 " poverty and hunger be small evil , " answer the bookseller , with 
 a grave , kind smile . " there be bad , - debt and degradation , 
 and - despair . " 

 " no , sir , no , you exaggerate ; these last be not the lot of all poet . " 

 " right , for most of our great poet have some private mean of their 
 own . and for other - why , all who have put into a lottery have not 
 draw blank . but who could advise another man to set his whole hope 
 of fortune on the chance of a prize in a lottery ? and such a lottery ! " 
 groan the publisher , glance towards sheet and ream of dead 
 author , lie , like lead , upon his shelf . 

 Leonard clutch his manuscript to his heart , and hurry away . 

 " yes , " he mutter , as Helen clung to he , and try to console , - " yes , 
 you be right : London be very vast , very strong , and very cruel ; " and

 Leonard 's eye brighten . " a great comfort , sir . " he press the hand

 " I do , sir . " 

 " very bad symptom indeed , and must be stop before a relapse ! here , I 
 have cure three prophet and ten poet with this novel specific . "

 " what be your christian name ? - I forget . " 

 " Helen . " 

 " Helen , listen . in a year or two you will be a young woman , and it would

 " he shall be so ; and to - morrow , while he be out , I will come and fetch

 " I should have a heart of stone if I do , " cry the doctor , vehemently ;

 CHAPTER XV . 

 before he go the doctor write a line to " Mr. Prickett , Bookseller , 
 Holborn , " and tell Leonard to take it the next morning , as address . " I

 and when Leonard go forth the next morning , Helen stand at the street 
 door watch he depart - slowly , slowly . no doubt , in that humble lane 
 there be many sad heart ; but no heart so heavy as that of the still , 
 quiet child , when the form she have watch be to be see no more , and , 
 still stand on the desolate threshold , she gaze into space , and all

 CHAPTER XVI . 

 Mr. Prickett be a believer in homeeopathy , and declare , to the 
 indignation of all the apothecary round Holborn , that he have be 
 cure of a chronic rheumatism by Dr. Morgan . the good doctor have , as 
 he promise , see Mr. Prickett when he leave Leonard , and ask he as a 
 favour to find some light occupation for the boy , that would serve as an 
 excuse for a modest weekly salary . " it will not be for long , " say the 
 doctor : " his relation be respectable and well off . I will write to his 
 grandparent , and in a few day I hope to relieve you of the charge . of 
 course , if you do not   want he , I will repay what he cost meanwhile . " 

 Mr. Prickett , thus prepare for Leonard , receive he very graciously ; 
 and , after a few question , say Leonard be just the person he want 
 to assist he in catalogue his book , and offer he most handsomely 
 l1 a week for the task . 

 plunge at once into a world of book vast than he have ever before win

 standard work , but several curious and rare one . and Leonard pause

 of the mystery of the bibliographist . 

 nothing could be more dark and dingy than the shop . there be a booth

 always an attentive group ; within , a gas - lamp burn night and day . 

 but time pass quickly to Leonard . he miss not the green field , he 
 forget his disappointment , he cease to remember even Helen . o strange 
 passion of knowledge ! nothing like thee for strength and devotion ! 

 Mr. Prickett be a bachelor , and ask Leonard to dine with he on a

 publisher , at which Mr. Prickett rub his hand and laugh , as at a 
 capital joke . " oh , give up poetry , and stick to a shop , " cry he ; " and 
 to cure you forever of the mad whim to be author , I will just lend you the 
 ' Life and Works of Chatterton . ' you may take it home with you and read

 not till night , when the shop be closed , do Leonard return to his 
 lodging . and when he enter the room , he be strike to the soul by the

 there be a rose - tree in its pot on the table at which he write , and by

  DEAR , dear brother Leonard , God bless you . I will let you know when 
  we can meet again . take care of this rose , Brother , and do not 
  forget poor 

  HELEN . 

 over the word " forget " there be a big round blister spot that nearly 
 efface the word . 

 Leonard lean his face on his hand , and for the first time in his life 
 he feel what solitude really be . he could not stay long in the room . 
 he walk out again , and wander objectless to and fro the street . he 
 pass that stiller and humbler neighbourhood , he mix with the throng 
 that swarm in the more populous thoroughfare . hundred and thousand 
 pass he by , and still - still such solitude . 

 he come back , light his candle , and resolutely draw forth the 
 " Chatterton " which the bookseller have lend he . it be an old edition , 
 in one thick volume . it have evidently belong to some contemporary 
 of the poet 's , - apparently an inhabitant of Bristol , - some one who 
 have gather up many anecdote respect Chatterton 's habit , and who 
 appear even to have see he , nay , be in his company ; for the book 
 be interleave , and the leaf cover with note and remark , in 
 a stiff clear hand , - all evince personal knowledge of the mournful 
 immortal dead . at first , Leonard read with an effort ; then the strange 
 and fierce spell of that dread life seize upon he , - seize with pain 
 and gloom and terror , - this boy die by his own hand , about the age 
 Leonard have attain himself . this wondrous boy , of a genius beyond all 
 comparison the great that ever yet be develop and extinguish 
 at the age of eighteen , - self - teach , self - struggle , self - immolated . 
 nothing in literature like that life and that death ! 

 with intense interest Leonard peruse the tale of the brilliant 
 imposture , which have be so harshly and so absurdly construe into the 
 crime of a forgery , and which be   if not wholly innocent   so akin to 
 the literary device always in other case view with indulgence , 
 and exhibit , in this , intellectual quality in themselves so 
 amazing , - such patience , such forethought , such labour , such courage , 
 such ingenuity , - the quality that , well direct , make man great , 
 not only in book , but action . and , turn from the history of the 
 imposture to the poem themselves , the young reader bend before their 
 beauty , literally awed and breathless . how this strange Bristol boy 
 tame and master his rude and motley material into a music that 
 comprehend every tune and key , from the simple to the sublimest ! 
 he turn back to the biography ; he read on ; he see the proud , daring , 
 mournful spirit alone in the Great City , like himself . he follow its 
 dismal career , he see it fall with bruised and soil wing into 
 the mire . he turn again to the later work , wring forth as task for 
 bread , - the satire without moral grandeur , the politic without honest 
 faith . he shudder and sicken as he read . true , even here his poet 
 mind appreciate   what perhaps only poet can   the divine fire that

 hand of the young giant who have build up the stately verse of Rowley . 
 but alas ! how different from that " mighty line . " how all serenity

 journey - work ! then rapidly come on the catastrophe , - the closed door , 
 the poison , the suicide , the manuscript tear by the hand of despairing 
 wrath , and strew round the corpse upon the funereal floor . it be 
 terrible ! the spectre of the Titan boy   as describe in the note

 lustrous eye , haunt all the night the baffled and solitary child of 
 song . 




 CHAPTER XVII . 

 it will often happen that what ought to turn the human mind from some 
 peculiar tendency produce the opposite effect . one would think that the 
 perusal in the newspaper of some crime and capital punishment would 
 warn away all who have ever meditate the crime , or dread the chance 
 of detection . yet it be well known to we that many a criminal be make 
 by ponder over the fate of some predecessor in guilt . there be a 
 fascination in the Dark and Forbidden , which , strange to say , be only 
 lose in fiction . no man be more inclined to murder his nephew , or 
 stifle his wife , after read " Richard the Third " or " Othello . " it be 
 the reality that be necessary to constitute the danger of contagion . 
 now , it be this reality in the fate and life and crown suicide of 
 Chatterton that force itself upon Leonard 's thought , and sit there 
 like a visible evil thing , gather evil like cloud around it . there 
 be much in the dead poet 's character , his trial , and his doom , that 
 stand out to Leonard like a bold and colossal shadow of himself and his 
 fate . alas ! the book seller , in one respect , have say truly . Leonard 
 come back to he the next day a new man ; and it seem even to himself 
 as if he have lose a good angel in lose Helen . " oh , that she have be 
 by my side ! " think he . " oh , that I could have feel the touch of she 
 confide hand ; that , look up from the scathed and dreary ruin of 
 this life , that have sublimely lift itself from the plain , and 
 seek to tower aloft from a deluge , her mild look have speak to I 
 of innocent , humble , unaspire childhood ! ah ! if indeed I be still 
 necessary to she , - still the sole guardian and protector , - then could I 
 say to myself ; ' Thou must not despair and die ! thou hast she to live 
 and to strive for . ' but no , no ! only this vast and terrible London , - the 
 solitude of the dreary garret , and those lustrous eye , glare alike 
 through the throng and through the solitude . " 




 CHAPTER XVIII . 

 on the following Monday Dr. Morgan 's shabby man - servant open the door

  DR . MORGAN

 Leonard lay down this letter very calmly , and , except by a slight

 a name ! be this but an idle boast , or be it one of those flash of

 will here repeat from his account more succinctly . 

 Nora Avenel , while yet very young , leave her native village , or rather

 " there be no wedding - ring on Nora 's finger , never any rumour of her

 parent 's home , - these be all the evidence against she . but Mrs. Avenel

 motherless child ? " 

 " Inquiries ! Mrs. Avenel would have die first . your grandmother 's nature 
 be very rigid . have she come from prince , from Cadwallader himself , "

 CHAPTER XIX . 

 Leonard do not appear at the shop of Mr. Prickett that day . needless it

 on the second evening after Dr. Morgan 's departure , as Leonard be just

 Mr. Prickett mutter , " a very bad bargain indeed , " as Mr. Burley , with

 CHAPTER XX . 

 at first Leonard have always return home through the crowd

 his whole air . yet , vulgar as be his costume , he do not himself seem

 convention . his face look more pale and more puff than before , 
 the tip of his nose redder ; but the spark in his eye be of a lively 
 light , and there be self - enjoyment in the corner of his sensual , 
 humorous lip . 

 " you be an author , sir , " repeat Leonard . " well ; and what be your 
 report of the calling ? yonder column prop an urn . the column be tall , 
 and the urn be graceful . but it look out of place by the roadside : what 
 say you ? " 

 MR . BURLEY . - " it would look well in the churchyard . " 

 LEONARD . - " so I be think . and you be an author ! " 

 MR . BURLEY . - " ah , I say you have a quick sense of allegory . and so you 
 think an author look well in a churchyard , when you see he but as a 
 muffle urn under the moonshine , than stand beneath the gas - lamp in a 
 white hat , and with a red tip to his nose . abstractedly , you be right . 
 but , with your leave , the author would rather be where he be . let we 
 walk on . " the two man feel an interest in each other , and they walk 
 some yard in silence . 

 " to return to the urn , " say Mr. Burley , - " you think of fame and 
 churchyard . natural enough , before illusion die ; but I think of the 
 moment , of existence , - and I laugh at fame . Fame , sir - not worth a 
 glass of cold - without ! and as for a glass of warm , with sugar - and five 
 shilling in one 's pocket to spend as one please - what be there in 
 Westminster Abbey to compare with it ? " 

 " talk on , sir , - I should like to hear you talk . let I listen and hold 
 my tongue . " Leonard pull his hat over his brow , and give up his 
 moody , questioning , turbulent mind to his new acquaintance . 

 and John Burley talk on . a dangerous and fascinating talk it be , - the 
 talk of a great intellect fall ; a serpent trail its length on the

 serpent , yet without the serpent 's guile . if John Burley deceive and 
 tempt , he mean it not , - he crawl and glitter alike honestly . no 
 dove could be more simple . 

 laugh at fame , he yet dwelt with an eloquent enthusiasm on the joy 
 of composition . " what do I care what man without be to say and think 
 of the word that gush forth on my page ? " cry he . " if you think of the 
 public , of urn , and laurel , while you write , you be no genius ; you 
 be not fit to be an author . I write because it rejoice I , because it 
 be my nature . write , I care no more what become of it than the lark 
 for the effect that the song have on the peasant it wake to the plough . 
 the poet , like the lark , sing ' from his watch - tower in the sky . ' be 
 this true ? " 

 " yes , very true ! " 

 " what can rob we of this joy ? the bookseller will not buy ; the 
 public will not read . let they sleep at the foot of the ladder of the 
 angel , - we climb it all the same . and then one settle down into such 
 good - temper lucianic contempt for man . one want so little from they , 
 when one know what one 's self be worth , and what they be . they be 
 just worth the coin one can extract from they , in order to live . 

 " our life - that be worth so much to we . and then their joy , so vulgar 
 to they , we can make they golden and kingly . do you suppose Burns 
 drinking at the alehouse , with his boor around he , be drink , like 
 they , only beer and whiskey ? no , he be drink nectar ; he be imbibe

 the coarse human liquid be just need to unlock his spirit from the 
 clay , - take it from jerkin and corduroys , and wrap it in the ' singing

 but for that , and then it change at once into the drink of Hebe . but 
 come , you have not know this life , - you have not see it . come , give 
 I this night . I have money about I , - I will fling they abroad as 
 liberally as Alexander himself , when he leave to his share but hope . 
 come ! " 

 " Whither ? " 

 " to my throne . on that throne last sit Edmund Kean , mighty mime ! I be 
 his successor . we will see whether in truth these wild son of genius , 
 who be cite but ' to point a moral and adorn a tale , ' be object of 
 compassion . sober - suit tit to lament over a Savage or a Morland , a 
 Porson and a Burns ! " 

 " or a Chatterton , " say Leonard , gloomily . 

 " Chatterton be an impostor in all thing ; he feign excess that he 
 never know . he a bacchanalian , a royster ! he ! no . we will talk of he . 
 come ! " 

 Leonard go . 




 CHAPTER XXI . 

 the Room ! and the smoke - reek , and the gas glare of it ! the whitewash of 
 the wall , and the print thereon of the actor in their mime - robe , and 
 stage posture , - actor as far back as their own lose Augustan era , when 
 the stage be a real live influence on the manner and the age ! there 
 be Betterton , in wig and gown , - as Cato , moralize on the soul 's 
 eternity , and halt between Plato and the dagger . there be Woodward 
 as " The Fine Gentleman , " with the inimitable rake - hell in which the 
 hero of Wycherly and Congreve and Farquhar live again . there be 
 jovial Quin as Falstaff , with round buckler and " fair round belly . " 
 there be Colley Cibber in brocade , take snuff as with " his Lord , " 
 the thumb and forefinger raise in air , and look at you for applause . 
 there be Macklin as Shylock , with knife in hand : and kemble in the 
 solemn weed of the dane ; and Kean in the place of honour over the 
 chimneypiece . 

 when we be suddenly take from practical life , with its real workday 
 man , and present to the portrait of those sole hero of a world 
 Fantastic and Phantasmal , in the garment wherein they do ' strut and 
 fret their hour upon the stage , " verily there be something in the sight

 sense of some existence , apart from the one that wear away our day : 
 an existence that , afar from St. James 's and St. Giles 's , the Law Courts 
 and Exchange , go its way in terror or mirth , in smile or in tear ,

 be the man who live it - to whom our world be the false one , to whom 
 the Imaginary be the actual ! and do Shakspeare himself , in his life , 
 ever hearken to such applause as thunder round the personator of his 
 airy image ? vague child of the most transient of the art , fleet 
 shadow on run water , though throw down from the steadfast star , 
 be ye not happy than we who live in the real ? how strange you 
 must feel in the great circuit that ye now take through eternity ! no 
 prompt - book , no lamp , no act Congreve and Shakspeare there ! for 
 what part in the sky have your study on the earth fit you ? your 
 ultimate destiny be very puzzling . hail to your effigy , and pass we

 there , too , on the whitewashed wall , be admit the portrait of

 might be suppose to have be sufficiently good fellow to be at home

 Ben Jonson , with his heavy scowl ; Burns and Byron cheek by jowl . but

 imperious . what the deuce do he do there amongst prize - fighter and 
 actor and poet ? it seem an insult to his grand memory . nevertheless 
 there he be , very erect , and with a look of ineffable disgust in his

 crambo in the mind of ordinary man , - very like the motley picture

 and the company ? indescribable ! comedian , from small theatre , out of 
 employ ; pale , haggard - look boy , probably the son of worthy trader , 
 try their good to break their father ' heart ; here and there the 
 mark feature of a Jew . now and then you might see the curious puzzle 
 face of some greenhorn about town , or perhaps a Cantab ; and man of grave 
 age , and grayhaire , be there , and amongst they a wondrous proportion

 stamping and hallooing , and such hurrah for " Burley John . " and the 
 gentleman who have fill the great high leathern chair in his absence 
 give it up to John Burley ; and Leonard , with his grave , observant eye ,

 introducer . there be a nameless , expectant stir through the assembly ,

 the lamp , and begin , " Di tanti palpiti . " time fly . look at the dutch

 luscious roll in it . 

 " he will be grand to - night , " whisper a thin man , who look like a 
 tailor , seat on the other side of Leonard . time fly , - an hour . look

 his culminate point . what magnificent drollery ! what luxuriant humour ! 
 how the Rabelais shake in his easy - chair ! under the rush and the roar

 and Leonard listen too . not , as he would some night ago , in innocent 
 unquestione delight . no ; his mind have pass through great sorrow ,

 look again at the dutch clock . two hour have go . John Burley have 
 break out again from his silence , his voice thick and husky , and his

 look again at the dutch clock . three hour have pass . be John Burley

 beard , accompany with unctuous sob ; and now and then in the midst of

 CHAPTER XXII . 

 well , Leonard , this be the first time thou hast show that thou hast in 
 thee the iron out of which true manhood be forge and shape . Thou hast 
 the power to resist . forth , unebriate , unpolluted , he come from the 
 orgy , as yon star above he come from the cloud . 

 he have a latch - key to his lodging . he let himself in and walk 
 noiselessly up the creak wooden stair . it be dawn . he pass on to 
 his window and throw it open . the green elm - tree from the carpenter 's 
 yard look as fresh and fair as if root in solitude , league away 
 from the smoke of Babylon . 

 " Nature , Nature ! " murmur Leonard , " I hear thy voice now . this still , 
 this strengthen . but the struggle be very dread . here , despair of 
 life , - there , faith in life . nature think of neither , and life 
 serenely on . " 

 by and by a bird slide softly from the heart of the tree , and drop on 
 the ground below out of sight . but Leonard hear its carol . it awake its 
 companion ; wing begin to glance in the air , and the cloud grow red 
 towards the east . 

 Leonard sigh and leave the window . on the table , near Helen 's 
 rose - tree , which he bent over wistfully , lie a letter . he have not 
 observe it before . it be in Helen 's hand . he take it to the light , and 
 read it by the pure , healthful gleam of morn : - 

  IVY LODGE . 

  oh , my dear brother Leonard , will this find you well , and   more 
  happy I dare not say , but   less sad than when we part ? I write 
  kneeling , so that it seem to I as if I write and pray at the 
  same time . you may come and see I to - morrow evening , Leonard . do 
  come , do , - we shall walk together in this pretty garden ; and there 
  be an arbour all cover with jessamine and honeysuckle , from which 
  we can look down on London . I have look from it so many time , - 
  so many - try if I can guess the roof in our poor little street , 
  and fancy that I do see the dear elm - tree . 

  Miss Starke be very kind to I ; and I think after I have see you , 
  that I shall be happy here , - that be , if you be happy . 

  your own grateful sister , 

  HELEN . 

  P. S. - any one will direct you to our house ; it lie to the left 
  near the top of the hill , a little way down a lane that be overhung 
  on one side with chestnut - tree and lilac . I shall be watch for 
  you at the gate . 

 Leonard 's brow soften , he look again like his former self . up from 
 the dark sea at his heart smile the meek face of a child , and the wave 
 lie still as at the charm of a spirit . 




 CHAPTER XXIII . 

 " and what be Mr. Burley , and what have he write ? " ask Leonard of Mr. 
 Prickett , when he return to the shop . 

 let we reply to that question in our own word , for we know more about 
 Mr. Burley than Mr. Prickett do . 

 John Burley be the only son of a poor clergyman , in a village near 
 Ealing , who have scrap and save and pinch , to send his son to an 
 excellent provincial school in a northern county , and thence to college . 
 at the latter , during his first year , young Burley be remark by the 
 undergraduate for his thick shoe and coarse linen , and remarkable to 
 the authority for his assiduity and learning . the high hope be 
 entertain of he by the tutor and examiner . at the beginning of the 
 second year his high animal spirit , before keep down by study , break 
 out . reading have become easy to he . he knock off his task with a 
 facile stroke , as it be . he give up his leisure hour to Symposia by 
 no mean Socratical . he fall into an idle , hard - drinking set . he get 
 into all kind of scrape . the authority be at first kind and 
 forbear in their admonition , for they respect his ability , and 
 still hop he might become an honour to the University . but at last 
 he go drunk into a formal examination , and send in paper , after 
 the manner of Aristophanes , contain capital joke upon the Dons and 
 big - wig themselves . the offence be the great and seem the more 
 premeditate for be clothe in Greek . John Burley be expel . he 
 go home to his father 's a miserable man , for , with all his folly , 
 he have a good heart . remove from ill example , his life for a year be 
 blameless . he get admit as usher into the school in which he have 
 receive instruction as a pupil . this school be in a large town . John 
 Burley become member of a club form among the tradesman , and 
 spend three evening a week there . his astonishing convivial and 
 conversational power begin to declare themselves . he grow the oracle 
 of the club ; and , from be the most sober , peaceful assembly in which 
 grave father of a family ever smoke a pipe or sip a glass , it 
 grow under Mr. Burley 's auspex the parent of revel as frolic and 
 frantic as those out of which the old Greek Goat Song ever tipsily rise . 
 this would not do . there be a great riot in the street one night , and 
 the next morning the usher be dismiss . fortunately for John Burley 's 
 conscience , his father have die before this happen , - die believe 
 in the reform of his son . during his ushership Mr. Burley have scrap 
 acquaintance with the editor of the county newspaper , and give he 
 some capital political article ; for Burley be , like Parr and Porson , 
 a notable politician . the editor furnish he with letter to the 
 journalist in London , and John come to the metropolis and get employ 
 on a very respectable newspaper . at college he have know Audley Egerton , 
 though but slightly : that gentleman be then just rise into repute in 
 parliament . Burley sympathize with some question on which Audley 
 have distinguish himself , and write a very good article thereon , - an 
 article so good that Egerton inquire into the authorship , find out 
 Burley , and resolve in his own mind to provide for he whenever he 
 himself come into office . but Burley be a man whom it be impossible to 
 provide for . he soon lose his connection with the news paper . first , he 
 be so irregular that he could never be depend upon . secondly , he have 
 strange , honest , eccentric twist of thinking , that could coalesce 
 with the thought of no party in the long run . an article of his , 
 inadvertently admit , have horrify all the proprietor , staff , and 
 reader of the paper . it be diametrically opposite to the principle 
 the paper advocate , and compare its pet politician to catiline . then 
 John Burley shut himself up and write book . he write two or three 
 book , very clever , but not at all to the popular taste , - abstract and 
 learn , full of whim that be caviare to the multitude , and lard 
 with Greek . nevertheless they obtain for he a little money , and among 
 literary man some reputation . now Audley Egerton come into power , and 
 get he , though with great difficulty , - for there be many prejudice 
 against this scampish , harum - scarum son of the Muses , - a place in a 
 public office . he keep it about a month , and then voluntarily resign 
 it . " my crust of bread and liberty ! " quoth John Burley , and he vanish 
 into a garret . from that time to the present he live - Heaven know how ! 
 literature be a business , like everything else ; John Burley grow more 
 and more incapable of business . " he could not do task - work , " he say ; he 
 write when the whim seize he , or when the last penny be in his pouch , 
 or when he be actually in the spunge - house or the Fleet , - migrations 
 which occur to he , on an average , twice a year . he could generally 
 sell what he have actually write , but no one would engage he 
 beforehand . editor of magazine and other periodical be very glad 
 to have his article , on the condition that they be anonymous ; and 
 his style be not necessarily detect , for he could vary it with 
 the facility of a practised pen . Audley Egerton continue his good 
 supporter , for there be certain question on which no one write with 
 such force as John Burley , - question connect with the metaphysics of 
 politic , such as law reform and economical science . and Audley Egerton 
 be the only man John Burley put himself out of the way to serve , and 
 for whom he would give up a drinking bout and do task - work ; for John 
 Burley be grateful by nature , and he feel that Egerton have really try 
 to befriend he . indeed , it be true , as he have state to Leonard by the 
 Brent , that even after he have resign his desk in the London office , 
 he have have the offer of an appointment in Jamaica , and a place in India , 
 from the minister . but probably there be other charm then than those 
 exercise by the one - eyed perch that keep he to the neighbourhood of 
 London . with all his grave fault of character and conduct , John Burley 
 be not without the fine quality of a large nature . he be most 
 resolutely his own enemy , it be true , but he could hardly be say to be 
 any one else 's . even when he criticise some more fortunate writer , he 
 be good - humour in his very satire : he have no bile , no envy . and as 
 for freedom from malignant personality , he might have be a model to 
 all critic . I must except politic , however , for in these he could 
 be rabid and savage . he have a passion for independence , which , though 
 push to excess , be not without grandeur . no lick - platter , no 
 parasite , no toad - eater , no literary beggar , no hunter after patronage 
 and subscription ; even in his dealing with Audley Egerton , he insist 
 on name the price for his labour . he take a price , because , as the 
 paper require by Audley demand much reading and detail , which 
 be not at all to his taste , he consider himself entitle fairly 
 to something more than the editor of the journal wherein the paper 
 appear be in the habit of giving . but he assess this extra price 
 himself , and as he would have do to a bookseller . and when in debt and 
 in prison , though he know a line to Egerton would have extricate he , 
 he never write that line . he would depend alone on his pen , - dip it 
 hastily in the ink , and scrawl himself free . the most debase point 
 about he be certainly the incorrigible vice of drinking , and with it 
 the usual concomitant of that vice , - the love of low company . to be king 
 of the Bohemians , to dazzle by his wild humour , and sometimes to exalt 
 by his fanciful eloquence , the rude , gross nature that gather round 
 he , - this be a royalty that repay he for all sacrifice of solid 
 dignity ; a foolscap crown that he would not have change for an 
 emperor 's diadem . indeed , to appreciate rightly the talent of John 
 Burley , it be necessary to hear he talk on such occasion . as a 
 writer , after all , he be now only capable of unequal desultory effort ; 
 but as a talker , in his own wild way , he be original and matchless . and 
 the gift of talk be one of the most dangerous gift a man can possess 
 for his own sake , - the applause be so immediate , and gain with so 
 little labour . low and low and low have sink John Burley , not only 
 in the opinion of all who know his name , but in the habitual exercise of 
 his talent . and this seem wilfully - from choice . he would write for 
 some unstamped journal of the populace , out of the pale of the law , for 
 penny , when he could have get pound from journal of high repute . he 
 be very fond of scribble off penny ballad , and then stand in the 
 street to hear they sung . he actually once make himself the poet of an 
 advertising tailor , and enjoy it excessively . but that do not last 
 long , for John Burley be a Pittite , - not a Tory , he use to say , but 
 a Pittite . and if you have hear he talk of Pitt , you would never have 
 know what to make of that great statesman . he treat he as the german 
 commentator do Shakspeare , and invest he with all imaginary meaning 
 and object , that would have turn the grand practical man into a 
 sibyl . well , he be a Pittite ; the tailor a fanatic for Thelwall and 
 Cobbett . Mr. Burley write a poem wherein Britannia appear to the 
 tailor , compliment he highly on the art he exhibit in adorn the 
 person of her son ; and bestow upon he a gigantic mantle , say that 
 he , and he alone , might be enable to fit it to the shoulder of live 
 man . the rest of the poem be occupy in Mr. Snip 's unavaile attempt 
 to adjust this mantle to the eminent politician of the day , when , 
 just as he have sink down in despair , Britannia reappear to he , and 
 console he with the information that he have do all mortal man could 
 do , and that she have only desire to convince pigmy that no human art 
 could adjust to their proportion the mantle of William Pitt . sic itur 
 ad astra , - she go back to the star , mantle and all ! Mr. Snip be 
 exceedingly indignant at this allegorical effusion , and with wrathful 
 shear cut the tie between himself and his poet . 

 thus , then , the reader have , we trust , a pretty good idea of John 
 Burley , - a speciman of his genus not very common in any age , and now 
 happily almost extinct , since author of all degree share in the 
 general improvement in order , economy , and sober decorum , which have 
 obtain in the national manner . Mr. Prickett , though enter 
 into less historical detail than we have do , convey to Leonard a 
 tolerably accurate notion of the man , represent he as a person of 
 great power and learning , who have thoroughly throw himself away . 

 Leonard do not , however , see how much Mr. Burley himself be to be 
 blame for his waste of life ; he could not conceive a man of genius 
 voluntarily seat himself at the low step in the social ladder . he 
 rather suppose he have be thrust down there by Necessity . 

 and when Mr. Prickett , concluding , say , " well , I should think Burley 
 would cure you of the desire to be an author even more than Chatterton , " 
 the young man answer gloomily , " perhaps , " and turn to the 
 book - shelf . 

 with Mr. Prickett 's consent , Leonard be release early than usual 
 from his task , and a little before sunset he take his way to Highgate . 
 he be fortunately direct to take the new road by the Regent 's Park , 
 and so on through a very green and smile country . the walk , the 
 freshness of the air , the song of the bird , and , above all , when he 
 have get half - way , the solitude of the road , serve to rouse he from his 
 stern and sombre meditation . and when he come into the lane overhung 
 with chestnut - tree , and suddenly catch sight of Helen 's watchful and 
 then brighten face , as she stand by the wicket , and under the shadow 
 of cool , murmurous bough , the blood rush gayly through his vein , and 
 his heart beat loud and gratefully . 




 CHAPTER XXIV . 

 she draw he into the garden with such true childlike joy . now behold 
 they seat in the arbour , - a perfect bower of sweet and blossom ; 
 the wilderness of roof - top and spire stretch below , broad and far ; 
 London see dim and silent , as in a dream . 

 she take his hat from his brow gently , and look he in the face with 
 tearful penetrate eye . 

 she do not say , " you be change . " she say , " why , why do I leave 
 you ? " and then turn away . 

 " never mind I , Helen . I be man , and rudely bear ; speak of yourself . 
 this lady be kind to you , then ? " 

 " do she not let I see you ? oh , very kind , - and look here . " 

 Helen point to fruit and cake set out on the table . " a feast , 
 brother . " 

 and she begin to press her hospitality with pretty win way , more 
 playful than be usual to she , and talk very fast , and with force ,

 by degree she steal he from his gloom and reserve ; and though he could

 have suffer much . he would not have own that to another living being .

 that the bad be over , he seek to amuse she by speak of his new 
 acquaintance with the perch - fisher . but when he speak of this man with

 " oh , brother , do not go there again , - do not see more of this bad man . " 

 " bad ! - no ! hopeless and unhappy , he have stoop to stimulant and 
 oblivion - but you can not understand these thing , my pretty preacher . " 

 " yes , I do , Leonard . what be the difference between be good and bad ? 
 the good do not yield to temptation , and the bad do . " 

 the definition be so simple and so wise that Leonard be more strike 
 with it than he might have be by the most elaborate sermon by Parson 
 Dale . 

 " I have often murmur to myself since I lose you , ' Helen be my good 
 angel ; ' - say on . for my heart be dark to myself , and while you speak 
 light seem to dawn on it . " 

 this praise so confused Helen that she be long before she could obey 
 the command annex to it . but , by little and little , word come to 
 both more frankly . and then he tell she the sad tale of Chatterton , and 
 wait , anxious to hear her comment . 

 " well , " he say , see that she remain silent , " how can I hope , when 
 this mighty genius labour and despair ? what do he want , save birth 
 and fortune and friend and human justice ? " 

 " do he pray to God ? " ask Helen , dry her tear . again Leonard be 
 startle . in read the life of Chatterton he have not much note 
 the scepticism , assume or real , of the ill - fat aspirer to earthly 
 immortality . at Helen 's question , that scepticism strike he forcibly . 
 " why do you ask that , Helen ? " 

 " because , when we pray often , we grow so very , very patient , " answer 
 the child . " perhaps , have he be patient a few month more , all would 
 have be win by he , as it will be by you , brother , for you pray , and 
 you will be patient . " 

 Leonard bow his head in deep thought , and this time the thought be 
 not gloomy . then out from that awful life there glow another passage , 
 which before he have not heed duly , but regard rather as one of the 
 dark mystery in the fate of Chatterton . 

 at the very time the despairing poet have lock himself up in his 
 garret , to dismiss his soul from its earthly ordeal , his genius have just 
 find its way into the light of renown . good and learn and powerful 
 man be prepare to serve and save he . another year - nay , perchance 
 another month - and he might have stand acknowledge sublime in the 
 foremost rank of his age . 

 " oh , Helen ! " cry Leonard , raise his brow , from which the cloud have 
 pass , " why , indeed , do you leave I ? " 

 Helen start in her turn as he repeat this regret , and in her turn 
 grow thoughtful . at length she ask he if he have write for the box 
 which have belong to her father and be leave at the inn . 

 and Leonard , though a little chafed at what he think a childish 
 interruption to theme of graver interest , own , with self - reproach , 
 that he have forget to do so . should he not write now to order the box 
 to be send to she at Miss Starke 's ? 

 " no ; let it be send to you . take care of it . I should like to know that 
 something of mine be with you ; and perhaps I may not stay here long . " 

 " not stay here ? that you must , my dear Helen , - at least as long as 
 Miss Starke will keep you , and be kind . by and by "   add Leonard , with 
 something of his former sanguine tone   " I may yet make my way , and we 
 shall have our cottage to ourselves . but - oh , Helen ! - I forget - you 
 wound I ; you leave your money with I . I only find it in my drawer 
 the other day . Fie ! I have bring it back . " 

 " it be not mine , - it be yours . we be to share together , - you pay 
 all ; and how can I want it here , too ? " but Leonard be obstinate ; and 
 as Helen mournfully receive back all that of fortune her father have 
 bequeath to she , a tall female figure stand at the entrance of the 
 arbour , and say , in a voice that scatter all sentiment to the wind , 
 " young man , it be time to go . " 




 CHAPTER XXV . 

 " already ? " say Helen , with falter accent , as she creep to Miss 
 Starke 's side while Leonard rise and bow . " I be very grateful to you , 
 madam , " say he , with the grace that come from all refinement of idea , 
 " for allow I to see Miss Helen . do not let I abuse your kindness . " 

 Miss Starke seem strike with his look and manner , and make a stiff 
 half courtesy . 

 a form more rigid than Miss Starke 's it be hard to conceive . she be 
 like the Grim White Woman in the nursery ballad . yet , apparently , there 
 be a good - nature in allow the stranger to enter her trim garden , 
 and provide for he and her little charge those fruit and cake which 
 belie her aspect . " may I go with he to the gate ? " whisper Helen , as 
 Leonard have already pass up the path . 

 " you may , child ; but do not loiter . and then come back , and lock up the 
 cake and cherry , or Patty will get at they . " 

 Helen run after Leonard . 

 " write to I , brother , - write to I ; and do not , do not be friend with 
 this man , who take you to that wicked , wicked place . " 

 " oh , Helen , I go from you strong enough to brave bad danger than 
 that , " say Leonard , almost gayly . 

 they kiss each other at the little wicket gate , and part . 

 Leonard walk home under the summer moonlight , and on enter his 
 chamber look first at his rose - tree . the leaf of yesterday 's flower 
 lie strew around it ; but the tree have put forth new bud . 

 " nature ever restore , " say the young man . he pause a moment , and 
 add , " be it that nature be very patient ? " his sleep that night be not 
 break by the fearful dream he have lately know . he rise refresh , 
 and go his way to his day 's work , - not steal along the less 
 crowded path , but with a firm step , through the throng of man . be bold , 
 adventurer , - thou hast more to suffer ! wilt thou sink ? I look into thy 
 heart , and I can not answer . 




 BOOK SEVENTH . 




 INITIAL CHAPTER . 

 MR . CAXTON UPON COURAGE and PATIENCE . 

 " what be courage ? " say my uncle Roland , rouse himself from a revery 
 into which he have fall , after the Sixth Book in this history have be 
 read to our family circle . 

 " what be courage ? " he repeat more earnestly . " be it insensibility to 
 fear ? that may be the mere accident of constitution ; and if so , there be 
 no more merit in be courageous than in be this table . " 

 " I be very glad to hear you speak thus , " observe Mr. Caxton , " for I 
 should not like to consider myself a coward ; yet I be very sensible to 
 fear in all danger , bodily and moral . " 

 " La , Austin , how can you say so ? " cry my mother , fire up ; " be it 
 not only last week that you face the great bull that be rush after 
 Blanche and the child ? " 

 Blanche at that recollection steal to my father 's chair , and , hang 
 over his shoulder , kiss his forehead . 

 MR . CAXTON   sublimely unmove by these flattery . - " I do not   deny that 
 I face the bull , but I assert that I be horribly frightened . " 

 ROLAND . - " the sense of honour which conquer fear be the true courage 
 of chivalry : you could not run away when other be look on , - no 
 gentleman could . " 

 MR . CAXTON . - " Fiddledee ! it be not on my gentility that I stand , 
 Captain . I should have run fast enough , if it have do any good . I stand 
 upon my understanding . as the bull could run fast than I could , the 
 only chance of escape be to make the brute as frightened as myself . " 

 BLANCHE . - " ah , you do not think of that ; your only thought be to save 
 I and the child . " 

 MR . CAXTON . - " possibly , my dear , very possibly , I might have be afraid 
 for you too ; but I be very much afraid for myself . however , luckily I 
 have the umbrella , and I spring it up and spread it forth in the animal 's 
 stupid eye , hurl at he simultaneously the big line I could 
 think of in the First Chorus of the ' Seven against Thebes . ' I begin with 
 ELEDEMNAS PEDIOPLOKTUPOS ; and when I come to the grand howl of   a line 
 in Greek , the beast stand appal as at the roar of a lion . I shall 
 never forget his amazed snort at the Greek . then he kick up his hind 
 leg , and go bolt through the gap in the hedge . thus , arm with 
 AEschylus and the umbrella , I remain master of the field ; but " 
  continue Mr. Caxton ingenuously   " I should not like to go through that 
 half - minute again . " 

 " no man would , " say the captain , kindly . " I should be very sorry to 
 face a bull myself , even with a big umbrella than your , and even 
 though I have AEschylus , and Homer to boot , at my finger ' end . " 

 MR . CAXTON . - " you would not have minded if it have be a Frenchman with 
 a sword in his hand ? " 

 CAPTAIN . - " of course not . rather like it than otherwise , " he add 
 grimly . 

 MR . CAXTON . - " yet many a spanish matador , who do not   care a button 
 for a bull , would take to his heel at the first lunge en carte from a 
 Frenchman . therefore , in fact , if courage be a matter of constitution , 
 it be also a matter of custom . we face calmly the danger we be 
 habituate to , and recoil from those of which we have no familiar 
 experience . I doubt if Marshal Turenue himself would have be quite 
 at his ease on the tight - rope ; and a rope - dancer , who seem disposed to 
 scale the heaven with titanic temerity , might possibly object to charge 
 on a cannon . " 

 CAPTAIN ROLAND . - " still , either this be not the courage I mean , or it be 
 another kind of it . I mean by courage that which be the especial force 
 and dignity of the human character , without which there be no reliance 
 on principle , no constancy in virtue , - a something , " continue my uncle , 
 gallantly , and with a half bow towards my mother , " which your sex share 
 with our own . when the lover , for instance , clasp the hand of his 
 betrothed , and say , ' wilt thou be true to I , in spite of absence and 
 time , in spite of hazard and fortune , though my foe malign I , though 
 thy friend may dissuade thee , and our lot in life may be rough and 
 rude ? ' and when the betroth answer , ' I will be true , ' do not the 
 lover trust to her courage as well as her love ? " 

 " admirably put , Roland , " say my father . " but a propos of what do you 
 puzzle we with these query on courage ? " 

 CAPTAIN ROLAND   with a slight blush . - " I be lead to the inquiry   though 
 perhaps it may be frivolous to take so much thought of what , no doubt , 
 cost Pisistratus so little   by the last chapter in my nephew 's story . 
 I see this poor boy Leonard , alone with his fall hope   though very 
 irrational they be   and his sense of shame . and I read his heart , I 
 dare say , well than Pisistratus do , for I could feel like that 
 boy if I have be in the same position ; and conjecture what he and 
 thousand like he must go through , I ask myself , ' what can save he 
 and they ? ' I answer , as a soldier would answer , ' courage . ' very well . 
 but pray ; Austin , what be courage ? " 

 MR . CAXTON   prudently back out of a reply . - " Papae ! ' brother , since 
 you have just compliment the lady on that quality , you have well 
 address your question to they . " 

 Blanche here lean both hand on my father 's chair , and say , look 
 down at first bashfully , but afterwards warm with the subject , " do 
 you not think , sir , that little Helen have already suggest , if not 
 what be courage , what at least be the real essence of all courage that 
 endure and conquer , that ennoble and hallow and redeem ? be it not 
 PATIENCE , Father ? and that be why we woman have a courage of our own . 
 Patience do not affect to be superior to fear , but at least it never 
 admit despair . " 

 PISISTRATUS . - " kiss I , my Blanche , for you have come near to the truth 
 which perplex the soldier and puzzle the sage . " 

 MR . CAXTON   tartly . - " if you mean I by the sage , I be not puzzle at 
 all . Heaven know you do right to inculcate patience , - it be a virtue 
 very much require - in your reader . nevertheless , " add my father , 
 soften with the enjoyment of his joke , - " nevertheless Blanche and 
 Helen be quite right . patience be the courage of the conqueror ; it be 
 the virtue , par excellence , of Man against Destiny , - of the one against 
 the World , and of the Soul against Matter . therefore this be the courage 
 of the Gospel ; and its importance in a social view - its importance to 
 race and institution - can not be too earnestly inculcate . what be it 
 that distinguish the Anglo - Saxon from all other branch of the human 
 family , - people desert with his child and consign to they the 
 heritage of rise world ? what but his faculty to brave , to suffer , to 
 endure , - the patience that resist firmly and innovate slowly ? compare 
 he with the Frenchman . the Frenchman have plenty of valour , - that there 
 be no deny ; but as for fortitude , he have not enough to cover the 
 point of a pin . he be ready to rush out of the world if he be bite by 
 a flea . " 

 CAPTAIN ROLAND . - " there be a case in the paper the other day , Austin , 
 of a Frenchman who actually do destroy himself because he be so tease 
 by the little creature you speak of . he leave a paper on his table , 
 say that ' life be not worth have at the price of such torment . ' " 

 MR . CAXTON   solemnly . - " Sir , their whole political history , since the 
 great meeting of the Tiers Etat , have be the history of man who would 
 rather go to the devil than be bite by a flea . it be the record of 
 human impatience that seek to force time , and expect to grow forest 
 from the spawn of a mushroom . wherefore , run through all extreme of 
 constitutional experiment , when they be near to democracy they 
 be next door to a despot ; and all they have really do be to destroy 
 whatever constitute the foundation of every tolerable government . 
 a constitutional monarchy can not exist without aristocracy , nor a 
 healthful republic endure with corruption of manner . the cry of 
 equality be incompatible with civilization , which , of necessity , 
 contrast poverty with wealth ; and , in short , whether it be an emperor 
 or a mob I that be to rule , Force be the sole hope of order , and the 
 government be but an army . " 

   publish more than a year before the date of the french empire 
  under Louis Napoleon . 

 " impress , o Pisistratus ! impress the value of patience as regard man 
 and man . you touch there on the kernel of the social system , - the secret 
 that fortify the individual and discipline the million . I care not , 
 for my part , if you be tedious so long as you be earnest . be minute 
 and detail . let the real Human Life , in its war with circumstance , 
 stand out . never mind if one can read you but slowly , - well chance 
 of be less quickly forget . patience , patience ! by the soul of 
 Epictetus , your reader shall set you an example . " 




 CHAPTER II . 

 Leonard have write twice to Mrs. Fairfield , twice to Riccabocca , and 
 once to Mr. Dale ; and the poor proud boy could not bear to betray 
 his humiliation . he write as with cheerful spirit , - as if perfectly 
 satisfied with his prospect . he say that he be well employ , in the 
 midst of book , and that he have find kind friend . then he turn from 
 himself to write about those whom he address , and the affair and 
 interest of the quiet world wherein they live . he do not give his 
 own address , nor that of Mr. Prickett . he date his letter from a small 
 coffee - house near the bookseller 's , to which he occasionally go for 
 his simple meal . he have a motive in this . he do not desire to be 
 find out . Mr. Dale reply for himself and for Mrs. Fairfield , to the 
 epistle address to these two . Riccabocca write also . 

 nothing could be more kind than the reply of both . they come to 
 Leonard in a very dark period in his life , and they strengthen he in 
 the noiseless battle with despair . 

 if there be a good in the world that we do without know it , without 
 conjecture the effect it may have upon a human soul ; it be when we 
 show kindness to the young in the first barren footpath up the mountain 
 of life . 

 Leonard 's face resume its serenity in his intercourse with his 
 employer ; but he do not recover his boyish ingenuous frankness . the 
 under - current flow again pure from the turbid soil and the splinter 
 fragment uptorn from the deep ; but they be still too strong and too 
 rapid to allow transparency to the surface . and now he stand in the 
 sublime world of book , still and earnest as a seer who invoke the 
 dead ; and thus , face to face with knowledge , hourly he discover how 
 little he know . Mr. Prickett lend he such work as he select and 
 ask to take home with he . he spend whole night in reading , and no 
 long desultorily . he read no more poetry , no more life of poet . he 
 read what poet must read if they desire to be great - Sapere principium 
 et fon , - strict reasoning on the human mind ; the relation between 
 motive and conduct , thought and action ; the grave and solemn truth of 
 the past world ; antiquity , history , philosophy . he be take out of 
 himself ; he be carry along the ocean of the universe . in that ocean , 
 o seeker , study the law of the tide ; and see Chance nowhere , 
 thought preside over all , Fate , that dread phantom , shall vanish from 
 creation , and Providence alone be visible in heaven and on earth ! 




 CHAPTER III . 

 there be to be a considerable book - sale at a country house one day 's 
 journey from London . Mr. Prickett mean to have attend it on his own 
 behalf , and that of several gentleman who have give he commission for 
 purchase ; but on the morning fix for his departure , he be seize with 
 a severe return of his old foe the rheumatism . he request Leonard to 
 attend instead of himself . Leonard go , and be absent for the three 
 day during which the sale last . he return late in the evening , and 
 go at once to Mr. Prickett 's house . the shop be close ; he knock 
 at the private entrance ; a strange person open the door to he , and in 
 reply to his question if Mr. Prickett be at home , say , with a long and 
 funereal face , " young man , Mr. Prickett senior be go to his long home , 
 but Mr. Richard Prickett will see you . " 

 at this moment a very grave - looking man , with lank hair , look forth 
 from the side - door communicating between the shop and the passage , and 
 then step forward . " come in , sir ; you be my late uncle 's assistant , 
 Mr. Fairfield , I suppose ? " 

 " your late uncle ! Heavens , sir , do I understand aright , can Mr. Prickett 
 be dead since I leave London ? " 

 " die , sir , suddenly , last night . it be an affection of the heart . the 
 doctor think the rheumatism attack that organ . he have small time to 
 provide for his departure , and his account - book seem in sad disorder : I 
 be his nephew and executor . " 

 Leonard have now - follow the nephew into the shop . there still burn 
 the gas - lamp . the place seem more dingy and cavernous than before . 
 death always make its presence feel in the house it visit . 

 Leonard be greatly affect , - and yet more , perhaps , by the utter want 
 of feeling which the nephew exhibit . in fact the deceased have not 
 be on friendly term with this person , his near relative and 
 heir - at - law , who be also a bookseller . 

 " you be engage but by the week , I find , young man , on reference to 
 my late uncle 's paper . he give you l1 a week , - a monstrous sum ! I shall 
 not require your service any far . I shall move these book to my 
 own house . you will be good enough to send I a list of those you buy 
 at the sale , and your account of travel expense , etc . what may be 
 due to you shall be send to your address . good - evening . " 

 Leonard go home , shock and sadden at the sudden death of his kind 
 employer . he do not think much of himself that night ; but when he rise 
 the next day , he suddenly feel that the world of London lie before he , 
 without a friend , without a calling , without an occupation for bread . 

 this time it be no fancied sorrow , no poetic dream disappoint . 
 before he , gaunt and palpable , stand Famine . Escape ! - yes . back to the 
 village : his mother 's cottage ; the exile 's garden ; the radish and the 
 fount . why could he not escape ? ask why civilization can not escape its 
 ill , and fly back to the wild and the wigwam . 

 Leonard could not have return to the cottage , even if the Famine that 
 face have already seize he with her skeleton hand . London release not 
 so readily her fat step - son . 




 CHAPTER IV . 

 one day three person be stand before an old bookstall in a 
 passage lead from Oxford Street into Tottenham Court Road . two be 
 gentleman ; the third , of the class and appearance of those who more 
 habitually halt at old bookstall . 

 " look , " say one of the gentleman to the other , " I have discover here 
 what I have search for in vain the last ten year , - the Horace of 
 1580 , the Horace of the Forty Commentators , a perfect treasury of 
 learning , and mark only fourteen shilling ! " 

 " Hush , Norreys , " say the other , " and observe what be yet more worth 
 your study ; " and he point to the third bystander , whose face , sharp 
 and attenuate , be bent with an absorbed , and , as it be , with a 
 hunger attention over an old worm - eat volume . 

 " what be the book , my lord ? " whisper Mr. Norreys . his companion 
 smile , and reply by another question , " what be the man who read the 
 book ? " 

 Mr. Norreys move a few pace , and look over the student 's shoulder . 
 " Preston 's translation of Boethius 's ' the Consolations of Philosophy , ' " 
 he say , come back to his friend . 

 " he look as if he want all the consolation philosophy can give he , 
 poor boy . " 

 at this moment a fourth passenger pause at the bookstall , and , 
 recognize the pale student , place his hand on his shoulder , and say , 
 " aha , young sir , we meet again . so poor Prickett be dead . but you be 
 still haunt by association . book , book , - magnet to which all iron 
 mind move insensibly . what be this ? Boethius ! ah , a book write in 
 prison , but a little time before the advent of the only philosopher who 
 solve to the simple understanding every mystery of life - " 

 " and that philosopher ? " 

 " be death ! " say Mr. Burley . " how can you be dull enough to ask ? Poor 
 Boethius , rich , nobly bear , a consul , his son consul , the world one 
 smile to the last Philosopher of Rome . then suddenly , against this type 
 of the old world 's depart WISDOM stand frown the new world 's 
 grim genius , FORCE , - Theodoric the Ostrogoth condemn Boethius the 
 schoolman ; and Boethius in his pavian dungeon hold a dialogue with 
 the shade of Athenian Philosophy . it be the fine picture upon which 
 linger the glimmering of the western golden day , before night rush 
 over time . " 

 " and , " say Mr. Norreys , abruptly , " Boethius come back to we with the 
 faint gleam of return light , translate by Alfred the great ; and , 
 again , as the sun of knowledge burst forth in all its splendour by 
 Queen Elizabeth . Boethius influence we as we stand in this passage ; 
 and that be the good of all the Consolations of Philosophy , - eh , Mr. 
 Burley ? " 

 Mr. Burley turn and bow . 

 the two man look at each other ; you could not see a great 
 contrast , - Mr. Burley , his gay green dress already shabby and soil , 
 with a rent in the skirt and his face speak of habitual night - cup ; 
 Mr. Norreys , neat and somewhat precise in dress , with firm , lean figure , 
 and quiet , collected , vigorous energy in his eye and aspect . 

 " if , " reply Mr. Burley , " a poor devil like I may argue with a 
 gentleman who may command his own price with the bookseller , I should 
 say it be no consolation at all , Mr. Norreys . and I should like to see 
 any man of sense accept the condition of Boethius in his prison , with 
 some strangler or headsman wait behind the door , upon the promise 
 proviso that he should be translate , century afterwards , by king 
 and queen , and help indirectly to influence the mind of northern 
 barbarian , babble about he in an alley , jostle by passer - by 
 who never hear the name of Boethius , and who do not   care a fig for 
 philosophy . your servant , sir , young man , come and talk . " 

 Burley hook his arm within Leonard 's , and lead the boy passively away . 

 " that be a clever man , " say Harley L'Estrange . " but I be sorry to see 
 yon young student , with his bright earnest eye , and his lip that have 
 the quiver of passion and enthusiasm , lean on the arm of a guide 
 who seem disenchanted of all that give purpose to learn , and link 
 philosophy with use to the world . who and what be this clever man whom 
 you call Burley ? " 

 " a man who might have be famous , if he have condescend to be 
 respectable ! the boy listen to we both so attentively interested 
 I too , - I should like to have the making of he . but I must buy this 
 Horace . " 

 the shopman , lurk within his hole like a spider for fly , be now 
 call out . and when Mr. Norreys have buy the Horace , and give an 
 address where to send it , Harley ask the shopman if he know the young 
 man who have be read Boethius . 

 " only by sight . he have come here every day the last week , and spend 
 hour at the stall . when once he fasten on a book , he read it 
 through . " 

 " and never buy ? " say Mr. Norreys . 

 " Sir , " say the shopman , with a good - natured smile , " they who buy seldom 
 read . the poor boy pay I twopence a day to read as long as he please . 
 I would not take it , but he be proud . " 

 " I have know man amass great learning in that way , " say Mr. Norreys . 
 " yes , I should like to have that boy in my hand . and now , my lord , I be 
 at your service , and we will go to the studio of your artist . " 

 the two gentleman walk on towards one of the street out of Fitzroy 
 Square . 

 in a few minute more Harley L'Estrange be in his element , seat 
 carelessly on a deal table smoke his cigar , and discuss art with 
 the gusto of a man who honestly love , and the taste of a man who 
 thoroughly understand it . the young artist , in his dress robe , add 
 slow touch upon touch , pause often to listen the well . and Henry 
 Norrey s , enjoy the brief respite from a life of great labour , be 
 gladly remind of idle hour under rosy sky ; for these three man 
 have form their friendship in Italy , where the band of friendship be 
 weave by the hand of the Graces . 




 CHAPTER V. 

 Leonard and Mr. Burley walk on into the suburb round the north road 
 from London , and Mr. Burley offer to find literary employment for 
 Leonard , - an offer eagerly accept . 

 then they go into a public - house by the wayside . Burley demand 
 a private room , call for pen , ink , and paper ; and place these 
 implement before Leonard , say , " write what you please , in prose , five 
 sheet of letter - paper , twenty - two line to a page , - neither more nor 
 less . " 

 " I can not write so . " 

 " tut , ' t be for bread . " 

 the boy 's face crimson . 

 " I must forget that , " say he . 

 " there be an arbour in the garden , under a weeping - ash , " return 
 Burley . " go there , and fancy yourself in Arcadia . " 

 Leonard be too pleased to obey . he find out the little arbour at one 
 end of a deserted bowling - green . all be still , - the hedgerow shut 
 out the sight of the inn . the sun lie warm on the grass , and glint 
 pleasantly through the leaf of the ash . and Leonard there write the 
 first essay from his hand as author by profession . what be it that he 
 write ? his dreamy impression of London , an anathema on its street and 
 its heart of stone , murmur against poverty , dark elegy on fate ? 

 oh , no ! little know thou true genius , if thou ask such question , 
 or think that there under the weeping - ash the task - work for bread be 
 remember ; or that the sunbeam glint but over the practical world , 
 which , vulgar and sordid , lie around . Leonard write a fairy tale , - one 
 of the lovely you can conceive , with a delicate touch of playful 
 humour , in a style all flower over with happy fancy . he smile as 
 he write the last word , - he be happy . in rather more than an hour Mr. 
 Burley come to he , and find he with that smile on his lip . 

 Mr. Burley have a glass of brandy - and - water in his hand ; it be his 
 third . he too smile , he too look happy . he read the paper aloud , 
 and well . he be very complimentary . " you will do ! " say he , clapping 
 Leonard on the back . " perhaps some day you will catch my one - eye 
 perch . " then he fold up the manuscript , scribble off a note , put the 
 whole in one envelope , and they return to London . 

 Mr. Burley disappear within a dingy office near Fleet Street , on which 
 be inscribe , " Office of the ' beehive , ' " and soon come forth with a 
 golden sovereign in his hand , Leonard 's first - fruit . Leonard think 
 Peru lie before he . he accompany Mr. Burley to that gentleman 's 
 lodge in Maida Hill . the walk have be very long ; Leonard be not 
 fatigue . he listen with a lively attention than before to Burley 's 
 talk . and when they reach the apartment of the latter , and Mr. Burley 
 send to the cookshop , and their joint supper be take out of the golden 
 sovereign , Leonard feel proud , and for the first time for week he 
 laugh the heart 's laugh . the two writer grow more and more intimate 
 and cordial . and there be a vast deal in Burley by which any young man 
 might be make the wiser . there be no apparent evidence of poverty in 
 the apartment , - clean , new , well - furnish ; but all thing in the most 
 horrible litter , - all speak of the huge literary sloven . 

 for several day Leonard almost live in those room . he write 
 continuously , save when Burley 's conversation fascinate he into 
 idleness . Nay , it be not idleness , - his knowledge grow large as he 
 listen ; but the cynicism of the talker begin slowly to work its way . 
 that cynicism in which there be no faith , no hope , no vivifying breath 
 from Glory , from Religion , - the cynicism of the epicurean , more degraded 
 in his sty than ever be Diogenes in his tub ; and yet present with 
 such ease and such eloquence , with such art and such mirth , so adorn 
 with illustration and anecdote , so unconscious of debasement ! 

 strange and dread philosophy , that make it a maxim to squander the gift 
 of mind on the mere care for matter , and fit the soul to live but as 
 from day to day , with its scornful cry , " a fig for immortality and 
 laurel ! " an author for bread ! oh , miserable calling ! be there 
 something grand and holy , after all , even in Chatterton 's despair ? 




 CHAPTER VI . 

 the villanous " beehive " ! Bread be work out of it , certainly ; but 
 fame , but hope for the future , - certainly not . Milton 's Paradise Lost 
 would have perish without a sound have it appear in the " Beehive . " 

 fine thing be there in a fragmentary crude state , compose by Burley 
 himself . at the end of a week they be dead and forget , - never 
 read by one man of education and taste ; take simultaneously and 
 indifferently with shallow politic and wretched essay , yet sell , 
 perhaps , twenty or thirty thousand copy , - an immense sale ; and nothing 
 get out of they but bread and brandy ! 

 " what more would you have ? " cry John Burley . " do not stern old Sam 
 Johnson say he could never write but from want ? " 

 " he might say it , " answer Leonard ; " but he never mean posterity to 
 believe he . and he would have die of want , I suspect , rather than have 
 write ' Rasselas ' for the ' beehive ' ! want be a grand thing , " continue 
 the boy , thoughtfully , - " a parent of grand thing . necessity be strong , 
 and should give we its own strength ; but want should shatter asunder , 
 with its very writhing , the wall of our prison - house , and not sit 
 content with the allowance the jail give we in exchange for our 
 work . " 

 " there be no prison - house to a man who call upon Bacchus ; stay , I will 
 translate to you Schiller 's Dithyramb . ' then see I Bacchus ; then up come 
 Cupid and Phoebus , and all the Celestials be fill my dwelling . ' " 

 break into impromptu careless rhyme , Burley throw off a rude but 
 spirited translation of that divine lyric . " o materialist ! " cry the 
 boy , with his bright eye suffuse . " schiller call on the god to 
 take he to their heaven with they ; and you would debase the god to a 
 ginpalace . " 

 " Ho , ho ! " cry Burley , with his giant laugh . " drink , and you will 
 understand the Dithyramb . " 




 CHAPTER VII . 

 suddenly one morning , as Leonard sit with Burley , a fashionable 
 cabriolet , with a very handsome horse , stop at the door . a loud 
 knock , a quick step on the stair , and Randal Leslie enter . Leonard

 and in single combat , " answer Randal , smile ; and he tell the story

 " ah , " say Randal , with the secret contempt which man elaborately 
 cultivate be apt to feel for those who seek to educate 
 themselves , - " ah , you make literature your calling , sir ? at what school

 public school . " 

 " I be at school now for the first time , " answer Leonard , dryly .

 Randal slightly shrug his shoulder , and without waste another

 " I can not   quite agree with you , " say Randal , take his leave ; " but you 
 must allow I to call again , - will the same hour tomorrow suit you ? " 

 " yes , " say Burley . 

 away go the young man in his cabriolet . Leonard watch he from the

 for five day , consecutively , do randal call and discuss the question 
 in all its bearing ; and Burley , after the second day , get interested

 spend an hour or two in the Library of the British Museum .

 say on his side of the question . 

 Leonard , during these colloquy , have sit apart seemingly absorb in

 purposeless man , and say aloud , " true ; knowledge be not power . " 

 " certainly not , " say Burley , dryly , - " the weak thing in the world . "

 lip , he drive from the door . 

 not many day after this last interview there appear a short pamphlet ;

 " can you doubt the author ? " cry Leonard , in deep disgust and ingenuous

 knowledge - "

 pain . " well , this be very mean ; I shall tell he so when he come . "

 send Mr. Burley a copy of the pamphlet with a polite note , say , with

 Burley 's hint and remark . " 

 and now it be in all the paper that the pamphlet which have make so

 and high hope be express of the future career of Mr. Randal Leslie . 

 Burley still attempt to laugh , and still his pain be visible . leonard

 of debase content . 

 Randal have rise into grave repute upon the strength of Burley 's

 knowledge quality all his own , - a style simple , strong , and logical ;

 have Burley write the pamphlet , it would have show more genius , it

 randal Leslie might be mean in his plagiarism , but he turn the useless

 rest where Leonard 's rest , - with the poor , riotous , lawless , big ,

 CHAPTER VIII .

 CHAPTER IX . 

 nothing , perhaps , could have sever Leonard from Burley but Helen 's

 CHAPTER X.
