 CHAPTER XI . 

 meanwhile , on leave Helen , Burley stride on ; and , as if by some well 
 instinct , for he be unconscious of his own step , he take his way

 with this cottage Burley be familiar ; it be inhabit by a good old

 that the country be a heaven , and convince before the third that it

 an old woman , of neat and tidy exterior , come forth to greet he .

 Burley shake his head . " no brandy , Mrs. Goodyer ; only fresh milk . I will

 the third day it rain incessantly . " have you no book , Mrs. Goodyer ? "

 " oh , yes , some that the dear lady leave behind she ; and perhaps you would

 the book be bring up , - poetry and essay - John know they by heart .

 " Nature , Nature ! " he exclaim , when he be out in the air and hurrying

 thus John Burley make his way into town , and pause at the first 
 public - house . out of that house he come with a jovial air , and on he 
 stride towards the heart of London . now he be in Leicester Square , and

 now through the maze of passage towards St. Martin 's he thread his

 " hail to thee , o freedom ! " mutter John Burley , " thy dwelling be in 
 city , and thy palace be the tavern . " 

 " in the king 's name , " quoth a gruff voice ; and John Burley feel the 
 horrid and familiar tap on the shoulder . 

 the two bailiff who dog have seize their prey . " at whose suit ? " 
 ask John Burley , falteringly . " Mr. Cox , the wine - merchant . " 

 " Cox ! a man to whom I give a check on my banker not three month ago ! " 

 " but it war not   cash . " 

 " what do that signify ? - the intention be the same . a good heart take 
 the will for the deed . Cox be a monster of ingratitude , and I withdraw

 " sarve he right . would your honour like a jarvey ? " 

 " I would rather spend the money on something else , " say John Burley . 
 " give I your arm , I be not proud . after all , thank Heaven , I shall not 
 sleep in the country . " 

 and John Burley make a night of it in the Fleet . 




 CHAPTER XII . 

 Miss Starke be one of those lady who pass their life in the dire 
 of all civil strife , - war with their servant . she look upon the 
 member of that class as the unrelenting and sleepless enemy of the 
 unfortunate householder condemn to employ they . she think they eat 
 and drink to their villanous utmost , in order to ruin their benefactor ; 
 that they live in one constant conspiracy with one another and the 
 tradesman , the object of which be to cheat and pilfer . Miss Starke be 
 a miserable woman . as she have no relation or friend who care enough 
 for she to share her solitary struggle against her domestic foe ; and 
 her income , though easy , be an annuity that die with herself , thereby 
 reduce various nephew , niece , or cousin to the strict bound of a 
 natural affection , - that do not exist ; and as she feel the want of some 
 friendly face amidst this world of distrust and hate , - so she have try 
 the resource of venal companion . but the venal companion have never 
 stay long , either they dislike Miss Starke , or Miss Starke dislike 
 they . therefore the poor woman have resolve upon bring up some 
 little girl , whose heart , as she say to herself , would be fresh and 
 uncorrupted , and from whom she might expect gratitude . she have be 
 content , on the whole , with Helen , and have mean to keep that child in 
 her house as long as she   Miss Starke   remain upon the earth , - perhaps 
 some thirty year long ; and then , have carefully seclude she from 
 marriage and other friendship , to leave she nothing but the regret of 
 have lose so kind a benefactress . conformably with this notion , and in 
 order to secure the affection of the child , Miss Starke have relax 
 the frigid austerity natural to her manner and mode of thought , and be 
 kind to Helen in an iron way . she have neither slap nor pinch she , 
 neither have she starve . she have allow she to see Leonard , accord 
 to the agreement make with Dr. Morgan , and have lay out tenpence 
 on cake , besides contribute fruit from her garden for the first 
 interview , - a hospitality she do not think it fit to renew on 
 subsequent occasion . in return for this , she conceive she have 
 purchase the right to Helen bodily and spiritually , and nothing could 
 exceed her indignation when she rise one morning and find the child have 
 go . as it never have occur to she to ask Leonard 's address , though 
 she suspect Helen have go to he , she be at a loss what to do , and 
 remain for twenty - four hour in a state of inane depression . but then 
 she begin to miss the child so much that her energy wake , and she 
 persuade herself that she be actuate by the pure benevolence in 
 try to reclaim this poor creature from the world into which Helen have 
 thus rashly plunge . 

 accordingly she put an advertisement into the " Times , " to the follow 
 effect , liberally imitate from one by which in former year she have 
 recover a favourite Blenheim : - 

  two GUINEAS ' reward . 

  STRAYED , from Ivy Cottage , Highgate , a little Girl , - answer to the 
  name of Helen ; with blue eye and brown hair ; white muslin frock , 
  and straw hat with blue ribbon . whoever will bring the same to Ivy 
  Cottage , shall receive the above Reward . 

  N. B. - Nothing more will be offer . 

 now it so happen that Mrs. Smedley have put an advertisement in the 
 " Times " on her own account , relative to a niece of hers who be come 
 from the country , and for whom she desire to find a situation . so , 
 contrary to her usual habit , she send for the newspaper , and close by 
 her own advertisement , she see Miss Starke 's . 

 it be impossible that she could mistake the description of Helen ; and 
 as this advertisement catch her eye the very day after the whole house 
 have be disturb and scandalize by Burley 's noisy visit , and on which 
 she have resolve to get rid of a lodger who receive such visitor , the 
 good - hearted woman be delighted to think that she could restore Helen 
 to some safe home . while thus think , Helen herself enter the 
 kitchen where Mrs. Smedley sit , and the landlady have the imprudence to 
 point out the advertisement , and talk , as she call it , ' seriously , " to 
 the little girl . 

 Helen in vain and with tear entreat she to take no step in reply to 
 the advertisement . Mrs. Smedley feel that it be an affair of duty , 
 and be obdurate , and shortly afterwards put on her bonnet and leave the 
 house . Helen conjecture that she be on her way to Miss Starke 's , and 
 her whole soul be bent on flight . Leonard have go to the office of 
 the " beehive " with his manuscript ; but she pack up all their joint 
 effect , and just as she have do so , he return . she communicate 
 the news of the advertisement , and say she should be so miserable if 
 compel to go back to Miss Starke 's , and implore he so pathetically 
 to save she from such sorrow , that he at once assent to her proposal 
 of flight . luckily , little be owe to the landlady , - that little be 
 leave with the maid - servant ; and , profit by Mrs. Smedley 's absence , 
 they escape without scene or conflict . their effect be take by 
 Leonard to a stand of hackney vehicle , and then leave at a coach - office 
 while they go in search of lodging . it be wise to choose an entirely 
 new and remote district ; and before night they be settle in an attic 
 in Lambeth . 




 CHAPTER XIII . 

 as the reader will expect , no trace of Burley could Leonard find : the 
 humourist have cease to communicate with the " Beehive . " but Leonard 
 grieve for Burley 's sake ; and , indeed , he miss the intercourse of the 
 large , wrong mind . but he settle down by degree to the simple , love 
 society of his child companion , and in that presence grow more tranquil . 
 the hour in the daytime that he do not pass at work , he spend as 
 before , pick up knowledge at book - stall ; and at dusk he and Helen 
 would stroll out , - sometimes strive to escape from the long suburb 
 into fresh rural air ; more often wander to and fro the bridge that 
 lead to glorious Westminster - London 's classic land - and watch the 
 vague lamp reflect on the river . this haunt suit the musing , 
 melancholy boy . he would stand long and with wistful silence by the 
 balustrade , seat Helen thereon , that she too might look along the 
 dark mournful water , which , dark though they be , still have their charm 
 of mysterious repose . 

 as the river flow between the world of roof , and the roar of human 
 passion on either side , so in those two heart flow Thought - and all 
 they know of London be its shadow . 




 CHAPTER XIV . 

 there appear in the " beehive " certain very truculent political 
 paper , - paper very like the tract in the tinker 's bag . Leonard do 
 not heed they much , but they make far more sensation in the public that 
 read the " beehive " than Leonard 's paper , full of rare promise though 
 the last be . they greatly increase the sale of the periodical in the 
 manufacturing town , and begin to awake the drowsy vigilance of the 
 Home Office . suddenly a descent be make upon the " beehive " and all 
 its paper and plant . the editor see himself threaten with a criminal 
 prosecution , and the certainty of two year ' imprisonment : he do 
 not like the prospect , and disappear . one evening , when Leonard , 
 unconscious of these mischance , arrive at the door of the office , he 
 find it close . an agitated mob be before it , and a voice that be 
 not new to his ear be harangue the bystander , with many imprecation 
 against " tyrant . " he look , and , to his amaze , recognize in the 
 orator Mr. Sprott the Tinker . 

 the police come in number to disperse the crowd , and Mr. Sprott 
 prudently vanish . Leonard learn , then , what have befall , and again 
 see himself without employment and the mean of bread . 

 slowly he walk back . " o knowledge , knowledge ! - powerless , indeed ! " he 
 murmur . 

 as he thus speak , a handbill in large capital meet his eye on a dead

 a crimp accost he . " you would make a fine soldier , my man . you have 
 stout limb of your own . " Leonard move on . 

 " it have come back then to this , - brute physical force after all ! O 
 Mind , despair ! o Peasant , be a machine again ! " he enter his attic 
 noiselessly , and gaze upon Helen as she sit at work , strain her eye

 be pale and hollow , and the hand look so thin ! his heart be deeply 
 touch , and at that moment he have not one memory of the baffle Poet , 
 one think that proclaim the Egotist . 

 he approach she gently , lay his hand on her shoulder , " Helen , put on 
 your shawl and bonnet , and walk out , - I have much to say . " 

 in a few moment she be ready , and they take their way to their 
 favourite haunt upon the bridge . pause in one of the recess , or 
 nook , Leonard then begin , " Helen , we must part ! " 

 " Part ? - oh , brother ! " 

 " listen . all work that depend on mind be over for I , nothing remain 
 but the labour of thew and sinew . I can not go back to my village and 
 say to all , ' my hope be self - conceit , and my intellect a delusion ! ' I 
 can not . neither in this sordid city can I turn menial or porter . I might 
 be bear to that drudgery , but my mind have , it may be unhappily , raise 
 I above my birth . what , then , shall I do ? I know not yet , - serve as 
 a soldier , or push my way to some wilderness afar , as an emigrant , 
 perhaps . but whatever my choice , I must henceforth be alone ; I have a 
 home no more . but there be a home for you , Helen , a very humble one 
  for you too , so well bear , but very safe , - the roof of - of - my peasant 
 mother . she will love you for my sake , and - and - " 

 Helen clung to he tremble , and sob out , " anything , anything you 
 will . but I can work ; I can make money , Leonard . I do , indeed , make 
 money , - you do not know how much , but enough for we both till well 
 time come to you . do not let we part . " 

 " and I - a man , and bear to labour - to be maintain by the work of an 
 infant ! no , Helen , do not so degrade I . " 

 she draw back as she look on his flushed brow , bow her head 
 submissively , and murmur , " Pardon . " 

 " ah , " say Helen , after a pause , " if now we could but find my poor 
 father 's friend ! I never so much care for it before . " 

 " yes , he would surely provide for you . " 

 " for I ! " repeat Helen , in a tone of soft , deep reproach , and she 
 turn away her head to conceal her tear . 

 " you be sure you would remember he , if we meet he by chance ? " 

 " oh , yes . he be so different from all we see in this terrible city , and 
 his eye be like yonder star , so clear and so bright ; yet the light 
 seem to come from afar off , as the light do in your , when your 
 thought be away from all thing round you . and then , too , his dog , 
 whom he call Nero - I could not forget that . " 

 " but his dog may not be always with he . " 

 " but the bright clear eye be ! ah , now you look up to heaven , and your 
 seem to dream like his . " 

 Leonard do not answer , for his thought be indeed less on earth than 
 struggle to pierce into that remote and mysterious heaven . 

 both be silent long ; the crowd pass they by unheedingly . night 
 deepen over the river , but the reflection of the lamp - light on its 
 wave be more visible than that of the star . the beam show the 
 darkness of the strong current ; and the craft that lie eastward on 
 the tide , with sail - less spectral mast and black dismal hulk , look 
 death - like in their stillness . 

 Leonard look down , and the thought of Chatterton 's grim suicide come 
 back to his soul ; and a pale , scornful face , with luminous haunt 
 eye , seem to look up from the stream , and murmur from livid lip , 
 " struggle no more against the tide on the surface , - all be calm and 
 rest within the deep . " 

 start in terror from the gloom of his revery , the boy begin to talk 
 fast to Helen , and try to soothe she with description of the lowly 
 home which he have offer . 

 he speak of the light care which she would participate with his 
 mother   for by that name he still call the widow , and dwelt , with an 
 eloquence that the contrast round he make sincere and strong , on the 
 happy rural life , the shadowy woodland , the ripple cornfield , the 
 solemn , lone churchspire soar from the tranquil landscape . 

 Flatteringly he paint the flowery terrace of the italian exile , and 
 the playful fountain that , even as he speak , be fling up its spray 
 to the star , through serene air untrouble by the smoke of city , 
 and untainted by the sinful sigh of man . he promise she the love and 
 protection of nature akin to the happy scene : the simple , affectionate 
 mother , the gentle pastor , the exile wise and kind , Violante , with 
 dark eye full of the mystic thought that solitude call from 
 childhood , - Violante should be her companion . 

 " and , oh ! " cry Helen , " if life be thus happy there , return with I , 
 return ! return ! " 

 " alas ! " murmur the boy , " if the hammer once strike the spark from the 
 anvil , the spark must fly upward ; it can not fall back to earth until 
 light have leave it . Upward still , Helen , - let I go upward still ! " 




 CHAPTER XV . 

 the next morning Helen be very ill , - so ill that , shortly after rise , 
 she be force to creep back to bed . her frame shiver , her eye be 
 heavy , her hand burn like fire . Fever have set in . perhaps she might 
 have catch cold on the bridge , perhaps her emotion have prove too 
 much for her frame . Leonard , in great alarm , call in the near 
 apothecary . the apothecary look grave , and say there be danger . and 
 danger soon declare itself , - Helen become delirious . for several day 
 she lie in this state , be tween life and death . Leonard then feel that 
 all the sorrow of earth be light , compare with the fear of lose 
 what we love . how valueless the envy laurel seem beside the dying 
 rise ! 

 thank , perhaps , more to his heed and tend than to medical skill , she 
 recover sense at last . immediate peril be over ; but she be very weak 
 and reduce , her ultimate recovery doubtful , convalescence , at well , 
 likely to be very slow . 

 but when she learn how long she have be thus ill , she look

 " give I my work ; I be strong enough for that now , - it would amuse I . " 

 Leonard burst into tear . 

 alas ! he have no work himself ; all their joint money have melt away . the 
 apothecary be not like good Dr. Morgan ; the medicine be to be pay 
 for , and the rent . two day before , Leonard have pawn Riccabocca 's 
 watch ; and when the last shilling thus raise be go , how should he 
 support Helen ? nevertheless he conquer his tear , and assure she that 
 he have employment ; and that so earnestly that she believe he , and sink 
 into soft sleep . he listen to her breathing , kiss her forehead , and 
 leave the room . he turn into his own neighbouring garret , and lean 
 his face on his hand , collect all his thought . 

 he must be a beggar at last . he must write to Mr. Dale for money , - Mr. 
 Dale , too , who know the secret of his birth . he would rather have beg 
 of a stranger ; it seem to add a new dishonour to his mother 's memory 
 for the child to beg of one who be acquaint with her shame . have he 
 himself be the only one to want and to starve , he would have sink inch 
 by inch into the grave of famine , before he would have so subdue his 
 pride . but Helen , there on that bed , - Helen needing , for week perhaps , 
 all support , and illness make luxury themselves like necessary ! 
 beg he must . and when he so resolve , have you but see the proud , 
 bitter soul he conquer , you would have say , " this , which he think 
 be degradation , - this be heroism . " oh , strange human heart ! no epic ever 
 write achieve the Sublime and the Beautiful which be graven , unread 
 by human eye , in thy secret leaf . 

 of whom else should he beg ? his mother have nothing , Riccabocca be poor , 
 and the stately Violante , who have exclaim , " would that I be a man ! 
 " - he could not endure the thought that she should pity he and despise . 
 the Avenels ! no , - thrice no . he draw towards he hastily ink and paper , 
 and write rapid line that be wring from he as from the bleeding 
 string of life . 

 but the hour for the post have pass , the letter must wait till the next 
 day ; and three day at least would elapse before he could receive an 
 answer . he leave the letter on the table , and , stifle as for air , go 
 forth . he cross the bridge , he pass on mechanically , and be bear 
 along by a crowd press towards the door of parliament . a debate that 
 excited popular interest be fix for that evening , and many bystander 
 collect in the street to see the member pass to and fro , or hear what 
 speaker have yet rise to take part in the debate , or try to get order 
 for the gallery . 

 he halt amidst these loiterer , with no interest , indeed , in common 
 with they , but look over their head abstractedly towards the tall 
 Funeral Abbey , - imperial Golgotha of Poets and Chiefs and king . 

 suddenly his attention be divert to those around by the sound of a 
 name , displeasingly know to he . " how be you , Randal Leslie ? come to 
 hear the debate ? " say a member , who be pass through the street . 

 " yes ; Mr. Egerton promise to get I under the gallery . he be to speak 
 himself to - night , and I have never hear he . as you be go into the 
 House , will you remind he of his promise to I ? " 

 " I can not   now , for he be speak already , - and well too . I hurry from 
 the Athenaeum , where I be dining , on purpose to be in time , as I hear 
 that his speech be make a great effect . " 

 " this be very unlucky , " say randal . " I have no idea he would speak so 
 early . " 

 " C - - - bring he up by a direct personal attack . but follow I ; 
 perhaps I can get you into the House ; and a man like you , Leslie , from 
 whom we expect great thing some day , I can tell you , should not 
 miss any such opportunity of know what this House of our be on a 
 field - night . come on ! " 

 the member hurry towards the door ; and as Randal follow he , 
 a bystander cry , " that be the young man who write the famous 
 pamphlet , - Egerton 's relation . " 

 " oh , indeed ! " say another . " clever man , Egerton , - I be wait for 
 he . " 

 " so be i. " 

 " why , you be not a constituent , as I be . " 

 " no ; but he have be very kind to my nephew , and I must thank he . you 
 be a constituent - he be an honour to your town . " 

 " so he be : enlighten man ! " 

 " and so generous ! " 

 " bring forward really good measure , " quoth the politician . 

 " and clever young man , " say the uncle . 

 Therewith one or two other join in the praise of Audley Egerton , and 
 many anecdote of his liberality be tell . Leonard listen at first 
 listlessly , at last with thoughtful attention . he have hear Burley , 
 too , speak highly of this generous statesman , who , without pretend to 
 genius himself , appreciate it in other . he suddenly remember , too , 
 that Egerton be half - brother to the squire . vague notion of some 
 appeal to this eminent person , not for charity , but employment to his 
 mind , gleam across he , - inexperienced boy that he yet be ! and 
 while thus meditate , the door of the House open and out come Audley 
 Egerton himself . a partial cheering , follow by a general murmur , 
 apprise Leonard of the presence of the popular statesman . Egerton be 
 catch hold of by some five or six person in succession ; a shake of 
 the hand , a nod , a brief whisper word or two , suffice the practised 
 member for graceful escape ; and soon , free from the crowd , his tall , 
 erect figure pass on , and turn towards the bridge . he pause at the 
 angle and take out his watch , look at it by the lamp - light . 

 " Harley will be here soon , " he mutter , - " he be always punctual ; and 
 now that I have speak , I can give he an hour or so . that be well . " 

 as he replace his watch in his pocket and re - button his coat over 
 his firm , broad chest , he lift his eye , and see a young man stand 
 before he . 

 " do you want I ? " ask the statesman , with the direct brevity of his 
 practical character . 

 " Mr. Egerton , " say the young man , with a voice that slightly tremble 
 and yet be manly amidst emotion , " you have a great name , and great 
 power ; I stand here in these street of London without a friend , and 
 without employment . I believe that I have it in I to do some nobler 
 work than that of bodily labour , have I but one friend , - one opening for 
 my thought . and now I have say this , I scarcely know how , or why , but 
 from despair , and the sudden impulse which that despair take from the 
 praise that follow your success , I have nothing more to add . " 

 Audley Egerton be silent for a moment , strike by the tone and 
 address of the stranger ; but the consummate and wary man of the world , 
 accustom to all manner of strange application and all variety of 
 imposture , quickly recover from a passing and slight effect . 

 " be you a native of ? "   name the town which the statesman 
 represent . 

 " no , sir . " 

 " well , young man , I be very sorry for you ; but the good sense you 
 must possess   for I judge of that by the education you have evidently 
 receive   must tell you that a public man , whatever be his patronage , 
 have it too fully absorb by claimant who have a right to demand it , to 
 be able to listen to stranger . " 

 he pause a moment , and as Leonard stand silent , add with more 
 kindness than most public man so accosted would have show , 

 " you say you be friendless , - poor fellow ! in early life that happen 
 to many of we , who find friend enough before the close . be honest , and 
 well - conduct : lean on yourself , not on stranger ; work with the body 
 if you can not   with the mind ; and , believe I , that advice be all I can 
 give you , unless this trifle " - and the minister hold out a crown - piece . 

 Leonard bow , shake his head sadly , and walk away . Egerton look 
 after he with a slight pang . 

 " Pooh ! " say he to himself , " there must be thousand in the same 
 state in these street of London . I can not redress the necessity of 
 civilization . well educate ! it be not from ignorance henceforth that 
 society will suffer , - it be from over - educate the hungry thousand 
 who , thus unfit for manual toil , and with no career for mental , will 
 some day or other stand like that boy in our street , and puzzle wiser 
 minister than I be . " 

 as Egerton thus muse , and pass on to the bridge , a bugle - horn rang 
 merrily from the box of a gay four - in - hand . a drag - coach with superb 
 blood - horse rattle over the causeway , and in the driver Egerton 
 recognize his nephew , Frank Hazeldean . 

 the young Guardsman be return with a lively party of man from dining 
 at Greenwich , and the careless laughter of these child of pleasure 
 float far over the still river ; it vex the ear of the careworn 
 statesman , - sad , perhaps , with all his greatness , lonely amidst all his 
 crowd of friend . it remind he , perhaps , of his own youth , when such 
 party and companionship be familiar to he , though through they all 
 he have bear an ambitious , aspiring soul . " Le jeu vaut - il la chandelle ? " 
 say he , shrug his shoulder . 

 the coach roll rapidly past Leonard , as he stand lean against the 
 corner of the bridge , and the mire of the kennel splash over he 
 from the hoof of the fiery horse . the laughter smite on his ear more 
 discordantly than on the minister 's , but it begot no envy . 

 " life be a dark riddle , " say he , smite his breast . 

 and he walk slowly on , gain the recess where he have stand several 
 night before with Helen , and , dizzy with want of food , and wear out for 
 want of sleep , he sink down into the dark corner ; while the river that 
 roll under the arch of stone muttered dirge - like in his ear , - as under 
 the social key - stone wail and roll on forever the mystery of Human 
 Discontent . take comfort , o Thinker by the stream ! ' t be the river that 
 found and give pomp to the city ; and , without the discontent , where 
 be progress , what be man ? take comfort , o THINKER ! wherever the 
 stream over which thou bend , or beside which thou sinkest , weary 
 and desolate , fret the arch that support thee , never dream that , by 
 destroy the bridge , thou canst silence the moan of the wave ! 




 CHAPTER XVI . 

 before a table , in the apartment appropriate to he in his father 's 
 house at Knightsbridge , sit Lord L'Estrange , sort or destroy 
 letter and paper , - an ordinary symptom of change of residence . there 
 be certain trifle by which a shrewd observer may judge of a man 's 
 disposition . thus , range on the table , with some elegance , but with 
 soldier - like precision , be sundry little relic of former day , 
 hallow by some sentiment of memory , or perhaps endear solely by 
 custom ; which , whether he be in Egypt , Italy , or England , always make 
 part of the furniture of Harley 's room . even the small , old - fashion , 
 and somewhat inconvenient inkstand into which he dip the pen as he 
 label the letter he put aside , belong to the writing - desk which 
 have be his pride as a schoolboy . even the book that lay scattered 
 round be not new work , not those to which we turn to satisfy the 
 curiosity of an hour , or to distract our graver thought ; they be 
 chiefly either latin or italian poet , with many a pencil - mark on the 
 margin ; or book which , make severe demand on thought , require slow 
 and frequent perusal , and become companion . somehow or other , in 
 remark that even in dumb , inanimate thing the man be averse to 
 change , and have the habit of attach himself to whatever be connect 
 with old association , you might guess that he clung with pertinacity to 
 affection more important , and you could well comprehend the freshness 
 of his friendship for one so dissimilar in pursuit and character as 
 Audley Egerton . an affection once admit into the heart of Harley 
 L'Estrange seem never to be question or reason with ; it become 
 tacitly fix , as it be , into his own nature , and little less than a 
 revolution of his whole system could dislodge or disturb it . 

 Lord L'Estrange 's hand rest now upon a letter in a stiff , legible 
 italian character , and instead of dispose of it at once as he have do 
 with the rest , he spread it before he , and re - read the content . it be 
 a letter from Riccabocca , receive a few week since , and run thus : - 

  letter from SIGNOR riccabocca to LORD L'ESTRANGE . 

 I thank you , my noble friend , for judge of I with faith in my honour , 
 and respect for my revers . 

 no , and thrice no , to all concession , all overture , all treaty with 
 Giulio Franzini . I write the name , and my emotion choke I . I must 
 pause , and cool back into disdain . it be over . pass from that subject . 
 but you have alarm I . this sister ! I have not see she since her 
 childhood ; but she be bring up under his influence , 

  - she can but work as his agent . she wish to learn my residence ! it can 
 be but for some hostile and malignant purpose . I may trust in you , - I 
 know that . you say I may trust equally in the discretion of your friend . 
 pardon I , - my confidence be not so elastic . a word may give the clew 
 to my retreat . but , if discover , what harm can ensue ? an english roof 
 protect I from austrian despotism : true ; but not the brazen tower 
 of Danae could protect I from italian craft . and , be there nothing 
 bad , it would be intolerable to I to live under the eye of a 
 relentless spy . truly saith our proverb , ' he sleep ill for whom the 
 enemy wake . ' look you , my friend , I have do with my old life , - I wish 
 to cast it from I as a snake its skin . I have deny myself all that 
 exile deem consolation . no pity for misfortune , no message from 
 sympathize friendship , no news from a lost and bereaved country follow 
 I to my hearth under the sky of the stranger . from all these I have 
 voluntarily cut thyself off . I be as dead to the life I once live as 
 if the Styx roll between it and I . with that sternness which 
 be admissible only to the afflict , I have deny myself even the 
 consolation of your visit . I have tell you fairly and simply that 
 your presence would unsettle all my enforced and infirm philosophy , and 
 remind I only of the past , which I seek to blot from remembrance . you 
 have comply on the one condition , that whenever I really want your aid 
 I will ask it ; and , meanwhile , you have generously seek to obtain I 
 justice from the cabinet of minister and in the court of king . I do 
 not refuse your heart this luxury ; for I have a child - ah ! I have teach 
 that child already to revere your name , and in her prayer it be 
 not forget . but now that you be convince that even your zeal be 
 unavaile , I ask you to discontinue attempt which may but bring the 
 spy upon my track , and involve I in new misfortune . believe I , O 
 brilliant Englishman , that I be satisfied and content with my lot . 
 I be sure it would not be for my happiness to change it , ' Chi non ha 
 provato il male non conosce il bone . ' 

   " one do not know when one be well off till one have know 
  misfortune . " 

 you ask I how I live , - I answer , alla giornata , -   to the day   - not 
 for the morrow , as I do once . I have accustom myself to the calm 
 existence of a village . I take interest in its detail . there be my 
 wife , good creature , sit opposite to I , never ask what I write , 
 or to whom , but ready to throw aside her work and talk the moment the 
 pen be out of my hand . talk - and what about ? Heaven know ! but I would 
 rather hear that talk , though on the affair of a hamlet , than babble 
 again with recreant noble and blunder professor about commonwealth 
 and constitution . when I want to see how little those last influence 
 the happiness of wise man , have I not Machiavelli and Thucydides ? then , 
 by and by , the parson will drop in , and we argue . he never know when he 
 be beat , so the argument be everlaste . on fine day I ramble out by 
 a wind rill with my Violante , or stroll to my friend the squire 's , 
 and see how healthful a thing be true pleasure ; and on wet day I shut 
 myself up , and mope , perhaps till , hark ! a gentle tap at the door , 
 and in come Violante , with her dark eye , that shine out through 
 reproachful tear , - reproachful that I should mourn alone , while she be 
 under my roof ; so she put her arm round I , and in five minute all be 
 sunshine within . what care we for your english gray cloud without ? 

 leave I , my dear Lord , - leave I to this quiet happy passage towards 
 old age , serene than the youth that I waste so wildly ; and guard well 
 the secret on which my happiness depend . 

 now to yourself , before I close . of that same yourself you speak 
 too little , as of I too much . but I so well comprehend the profound 
 melancholy that lie underneath the wild and fanciful humour with which 
 you but suggest , as in sport , what you feel so in earnest . the laborious 
 solitude of city weigh on you . you be fly back to the dolce 
 far niente , - to friend few , but intimate ; to life monotonous , but 
 unrestrained ; and even there the sense of loneliness will again 
 seize upon you ; and you do not seek , as I do , the annihilation of 
 memory , - your dead passion be turn to ghost that haunt you , and 
 unfit you for the live world . I see it all , - I see it still , in your 
 hurried fantastic line , as I see it when we two sit amidst the pine 
 and beheld the blue lake stretch below , I trouble by the shadow of 
 the future , you disturb by that of the Past . 

 well , but you say , half seriously , half in jest , " I will escape from 
 this prison - house of memory ; I will form new tie , like other man , and 
 before it be too late ; I will marry . ay , but I must love , - there be the 
 difficulty . " Difficulty , - yes , and Heaven be thank for it ! recall all 
 the unhappy marriage that have come to your knowledge : pray , have not 
 eighteen out of twenty be marriage for Love ? it always have be so , 
 and it always will ; because , whenever we love deeply , we exact so much 
 and forgive so little . be content to find some one with whom your hearth 
 and your honour be safe . you will grow to love what never wound your 
 heart , you will soon grow out of love with what must always disappoint 
 your imagination . cospetto ! I wish my Jemima have a young sister for 
 you . yet it be with a deep groan that I settle myself to a - Jemima . 

 now , I have write you a long letter , to prove how little I need 
 of your compassion or your zeal . once more let there be long silence 
 between we . it be not easy for I to correspond with a man of your rank , 
 and not incur the curious gossip of my still little pool of a world 
 which the splash of a pebble can break into circle . I must take this 
 over to a post - town some ten mile off , and drop it into the box by 
 stealth . Adieu , dear and noble friend , gentle heart and subtle fancy 
 that I have meet in my walk through life . adieu . write I word when you 
 have abandon a day - dream and find a Jemima . 

  ALPHONSO . 

 P. S. - For Heaven 's sake , caution and recaution your friend the minister 
 not to drop a word to this woman that may betray my hiding - place . 

 " be he really happy ? " murmur Harley , as he close the letter ; and he 
 sink for a few moment into a revery . 

 " this life in a village , this wife in a lady who put down her work to 
 talk about villager - what a contrast to Audley 's full existence ! and I 
 can not envy nor comprehend either ! yet my own existence - what be it ? " 

 he rise , and move towards the window , from which a rustic stair 
 descend to a green lawn , stud with large tree than be often 
 find in the ground of a suburban residence . there be calm and 
 coolness in the sight , and one could scarcely have suppose that London 
 lie so near . 

 the door open softly , and a lady past middle age enter , and 
 approach Harley , as he still stand muse by the window , lay she 
 hand on his shoulder . what character there be in a hand ! Hers be a hand 
 that Titian would have paint with elaborate care ! thin , white , and 
 delicate , with the blue vein raise from the surface . yet there be 
 something more than mere patrician elegance in the form and texture . 
 a true physiologist would have say at once , " there be intellect and 
 pride in that hand , which seem to fix a hold where it rest ; and lie 
 so lightly , yet will not be as lightly shake off . " 

 " Harley , " say the lady - and Harley turn - " you do not deceive I by 
 that smile , " she continue sadly ; " you be not smile when I enter . " 

 " it be rarely that we smile to ourselves , my dear mother ; and I have 
 do nothing lately so foolish as to cause I to smile at myself . " 

 " my son , " say Lady Lansmere , somewhat abruptly , but with great 
 earnestness , " you come from a line of illustrious ancestor ; and 
 methink they ask from their tomb why the last of their race have no aim 
 and no object , no interest , no home , in the land which they serve , and 
 which reward they with its honour . " 

 " Mother , " say the soldier , simply , " when the land be in danger I 
 serve it as my forefather serve , - and my answer would be the scar on 
 my breast . " 

 " be it only in danger that a country be serve , only in war that duty 
 be fulfil ? do you think that your father , in his plain , manly life 
 of country gentleman , do not fulfil , though perhaps too obscurely , the 
 object for which aristocracy be create , and wealth be bestow ? " 

 " Doubtless he do , ma'am , - and well than his vagrant son ever can . " 

 " yet his vagrant son have receive such gift from nature , his youth be 
 so rich in promise , his boyhood so glow at the dream of glory ! " 

 " ay , " say Harley , very softly , " it be possible , - and all to be bury 
 in a single grave ! " 

 the countess start , and withdraw her hand from Harley 's shoulder . 

 Lady Lansmere 's countenance be not one that much varied in expression . 
 she have in this , as in her cast of feature , little resemblance to her 
 son . 

 her feature be slightly aquiline , - the eyebrow of that arch which 
 give a certain majesty to the aspect ; the line round the mouth be 
 habitually rigid and compressed . her face be that of one who have go 
 through great emotion and subdue it . there be something formal , 
 and even ascetic , in the character of her beauty , which be still 
 considerable , in her air and in her dress . she might have suggest 
 to you the idea of some Gothic baroness of old , half chatelaine , 
 half - abbess ; you would see at a glance that she do not live in the 
 light world around she , and disdain its fashion and its mode of 
 thought ; yet with all this rigidity it be still the face of the woman 
 who have know human tie and human affection . and now , as she gaze 
 long on Harley 's quiet , sadden brow , it be the face of a mother . 

 " a single grave , " she say , after a long pause . " and you be then but 
 a boy , Harley ! can such a memory influence you even to this day ? it be 
 scarcely possible : it do not seem to I within the reality of man 's 
 life , - though it might be of woman 's . " 

 " I believe , " say Harley , half soliloquize , " that I have a great deal 
 of the woman in I . perhaps man who live much alone , and care not for 
 man 's object , do grow tenacious of impression , as your sex do . but 
 oh , " he cry , aloud , and with a sudden change of countenance , " oh , the 
 hard and the cold man would have feel as I do , have he know she , 
 have he love she . she be like no other woman I have ever meet . bright 
 and glorious creature of another sphere ! she descend on this earth and 
 darken it when she pass away . it be no use strive . mother , I have 
 as much courage as our steel - clothe father ever have . I have dare in 
 battle and in desert , against man and the wild beast , against the storm 
 and the ocean , against the rude power of Nature , - danger as dread as 
 ever pilgrim or Crusader rejoice to brave . but courage against that one 
 memory ! no , I have none ! " 

 " Harley , Harley , you break my heart ! " cry the countess , clasp her 
 hand . 

 " it be astonishing , " continue her son , so rapt in his own thought that 
 he do not , perhaps , hear her outcry . " yea , verily , it be astonishing , 
 that consider the thousand of woman I have see and speak with , I 
 never see a face like her , - never hear a voice so sweet . and all this 
 universe of life can not afford I one look and one tone that can restore 
 I to man 's privilege , - love . well , well , well , life have other thing 
 yet ; Poetry and Art live still ; still smile the heaven and still wave 
 the tree . leave I to happiness in my own way . " 

 the countess be about to reply , when the door be throw hastily open , 
 and Lord Lansmere walk in . 

 the earl be some year old than the countess , but his placid face 
 show less wear and tear , - a benevolent , kindly face , without any 
 evidence of command intellect , but with no lack of sense in its 
 pleasant line ; his form not tall , but upright and with an air of 
 consequence , - a little pompous , but good - humouredly so , - the pomposity 
 of the Grand Seigneur who have live much in province , whose will 
 have be rarely dispute , and whose importance have be so feel and 
 acknowledge as to react insensibly on himself ; - an excellent man ; but 
 when you glance towards the high brow and dark eye of the countess , 
 you marvel a little how the two have come together , and , accord to 
 common report , live so happily in the union . 

 " Ho , ho ! my dear Harley , " cry Lord Lansmere , rub his hand with an 
 appearance of much satisfaction , " I have just be pay a visit to the 
 duchess . " 

 " what duchess , my dear father ? " 

 " why , your mother 's first cousin , to be sure , - the Duchess of 
 Knaresborough , whom , to oblige I , you condescend to call upon ; and 
 delight I be to hear that you admire Lady Mary - " 

 " she be very high breed , and rather - high - nosed , " answer Harley . then , 
 observe that his mother look pained , and his father disconcert , he 
 add seriously , " but handsome certainly . " 

 " well , Harley , " say the earl , recover himself , " the duchess , take 
 advantage of our connection to speak freely , have intimate to I that 
 Lady Mary have be no less strike with yourself ; and to come to the 
 point , since you allow that it be time you should think of marry , I 
 do not know a more desirable alliance . what do you say , Katherine ? " 

 " the duke be of a family that rank in history before the Wars of the 
 Roses , " say Lady Lansmere , with an air of deference to her husband ; 
 " and there have never be one scandal in its annal , nor one blot on its 
 scutcheon . but I be sure my dear Lord must think that the duchess should 
 not have make the first overture , - even to a friend and a kinsman ? " 

 " why , we be old - fashioned people , " say the earl , rather embarrassed , 
 " and the duchess be a woman of the world . " 

 " let we hope , " say the countess , mildly , " that her daughter be not . " 

 " I would not marry Lady Mary , if all the rest of the female sex be 
 turn into ape , " say Lord L'Estrange , with deliberate fervour . 

 " good heaven ! " cry the earl , " what extraordinary language be this ? 
 and pray why , sir ? " 

 HARLEY . - " I can not   say ; there be no why in these case . but , my dear 
 father , you be not keep faith with I . " 

 LORD LANSMERE . - " how ? " 

 HARLEY . - " you and my Lady , here , entreat I to marry ; I promise to do 
 my good to obey you , but on one condition , that I choose for myself , 
 and take my time about it . agree on both side . Whereon , off go 
 your lordship - actually before noon , at an hour when no lady , without 
 a shudder , could think of cold blonde and damp orange flower - off go 
 your Lordship , I say , and commit poor Lady Mary and your unworthy son 
 to a mutual admiration , - which neither of we ever feel . pardon I , my 
 father , but this be grave . again let I claim your promise , - full choice 
 for myself , and no reference to the Wars of the Roses . what War of the 
 rose like that between Modesty and Love upon the cheek of the virgin ! " 

 LADY LANSMERE . - " full choice for yourself , Harley : so be it . but we , 
 too , name a condition , - do we not , Lansmere ? " 

 the EARL   puzzle . - " eh , do we ? certainly we do . " 

 HARLEY . - " what be it ? " 

 LADY LANSMERE . - " the son of Lord Lansmere can only marry the daughter of 
 a gentleman . " 

 the EARL . - " of course , of course . " 

 the blood rush over Harley 's fair face , and then as suddenly leave it 
 pale . 

 he walk away to the window ; his mother follow he , and again lay 
 her hand on his shoulder . 

 " you be cruel , " say he , gently , and in a whisper , as he wince under 
 the touch of the hand . then turn to the earl , who be gaze at he 
 in blank surprise , - it never occur to Lord Lansmere that there could 
 be a doubt of his son 's marrying beneath the rank modestly state by 
 the countess , - Harley stretch forth his hand , and say , in his 
 soft win tone , " you have ever be most gracious to I , and most 
 forbearing ; it be but just that I should sacrifice the habit of an 
 egotist , to gratify a wish which you so warmly entertain . I agree with 
 you , too , that our race should not close in I , - Noblesse oblige . but 
 you know I be ever romantic ; and I must love where I marry ; or , if not 
 love , I must feel that my wife be worthy of all the love I could once 
 have bestow . now , as to the vague word ' gentleman ' that my mother 
 employ - word that mean so differently on different lip - I confess 
 that I have a prejudice against young lady bring up in the 
 ' excellent foppery of the world , ' as the daughter of gentleman of our 
 rank mostly be . I crave , therefore , the most liberal interpretation of 
 this word ' gentleman . ' and so long as there be nothing mean or sordid 
 in the birth , habit , and education of the father of this bride to be , 
 I trust you will both agree to demand nothing more , - neither title nor 
 pedigree . " 

 " Titles , no , assuredly , " say Lady Lansmere ; " they do not make 
 gentleman . " 

 " certainly not , " say the earl ; " many of our good family be 
 untitled . " 

 " Titles - no , " repeat Lady Lansmere ; " but ancestor yes . " 

 " ah , my mother , " say Harley , with his most sad and quiet smile , " it be 
 fat that we shall never agree . the first of our race be ever the one 
 we be most proud of ; and pray , what ancestor have he ? Beauty , virtue , 
 modesty , intellect , - if these be not nobility enough for a man , he be a 
 slave to the dead . " 

 with these word Harley take up his hat and make towards the door . 

 " you say yourself , ' Noblesse oblige , ' " say the countess , follow he 
 to the threshold ; " we have nothing more to add . " 

 Harley slightly shrug his shoulder , kiss his mother 's hand ; 
 whistle to Nero , who start up from a doze by the window , and go his 
 way . 

 " do he really go abroad next week ? " say the earl . " so he say . " 

 " I be afraid there be no chance for Lady Mary , " resume Lord Lansmere , 
 with a slight but melancholy smile . 

 " she have not intellect enough to charm he . she be not worthy of 
 Harley , " say the proud mother . 

 " between you and I , " rejoin the earl , rather timidly , " I do not   see 
 what good his intellect do he . he could not be more unsettled and 
 useless if he be the mere dunce in the three kingdom . and so 
 ambitious as he be when a boy ! Katherine , I sometimes fancy that you 
 know what change he . " 

 " I ! Nay , my dear Lord , it be a common change enough with the young , when 
 of such fortune , who find , when they enter life , that there be really 
 little leave for they to strive for . have Harley be a poor man 's son , it 
 might have be different . " 

 " I be bear to the same fortune as Harley , " say the earl , shrewdly , 
 " and yet I flatter myself I be of some use to old England . " 

 the countess seize upon the occasion , compliment her Lord , and turn 
 the subject . 




 CHAPTER XVII . 

 Harley spend his day in his usual desultory , lounge manner , - din 
 in his quiet corner at his favourite club . Nero , not admit into the 
 club , patiently wait for he outside the door . the dinner over , 
 dog and man , equally indifferent to the crowd , saunter down that 
 thoroughfare which , to the few who can comprehend the Poetry of London , 
 have association of glory and of woe sublime as any that the ruin of 
 the dead eld world can furnish , - thoroughfare that traverse what 
 be once the courtyard of Whitehall , have to its leave the site of 
 the palace that lodge the royalty of Scotland ; gain , through a narrow 
 strait , that old isle of Thorney , in which Edward the Confessor receive 
 the ominous visit of the Conqueror ; and , widen once more by the Abbey 
 and the Hall of Westminster , then lose itself , like all memory of 
 earthly grandeur , amidst humble passage and mean defile . 

 thus think Harley L'Estrange - ever less amidst the actual world around 
 he than the image invoke by his own solitary soul - as he gain the 
 bridge , and see the dull , lifeless craft sleep on the " Silent Way , " 
 once loud and glitter with the gild bark of the antique Seignorie 
 of England . 

 it be on that bridge that Audley Egerton have appoint to meet 
 L'Estrange , at an hour when he calculate he could well steal a respite 
 from debate . for Harley , with his fastidious dislike to all the resort 
 of his equal , have decline to seek his friend in the crowded region of 
 Bellamy 's . 

 Harley 's eye , as he pass along the bridge , be attract by a still 
 form , seat on the stone in one of the nook , with its face cover 
 by its hand . " if I be a sculptor , " say he to himself , " I 
 should remember that image whenever I wish to convey the idea of 
 Despondency ! " he lift his look and see , a little before he in the 
 midst of the causeway , the firm , erect figure of Audley Egerton . the 
 moonlight be full on the bronzed countenance of the strong public man , 
 with its line of thought and care , and its vigorous but cold expression 
 of intense self - control . 

 " and look yonder , " continue Harley 's soliloquy , " I should remember 
 that form , when I wish to hew out from the granite the idea of 
 Endurance . " 

 " so you be come , and punctually , " say Egerton , link his arm in 
 Harley 's . 

 HARLEY - " Punctually , of course , for I respect your time , and I will not 
 detain you long . I presume you will speak to - night ? " 

 EGERTON . - " I have speak . " 

 HARLEY   with interest . - " and well , I hope ? " 

 EGERTON . - " with effect , I suppose , for I have be loudly cheer , which 
 do not always happen to I . " 

 HARLEY . - " and that give you pleasure ? " 

 egerton   after a moment 's thought . - " no , not the least . " 

 HARLEY . - " what , then , attach you so much to this life , - constant 
 drudgery , constant warfare , the more pleasurable faculty dormant , all 
 the harsh one arouse , if even its reward   and I take the good of 
 those to be applause   do not please you ? " 

 EGERTON . - " what ? Custom . " 

 HARLEY . - " Martyr . " 

 EGERTON . - " you say it : but turn to yourself ; you have decide , then , to 
 leave England next week ? " 

 HARLEY   moodily . - " yes . this life in a capital , where all be so 
 active , myself so objectless , prey on I like a low fever . nothing here 
 amuse I , nothing interest , nothing comfort and console . but I be 
 resolve , before it be too late , to make one great struggle out of the 
 Past , and into the natural world of man . in a word , I have resolve to 
 marry . " 

 EGERTON . - " whom ? " 

 HARLEY   seriously . - " upon my life , my dear fellow , you be a great 
 philosopher . you have hit the exact question . you see I can not marry a 
 dream ; and where , out of dream , shall I find this ' whom ' ? " 

 EGERTON . - " you do not search for she . " 

 HARLEY . " do we ever search for love ? do it not flash upon we when we 
 least expect it ? be it not like the inspiration to the muse ? what poet 
 sit down and say , ' I will write a poem ' ? what man look out and say , 
 ' I will fall in love ' ? no ! happiness , as the great German tell we , 
 ' fall suddenly from the bosom of the god ; ' so do love . " 

 EGERTON . - " you remember the old line in Horace : ' the tide flow away 
 while the boor sit on the margin and wait for the ford . ' " 

 harlev . - " an idea which incidentally drop from you some week ago , 
 and which I have before half - meditate , have since haunt I . if I could 
 but find some child with sweet disposition and fair intellect not yet 
 form , and train she up accord to my ideal . I be still young enough 
 to wait a few year . and meanwhile I shall have gain what I so sadly 
 want , - an object in life . " 

 EGERTON . - " you be ever the child of romance . but what - " 

 here the minister be interrupt by a messenger from the House of 
 Commons , whom Audley have instruct to seek he on the bridge should his 
 presence be require . " Sir , the opposition be take advantage of the 
 thinness of the House to call for a division . Mr. - - - be put up to 
 speak for time , but they will not   hear he . " 

 Egerton turn hastily to Lord L'Estrange . " you see , you must excuse I 
 now . to - morrow I must go to Windsor for two day : but we shall meet on 
 my return . " 

 " it do not matter , " answer Harley ; " I stand out of the pale of 
 your advice , o practical man of sense . and if , " add Harley , with 
 affectionate and mournful sweetness , - " if I weary you with complaint 
 which you can not understand , it be only because of old schoolboy habit . 
 I can have no trouble that I do not confide to you . " 

 Egerton 's hand tremble as it press his friend 's , and without a word , 
 he hurry away abruptly . Harley remain motionless for some second , 
 in deep and quiet revery ; then he call to his dog , and turn back 
 towards Westminster . 

 he pass the nook in which have sit the still figure of Despondency ; but 
 the figure have now rise , and be lean against the balustrade . the 
 dog , who precede his master , pass by the solitary form and sniff it 
 suspiciously . 

 " Nero , sir , come here , " say Harley . 

 " Nero , " - that be the name by which Helen have say that her father 's 
 friend have call his dog ; and the sound startle Leonard as he 
 lean , sick at heart , against the stone . he lift his head and look 
 wistfully , eagerly into Harley 's face . those eye , bright , clear , yet so 
 strangely deep and absent , which Helen have describe , meet his own , and 
 chain they . for L'Estrange halt also ; the boy 's countenance be not 
 unfamiliar to he . he return the inquire look fix on his own , and 
 recognize the student by the bookstall . 

 " the dog be quite harmless , sir , " say L'Estrange , with a smile . 

 " and you call he ' nero ' ? " say Leonard , still gaze on the stranger . 

 Harley mistake the drift of the question . 

 " Nero , sir ; but he be free from the sanguinary propensity of his roman 
 namesake . " Harley be about to pass on , when Leonard say falteringly , 

 " pardon I , but can it be possible that you be one whom I have seek 
 in vain on behalf of the child of Captain Digby ? " 

 Harley stop short . " Digby ! " he exclaim , " where be he ? he should 
 have find I easily . I give he an address . " 

 " ah , Heaven be thank ! " cry Leonard . " Helen be save - she will not 
 die , " and he burst into tear . 

 a very few moment and a very few word suffice to explain to Harley 
 the state of his old fellow - soldier 's orphan . and Harley himself soon 
 stand in the young sufferer 's room , support her burn temple on 
 his breast , and whisper into ear that hear he as in a happy dream , 
 " Comfort , comfort ; your father yet live in I . " 

 and then Helen , raise her eye , say , " but Leonard be my brother - more 
 than brother - and he need a father 's care more than I do . " 

 " hush , hush , Helen . I need no one , nothing now ! " cry Leonard , and his 
 tear gush over the little hand that clasp his own . 




 CHAPTER XVIII . 

 Harley L'Estrange be a man whom all thing that belong to the romantic 
 and poetic side of our human life deeply impressed . when he come to 
 learn the tie between these two child of Nature , stand side by 
 side , alone amidst the storm of fate , his heart be more deeply move 
 than it have be for many year . in those dreary attic , overshadow 
 by the smoke and reek of the humble suburb , the workday world in its 
 harsh and tritest form below and around they , he recognize that 
 divine poem which come out from all union between the mind and the 
 heart . here , on the rough deal table   the ink scarcely dry , lie the 
 writing of the young wrestler for fame and bread ; there , on the 
 other side of the partition , on that mean pallet , lie the boy 's sole 
 comforter , the all that warm his heart with live mortal affection . 
 on one side the wall , the world of imagination ; on the other , this world 
 of grief and of love . and in both , a spirit equally sublime , - unselfish 
 devotion , - " the something afar from the sphere of our sorrow . " 

 he look round the room into which he have follow Leonard , on quit 
 Helen 's bedside . he note the manuscript on the table , and point to 
 they , say gently , " and these be the labour by which you support the 
 soldier 's orphan ? - soldier yourself in a hard battle ! " 

 " the battle be lose , - I could not support she , " reply Leonard , 
 mournfully . 

 " but you do not desert she . when Pandora 's box be open , they say 
 Hope linger last - " 

 " false , false , " say Leonard ; " a heathen 's notion . there be deity 
 that linger behind Hope , - Gratitude , Love , and Duty . " 

 " yours be no common nature , " exclaim Harley , admiringly , " but I must 
 sound it more deeply hereafter : at present I hasten for the physician ; I 
 shall return with he . we must move that poor child from this low close 
 air as soon as possible . meanwhile , let I qualify your rejection of the 
 old fable . Wherever Gratitude , Love , and Duty remain to man , believe I 
 that Hope be there too , though she may be often invisible , hide behind 
 the shelter wing of the nobler deity . " 

 Harley say this with that wondrous smile of his , which cast a 
 brightness over the whole room , and go away . Leonard steal softly 
 towards the grimy window ; and look up towards the star that shine 
 pale over the roof - top , he murmur , " o Thou , the all - seeing and 
 all - merciful ! how it comfort I now to think that , though my dream of 
 knowledge may have sometimes obscure the heaven , I never doubt that 
 Thou wert there ! as luminous and everlasting , though behind the cloud ! " 
 so , for a few minute , he pray silently , then pass into Helen 's 
 room , and sit beside her motionless , for she sleep . she wake just as 
 Harley return with a physician ; and then Leonard , return to his own 
 room , see amongst his paper the letter he have write to Mr. Dale , and 
 muttering , " I need not disgrace my calling , - I need not be the mendicant 
 now " - hold the letter to the flame of the candle . and while he say 
 this , and as the burn tinder drop on the floor , the sharp hunger , 
 unfelt during his late anxious emotion , gnaw at his entrail . still , 
 even hunger could not reach that noble pride which have yield to 
 a sentiment nobler than itself , and he smile as he repeat , " no 
 mendicant ! - the life that I be swear to guard be save . I can raise 
 against Fate the front of Man once more . " 




 CHAPTER XIX . 

 a few day afterwards , and Helen , remove to a pure air , and under the 
 advice of the first physician , be out of all danger . 

 it be a pretty detached cottage , with its window look over the wild 
 heath of Norwood , to which Harley ride daily to watch the convalescence 
 of his young charge : an object in life be already find . as she grow 
 well and strong , he coax she easily into talking , and listen 
 to she with pleased surprise . the heart so infantine and the sense so 
 womanly strike he much by its rare contrast and combination . Leonard , 
 whom he have insist on place also in the cottage , have stay there 
 willingly till Helen 's recovery be beyond question . then he come to 
 Lord L'Estrange , as the latter be about one day to leave the cottage , 
 and say quietly , " now , my Lord , that Helen be safe , and now that she 
 will need I no more , I can no long be a pensioner on your bounty . I 
 return to London . " 

 " you be my visitor , not my pensioner , foolish boy , " say Harley , who 
 have already notice the pride which speak in that farewell ; " come into 
 the garden and let we talk . " 

 Harley seat himself on a bench on the little lawn ; Nero crouch at 
 his foot ; Leonard stand beside he . 

 " so , " say Lord L'Estrange , " you would return to London ? what to do ? " 

 " Fulfil my fate . " 

 " and that ? " 

 " I can not guess . Fate be the isis whose veil no mortal can ever raise . " 

 " you should be bear for great thing , " say Harley , abruptly . " I be sure 
 that you write well . I have see that you study with passion . well 
 than write and well than study , you have a noble heart , and the 
 proud desire of independence . let I see your manuscript , or any copy 
 of what you have already print . do not hesitate , - I ask but to be a 
 reader . I do not   pretend to be a patron : it be a word I hate . " 

 Leonard 's eye sparkle through their sudden moisture . he bring out 
 his portfolio , place it on the bench beside Harley , and then go 
 softly to the farther part of the garden . Nero look after he , and 
 then rise and follow he slowly . the boy seat himself on the turf , 
 and Nero rest his dull head on the loud heart of the poet . 

 Harley take up the various paper before he , and read they through 
 leisurely . certainly he be no critic . he be not accustomed to analyze 
 what please or displease he ; but his perception be quick , and 
 his taste exquisite . as he read , his countenance , always so genuinely 
 expressive , exhibit now doubt and now admiration . he be soon strike 
 by the contrast , in the boy 's writing , between the piece that sport 
 with fancy and those that grapple with thought . in the first , the young 
 poet seem so unconscious of his own individuality . his imagination , 
 afar and aloft from the scene of his suffering , run riot amidst a 
 paradise of happy golden creation . but in the last , the THINKER stand 
 out alone and mournful , question , in troubled sorrow , the hard world 
 on which he gaze . all in the thought be unsettled , tumultuous ; all 
 in the fancy serene and peaceful . the genius seem divide into twain 
 shape , - the one bath its wing amidst the starry dew of heaven ; 
 the other wandering , " melancholy , slow , " amidst desolate and boundless 
 sand . Harley gently lay down the paper and muse a little while . then 
 he rise and walk to Leonard , gaze on his countenance as he near 
 the boy , with a new and a deep interest . 

 " I have read your paper , " he say , " and recognize in they two man , 
 belong to two world , essentially distinct . " Leonard start , and 
 murmur , " true , true ! " 

 " I apprehend , " resume Harley , " that one of these man must either 
 destroy the other , or that the two must become fuse and harmonize into 
 a single existence . get your hat , mount my groom 's horse , and come with 
 I to London ; we will converse by the way . look you , I believe you and 
 I agree in this , - that the first object of every noble spirit be 
 independence . it be towards this independence that I alone presume to 
 assist you , and this be a service which the proud man can receive 
 without a blush . " 

 Leonard lift his eye towards Harley 's , and those eye swam with 
 grateful tear ; but his heart be too full to answer . " I be not one of 
 those , " say Harley , when they be on the road , " who think that because 
 a young man write poetry he be fit for nothing else , and that he must 
 be a poet or a pauper . I have say that in you there seem to I to be 
 two man , - the man of the actual world , the man of the Ideal . to each of 
 these man I can offer a separate career . the first be perhaps the more 
 tempting . it be the interest of the State to draw into its service all 
 the talent and industry it can obtain ; and under his native State every 
 citizen of a free country should be proud to take service . I have a 
 friend who be a minister , and who be know to encourage talent , - Audley 
 Egerton . I have but to say to he , ' there be a young man who will repay 
 the government whatever the government bestow on he ; ' and you will 
 rise to - morrow independent in mean , and with fair occasion to attain 
 to fortune and distinction . this be one offer , - what say you to it ? " 

 Leonard think bitterly of his interview with Audley Egerton , and the 
 minister 's proffered crown - piece . he shake his head , and reply , 

 " oh , my Lord , how have I deserve such kindness ? do with I what you 
 will ; but if I have the option , I would rather follow my own calling . 
 this be not the ambition that inflame I . " 

 " hear , then , the other offer . I have a friend with whom I be less 
 intimate than Egerton , and who have nothing in his gift to bestow . I 
 speak of a man of letter , - Henry Norreys , - of whom you have doubtless 
 heard , who , I should say , conceive an interest in you when he observe 
 you read at the bookstall . I have often hear he say that literature 
 as a profession be misunderstood , and that rightly follow , with the 
 same pain and the same prudence which be bring to bear on other 
 profession , a competence at least can be always ultimately obtain . 
 but the way may be long and tedious , and it lead to no power but over 
 thought ; it rarely attain to wealth ; and though reputation may be 
 certain , fame , such as poet dream of , be the lot of few . what say you 
 to this course ? " 

 " my Lord , I decide , " say Leonard , firmly ; and then , his young face 
 light up with enthusiasm , he exclaim , " yes , if , as you say , 
 there be two man within I , I feel that be I condemn wholly to the 
 mechanical and practical world , one would indeed destroy the other . and 
 the conqueror would be the ruder and the coarser . let I pursue those 
 idea that , though they have but flit across I , vague and formless , 
 have ever soar towards the sunlight . no matter whether or not 
 they lead to fortune or to fame , - at least they will lead I upward ! 
 knowledge for itself I desire ; what care I if it be not power ! " 

 " enough , " say Harley , with a pleased smile at his young companion 's 
 outburst . " as you decide so shall it be settle . and now permit I , 
 if not impertinent , to ask you a few question . your name be Leonard 
 Fairfield ? " 

 the boy blush deeply , and bow his head as if in assent . 

 " Helen say you be self - teach ; for the rest she refer I to 
 you , - thinking , perhaps , that I should esteem you less - rather than yet 
 more highly - if she say you be , as I presume to conjecture , of humble 
 birth . " 

 " my birth , " say Leonard , slowly , " be very - very - humble . " 

 " the name of Fairfield be not unknown to I . there be one of that name 
 who marry into a family in Lansmere , marry an avenel , " continue 
 Harley , and his voice quiver . " you change countenance . oh , could your 
 mother 's name have be Avenel ? " 

 " yes , " say Leonard , between his set tooth . Harley lay his hand on the 
 boy 's shoulder . " then , indeed , I have a claim on you ; then , indeed , we 
 be friend . I have a right to serve any of that family . " 

 Leonard look at he in surprise - " for , " continue Harley , recover 
 himself , " they always serve my family ; and my recollection of 
 Lansmere , though boyish , be indelible . " he spur on his horse as 
 the word close , and again there be a long pause ; but from that time 
 Harley always speak to Leonard in a soft voice , and often gaze on he 
 with earnest and kindly eye . 

 they reach a house in a central , though not fashionable street . a 
 man - servant of a singularly grave and awful aspect open the door , - a 
 man who have live all his life with author . poor fellow , he be indeed 
 prematurely old ! the care on his lip and the pomp on his brow - no 
 mortal 's pen can describe ! 

 " be Mr. Norreys at home ? " ask Harley . 

 " he be at home - to his friend , my Lord , " answer the man , 
 majestically ; and he stalk across the hall with the step of a Dangeau 
 usher some Montmorenci into the presence of Louis le Grand . 

 " stay ; show this gentleman into another room . I will go first into the 
 library ; wait for I , Leonard . " the man nod , and conduct Leonard 
 into the dining - room . then pause before the door of the library , and 
 listen an instant , as if fearful to disturb some mood of inspiration , 
 open it very softly . to his ineffable disgust , Harley push before , 
 and enter abruptly . it be a large room , line with book from the 
 floor to the ceiling . book be on all the table , book be on all 
 the chair . Harley seat himself on a folio of Raleigh 's " history of 
 the World , " and cry , " I have bring you a treasure ! " 

 " what be it ? " say Norreys , good - humouredly , look up from his desk . 

 " a mind ! " 

 " a mind ! " echo Norreys , vaguely . 

 " your own ? " 

 " Pooh ! I have none , - I have only a heart and a fancy . listen . you 
 remember the boy we see read at the book stall . I have catch he for 
 you , and you shall train he into a man . I have the warm interest in 
 his future , for I know some of his family , and one of that family be 
 very dear to I . as for money , he have not a shilling , and not a shilling 
 would he accept gratis from you or I either . but he come with bold 
 heart to work , - and work you must find he . " Harley then rapidly tell 
 his friend of the two offer he have make to Leonard , and Leonard 's 
 choice . 

 " this promise very well ; for letter a man must have a strong vocation , 
 as he should have for law . I will do all that you wish . " 

 Harley rise with alertness , shake Norreys cordially by the hand , hurry 
 out of the room , and return with Leonard . 

 Mr. Norreys eye the young man with attention . he be naturally rather 
 severe than cordial in his manner to stranger , - contrast in this , as 
 in most thing , the poor vagabond Burley ; but he be a good judge of the 
 human countenance , and he like Leonard 's . after a pause he hold out his 
 hand . 

 " Sir , " say he , " Lord L'Estrange tell I that you wish to enter 
 literature as a calling , and no doubt to study it as an art . I may help 
 you in this , and you meanwhile can help I . I want an amanuensis , - I 
 offer you that place . the salary will be proportion to the service 
 you will render I . I have a room in my house at your disposal . when 
 I first come up to London , I make the same choice that I hear you have 
 do . I have no cause , even in a worldly point of view , to repent my 
 choice . it give I an income large than my want . I trace my success 
 to these maxim , which be applicable to all profession : 1st , never 
 to trust to genius for what can be obtain by labour ; 2dly , never to 
 profess to teach what we have not study to understand ; 3dly , never to 
 engage our word to what we do not our good to execute . 

 " with these rule , literature - provide a man do not mistake his 
 vocation for it , and will , under good advice , go through the preliminary 
 discipline of natural power , which all vocation require - be as good a 
 call as any other . without they , a shoeblack 's be infinitely well . " 

 " possibly enough , " mutter Harley ; " but there have be great writer 
 who observe none of your maxim . " 

 " great writer , probably , but very unenviable man . my Lord , my Lord , 
 do not   corrupt the pupil you bring to I . " Harley smile , and take his 
 departure , and leave Genius at school with Common - sense and experience . 




 CHAPTER XX . 

 while Leonard Fairfield have be obscurely wrestle against poverty , 
 neglect , hunger , and dread temptation , bright have be the opening day 
 and smooth the upward path of Randal Leslie . certainly no young 
 man , able and ambitious , could enter life under fairer auspex ; the 
 connection and avow favourite of a popular and energetic statesman , 
 the brilliant writer of a political work that have lift he at once 
 into a station of his own , receive and court in those high 
 circle , to which neither rank nor fortune alone suffice for a familiar 
 passport , - the circle above fashion itself the circle of power , - with 
 every facility of augment information , and learn the world betime 
 through the talk of its acknowledge master , - randal have but to move 
 straight onward , and success be sure . but his tortuous spirit delight 
 in scheme and intrigue for their own sake . in scheme and intrigue he see 
 short path to fortune , if not to fame . 

 his besetting sin be also his besetting weakness . he do not 
 aspire , - he covet . though in a far high social position than Frank 
 Hazeldean , despite the worldly prospect of his old schoolfellow , he 
 covet the very thing that keep Frank Hazeldean below he , - covet 
 his idle gayetie , his careless pleasure , his very waste of youth . 
 thus , also , Randal less aspire to Audley Egerton 's repute than he 
 covet Audley Egerton 's wealth and pomp , his princely expenditure , and 
 his Castle Rackrent in Grosvenor Square . it be the misfortune of his 
 birth to be so near to both these fortune , - near to that of Leslie , as 
 the future head of that fallen House ; near even to that of Hazeldean , 
 since , as we have see before , if the squire have have no son , Randal 's 
 descent from the Hazeldeans suggest himself as the one on whom these 
 broad land should devolve . Most young man bring into intimate contact 
 with Audley Egerton would have feel for that personage a certain loyal 
 and admire , if not very affectionate , respect . for there be something 
 grand in Egerton , - something that command and fascinate the young . 
 his determined courage , his energetic will , his almost regal liberality , 
 contrast a simplicity in personal taste and habit that be almost 
 austere , his rare and seemingly unconscious power of charming even the 
 woman most weary of homage , and persuade even the man most obdurate 
 to counsel , - all serve to invest the practical man with those spell 
 which be usually confine to the ideal one . but , indeed , Audley 
 Egerton be an Ideal , - the ideal of the Practical . not the mere vulgar , 
 plodding , red - tape machine of petty business , but the man of strong 
 sense , inspire by inflexible energy and guide to definite earthly 
 object . in a dissolute and corrupt form of government , under a decrepit 
 monarchy or a vitiated republic , Audley Egerton might have be a most 
 dangerous citizen : for his ambition be so resolute , and his sight to 
 its end be so clear . but there be something in public life in England 
 which compel the really ambitious man to honour , unless his eye be 
 jaundice and oblique , like Randal Leslie 's . it be so necessary in 
 England to be a gentleman . and thus Egerton be emphatically consider 
 a gentleman . without the least pride in other matter , with little 
 apparent sensitiveness , touch he on the point of gentleman , and no one 
 so sensitive and so proud . as Randal see more of he , and watch his 
 mood with the lynx - eye of the household spy , he could perceive that 
 this hard mechanical man be subject to fit of melancholy , even of 
 gloom ; and though they do not last long , there be even in his habitual 
 coldness an evidence of something compress , latent , painful , lie 
 deep within his memory . this would have interest the kindly feeling 
 of a grateful heart ; but Randal detect and watch it only as a clew 
 to some secret it might profit he to gain . for Randal Leslie hat 
 Egerton ; and hat he the more because , with all his book - knowledge and 
 his conceit in his own talent , he could not despise his patron ; 
 because he have not yet succeed in make his patron the mere tool or 
 step - stone ; because he think that Egerton 's keen eye see through 
 his wily heart , even while , as if in profound disdain , the minister 
 help the protege . but this last suspicion be unsound . Egerton have not 
 detect Leslie 's corrupt and treacherous nature . he might have other 
 reason for keep he at a certain distance , but he inquire 
 too little into Randal 's feeling towards himself to question the 
 attachment , or doubt the sincerity , of one who owe to he so much . but 
 that which more than all embitter Randal 's feeling towards Egerton 
 be the careful and deliberate frankness with which the latter have , more 
 than once , repeat and enforce the odious announcement , that Randal 
 have nothing to expect from the minister 's WILL , nothing to expect from 
 that wealth which glare in the hungry eye of the pauper heir to 
 the Leslies of Rood . to whom , then , could Egerton mean to devise his 
 fortune ? to whom but Frank Hazeldean ? yet Audley take so little notice 
 of his nephew , seem so indifferent to he , that that supposition , 
 however natural , be expose to doubt . the astuteness of Randal be 
 perplexed . meanwhile , however , the less he himself could rely upon 
 Egerton for fortune , the more he revolve the possible chance of 
 oust Frank from the inheritance of Hazeldean , - in part , at least , if 
 not wholly . to one less scheming , crafty , and remorseless than randal 
 Leslie , such a project would have seem the wild delusion . but there 
 be something fearful in the manner in which this young man seek to 
 turn knowledge into power , and make the study of all weakness in other 
 subservient to his own end . he worm himself thoroughly into Frank 's

 thought and temper , and ponder over each word in the father 's letter ,

 eye of his friend . randal see that the squire have two characteristic , 
 which be very common amongst proprietor , and which might be invoke as 
 antagonist to his warm fatherly love . first , the squire be as fond of 
 his estate as if it be a living thing , and part of his own flesh and 
 blood ; and in his lecture to Frank upon the sin of extravagance , the

 if it fall into the hand of a spendthrift ? no man should make duck

 squire be not only fond of his land , but he be jealous of they , - that

 self - interest , as if to show that those be the last kind of appeal

 expectation . " 

 the minister and his protege be seat at breakfast , the first reading

 " I have think of place he under your care . I know you would provide
