 " I thank you from my heart ; the Audley of my boyhood speak now . but the

 " and that career be - " 

 " letter . " 

 " letter ! literature ! " exclaim the statesman . " Beggary ! no , no ,

 " no , she be of gentle blood , - a soldier 's daughter ; the daughter of that

 puzzle .

 CHAPTER XXI . 

 Randal Leslie , on leave Audley , repair to Frank 's lodging , and

 randal   smile . - " I be not likely to forget that ; it be the boast of

 SQUIRE   heartily . - " shake hand again on it , my boy . you do not   want a

 " well . " 

 " and then you suddenly come up to town . " 

 " well . " 

 " well . and Frank be ashamed to meet you . for , as you say , he have be

 and I promise you I will not say an angry word to Frank . I dare say , poor 
 boy , he be very much afflict , and I long to shake hand with he . so , 
 set his mind at ease . " 

 " ah , sir , " say Randal , with much apparent emotion , " your son may well 
 love you : and it seem to be a hard matter for so kind a heart as your 
 to preserve the proper firmness with he . " 

 " oh , I can be firm enough , " quoth the squire , - " especially when I do not 
 see he , - handsome dog that he be : very like his mother - do not   you think 
 so ? 

 " I never see his mother , sir . " 

 " ' Gad ! not see my Harry ? no more you have ; you must come and pay we a 
 visit . I suppose my half - brother will let you come ? " 

 " to be sure , sir . will you not call on he while you be in town ? " 

 " not i. he would think I expect to get something from the Government . 
 tell he the minister must go on a little well , if they want my vote 
 for their member . but go , I see you be impatient to tell Frank that all 
 's forgot and forgive . come and dine with he here at six , and let he 
 bring his bill in his pocket . oh , I sha'n't   scold he . " 

 " why , as to that , " say randal , smile , " I think   forgive I still 
 that you should not take it too easily ; just as I think that you have 
 well not blame he for his very natural and praiseworthy shame in 
 approach you , so I think , also , that you should do nothing that would 
 tend to diminish that shame , - it be such a check on he . and therefore , 
 if you can contrive to affect to be angry with he for his extravagance , 
 it will do good . " 

 " you speak like a book , and I will try my good . " 

 " if you threaten , for instance , to take he out of the army , and settle 
 he in the country , it would have a very good effect . " 

 " what ! would he think it so great a punishment to come home and live 
 with his parent ? " 

 " I do not   say that ; but he be naturally so fond of London . at his age , 
 and with his large inheritance , that be natural . " 

 " Inheritance ! " say the squire , moodily , - " inheritance ! he be not 
 think of that , I trust ? Zounds , sir , I have as good a life as his 
 own . Inheritance ! - to be sure the casino property be entail on he ;

 Hazeldean land to my ploughman , if I choose it . inheritance ; indeed ! "

 leave Limmer 's , Randal hasten to Frank 's room in St. James 's

 anger . but , indeed , I fear his kindness still more . what a reckless 
 hound I have be ! however , it shall be a lesson to I . and my debt

 " quite right , Frank . and , indeed , I be a little afraid that , when your

 the dinner at Limmer 's go off very differently from what it ought

 " my dear father - " 

 " hold your tongue , you puppy . oh , I dare say , if you be in my shoe ,

 " the wedge be in the tree , " say randal to himself , " and there be a gap

 CHAPTER XXII 

 Harley L'Estrange be seat beside Helen at the lattice - window in the 
 cottage at Norwood . the bloom of revive health be on the child 's face , 
 and she be listen with a smile , for Harley be speak of Leonard 
 with praise , and of Leonard 's future with hope . " and thus , " he 
 continue , ' secure from his former trial , happy in his occupation , and 
 pursue the career he have choose , we must be content , my dear child , to 
 leave he . " 

 " leave he ! " exclaim Helen , and the rise on her cheek fade . 

 Harley be not displeased to see her emotion . he would have be 
 disappointed in her heart if it have be less susceptible to affection . 

 " it be hard on you , Helen , " say he , " to be separate from one who have 
 be to you as a brother . do not hate I for do so . but I consider 
 myself your guardian , and your home as yet must be mine . we be go 
 from this land of cloud and mist , go as into the world of summer . 
 well , that do not content you . you weep , my child ; you mourn your 
 own friend , but do not forget your father 's . I be alone , and often sad , 
 Helen ; will you not comfort I ? you press my hand , but you must learn to 
 smile on I also . you be bear to be the comforter . comforter be not 
 egotist ; they be always cheerful when they console . " 

 the voice of Harley be so sweet and his word go so home to the 
 child 's heart , that she look up and smile in his face as he kiss 
 her ingenuous brow . but then she think of Leonard , and feel so 
 solitary , so bereft , that tear burst forth again . before these be 
 dry , Leonard himself enter , and , obey an irresistible impulse , 
 she spring to his arm , and lean her head on his shoulder , sob 
 out , 

 " I be go from you , brother ; do not grieve , do not miss I . " 

 Harley be much move : he fold his arm , and contemplate they both 
 silently , - and his own eye be moist . " this heart , " think he , " will 
 be worth the winning ! " 

 he draw aside Leonard , and whisper , " Soothe , but encourage and support 
 she . I leave you together ; come to I in the garden later . " 

 it be nearly an hour before Leonard join Harley . 

 " she be not weep when you leave she ? " ask L'Estrange . 

 " no ; she have more fortitude than we might suppose . Heaven know how that 
 fortitude have support mine . I have promise to write to she often . " 

 Harley take two stride across the lawn , and then , come back to 
 Leonard , say , " keep your promise , and write often for the first year . I 
 would then ask you to let the correspondence drop gradually . " 

 " drop ! ah , my Lord ! " 

 " look you , my young friend , I wish to lead this fair mind wholly from 
 the sorrow of the past . I wish Helen to enter , not abruptly , but 
 step by step , into a new life . you love each other now , as do two 
 child , - as brother and sister . but later , if encourage , would the 
 love be the same ? and be it not well for both of you that youth 
 should open upon the world with youth 's natural affection free and 
 unforestalled ? " 

 " true ! and she be so above I , " say Leonard , mournfully . 

 " no one be above he who succeed in your ambition , Leonard . it be not 
 that , believe I . " 

 Leonard shake his head . 

 " perhaps , " say Harley , with a smile , " I rather feel that you be above 
 I . for what vantage - ground be so high as youth ? perhaps I may become 
 jealous of you . it be well that she should learn to like one who be to 
 be henceforth her guardian and protector . yet how can she like I as she 
 ought , if her heart be to be full of you ? " 

 the boy bow his head ; and Harley hasten to change the subject , and 
 speak of letter and of glory . his word be eloquent and his voice 
 kindling ; for he have be an enthusiast for fame in his boyhood , and 
 in Leonard 's his own seem to he to revive . but the poet 's heart give 
 back no echo , - suddenly it seem void and desolate . yet when Leonard 
 walk back by the moonlight , he mutter to himself , " strange , strange , 
 so mere a child ! this can not be love ! still , what else to love be there 
 leave to I ? " 

 and so he pause upon the bridge where he have so often stand with Helen , 
 and on which he have find the protector that have give to she a home , 
 to himself a career . and life seem very long , and fame but a dreary 
 phantom . courage still , Leonard ! these be the sorrow of the heart that 
 teach thee more than all the precept of sage and critic . 

 another day , and Helen have leave the shore of England , with her fanciful 
 and dream guardian . year will pass before our tale re - open . life 
 in all the form we have see it travel on . and the squire farm and 
 hunt ; and the parson preache and chide and soothe ; and Riccabocca 
 read his Machiavelli , and sigh and smile as he moralize on Men and 
 States ; and Violante 's dark eye grow deep and more spiritual in 
 their lustre , and her beauty take thought from solitary dream . and Mr. 
 Richard Avenel have his house in London , and the Honourable Mrs. Avenel 
 her opera - box ; and hard and dire be their struggle into fashion , 
 and hotly do the new man , scorn the aristocracy , pant to become 
 aristocrat . and Audley Egerton go from the office to the parliament , 
 and drudge , and debate , and help to govern the empire in which the 
 sun never set . poor sun , how tired he must be - but not more tired than 
 the Government ! and Randal Leslie have an excellent place in the bureau 
 of a minister , and be look to the time when he shall resign it to 
 come into parliament , and on that large arena turn knowledge into power . 
 and meanwhile he be much where he be with Audley Egerton ; but he have 
 establish intimacy with the squire , and visit Hazeldean twice , and 
 examine the house and the map of the property , and very nearly fall 
 a second time into the ha - ha , and the squire believe that Randal Leslie 
 alone can keep Frank out of mischief , and have speak rough word to his 
 Harry about Frank 's continued extravagance . and Frank do continue to 
 pursue pleasure , and be very miserable , and horribly in debt . and 
 Madame di Negra have go from London to Paris , and take a tour into 
 Switzerland , and come back to London again , and have grow very intimate 
 with Randal Leslie ; and Randal have introduce Frank to she ; and Frank 
 think she the lovely woman in the world , and grossly slander by 
 certain evil tongue . and the brother of Madame di Negra be expect 
 in England at last ; and what with his repute for beauty and for wealth , 
 people anticipate a sensation . and Leonard , and Harley , and Helen ? 
 Patience , - they will all re - appear . 




 BOOK EIGHTH . 




 INITIAL chapter . 

 the ABUSE of INTELLECT . 

 there be at present so vehement a flourish of trumpet , and so 
 prodigious a roll of the drum , whenever we be call upon to throw up 
 our hat , and cry " Huzza " to the " March of Enlightenment , " that , out of 
 that very spirit of contradiction natural to all rational animal , 
 one be tempt to stop one 's ear , and say , " gently , gently ; light be 
 noiseless : how come ' Enlightenment ' to make such a clatter ? meanwhile , 
 if it be not impertinent , pray , where be Enlightenment march to ? " ask 
 that question of any six of the loud bawler in the procession , and 
 I will wager tenpence to California that you get six very unsatisfactory 
 answer . one respectable gentleman , who , to our great astonishment , 
 insist upon call himself " a slave , " but have a remarkably free way of 
 express his opinion , will reply , " Enlightenment be march towards 
 the seven point of the Charter . " another , with his hair a la jeune 
 France , who have take a fancy to his friend 's wife , and be rather 
 embarrassed with his own , assert that Enlightenment be proceed 
 towards the Rights of Women , the reign of Social Love , and the 
 annihilation of Tyrannical Prejudice . a third , who have the air of a man 
 well - to - do in the middle class , more modest in his hope , because he 
 neither wish to have his head break by his errand - boy , nor his 
 wife carry off to an Agapemone by his apprentice , do not take 
 Enlightenment a step far than a siege on Debrett , and a cannonade 
 on the Budget . illiberal man ! the march that he swell will soon trample 
 he under foot . no one fare so ill in a crowd as the man who be wedge 
 in the middle . a fourth , look wild and dreamy , as if he have come out 
 of the cave of Trophonius , and who be a mesmerizer and a mystic , think 
 Enlightenment be in full career towards the good old day of alchemist 
 and necromancer . a fifth , whom one might take for a Quaker , assert 
 that the march of Enlightenment be a crusade for universal philanthropy , 
 vegetable diet , and the perpetuation of peace by mean of speech , 
 which certainly do produce a very contrary effect from the Philippics of 
 demosthene ! the sixth - good fellow without a rag on his back - do not 
 care a straw where the march go . he can not   be bad off than he be ; and 
 it be quite immaterial to he whether he go to the dog - star above , 
 or the bottomless pit below . I say nothing , however , against the march , 
 while we take it altogether . whatever happen , one be in good company ; 
 and though I be somewhat indolent by nature , and would rather stay at 
 home with Locke and Burke   dull dog though they be   than have my 
 thought set off helter - skelter with those cursed trumpet and drum , 
 blow and dub - a - dub by fellow whom I vow to heaven I would not trust 
 with a five - pound note , - still , if I must march , I must ; and so deuce 
 take the hindmost ! but when it come to individual marcher upon their 
 own account , - privateer and condottieri of Enlightenment , - who have 
 fill their pocket with Lucifer match , and have a sublime contempt 
 for their neighbour 's barn and hay - rick , I do not   see why I should 
 throw myself into the seventh heaven of admiration and ecstasy . 

 if those who be eternally rhapsodize on the celestial blessing that 
 be to follow Enlightenment , Universal Knowledge , and so forth , would 
 just take their eye out of their pocket , and look about they , I 
 would respectfully inquire if they have never meet any very knowing and 
 enlighten gentleman , whose acquaintance be by no mean desirable . 
 if not , they be monstrous lucky . every man must judge by his own 
 experience ; and the bad rogue I have ever encounter be amazingly 
 well - inform clever fellow . from dunderhead and dunce we can protect 
 ourselves , but from your sharpwitte gentleman , all enlightenment and no 
 prejudice , we have but to cry , " Heaven defend we ! " it be true , that 
 the rogue   let he be ever so enlighten   usually come to no good 
 himself , - though not before he have do harm enough to his neighbour . 
 but that only show that the world want something else in those it 
 reward besides intelligence per se and in the abstract ; and be much too 
 old a world to allow any Jack Horner to pick out its plum for his own 
 personal gratification . hence a man of very moderate intelligence , who 
 believe in God , suffer his heart to beat with human sympathy , and 
 keep his eye off your strongbox , will perhaps gain a vast deal more 
 power than knowledge ever give to a rogue . 

 wherefore , though I anticipate an outcry against I on the part of the 
 blockhead , who , strange to say , be the most credulous idolator of 
 Enlightenment , and if knowledge be power , would rot on a dunghill , 
 yet , nevertheless , I think all really enlighten man will agree with 
 I , that when one fall in with detach sharpshooter from the general 
 March of Enlightenment , it be no reason that we should make ourselves 
 a target , because Enlightenment have furnish they with a gun . it have , 
 doubtless , be already remark by the judicious reader that of the 
 numerous character introduce into this work , the large portion belong 
 to that specie which we call the INTELLECTUAL , - that through they be 
 analyze and develop human intellect , in various form and direction . 
 so that this history , rightly consider , be a kind of humble familiar 
 Epic , or , if you prefer it , a long Serio - Comedy , upon the Varieties of 
 English Life in this our Century , set in movement by the intelligence 
 most prevalent . and where more ordinary and less refined type of the 
 species round and complete the survey of our pass generation , 
 they will often suggest , by contrast , the deficiency which mere 
 intellectual culture leaf in the human being . certainly , I have no 
 spite against intellect and enlightenment . Heaven forbid I should be 
 such a Goth ! I be only the advocate for common - sense and fair play . I 
 do not   think an able man necessarily an angel ; but I think if his heart 
 match his head , and both proceed in the Great March under the divine 
 Oriflamine , he go as near to the angel as humanity will permit : if 
 not , if he have but a penn'orth of heart to a pound of brain , I 
 say , " Bon jour , mon ange ! I see not the starry upward wing , but the 
 grovel cloven - hoof . " I would rather be obfuscate by the Squire of 
 Hazeldean than en lighten by Randal Leslie . every man to his taste . 
 but intellect itself   not in the philosophical but the ordinary sense of 
 the term   be rarely , if ever , one complete harmonious agency ; it be not 
 one faculty , but a compound of many , some of which be often at war with 
 each other , and mar the concord of the whole . few of we but have 
 some predominant faculty , in itself a strength ; but which , usurp 
 unseasonably dominion over the rest , share the lot of all tyranny , 
 however brilliant , and leave the empire weak against disaffection 
 within , and invasion from without . hence , intellect may be pervert 
 in a man of evil disposition , and sometimes merely waste in a man of 
 excellent impulse , for want of the necessary discipline , or of a strong 
 ruling motive . I doubt if there be one person in the world who have 
 obtain a high reputation for talent , who have not meet somebody much 
 cleverer than himself , which say somebody have never obtain any 
 reputation at all ! man like Audley Egerton be constantly see in the 
 great position of life ; while man like Harley L'Estrange , who could 
 have beat they hollow in anything equally strive for by both , float 
 away down the stream , and , unless some sudden stimulant arouse their 
 dreamy energy , vanish out of sight into silent graf . if Hamlet and 
 Polonius be live now , Polonius would have a much well chance of 
 be a Cabinet Minister , though Hamlet would unquestionably be a much 
 more intellectual character . what would become of Hamlet ? Heaven know ! 
 Dr. Arnold say , from his experience of a school , that the difference 
 between one man and another be not mere ability , - it be energy . there 
 be a great deal of truth in that saying . 

 submit these hint to the judgment and penetration of the sagacious , 
 I enter on the fresh division of this work , and see already randal 
 Leslie gnaw his lip on the background . the german poet observe that 
 the cow of Isis be to some the divine symbol of knowledge , to other but 
 the milch cow , only regard for the pound of butter she will yield . O 
 tendency of our age , to look on isis as the milch cow ! o prostitution 
 of the grand desire to the basest us ! Gaze on the goddess , randal 
 Leslie , and get ready thy churn and thy scale . let we see what the 
 butter will fetch in the market . 




 CHAPTER II . 

 a new reign have commence . there have be a general election ; the 
 unpopularity of the Administration have be apparent at the husting . 
 Audley Egerton , hitherto return by vast majority , have barely escape 
 defeat - thank to a majority of five . the expense of his election be 
 say to have be prodigious . " but who can stand against such wealth 
 as Egerton 's , - no doubt back , too , by the Treasury purse ? " say 
 the defeat candidate . it be towards the close of October ; London be 
 already full ; parliament will meet in less than a fortnight . 

 in one of the principal apartment of that hotel in which foreigner 
 may discover what be mean by English comfort , and the price which 
 foreigner must pay for it , there sit two person side by side , engage 
 in close conversation . the one be a female , in whose pale clear 
 complexion and raven hair , in whose eye , vivid with a power of 
 expression rarely bestow on the beauty of the North , we recognize 
 Beatrice , Marchesa di Negra . undeniably handsome as be the italian 
 lady , her companion , though a man , and far advanced into middle age , be 
 yet more remarkable for personal advantage . there be a strong family 
 likeness between the two ; but there be also a striking contrast in air , 
 manner , and all that stamp on the physiognomy the idiosyncrasy of 
 character . there be something of gravity , of earnestness and passion , 
 in Beatrice 's countenance when carefully examine ; her smile at time 
 might be false , but it be rarely ironical , never cynical . her gesture , 
 though graceful , be unrestrained and frequent . you could see she be a 
 daughter of the South . her companion , on the contrary , preserve on the 
 fair , smooth face , to which year have give scarcely a line or wrinkle , 
 something that might have pass , at first glance , for the levity and 
 thoughtlessness of a gay and youthful nature ; but the smile , though 
 exquisitely polish , take at time the derision of a sneer . in his 
 manner he be as compose and as free from gesture as an Englishman . 
 his hair be of that red brown with which the italian painter produce 
 such marvellous effect of colour ; and if here and there a silver thread 
 gleam through the lock , it be lose at once amidst their luxuriance . 
 his eye be light , and his complexion , though without much colour , 
 be singularly transparent . his beauty , indeed , would have be rather 
 womanly than masculine , but for the height and sinewy spareness of a 
 frame in which muscular strength be rather adorn than conceal by an 
 admirable elegance of proportion . you would never have guess this man 
 to be an italian ; more likely you would have suppose he a Parisian . 
 he converse in French , his dress be of french fashion , his mode of 
 thought seem french . not that he be like the Frenchman of the present 
 day , - an animal , either rude or reserve ; but your ideal of the marquis 
 of the old regime , the roue of the Regency . 

 italian , however , he be , and of a race renowned in italian history . 
 but , as if ashamed of his country and his birth , he affect to be 
 a citizen of the world . Heaven help the world if it hold only such 
 citizen ! 

 " but , Giulio , " say Beatrice di Negra , speak in Italian , " even 
 grant that you discover this girl , can you suppose that her father 
 will ever consent to your alliance ? surely you know too well the nature 
 of your kinsman ? " 

 " tu to trompe , ma soeur , " reply Giulio Franzini , Count di Peschiera , 
 in French as usual , - " tu to trompe ; I know it before he have go 
 through exile and penury . how can I know it now ? but comfort yourself , 
 my too anxious Beatrice , I shall not care for his consent , till I ' ve 
 make sure of his daughter 's . " 

 " but how win that in despite of the father ? " 

 " eh , mordieu ! " interrupt the count , with true french gayety ; " what 
 would become of all the comedy ever write , if marriage be not 
 make in despite of the father ? look you , " he resume , with a very 
 slight compression of his lip , and a still slighter movement in his 
 chair , - " look you , this be no question of if and but ! it be a question 
 of must and shall , - a question of existence to you and to I . when 
 Danton be condemn to the guillotine , he say , fling a pellet of 
 bread at the nose of his respectable judge , ' Mon individu sera bientot 
 dans le neant . ' my patrimony be there already ! I be load with debt . 
 I see before I , on the one side , ruin or suicide ; on the other side , 
 wedlock and wealth . " 

 " but from those vast possession which you have be permit to enjoy 
 so long , have you really save nothing against the time when they might 
 be reclaim at your hand ? " 

 " my sister , " reply the count , " do I look like a man who save ? 
 besides , when the Austrian Emperor , unwilling to raze from his Lombard 
 domain a name and a House so illustrious as our kinsman 's , and 
 desirous , while punish that kinsman 's rebellion , to reward my 
 adherence , forbore the peremptory confiscation of those vast possession 
 at which my mouth water while we speak , but , annex they to the crown 
 during pleasure , allow I , as the next of male kin , to retain the 
 revenue of one half for the same very indefinite period , - have I not 
 every reason to suppose that before long I could so influence his 
 Imperial Majesty , or his minister , as to obtain a decree that might 
 transfer the whole , unconditionally and absolutely , to myself ? and 
 methink I should have do so , but for this accursed , intermeddle 
 English Milord , who have never cease to besiege the court or the 
 minister with alleged extenuation of our cousin 's rebellion , and 
 proofless assertion that I share it in order to entangle my kinsman , 
 and betray it in order to profit by his spoil . so that , at last , in 
 return for all my service , and in answer to all my claim , I receive 
 from the minister himself this cold reply , Count of Peschiera , your aid 
 be important , and your reward have be large . that reward it would 
 not be for your honour to extend , and justify the ill opinion of your 
 italian countryman by formally appropriate to yourself all that be 
 forfeit by the treason you denounce . a name so noble as yours should 
 be dearer to you than fortune itself . ' " 

 " Ah Giulio , " cry Beatrice , her face light up , change in its whole 
 character , " those be word that might make the demon that tempt to 
 avarice fly from your breast in shame . " 

 the count open his eye in great amaze ; then he glance round the 
 room , and say quietly , 

 " nobody else hear you , my dear Beatrice ; talk commonsense . heroic 
 sound well in mixed society ; but there be nothing less suited to the 
 tone of a family conversation . " 

 Madame di Negra bend down her head abash , and that sudden change 
 in the expression of her countenance which have seem to betray 
 susceptibility to generous emotion , fade as suddenly away . 

 " but still , " she say coldly , " you enjoy one half of those ample 
 revenue : why talk , then , of suicide and ruin ? " 

 " I enjoy they at the pleasure of the crown ; and what if it be the 
 pleasure of the crown to recall our cousin , and reinstate he in his 
 possession ? " 

 " there be a probability , then , of that pardon ? when you first employ 
 I in your research you only think there be a possibility . " 

 " there be a great probability of it , and therefore I be here . I learn 
 some little time since that the question of such recall have be 
 suggest by the emperor , and discuss in Council . the danger to 
 the State , which might arise from our cousin 's wealth , his allege 
 ability , - ability ! bah ! and his popular name , defer any decision 
 on the point ; and , indeed , the difficulty of deal with myself must 
 have embarrass the minister . but it be a mere question of time . he 
 can not long remain exclude from the general amnesty already extend to 
 the other refugee . the person who give I this information be high in 
 power , and friendly to myself ; and he add a piece of advice on which 
 I act . ' it be intimate , ' say he , ' by one of the partisan of your 
 kinsman , that the exile could give a hostage for his loyalty in the 
 person of his daughter and heiress ; that she have arrive at marriageable 
 age ; that if she be to we d , with the emperor 's consent , some one whose 
 attachment to the austrian crown be unquestionable , there would be a 
 guarantee both for the faith of the father , and for the transmission of 
 so important a heritage to safe and loyal hand . why not '   continue 
 my friend   ' apply to the emperor for his consent to that alliance for 
 yourself , - you , on whom he can depend ; you who , if the daughter should 
 die , would be the legal heir to those land ? ' on that hint I speak . " 

 " you see the emperor ? " 

 " and after combat the unjust prepossession against I , I state that 
 so far from my cousin have any fair cause of resentment against 
 I , when all be duly explain to he , I do not doubt that he would 
 willingly give I the hand of his child . " 

 " you do ! " cry the marchesa , amazed . 

 " and , " continue the count , imperturbably , as he smooth , with careless 
 hand , the snowy plait of his shirt front , - " and that I should thus have 
 the happiness of become myself the guarantee of my kinsman 's loyalty , 
 the agent for the restoration of his honour , while , in the eye of the 
 envious and malignant , I should clear up my own name from all suspicion 
 that I have wrong he . " 

 " and the emperor consent ? " 

 " Pardieu , my dear sister , what else could his Majesty do ? my proposition 
 smooth every obstacle , and reconcile policy with mercy . it 
 remain , therefore , only to find out what have hitherto baffle all 
 our research , the retreat of our dear kinsfolk , and to make myself 
 a welcome lover to the demoiselle . there be some disparity of year , I 
 own ; but - unless your sex and my glass flatter I overmuch - I be still a 
 match for many a gallant of five - and - twenty . " 

 the count say this with so charming a smile , and look so 
 pre - eminently handsome , that he carry off the coxcombry of the word 
 as gracefully as if they have be speak by some dazzle hero of the 
 grand old comedy of parisian life . 

 then interlace his finger and lightly lean his hand , thus 
 clasp , upon his sister 's shoulder , he look into her face , and say 
 slowly , " and now , my sister , for some gentle but deserved reproach . 
 have you not sadly fail I in the task I impose on your regard for my 
 interest ? be it not some year since you first come to England on the 
 mission of discover these worthy relation of our ? do I not entreat 
 you to seduce into your toil the man whom I new to be my enemy , and who 
 be indubitably acquaint with our cousin 's retreat , - a secret he have 
 hitherto lock within his bosom ? do you not tell I , that though he 
 be then in England , you could find no occasion even to meet he , but 
 that you have obtain the friendship of the statesman to whom I direct 
 your attention , as his most intimate associate ? and yet you , whose 
 charm be usually so irresistible , learn nothing from the statesman , as 
 you see nothing of Milord . Nay , baffle and mislead , you actually suppose 
 that the quarry have take refuge in France . you go thither , you pretend 
 to search the capital , the province , Switzerland , que sais je ? all 
 in vain , - though - foi de gentilhomme - your police cost I dearly . you 
 return to England ; the same chase , and the same result . Palsambleu , ma 
 soeur , I do too much credit to your talent not to question your zeal . 
 in a word , have you be in earnest , - or have you not have some womanly 
 pleasure in amuse yourself and abuse my trust ? " 

 " Giulio , " answer Beatrice , sadly , " you know the influence you have 
 exercise over my character and my fate . your reproach be not just . I 
 make such inquiry as be in my power , and I have now cause to believe 
 that I know one who be possess of this secret , and can guide we to 
 it . " 

 " ah , you do ! " exclaim the count . Beatrice do not heed the 
 exclamation , and hurry on . 

 " but grant that my heart shrink from the task you impose on I , would 
 it not have be natural ? when I first come to England , you inform 
 I that your object in discover the exile be one which I could 
 honestly aid . you naturally wish first to know if the daughter live ; 
 if not , you be the heir . if she do , you assure I you desire to 
 effect , through my mediation , some liberal compromise with Alphonso , by 
 which you would have seek to obtain his restoration , provide he would 
 leave you for life in possession of the grant you hold from the crown . 
 while these be your object , I do my good , ineffectual as it be , to 
 obtain the information require . " 

 " and what make I lose so important , though so ineffectual an ally ? " 
 ask the count , still smile ; but a gleam that belie the smile shot 
 from his eye . 

 " what ! when you bade I receive and co - operate with the miserable 
 spy - the false Italians - whom you send over , and seek to entangle this 
 poor exile , when find , in some rash correspondence to be reveal 
 to the court ; when you seek to seduce the daughter of the Count of 
 Peschiera , the descendant of those who have rule in Italy , into 
 the informer , the corrupter , and the traitress , - no , Giulio , then I 
 recoil ; and then , fearful of your own sway over I , I retreat into 
 France . I have answer you frankly . " 

 the count remove his hand from the shoulder on which they have recline 
 so cordially . 

 " and this , " say he , " be your wisdom , and this your gratitude ! you , 
 whose fortune be bind up in mine ; you , who subsist on my bounty ; you , 
 who - " 

 " hold , " cry the marchesa , rise , and with a burst of emotion , as 
 if sting to the utmost , and break into revolt from the tyranny of 
 year , - " hold ! Gratitude ! bounty ! Brother , brother ! what , indeed , do I 
 owe to you ? the shame and the misery of a life . while yet a child , you 
 condemn I to marry against my will , against my heart , against my 
 prayer , - and laugh at my tear when I kneel to you for mercy . I be 
 pure then , Giulio , - pure and innocent as the flower in my virgin crown . 
 and now - now - " 

 Beatrice stop abruptly , and clasp her hand before her face . 

 " now you upbraid I , " say the count , unruffled by her sudden passion , 
 " because I give you in marriage to a man young and noble ? " 

 " old in vice , and mean of soul ! the marriage I forgave you . you have the 
 right , accord to the custom of our country , to dispose of my hand . 
 but I forgave you not the consolation that you whisper in the ear of 
 a wretched and insulted wife . " 

 " pardon I the remark , " reply the count , with a courtly bend of his 
 head , " but those consolation be also conformable to the custom of 
 our country , and I be not aware till now that you have wholly disdain 
 they . and , " continue the count , " you be not so long a wife that the 
 gall of the chain should smart still . you be soon leave a widow , - free , 
 childless , young , beautiful . " 

 " and penniless . " 

 " true , Di Negra be a gambler , and very unlucky ; no fault of mine . I 
 could neither keep the card from his hand , nor advise he how to play 
 they . " 

 " and my own portion ? o Giulio , I know but at his death why you have 
 condemn I to that renegade Genoese . he owe you money , and , against 
 honour , and I believe against law , you have accept my fortune in 
 discharge of the debt . " 

 " he have no other way to discharge it ; a debt of honour must be 
 pay , - old story these . what matter ? since then my purse have be 
 open to you . " 

 " yes , not as your sister , but your instrument , your spy ! yes , your purse 
 have be open - with a niggard hand . " 

 " Un peu de conscience , ma chere , - you be so extravagant . but come , be 
 plain . what would you ? " 

 " I would be free from you . " 

 " that be , you would form some second marriage with one of these rich 
 island lord . Ma foi , I respect your ambition . " 

 " it be not so high . I aim but to escape from slavery , - to be place 
 beyond dishonourable temptation . I desire , " cry Beatrice , with 
 increase emotion , - " I desire to re - enter the life of woman . " 

 " Eno ' ! " say the count , with a visible impatience ; " be there anything 
 in the attainment of your object that should render you indifferent to 
 mine ? you desire to marry , if I comprehend you right . and to marry as 
 become you , you should bring to your husband not debt , but a dowry . 
 be it so . I will restore the portion that I save from the spendthrift 
 clutch of the Genoese , - the moment that it be mine to bestow , the moment 
 that I be husband to my kinsman 's heiress . and now , Beatrice , you imply 
 that my former notion revolt your conscience ; my present plan should 
 content it , for by this marriage shall our kinsman regain his country , 
 and repossess , at least , half his land . and if I be not an excellent 
 husband to the demoiselle , it will be her own fault . I have sow my wild 
 oat . Je suis bon prince , when I have thing a little my own way . it 
 be my hope and my intention , and certainly it will be my interest , 
 to become digne epoux et irreprochable pere de famille . I speak 
 lightly , - ' t be my way . I mean seriously . the little girl will be very 
 happy with I , and I shall succeed in soothe all resentment her father 
 may retain . will you aid I then , yes or no ? aid I , and you shall 
 indeed be free . the magician will release the fair spirit he have bind 
 to his will . aid I not , ma chere , and mark , I do not threaten - I do but 
 warn - aid I not ; grant that I become a beggar , and ask yourself what 
 be to become of you , - still young , still beautiful , and still penniless ? 
 Nay , bad than penniless ; you have do I the honour , " and here the 
 count , look on the table , draw a letter from a portfolio emblazon 
 with his arm and coronet , - " you have do I the honour to consult I 
 as to your debt . " 

 " you will restore my fortune ? " say the marchesa , irresolutely , - and 
 avert her head from an odious schedule of figure . 

 " when my own , with your aid , be secure . " 

 " but do you not overrate the value of my aid ? " 

 " possibly , " say the count , with a caress suavity - and he kiss his 
 sister 's forehead . " possibly ; but , by my honour , I wish to repair to you 
 any wrong , real or suppose , I may have do you in past time . I wish 
 to find again my own dear sister . I may over - value your aid , but not the 
 affection from which it come . let we be friend , cara Beatrice mia , " 
 add the count , for the first time employ italian word . 

 the marchesa lay her head on his shoulder , and her tear flow 
 softly . evidently this man have great influence over she , - and evidently , 
 whatever her cause for complaint , her affection for he be still 
 sisterly and strong . a nature with fine flash of generosity , spirit , 
 honour , and passion be her ; but uncultured , unguided , spoilt by the 
 bad social example , easily lead into wrong , not always aware where the 
 wrong be , let affection good or bad whisper away her conscience or 
 blind her reason . such woman be often far more dangerous when induce 
 to wrong than those who be thoroughly abandon , - such woman be the 
 accomplice man like the Count of Peschiera most desire to obtain . 

 " ah , Giulio , " say Beatrice , after a pause , and look up at he 
 through her tear , " when you speak to I thus , you know you can do with 
 I what you will . fatherless and motherless , whom have my childhood to 
 love and obey but you ? " 

 " Dear Beatrice , " murmur the count , tenderly , and he again kiss she 
 forehead . " so , " he continue , more carelessly , - ' so the reconciliation 
 be effect , and our interest and our heart re - allied . now , alas ! to 
 descend to business . you say that you know some one whom you believe 
 to be acquaint with the lurking - place of my father - in - law - that be to 
 be ! " 

 " I think so . you remind I that I have an appointment with he this day : 
 it be near the hour , - I must leave you . " 

 " to learn the secret ? - quick , quick . I have no fear of your success , if 
 it be by his heart that you lead he ! " 

 " you mistake ; on his heart I have no hold . but he have a friend who love 
 I , and honourably , and whose cause he plead . I think here that I have 
 some mean to control or persuade he . if not - ah , he be of a character 
 that perplex I in all but his worldly ambition ; and how can we 
 foreigner influence he through that ? " 

 " be he poor , or be he extravagant ? " 

 " not extravagant , and not positively poor , but dependent . " 

 " then we have he , " say the count , composedly . " if his assistance be 
 worth buy , we can bid high for it . sur mon ame , I never yet know 
 money fail with any man who be both worldly and dependent . I put he 
 and myself in your hand . " 

 thus say , the count open the door , and conduct his sister with 
 formal politeness to her carriage . he then return , reseat himself , 
 and muse in silence . as he do so , the muscle of his countenance 
 relaxed . the levity of the Frenchman flee from his visage , and in his 
 eye , as it gaze abstractedly into space , there be that steady depth 
 so remarkable in the old portrait of Florentine diplomatist or venetian 
 oligarch . thus see , there be in that face , despite all its 
 beauty , something that would have awe back even the fond gaze of 
 love , - something hard , collect , inscrutable , remorseless . but this 
 change of countenance do not last long . evidently think , though 
 intense for the moment , be not habitual to the man ; evidently he have 
 live the life which take all thing lightly , - so he rise with a look 
 of fatigue , shake and stretch himself , as if to cast off , or grow 
 out of , an unwelcome and irksome mood . an hour afterwards , the Count of 
 Peschiera be charming all eye , and please all ear , in the saloon of 
 a high - bear beauty , whose acquaintance he have make at Vienna , and whose 
 charm , accord to that old and never - truth - speak oracle , Polite 
 scandal , be now say to have attract to London the brilliant 
 foreigner . 




 CHAPTER III . 

 the marehesa regain her house , which be in Curzon Street , and 
 withdraw to her own room , to readjust her dress , and remove from her 
 countenance all trace of the tear she have shed . 

 half an hour afterwards she be seat in her drawing - room , compose and 
 calm ; nor , see she then , could you have guess that she be capable 
 of so much emotion and so much weakness . in that stately exterior , in 
 that quiet attitude , in that elaborate and finished elegance which come 
 alike from the art of the toilet and the conventional repose of rank , 
 you could see but the woman of the world and the great lady . 

 a knock at the door be hear , and in a few moment there enter a 
 visitor , with the easy familiarity of intimate acquaintance , - a young 
 man , but with none of the bloom of youth . his hair , fine as a woman 's , 
 be thin and scanty , but it fall low over the forehead , and conceal 
 that noblest of our human feature . " a gentleman , " say Apuleius , " ought 
 to wear his whole mind on his forehead . " the young visitor would never 
 have commit so frank an imprudence . his cheek be pale , and in his 
 step and his movement there be a languor that speak of fatigued nerve 
 or delicate health . but the light of the eye and the tone of the voice 
 be those of a mental temperament control the bodily , - vigorous and 
 energetic . for the rest , his general appearance be distinguish by 
 a refinement alike intellectual and social . once see , you would not 
 easily forget he ; and the reader , no doubt , already recognize 
 Randal Leslie . his salutation , as I before say , be that of intimate 
 familiarity ; yet it be give and reply to with that unreserved 
 openness which denote the absence of a more tender sentiment . 

 seat himself by the marchesa 's side , Randal begin first to converse 
 on the fashionable topic and gossip of the day ; but it be observable 
 that while he extract from she the current anecdote and scandal of the 
 great world , neither anecdote nor scandal do he communicate in return . 
 Randal Leslie have already learn the art not to commit himself , nor to 
 have quote against he one ill - natured remark upon the eminent . nothing 
 more injure the man who would rise beyond the fame of the salon 
 than to be consider backbiter and gossip ; " yet it be always useful , " 
 think Randal Leslie , " to know the foible , the small social and 
 private spring , by which the great be move . critical occasion 
 may arise in which such a knowledge may be power . " and hence , perhaps 
  besides a more private motive , soon to be perceive , Randal do 
 not consider his time throw away in cultivate Madame di Negra 's 
 friendship . for , despite much that be whisper against she , she have 
 succeed in dispel the coldness with which she have at first be 
 receive in the London circle . her beauty , her grace , and her high 
 birth have raise she into fashion , and the homage of man of the first 
 station , while it perhaps injure her reputation as woman , add to her 
 celebrity as fine lady . so much do we cold English , prude though we be , 
 forgive to the foreigner what we avenge on the native . 

 slide at last from these general topic into very well - breed and 
 elegant personal compliment , and recite various eulogy , which Lord 
 this and the Duke of that have pass on the marchesa 's charm , Randal 
 lay his hand on her , with the license of admit friendship , and 
 say , 

 " but since you have deign to confide in I , since when   happily for 
 I , and with a generosity of which no coquette could have be capable 
 you , in good time , repress into friendship feeling that might else 
 have ripen into those you be form to inspire and disdain to return , 
 you tell I with your charming smile , ' let no one speak to I of love 
 who do not offer I his hand , and with it the mean to supply taste 
 that I fear be terribly extravagant , ' - since thus you allow I to 
 divine your natural object , and upon that understand our intimacy 
 have be found , you will pardon I for say that the admiration you 
 excite amongst these grand seigneur I have name only serf to defeat 
 your own purpose , and scare away admirer less brilliant , but more in 
 earnest . Most of these gentleman be unfortunately marry ; and they 
 who be not belong to those member of our aristocracy who , in marriage , 
 seek more than beauty and wit , - namely , connection to strengthen their 
 political station , or wealth to redeem a mortgage and sustain a title . " 

 " my dear Mr. Leslie , " reply the marchesa , - and a certain sadness might 
 be detect in the tone of the voice and the droop of the eye , - " I have 
 live long enough in the real world to appreciate the baseness and the 
 falsehood of most of those sentiment which take the noble name . I 
 see through the heart of the admirer you parade before I , and know 
 that not one of they would shelter with his ermine the woman to whom he 
 talk of his heart . ah , " continue Beatrice , with a softness of which 
 she be unconscious , but which might have be extremely dangerous to 
 youth less steel and self - guard than be Randal Leslie 's , - " ah , I 
 be less ambitious than you suppose . I have dream of a friend , a 
 companion , a protector , with feeling still fresh , undebase by the low 
 round of vulgar dissipation and mean pleasure , - of a heart so new , that 
 it might restore my own to what it be in its happy spring . I have see 
 in your country some marriage , the mere contemplation of which have 
 fill my eye with delicious tear . I have learn in England to know 
 the value of home . and with such a heart as I describe , and such a home , 
 I could forget that I ever know a less pure ambition . " 

 " this language do not surprise I , " say randal ; " yet it do not 
 harmonize with your former answer to I . " 

 " to you , " repeat Beatrice , smile , and regain her light manner ; 
 " to you , - true . but I never have the vanity to think that your affection 
 for I could bear the sacrifice it would cost you in marriage ; that 
 you , with your ambition , could bind your dream of happiness to home . 
 and then , too , " say she , raise her head , and with a certain grave 
 pride in her air , - " and then , I could not have consent to share my 
 fate with one whom my poverty would cripple . I could not listen to my 
 heart , if it have beat for a lover without fortune , for to he I could 
 then have bring but a burden , and betray he into a union with 
 poverty and debt . now , it may be different . now I may have the dowry 
 that befit my birth . and now I may be free to choose accord to my 
 heart as woman , not accord to my necessity , as one poor , harass , 
 and despairing . " 

 " ah , " say Randal , interested , and draw still close towards his fair 
 companion , - " ah , I congratulate you sincerely ; you have cause , then , to 
 think that you shall be - rich ? " 

 the marchesa pause before she answer , and during that pause Randal 
 relax the web of the scheme which he have be secretly weave , and 
 rapidly consider whether , if Beatrice di Negra would indeed be rich , 
 she might answer to himself as a wife ; and in what way , if so , he have 
 well change his tone from that of friendship into that of love . while 
 thus reflect , Beatrice answer , 

 " not rich for an Englishwoman ; for an italian , yes . my fortune should be 
 half a million - " 

 " half a million ! " cry Randal , and with difficulty he restrain 
 himself from fall at her foot in adoration . " of franc ! " continue 
 the marchesa . 

 " franc ! ah , " say Randal , with a long - draw breath , and recover from 
 his sudden enthusiasm , " about L20,000 ? eight hundred a year at four per 
 cent . a very handsome portion , certainly   Genteel poverty ! " he murmur 
 to himself . " what an escape I have have ! but I see - I see . this will 
 smooth all difficulty in the way of my well and early project . I 
 see , - a very handsome portion , " he repeat aloud , - " not for a grand 
 seigneur , indeed , but still for a gentleman of birth and expectation 
 worthy of your choice , if ambition be not your first object . ah , while 
 you speak with such endear eloquence of feeling that be fresh , of 
 a heart that be new , of the happy English home , you might guess that 
 my thought run to my friend who love you so devotedly , and who so 
 realize your ideal . proverbially , with we , happy marriage and happy 
 home be find not in the gay circle of London fashion , but at the 
 hearth of our rural nobility , our untitled country gentleman . and who , 
 amongst all your adorer , can offer you a lot so really enviable as the 
 one whom , I see by your blush , you already guess that I refer to ? " 

 " do I blush ? " say the marchesa , with a silvery laugh . " Nay , I think 
 that your zeal for your friend mislead you . but I will own frankly , I 
 have be touch by his honest ingenuous love , - so evident , yet rather

 the marchesa lean her cheek on her hand in silence . to she , marriage

 the advocate see that he have make an impression , and with the marvellous

 truly and sincerely my friend ? " 

 " can you doubt it ? " 

 " I prove that I do not , for I ask your assistance . "

 " listen ; my brother have arrive in London - " 

 " I see that arrival announce in the paper . "

 CHAPTER IV . 

 with his hand behind he , and his head droop on his breast , slow ,

 the Italian 's house . across the scheme he have before revolve , there 
 glance another yet more glittering , for its gain might be more sure and 
 immediate . if the exile 's daughter be heiress to such wealth , might he 
 himself hope - he stop short even in his own soliloquy , and his breath 
 come quick . now , in his last visit to Hazeldean , he have come in contact 
 with Riccabocca , and be strike by the beauty of Violante . a vague

 the marchesa be in search , and the suspicion have be confirm by 
 Beatrice 's description of the refugee she desire to discover . but as 
 he have not then learn the reason for her inquiry , nor conceive the

 " because you know that Mr. Hazeldean be a relation of mine , - that my

 " Hum . be the squire not on good term with his parson ? any quarrel about 
 tithe ? "

 and praise you very much , sir . "

 " that your heart be as sound as your head ; that he have once see you 
 about some old parishioner of his , and that he have be much impressed

 world , and a statesman . " 

 " oh , that be all ; some affair when I be member for Lansmere ? " 

 " I suppose so . " 

 here the conversation have break off ; but the next time Randal be lead 
 to visit the squire he have formally ask Egerton 's consent , who , after 
 a moment 's hesitation , have as formally reply , " I have no objection . " 

 on return from this visit , Randal mention that he have see 
 Riccabocca : and Egerton , a little startled at first , say composedly ,

 di Negra on his track . remember , she be suspect of be a spy of the 
 austrian government . "

 scarcely be the person she seek to discover . "

 gentleman , and make not a step towards the secret of another . "

 " my card , " say randal to himself , as with a deep - draw sigh he resume

 " well ; besides seven large chest of the bad cigar you ever smoke ,

 RANDAL   solemnly . - " Hum ! " 

 FRANK . - " what ? do not   you think it would be the good way ? I never

 coldness ; " and do not   blame I if I believe you . "

 " at my poor father 's death ? oh , no , no ! I can not bear the idea of this 
 cold - blooded calculation on a father 's death . I know it be not uncommon ;

 " but even if she , who be so accomplished , so admired , even if she would

 Frank shake his head . " I do not   think the Governor would care a straw

 " I love she the more , " say young Hazeldean , raise his front with

 slander her name , - because I believe she to be pure and wrong . but

 " my dear , dear Randal , how can I thank you ? if ever a poor fellow like

 " ah ! " say Frank , with a groan , " that be because your mind be 
 cultivate , - you have so many resource ; and all my fault have come 
 from idleness . if I have have anything to do on a rainy day , I should 
 never have get into these scrape . " 

 " oh , you will have enough to do some day manage your property . we who 
 have no property must find one in knowledge . Adieu , my dear Frank , I 
 must go home now . by the way , you have never , by chance , speak of the 
 Riccaboccas to Madame di Negra . " 

 " the Riccaboccas ? no . that be well think of . it may interest she to 
 know that a relation of mine have marry her countryman . very odd that 
 I never do mention it ; but , to say truth , I really do talk so little to 
 she : she be so superior , and I feel positively shy with she . " 

 " do I the favour , Frank , " say Randal , wait patiently till this 
 reply end , - for he be devise all the time what reason to give for 
 his request , - " never to allude to the Riccaboccas either to she or to 
 her brother , to whom you be sure to be present . " 

 " why not allude to they ? " 

 Randal hesitate a moment . his invention be still at fault , and , for a 
 wonder , he think it the good policy to go pretty near the truth . 

 " why , I will tell you . the marchesa conceal nothing from her brother , 
 and he be one of the few Italians who be in high favour with the 
 austrian court . " 

 " well ! " 

 " and I suspect that poor Dr. Riccabocca flee his country from some mad 
 experiment at revolution , and be still hide from the austrian police . " 

 " but they can not   hurt he here , " say Frank , with an Englishman 's dog 
 inborn conviction of the sanctity of his native island . " I should like 
 to see an austrian pretend to dictate to we whom to receive and whom to 
 reject . " 

 " Hum - that be true and constitutional , no doubt ; but Riccabocca may have 
 excellent reason - and , to speak plainly , I know he have   perhaps as 
 affect the safety of friend in Italy   - for preserve his incognito , 
 and we be bind to respect those reason without inquire far . " 

 " still I can not think so meanly of Madame di Negra , " persist Frank 
  shrewd here , though credulous elsewhere , and both from his sense of 
 honour , " as to suppose that she would descend to be a spy , and injure 
 a poor countryman of her own , who trust to the same hospitality she 
 receive herself at our english hand . oh , if I think that , I could 
 not love she ! " add Frank , with energy . 

 " certainly you be right . but see in what a false position you would 
 place both her brother and herself . if they know Riccabocca 's secret , 
 and proclaim it to the Austrian Government , as you say , it would be 
 cruel and mean ; but if they know it and conceal it , it might involve 
 they both in the most serious consequence . you know the austrian policy 
 be proverbially so jealous and tyrannical ? " 

 " well , the newspaper say so , certainly . " 

 " and , in short , your discretion can do no harm , and your indiscretion 
 may . therefore , give I your word , Frank . I can not   stay to argue now . " 

 " I will not allude to the Riccaboccas , upon my honour , " answer Frank ; 
 ' still , I be sure that they would be as safe with the marchesa as 
 with - " 

 " I rely on your honour , " interrupt randal , hastily , and hurry off . 




 CHAPTER V. 

 towards the evening of the follow day , Randal Leslie walk slowly 
 from a village in the main road   about two mile from Rood Hall , 
 at which he have get out of the coach . he pass through mead and 
 cornfield , and by the skirt of wood which have formerly belong to 
 his ancestor , but have be long since alienate . he be alone amidst 
 the haunt of his boyhood , the scene in which he have first invoke the 
 grand Spirit of Knowledge , to bid the Celestial still one minister to 
 the command of an earthly and turbulent ambition . he pause often in 
 his path , especially when the undulation of the ground give a glimpse 
 of the gray church tower , or the gloomy fir that rise above the 
 desolate waste of Rood . 

 " here , " think Randal , with a soften eye , - " here , how often , 
 compare the fertility of the land pass away from the inheritance 
 of my father , with the forlorn wild that be leave to their mouldering 
 Hall , - here how often have I say to myself , ' I will rebuild the 
 fortune of my House . ' and straightway Toil lose its aspect of drudge , 
 and grow kingly , and book become as live army to serve my thought . 
 again - again o thou haughty Past , brace and strengthen I in the battle 
 with the future . " his pale lip writhe as he soliloquize , for his 
 conscience speak to he while he thus address his will , and its voice 
 be hear more audibly in the quiet of the rural landscape , than amidst 
 the turmoil and din of that armed and sleepless camp which we call a 
 city . 

 Doubtless , though Ambition have object more vast and beneficent than 
 the restoration of a name , that in itself be high and chivalrous , and 
 appeal to a strong interest in the human heart . but all emotion and 
 all end of a nobler character have seem to filter themselves free 
 from every golden grain in pass through the mechanism of Randal 's 
 intellect , and come forth at last into egotism clear and unalloyed . 
 nevertheless , it be a strange truth that , to a man of cultivate mind , 
 however perverted and vicious , there be vouchsafed gleam of bright 
 sentiment , irregular perception of moral beauty , deny to the brutal 
 unreasoning wickedness of uneducated villany , - which perhaps ultimately 
 serve as his punishment , accord to the old thought of the satirist , 
 that there be no great curse than to perceive virtue yet adopt 
 vice . and as the solitary schemer walk slowly on , and his 
 childhood - innocent at least indeed - come distinct before he through 
 the halo of bygone dream , - dream far pure than those from which he 
 now rise each morning to the active world of Man , - a profound melancholy 
 creep over he , and suddenly he exclaim aloud , " then I aspire to be 
 renowned and great ; now , how be it that , so advanced in my career , all 
 that seem lofty in the end have vanish from I , and the only mean 
 that I contemplate be those which my childhood would have call poor 
 and vile ? ah , be it that I then read but book , and now my knowledge have 
 pass onward , and man contaminate more than book ? but , " he continue , 
 in a low voice , as if argue with himself , " if power be only so to be 
 win , - and of what use be knowledge if it be not power - do not success 
 in life justify all thing ? and who prize the wise man if he fail ? " he 
 continue his way , but still the soft tranquillity around rebuke he , 
 and still his reason be dissatisfy , as well as his conscience . there 
 be time when nature , like a bath of youth , seem to restore to the 
 jade soul its freshness , - time from which some man have emerge , as if 
 reborn . the crisis of life be very silent . suddenly the scene open on 
 Randal Leslie 's eye , - the bare desert common , the dilapidated church , 
 the old house , partially see in the dank dreary hollow , into which it 
 seem to randal to have sink deeply and lowly than when he see 
 it last . and on the common be some young man play at hockey . that 
 old - fashioned game , now very uncommon in England , except at school , be 
 still preserve in the primitive vicinity of Rood by the young yeoman 
 and farmer . randal stand by the stile and look on , for among the 
 player he recognize his brother Oliver . presently the ball be strike 
 towards Oliver , and the group instantly gather round that young 
 gentleman , and snatch he from Randal 's eye ; but the eld brother 
 hear a displeasing din , a derisive laughter . Oliver have shrink from the 
 danger of the thick clubbed stick that ply around he , and receive

 by shout of , " go to your mammy . that be Noll Leslie all over . butter 
 shin ! " 

 Randal 's sallow face become scarlet . " the jest of boor - a Leslie ! " he 
 mutter , and grind his tooth . he spring over the stile , and 
 walk erect and haughtily across the ground . the player cry out 
 indignantly . randal raise his hat , and they recognize he , and stop

 and then steal a timid glance towards Randal 's severe and moody 
 countenance . 

 " you be not angry that I be play at hockey with our neighbour , " 
 say he , deprecatingly , observe that Randal would not break the 
 silence . 

 " no , " reply the eld brother ; " but in associate with his inferior , 
 a gentleman still know how to maintain his dignity . there be no harm 
 in play with inferior , but it be necessary to a gentleman to play so 
 that he be not the laugh - stock of clown . " 

 Oliver hang his head , and make no answer . they come into the slovenly 
 precinct of the court , and the pig star at they from the paling , as 
 their progenitor have star , year before , at Frank Hazeldean .

 Randal 's sister , her hair still and forever hang about her ear , be

 fidget and complaint . 

 somehow or other , as the young heir to all this helpless poverty stand

 leave solely to the egotism of his knowledge and his ambition , in such

 take so little nutriment from the heart , and how that affection and

 " ha , Randal , boy , " say Mr. Leslie , look up lazily , " how d ' ye do ?

 want dinner , or supper , or something . " but , in the mean while ,

 " I do not expect you , dear Randal ; you always come so suddenly , and

 here Mrs. Leslie , in a state of intense excitement - have rush

 randal seat himself on the broken pale . " take care they do not   come

 " reading ! those trashy novel ! "

 else have do so ? " 

 then Mrs. Leslie go into a minute and most irritate catalogue of

 a petty provincial family , with much pretension and small power , - of all 
 people , indeed , without the disposition to please - without the ability

 randal spring from the pal , a movement which frighten the

 such be the hearth which warm the viper that nestle and gnaw at

 CHAPTER VI . 

 when the rest of the household be in deep sleep , Randal stand long at 
 his open window , look over the dreary , comfortless scene , - the moon 
 gleam from sky half - autumnal , half - wintry , upon squalid decay , 
 through the ragged fissure of the fir ; and when he lie down to rest , 
 his sleep be feverish , and trouble by turbulent dream . 

 however , he be up early , and with an unwonted colour in his cheek , 
 which his sister ascribe to the country air . after breakfast , he take 
 his way towards Hazeldean , mount upon a tolerable , horse , which he 
 borrow of a neighbouring farmer who occasionally hunt . before noon , 
 the garden and ter race of the Casino come in sight . he rein in his 
 horse , and by the little fountain at which Leonard have be will not to eat 
 his radish and con his book , he see Riccabocca seat under the shade 
 of the red umbrella . and by the Italian 's side stand a form that a Greek 
 of old might have deem the Naiad of the Fount ; for in its youthful 
 beauty there be something so full of poetry , something at once so sweet 
 and so stately , that it speak to the imagination while it charm the 
 sense . 

 Randal dismount , tie his horse to the gate , and , walk down a 
 trellis alley , come suddenly to the spot . his dark shadow fall over 
 the clear mirror of the fountain just as Riccabocca have say , " all here 
 be so secure from evil ! - the wave of the fountain be never trouble 
 like those of the river ! " and Violante have answer in her soft native 
 tongue , and lift her dark , spiritual eye , " but the fountain would 
 be but a lifeless pool , oh my father , if the spray do not mount towards 
 the sky ! " 




 CHAPTER VII . 

 RANDAL advanced - " I fear , Signor Riccabocca , that I be guilty of some 
 want of ceremony . " 

 " to dispense with ceremony be the most delicate mode of confer a 
 compliment , " reply the urbane Italian , as he recover from his first 
 surprise at Randal 's sudden address , and extend his hand . 

 Violante bow her graceful head to the young man 's respectful 
 salutation . " I be on my way to Hazeldean , " resume Randal , " and , see 
 you in the garden , could not resist this intrusion . " 

 RICCOBOCCA . - " you come from London ? stir time for you English , but 
 I do not ask you the news . no news can affect we . " 

 RANDAL   softly . - " perhaps yes . " 

 riccabocca   startle . - " how ? " 

 VIOLANTE . - " surely he speak of Italy , and news from that country 
 affect you still , my father . " 

 RICCABOCCA . - " Nay , nay , nothing affect I like this country ; its east 
 wind might affect a pyramid ! draw your mantle round you , child , and go 
 in ; the air have suddenly grow chill . " 

 Violante smile on her father , glance uneasily towards Randal 's grave 
 brow , and go slowly towards the house . Riccabocca , after wait some 
 moment in silence , as if expect Randal to speak , say , with affected 
 carelessness , 

 " so you think that you have news that might affect I ? Corpo di Bacco ! I 
 be curious to learn what ? " 

 " I may be mistake - that depend on your answer to one question . do you 
 know the Count of Peschiera ? " 

 Riccabocca wince , and turn pale . he could not baffle the watchful eye 
 of the questioner . 

 " Enough , " say randal ; " I see that I be right . believe in my sincerity . 
 I speak but to warn and to serve you . the count seek to discover the 
 retreat of a countryman and kinsman of his own . " 

 " and for what end ? " cry Riccabocca , throw off his guard , and his 
 breast dilate , his crest rise , and his eye flash ; valour and defiance 
 break from habitual caution and self - control . " but - pooh ! " he add , 
 strive to regain his ordinary and half - ironical calm , " it matter not 
 to I . I grant , sir , that I know the Count di Peschiera ; but what have 
 Dr. Riccabocca to do with the kinsman of so grand a personage ? " 

 " Dr. Riccabocca - nothing . but - " here Randal put his lip close to the 
 Italian 's ear , and whisper a brief sentence . then retreat a step , 
 but lay his hand on the exile 's shoulder , he add , " need I say that 
 your secret be safe with I ? " 

 Riccabocca make no answer . his eye rest on the ground musingly . 

 Randal continue , " and I shall esteem it the high honour you can 
 bestow on I , to be permit to assist you in forestall danger . " 

 riccabocca   slowly . - " Sir , I thank you ; you have my secret , and I feel 
 assure it be safe , for I speak to an english gentleman . there may be 
 family reason why I should avoid the Count di Peschiera ; and , indeed , 
 he be safe from shoal who steer clear of his relation . " 

 the poor Italian regain his caustic smile as he utter that wise , 
 villanous italian maxim . 

 RANDAL . - " I know little of the Count of Peschiera save from the current 
 talk of the world . he be say to hold the estate of a kinsman who take 
 part in a conspiracy against the austrian power . " 

 RICCABOCCA . - " it be true . let that content he ; what more do he 
 desire ? you speak of forestall danger ; what danger ? I be on the soil 
 of England , and protect by its law . " 

 RANDAL . - " allow I to inquire if , have the kinsman no child , the Count di 
 Peschiera would be legitimate and natural heir to the estate he hold ? " 

 RICCABOCCA . - " he would - what then ? " 

 RANDAL . - " do that thought suggest no danger to the child of the 
 kinsman ? " 

 Riccabocca recoil , and gasp forth , " the child ! you do not mean to 
 imply that this man , infamous though he be , can contemplate the crime of 
 an assassin ? " 

 randal pause perplexed . his ground be delicate . he know not what 
 cause of resentment the exile entertain against the count . he know 
 not whether Riccabocca would not assent to an alliance that might 
 restore he to his country , - and he resolve to feel his way with 
 precaution . 

 " I do not , " say he , smile gravely , " mean to insinuate so horrible a 
 charge against a man whom I have never see . he seek you , - that be all 
 I know . I imagine , from his general character , that in this search he 
 consult his interest . perhaps all matter might be conciliate by an 
 interview ! " 

 " an interview ! " exclaim Riccabocca ; " there be but one way we should 
 meet , - foot to foot , and hand to hand . " 

 " be it so ? then you would not listen to the count if he propose some 
 amicable compromise , - if , for instance , he be a candidate for the hand 
 of your daughter ? " 

 the poor italian , so wise and so subtle in his talk , be as rash and 
 blind when it come to action as if he have be bear in Ireland and 
 nourish on potato and repeal . he bar his whole soul to the 
 merciless eye of Randal . 

 " my daughter ! " he exclaim . " Sir , your very question be an insult . " 

 Randal 's way become clear at once . " forgive I , " he say mildly ; " I 
 will tell you frankly all that I know . I be acquaint with the count 's 
 sister . I have some little influence over she . it be she who inform 
 I that the count have come here , bent upon discover your refuge , and 
 resolve to we d your daughter . this be the danger of which I speak . and 
 when I ask your permission to aid in forestall it , I only intend 
 to suggest that it might be wise to find some securer home , and that I , 
 if permit to know that home , and to visit you , could apprise you from 
 time to time of the count 's plan and movement . " 

 " Sir , I thank you sincerely , " say Riccabocca , with emotion ; " but be I 
 not safe here ? " 

 " I doubt it . many people have visit the squire in the shooting season , 
 who will have hear of you , - perhaps see you , and who be likely 
 to meet the count in London . and Frank Hazeldean , too , who know the 
 count 's sister - " 

 " true , true " interrupted Riccabocca . " I see , I see . I will consider , I 
 will reflect . meanwhile you be go to Hazel dean . do not say a word 
 to the squire . he know not the secret you have discover . " 

 with those word Riccabocca turn slightly away , and Randal take the 
 hint to depart . 

 " at all time command and rely on I , " say the young traitor , and he 
 regain the pale to which he have fasten his horse . 

 as he remount , he cast his eye towards the place where he have leave 
 Riccabocca . the Italian be still stand there . presently the form of 
 Jackeymo be see emerge from the shrub . Riccabocca turn hastily 
 round , recognize his servant , utter an exclamation loud enough to 
 reach Randal 's ear , and then , catch Jackeymo by the arm , disappear 
 with he amidst the deep recess of the garden . 

 " it will be indeed in my favour , " think Randal , as he ride on , " if I 
 can get they into the neighbourhood of London , - all occasion there to 
 woo , and if expedient , to win , the heiress . " 




 CHAPTER VIII .

 insist upon carry he off to the home - farm ; and Harry turn

 Randal , " know that you will excuse my morning dress , I venture to 
 invite myself to dine and sleep at the Hall . " 

 on approach the farm - building , Randal be seize with the terror of 
 an impostor ; for , despite all the theoretical learning on Bucolics and 
 Georgics with which he have dazzle the squire , poor Frank , so despised , 
 would have beat he hollow when it come to the judging of the point of 
 an ox , or the show of a crop . 

 " ha , ha , " cry the squire , chuckle , " I long to see how you will 
 astonish Stirn . why , you will guess in a moment where we put the 
 top - dressing ; and when you come to handle my short - horn , I dare swear

 " oh , you do I too much honour , - indeed you do . I only know the general 
 principle of agriculture ; the detail be eminently interesting , but I 
 have not have the opportunity to acquire they . " 

 " Stuff ! " cry the squire . " how can a man know general principle unless 
 he have first study the detail ? you be too modest , my boy . Ho ! 
 there be stirn look out for we ! " randal see the grim visage of Stirn 
 peer out of a cattleshe , and feel undone . he make a desperate rush 
 towards change the squire 's humour . 

 " well , sir , perhaps Frank may soon gratify your wish , and turn farmer 
 himself . " 

 " eh ! " quoth the squire , stop short , - " what now ? " 

 " suppose he be to marry ? " 

 " I would give he the two good farm on the property rent free . ha , ha ! have 
 he see the girl yet ? I would leave he free to choose ; sir , I choose for 
 myself , - every man should . not but what Miss Stick - to - right be an 
 heiress , and , I hear , a very decent girl , and that would join the two 
 property , and put an end to that law - suit about the right of way , 
 which begin in the reign of King Charles the Second , and be likely 
 otherwise to last till the day of judgment . but never mind she ; let 
 Frank choose to please himself . " 

 " I will not fail to tell he so , sir . I do fear you might have some 
 prejudice . but here we be at the farmyard . " 

 " burn the farmyard ! how can I think of farmyard when you talk of 
 Frank 's marriage ? come on - this way . what be you say about 
 prejudice ? " 

 " why , you might wish he to marry an Englishwoman , for instance . " 

 " english ! good heavens , sir , do he mean to marry a Hindoo ? " 

 " Nay , I do not   know that he mean to marry at all ; I be only surmise ; 
 but if he do fall in love with a foreigner - " 

 " a foreigner ! ah , then Harry be - " the squire stop short . 

 " who might , perhaps , " observe randal - not truly , if he refer to 
 Madame di Negra - " who might , perhaps , speak very little English ? " 

 " Lord ha ' mercy ! " 

 " and a roman Catholic - " 

 " worship idol , and roast people who do not   worship they . " 

 " Signor Riccabocca be not so bad as that . " 

 " Rickeybockey ! well , if it be his daughter ! but not speak English ! 
 and not go to the parish church ! by George , if Frank think of such a 
 thing , I would cut he off with a shilling . do not   talk to I , sir ; I would . 
 I ' m a mild man , and an easy man ; but when I say a thing , I say it , Mr. 
 Leslie . oh , but it be a jest , - you be laugh at I . there be no such 
 paint good - for - nothing creature in Frank 's eye , eh ? "

 daughter - in - law . you say you have no prejudice . " 

 " no more I have , - not a bit of it . " 

 " you do not   like a foreigner and a Catholic ? " 

 " who the devil would ? " 

 " but if she have rank and title ? " 

 " rank and title ! bubble and squeak ! no , not half so good as bubble 
 and squeak . english beef and good cabbage . but foreign rank and 
 title ! - foreign cabbage and beef ! - foreign bubble and foreign squeak ! " 
 and the squire make a wry face , and spat forth his disgust and 
 indignation . 

 " you must have an Englishwoman ? " 

 " of course . " 

 " money ? " 

 " do not   care , provide she be a tidy , sensible , active lass , with a good 
 character for her dower . "

 I . he be not go to run off with a divorced woman , or a - "

 his will . 

 therefore he hasten to relieve Mr. Hazeldean 's mind , and assure he

 while thus seat , and thus think , a footstep approach cautiously ,

 Randal , who have make himself an excellent linguist , nodded assent ; and

 randal obey , and the two gain the shade of a stately chestnut 
 avenue . 

 " Sir , " then say Jackeymo , speak in his native tongue , and express 
 himself with a certain simple pathos , " I be but a poor man ; my name

 know danger together ; and of all his friend and follower , I alone 
 come with he to the stranger 's land . " 

 " good , faithful fellow , " say Randal , examine the man 's face , ' say on . 
 your master confide in you ? he have confide that which I tell he this 
 day ? " 

 " he do . ah , sir ; the padrone be too proud to ask you to explain 
 more , - too proud to show fear of another . but he do fear , he ought

 that this man might wish to marry the signora . marry she ! - I could cut 
 his throat at the altar ! " 

 " indeed , " say Randal , " I believe that such be his object . " 

 " but why ? he be rich , she be penniless , - no , not quite that , for we have 
 save - but penniless , compare to he . " 

 " my good friend , I know not yet his motif ; but I can easily learn 
 they . if , however , this count be your master 's enemy , it be surely well 
 to guard against he , whatever his design ; and to do so , you should 
 move into London or its neighbourhood . I fear that , while we speak , the

 " he have well not come here ! " cry the servant , menacingly , and 
 put his hand where the knife be not . 

 " beware of your own anger , Giacomo . one act of violence , and you would

 Jackeymo seem strike by this caution . 

 " and if the padrone be to meet he , do you think the padrone would 
 meekly say , ' come sta sa Signoria ' ? the padrone would strike he dead ! " 

 " hush ! hush ! you speak of what in England be call murder , and be 
 punish by the gallow . if you really love your master , for Heaven 's 
 sake get he from this place , get he from all chance of such passion 
 and peril . I go to town to - morrow ; I will find he a house , that shall 
 be safe from all spy , all discovery . and there , too , my friend . I can 
 do what I can not at this distance , - watch over he , and keep watch also 
 on his enemy . " 

 Jackeymo seize Randal 's hand , and lift it towards his lip ; then , as 
 if strike by a sudden suspicion , drop the hand , and say bluntly ,

 " be it so uncommon to take interest even in a stranger who be menace by 
 some peril ? " 

 Jackeymo , who believe little in general philanthropy , shake his head 
 sceptically .

 Egerton ; and Mr. Egerton 's most intimate friend be Lord L'Estrange ; and

 " certainly . now , tell I , Giacomo , be this count really unprincipled and
