 " he have neither heart nor conscience . " 

 " that defect make he dangerous to man ; perhaps not less so to woman . 
 could it be possible , if he obtain any interview with the signora ,

 make no answer . 

 " I have hear that he be still very handsome . " Jackeymo groan . 

 Randal resume , " enough ; persuade the padrone to come to town . " 

 " but if the count be in town ? " 

 " that make no difference ; the safe place be always the large city . 
 everywhere else , a foreigner be in himself an object of attention and 
 curiosity . " 

 " true . " 

 " let your master , then , come to London , or rather , into its 
 neighbourhood . he can reside in one of the suburb most remote from the 
 count 's haunt . in two day I will have find he a lodging and write to

 " I do indeed , - I do , Excellency . ah , if the signorina be marry , we 
 would not care ! " 

 " married ! but she look so high ! " 

 " alas ! not now ! not here ! " 

 Randal sigh heavily . Jackeymo 's eye sparkle . he think he have

 Jackeymo stride off through the fade tree , smile and muttering as

 the first dinner - bell rang , and on enter the drawingroom , Randal 
 find Parson Dale and his wife , who have be invite in haste to meet

 the parson nod , and there be a moment 's silence .

 " indeed , yes . by the way , now you speak of it , I meet my old opponent in 
 London the first year I go up to it . " 

 " you do ! where ? " 

 " at a literary scamp 's , - a cleverish man call Burley . "

 " no doubt the same person . he have disappear , - go to the dog , I dare 
 say . Burlesque Greek be not a knowledge very much in power at present . " 

 " well , but Leonard Fairfield - you have see he since ? " 

 " no . " 

 " nor hear of he ? " 

 " no ; have you ? " 

 " strange to say , not for a long time . but I have reason to believe that 
 he must be do well . " 

 " you surprise I ! why ? " 

 " because two year ago he send for his mother . she go to he . " 

 " be that all ? " 

 " it be enough ; for he would not have send for she if he could not 
 maintain she . " 

 here the Hazeldeans enter , arm - in - arm , and the fat butler announce 
 dinner . 

 the squire be unusually taciturn , Mrs. Hazeldean thoughtful , Mrs. 
 Dale languid and headachy . the parson , who seldom enjoy the luxury of 
 converse with a scholar , save when he quarrel with Dr. Riccaboeca , 
 be animate by Randal 's repute for ability into a great desire for 
 argument . 

 " a glass of wine , Mr. Leslie . you be say , before dinner , that 
 burlesque Greek be not a knowledge very much in power at present . pray , 
 Sir , what knowledge be in power ? " 

 RANDAL   laconically . - " practical knowledge . " 

 parson . - " what of ? " 

 RANDAL . - " Men . " 

 parson   candidly . - " well , I suppose that be the most available sort of 
 knowledge , in a worldly point of view . how do one learn it ? do book 
 help ? " 

 RANDAL . - " accord as they be read , they help or injure . " 

 parson . - " how should they be read in order to help ? " 

 RANDAL . - " read specially to apply to purpose that lead to power . " 

 parson   very much strike with Randal 's pithy and spartan logic . - " upon 
 my word , Sir , you express yourself very well . I must own that I begin 
 these question in the hope of differ from you ; for I like an 
 argument . " 

 " that he do , " growl the squire ; " the most contradictory creature ! " 

 parson . - " Argument be the salt of talk . but now I be afraid I must agree 
 with you , which I be not at all prepare for . " 

 randal bow and answer , " no two man of our education can dispute upon 
 the application of knowledge . " 

 parson   prick up his ear . - " Eh ? - what to ? " 

 RANDAL . - " Power , of course . " 

 parson   overjoyed . - " Power ! - the vulgar application of it , or the 
 lofty ? but you mean the lofty ? " 

 RANDAL   in his turn interested and interrogative . - " what do you call 
 the lofty , and what the vulgar ? " 

 parson . - " the vulgar , self - interest ; the lofty , beneficence . " 

 Randal suppress the half - disdainful smile that rise to his lip . 

 " you speak , Sir , as a clergyman should do . I admire your sentiment , and 
 adopt it ; but I fear that the knowledge which aim only at beneficence 
 very rarely in this world get any power at all . " 

 SQUIRE   seriously . - " that be true ; I never get my own way when I want 
 to do a kindness , and Stirn always get his when he insist on something 
 diabolically brutal and harsh . " 

 parson . - " pray , Mr. Leslie , what do intellectual power refine to the 
 utmost , but entirely strip of beneficence , most resemble ? " 

 RANDAL . - " Resemble ? - I can hardly say . some very great man - almost 
 any very great man - who have baffle all his foe , and attain all his 
 end . " 

 parson . - " I doubt if any man have ever become very great who have not 
 mean to be beneficent , though he might err in the mean . Caesar be 
 naturally beneficent , and so be Alexander . but intellectual power 
 refine to the utmost , and wholly void of beneficence , resemble only 
 one being , and that , sir , be the Principle of Evil . " 

 RANDAL   startle . - " do you mean the Devil ? " 

 parson . - " yes , Sir , the Devil ; and even he , Sir , do not succeed ! even 
 he , Sir , be what your great man would call a most decide failure . " 

 MRS . DALE . - " my dear , my dear ! " 

 parson . - " our religion prove it , my love ; he be an angel , and he 
 fall . " 

 there be a solemn pause . randal be more impressed than he like to 
 own to himself . by this time the dinner be over , and the servant have

 the gentleman remain over their wine ; and the parson , satisfied with 
 what he deem a clencher upon his favourite subject of discussion , 
 change the subject to light topic , till , happen to fall upon 
 tithe , the squire strike in , and by dint of loudness of voice , and 
 truculence of brow , fairly overwhelm both his guest , and prove to 
 his own satisfaction that tithe be an unjust and unchristianlike 
 usurpation on the part of the Church generally , and a most especial and 
 iniquitous infliction upon the hazeldean estate in particular . 




 CHAPTER IX . 

 on enter the drawing - room , Randal find the two lady seat close 
 together , in a position much more appropriate to the familiarity of 
 their school - day than to the politeness of the friendship now exist 
 between they . Mrs. Hazeldean 's hand hang affectionately over Carry 's 
 shoulder , and both those fair english face be bent over the same 
 book . it be pretty to see these sober matron , so different from 
 each other in character and aspect , thus unconsciously restore to the 
 intimacy of happy maiden youth by the golden link of some Magician from 
 the still land of Truth or Fancy , bring together in heart , as each eye 
 rest on the same thought ; close and close , as sympathy , lose in the 
 actual world , grow out of that world which unite in one bond of feel 
 the reader of some gentle book . 

 " and what work interest you so much ? " ask Randal , pause by the 
 table . 

 " one you have read , of course , " reply Mrs. Dale , put a book - mark 
 embroider by herself into the page , and hand the volume to Randal . 
 " it have make a great sensation , I believe . " 

 randal glance at the title of the work . " true , " say he , " I have hear 
 much of it in London , but I have not yet have time to read it . " 

 MRS . DALE . - " I can lend it to you , if you like to look over it to - night , 
 and you can leave it for I with Mrs. Hazeldean . " 

 parson   approach . - " oh , that book ! - yes , you must read it . I do not 
 know a work more instructive . " 

 RANDAL . - " Instructive ! certainly I will read it then . but I think it 
 be a mere work of amusement , - of fancy . it seem so as I look over it . " 

 parson . - " so be the ' Vicar of Wakefield ; ' yet what book more 
 instructive ? " 

 RANDAL . - " I should not have say that of the ' Vicar of Wakefield . ' a 
 pretty book enough , though the story be most improbable . but how be it 
 instructive ? " 

 parson . - " by its result : it leave we happy and well . what can any 
 instruction do more ? some work instruct through the head , some through 
 the heart . the last reach the wide circle , and often produce the most 
 genial influence on the character . this book belong to the last . you 
 will grant my proposition when you have read it . " 

 randal smile and take the volume . 

 MRS . DALE . - " be the author know yet ? " 

 RANDAL . - " I have hear it ascribe to many writer , but I believe no one 
 have claim it . " 

 parson . - " I think it must have be write by my old college friend , 
 Professor Moss , the naturalist , - its description of scenery be so 
 accurate . " 

 MRS . DALE . - " La , Charles dear ! that snuffy , tiresome , prosy professor ? 
 how can you talk such nonsense ? I be sure the author must be young , 
 there be so much freshness of feeling . " 

 MRS . HAZELDEAN   positively . - " yes , certainly , young . " 

 parson   no less positively . - " I should say just the contrary . its tone 
 be too serene , and its style too simple , for a young man . besides , I 
 do not   know any young man who would send I his book , and this book have 
 be send I , very handsomely bind , too , you see . depend upon it Moss 
 be the loan - quite his turn of mind . " 

 MRS . DALE . - " you be too provoking , Charles dear ! Mr. Moss be so 
 remarkably plain , too . " 

 RANDAL . - " must an author be handsome ? " 

 parson . - " ha ! ha ! answer that if you can , Carry . " Carry remain mute 
 and disdainful . 

 SQUIRE   with great naivete . - " well , I do not   think there be much in the 
 book , whoever write it ; for I have   read it myself , and understand every 
 word of it . " 

 MRS . DALE . - " I do not   see why you should suppose it be write by a man 
 at all . for my part , I think it must be a woman . " 

 MRS . HAZELDEAN . - " yes , there be a passage about maternal affection , which 
 only a woman could have write . " 

 parson . - " Pooh ! pooh ! I should like to see a woman who could have 
 write that description of an August evening before a thunderstorm ; 
 every wild - flower in the hedgerow exactly the flower of August , every 
 sign in the air exactly those of the month . bless you ! a woman would 
 have fill the hedge with violet and cowslip . nobody else but my 
 friend Moss could have write that description . " 

 SQUIRE . - " I do not   know ; there be a simile about the waste of corn - seed in 
 hand - sowing , which make I think he must be a farmer ! " 

 MRS . DALE   scornfully . - " a farmer ! in hobnailed shoe , I suppose ! I say 
 it be a woman . " 

 MRS . HAZELDEAN . - " a woman , and a mother ! " 

 parson . - " a middle - aged man , and a naturalist . " 

 SQUIRE . - " no , no , Parson , certainly a young man ; for that love - scene 
 put I in mind of my own young day , when I would have give my ear 
 to tell Harry how handsome I think she ; and all I could say be , ' fine 
 weather for the crop , Miss. ' yes , a young man and a farmer . I should 
 not wonder if he have hold the plough himself . " 

 RANDAL   who have be turn over the page . - " this sketch of Night in 
 London come from a man who have live the life of city and look at 
 wealth with the eye of poverty . not bad ! I will read the book . " 

 " strange , " say the parson , smile , " that this little work should so 
 have enter into our mind , suggest to all of we different idea , yet 
 equally charm all , - give a new and fresh current to our dull country 
 life , animate we as with the sight of a world in our breast we have 
 never see before save in dream : a little work like this by a man we 
 do not   know and never may ! well , that knowledge be power , and a noble 
 one ! " 

 " a sort of power , certainly , sir , " say Randal , candidly ; and that 
 night , when randal retire to his own room , he suspend his scheme and 
 project , and read , as he rarely do , without an object to gain by the 
 reading . 

 the work surprise he by the pleasure it give . its charm lie in the 
 writer 's calm enjoyment of the beautiful . it seem like some happy 
 soul sun itself in the light of its own thought . its power be so 
 tranquil and even , that it be only a critic who could perceive how much 
 force and vigour be necessary to sustain the wing that float aloft 
 with so imperceptible an effort . there be no one faculty predominate 
 tyrannically over the other ; all seem proportion in the felicitous 
 symmetry of a nature rounded , integral , and complete . and when the work 
 be close , it leave behind it a tender warmth that play round the 
 heart of the reader and vivified feeling which seem unknown before . 
 randal lay down the book softly ; and for five minute the ignoble and 
 base purpose to which his own knowledge be apply stand before he , 
 naked and unmasked . 

 " Tut ! " say he , wrench himself violently away from the benign 
 influence , " it be not to sympathize with Hector , but to conquer with 
 Achilles , that Alexander of Macedon keep Homer under his pillow . such 
 should be the true use of book to he who have the practical world to 
 subdue ; let parson and woman construe it otherwise , as they may ! " 

 and the Principle of Evil descend again upon the intellect from which 
 the guide of Beneficence be go . 




 CHAPTER X. 

 Randal rise at the sound of the first breakfast - bell , and on the 
 staircase meet Mrs. Haaeldean . he give she back the book ; and as he 
 be about to speak , she beckon to he to follow she into a little 
 morning - room appropriate to herself , - no boudoir of white and gold , 
 with picture by Watteau , but line with large walnut - tree press , 
 that hold the old heirloom linen , strew with lavender , store for the 
 housekeeper , and medicine for the poor . 

 seat herself on a large chair in this sanctum , Mrs. Hazeldean look 
 formidably at home . 

 " pray , " say the lady , come at once to the point , with her usual 
 straightforward candour , " what be all this you have be say to my 
 husband as to the possibility of Frank 's marry a foreigner ? " 

 RANDAL . - " would you be as averse to such a notion as Mr. Hazeldean be ? " 

 MRS . HAZELDEAN . - " you ask I a question , instead of answer mine . " 

 Randal be greatly put out in his fence by these rude thrust . for 
 indeed he have a double purpose to serve , - first , thoroughly to know if 
 Frank 's marriage with a woman like Madame di Negra would irritate the 
 squire sufficiently to endanger the son 's inheritance ; and , secondly , to 
 prevent Mr. and Mrs. Hazeldean believe seriously that such a marriage 
 be to be apprehend , lest they should prematurely address Frank on 
 the subject , and frustrate the marriage itself . yet , withal , he must so 
 express himself , that he could not be afterwards accuse by the parent 
 of disguise matter . in his talk to the squire the precede day , he 
 have go a little too far , - far than he would have do but for his 
 desire of escape the cattle - shed and short - horn . while he muse , 
 Mrs. Hazeldean observe he with her honest sensible eye , and finally 
 exclaim , 

 " out with it , Mr. Leslie ! " 

 " out with what , my dear madam ? the squire have sadly exaggerate the 
 importance of what be say mainly in jest . but I will own to you 
 plainly , that Frank have appear to I a little smitten with a certain 
 fair italian . " 

 " italian ! " cry Mrs. Hazeldean . " well , I say so from the first . 
 italian ! - that be all , be it ? " and she smile . Randal be more and more 
 perplexed . the pupil of his eye contract , as it do when we retreat 
 into ourselves , and think , watch , and keep guard . 

 " and perhaps , " resume Mrs. Hazeldean , with a very sunny expression of 
 countenance , " you have notice this in Frank since he be here ? " 

 " it be true , " murmur Randal ; " but I think his heart or his fancy be 
 touch even before . " 

 " very natural , " say Mrs. Hazeldean ; " how could he help it ? - such a 
 beautiful creature ! well , I must not ask you to tell Frank 's secret ; 
 but I guess the object of attraction ; and though she will have no 
 fortune to speak of , and it be not such a match as he might form , still 
 she be so amiable , and have be so well bring up , and be so little 
 like one 's general notion of a Roman Catholic , that I think I could 
 persuade Hazeldean into give his consent . " 

 " ah , " say Randal , draw a long breath , and beginning , with his 
 practise acuteness , to detect Mrs. Ilazeldean 's error , " I be very much 
 relieve and rejoice to hear this ; and I may venture to give Frank some 
 hope , if I find he dishearten and desponding , poor fellow ? " 

 " I think you may , " reply Mrs. Hazeldean , laugh pleasantly . " but you 
 should not have frighten poor William so , hint that the lady know 
 very little english . she have an accent , to be sure ; but she speak our 
 tongue very prettily . I always forget that she be not English bear ! ha , 
 ha , poor William ! " 

 RANDAL . - " ha , ha ! " 

 MRS . HAZELDEAN . - " we have once think of another match for Frank , - a 
 girl of good English family . " 

 RANDAL . - " Miss Sticktorights ? " 

 MRS . HAZELDEAN . - " no ; that be an old whim of Hazeldean 's . but I doubt if 
 the Sticktorights would ever merge their property in our . bless you ! it 
 would be all off the moment they come to settlement , and have to give up 
 the right of way . we think of a very different match ; but there be no 
 dictate to young heart , Mr. Leslie . " 

 RANDAL . - " indeed no , Mrs. Hazeldean . but since we now understand each 
 other so well , excuse I if I suggest that you have well leave thing 
 to themselves , and not write to Frank on the subject . young heart , you 
 know , be often stimulate by apparent difficulty , and grow cool when 
 the obstacle vanish . " 

 MRS . HAZELDEAN . - " very possibly ; it be not so with Hazeldean and 
 I . but I shall not write to Frank on the subject for a different 
 reason - though I would consent to the match , and so would William ; yet 
 we both would rather , after all , that Frank marry an Englishwoman , and 
 a protestant . we will not , therefore , do anything to encourage the idea . 
 but if Frank 's happiness become really at stake , then we will step in . 
 in short , we would neither encourage nor oppose . you understand ? " 

 " perfectly . " 

 " and in the mean while , it be quite right that Frank should see the 
 world , and try to distract his mind , or at least to know it . and I dare 
 say it have be some thought of that kind which have prevent his coming 
 here . "

 all thing favour his project , - even chance have befriend he in Mrs.

 CHAPTER XI . 

 Violante be seat in her own little room , and look from the window

 CHAPTER XII . 

 and Violante , thus absorb in revery , forget to keep watch on the

 the exile enter his daughter 's room , and she start to feel his hand

 be of tent - stitch . " 

 " she sing beautifully . " 

 " just as bird do , against all the rule , and in defiance of gamut .

 " all place with you be the same to I , Alphonso . when shall we go ? "

 put his arm round his wife 's waist , with genuine affection , and without 
 a single proverb at his heart . " Carissima , do not grieve so ; we shall be

 Mrs. Riccabocca gently escape from her husband 's arm . she withdraw she 
 hand from her face and brush away the tear that stand in her eye . 

 " Alphonso , " she say touchingly , " hear I ! what you think good , that 
 shall ever be good to I . but do not think that I grieve solely because

 humbly , to do my duty to you and your , and could have wish that you

 " Trust ! " repeat Riccabocca , startled and conscience - stricken ; " why 
 do you say ' trust ' ? in what have I distrust you ? I be sure , " he

 pry into your letter ; never inquire into your solitary walk ; never

 Father , can you resist this ? trust she ! trust she ! - I be a woman like 
 she ! I answer for her woman 's faith . be yourself , - ever noble than all

 " Diavolo ! never one door shut but another open , " groan Riccabocca .

 fear , and would be cautious ? "

 meanness . for mine ! be I not your daughter , - the descendant of man who

 CHAPTER XIII . 

 on Randal 's return to town , he hear mixed and contradictory rumour

 true , that certain change in policy , a year or two before , have divide

 the name be not unknown to Randal . it be a name too often on the lip

 CHAPTER XIV . 

 Egerton have throw himself at full length on the sofa , a position 
 exceedingly rare with he ; and about his whole air and manner , as Levy 
 enter , there be something singularly different from that stateliness 
 of port common to the austere legislator . the very tone of his voice

 vanish ; it be rather the man of fashion and the idler who , nod

 year ? " 

 " the estate will bear very little more . my dear fellow , that last 
 election be the very devil . you can not go on thus much long . "

 and Audley Egerton , perhaps , see nothing strange in the word , though 
 his lip curl .

 Baron Levy glance towards the thick mahogany door , - thick and 
 impervious , as should be the door of statesman . " why , you know that ,

 the usurer 's brow darken , but he dispel the cloud with an easy

 pecuniary favour from Lord L'Estrange ! - I ! "

 that act in your life which - "

 " hate you ! how have I show hatred ? would you ever have live in this 
 palace , and rule this country as one of the most influential of its 
 minister , but for my management , my whisper to the wealthy Miss 
 Leslie ? come , but for I what would you have be , - perhaps a beggar . " 

 " what shall I be now , if I live ? and this fortune which my marriage 
 bring to I - it have pass for the main part into your hand . be 
 patient , you will have it all ere long . but there be one man in the 
 world who have love I from a boy , and woe to you if ever he learn that 
 he have the right to despise I ! " 

 " Egerton , my good fellow , " say Levy , with great composure , " you need 
 not threaten I , for what interest can I possibly have in tale - telling 
 to Lord L'Estrange ? again , dismiss from your mind the absurd thought 
 that I hate you . true , you snub I in private , you cut I in public , 
 you refuse to come to my dinner , you will not ask I to your own ; still , 
 there be no man I like well , nor would more willingly serve . when do 
 you want the L5,000 ? " 

 " perhaps in one month , perhaps not for three or four . let it be ready 
 when require . " 

 " enough ; depend on it . have you any other command ? " 

 " none . " 

 " I will take my leave , then . by - the - by , what do you suppose the 
 Hazeldean rental be worth - net ? " 

 " I do not   know , nor care . you have no design upon that too ? " 

 " well , I like keep up family connection . Mr. Frank seem a liberal 
 young gentleman . " 

 before Egerton could answer , the baron have glide to the door , and , 
 nod pleasantly , vanish with that nod . Egerton remain , stand 
 on his solitary hearth . a drear , single man 's room it be , from wall 
 to wall , despite its fretted ceiling and official pomp of Brahmah 
 escritoire and red box . drear and cheerless , - no trace of woman 's 
 habitation , no vestige of intrude , happy child . there stand the 
 austere man alone . and then with a deep sigh he mutter , " thank Heaven , 
 not for long , - it will not last long . " 

 repeat those word , he mechanically lock up his paper , and press 
 his hand to his heart for an instant , as if a spasm have shoot through it . 

 " so - I must shun all emotion ! " say he , shake his head gently . 

 in five minute more Audley Egerton be in the street , his mien erect , 
 and his step firm as ever . 

 " that man be make of bronze , " say a leader of the Opposition to a 
 friend as they ride past the minister . " what would I not give for his 
 nerve ! " 




 BOOK NINTH . 




 INITIAL chapter . 

 on PUBLIC LIFE . 

 now that I be fairly in the heart of my story , these preliminary 
 chapter must shrink into comparatively small dimension , and not 
 encroach upon the space require by the various personage whose 
 acquaintance I have pick up here and there , and who be now all 
 crowd upon I like poor relation to whom one have unadvisedly give 
 a general invitation , and who descend upon one simultaneously about 
 Christmas time . where they be to be stow , and what be to become of 
 they all , Heaven know ; in the mean while , the reader will have already 
 observe that the Caxton Family themselves be turn out of their own 
 room , send a packing , in order to make way for the new comer . 

 but to proceed : note the heading to the present chapter , " ON PUBLIC 
 life , " - a thesis pertinent to this portion of my narrative ; and if 
 somewhat trite in itself , the great be the stimulus to suggest thereon 
 some original hint for reflection . 

 be you ever in public life , my dear reader ? I do not   mean , by that 
 question , to ask whether you be ever Lord Chancellor , Prime Minister , 
 Leader of the Opposition , or even a member of the House of Commons . an 
 author hop to find reader far beyond that very egregious but very 
 limited segment of the Great Circle . be you ever a busy man in your 
 vestry , active in a municipal corporation , one of a committee for 
 further the interest of an enlightened candidate for your native 
 burgh , town , or shire , - in a word , do you ever resign your private 
 comfort as man in order to share the public trouble of mankind ? if 
 ever you have so far depart from the lucretian philosophy , just look 
 back - be it life at all that you live ? be you an individual 
 distinct existence , - a passenger in the railway , - or be you merely 
 an indistinct portion of that common flame which heat the boiler and 
 generate the steam that set off the monster train ? - very hot , very 
 active , very useful , no doubt ; but all your identity fuse in flame , and 
 all your force vanish in gas . 

 and do you think the people in the railway carriage care for you ? do 
 you think that the gentleman in the worsted wrapper be say to his 
 neighbour with the striped rug on his comfortable knee , " how grateful 
 we ought to be for that fiery particle which be crackle and hiss 
 under the boiler . it help we on a fraction of an inch from Vauxhall 
 to Putney ! " not a bit of it . ten to one but he be say , " not sixteen 
 mile an hour ! what the deuce be the matter with the stoker ? " 

 look at our friend Audley Egerton . you have just have a glimpse of the 
 real being that struggle under the huge copper ; you have hear the 
 hollow sound of the rich man 's coffer under the tap of Baron Levy 's 
 friendly knuckle , hear the strong man 's heart give out its dull warning 
 sound to the scientific ear of Dr. F - - - . and away once more vanishe 
 the separate existence , lose again in the flame that heat the boiler , 
 and the smoke that curl into air from the grimy furnace . 

 look to it , o Public Man , whoever thou art , and whatsoever thy 
 degree , - see if thou canst not compound matter , so as to keep a little 
 nook apart for thy private life ; that be , for thyself ! let the Great 
 Popkins Question not absorb wholly the individual soul of thee , as Smith 
 or Johnson . do not   so entirely consume thyself under that insatiable 
 boiler , that when thy poor little monad rush out from the sooty 
 furnace , and arrive at the star , thou mayest find no vocation for 
 thee there , and feel as if thou hadst nothing to do amidst the still 
 splendour of the Infinite . I do not   deny to thee the us of " Public 
 life ; " I grant that it be much to have help to carry that Great 
 Popkins Question ; but private Life , my friend , be the life of thy 
 private soul ; and there may be matter concerned with that which , on 
 consideration , thou mayest allow can not be wholly mix up with the 
 Great Popkins Question , and be not finally settle when thou didst 
 exclaim , " I have not live in vain , - the Popkins Question be carry 
 at last ! " oh , immortal soul , for one quarter of an hour per diem 
 de - popkinize thine immortality ! 




 CHAPTER II . 

 it have not be without much persuasion on the part of Jackeymo that 
 Riccabocca have consent to settle himself in the house which Randal have 
 recommend to he . not that the exile conceive any suspicion of the 
 young man beyond that which he might have share with Jackeymo , namely , 
 that Randal 's interest in the father be increase by a very natural 
 and excusable admiration of the daughter ; but the Italian have the pride 
 common to misfortune , - he do not like to be indebted to other , and he 
 shrink from the pity of those to whom it be know that he have hold a 
 high station in his own land . these scruple give way to the strength 
 of his affection for his daughter and his dread of his foe . good man , 
 however able and brave , who have suffer from the wicked , be apt to 
 form exaggerated notion of the power that have prevail against 
 they . Jackeymo have conceive a superstitious terror of Peschiera ; and 
 Riccabocca , though by no mean addict to superstition , still have a 
 certain creep of the flesh whenever he think of his foe . 

 but Riccabocca - than whom no man be more physically brave , and no man , 
 in some respect , more morally timid - fear the count less as a foe 
 than as a gallant . he remember his kinsman 's surpass beauty , the 
 power he have obtain over woman . he know he verse in every art that 
 corrupt , and wholly void of the conscience that deter . and Riccabocca 
 have unhappily nurse himself into so poor an estimate of the female 
 character , that even the pure and lofty nature of Violante do not seem 
 to he a sufficient safeguard against the craft and determination of a 
 practised and remorseless intriguer . but of all the precaution he 
 could take , none appear more likely to conduce to safety than his 
 establish a friendly communication with one who profess to be able 
 to get at all the count 's plan and movement , and who could apprise 
 Riccabocca at once should his retreat be discover . " forewarned be 
 forearmed , " say he to himself , in one of the proverb common to all 
 nation . however , as with his usual sagacity he come to reflect upon the 
 alarming intelligence convey to he by Randal , namely , that the 
 count seek his daughter 's hand , he divine that there be some strong 
 personal interest under such ambition ; and what could be that interest 
 save the probability of Riccabocca 's ultimate admission to the imperial 
 grace , and the count 's desire to assure himself of the heritage to an 
 estate that he might be permit to retain no more ? Riccabocca be 
 not indeed aware of the condition   not accord to usual custom in 
 Austria   on which the count hold the forfeit domain . he know not that 
 they have be grant merely on pleasure ; but he be too well aware of 
 Peschiera 's nature to suppose that he would woo a bride without a 
 dower , or be move by remorse in any overture of reconciliation . he 
 feel assure too - and this increase all his fear - that Peschiera would 
 never venture to seek an interview with himself ; all the count 's design 
 on Violante would be dark , secret , and clandestine . he be perplexed and 
 torment by the doubt whether or not to express openly to Violante his 
 apprehension of the nature of the danger to be apprehend . he have 
 tell she vaguely that it be for her sake that he desire secrecy and 
 concealment . but that might mean anything : what danger to himself would 
 not menace she ? yet to say more be so contrary to a man of his italian 
 notion and machiavellian maxim ! to say to a young girl , " there be a 
 man come over to England on purpose to woo and win you . for Heaven 's 
 sake take care of he ; he be diabolically handsome ; he never fail 
 where he set his heart . - Cospetto ! " cry the doctor , aloud , as these 
 admonition shape themselves to speech in the camera obscura of his 
 brain ; ' such a warning would have undo a Cornelia while she be yet 
 an innocent spinster . " no , he resolve to say nothing to Violante of the 
 count 's intention , only to keep guard , and make himself and Jackeymo all 
 eye and all ear . 

 the house Randal have select pleased Riccabocca at first glance . it 
 stand alone , upon a little eminence ; its upper window command the 
 high road . it have be a school , and be surround by high wall , which 
 contain a garden and lawn sufficiently large for exercise . the garden 
 door be thick , fortify by strong bolt , and have a little wicket 
 lattice , shut and open at pleasure , from which Jackeymo could inspect 
 all visitor before he permit they to enter . 

 an old female servant from the neighbourhood be cautiously hire ; 
 Riccabocca renounce his italian name , and abjure his origin . he speak 
 English sufficiently well to think he could pass as an Englishman . he 
 call himself Mr. Richmouth   a liberal translation of Riccabocca . he 
 buy a blunderbuss , two pair of pistol , and a huge housedog . 
 thus provide for , he allow Jackeymo to write a line to randal and 
 communicate his arrival . 

 Randal lose no time in call . with his usual adaptability and his 
 power of dissimulation , he contrive easily to please Mrs. Riccabocca , 
 and to increase the good opinion the exile be disposed to form of he . 
 he engage Violante in conversation on Italy and its poet . he promise 
 to bring her book . he begin , though more distantly than he could have 
 desire , - for her sweet stateliness awe he , - the preliminary of 
 courtship . he establish himself at once as a familiar guest , rid 
 down daily in the dusk of evening , after the toil of office , and 
 return at night . in four or five day he think he have make great 
 progress with all . Riccabocca watch he narrowly , and grow absorb 
 in thought after every visit . at length one night , when he and Mrs. 
 Riccabocca be alone in the drawing - room , Violante have retire to 
 rest , he thus speak as he fill his pipe , - 

 " Happy be the man who have no child ! Thrice happy he who have no 
 girl ! " 

 " my dear Alphonso ! " say the wife , look up from the waistband to 
 which she be attach a neat mother - o'-pearl button . she say no more ; 
 it be the sharp rebuke she be in the custom of administer to her 
 husband 's cynical and odious observation . Riccabocca light his pipe 
 with a thread paper , give three great puff , and resume , 

 " one blunderbuss , four pistol , and a house - dog call Pompey , who would 
 have make mincemeat of Julius Caesar ! " 

 " he certainly eat a great deal , do Pompey ! " say Mrs. Riccabocca , 
 simply . " but if he relieve your mind ! " 

 " he do not relieve it in the least , ma'am , " groan Riccabocca ; " and 
 that be the point I be come to . this be a most harass life , and a 
 most undignified life . and I who have only ask from Heaven dignity 
 and repose ! but if Violante be once marry , I should want neither 
 blunderbuss , pistol , nor Pompey . and it be that which would relieve my 
 mind , cara mia , - Pompey only relieve my larder . " 

 now Riccabocca have be more communicative to Jemima than he have be to 
 Violante . have once trust she with one secret , he have every motive 
 to trust she with another ; and he have accordingly speak out his fear 
 of the Count di Peschiera . therefore she answer , lay down the work , 
 and take her husband 's hand tenderly , 

 " indeed , my love , since you dread so much   though I own that I must 
 think unreasonably   this wicked , dangerous man , it would be the happy 
 thing in the world to see dear Violante well marry ; because , you see , 
 if she be married to one person she can not be married to another ; and 
 all fear of this count , as you say , would be at an end . " 

 " you can not express yourself well . it be a great comfort to unbosom 
 one's - self to a wife , after all , " quoth Riccabocca . 

 " but , " say the wife , after a grateful kiss , - " but where and how can we 
 find a husband suitable to the rank of your daughter ? " 

 " there ! there ! there ! " cry Riccabocca , push back his chair to the 
 farther end of the room , " that come of unbosom one's - self ! out fly 
 one secret ; it be open the lid of Pandora 's box ; one be betray , 
 ruin , undo ! " 

 " why , there be not a soul that can hear we ! " say Mrs. Riccabocca , 
 soothingly . 

 " ' that 's chance , ma'am ! if you once contract the habit of blab out 
 a secret when nobody be by , how on earth can you resist it when you 
 have the pleasurable excitement of tell it to all the world ? vanity , 
 vanity , - woman 's vanity ! woman never could withstand rank , - never ! " 
 the doctor go on rail for a quarter of an hour , and be very 
 reluctantly appease by Mrs. Riccabocca 's repeat and tearful 
 assurance that she would never even whisper to herself that her husband 
 have ever hold any other rank than that of doctor . Riccabocca , with a 
 dubious shake of the head , renew , 

 " I have do with all pomp and pretension . besides , the young man be a 
 bear gentleman : he seem in good circumstance ; he have energy and latent 
 ambition ; he be akin to L'Estrange 's intimate friend : he seem attach 
 to Violante . I do not   think it probable that we could do well . Nay , if 
 Peschiera fear that I shall be restore to my country , and I learn 
 the wherefore , and the ground to take , through this young man - why , 
 gratitude be the first virtue of the noble ! " 

 " you speak , then , of Mr. Leslie ? " 

 " to be sure - of whom else ? " 

 Mrs. Riccabocca lean her cheek on her hand thoughtfully . " now you have 
 tell I that , I will observe he with different eye . " 

 " Anima mia , I do not   see how the difference of your eye will alter the 
 object they look upon ! " grumble Riccabocca , shake the ash out of 
 his pipe . 

 " the object alter when we see it in a different point of view ! " reply 
 Jemima , modestly . " this thread do very well when I look at it in order 
 to sew on a button , but I should say it would never do to tie up Pompey 
 in his Kennel . " 

 " reason by illustration , upon my soul ! " ejaculate Riccabocca , 
 amazed . 

 " and , " continue Jemima , " when I be to regard one who be to constitute 
 the happiness of that dear child , and for life , can I regard he as I 
 would the pleasant guest of an evening ? ah , trust I , Alphonso ; I do not 
 pretend to be wise like you ; but when a woman consider what a man be 
 likely to prove to woman , - his sincerity , his honour , his heart , - oh , 
 trust I , she be wise than the wise man ! " 

 Riccabocca continue to gaze on Jemima with unaffected admiration and 
 surprise . and certainly , to use his phrase , since he have unbosom 
 himself to his well half , since he have confide in she , consult with 
 she , her sense have seem to quicken , her whole mind to expand . 

 " my dear , " say the sage , " I vow and declare that Machiavelli be a fool 
 to you . and I have be as dull as the chair I sit upon , to deny myself 
 so many year the comfort and counsel of such a - But , corpo di Bacco ! 
 forget all about rank ; and so now to bed . - one must not holloa till 
 one be out of the wood , " mutter the ungrateful , suspicious villain , as 
 he light the chamber candle . 




 CHAPTER III . 

 RICCABOCCA could not confine himself to the precinct within the wall 
 to which he condemn Violante . resume his spectacle , and wrap 
 in his cloak , he occasionally sally forth upon a kind of outwatch 
 or reconnoitre expedition , - restrict himself , however , to the 
 immediate neighbourhood , and never go quite out of sight of his 
 house . his favourite walk be to the summit of a hillock overgrown with 
 stunt bush - wood . here he would sit himself musingly , often till the 
 hoof of Randal 's horse ring on the wind road , as the sun set , over 
 fade herbage , red and vaporous , in autumnal sky . just below the 
 hillock , and not two hundred yard from his own house , be the only 
 other habitation in view , - a charming , thoroughly english cottage , 
 though somewhat imitate from the Swiss , with gable end , thatched roof , 
 and pretty , project casement , open through creeper and climbing 
 rose . from his height he command the garden of this cottage , and his 
 eye of artist be pleased , from the first sight , with the beauty which 
 some exquisite taste have give to the ground . even in that cheerless 
 season of the year , the garden wear a summer smile ; the evergreen be 
 so bright and various , and the few flow er still leave so hardy and so 
 healthful . face the south , a colonnade , or cover gallery , of rustic 
 woodwork have be form , and creep plant , lately set , be already 
 begin to clothe its column . opposite to this colonnade there be a 
 fountain which remind Riccabocca of his own at the deserted casino . it 
 be indeed singularly like it ; the same circular shape , the same girdle 
 of flower around it . but the jet from it vary every day , fantastic 
 and multiform , like the sport of a Naiad , - sometimes shoot up like 
 a tree , sometimes shape as a convolvulus , sometimes toss from its 
 silver spray a flower of vermilion , or a fruit of gold , - as if at play 
 with its toy like a happy child . and near the fountain be a large 
 aviary , large enough to enclose a tree . the italian could just catch a 
 gleam of rich colour from the wing of the bird , as they glance to 
 and fro within the network , and could hear their song , contrast the 
 silence of the free populace of air , whom the come winter have already 
 still . 

 Riccabocca 's eye , so alive to all aspect of beauty , luxuriate in the 
 view of this garden . its pleasantness have a charm that steal he from 
 his anxious fear and melancholy memory . 

 he never see but two form within the demesne , and he could not 
 distinguish their feature . one be a woman , who seem to he of staid 
 manner and homely appearance : she be see but rarely . the other a man , 
 often pace to and fro the colonnade , with frequent pause before the 
 playful fountain , or the bird that sing louder as he approach . this 
 latter form would then disappear within a room , the glass door of which 
 be at the extreme end of the colonnade ; and if the door be leave open , 
 Riccabocca could catch a glimpse of the figure bend over a table 
 cover with book . 

 always , however , before the sun set , the man would step forth more 
 briskly , and occupy himself with the garden , often work at it with 
 good heart , as if at a task of delight ; and then , too , the woman would 
 come out , and stand by as if talk to her companion . Riccabocca 's 
 curiosity grow arouse . he bid Jemima inquire of the old maid - servant 
 who live at the cottage , and hear that its owner be a Mr. Oran , - a 
 quiet gentleman , and fond of his book . 

 while Riccabocca thus amuse himself , Randal have not be prevent , 
 either by his official care or his scheme on Violante 's heart and 
 fortune , from further the project that be to unite Frank Hazeldean 
 and Beatrice di Negra . indeed , as to the first , a ray of hope be 
 sufficient to fire the ardent and unsuspecting lover . and Randal 's 
 artful misrepresentation of his conference with Mrs. Hazeldean remove 
 all fear of parental displeasure from a mind always too disposed to give 
 itself up to the temptation of the moment . Beatrice , though her feeling 
 for Frank be not those of love , become more and more influence by 
 Randal 's argument and representation , the more especially as she 
 brother grow morose , and even menacing , as day slip on , and she 
 could give no clew to the retreat of those whom he seek for . her 
 debt , too , be really urgent . as Randal 's profound knowledge of human 
 infirmity have shrewdly conjecture , the scruple of honour and pride , 
 that have make her declare she would not bring to a husband her own 
 encumbrance , begin to yield to the pressure of necessity . she listen 
 already , with but faint objection , when Randal urge she not to wait 
 for the uncertain discovery that be to secure her dowry , but by a 
 private marriage with Frank escape at once into freedom and security . 
 while , though he have first hold out to young Hazeldean the inducement 
 of Beatrice 's dowry as a reason of self - justification in the eye of the 
 squire , it be still easy to drop that inducement , which have always 
 rather damp than fire the high spirit and generous heart of the poor 
 Guardsman . and Randal could conscientiously say , that when he have ask 
 the squire if he expect fortune with Frank 's bride , the squire have 
 reply , " I do not   care . " thus encourage by his friend and his own 
 heart , and the soften manner of a woman who might have charm many a 
 colder , and fool many a wise man , Frank rapidly yield to the snare 
 hold out for his perdition . and though as yet he honestly shrink from 
 propose to Beatrice or himself a marriage without the consent , and 
 even the knowledge , of his parent , yet Randal be quite content to 
 leave a nature , however good , so thoroughly impulsive and undisciplined , 
 to the influence of the first strong passion it have ever know . 
 meanwhile , it be so easy to dissuade Frank from even give a hint to 
 the folk at home . " for , " say the wily and able traitor , " though we 
 may be sure of Mrs. Hazeldean 's consent , and her power over your father , 
 when the step be once take , yet we can not count for certain on the 
 squire , he be so choleric and hasty . he might hurry to town , see Madame 
 di Negra , blurt out some passionate , rude expression , which would wake 
 her resentment , and cause her instant rejection . and it might be too 
 late if he repent afterwards , as he would be sure to do . " 

 meanwhile randal Leslie give a dinner at the Clarendon Hotel   an 
 extravagance most contrary to his habit , and invite Frank , Mr. 
 Borrowell , and Baron Levy . 

 but this house - spider , which glide with so much ease after its fly , 
 through webs so numerous and mazy , have yet to amuse Madame di Negra 
 with assurance that the fugitive seek for would soon or later be 
 discover . though Randal baffle and elude her suspicion that he 
 be already acquaint with the exile   " the person he have think 
 of be , " he say , " quite different from her description ; " and he even 
 present to she an old singing - master and a sallow - faced daughter , as 
 the Italians who have cause his mistake , it be necessary for Beatrice 
 to prove the sincerity of the aid she have promise to her brother , and 
 to introduce Randal to the count . it be no less desirable to Randal to 
 know , and even win the confidence of this man - his rival . 

 the two meet at Madame di Negra 's house . there be something very strange , 
 and almost mesmerical , in the rapport between two evil nature . bring 
 two honest man together , and it be ten to one if they recognize each 
 other as honest ; difference in temper , manner , even politic , may make 
 each misjudge the other . but bring together two man unprincipled and 
 pervert - man who , if bear in a cellar , would have be food for the 
 hulk or gallow - and they understand each other by instant sympathy . 
 the eye of Franzini , Count of Peschiera , and Randal Leslie no soon 
 meet than a gleam of intelligence shot from both . they talk on 
 indifferent subject , - weather , gossip , politic , - what not . they bow 
 and they smile ; but all the while , each be watch , plumb the 
 other 's heart , each measure his strength with his companion ; each inly 
 say , " this be a very remarkable rascal ; be I a match for he ? " it be 
 at dinner they meet ; and follow the english fashion , Madame di Negra 
 leave they alone with their wine . 

 then , for the first time , Count di Peschiera cautiously and adroitly 
 make a cover push towards the object of the meeting . 

 " you have never be abroad , my dear sir ? you must contrive to visit 
 I at Vienna . I grant the splendour of your London world ; but , honestly 
 speak , it want the freedom of ours , - a freedom which unite gayety 
 with polish . for as your society be mixed , there be pretension 
 and effort with those who have no right to be in it , and artificial 
 condescension and chilling arrogance with those who have to keep their 
 inferior at a certain distance . with we , all be of fix rank and 
 acknowledge birth , familiarity be at once establish . hence , " add 
 the count , with his french lively smile , - " hence there be no place like 
 Vienna for a young man , no place like Vienna for bonne fortune . " 

 " those make the paradise of the idle , " reply Randal , " but the 
 purgatory of the busy . I confess frankly to you , my dear count , that 
 I have as little of the leisure which become the aspirer to bonne 
 fortune as I have the personal grace which obtain they without an 
 effort ; " and he incline his head as in compliment . 

 " so , " think the count , " woman be not his weak side . what be ? " 

 " Morbleu ! my dear Mr. Leslie , have I think as you do some year since , 
 I have save myself from many a trouble . after all , Ambition be the good 
 mistress to woo ; for with she there be always the hope , and never the 
 possession . " 

 " Ambition , Count , " reply Randal , still guard himself in dry 
 sententiousness , " be the luxury of the rich , and the necessity of the 
 poor . " 

 " aha , " think the count , " it come , as I anticipate from the 
 first , - come to the bribe . " he pass the wine to Randal , fill his 
 own glass , and drain it carelessly ; " sur mon ame , mon cher , " say the 
 count , " luxury be ever pleasanter than necessity ; and I be resolve 
 at least to give Ambition a trial ; je vais me refugier dans le sein du 
 bonheur domestique , - a married life and a settle home . peste ! if it 
 be not for ambition , one would die of ennui . a propos , my dear sir , I 
 have to thank you for promise my sister your aid in find a near and 
 dear kinsman of mine , who have take refuge in your country , and hide 
 himself even from I . " 

 " I should be most happy to assist in your search . as yet , however , I 
 have only to regret that all my good wish be fruitless . I should have 
 think , however , that a man of such rank have be easily find , even 
 through the medium of your own ambassador . " 

 " our own ambassador be no very warm friend of mine ; and the rank would 
 be no clew , for it be clear that my kinsman have never assume it since 
 he quit his country . " 

 " he quit it , I understand , not exactly from choice , " say randal , 
 smile . " pardon my freedom and curiosity , but will you explain to I 
 a little more than I learn from english rumour   which never accurately 
 report upon foreign matter still more notorious , how a person who have 
 so much to lose , and so little to win , by revolution , could put himself 
 into the same crazy boat with a crew of hair - brain adventurer and 
 visionary professor . " 

 " professor ! " repeat the count ; " I think you have hit on the very 
 answer to your question ; not but what man of high birth be as mad as 
 the canaille . I be the more willing to gratify your curiosity , since 
 it will perhaps serve to guide your kind search in my favour . you 
 must know , then , that my kinsman be not bear the heir to the rank he 
 obtain . he be but a distant relation to the head of the House which 
 he afterwards represent . bring up in an italian university , he be 
 distinguish for his learning and his eccentricity . there too , I 
 suppose , brood over old wife ' tale about freedom , and so forth , 
 he contract his carbonaro , chimerical notion for the independence of 
 Italy . suddenly , by three death , he be elevate , while yet young , to 
 a station and honour which might have satisfy any man in his sens . 
 Que diable ! what could the independence of Italy do for he ? he and I 
 be cousin ; we have play together as boy ; but our life have be 
 separate till his succession to rank bring we necessarily together . 
 we become exceedingly intimate . and you may judge how I love he , " 
 say the count , avert his eye slightly from Randal 's quiet , watchful 
 gaze , " when I add , that I forgive he for enjoy a heritage that , but 
 for he , have be mine . " 

 " ah , you be next heir ? " 

 " and it be a hard trial to be very near a great fortune , and yet just to 
 miss it . " 

 " true , " cry Randal , almost impetuously . the count now raise his eye , 
 and again the two man look into each other 's soul . 

 " hard still , perhaps , " resume the count , after a short 
 pause , - " hard still might it have be to some man to forgive the 
 rival as well as the heir . " 

 " Rival ! how ? " 

 " a lady , who have be destine by her parent to myself , though we have

 " I consent . " 

 " that be generous . you must indeed have be much attach to your 
 kinsman . as a lover , I can not comprehend it ; perhaps , my dear count , you 
 may enable I to understand it well - as a man of the world . " 

 " well , " say the count , with his most roue air , " I suppose we be both

 " Both ! certainly , " reply Randal , just in the tone which Peachum might 
 have use in court the confidence of Lockit .

 " very natural ; it might bring your wealthy kinsman and yourself still 
 close together . " 

 " this be really a very clever fellow ! " think the count , but he make no

 " enfin , to cut short a long story , my cousin afterwards get entangle in

 " I comprehend perfectly ; and I can also conceive that you , in profit 
 so largely , though so justly , by the fall of your kinsman , may have be 
 expose to much unpopularity , even to painful suspicion . " 

 " Entre nous , mon cher , I care not a stiver for popularity ; and as to

 " you say it . " 

 " but even without such marriage , since the emperor 's clemency have be

 " aver - untruly ? " 

 " Ma foi , it come to the same thing ; ' les absent ont toujour tort . ' 
 I speak to a man of the world . no ; without some such guarantee for his

 rise as he say this , - rise as if the mask of simulation have fairly

 " why , if my cousin have so abjure his birthright , and forswear his rank ;

 CHAPTER IV . 

 Randal arrive at the ambassador 's before the count , and contrive to

 acquaint , - the Count di Peschiera . " 

 " he be no countryman of mine . he be an italian . I know he but by sight 
 and by name , " say the prince , stiffly . 

 " he be of very ancient birth , I believe . " 

 " unquestionably . his ancestor be gentleman . " 

 " and very rich . " 

 " indeed ! I have understand the contrary . he enjoy , it be true , a large 
 revenue . " 

 a young attache , less discreet than the prince ; here observe , " oh , 
 Peschiera ! poor fellow , he be too fond of play to be rich . "

 " the count - ha ! I hear something of a scheme , - a wager of that - that 
 count 's . a daughter ! poor girl ! I hope she will escape his pursuit ; for ,

 " well . "

 while thus meditate , a member of parliament tap he on the

 " you be think of the place you be so soon to lose . " 

 " soon to lose ! " 

 " why , if minister go out , you could hardly keep it , I suppose . " 

 this ominous and horrid member of parliament , Squire Hazeldean 's 
 favourite county member , Sir John , be one of those legislator 
 especially odious to official , - an independent " large - acre " member , 
 who would no more take office himself than he would cut down the oak 
 in his park , and who have no bowel of human feeling for those who have 
 opposite taste and less magnificent mean . 

 " Hem ! " say Randal , rather surlily . " in the first place , Sir John , 
 minister be not go out . " 

 " oh , yes , they will go . you know I vote with they generally , and would 
 willingly keep they in ; but they be man of honour and spirit ; and if

 would turn round and vote they out myself ! "

 " why ? hang it , Leslie , you be laugh at I . a young fellow like you 
 could never be mean enough to stay in , under the very man who drive out 
 your friend Egerton ? " 

 " it be not usual for those in the public office to retire with every 
 change of government . " 

 " certainly not ; but always those who be the relation of a retire 
 minister ; always those who have be regard as politician , and who 
 mean to enter parliament , as of course you will do at the next election .

 SIR JOHN . - " no , certainly ; perfect gentleman , Egerton ! I wish we could 
 make it up with he and Hazeldean . " 

 randal   sigh . - " ah , I wish we could ! " 

 SIR JOHN . - " and some chance of it now ; for the time be come when all 
 true man of the old school must stick together . " 

 RANDAL . - " wisely , admirably say , my dear Sir John . but , pardon I , I 
 must pay my respect to the ambassador . " randal escape , and pass on ,

 Egerton unexpectedly join he . 

 " ah , Leslie , " say the minister , with more kindness than usual , " if you

 have send away the carriage . " 

 this condescension in his patron be so singular , that it quite startle

 " my dear Mr. Leslie , it be my hope and belief that I have provide for

 Randal feel as a man feel after a severe fall , - he be literally

 the pro and the con , you can well judge what to do , should the time

 " but I hope that time may not come . " 

 " I hope so too , and most sincerely , " say the minister , with deliberate 
 and genuine emphasis . 

 " what could be so bad for the country ? " ejaculate Pandal . " it do not 
 seem to I possible , in the nature of thing , that you and your party

 " and when we be once out , there will be plenty of wiseacre to say it 
 be out of the nature of thing that we should ever come in again . here 
 we be at the door . " 




 CHAPTER V. 

 Randal pass a sleepless night ; but , indeed , he be one of those 
 person who neither need , nor be accustom to , much sleep . however , 
 towards morning , when dream be say to be prophetic , he fall into a 
 most delightful slumber , a slumber people by vision fit to lure on , 
 through labyrinth of law , predestine chancellor , or wreck upon the 
 rock of glory the inebriate soul of youthful ensign ; dream from 
 which Rood Hall emerge crown with the tower of Belvoir or Raby , 
 and look over subject land and manor wrest from the nefarious 
 usurpation of Thornhills and Hazeldeans ; dream in which Audley 
 Egerton 's gold and power , room in Downing Street , and saloon in 
 Grosvenor Square , have pass away to the smile dreamer , as the empire 
 of Chaldaea pass to Darius the Median . why vision so belie the 
 gloomy and anxious thought that precede they should visit the pillow 
 of Randal Leslie , surpass my philosophy to conjecture . he yield , 
 however , passively to their spell , and be startled to hear the clock 
 strike eleven as he descend the stair to breakfast . he be vex at

 behind a voice that have lately become familiar to he , and turn 
 round , see Baron Levy walk side by side , though not arm - in - arm , with 
 a gentleman almost as smart as himself , but with a jaunty step and a 
 brisker air , - a step that , like Diomed 's , as describe by Shakspeare , - 

  " rise on the toe ; that spirit of his 
  in aspiration lift he from the earth . " 

 indeed , one may judge of the spirit and disposition of a man by his 
 ordinary gait and mien in walk . he who habitually pursue abstract 
 thought look down on the ground . he who be accustomed to sudden 
 impulse , or be try to seize upon some necessary recollection , look 
 up with a kind of jerk . he who be a steady , cautious , merely practical 
 man , walk on deliberately , his eye straight before he ; and , even in 
 his most musing mood , observe thing around sufficiently to avoid a 
 porter 's knot or a butcher 's tray . but the man with strong ganglion - of 
 push , lively temperament , who , though practical , be yet speculative ; 
 the man who be emulous and active , and ever try to rise in life ; 
 sanguine , alert , bold - walk with a spring , look rather above the head 
 of his fellow - passenger , but with a quick , easy turn of his own , which 
 be lightly set on his shoulder ; his mouth be a little open , his eye 
 be bright , rather restless , but penetrative , his port have something 
 of defiance , his form be erect , but without stiffness . such be the 
 appearance of the baron 's companion . and as Randal turn round at 
 Levy 's voice , the baron say to his companion , " a young man in the first 
 circle - you should book he for your fair lady 's party . how d ' ye 
 do , Mr. Leslie ? let I introduce you to Mr. Richard Avenel . " then , as he 
 hook his arm into Randal 's , he whisper , " Man of first - rate talent , 
 monstrous rich , have two or three parliamentary seat in his pocket , wife 
 give party , - her foible . " 

 " proud to make your acquaintance , sir , " say Mr. Avenel , lift his 
 hat . " fine day . " 

 " rather cold too , " say Leslie , who , like all thin person with weak 
 digestion , be chilly by temperament ; besides , he have enough on his 
 mind to chill his body . 

 " so much the healthy , - brace the nerve , " say Mr. Avenel ; " but you 
 young fellow relax the system by hot room and late hour . fond of 
 dancing , of course , sir ? " then , without wait for Randal 's negative , 
 Mr. Richard continue rapidly , " Mrs. Avenel have a soiree dansante on 
 Thursday , - shall be very happy to see you in Eaton Square . stop , I have

 gentleman 's arm , and Randal reply courteously that it would give he

 " that young man will make a figure some day , " say the baron . " I do not 
 know any one of his age with so few prejudice . he be a connection by 
 marriage to Audley Egerton , who - " 

 " Audley Egerton ! " exclaim Mr. Avenel ; " a d - -d haughty , aristocratic , 
 disagreeable , ungrateful fellow ! " 

 " why , what do you know of he ? " 

 " he owe his first seat in parliament to the vote of two near relation 
 of mine , and when I call upon he some time ago , in his office , he 
 absolutely order I out of the room . hang his impertinence ; if ever I 
 can pay he off , I guess I sha'n't   fail for want of good will ! " 

 " order you out of the room ? that be not like Egerton , who be civil , if 
 formal , - at least to most man . you must have offend he in his weak 
 point . " 

 " a man whom the public pay so handsomely should have no weak point . 
 what be Egerton 's ? " 

 " oh , he value himself on be a thorough gentleman , - a man of the 
 nice honour , " say Levy , with a sneer . " you must have ruffle his

 " I forget , " answer Mr. Avenel , who be far too well verse in the

 week . " 

 " rely on it . " 

 " and you will not let my bill get into the market ; keep they under lock 
 and key . " 

 " so we agree . " 

 " it be but a temporary difficulty , - royal mourning , such nonsense ; panic 
 in trade , lest these precious minister go out . I shall soon float over 
 the troubled water . " 

 " by the help of a paper boat , " say the baron , laughing ; and the two

 CHAPTER VI . 

 meanwhile Audley Egerton 's carriage have deposit he at the door of 
 Lord Lansmere 's house , at Knightsbridge . he ask for the countess , and 
 be show into the drawing - room , which be desert . Egerton be pale

 too , on enter , show an emotion almost equally unusual to her 
 self - control . she press Audley 's hand in silence , and seat herself 
 by his side , seem to collect her thought . at length she say , 

 " it be rarely indeed that we meet , Mr. Egerton , in spite of your 
 intimacy with Lansmere and Harley . I go so little into your world , and 
 you will not voluntarily come to I . " 

 " Madam , " reply Egerton , " I might evade your kind reproach by state 
 that my hour be not at my disposal ; but I answer you with plain 
 truth , - it must be painful to both of we to meet . " 

 the countess colour and sigh , but do not dispute the assertion . 

 Audley resume : " and therefore , I presume that , in send for I , you 
 have something of moment to communicate ? " 

 " it relate to Harley , " say the countess , as if in apology ; " and I 
 would take your advice . " 

 " to Harley ! speak on , I beseech you . " 

 " my son have probably tell you that he have educate and rear a young 
 girl , with the intention to make her Lady L'Estrange , and hereafter 
 Countess of Lansmere . " 

 " Harley have no secret from I , " say Egerton , mournfully . " this young 
 lady have arrive in England , be here , in this house . " 

 " and Harley too ? " 

 " no , she come over with Lady N - - - and her daughter . Harley be to 
 follow shortly , and I expect he daily . here be his letter . Observe , 
 he have never yet communicate his intention to this young person , now 
 entrust to my care , never speak to she as the lover . " 

 Egerton take the letter and read it rapidly , though with attention . 

 " true , " say he , as he return the letter : " and before he do so he 
 wish you to see Miss Digby and to judge of her yourself , - wish to 
 know if you will approve and sanction his choice . " 

 " it be on this that I would consult you : a girl without rank ; the 
 father , it be true , a gentleman , though almost equivocally one , but the 
 mother , I know not what . and Harley , for whom I hop an alliance 
 with the first house in England ! " the countess press her hand 
 convulsively together . 

 EGERTON . - " he be no more a boy . his talent have be waste , his life 
 a wanderer 's . he present to you a chance of resettle his mind , 
 of re - arouse his native power , of a home besides your own . Lady 
 Lansmere , you can not hesitate ! " 

 LADY LANSMERE . - " I do , I do ? after all that I have hop after all that 
 I do to prevent - " 

 egerton   interrupt she . - " you owe he now an atonement ; that be in 
 your power , - it be not in mine . " the countess again press Audley 's 
 hand , and the tear gush from her eye . 

 " it shall be so . I consent , I consent . I will silence , I will crush back 
 this proud heart . alas ! it well - nigh break his own ! I be glad you 
 speak thus . I like to think he owe my consent to you . in that there be 
 atonement for both . " 

 " you be too generous , madam , " say Egerton , evidently move , though 
 still , as ever , strive to repress emotion . " and now may I see the 
 young lady ? this conference pain I ; you see even my strong nerve 
 quiver ; and at this time I have much to go through , - need of all my 
 strength and firmness . " 

 " I hear , indeed , that the Government will probably retire . but it be 
 with honour : it will be soon call back by the voice of the nation . " 

 " let I see the future wife of Harley L'Estrange , " say Egerton , without 
 heed of this consolatory exclamation . 

 the countess rise and leave the room . in a few minute she return with 
 Helen Digby . 

 Helen be wondrously improve from the pale , delicate child , with the 
 soft smile and intelligent eye , who have sit by the side of Leonard 
 in his garret . she be about the middle height , still slight , but 
 beautifully form ; that exquisite roundness of proportion which convey 
 so well the idea of woman , in its undulating , pliant grace , - form to 
 embellish life , and soften away its rude angle ; form to embellish , 
 not to protect . her face might not have satisfy the critical eye of 
 an artist , - it be not without defect in regularity ; but its expression 
 be eminently gentle and prepossess ; and there be few who would not 
 have exclaim , " what a lovely countenance ! " the mildness of her brow 
 be touch with melancholy - her childhood have leave its trace on her 
 youth . her step be slow , and her manner shy , subdue , and timid . 

 Audley gaze on she with earnestness as she approach he ; and then 
 come forward , take her hand and kiss it . " I be your guardian 's 
 constant friend , " say he , and he draw she gently to a seat beside he , 
 in the recess of a window . with a quick glance of his eye towards the 
 countess , he seem to imply the wish to converse with Helen somewhat 
 apart . so the countess interpret the glance ; and though she remain 
 in the room , she seat herself at a distance , and bent over a book . 

 it be touch to see how the austere man of business lend himself 
 to draw forth the mind of this quiet , shrink girl ; and if you have 
 listen , you would have comprehend how he come to possess such social 
 influence , and how well , some time or other in the course of his life , 
 he have learn to adapt himself to woman . 

 he speak first of Harley L'Estrange , - speak with tact and delicacy . 
 Helen at first answer by monosyllable , and then , by degree , with 
 grateful and open affection . Audley 's brow grow shade . he then speak 
 of Italy ; and though no man have less of the poet in his nature , yet 
 with the dexterity of one long verse in the world , and who have be 
 accustomed to extract evidence from character most oppose to his 
 own , he suggest such topic as might serve to arouse poetry in other . 
 Helen 's reply betray a cultivate taste , and a charming womanly 
 mind ; but they betray , also , one accustom to take its colouring 
 from another 's , - to appreciate , admire , revere the Lofty and the 
 Beautiful , but humbly and meekly . there be no vivid enthusiasm , no 
 remark of strike originality , no flash of the self - kindle , creative 
 faculty . lastly , Egerton turn to England , - to the critical nature of 
 the time , to the claim which the country possess upon all who have 
 the ability to serve and guide its troubled destiny . he enlarge 
 warmly on Harley 's natural talent , and rejoice that he have return to 
 England , perhaps to commence some great career . Helen look surprised , 
 but her face catch no correspondent glow from Audley 's eloquence . 
 he rise , and an expression of disappointment pass over his grave , 
 handsome feature , and as quickly vanish . 

 " Adieu , my dear Miss Digby ; I fear I have weary you , especially with 
 my politic . Adieu , Lady Lansmere ; no doubt I shall see Harley as soon 
 as he return . " 

 then he hasten from the room , gain his carriage , and order the 
 coachman to drive to Downing Street . he draw down the blind , and lean 
 back . a certain languor become visible in his face , and once or twice , 
 he mechanically put his hand to his heart . 

 " she be good , amiable , docile , - will make an excellent wife , no doubt , " 
 say he , murmuringly . " but do she love Harley as he have dream of 
 love ? no ! have she the power and energy to arouse his faculty , and 
 restore to the world the Harley of old ? no ! mean by Heaven to be the 
 shadow of another 's sun - not herself the sun , - this child be not the one 
 who can atone for the Past and illume the Future . " 




 CHAPTER VII . 

 that evening Harley L'Estrange arrive at his father 's house . the few 
 year that have pass since we see he last have make no perceptible 
 change in his appearance . he still preserve his elastic youthfulness 
 of form , and singular variety and play of countenance . he seem 
 unaffectedly rejoice to greet his parent , and have something of the 
 gayety and tenderness of a boy return from school . his manner to Helen 
 bespoke the chivalry that pervade all the complexity and curve 
 of his character . it be affectionate , but respectful , - her to he , 
 subdue , but innocently sweet and gently cordial . Harley be the chief 
 talker . the aspect of the time be so critical that he could not avoid 
 question on politic ; and , indeed , he show an interest in they which 
 he have never evince before . Lord Lansmere be delighted . 

 " why , Harley , you love your country after all ? " 

 " the moment she seem in danger , yes ! " reply the Patrician ; and the 
 Sybarite seem to rise into the Athenian . then he ask with eagerness 
 about his old friend Audley ; and , his curiosity satisfy there , he 
 inquire the last literary news . he have hear much of a book lately 
 publish . he name the one ascribe by Parson Dale to Professor Moss ; 
 none of his listener have read it . 

 Harley pishe at this , and accuse they all of indolence and stupidity , 
 in his own quaint , metaphorical style . then he say , " and town gossip ? " 

 " we never hear it , " say Lady Lansmere . 

 " there be a new plough much talk of at Boodle 's , " say Lord Lansmere . 

 " God speed it . but be not there a new man much talk of at White 's ? " 

 " I do not   belong to White 's . " 

 " nevertheless , you may have hear of he , - a foreigner , a Count di 
 Peschiera . " 

 " yes , " say Lord Lansmere ; " he be point out to I in the Park , - a 
 handsome man for a foreigner ; wear his hair properly cut ; look 
 gentlemanlike and English . " 

 " ah , ah ! he be here then ! " and Harley rub his hand . 

 " which road do you take ? do you pass the Simplon ? " 

 " no ; I come straight from Vienna . " 

 then , relate with lively vein his adventure by the way , he continue 
 to delight Lord Lansmere by his gayety till the time come to retire to 
 rest . as soon as Harley be in his own room his mother join he . 

 " well , " say he , " I need not ask if you like Miss Digby ? who would not ? " 

 " Harley , my own son , " say the mother , burst into tear , " be happy 
 your own way ; only be happy , that be all I ask . " 

 Harley , much affect , reply gratefully and soothingly to this fond 
 injunction . and then gradually lead his mother on to converse 
 of Helen , ask abruptly , " and of the chance of our happiness , - her 
 happiness as well as mine , - what be your opinion ? speak frankly . " 

 " of her happiness there can be no doubt , " reply the mother , proudly . 
 " of your , how can you ask I ? have you not decide on that yourself ? " 

 " but still it cheer and encourage one in any experiment , however well 
 consider , to hear the approval of another . Helen have certainly a most 
 gentle temper . " 

 " I should conjecture so . but her mind - " 

 " be very well store . " 

 " she speak so little - " 

 " yes . I wonder why ? she be surely a woman ! " 

 " Pshaw , " say the countess , smile in spite of herself . 

 " but tell I more of the process of your experiment . you take she as a 
 child , and resolve to train she accord to your own ideal . be that 
 easy ? " 

 " it seem so . I desire to instil habit of truth : she be already by 
 nature truthful as the day ; a taste for Nature and all thing natural : 
 that seem inborn ; perception of art as the interpreter of Nature :

 hear her play and sing ? " 

 " no . " 

 " she will surprise you . she have less talent for drawing ; still , all 
 that teaching could do have be do , - in a word , she be accomplish . 
 Temper , heart , mind , - these all be excellent . " Harley stop , and 
 suppress a sigh . " certainly I ought to be very happy , " say he ; and he 
 begin to wind up his watch . 

 " of course she must love you , " say the countess , after a pause . " how 
 could she fail ? " 

 " love I ! my dear mother , that be the very question I shall have to 
 ask . " 

 " Ask ! love be discover by a glance ; it have no need of ask . " 

 " I have never discover it , then , I assure you . the fact be , that 
 before her childhood be pass , I remove she , as you may suppose , 
 from my roof . she reside with an italian family near my usual abode . I 
 visit she often , direct her study , watch her improvement - " 

 " and fall in love with she ? " 

 " fall be such a very violent word . no ; I do not   remember to have have a 
 fall . it be all a smooth incline plane from the first step , until at 
 last I say to myself , ' Harley L'Estrange , thy time have come . the bud 
 have blossom into flower . take it to thy breast . ' and myself reply 
 to myself , meekly , ' so be it . ' then I find that Lady N - - - , with her 
 daughter , be come to England . I ask her ladyship to take my 
 ward to your house . I write to you , and pray your assent ; and , that 
 grant , I know you would obtain my father 's . Iam here , - you give I the

 all , she may reject I . " 

 " strange , strange ! you speak thus coldly , thus lightly , you , so capable 
 of ardent love ! " 

 " Mother , " say Harley , earnestly , " be satisfied ! I be ! love as of old , I 
 feel , alas ! too well , can visit I never more . but gentle companionship ,

 hereafter the voice of child , - music that , strike on the heart 
 of both parent , waken the most lasting and the pure of all 
 sympathy , - these be my hope . be the hope so mean , my fond mother ? "
