 CHAPTER VIII . 

 oh , Helen , fair Helen , - type of the quiet , serene , unnoticed , deep - feel 
 excellence of woman ! woman , less as the ideal that a poet conjure from 
 the air , than as the companion of a poet on the earth ! woman , who , with 
 her clear sunny vision of thing actual , and the exquisite fibre of her 
 delicate sense , supply the deficiency of he whose foot stumble 
 on the soil , because his eye be too intent upon the star ! woman , the 
 provident , the comforting , angel whose pinion be fold round the 
 heart , guard there a divine spring unmarre by the winter of the 
 world ! Helen , soft Helen , be it indeed in thee that the wild and 
 brilliant " lord of wantonness and ease " be to find the regeneration 
 of his life , the rebaptism of his soul ? of what avail thy meek prudent 
 household virtue to one whom Fortune screen from rough trial ; whose 
 sorrow lie remote from thy ken ; whose spirit , erratic and perturb , 
 now rise , now fall , need a vision more subtle than thine to 
 pursue , and a strength that can sustain the reason , when it droop , on 
 the wing of enthusiasm and passion ? 

 and thou , thyself , o nature , shrink and humble , that needest to be 
 court forth from the shelter , and develop under the calm and genial 
 atmosphere of holy , happy love - can such affection as Harley L'Estrange 
 may proffer suffice to thee ? will not the blossom , yet fold in the 
 petal , wither away beneath the shade that may protect they from the 
 storm , and yet shut they from the sun ? Thou who , where thou giv 
 love , seek , though meekly , for love in return ; to be the soul 's sweet 
 necessity , the life 's household partner to he who receive all thy 
 faith and devotion , - canst thou influence the source of joy and of 
 sorrow in the heart that do not heave at thy name ? Hast thou the charm 
 and the force of the moon , that the tide of that wayward sea shall ebb 
 and flow at thy will ? yet who shall say , who conjecture how near two 
 heart can become , when no guilt lie between they , and time bring the 
 tie all its own ? Rarest of all thing on earth be the union in which 
 both , by their contrast , make harmonious their blending ; each supply 
 the defect of the helpmate , and complete , by fusion , one strong human 
 soul ! happiness enough , where even peace do but seldom preside , when 
 each can bring to the altar , if not the flame , still the incense . where 
 man 's thought be all noble and generous , woman 's feeling all gentle 
 and pure , love may follow if it do not precede ; and if not , if the 
 rose be miss from the garland , one may sigh for the rose , but one be 
 safe from the thorn . 

 the morning be mild , yet somewhat overcast by the mist which announce 
 come winter in London , and Helen walk musingly beneath the tree 
 that surround the garden of Lord Lansmere 's house . many leaf be 
 yet leave on the bough ; but they be sere and wither . and the bird 
 chirp at time ; but their note be mournful and complain . all 
 within this house , until Harley 's arrival , have be strange and 
 saddening to Helen 's timid and subdued spirit . Lady Lansmere have 
 receive she kindly , but with a certain restraint ; and the loftiness of 
 manner , common to the countess with all but Harley , have awe and chill 
 the diffident orphan . Lady Lansmere 's very interest in Harley 's choice , 
 her attempt to draw Helen out of her reserve , her watchful eye 
 whenever Helen shyly speak or shyly move , frighten the poor child , 
 and make she unjust to herself . 

 the very servant , though staid , grave , and respectful , as suit a 
 dignified , old - fashioned household , painfully contrast the bright 
 welcome smile and free talk of italian domestic . her recollection 
 of the happy , warm Continental manner , which so set the bashful at 
 their ease , make the stately and cold precision of all around her doubly 
 awful and dispiriting . Lord Lansmere himself , who do not as yet know 
 the view of Harley , and little dream that he be to anticipate a 
 daughter - in - law in the ward , whom he understand Harley , in a freak of 
 generous romance , have adopt , be familiar and courteous , as become a 
 host ; but he look upon Helen as a mere child , and naturally leave 
 she to the countess . the dim sense of her equivocal position , of her 
 comparative humbleness of birth and fortune , oppress and pain she ; 
 and even her gratitude to Harley be make burdensome by a sentiment of 
 helplessness . the grateful long to requite . and what could she ever do 
 for he ? 

 thus musing , she wander alone through the curve walk ; and this sort 
 of mock - country landscape - London loud , and even visible , beyond the 
 high gloomy wall , and no escape from the window of the square formal 
 house - seem a type of the prison bound of Rank to one whose soul 
 yearn for simple love nature . 

 Helen 's revery be interrupt by Nero 's joyous bark . he have catch 
 sight of she , and come bound up , and thrust his large head into her 
 hand . as she stoop to caress the dog , happy at his honest greeting , 
 and tear that have be long gathering at the lid fall silently on his 
 face   for I know nothing that more move we to tear than the hearty 
 kindness of a dog , when something in human being have pain or chill 
 we , she hear behind the musical voice of Harley . hastily she dry or 
 repress her tear , as her guardian come up , and draw her arm within 
 his own . 

 " I have so little of your conversation last evening , my dear ward , that 
 I may well monopolize you now , even to the privation of Nero . and so you 
 be once more in your native land ? " 

 Helen sigh softly . 

 " may I not hope that you return under fairer auspex than those which 
 your childhood know ? " 

 Helen turn her eye with ingenuous thankfulness to her guardian , and 
 the memory of all she owe to he rush upon her heart . 

 Harley renew , and with earnest , though melancholy sweetness , " Helen , 
 your eye thank I ; but hear I before your word do . I deserve no 
 thank . I be about to make to you a strange confession of egotism and 
 selfishness . " 

 " you ! - oh , impossible ! " 

 " judge yourself , and then decide which of we shall have cause to be 
 grateful . Helen , when I be scarcely your age - a boy in year , but 
 more , methink , a man at heart , with man 's strong energy and sublime 
 aspiring , than I have ever since be - I love , and deeply - " 

 he pause a moment , in evident struggle . Helen listen in mute 
 surprise , but his emotion awaken her own ; her tender woman 's heart 
 yearn to console . unconsciously her arm rest on his less lightly . 

 " deeply , and for sorrow . it be a long tale , that may be tell hereafter . 
 the worldly would call my love a madness . I do not reason on it then , I 
 can not reason on it now . enough : death smite suddenly , terribly , and 
 to I , mysteriously , she whom I love . the love live on . fortunately , 
 perhaps , for I , I have quick distraction , not to grief , but to its inert 
 indulgence . I be a soldier ; I join our army . man call I brave . 
 Flattery ! I be a coward before the thought of life . I seek death : 
 like sleep , it do not come at our call . peace ensue . as when the 
 wind fall the sail droop , so when excitement cease , all seem to I 
 flat and objectless . heavy , heavy be my heart . perhaps grief have be 
 less obstinate , but that I fear I have cause for self - reproach . since 
 then I have be a wanderer , a self - make exile . my boyhood have be 
 ambitious , - all ambition cease . flame , when they reach the core of the 
 heart , spread , and leave all in ash . let I be brief : I do not mean 
 thus weakly to complain , - I to whom Heaven have give so many blessing ! 
 I feel , as it be , separate from the common object and joy of man . I 
 grow startled to see how , year by year , wayward humour possess I . 
 I resolve again to attach myself to some live heart - it be my sole 
 chance to rekindle my own . but the one I have love remain as my type 
 of woman , and she be different from all I see . therefore I say to 
 myself , ' I will rear from childhood some young fresh life , to grow up 
 into my ideal . ' as this thought begin to haunt I , I chance to discover 
 you . strike with the romance of your early life , touch by your 
 courage , charm by your affectionate nature , I say to myself , ' here be 
 what I seek . ' Helen , in assume the guardianship of your ' life , in all 
 the culture which I have seek to bestow on your docile childhood , I 
 repeat , that I have be but the egotist . and now , when you have reach 
 that age when it become I to speak , and you to listen ; now , when 
 you be under the sacred roof of my own mother ; now I ask you , can you 
 accept this heart , such as waste year , and grief too fondly nurse , 
 have leave it ? can you be , at least , my comforter ? can you aid I to 
 regard life as a duty , and recover those aspiration which once soar 
 from the paltry and miserable confine of our frivolous daily being ? 
 Helen , here I ask you , can you be all this , and under the name 
 of - wife ? " 

 it would be in vain to describe the rapid , varying , indefinable emotion 
 that pass through the inexperienced heart of the youthful listener 
 as Harley thus speak . he so move all the spring of amaze , compassion , 
 tender respect , sympathy , child - like gratitude , that when he pause and 
 gently take her hand , she remain bewildered , speechless , overpower . 
 Harley smile as he gaze upon her blushing , downcast , expressive face . 
 he conjecture at once that the idea of such proposal have never cross 
 her mind ; that she have never contemplate he in the character of wooer ; 
 never even sound her heart as to the nature of such feeling as his 
 image have arouse . 

 " my Helen , " he resume , with a calm pathos of voice , " there be some 
 disparity of year between we , and perhaps I may not hope henceforth for 
 that love which youth give to the young . permit I simply to ask , what 
 you will frankly answer , can you have see in our quiet life abroad , or 
 under the roof of your italian friend , any one you prefer to I ? " 

 " no , indeed , no ! " murmur Helen . " how could I ; who be like you ? " then , 
 with a sudden effort - for her innate truthfulness take alarm , and her 
 very affection for Harley , childlike and reverent , make her tremble lest 
 she should deceive he - she draw a little aside , and speak thus , 

 " oh , my dear guardian , noble of all human being , at least in my eye , 
 forgive , forgive I , if I seem ungrateful , hesitating ; but I can not , 
 can not think of myself as worthy of you . I never so lift my eye . your 
 rank , your position - " 

 " why should they be eternally my curse ? forget they , and go on . " 

 " it be not only they , " say Helen , almost sob , " though they be 
 much ; but I your type , your ideal ! - I ? - impossible ! oh , how can I ever 
 be anything even of use , of aid , of comfort to one like you ! " 

 " you can , Helen - you can , " cry Harley , charm by such ingenuous 
 modesty . " may I not keep this hand ? " and Helen leave her hand in 
 Harley 's , and turn away her face , fairly weep . 

 a stately step pass under the wintry tree . 

 " my mother , " say Harley L'Estrange , look up , " I present to you my 
 future wife . " 




 CHAPTER IX . 

 with a slow step and an abstract air , Harley L'Estrange bend his way 
 towards Egerton 's house , after his eventful interview with Helen . he have 
 just enter one of the street lead into Grosvenor Square , when 
 a young man , walk quickly from the opposite direction , come full 
 against he , and draw back with a brief apology , recognize he , 
 and exclaim , " what ! you in England , Lord L'Estrange ! accept my 
 congratulation on your return . but you seem scarcely to remember I . " 

 " I beg your pardon , Mr. Leslie . I remember you now by your smile ; but 
 you be of an age in which it be permit I to say that you look old 
 than when I see you last . " 

 " and yet , Lord L'Estrange , it seem to I that you look young . " 

 indeed , this reply be so far true that there appear less difference 
 of year than before between Leslie and L'Estrange ; for the wrinkle 
 in the schemer 's mind be visible in his visage , while Harley 's dreamy 
 worship of Truth and Beauty seem to have preserve to the votary the 
 endure youth of the divinity . 

 Harley receive the compliment with a supreme indifference , which might 
 have be suitable to a stoic , but which seem scarcely natural to 
 a gentleman who have just propose to a lady many year young than 
 himself . 

 Leslie renew : " perhaps you be on your way to Mr. Egerton 's . if so , 
 you will not find he at home ; he be at his office . " 

 " thank you . then to his office I must re - direct my step . " 

 " I be go to he myself , " say Randal , hesitatingly . L'Estrange have no 
 prepossession in favour of Leslie from the little he have see of that 
 young gentleman ; but Randal 's remark be an appeal to his habitual 
 urbanity , and he reply , with well - breed readiness , " let we be 
 companion so far . " 

 Randal accept the arm proffer to he ; and Lord L'Estrange , as

 " Egerton be always the same man , I suppose , - too busy for illness , and 
 too firm for sorrow ? " 

 " if he ever feel either , he will never stoop to complain . but , indeed ,

 " Nay , I do not mean to do that ; and pray do not let he know that 
 I go so far . but I have fancy that he look a little worn and 
 suffering . "

 I . " 

 and Harley 's voice be more cordial to randal than it have ever be

 " Loss of office could not , I think , affect a man like Audley , " observe

 and as to emolument - " 

 " the emolument be good , " interpose Randal , with a half - sigh . 

 " good enough , I suppose , to pay he back about a tenth of what his 
 place cost our magnificent friend . no , I will say one thing for english 
 statesman , no man amongst they ever yet be the rich for place . " 

 " and Mr. Egerton 's private fortune must be large , I take for grant , " 
 say Randal , carelessly . 

 " it ought to be , if he have time to look to it . " 

 here they pass by the hotel in which lodge the Count di Peschiera . 

 Randal stop . " will you excuse I for an instant ? as we be pass 
 this hotel , I will just leave my card here . " so say he give his card 
 to a waiter lounge by the door . " for the Count di Peschiera , " say he , 
 aloud . 

 L'Estrange start ; and as Randal again take his arm , say , " so that 
 italian lodge here ; and you know he ? " 

 " I know he but slightly , as one know any foreigner who make a 
 sensation . " 

 " he make a sensation ? " 

 " naturally ; for he be handsome , witty , and say to be very rich , - that 
 be , as long as he receive the revenue of his exile kinsman . " 

 " I see you be well inform , Mr. Leslie . and what be suppose to bring 
 hither the Count di Peschiera ? " 

 " I do hear something , which I do not quite understand , about a bet 
 of his that he would marry his kinsman 's daughter , and so , I conclude , 
 secure to himself all the inheritance ; and that he be therefore here 
 to discover the kinsman and win the heiress . but probably you know 
 the right of the story , and can tell I what credit to give to such 
 gossip . " 

 " I know this at least , that if he do lay such a wager , I would advise 
 you to take any odd against he that his backer may give , " say

 gleam with arch ironical humour . 

 " you think , then , that this poor kinsman will not need such an alliance 
 in order to regain his estate ? " 

 " yes ; for I never yet know a rogue whom I would not bet against , when he 
 back his own luck as a rogue against Justice and Providence . " 

 Randal wince , and feel as if an arrow have graze his heart ; but he soon 
 recover . 

 " and indeed there be another vague rumour that the young lady in

 undo all ! an Englishman just at this moment ! but some Englishman of 
 correspondent rank I trust , or at least one know for opinion oppose 
 to what an Austrian would call Revolutionary doctrine ? " 

 " I know nothing . but it be suppose merely a private gentleman of 
 good family . would not that suffice ? can the Austrian Court dictate a 
 marriage to the daughter as a condition for grace to the father ? " 

 " no , - not that ! " say Harley , greatly disturb . " but put yourself in 
 the position of any minister to one of the great european monarchy . 
 suppose a political insurgent , formidable for station and wealth , have 
 be proscribe , much interest make on his behalf , a powerful party 
 strive against it ; and just when the minister be disposed to relent , 
 he hear that the heiress to this wealth and this station be marry 
 to the native of a country in which sentiment friendly to the 
 very opinion for which the insurgent be proscribe be popularly 
 entertain , and thus that the fortune to be restore may be so 
 employ as to disturb the national security , the exist order of 
 thing , - this , too , at the very time when a popular revolution have just 
 occur in France , and its effect be feel most in the very land of 
 the exile ; - suppose all this , and then say if anything could be more 
 untoward for the hope of the banish man , or furnish his adversary 
 with strong argument against the restoration of his fortune ? but 
 pshaw ! this must be a chimera ! if true , I should have know of it . " 

   as there have be so many revolution in France , it may be 
  convenient to suggest that , accord to the date of this story , 
  Harley no doubt allude to that revolution which exile Charles X. 
  and place Louis Philippe on the throne . 

 " I quite agree with your lordship , - there can be no truth in such a 
 rumour . some Englishman , hearing , perhaps , of the probable pardon of the 
 exile , may have count on an heiress , and spread the report in order to 
 keep off other candidate . by your account , if successful in his suit , 
 he might fail to find an heiress in the bride . " 

 " no doubt of that . whatever might be arrange , I can not   conceive that 
 he would be allow to get at the fortune , though it might be hold 
 in suspense for his child . but indeed it so rarely happen that an 
 italian girl of high name marry a foreigner that we must dismiss this 
 notion with a smile at the long face of the hypothetical fortune - hunter . 
 Heaven help he , if he exist ! " 

 " amen ! " echo Randal , devoutly . 

 " I hear that Peschiera , 's sister be return to England . do you know she 
 too ? " 

 " a little . " 

 " my dear Mr. Leslie , pardon I if I take a liberty not warrant by our 
 acquaintance . against the lady I say nothing . indeed , I have hear some 
 thing which appear to entitle she to compassion and respect . but as to 
 Peschiera all who prize honour suspect he to be a knave , - I know he 
 to be one . now , I think that the long we preserve that abhorrence for 
 knavery which be the generous instinct of youth , why , the fairer will 
 be our manhood , and the more reverend our age . you agree with I ? " 
 and Harley suddenly turn , his eye fall like a flood of light upon 
 Randal 's pale and secret countenance . 

 " to be sure , " murmur the schemer . 

 Harley , survey he , mechanically recoil , and withdraw his arm . 

 fortunately for Randal , who somehow or other feel himself slip into 
 a false position , he scarce know how or why , he be here seize by the 
 arm ; and a clear , open , manly voice cry , " my dear fellow , how be you ? 
 I see you be engage now ; but look into my room when you can , in the 
 course of the day . " 

 and with a bow of excuse for his interruption to Lord L'Estrange , the 
 speaker be then turn away , when Harley say , 

 " no , do not   let I take you from your friend , Mr. Leslie . and you need 
 not be in a hurry to see Egerton ; for I shall claim the privilege of 
 old friendship for the first interview . " 

 " it be Mr. Egerton 's nephew Frank Hazeldan . " 

 " pray , call he back , and present I to he . he have a face that would 
 have go far to reconcile Timon to Athens . " randal obey , and after a 
 few kindly word to Frank , Harley insist on leave the two young man 
 together , and walk on to Downing Street with a brisker step . 




 CHAPTER X. 

 " that Lord L'Estrange seem a very good fellow . " 

 " so - so ; an effeminate humourist , - say the most absurd thing , and 
 fancy they wise . never mind he . you want to speak to I , Frank ? " 

 " yes ; I be so obliged to you for introduce I to Levy . I must tell you

 CHAPTER XV . 

 who have not see , who not admire , that noble picture by Daniel Maclise , 
 which refresh the immortal name of my ancestor Caxton ! for myself , 
 while with national pride I hear the admire murmur of the foreigner 
 who group around it   nothing , indeed , of which our nation may be more 
 proud have they see in the Crystal Palace , - hear , with no less a pride 
 in the generous nature of fellow - artist , the warm applause of living 
 and deathless master sanction the enthusiasm of the popular crowd , 
 what strike I more than the precision of drawing , for which the artist 
 have be always renowne , and the just , though gorgeous affluence of 
 colour which he have more recently acquire , be the profound depth of 
 conception , out of which this great work have so elaborately arise . that 
 monk , with his scowl towards the printer and his back on the Bible over 
 which his form cast a shadow - the whole transition between the medieval 
 Christianity of cell and cloister , and the modern Christianity that 
 rejoice in the daylight , be depict there , in the shadow that obscure 
 the Book , in the scowl that be fix upon the Book - diffuser ; - that 
 sombre , muse face of Richard , Duke of Gloucester , with the beauty 
 of Napoleon , darken to the expression of a fiend , look far and 
 anxiously into futurity , as if foresee there what antagonism be 
 about to be create to the scheme of secret crime and unrelenting 
 force ; the chivalrous head of the accomplished Rivers , see but in 
 profile , under his helmet , as if the age when Chivalry must defend its 
 noble attribute in steel be already half pass away ; and , not least 
 grand of all , the rude thews and sinew of the artisan force into 
 service on the type , and the ray of intellect , fierce , and menacing 
 revolution yet to be , struggle through his rugged feature , and 
 across his low knitted brow , - all this , which show how deeply the 
 idea of the discovery in its good and its evil , its save light and 
 its perilous storm , have sink into the artist 's soul , charm I as 
 effect the exact union between sentiment and execution , which be the 
 true and rare consummation of the Ideal in Art . but observe , while 
 in these personage of the group be depict the deep and graver 
 agency implicate in the bright but terrible invention , observe how 
 little the light epicure of the hour hee the scowl of the monk , or the 
 restless gesture of Richard , or the troubled gleam in the eye of the 
 artisan , King Edward , handsome Poco curante , delight in the surprise 
 of a child , with a new toy , and Clarence , with his curious , yet 
 careless , glance , - all the while Caxton himself , calm , serene , 
 untroubled , intent solely upon the manifestation of his discovery , and 
 no doubt supremely indifferent whether the first proof of it shall be 
 dedicate to a Rivers or an Edward , a Richard or a Henry , Plantagenet or 
 Tudor - ' t be all the same to that comely , gentle - looking man . so be it 
 ever with your Abstract Science ! - not a jot care its passionless logic 
 for the woe or weal of a generation or two . the stream , once emerge 
 from its source , pass on into the great Intellectual Sea , smile over 
 the wretch that it drown , or under the keel of the ship which it serve 
 as a slave . 

 now , when about to commence the present chapter on the Varieties 
 of Life , this masterpiece of thoughtful art force itself on my 
 recollection , and illustrate what I design to convey . in the surface 
 of every age it be often that which but amuse for the moment the 
 ordinary child of pleasant existence , the Edwards and the clarence 
  be they king and duke , or simple of simple subject , which 
 afterwards tower out as the great serious epoch of the time . when we 
 look back upon human record , how the eye settle upon writer as 
 the main landmark of the past ! we talk of the age of Augustus , of 
 Elizabeth , of Louis XIV . , of Anne , as the notable era of the world . 
 why ? because it be their writer who have make they so . interval 
 between one age of author and another lie unnoticed , as the flat and 
 common land of uncultured history . and yet , strange to say , when these 
 author be live amongst we , they occupy a very small portion of our 
 thought , and fill up but desultory interstice in the bituman and tufo 
 wherefrom we build up the Babylon of our life . so it be , and perhaps so 
 it should be , whether it please the conceit of penman or not . life be 
 mean to be active ; and book , though they give the action to future 
 generation , administer but to the holiday of the present . 

 and so , with this long preface , I turn suddenly from the Randals and 
 the Egertons , and the Levys , Avenels , and Peschieras , from the plot 
 and passion of practical life , and drop the reader suddenly into one of 
 those obscure retreat wherein Thought weave , from unnoticed moment , a 
 new link to the chain that unite the age . 

 within a small room , the single window of which open on a fanciful and 
 fairy - like garden that have be before describe , sit a young man alone . 
 he have be write ; the ink be not dry on his manuscript , but his 
 thought have be suddenly interrupt from his work , and his eye , now 
 lift from the letter which have occasion that interruption , sparkle 
 with delight . " he will come , " exclaim the young man ; " come here , - to 
 the home which I owe to he . I have not be unworthy of his friendship . 
 and she - " his breast heave , but the joy fade from his face . " oh , 
 strange , strange , that I feel sad at the thought to see she again ! see 
 she - ah , no ! my own comfort Helen , my own child - angel ! she I can 
 never see again ! the grown woman - that be not my Helen . and yet - and 
 yet , " he resume after a pause , " if ever she read the page in which 
 think flow and tremble under her distant starry light , if ever 
 she see how her image have rest with I , and feel that , while other 
 believe that I invent , I have but remember , will she not , for a 
 moment , be my own Helen again ? again , in heart and in fancy , stand by my 
 side on the desolate bridge , hand in hand , orphan both , as we stand 
 in the day so sorrowful , yet , as I recall they , so sweet ? Helen in 
 England - it be a dream ! " 

 he rise , half - consciously , and go to the window . the fountain play 
 merrily before his eye , and the bird in the aviary carol loud to 
 his ear . " and in this house , " he murmur , " I see she last ! and there , 
 where the fountain now throw its spray on high , - there her benefactor 
 and mine tell I that I be to lose she , that I might win - fame . alas ! " 

 at this time a woman , whose dress be somewhat above her mien and air , 
 which , though not without a certain respectability , be very homely , 
 enter the room ; and see the young man stand thus thoughtful by 
 the window , pause . she be use to his habit ; and since his success 
 in life , have learn to respect they . so she do not disturb his revery , 
 but begin softly to arrange the room , dust , with the corner of her

 rare as virtuous ! 

 the young man turn at last , with a deep , yet not altogether painful 
 sigh , 

 " my dear mother , good day to you . ah , you do well to make the room look 
 its good . happy news ! I expect a visitor ! " 

 " dear I , Leonard , will he want lunch - or what ? " 

 " Nay , I think not , Mother . it be he to whom we owe all , - ' Haec otia 
 fecit . ' pardon my Latin ; it be Lord L'Estrange . " 

 the face of Mrs. Fairfield   the reader have long since divine the name 
 change instantly , and betray a nervous twitch of all the muscle , 
 which give she a family likeness to old Mrs. Avenel . 

 " do not be alarm , Mother . he be the kind - " 

 " do not   talk so ; I can not   bear it ! " cry Mrs. Fairfield . 

 " no wonder you be affect by the recollection of all his benefit . but 
 when once you have see he , you will find yourself ever after at your 
 ease . and so , pray smile and look as good as you be ; for I be proud of 
 your open honest look when you be pleased , Mother . and he must see your 
 heart in your face , as I do . " 

 with this , Leonard put his arm round the widow 's neck and kiss she . 
 she cling to he fondly for a moment , and he feel her tremble from head 
 to foot . then she break from his embrace , and hurry out of the room . 
 Leonard think perhaps she have go to improve her dress , or to carry 
 her housewife energie to the decoration of the other room ; for " the 
 house " be Mrs. Fairfield 's hobby and passion ; and now that she work 
 no more , save for her amusement , it be her main occupation . the hour 
 she contrive to spend daily in bustle about those little room , and 
 leave everything therein to all appearance precisely the same , 
 be among the marvel in life which the genius of Leonard have never 
 comprehend . but she be always so delighted when Mr. Norreys , or some 
 rare visitor come , and say , - Mr. Norreys never fail to do so,-"how 
 neatly all be keep here . what could Leonard do without you , Mrs. 
 Fairfield ? " 

 and , to Norreys 's infinite amusement , Mrs. Fairfield always return the 
 same answer . " ' Deed , sir , and thank you kindly , but ' t be my belief that 
 the drawin'-room would be awful dusty . " 

 once more leave alone , Leonard 's mind return to the state of revery , 
 and his face assume the expression that have now become to it habitual . 
 thus see , he be change much since we last behold he . his cheek be 
 more pale and thin , his lip more firmly compress , his eye more 
 fixed and abstract . you could detect , if I may borrow a touching french 
 expression , that " Sorrow have pass by there . " but the melancholy on his 
 countenance be ineffably sweet and serene , and on his ample forehead 
 there be that power , so rarely see in early youth , - the power that have 
 conquer , and betray its conquest but in calm . the period of doubt , 
 of struggle , of defiance , be go , perhaps forever ; genius and soul 
 be reconcile to human life . it be a face most lovable ; so gentle and 
 peaceful in its character . no want of fire ; on the contrary , the fire 
 be so clear and so steadfast , that it convey but the impression of 
 light . the candour of boyhood , the simplicity of the villager , be 
 still there , - refine by intelligence , but intelligence that seem to 
 have traverse through knowledge , not with the ' footstep , but the wing , 
 unsullie by the mire , tend towards the star , seek through the 
 various grade of be but the lovelier form of truth and goodness ; at 
 home , as should be the art that consummate the beautiful , - 

  " in den heitern Regionen 
  wo die reinen Formen wohnen . " 

   at home - " in the serene region 
  where dwell the pure form . " 

 from this revery Leonard do not seek to rouse himself , till the bell at 
 the garden gate rang loud and shrill ; and then start up and hurry 
 into the hall , his hand be grasp in Harley 's . 




 CHAPTER XVI . 

 a full and happy hour pass away in Harley 's question and Leonard 's 
 answer , - the dialogue that naturally ensue between the two , on the 
 first interview after an absence of year so eventful to the young 
 man . 

 the history of Leonard during this interval be almost solely internal , 
 the struggle of intellect with its own difficulty , the wandering of 
 imagination through its own adventurous world . 

 the first aim of Norreys , in prepare the mind of his pupil for its 
 vocation , have be to establish the equilibrium of its power , to calm 
 into harmony the element rudely shake by the trial and passion of 
 the old hard outer life . 

 the theory of Norreys be briefly this : the education of a superior 
 human being be but the development of idea in one for the benefit of 
 other . to this end , attention should be direct , - 1st , to the value 
 of the idea collect ; 2dly , to their discipline ; 3dly , to their 
 expression . for the first , acquirement be necessary ; for the second , 
 discipline ; for the third , art . the first comprehend knowledge purely 
 intellectual , whether derive from observation , memory , reflection , 
 book , or man , Aristotle or Fleet Street . the second demand training , 
 not only intellectual , but moral ; the purifying and exaltation of 
 motif ; the formation of habit ; in which method be but a part of a 
 divine and harmonious symmetry , a union of intellect and conscience . 
 idea of value , store by the first process ; marshal into force , and 
 place under guidance , by the second , - it be the result of the third , to 
 place they before the world in the most attractive or commanding form . 
 this may be do by action no less than word ; but the adaptation of 
 mean to end , the passage of idea from the brain of one man into the 
 life and soul of all , no less in action than in book , require study . 
 Action have its art as well as literature . here Norreys have but to deal 
 with the calling of the scholar , the formation of the writer , and so 
 to guide the perception towards those variety in the sublime and 
 beautiful , the just combination of which be at once CREATION . Man 
 himself be but a combination of element . he who combine in nature , 
 create in art . such , very succinctly and inadequately express , be 
 the system upon which Norreys proceed to regulate and perfect the 
 great native power of his pupil ; and though the reader may perhaps say 
 that no system lay down by another can either form genius or dictate 
 to its result , yet probably nine - tenth at least of those in whom 
 we recognize the luminary of our race have pass , unconsciously 
 to themselves   for self - education be rarely conscious of its phase , 
 through each of these process . and no one who pause to reflect 
 will deny , that accord to this theory , illustrate by a man of vast 
 experience , profound knowledge , and exquisite taste , the struggle 
 of genius would be infinitely lessen , its vision clear and 
 strengthen , and the distance between effort and success notably 
 abridge . 

 norrey , however , be far too deep a reasoner to fall into the error of 
 modern teacher , who suppose that education can dispense with labour . no 
 mind become muscular without rude and early exercise . labour should be 
 strenuous , but in right direction . all that we can do for it be to save 
 the waste of time in blunder into needless toil . 

 the master have thus first employ his neophyte in arrange and 
 compile material for a great critical work in which Norreys himself 
 be engage . in this stage of scholastic preparation , Leonard be 
 necessarily lead to the acquisition of language , for which he have great 
 aptitude ; the foundation of a large and comprehensive erudition be 
 solidly construct . he trace by the ploughshare the wall of the 
 destine city . habit of accuracy and of generalization become form 
 insensibly ; and that precious faculty which seize , amidst 
 accumulate material , those that serve the object for which they be 
 explore , - that faculty which quadruple all force , by concentrate it 
 on one point , - once rouse into action , give purpose to every toil and 
 quickness to each perception . but Norreys do not confine his pupil 
 solely to the mute world of a library ; he introduce he to some of the 
 first mind in art , science , and letter , and active life . " these , " 
 say he , " be the live idea of the present , out of which book for 
 the future will be write : study they ; and here , as in the volume of 
 the past , diligently amass and deliberately compile . " 

 by degree Norreys lead on that young ardent mind from the selection of 
 idea to their aesthetic analysis , - from compilation to criticism ; but 
 criticism severe , close , and logical , - a reason for each word of praise 
 or of blame . lead in this stage of his career to examine into the law 
 of beauty , a new light break upon his mind ; from amidst the mass of 
 marble he have pile around he rise the vision of the statue . 

 and so , suddenly , one day Norreys say to he , " I need a compil no 
 long , - maintain yourself by your own creation . " and Leonard write , 
 and a work flower up from the seed deep bury , and the soil well 
 clear to the ray of the sun and the healthful influence of expand 
 air . 

 that first work do not penetrate to a very wide circle of reader , not 
 from any perceptible fault of its own - there be luck in these thing ; 
 the first anonymous work of an original genius be rarely at once 
 eminently successful . but the more experienced recognize the promise of 
 the book . publisher , who have an instinct in the discovery of 
 available talent , which often forestall the appreciation of the public , 
 volunteer liberal offer . " be fully successful this time , " say 
 Norreys ; " think not of model nor of style . strike at once at the 
 common human heart , - throw away the cork , swim out boldly . one word 
 more , - never write a page till you have walk from your room to Temple 
 Bar , and , mingle with man , and read the human face , learn why great 
 poet have mostly pass their life in city . " 

 thus Leonard write again , and wake one morning to find himself famous . 
 so far as the chance of all profession dependent on health will 
 permit , present independence , and , with foresight and economy , the 
 prospect of future competence be secure . 

 " and , indeed , " say Leonard , conclude a long but a simple narrative 
 than be here tell , - " indeed , there be some chance that I may obtain at 
 once a sum that will leave I free for the rest of my life to select my 
 own subject , and write without care for remuneration . this be what I 
 call the true   and , perhaps , alas ! the rare   independence of he who 
 devote himself to letter . Norreys , have see my boyish plan for the 
 improvement of certain machinery in the steam engine , insist on my 
 give much time to mechanic . the study that once please I so greatly 
 now seem dull ; but I go into it with good heart ; and the result be , 
 that I have improve so far on my original idea , that my scheme have 
 meet the approbation of one of our most scientific engineer : and I be 
 assure that the patent for it will be purchase of I upon term which 
 I be ashamed to name to you , so disproportioned do they seem to the 
 value of so simple a discovery . meanwhile , I be already rich enough to 
 have realize the two dream of my heart , - to make a home in the cottage 
 where I have last see you and Helen - I mean Miss Digby ; and to invite to 
 that home she who have shelter my infancy . " 

 " your mother , where be she ? let I see she . " 

 Leonard run out to call the widow , but to his surprise and vexation 
 learn that she have quit the house before L'Estrange arrive . 

 he come back , perplex how to explain what seem ungracious and 
 ungrateful , and speak with hesitate lip and flush cheek of the 
 widow 's natural timidity and sense of her own homely station . " and so 
 overpower be she , " add Leonard , " by the recollection of all that we 
 owe to you , that she never hear your name without agitation or tear , 
 and tremble like a leaf at the thought of see you . " 

 " ha ! " say Harley , with visible emotion . " be it so ? " and he bend down , 
 shade his face with his hand . " and , " he renew , after a pause , but 
 not look up - " and you ascribe this fear of see I , this agitation 
 at my name , solely to an exaggerated sense of - of the circumstance 
 attend my acquaintance with yourself ? " 

 " and , perhaps , to a sort of shame that the mother of one you have make 
 her proud of be but a peasant . " 

 " that be all ? " say Harley , earnestly , now look up and fix eye in 
 which stand tear upon Leonard 's ingenuous brow . 

 " oh , my dear Lord , what else can it be ? do not judge she harshly . " 

 L'Estrange arise abruptly , press Leonard 's hand , mutter something 
 not audible , and then draw his young friend 's arm in his , lead he 
 into the garden , and turn the conversation back to its former topic . 

 Leonard 's heart yearn to ask after Helen , and yet something withhold

 she , he could not resist his impulse . " and Helen - Miss Digby - be she 
 much change ? " 

 " change , no - yes ; very much . " 

 " very much ! " Leonard sigh . 

 " I shall see she again ? " 

 " certainly , " say Harley , in a tone of surprise . " how can you doubt it ? 
 and I reserve to you the pleasure of say that you be renowne . 
 you blush ; well , I will say that for you . but you shall give she your 
 book . " 

 " she have not yet read they , then ? - not the last ? the first be not 
 worthy of her attention , " say Leonard , disappointed . " she have only just 
 arrive in England ; and , though your book reach I in Germany , she

 not have conceive that so much beauty could be draw from what appear 
 to I the most commonplace of all suburban garden . why , surely , where 
 that charming fountain now play stand the rude bench in which I read 
 your verse . " 

 " it be true ; I wish to unite all together my happy association . I 
 think I tell you , my Lord , in one of my letter , that I have owe a very

 and generous instruction of a foreigner whom I serve . this fountain 
 be copy from one that I make in his garden , and by the margin of which 
 many a summer day I have sit and dream of fame and knowledge . " 

 " true , you tell I of that ; and your foreigner will be pleased to hear 
 of your success , and no less so of your grateful recollection . by the 
 way , you do not mention his name . " 

 " Riccabocca . " 

 " Riccabocca ! my own dear and noble friend ! - be it possible ? one of my 
 reason for return to England be connect with he . you shall go 
 down with I and see he . I mean to start this evening . " 

 " my dear Lord , " say Leonard , " I think that you may spare yourself so 
 long a journey . I have reason to suspect that Signor Riccabocca be 
 my near neighbour . two day ago I be in the garden , when suddenly

 forbid all scruple of delicacy , and I will go at once to his house . "

 Riccabocca , because he have change his name , - why have you drop your

 " proud poet , this I can comprehend . but from what reason do you assume

 the flush on Leonard 's face become deep . " my Lord , " say he , in a low

 " ah ! " 

 " at a time when my craving after knowledge be likely much to mislead ,

 Harley be no less so . but , as if by a sudden impulse , the soldier bend

 CHAPTER XVII . 

 Lord L'Estrange do not proceed at once to Riecabocca 's house . he be 
 under the influence of a remembrance too deep and too strong to yield 
 easily to the lukewarm claim of friendship . he ride fast and far ; and 
 impossible it would be to define the feeling that pass through a mind 
 so acutely sensitive , and so rootedly tenacious of all affection . when , 
 recall his duty to the italian , he once more strike into the road to 
 Norwood , the slow pace of his horse be significant of his own exhausted 
 spirit ; a deep dejection have succeed to feverish excitement . 
 " vain task , " he murmur , " to wean myself from the dead ! yet I be now 
 betroth to another ; and she , with all her virtue , be not the one 
 to - " he stop short in generous self - rebuke . " too late to think of 
 that ! now , all that should remain to I be to insure the happiness 
 of the life to which I have pledge my own . but - " he sigh as he so 
 murmur . on reach the vicinity of Riccabocca 's house , he put up 
 his horse at a little inn , and proceed on foot across the heathland 
 towards the dull square building , which Leonard 's description have 
 suffice to indicate as the exile 's new home . it be long before any 
 one answer his summon at the gate . not till he have thrice rung do he 
 hear a heavy step on the gravel walk within ; then the wicket within the 
 gate be partially draw aside , a dark eye gleam out , and a voice in 
 imperfect English ask who be there . 

 " Lord L'Estrange ; and if I be right as to the person I seek , that name 
 will at once admit I . " 

 the door fly open as do that of the mystic cavern at the sound 
 of " Open , Sesame ; " and Giacomo , almost weep with joyous emotion , 
 exclaim in Italian , " the good Lord ! Holy San Giacomo ! thou hast hear 
 I at last ! we be safe now . " and drop the blunderbuss with which he 
 have take the precaution to arm himself , he lift Harley 's hand to his 
 lip , in the affectionate greeting familiar to his countryman . 

 " and the padrone ? " ask Harley , as he enter the jealous precinct . 

 " oh , he be just go out ; but he will not be long . you will wait for 
 he ? " 

 " certainly . what lady be that I see at the far end of the garden ? " 

 " bless she , it be our signorina . I will run and tell she you be come . " 

 " that I be come ; but she can not know I even by name . " 

 " ah , Excellency , can you think so ? many and many a time have she talk 
 to I of you , and I have hear she pray to the holy Madonna to bless 
 you , and in a voice so sweet - " 

 " Stay , I will present myself to she . go into the house , and we will wait 
 without for the padrone . Nay , I need the air , my friend . " Harley , as he 
 say this , break from Giacomo , and approach Violante . 

 the poor child , in her solitary walk in the obscurer part of the dull 
 garden , have escape the eye of Giacomo when he have go forth to answer 
 the bell ; and she , unconscious of the fear of which she be the object , 
 have feel something of youthful curiosity at the summon at the gate , 
 and the sight of a stranger in close and friendly conference with the 
 unsocial Giacomo . 

 as Harley now near she with that singular grace of movement which 
 belong to he , a thrill shot through her heart , she know not why . she 
 do not recognize his likeness to the sketch take by her father from 
 his recollection of Harley 's early youth . she do not guess who he be ; 
 and yet she feel herself colour , and , naturally fearless though she be , 
 turn away with a vague alarm . 

 " Pardon my want of ceremony , Signorina , " say Harley , in Italian ; " but I 
 be so old a friend of your father 's that I can not feel as a stranger to 
 yourself . " 

 then Violante lift to he her dark eye so intelligent and so 
 innocent , - eye full of surprise , but not displeased surprise . and 
 Harley himself stand amazed , and almost abash , by the rich and 
 marvellous beauty that beam upon he . " my father 's friend , " she say 
 hesitatingly , " and I never to have see you ! " 

 " ah , Signorina , " say Harley   and something of its native humour , half 
 arch , half sad , play round his lip , " you be mistaken there ; you have 
 see I before , and you receive I much more kindly then . " 

 " Signor ! " say Violante , more and more surprised , and with a yet rich 
 colour on her cheek . 

 Harley , who have now recover from the first effect of her beauty , and 
 who regard she as man of his year and character be apt to regard 
 lady in their teen , as more child than woman , suffer himself to be 
 amuse by her perplexity ; for it be in his nature that the graver and

 to his spirit . 

 " indeed , Signorina , " say he , demurely , " you insist then on place 
 one of those fair hand in mine ; the other   forgive I the fidelity of 
 my recollection   be affectionately throw around my neck . " 

 " Signor ! " again exclaim Violante ; but this time there be anger in her

 look of pride and resentment . 

 Harley smile again , but with so much kindly sweetness , that the anger

 since then , but I like you well as you be ; not but what I hope to

 " alas ! no ; but the female heart be so capricious and fickle ! you press 
 it upon I , I assure you . I own that I be not loath to accept it . " 

 " press it ! press what ? " 

 " your kiss , my child , " say Harley ; and then add , with a serious 
 tenderness , " and I again say that I hope to return it some day , when I 
 see you , by the side of father and of husband , in your native land , - the 
 fair bride on whom the sky of Italy ever smile ! and now , pardon a 
 hermit and a soldier for his rude jest , and give your hand , in token of

 Violante , who at the first word of his address have recoil , with a 
 vague belief that the stranger be out of his mind , spring forward as it 
 close , and in all the vivid enthusiasm of her nature press the hand 
 hold out to she with both her own . " Harley L'Estrange ! the preserver of 
 my father 's life ! " she cry ; and her eye be fix on his with such

 dream , - she think but of he who have save her father . but , as his 
 eye sink before her own , and his head , uncover , bow over the hand 
 he hold , she recognize the likeness to the feature on which she have 
 so often gaze . the first bloom of youth be go , but enough of youth 
 still remain to soften the lapse of year , and to leave to manhood the 
 attraction which charm the eye . instinctively she withdraw her hand 
 from his clasp , and in her turn look down . 

 in this pause of embarrassment to both , Riccabocca let himself into the 
 garden by his own latch - key , and , startle to see a man by the side of 
 Violante , spring forward with an abrupt and angry cry . Harley hear , and 
 turn . 

 as if restore to courage and self - possession by the sense of her 
 father 's presence , Violante again take the hand of the visitor . 
 " Father , " she say simply , " it be he , - he be come at last . " and then , 
 retire a few step , she contemplate they both ; and her face be 
 radiant with happiness , as if something , long silently miss and look 
 for , be as silently find , and life have no more a want , nor the heart a 
 void . 




 BOOK TENTH . 




 INITIAL chapter . 

 UPON this fact , - that the world be still MUCH the same as it always have 
 be . 

 it be observe by a very pleasant writer , read nowadays only by the 
 brave pertinacious few who still struggle hard to rescue from the House 
 of Pluto the soul of depart author , jostle and chase as those 
 soul be by the noisy footstep of the living , - it be observe by the 
 admirable Charron , that " judgment and wisdom be not only the good , but 
 the happy portion God Almighty hath distribute amongst man ; for 
 though this distribution be make with a very uneven hand , yet nobody 
 think himself stint or ill - deal with , but he that hath never so 
 little be content in this respect . " 

 and , certainly , the present narrative may serve in notable illustration 
 of the remark so dryly make by the witty and wise preacher . for whether 
 our friend Riccabocca deduce theory for daily life from the great 
 folio of Machiavelli ; or that promise young gentleman , Mr. Randal 
 Leslie , interpret the power of knowledge into the art of be too 
 know for dull honest folk to cope with he ; or acute Dick Avenel 
 push his way up the social ascent with a blow for those before , and a 
 kick for those behind he , after the approved fashion of your strong New 
 man ; or Baron Levy - that cynical impersonation of Gold - compare himself 
 to the Magnetic Rock in the arabian tale , to which the nail in every 
 ship that approach the influence of the loadstone fly from the plank , 
 and a shipwreck per day add its waif to the Rock , - questionless , at 
 least ; it be , that each of those personage believe that Providence have 
 bestow on he an eld son 's inheritance of wisdom . nor , be we to 
 glance towards the obscurer path of life , should we find good Parson 
 Dale deem himself bad off than the rest of the world in this precious 
 commodity , - as , indeed , he have signally evince of late in that shrewd 
 guess of his touching Professor Moss . even plain Squire Hazeldean take 
 it for grant that he could teach Audley Egerton a thing or two worth 
 know in politic ; Mr. Stirn think that there be no branch of useful 
 lore on which he could not instruct the squire ; while Sprott the tinker , 
 with his bag full of tract and lucifer match , regard the whole 
 framework of modern society , from a rick to a constitution , with the 
 profound disdain of a revolutionary philosopher . consider that every 
 individual thus bring into the stock of the world so vast a share of 
 intelligence , it can not but excite our wonder to find that Oxenstiern be 
 popularly hold to be right when he say , " see , my son , how little wisdom 
 it require to govern state , " - that be , Men ! that so many million 
 of person , each with a profound assurance that he be possess of an 
 exalt sagacity , should concur in the ascendancy of a few inferior 
 intellect , accord to a few stupid , prosy , matter - of - fact rule as 
 old as the hill , be a phenomenon very discreditable to the spirit and 
 energy of the aggregate human specie ! it create no surprise that one 
 sensible watch - dog should control the movement of a flock of silly 
 grass - eat sheep ; but that two or three silly grass - eat 
 sheep should give the law to whole flock of such mighty sensible 
 watch - dog - Diavolo ! Dr. Riecabocca , explain that , if you can ! and 
 wonderfully strange it be , that notwithstanding all the march of 
 enlightenment , notwithstanding our progressive discovery in the 
 law of Nature , our railway , steam - engine , animal magnetism , and 
 electrobiology , - we have never make any improvement that be generally 
 acknowledge , since man cease to be troglodyte and nomad , in the 
 old - fashioned gamut of flat and sharp , which attune into irregular 
 social jog - trot all the generation that pass from the cradle to the 
 grave ; still , " the desire for something have have not " impel all the 
 energy that keep we in movement , for good or for ill , accord to the 
 check or the direction of each favourite desire . 

 a friend of mine once say to a millionaire , whom he see forever engage 
 in make money which he never seem to have any pleasure in spending , 
 " pray , Mr - - , will you answer I one question : you be say to have 
 two million , and you spend L600 a year . in order to rest and enjoy , 
 what will content you ? " 

 " a little more , " answer the millionaire . that " little more " be the 
 mainspring of civilization . nobody ever get it ! 

 " Philus , " saith a latin writer , " be not so rich as Laelius ; Laelius be 
 not so rich as Scipio ; Scipio be not so rich as Crassus ; and Crassus 
 be not so rich - as he wish to be ! " if John Bull be once content , 
 Manchester might shut up its mill . it be the " little more " that make a 
 mere trifle of the National Debt ! - long life to it ! 

 still , mend our law - book as we will , one be force to confess that 
 knave be often see in fine linen , and honest man in the most shabby 
 old rag ; and still , notwithstanding the exception , knavery be a very 
 hazardous game , and honesty , on the whole , by far the good policy . 
 still , most of the ten Commandments remain at the core of all the 
 Pandects and Institutes that keep our hand off our neighbour ' 
 throat , wife , and pocket ; still , every year show that the parson 's 
 maxim - " non quieta movere " - be as prudent for the health of community 
 as when Apollo recommend his votary not to rake up a fever by 
 stir the Lake Camarina ; still , people , thank Heaven , decline to 
 reside in parallelogram , and the sure token that we live under a free 
 government be when we be govern by person whom we have a full 
 right to imply , by our censure and ridicule , be blockhead compare to 
 ourselves ! stop that delightful privilege , and , by Jove ! sir , there be 
 neither pleasure nor honour in be govern at all ! you might as well 
 be - a Frenchman ! 




 CHAPTER II . 

 the Italian and his friend be closet together . 

 " and why have you leave your home in - - -shire , and why this new change 
 of name ? " 

 " peschiera be in England . " 

 " I know it . " 

 " and bent on discover I ; and , it be say , of steal from I my 
 child . " 

 " he have have the assurance to lay wager that he will win the hand 
 of your heiress . I know that too ; and therefore I have come to 
 England , - first to baffle his design - for I do not think your fear 
 altogether exaggerated , - and next to learn from you how to follow up 
 a clew which , unless I be too sanguine , may lead to his ruin , and your 
 unconditional restoration . listen to I . you be aware that , after 
 the skirmish with Peschiera 's armed hireling send in search of you , I 
 receive a polite message from the austrian government , request I 
 to leave its italian domain . now , as I hold it the obvious duty of any 
 foreigner admit to the hospitality of a State , to refrain from all 
 participation in its civil disturbance , so I think my honour assail 
 at this intimation , and go at once to Vienna , to explain to the 
 minister there   to whom I be personally know , that though I have , as 
 become man to man , aid to protect a refugee , who have take shelter 
 under my roof , from the infuriate soldier at the command of his 
 private foe , I have not only not share in any attempt at revolt , but 
 dissuade , as far as I could , my italian friend from their enterprise ; 
 and that because , without discuss its merit , I believe , as a 
 military man and a cool spectator , the enterprise could only terminate 
 in fruitless bloodshed . I be enable to establish my explanation 
 by satisfactory proof ; and my acquaintance with the minister assume 
 something of the character of friendship . I be then in a position to 
 advocate your cause , and to state your original reluctance to enter into 
 the plot of the insurgent . I admit freely that you have such natural 
 desire for the independence of your native land , that , have the standard 
 of Italy be boldly hoist by its legitimate chief , or at the common 
 uprising of its whole people , you would have be find in the van , 
 amidst the rank of your countryman ; but I maintain that you would 
 never have share in a conspiracy frantic in itself , and defile by the 
 lawless scheme and sordid ambition of its main projector , have you not 
 be betray and decoy into it by the misrepresentation and 
 domestic treachery of your kinsman , - the very man who denounce you . 
 unfortunately , of this statement I have no proof but your own word . I 
 make , however , so far an impression in your favour , and , it may be , 
 against the traitor , that your property be not confiscate to the 
 State , nor hand over , upon the plea of your civil death , to your 
 kinsman . " 

 " how ! - I do not understand . Peschiera have the property ? " 

 " he hold the revenue but of one half upon pleasure , and they would be 
 withdraw , could I succeed in establish the case that exist against 
 he . I be forbid before to mention this to you ; the minister , not 
 inexcusably , submit you to the probation of unconditional exile . 
 your grace might depend upon your own forbearance from further 
 conspiracy - forgive the word . I need not say I be permit to return 
 to Lombardy . I find , on my arrival , that - that your unhappy wife 
 have be to my house , and exhibit great despair at hearing of my 
 departure . " 

 Riccabocca knit his dark brow , and breathe hard . 

 " I do not judge it necessary to acquaint you with this circumstance , 
 nor do it much affect I . I believe in her guilt - and what could now 
 avail her remorse , if remorse she feel ? shortly afterwards , I hear that 
 she be no more . " 

 " yes , " mutter Riccabocca , ' she die in the same year that I leave 
 Italy . it must be a strong reason that can excuse a friend for remind 
 I even that she once live ! " 

 " I come at once to that reason , " say L'Estrange , gently . " this autumn I 
 be roam through Switzerland , and , in one of my pedestrian excursion 
 amidst the mountain , I meet with an accident , which confine I for some 
 day to a sofa at a little inn in an obscure village . my hostess be 
 an italian ; and as I have leave my servant at a town at some distance , I 
 require her attention till I could write to he to come to I . I be 
 thankful for her care , and amuse by her italian babble . we become very 
 good friend . she tell I she have be servant to a lady of great rank , 
 who have die in Switzerland ; and that , be enrich by the generosity 
 of her mistress , she have marry a swiss innkeeper , and his people have 
 become her . my servant arrive , and my hostess learn my name , which 
 she do not know before . she come into my room greatly agitated . in 
 brief , this woman have be servant to your wife . she have accompany 
 she to my villa , and know of her anxiety to see I , as your friend . 
 the Government have assign to your wife your palace at Milan , with a 
 competent income . she have refuse to accept of either . fail to see 
 I , she have set off towards England , resolve upon see yourself ; for 
 the journal have state that to England you have escape . " 

 " she dare ! shameless ! and see , but a moment before , I have forget 
 all but her grave in a foreign soil , - and these tear have forgive she , " 
 murmur the italian . 

 " let they forgive she still , " say Harley , with all his exquisite 
 sweetness of look and tone . " I resume . on enter Switzerland your 
 wife 's health , which you know be always delicate , give way . to fatigue 
 and anxiety succeed fever , and delirium ensue . she have take with she 
 but this one female attendant - the sole one she could trust - on leave 
 home . she suspect Peschiera to have bribe her household . in the 
 presence of this woman she rave of her innocence , in accent of terror 
 and aversion denounce your kinsman , and call on you to vindicate she 
 name and your own . " 

 " raving indeed ! Poor Paulina ! " groan Riccabocca , cover his face 
 with both hand . 

 " but in her delirium there be lucid interval . in one of these she 
 rise , in spite of all her servant could do to restrain she , take from 
 her desk several letter , and read they over , exclaim piteously , 
 ' but how to get they to he ; whom to trust ? and his friend be go ! ' 
 then an idea seem suddenly to flash upon she , for she utter a joyous 
 exclamation , sit down , and write long and rapidly , enclose what she 
 write with all the letter , in one packet , which she seal carefully , 
 and bade her servant carry to the post , with many injunction to take 
 it with her own hand , and pay the charge on it . ' for oh ! ' say she   I 
 repeat the word as my informant tell they to I , - ' for oh ! this be my 
 sole chance to prove to my husband that , though I have err , I be not 
 the guilty thing he believe I ; the sole chance , too , to redeem my 
 error , and restore , perhaps , to my husband his country , to my child 
 her heritage . ' the servant take the letter to the post ; and when she 
 return , her lady be asleep , with a smile upon her face . but from that 
 sleep she wake again delirious , and before the next morning her soul 
 have flee . " here Riccabocca lift one hand from his face and grasp 
 Harley 's arm , as if mutely beseech he to pause . the heart of the man 
 struggle hard with his pride and his philosophy ; and it be long before 
 Harley could lead he to regard the worldly prospect which this last 
 communication from his wife might open to his ruin fortune , - not , 
 indeed , till Riccabocca have persuade himself , and half persuade Harley 
  for strong , indeed , be all presumption of guilt against the dead , 
 that his wife 's protestation of innocence from all but error have be 
 but raving . 

 " be this as it may , " say Harley , " there seem every reason to suppose 
 that the letter enclose be Peschiera 's correspondence , and that , if 
 so , these would establish the proof of his influence over your wife , 
 and of his perfidious machination against yourself . I resolve , before 
 come hither , to go round by Vienna . there I hear , with dismay , that 
 Peschiera have not only obtain the imperial sanction to demand your 
 daughter 's hand , but have boast to his profligate circle that he should 
 succeed ; and he be actually on his road to England . I see at once that 
 could this design , by any fraud or artifice , be successful with Violante 
  for of your consent , I need not say , I do not dream , the discovery 
 of the packet , whatever its content , would be useless ; Peschiera 's end 
 would be secure . I see also that his success would suffice forever to 
 clear his name ; for his success must imply your consent   it would be to 
 disgrace your daughter , to assert that she have marry without it , and 
 your consent would be his acquittal . I see , too , with alarm , that to 
 all mean for the accomplishment of his project he would be urge by 
 despair ; for his debt be great , and his character nothing but new 
 wealth can support . I know that he be able , bold , determine , and that 
 he have take with he a large supply of money borrow upon usury , - in 
 a word , I tremble for you both . I have now see your daughter , and I 
 tremble no more . accomplished seducer as Peschiera boast himself , the 
 first look upon her face so sweet , yet so noble , convince I that she 
 be proof against a legion of Peschieras . now , then , return we to this

 " do it exist still ? into whose hand would it have fall ?

 CHAPTER III . 

 the next day a somewhat old - fashioned , but exceedingly patrician ,

 " help I much by his instruction ; more , perhaps , by his very fault . 
 you can not guess , Helen , - I beg pardon , Miss Digby , but I forget that we 
 be no long child , - you can not guess how much we man , and more than 
 all , perhaps , we writer whose task it be to unravel the web of human 
 action , owe even to our own past error ; and if we learn nothing by 
 the error of other , we should be dull indeed . we must know where the 
 road divide , and have mark where they lead to , before we can erect 
 our sign - post ; and book be the sign - post in human life . " 

 " book ! and I have not yet read your . and Lord L'Estrange tell I you 
 be famous now . yet you remember I still , - the poor orphan child , whom 
 you first see weeping at her father 's grave , and with whom you burden 
 your own young life , over - burden already . no , still call I Helen - you 
 must always be to I a brother ! Lord L'Estrange feel that ; he say so 
 to I when he tell I that we be to meet again . he be so generous , so 
 noble . brother ! " cry Helen , suddenly , and extend her hand , with 
 a sweet but sublime look in her gentle face , - " brother , we will never 
 forfeit his esteem ; we will both do our good to repay he ! will we 
 not ? - say so ! " 

 Leonard feel overpower by contend and unanalyzed emotion . touch 
 almost to tear by the affectionate address , thrill by the hand 
 that press his own , and yet with a vague fear , a consciousness that 
 something more than the word themselves be imply , - something that 
 check all hope . and this word " brother , " once so precious and so dear , 
 why do he shrink from it now ; why could he not too say the sweet word 
 ' sister " ? 

 " she be above I now and evermore ! " he think mournfully ; and the tone 
 of his voice , when he speak again , be change . the appeal to renew 
 intimacy but make he more distant , and to that appeal itself he make no 
 direct answer ; for Mrs. Riccabocca , now turn round , and point to 
 the cottage which come in view , with its picturesque gable - end , cry 
 out , 

 " but be that your house , Leonard ? I never see anything so pretty . " 

 " you do not remember it then , " say Leonard to Helen , in accent of 
 melancholy reproach , - " there where I see you last ? I doubt whether 
 to keep it exactly as it be , and I say , ' - no ! the association be 
 not change because we try to surround it with whatever beauty we can 
 create ; the dearer the association , the more the Beautiful become to it 
 natural . ' perhaps you do not   understand this , - perhaps it be only we poor 
 poet who do . " 

 " I understand it , " say Helen , gently . she look wistfully at the 
 cottage . 

 " so change ! I have so often picture it to myself , never , never like 
 this ; yet I love it , commonplace as it be to my recollection ; and the 
 garret , and the tree in the carpenter 's yard . " 

 she do not give these thought utterance . and they now enter the 
 garden . 




 CHAPTER IV . 

 Mrs. Fairfield be a proud woman when she receive Mrs. Riccabocca and 
 Violante in her grand house ; for a grand house to she be that cottage 
 to which her boy Lenny have bring her home . Proud , indeed , ever be 
 Widow Fairfield ; but she think then in her secret heart , that if ever 
 she could receive in the drawing - room of that grand house the great Mrs. 
 Hazeldean , who have so lecture she for refuse to live any long in 
 the humble , tenement rent of the squire , the cup of human bliss would 
 be fill , and she could contentedly die of the pride of it . she do 
 not much notice Helen , - her attention be too absorb by the lady who 
 renew their old acquaintance with she , and she carry they all over 
 the house , yea , into the very kitchen ; and so , somehow or other , there

 as if he divine her thought , Leonard smile and say , " it would be a 
 cruel kindness to the spider , if the gentle band in the world try to 
 set its cobweb to right . " 

 HELEN . - " you be not quite so bad in the old day . "

 have more book now , and more money . my present housekeeper let I take

 Digby - " 

 HELEN . - " not Miss Digby ; sister , if you like . " 

 LEONARD   evade the word that imply so forbid an 
 affinity . - " Helen , will you grant I a favour ? your eye and your smile 
 say ' yes . ' will you lay aside , for one minute , your shawl and bonnet ? 
 what ! can you be surprised that I ask it ? can you not understand that 
 I wish for one minute to think that you be at home again under this 
 roof ? " 

 Helen cast down her eye , and seem troubled ; then she raise they , 
 with a soft angelic candour in their dovelike blue , and , as if in

 so there she sit , amongst the dull book , by his table , near the open 
 window , her fair hair part on her forehead , look so good , so calm ,

 must go now , " and she hastily take up her shawl and bonnet .

 child - angel she have not yet learn . 

 Helen receive these apology with her usual sweetness . " Nay , " she

 fair speaker more curiously than she have yet do . " pretty , nice - speak 
 thing , " think the widow ; " as nice - speak as Miss Violante , and 
 humbler - look like , - though , as to dress , I never see anything so

 Helen now appropriate Mrs. Riccabocca 's arm ; and , after a kind 
 leave - taking with the widow , the lady return towards Riccabocca 's

 Mrs. Fairfield , however , run after they with Leonard 's hat and glove , 
 which he have forget . 

 " ' deed , boy , " she say , kindly , yet scoldingly , " but there would be no more 
 fine book , if the Lord have not fix your head on your shoulder . you

 Helen could not resist turn round , and look at Leonard , with a sly 
 smile . 

 the widow see the smile , and catch Leonard by the arm , whisper , 
 " but where before have you see that pretty young lady ? old friend ! " 

 " ah , Mother , " say Leonard , sadly , " it be a long tale ; you have hear 
 the beginning , who can guess the end ? " and he escape . but Helen still

 yet he be by the side of Violante , and she speak to he with such

 CHAPTER V. 

 no soon have Lady Lansmere find herself alone with Riccabocca and

 to reveal to I your incognito , for I should have discover it . you

 world than I do now , during your first visit to England , and once sit 
 next to you at dinner at Carlton House . Nay , no compliment , but listen

 design of an audacious and unprincipled adventurer , I may call he ; for 
 adventurer be of all rank . suffer your daughter to come to I on a 
 visit , as long as you please . with I , at least , she will be safe ; and 
 if you , too , and the - " 

 " stop , my dear madam , " interrupt Riccabocca , with great vivacity ; 
 " your kindness overpower I . I thank you most gratefully for your 
 invitation to my child ; but - " 

 " Nay , " in his turn interrupt Harley , " no but . I be not aware of my 
 mother 's intention when she enter this room . but since she whisper 
 it to I , I have reflect on it , and be convinced that it be but a

 secret ; still I have reason to believe that the count guess Randal 's 
 acquaintance with you . Audley Egerton this morning tell I he have 
 gather that , not from the young man himself , but from question put to 
 himself by Madame di Negra ; and Peschiera might and would set spy to 
 track Leslie to every house that he visit , - might and would , still more

 conspirator . what he could do I know not ; but an assassin can penetrate 
 into a camp , and a traitor can creep through close wall to one 's 
 hearth . with my mother , Violante must be safe ; that you can not oppose . 
 and why not come yourself ? " 

 Riccabocca have no reply to these argument , so far as they affect 
 Violante ; indeed , they awaken the almost superstitious terror with 
 which he regard his enemy , and he consent at once that Violante 
 should accept the invitation proffer . but he refuse it for himself 
 and Jemima . 

 " to say truth , " say he , simply , " I make a secret vow , on re - enter 
 England , that I would associate with none who know the rank I have 
 formerly hold in my own land . I feel that all my philosophy be need 
 to reconcile and habituate myself to my altered circumstance . in order 
 to find in my present existence , however humble , those blessing which 
 make all life noble , - dignity and peace , - it be necessary for poor , 
 weak human nature wholly to dismiss the past . it would unsettle I 
 sadly , could I come to your house , renew awhile , in your kindness and 
 respect - nay , in the very atmosphere of your society - the sense of what 
 I have be ; and then   should the more than doubtful chance of recall 
 from my exile fail I   to awake , and find myself for the rest of life 
 what I be . and though , be I alone , I might trust myself perhaps to the 
 danger , yet my wife : she be happy and content now ; would she be so , 
 if you have once spoil she for the simple position of Dr. Riccabocca 's 
 wife ? should I not have to listen to regret and hope and fear that 
 would prick sharp through my thin cloak of philosophy ? even as it be , 
 since in a moment of weakness I confide my secret to she , I have have 
 ' my rank ' throw at I , - with a careless hand , it be true , but it hit 
 hard nevertheless . no stone hurt like one take from the ruin of one 's 
 own home ; and the grander the home , why , the heavy the stone ! protect , 
 dear madam , protect my daughter , since her father doubt his own power 
 to do so . but - ask no more . " 

 Riccabocca be immovable here ; and the matter be settle as he decide , 
 it be agree that Violante should be still style but the daughter of 
 Dr. Riccabocca . 

 " and now , one word more , " say Harley . " do not confide to Mr. Leslie 
 these arrangement ; do not let he know where Violante be place , - at 
 least , until I authorize such confidence in he . it be sufficient 
 excuse that it be no use to know unless he call to see she , and his 
 movement , as I say before , may be watch . you can give the same 
 reason to suspend his visit to yourself . suffer I , meanwhile , to 
 mature my judgment on this young man . in the meanwhile , also , I think 
 that I shall have mean of ascertain the real nature of Peschiera 's 
 scheme . his sister have seek to know I ; I will give she the occasion . 
 I have hear some thing of she in my last residence abroad , which make 
 I believe that she can not be wholly the count 's tool in any scheme 
 nakedly villanous ; that she have some fine quality in she than I once 
 suppose ; and that she can be win from his influence . it be a state of 
 war ; we will carry it into the enemy 's camp . you will promise I , then , 
 to refrain from all further confidence in Mr. Leslie ? " 

 " for the present , yes , " say Riccabocca , reluctantly . 

 " do not even say that you have see I , unless he first tell you that 
 I be in England , and wish to learn your residence . I will give he full 
 occasion to do so . pish ! do not   hesitate ; you know your own proverb - 

  " ' boccha chiusa , ed occhio aperto 
  Non fece mai nissun deserto . ' 

 " the closed mouth and the open eye , ' etc . " 

 " that be very true , " say the doctor , much strike . " very true . ' in boccha 
 chiusa non c'entrano mosche . ' one can not   swallow fly if one keep one 's 
 mouth shut . Corpo di Bacco ! that be very true indeed . " 




 CHAPTER VI . 

 Violante and Jemima be both greatly surprised , as the reader may 
 suppose , when they hear , on their return , the arrangement already 
 make for the former . the countess insist on take she at once , and 
 Riccabocca briefly say , " certainly , the soon the well . " Violante 
 be stunned and bewilder . Jemima hasten to make up a little bundle 
 of thing necessary , with many a woman 's sigh that the poor wardrobe 
 contain so few thing befit . but among the clothe she slip a 
 purse , contain the saving of month , perhaps of year , and with it a

 all that be proper for her father 's child . there be always something

 member from a quiet household . the small party break into still small

 Harley have first meet with Mr. Oran , whom he call his friend ; but she 
 be too highbred to inquire , or to express any wonder that rank should

 their acquaintance abroad . 

 Harley converse with Helen . - " you be not sorry that Violante be come 
 to we ? she will be just such a companion for you as I could desire ; of 
 your own year too . " 

 HELEN   ingenuously . - " it be hard to think I be not young than she 
 be . " 

 HARLEY . - " why , my dear Helen ? " 

 HELEN . - " she be so brilliant . she talk so beautifully . and I - " 

 HARLEY . - " and you want but the habit of talk , to do justice to your 
 own beautiful thought . " 

 Helen look at he gratefully , but shake her head . it be a common 
 trick of her , and always when she be praise . 

 at last the preparation be make , the farewell be say , Violante be

 Riccabocca , Jemima , and Jackeymo continue to gaze after it from the

 " she be go , " say Jackeymo , brush his eye with his coat - sleeve . 
 " but it be a load off one 's mind . " 

 " and another load on one 's heart , " murmur Riccabocca . " do not   cry , 
 Jemima ; it may be bad for you , and bad for he that be to come . it
