 should not like to have a son who have a more than usual propensity to 
 tear . " 

 the poor philosopher try to smile ; but it be a bad attempt . he go 
 slowly in , and shut himself with his book . but he could not read . his 
 whole mind be unsettled . and though , like all parent , he have be

 go but for a while , a string seem break in the Music of Home . 




 CHAPTER VII . 

 the evening of the same day , as Egerton , who be to entertain a large 
 party at dinner , be change his dress , Harley walk into his room . 

 Egerton dismiss his valet by a sign , and continue his toilet . 

 " excuse I , my dear Harley , I have only ten minute to give you . I 
 expect one of the royal duke , and punctuality be the stern virtue of 
 man of business , and the graceful courtesy of prince . " 

 Harley have usually a jest for his friend 's aphorism ; but he have none 
 now . he lay his hand kindly on Egerton 's shoulder . " before I speak of 
 my business , tell I how you be , - well ? " 

 " well , - nay , I be always well . Pooh ! I may look a little tired , - year 
 of toil will tell on the countenance . but that matter little : the 
 period of life have pass with I when one care how one look in the 
 glass . " 

 as he speak , Egerton complete his dress , and come to the hearth , 
 stand there , erect and dignified as usual , still far handsomer than 
 many a young man , and with a form that seem to have ample vigour to 
 support for many a year the sad and glorious burden of power . 

 " so now to your business , Harley . " 

 " in the first place , I want you to present I , at the early 
 opportunity , to Madame di Negra . you say she wish to know I . " 

 " be you serious ? " 

 " yes . " 

 " well , then , she receive this evening . I do not mean to go ; but when 
 my party break up - " 

 " you can call for I at the Travellers . do ! " 

 " next , you know Lady Jane Horton well even than I do , at least in the 
 last year of her life . " Harley sigh , and Egerton turn and stir 
 the fire . 

 " pray , do you ever see at her house , or hear she speak of , a Mrs. 
 Bertram ? " 

 " of whom ? " say Egerton , in a hollow voice , his face still turn 
 towards the fire . 

 " a Mrs. Bertram ; but heaven ! my dear fellow , what be the matter ? be 
 you ill ? " 

 " a spasm at the heart , that be all ; do not   ring , I shall be well 
 presently ; go on talk . Mrs. - why do you ask ? " 

 " why ? I have hardly time to explain ; but I be , as I tell you , resolve 
 on right my old italian friend , if Heaven will help I , as it ever 
 do help the just when they bestir themselves ; and this Mrs. Bertram be 
 mix up in my friend 's affair . " 

 " his ! how be that possible ? " 

 Harley rapidly and succinctly explain . Audley listen attentively , 
 with his eye fix on the floor , and still seem to labour under 
 great difficulty of breathing . 

 at last he answer , " I remember something of this Mrs. - Mrs. - Bertram . 
 but your inquiry after she would be useless . I think I have hear that 
 she be long since dead ; nay , I be sure of it . " 

 " dead ! - that be most unfortunate . but do you know any of her relation 
 or friend ? can you suggest any mode of trace this packet , if it come 
 to her hand ? " 

 " no . " 

 " and Lady Jane have scarcely any friend that I remember except my mother , 
 and she know nothing of this Mrs. Bertram . how unlucky ! I think I shall 
 advertise . yet , no . I could only distinguish this Mrs. Bertram from any 
 other of the same name , by state with whom she have go abroad , and 
 that would catch the attention of Peschiera , and set he to counterwork 
 we . " 

 " and what avail it ? " say Egerton . " she whom you seek be no more - no 
 more ! " he pause , and go on rapidly : " the packet do not arrive 
 in England till year after her death , be no doubt return to the 
 post - office , be destroy long ago . " 

 Harley look very much disappointed . Egerton go on in a sort of set , 
 mechanical voice , as if not think of what he say , but speak from 
 the dry practical mode of reasoning which be habitual to he , and by 
 which the man of the world destroy the hope of an enthusiast . then 
 start up at the sound of the first thunder knock at the street 
 door , he say , " Hark ! you must excuse I . " 

 " I leave you , my dear Audley . but I must again ask , be you well now ? " 

 " much , much , - quite well : I will call for you , - probably between eleven 
 and twelve . " 




 CHAPTER VIII . 

 if any one could be more surprised at see Lord L'Estrange at the 
 house of Madame di Negra that evening than the fair hostess herself , 
 it be randal Leslie . something instinctively tell he that this visit 
 threaten interference with whatever might be his ultimate project 
 in regard to Riccabocca and Violante . but Randal Leslie be not one of 
 those who shrink from an intellectual combat . on the contrary , he be 
 too confident of his power of intrigue not to take a delight in their 
 exercise . he could not conceive that the indolent Harley could be a 
 match for his own restless activity and dogged perseverance . but in a 
 very few moment fear creep on he . no man of his day could produce a 
 more brilliant effect than Lord L'Estrange , when he deign to desire 
 it . without much pretence to that personal beauty which strike at first 
 sight , he still retain all the charm of countenance , and all the grace 
 of manner , which have make he in boyhood the spoiled darling of society . 
 Madame di Negra have collect but a small circle round she ; still it be 
 of the elite of the great world , - not , indeed , those more precise and 
 reserve dame de chateau , whom the light and easy of the fair 
 dispenser of fashion ridicule as prude ; but nevertheless , lady 
 be there , as unblemished in reputation , as high in rank , flirt and 
 coquette , perhaps , - nothing more ; in short , " charming woman , " - the 
 gay butterfly that hover over the stiff parterre . and there be 
 ambassador and minister , and wit and brilliant debater , and 
 first - rate dandy   dandy , when first - rate , be generally very 
 agreeable man . amongst all these various person , Harley , so long a 
 stranger to the London world , seem to make himself at home with the 
 ease of an Alcibiades . many of the less juvenile lady remember he , 
 and rush to claim his acquaintance , with nod and beck , and wreathe 
 smile . he have ready compliment for each . and few indeed be there , 
 man or woman , for whom Harley L'Estrange have not appropriate attraction . 
 distinguished reputation as soldier and scholar for the grave ; whim and 
 pleasantry for the gay ; novelty for the sated ; and for the more vulgar 
 nature be he not Lord L'Estrange , unmarried , possess already of 
 a large independence , and heir to an ancient earldom , and some fifty 
 thousand a year ? 

 not till he have succeed in the general effect - which , it must be 
 own , he do his good to create - do Harley seriously and especially 
 devote himself to his hostess . and then he seat himself by her side ; 
 and , as if in compliment to both , less press admirer insensibly 
 slip away and edge off . 

 Frank Hazeldean be the last to quit his ground behind Madame di Negra 's 
 chair ; but when he find that the two begin to talk in Italian , and he 
 could not understand a word they say , he too - fancy , poor fellow , 
 that he look foolish , and curse his Eton education that have 
 neglect , for language speak by the dead , of which he have learn 
 little , those still in use among the living , of which he have learn 
 nought - retreat towards Randal , and ask wistfully , " pray , what age 
 should you say L'Estrange be ? he must be devilish old , in spite of his 
 look . why , he be at Waterloo ! " 

 " he be young enough to be a terrible rival , " answer Randal , with 
 artful truth . 

 Frank turn pale , and begin to meditate dreadful bloodthirsty thought , 
 of which hair - trigger and Lord 's Cricket - ground form the staple . 

 certainly there be apparent ground for a lover 's jealousy ; for Harley 
 and Beatrice now converse in a low tone , and Beatrice seem agitated , 
 and Harley earnest . randal himself grow more and more perplexed . be 
 Lord L'Estrange really enamour of the marchesa ? if so , farewell to all 
 hope of Frank 's marriage with she ! or be he merely play a part in 
 Riccabocca 's interest ; pretend to be the lover , in order to obtain 
 an influence over her mind , rule she through her ambition , and secure 
 an ally against her brother ? be this finesse compatible with Randal 's 
 notion of Harley 's character ? be it consistent with that chivalric and 
 soldierly spirit of honour which the frank nobleman affect , to make 
 love to a woman in mere ruse de guerre ? could mere friendship for 
 Riccabocca be a sufficient inducement to a man , who , whatever his 
 weakness or his error , seem to wear on his very forehead a soul 
 above deceit , to stoop to paltry mean , even for a worthy end ? at this 
 question , a new thought flash upon Randal , - might not Lord L'Estrange 
 have speculate himself upon win Violante ; would not that account 
 for all the exertion he have make on behalf of her inheritance at the 
 court of Vienna , - exertion of which Peschiera and Beatrice have both 
 complain ? those objection which the austrian government might take 
 to Violante 's marriage with some obscure Englishman would probably 
 not exist against a man like Harley L'Estrange , whose family not only 
 belong to the high aristocracy of England , but have always support 
 opinion in vogue amongst the lead government of Europe . Harley 
 himself , it be true , have never take part in politic , but his notion 
 be , no doubt , those of a high - bear soldier , who have fight , in 
 alliance with Austria , for the restoration of the Bourbons . and this 
 immense wealth - which Violante might lose , if she marry one like 
 randal himself - her marriage with the heir of the Lansmeres might 
 actually tend only to secure . could Harley , with all his own 
 expectation , be indifferent to such a prize ? - and no doubt he have 
 learn Violante 's rare beauty in his correspondence with Riccabocca . 

 thus consider , it seem natural to Randal 's estimate of human nature 
 that Harley 's more prudish scruple of honour , as regard what be due to 
 woman , could not resist a temptation so strong . mere friendship be not 
 a motive powerful enough to shake they , but ambition be . 

 while Randal be thus cogitate , Frank thus suffer , and many a 
 whisper , in comment on the evident flirtation between the beautiful 
 hostess and the accomplished guest , reach the ear both of the 
 brood schemer and the jealous lover , the conversation between the two 
 object of remark and gossip have take a new turn . indeed , Beatrice have 
 make an effort to change it . 

 " it be long , my Lord , " say she , still speak italian , ' since I have 
 hear sentiment like those you address to I ; and if I do not feel 
 myself wholly unworthy of they , it be from the pleasure I have feel in 
 reading sentiment equally foreign to the language of the world in which 
 I live . " she take a book from the table as she speak : " have you see 
 this work ? " 

 Harley glance at the title - page . " to be sure I have , and I know the 
 author . " 

 " I envy you that honour . I should so like also to know one who have 
 discover to I deep in my own heart which I have never explore . " 

 " Charming marchesa , if the book have do this , believe I that I have 
 pay you no false compliment , - form no overflattering estimate of your 
 nature ; for the charm of the work be but in its simple appeal to good 
 and generous emotion , and it can charm none in whom those emotion 
 exist not ! " 

 " Nay , that can not be true , or why be it so popular ? " 

 " because good and generous emotion be more common to the human heart 
 than we be aware of till the appeal come . " 

 " do not   ask I to think that ! I have find the world so base . " 

 " pardon I a rude question ; but what do you know of the world ? " 

 Beatrice look first in surprise at Harley , then glance round the room 
 with significant irony . 

 " as I think ; you call this little room ' the world . ' be it so . I will 
 venture to say , that if the people in this room be suddenly convert 
 into an audience before a stage , and you be as consummate in the 
 actor 's art as you be in all other that please and command - " 

 " well ? " 

 " and be to deliver a speech full of sordid and base sentiment , you 
 would be hiss . but let any other woman , with half your power , 
 arise and utter sentiment sweet and womanly , or honest and lofty , and 
 applause would flow from every lip , and tear rush to many a worldly 
 eye . the true proof of the inherent nobleness of our common nature be 
 in the sympathy it betray with what be noble wherever crowd be 
 collect . never believe the world be base ; if it be so , no society 
 could hold together for a day . but you would know the author of this 
 book ? I will bring he to you . " 

 " do . " 

 " and now , " say Harley , rise , and with his candid , win smile , " do 
 you think we shall ever be friend ? " 

 " you have startle I so that I can scarcely answer . but why would you 
 be friend with I ? " 

 " because you need a friend . you have none ? " 

 " strange flatterer ! " say Beatrice , smile , though very sadly ; and 
 look up , her eye catch Randal 's .

  " ' the hand of Douglas be his own . ' " 

 here Harley draw aside Randal . " Mr. Leslie , a word with you . if I wish

 CHAPTER IX . 

 Violante 's first evening at the Lansmeres have pass more happily to she

 L'Estrange 's mother . Violante , too , be one of those person whom a

 Lord Lansmere , too , like most gentleman of his age , clump all young

 and when Lady Lansmere conduct she to her room , and , kiss her cheek

 on leave Violante 's room , Lady Lansmere pause before the door of

 her form , thus see , look so youthful and child - like , the attitude

 here the countess come to a full pause ; and poor Helen , find herself

 scarce audibly , 

 " certainly , madam , I never dream of - "

 " I be very glad to hear it , my dear ; young lady never should have .

 CHAPTER X. 

 the next morning Harley appear at breakfast . he be in gay spirit ,

 CHAPTER XI . 

 Leonard enter on the scene , and join the party in the garden . the 
 countess , perhaps to please her son , be more than civil , - she be 
 markedly kind to he . she notice he more attentively than she have 
 hitherto do ; and , with all her prejudice of birth , be strike to find 
 the son of Mark Fairfield the carpenter so thoroughly the gentleman . he 
 might not have the exact tone and phrase by which convention stereotype 
 those bear and school in a certain world ; but the aristocrat of 
 nature can dispense with such trite minutia ? and Leonard have live , 
 of late at least , in the good society that exist for the polish of 
 language and the refinement of manner , - the society in which the most 
 graceful idea be clothe in the most graceful form ; the society which 
 really , though indirectly , give the law to court ; the society of 
 the most classic author , in the various age in which literature have 
 flower forth from civilization . and if there be something in the 
 exquisite sweetness of Leonard 's voice , look , and manner , which the 
 countess acknowledge to attain that perfection in high breeding , which , 
 under the name of ' suavity , " steal its way into the heart , so her 
 interest in he be arouse by a certain subdue melancholy which 
 be rarely without distinction , and never without charm . he and Helen 
 exchange but few word . there be but one occasion in which they could 
 have speak apart , and Helen herself contrive to elude it . his face 
 brighten at Lady Lansmere 's cordial invitation , and he glance at 
 Helen as he accept it ; but her eye do not meet his own . 

 " and now , " say Harley , whistle to Nero , whom his ward be silently 
 caressing , " I must take Leonard away . Adieu ! all of you , till to - morrow 
 at dinner . Miss Violante , be the doll to have blue eye or black ? " 

 Violante turn her own black eye in mute appeal to Lady Lansmere , and 
 nestle to that lady 's side as if in refuge from unworthy insult . 




 CHAPTER XII . 

 " let the carriage go to the Clarendon , " say Harley to his servant ; " I 
 and Mr. Oran will walk to town . Leonard , I think you would rejoice at an 
 occasion to serve your old friend , Dr. Riccabocca and his daughter ? " 

 " serve they ! oh , yes . " and there instantly return to Leonard the 
 recollection of Violante 's word when , on leave his quiet village , 
 he have sigh to part from all those he love ; and the little dark - eye 
 girl have say , proudly , yet consolingly , " but to SERVE those you love ! " 
 he turn to L'Estrange , with beaming , inquisitive eye . 

 " I say to our friend , " resume Harley , " that I would vouch for your 
 honour as my own . I be about to prove my word , and to confide the 
 secret which your penetration have indeed divine , - our friend be not 
 what he seem . " Harley then briefly relate to Leonard the particular 
 of the exile 's history , the rank he have hold in his native land , the 
 manner in which , partly through the misrepresentation of a kinsman he 
 have trust , partly through the influence of a wife he have love , he have 
 be draw into scheme which he believe bound to the emancipation 
 of Italy from a foreign yoke by the united exertion of her good and 
 brave son . 

 " a noble ambition ! " interrupt Leonard , manfully . " and pardon I , my 
 Lord , I should not have think that you would speak of it in a tone 
 that imply blame . " 

 " the ambition in itself be noble , " answer Harley ; " but the cause to 
 which it be devoted become defile in its dark channel through 
 Secret Societies . it be the misfortune of all miscellaneous political 
 combination , that with the pure motif of their more generous 
 member be ever mix the most sordid interest , and the fierce 
 passion of mean confederate . when those combination act openly , and 
 in daylight , under the eye of Public Opinion , the healthy element 
 usually prevail ; where they be shroud in mystery , where they 
 be subject to no censor in the discussion of the impartial and 
 dispassionate , where chief work in the dark exact blind obedience , 
 and every man who be at war with law be at once admit as a friend of 
 freedom , the history of the world tell we that patriotism soon pass 
 away . where all be in public , public virtue , by the natural sympathy 
 of the common mind , and by the wholesome control of shame , be likely to 
 obtain ascendancy ; where all be in private , and shame be but for he who 
 refuse the abnegation of his conscience , each man seek the indulgence 
 of his private vice . and hence in Secret Societies   from which may 
 yet proceed great danger to all Europe   we find but foul and hateful 
 Eleusinia , afford pretext to the ambition of the great , to the 
 license of the penniless , to the passion of the revengeful , to the 
 anarchy of the ignorant . in a word , the society of these italian 
 Carbonari do but engender scheme in which the abler chiefs disguise 
 new form of despotism , and in which the revolutionary many look 
 forward to the overthrow of all the institution that stand between Law 
 and Chaos . naturally , therefore , " add L'Estrange , dryly , " when their 
 scheme be detect , and the conspiracy foil , it be for the silly , 
 honest man entrap into the league to suffer , the leader turn 
 king 's evidence , and the common mercenary become - banditti . " Harley 
 then proceed to state that it be just when the soi - disant Riccabocca 
 have discover the true nature and ulterior view of the conspirator 
 he have join , and actually withdraw from their council , that he be 
 denounce by the kinsman who have dupe he into the enterprise , and who 
 now profit by his treason . Harley next speak of the packet despatch 
 by Riccabocca 's die wife , as it be suppose , to Mrs. Bertram ; and of 
 the hope he found on the content of that packet , if discover . he 
 then refer to the design which have bring Peschiera to England , - a 
 design which that personage have avow with such effrontery to his 
 companion at Vienna , that he have publicly lay wager on his success . 

 " but these man can know nothing of England , of the safety of english 
 law , " say Leonard , naturally . " we take it for grant that Riccabocca , 
 if I be still so to call he , refuse his consent to the marriage 
 between his daughter and his foe . where , then , the danger ? this count , 
 even if Violante be not under your mother 's roof , could not get an 
 opportunity to see she . he could not attack the house and carry she off 
 like a feudal baron in the middle age . " 

 " all this be very true , " answer Harley . " yet I have find through life 
 that we can not estimate danger by external circumstance , but by the 
 character of those from whom it be threaten . this count be a man of 
 singular audacity , of no mean natural talent , - talent practise in 
 every art of duplicity and intrigue ; one of those man whose boast it be 
 that they succeed in whatever they undertake ; and he be , here , urge on 
 the one hand by all that can whet the avarice , and on the other , by all 
 that can give invention to despair . therefore , though I can not guess 
 what plan he may possibly adopt , I never doubt that some plan , form 
 with cunning and pursue with daring , will be embrace the moment he 
 discover Violante 's retreat , - unless , indeed , we can forestall all 
 peril by the restoration of her father , and the detection of the fraud 
 and falsehood to which peschiera owe the fortune he appropriate . thus , 
 while we must prosecute to the utmost our inquiry for the missing 
 document , so it should be our care to possess ourselves , if possible , 
 of such knowledge of the count 's machination as may enable we to defeat 
 they . now , it be with satisfaction that I learn in Germany that 
 Peschiera 's sister be in London . I know enough both of his disposition 
 and of the intimacy between himself and this lady , to make I think it 
 probable he will seek to make she his instrument and accomplice , should 
 he require one . peschiera   as you may suppose by his audacious wager   be 
 not one of those secret villain who would cut off their right hand if 
 it could betray the knowledge of what be do by the left , - rather 
 one of those self - confident vaunt knave of high animal spirit , and 
 conscience so obtuse that it cloud their intellect , who must have 
 some one to whom they can boast of their ability and confide their 
 project . and Peschiera have do all he can to render this poor woman 
 so wholly dependent on he as to be his slave and his tool . but I 
 have learn certain trait in her character that show it to be 
 impressionable to good , and with tendency to honour . Peschiera have 
 take advantage of the admiration she excite , some year ago , in a rich 
 young Englishman , to entice this admirer into gambling , and seek 
 to make his sister both a decoy and an instrument in his design of 
 plunder . she do not encourage the address of our countryman , but she 
 warn he of the snare lay for he , and entreat he to leave the 
 place lest her brother should discover and punish her honesty . the 
 Englishman tell I this himself . in fine , my hope of detach this 
 lady from Peschiera 's interest , and induce she to forewarn we of his 
 purpose , consist but in the innocent , and , I hope , laudable artifice , 
 of redeem herself , - of appeal to , and call into disused 
 exercise , the well spring of her nature . " 

 Leonard listen with admiration and some surprise to the singularly 
 subtle and sagacious insight into character which Harley evince in 
 the brief clear stroke by which he have thus depict Peschiera and 
 Beatrice , and be strike by the boldness with which Harley rest a 
 whole system of action upon a few deduction draw from his reasoning 
 on human motive and characteristic bias . Leonard have not expect to 
 find so much practical acuteness in a man who , however accomplish , 
 usually seem indifferent , dreamy , and abstract to the ordinary 
 thing of life . but Harley L'Estrange be one of those whose power 
 lie dormant till circumstance apply to they all they need for 
 activity , - the stimulant of a motive . 

 Harley resume : " after a conversation I have with the lady last night , 
 it occur to I that in this part of our diplomacy you could render 
 us essential service . Madame di Negra - such be the sister 's name - have

 Egerton - and there be a cry from formal , indignant member , " order in

 Audley lift his dark brow , survey the baron for an instant with 
 flash eye , then walk down the narrow defile between the last 
 bench , and vanish from the scene , in which , alas ! so few of the most 
 admire performer leave more than an actor 's short - live name ! 




 CHAPTER XVI . 

 Baron Levy do not execute his threat of call on Egerton the next 
 morning . perhaps he shrink from again meet the flash of those 
 indignant eye . and indeed Egerton be too busy all the forenoon to 
 see any one not upon public affair , except Harley , who hasten to 
 console or cheer he . when the House meet , it be announce that the 
 minister have resign , only hold their office till their successor 
 be appoint . but already there be some reaction in their favour ; 
 and when it become generally know that the new administration be to be 
 form of man few indeed of whom have ever before hold office , the common 
 superstition in the public mind that government be like a trade , in 
 which a regular apprenticeship must be serve , begin to prevail ; and the 
 talk at the club be that the new man could not stand ; that the former 
 ministry , with some modification , would be back in a month . perhaps that 
 too might be a reason why Baron Levy think it prudent not prematurely 
 to offer vindictive condolence to Mr. Egerton . Randal spend part of his 
 morning in inquiry as to what gentleman in his situation mean to do 
 with regard to their place ; he hear with great satisfaction that very 
 few intend to volunteer retirement from their desk . as Randal himself 
 have observe to Egerton , " their country before their party ! " 

 Randal 's place be of great moment to he ; its duty be easy , its 
 salary amply sufficient for his want , and defray such expense as 
 be bestow on the education of Oliver and his sister . for I be bind 
 to do justice to this young man , - indifferent as he be towards his 
 specie in general , the tie of family be strong with he ; and he 
 stint himself in many temptation most alluring to his age , in the 
 endeavour to raise the dull honest Oliver and the loose - haired pretty 
 Juliet somewhat more to his own level of culture and refinement . man 
 essentially griping and unscrupulous often do make the care for their 
 family an apology for their sin against the world . even Richard III . , 
 if the chronicler be to be trust , excuse the murder of his nephew 
 by his passionate affection for his son . with the loss of that place , 
 randal lose all mean of support , save what Audley could give he ; and 
 if Audley be in truth ruin ? moreover , Randal have already establish 
 at the office a reputation for ability and industry . it be a career 
 in which , if he abstain from party politic , he might rise to a fair 
 station and to a considerable income . therefore , much content with 
 what he learn as to the general determination of his fellow official , 
 a determination warrant by ordinary precedent in such case , Randal 
 din at a club with good relish , and much christian resignation for 
 the reverse of his patron , and then walk to Grosvenor Square , on 
 the chance of find Audley within . learn that he be so , from the 
 porter who open the door , Randal enter the library . three gentleman 
 be seat there with Egerton : one of the three be Lord L'Estrange ; 
 the other two be member of the really defunct , though nominally still 
 exist , government . he be about to withdraw from intrude on this 
 conclave , when Egerton say to he gently , " come in , Leslie ; I be just 
 speak about yourself . " 

 " about I , sir ? " 

 " yes ; about you and the place you hold . I have ask Sir - -   pointing 
 to a fellow minister   whether I might not , with propriety , request your 
 chief to leave some note of his opinion of your talent , which I know be 
 high , and which might serve you with his successor . " 

 " oh , sir , at such a time to think of I ! " exclaim Randal , and he be 
 genuinely touch . 

 " but , " resume Audley , with his usual dryness , " Sir - - , to my 
 surprise , think that it would well become you that you should resign . 
 unless his reason , which he have not yet state , be very strong , such 
 would not be my advice . " 

 " my reason , " say Sir - - , with official formality , " be simply these : 
 I have a nephew in a similar situation ; he will resign , as a matter 
 of course . every one in the public office whose relation and near 
 connection hold high appointment in the Government will do so . I do 
 not think Mr. Leslie will like to feel himself a solitary exception . " 

 " Mr. Leslie be no relation of mine , - not even a near connection , " 
 answer Egerton . 

 " but his name be so associate with your own : he have reside so long in 
 your house , be so well know in society   and do not   think I compliment 
 when I add , that we hope so well of he , that I can not   think it worth 
 his while to keep this paltry place , which incapacitate he too from a 
 seat in parliament . " 

 Sir - - be one of those terribly rich man , to whom all consideration 
 of mere bread and cheese be paltry . but I must add that he suppose 
 Egerton to be still wealthy than himself , and sure to provide 
 handsomely for Randal , whom Sir - - rather like than not ; and for 
 Randal 's own sake , Sir - - think it would lower he in the estimation 
 of Egerton himself , despite that gentleman 's advocacy , if he do not 
 follow the example of his avowed and notorious patron . 

 " you see , Leslie , " say Egerton , check Randal 's meditate reply , 
 " that nothing can be say against your honour if you stay where you be ; 
 it be a mere question of expediency ; I will judge that for you ; keep 
 your place . " 

 unhappily the other member of the Government , who have hitherto be 
 silent , be a literary man . unhappily , while this talk have proceed , he 
 have place his hand upon Randal Leslie 's celebrated pamphlet , which lay 
 on the library table ; and , turn over the leaf , the whole spirit and 
 matter of that masterly composition in defence of the administration 
  a composition steep in all the essence of party   recur to his too 
 faithful recollection . he , too , like Randal ; he do more , - he admire 
 the author of that striking and effective pamphlet . and therefore , 
 rouse himself from the sublime indifference he have before feel for the 
 fate of a subaltern , he say , with a bland and complimentary smile , " no ; 
 the writer of this most able publication be no ordinary placeman . his 
 opinion here be too vigorously state ; this fine irony on the very 
 person who in all probability will be the chief in his office have 
 excite too lively an attention to allow he the sedet eternumque 
 sedebit on an official stool . ha , ha ! this be so good ! read it , 
 L'Estrange . what say you ? " Harley glance over the page point out 
 to he . the original be in one of Burley 's broad , coarse , but tell 
 burlesque , strain fine through Randal 's more polished satire . it 
 be capital . Harley smile , and lift his eye to Randal . the unlucky 
 plagiarist 's face be flushed , - the bead stand on his brow . Harley be 
 a good hater ; he love too warmly not to err on the opposite side ; but 
 he be one of those man who forget hate when its object be distressed 
 and humble . he put down the pamphlet and say , " I be no politician ; 
 but Egerton be so well known to be fastidious and over - scrupulous in 
 all point of official etiquette , that Mr. Leslie can not follow a safe 
 counsellor . " 

 " read that yourself , Egerton , " say Sir - - ; and he push the pamphlet 
 to Audley . 

 now Egerton have a dim recollection that that pamphlet be unlucky ; 
 but he have skim over its content hastily , and at that moment have 
 forget all about it . he take up the too famous work with a reluctant 
 hand , but he read attentively the passage point out to he , and then 
 say gravely and sadly , 

 " Mr. Leslie , I retract my advice . I believe Sir - - be right , - that the 
 nobleman here so keenly satirize will be the chief in your office . I 
 doubt whether he will not compel your dismissal ; at all event , he 
 could scarcely be expect to promote your advancement . under the 
 circumstance , I fear you have no option as a - " Egerton pause a 
 moment , and , with a sigh that seem to settle the question , conclude 
 with - " as a gentleman . " 

 never do Jack Cade , never do Wat Tyler , feel a more deadly hate to 
 that word " gentleman " than the well - bear Leslie feel then ; but he bow 
 his head , and answer with his usual presence of mind , 

 " you utter my own sentiment . " 

 " you think we be right , Harley ? " ask Egerton , with an irresolution 
 that surprise all present . 

 " I think , " answer Harley , with a compassion for Randal that be almost 
 over - generous , and yet with an equivoque on the word , despite the 
 compassion , - " I think whoever have serve Audley Egerton never yet have 
 be a loser by it ; and if Mr. Leslie write this pamphlet , he must have 
 well serve Audley Egerton . if he undergo the penalty , we may safely 
 trust to Egerton for the compensation . " 

 " my compensation have long since be make , " answer Randal , with grace ; 
 " and that Mr. Egerton could thus have care for my fortune , at an hour 
 so occupy , be a thought of pride which - " 

 " enough , Leslie ! enough ! " interrupt Egerton , rise and press his 
 protege 's hand . " see I before you go to bed . " 

 then the two other minister rise also and shake hand with Leslie , and 
 tell he he have do the right thing , and that they hop soon to see 
 he in parliament ; and hint , smilingly , that the next administration 
 do not promise to be very long - lived ; and one ask he to dinner , 
 and the other to spend a week at his country - seat . and amidst these 
 congratulation at the stroke that leave he penniless , the distinguished 
 pamphleteer leave the room . how he curse big John Burley ! 




 CHAPTER XVII . 

 it be past midnight when Audley Egerton summon Randal . the statesman 
 be then alone , seat before his great desk , with its manifold 
 compartment , and engage on the task of transfer various paper and 
 letter , some to the waste - basket , some to the flame , some to two great 
 iron chest with patent lock , that stand , open - mouthed , at his foot . 
 strong , stern , and grim look those iron chest , silently receive the 
 relic of power depart ; strong , stern , and grim as the grave . Audley 
 lift his eye at Randal 's entrance , sign to he to take a chair , 
 continue his task for a few moment , and then turn round , as if by 
 an effort he pluck himself from his master - passion , - Public Life , he 
 say , with deliberate tone , 

 " I know not , Randal Leslie , whether you think I needlessly cautious , 
 or wantonly unkind , when I tell you never to expect from I more than 
 such advance to your career as my then position could effect , - never to 
 expect from my liberality in life , nor from my testament in death , an 
 addition to your private fortune . I see by your gesture what would 
 be your reply , and I thank you for it . I now tell you , as yet in 
 confidence , though before long it can be no secret to the world , that my 
 pecuniary affair have be so neglect by I in my devotion to those 
 of the State , that I be somewhat like the man who portion out his 
 capital at so much a day , calculate to live just long enough to make 
 it last . unfortunately he live too long . " Audley smile - but the 
 smile be cold as a sunbeam upon ice - and go on with the same firm , 
 unfaltere accent . " the prospect that face I I be prepared for ; they 
 do not take I by surprise . I know long since how this would end , if 
 I survive the loss of office . I know it before you come to I , and 
 therefore I speak to you as I do , judge it manful and right to guard 
 you against hope which you might otherwise have naturally entertain . 
 on this head , I need say no more . it may excite your surprise , possibly 
 your blame , that I , esteem methodical and practical enough in the 
 affair of the State , should be so imprudent as to my own . " 

 " oh , sir ! you owe no account to I . " 

 " to you , at least , as much as to any one . I be a solitary man ; my few 
 relation need nothing from I . I have a right do spend what I possess 
 as I please ; and if I have spend it recklessly as regard myself , I 
 have not spend it ill in its effect on other . it have be my object for 
 many year to have no private life , - to dispense with its sorrow , joy , 
 affection ; and as to its duty , they do not exist for I . I have 
 say . " mechanically , as he end , the minister 's hand close the lid of 
 one of the iron box , and on the closed lid he rest his firm foot . 
 " but now , " he resume , " I have fail to advance your career . true , I 
 warn you that you draw into a lottery ; but you have more chance of 
 a prize than a blank . a blank , however , it have turn out , and the 
 question become grave , - what be you to do ? " 

 here , see that Egerton come to a full pause , Randal answer readily , 

 " still , sir , to go by your advice . " 

 " my advice , " say Audley , with a softened look , " would perhaps be rude 
 and unpalatable . I would rather place before you an option . on the one 
 hand , recommence life again . I tell you that I would keep your name 
 on your college book . you can return , you can take your degree , after 
 that , you can go to the Bar , - you have just the talent calculate to 
 succeed in that profession . success will be slow , it be true ; but , with 
 perseverance , it will be sure . and , believe I , Leslie , Ambition be only 
 sweet while it be but the lofty name for Hope . who would care for a 
 fox 's brush if it have not be render a prize by the excitement of the 
 chase ? " 

 " Oxford - again ! it be a long step back in life , " say Randal , drearily , 
 and little heed Egerton 's unusual indulgence of illustration . " a long 
 step back - and to what ? to a profession in which one never begin to 
 rise till one 's hair be gray . besides , how live in the mean while ? " 

 " do not let that thought disturb you . the modest income that suffice 
 for a student at the Bar , I trust , at least , to insure you from the 
 wreck of my fortune . " 

 " ah , sir , I would not burden you far . what right have I to such 
 kindness , save my name of Leslie ? " and in spite of himself , as Randal 
 conclude , a tone of bitterness , that betray reproach , break forth . 
 Egerton be too much the man of the world not to comprehend the 
 reproach , and not to pardon it . 

 " certainly , " he answer calmly , " as a Leslie you be entitle to my 
 consideration , and would have be entitle perhaps to more , have I not 
 so explicitly warn you to the contrary . but the Bar do not seem to 
 please you ? " 

 " what be the alternative , sir ? let I decide when I hear it , " answer 
 Randal , sullenly . he begin to lose respect for the roan who own he 
 could do so little for he , and who evidently recommend he to shift 
 for himself . 

 if one could have pierce into Egerton 's gloomy heart as he note the 
 young man 's change of tone , it may be a doubt whether one would have 
 see there pain or pleasure , - pain , for merely from the force of habit 
 he have begin to like Randal , or pleasure at the thought that he might 
 have reason to withdraw that liking . so lone and stoical have grow 
 the man who have make it his object to have no private life ! revealing , 
 however , neither pleasure nor pain , but with the compose calmness of a 
 judge upon the bench , Egerton reply , - 

 " the alternative be , to continue in the course you have begin , and still 
 to rely on I . " 

 " Sir , my dear Mr. Egerton , " exclaim Randal , regain all his usual 
 tenderness of look and voice , " rely on you ! but that be all I ask . only " 

 " only , you would say , I be go out of power , and you do not   see the 
 chance of my return ? " 

 " I do not mean that . " 

 " permit I to suppose that you do : very true ; but the party I belong to 
 be as sure of return as the pendulum of that clock be sure to obey the 
 mechanism that move it from left to right . our successor profess to 
 come in upon a popular question . all administration who do that be 
 necessarily short - lived . either they do not go far enough to please 
 present supporter , or they go so far as to arm new enemy in the 
 rival who outbid they with the people . ' t be the history of all 
 revolution , and of all reform . our own administration in reality be 
 destroy for have pass what be call a popular measure a year 
 ago , which lose we half our friend , and refuse to propose another 
 popular measure this year , in the which we be outstrip by the man 
 who halloo we on to the last . therefore , whatever our successor 
 do , we shall by the law of reaction , have another experiment of power 
 afford to ourselves . it be but a question of time ; you can wait for 
 it , - whether I can be uncertain . but if I die before that day arrive , I 
 have influence enough still leave with those who will come in , to obtain 
 a promise of a well provision for you than that which you have lose . 
 the promise of public man be proverbially uncertain ; but I shall 
 entrust your cause to a man who never fail a friend , and whose rank 
 will enable he to see that justice be do to you , - I speak of Lord 
 L'Estrange . " 

 " oh , not he ; he be unjust to I ; he dislike I ; he - " 

 " may dislike you   he have his whim , but he love I ; and though for no 
 other human being but you would I ask Harley L'Estrange a favour , yet 
 for you I will , " say Egerton , betraying , for the first time in that 
 dialogue , a visible emotion , - " for you , a Leslie , a kinsman , however 
 remote , to the wife from whom I receive my fortune ! and despite all my 
 caution , it be possible that in waste that fortune I may have wrong 
 you . enough : you have now before you the two option , much as you have 
 at first ; but you have at present more experience to aid you in your 
 choice . you be a man , and with more brain than most man ; think over it 
 well , and decide for yourself . now to bed , and postpone think till the 
 morrow . poor Randal , you look pale ! " 

 Audley , as he say the last word , put his hand on Randal 's shoulder , 
 almost with a father 's gentleness ; and then suddenly draw himself 
 up , as the hard inflexible expression , stamp on that face by year , 
 return , he move away and resettle to Public Life and the iron box . 




 CHAPTER XVIII . 

 early the next day Randal Leslie be in the luxurious business - room 
 of Baron Levy . how unlike the cold doric simplicity of the statesman 's 
 library ! axminster carpet , three inch thick ; portiere a la francaise 
 before the door ; parisian bronze on the chimney - piece ; and all the 
 receptacle that line the room , and contain title - deed and postobit 
 and bill and promise to pay and lawyer - like japan boxes , with many 
 a noble name write thereon in large white capital - " make ruin 
 pompous , " all these sepulchre of depart patrimony veneer in 
 rosewood that gleam with french polish , and blaze with ormulu . there 
 be a coquetry , an air of petit maitre , so diffused over the whole 
 room , that you could not , for the life of you , recollect you be with a 
 usurer ! Plutus wear the aspect of his enemy Cupid ; and how realize your 
 idea of Harpagon in that baron , with his easy french " Mon cher , " and 
 his white , warm hand that press yours so genially , and his dress so 
 exquisite , even at the early morn ? no man ever yet see that baron in 
 a dressing - gown and slipper ! as one fancy some feudal baron of old 
  not half so terrible   everlastingly clothe in mail , so all one 's notion 
 of this grand marauder of civilization be inseparably associate with 
 varnish boot and a camellia in the button - hole . 

 " and this be all that he do for you ! " cry the baron , press 
 together the point of his ten taper finger . " have he but let you 
 conclude your career at Oxford , I have hear enough of your scholarship 
 to know that you would have take high honour , be secure of a 
 fellowship , have betake yourself with content to a slow and laborious 
 profession , and prepare yourself to die on the woolsack . " 

 " he propose to I now to return to Oxford , " say randal . " it be not too 
 late ! " 

 " yes , it be , " say the baron . " neither individual nor nation ever go 
 back of their own accord . there must be an earthquake before a river 
 recede to its source . " 

 " you speak well , " answer Randal , " and I can not gainsay you . but now ! " 

 " ah , the now be the grand question in life , the then be obsolete , go 
 by , - out of fashion ; and now , mon cher , you come to ask my advice ? " 

 " no , Baron , I come to ask your explanation . " 

 " of what ? " 

 " I want to know why you speak to I of Mr. Egerton 's ruin ; why you speak 
 to I of the land to be sell by Mr. Thornhill ; and why you speak to 
 I of Count Peschiera . you touch on each of those point within ten 
 minute , you omit to indicate what link can connect they . " 

 " by Jove , " say the baron , rise , and with more admiration in his face 
 than you could have conceive that face , so smile and so cynical , 
 could exhibit , - " by Jove , Randal Leslie , but your shrewdness be 
 wonderful . you really be the first young man of your day ; and I 
 will ' help you , ' as I help Audley Egerton . perhaps you will be more 
 grateful . " 

 randal thought of Egerton 's ruin . the parallel imply by the baron do 
 not suggest to he the rare enthusiasm of gratitude . however , he merely 
 say , " pray , proceed ; I listen to you with interest . " 

 " as for politic , then , " say the baron , " we will discuss that topic 
 later . I be wait myself to see how these new man get on . the first 
 consideration be for your private fortune . you should buy this ancient 
 Leslie property - Rood and Dulmansberry - only L20,000 down ; the rest may 
 remain on mortgage forever - or at least till I find you a rich wife , - as 
 in fact I do for Egerton . Thornhill want the L20,000 now , - want they 
 very much . " 

 " and where , " say Randal , with an iron smile , " be the L20,000 you 
 ascribe to I to come from ? " 

 " ten thousand shall come to you the day Count Peschiera marry the 
 daughter of his kinsman with your help and aid ; the remain ten 
 thousand I will lend you . no scruple , I shall hazard nothing , the 
 estate will bear that additional burden . what say you , - shall it be 
 so ? " 

 " ten thousand pound from Count Peschiera ! " say Randal , breathing 
 hard . " you can not be serious ? such a sum - for what ? - for a mere piece 
 of information ? how otherwise can I aid he ? there must be trick and 
 deception intend here . " 

 " my dear fellow , " answer Levy , " I will give you a hint . there be such 
 a thing in life as be over - suspicious . if you have a fault , it be 
 that . the information you allude to be , of course , the first assistance 
 you be to give . perhaps more may be need , perhaps not . of that you 
 will judge yourself , since the L10,000 be contingent on the marriage 
 aforesaid . " 

 " over - suspicious or not , " answer Randal , " the amount of the sum be 
 too improbable , and the security too bad , for I to listen to this 
 proposition , even if I could descend to - " 

 " Stop , mon cher . business first , scruple afterwards . the security too 
 bad ; what security ? " 

 " the word of Count di Peschiera . " 

 " he have nothing to do with it , he need know nothing about it . ' t be my 
 word you doubt . I be your security . " 

 randal thought of that dry witticism in Gibbon , " Abu Rafe say he will 
 be witness for this fact , but who will be witness for Abu Rafe ? " but 
 he remain silent , only fix on Levy those dark observant eye , with 
 their contract , wary pupil . 

 " the fact be simply this , " resume Levy : " Count di Peschiera have 
 promise to pay his sister a dowry of L20,000 , in case he have the 
 money to spare . he can only have it to spare by the marriage we be 
 discuss . on my part , as I manage his affair in England for he , I 
 have promise that , for the say sum of L20,000 , I will guarantee the 
 expense in the way of that marriage , and settle with Madame di Negra . 
 now , though Peschiera be a very liberal , warm - hearted fellow , I do not 
 say that he would have name so large a sum for his sister 's dowry , if 
 in strict truth he do not owe it to she . it be the amount of her own 
 fortune , which by some arrangement with her late husband , not exactly 
 legal , he possess himself of . if Madame di Negra go to law with he 
 for it , she could get it back . I have explain this to he ; and , in 
 short , you now understand why the sum be thus assess . but I have 
 buy up Madame di Negra 's debt , I have buy up young Hazeldean 's 
  for we must make a match between these two a part of our arrangement . 
 I shall present to Peschiera , and to these excellent young person , an 
 account that will absorb the whole L20,000 . that sum will come into my 
 hand . if I settle the claim against they for half the money , which , 
 make myself the sole creditor , I have the right to do , the moiety will 
 remain . and if I choose to give it to you in return for the service 
 which provide Peschiera with a princely fortune , discharge the debt of 
 his sister , and secure she a husband in my promising young client , Mr. 
 Hazeldean , that be my lookout , - all party be satisfied , and no one 
 need ever be the wiser . the sum be large , no doubt ; it answer to I to 
 give it to you ; do it answer to you to receive it ? " 

 Randal be greatly agitated ; but vile as he be , and systematically as 
 in thought he have bring himself to regard other merely as they could 
 be make subservient to his own interest , still , with all who have not 
 harden themselves in actual crime , there be a wide distinction between 
 the thought and the act ; and though , in the exercise of ingenuity and 
 cunning , he would have have few scruple in that moral swindling which 
 be mildly call " outwit another , " yet thus nakedly and openly to 
 accept a bribe for a deed of treachery towards the poor italian who 
 have so generously trust he - he recoil . he be nerve himself to 
 refuse , when Levy , open his pocket - book , glance over the memoranda 
 therein , and say , as to himself , " Rood Manor - Dulmansberry , sell to the 
 Thornhills by Sir Gilbert Leslie , knight of the shire ; estimate present 
 net rental L2,250 7s . 0d . it be the great bargain I ever know . and 
 with this estate in hand , and your talent , Leslie , I do not   see why 
 you should not rise high than Audley Egerton . he be poor than you 
 once ! " 

 the old Leslie land - a positive stake in the country - the restoration 
 of the fallen family ; and on the other hand , either long drudgery at 
 the Bar , - a scanty allowance on Egerton 's bounty , his sister waste 
 her youth at slovenly , dismal Rood , Oliver debase into a boor ! - or 
 a mendicant 's dependence on the contemptuous pity of Harley 
 L'Estrange , - Harley , who have refuse his hand to he , Harley , who 
 perhaps would become the husband of Violante ! rage seize he as these 
 contrast picture rise before his view . he walk to and fro in 
 disorder , strive to re - collect his thought , and reduce himself from 
 the passion of the human heart into the mere mechanism of calculate 
 intellect . " I can not conceive , " say he , abruptly , " why you should tempt 
 I thus , - what interest it be to you ! " 

 Baron Levy smile , and put up his pocket - book . he see from that moment 
 that the victory be gain . 

 " my dear boy , " say he , with the most agreeable bonhommie , " it be very 
 natural that you should think a man would have a personal interest in 
 whatever he do for another . I believe that view of human nature be 
 call utilitarian philosophy , and be much in fashion at present . let 
 I try and explain to you . in this affair I sha'n't   injure myself . true , 
 you will say , if I settle claim which amount to L20,000 for L10,000 , I 
 might put the surplus into my own pocket instead of your . agree . but 
 I shall not get the L20,000 , nor repay myself Madame di Negra 's debt 
  whatever I may do as to Hazeldean 's , unless the count get this 
 heiress . you can help in this . I want you ; and I do not   think I could 
 get you by a less offer than I make . I shall soon pay myself back the 
 L10,000 if the count get hold of the lady and her fortune . Brief , I see 
 my way here to my own interest . do you want more reason , - you shall 
 have they . I be now a very rich man . how have I become so ? through 
 attach myself from the first to person of expectation , whether from 
 fortune or talent . I have make connection in society , and society have 
 enrich I . I have still a passion for make money . ' Que voulez - vous ? ' 
 it be my profession , my hobby . it will be useful to I in a thousand 
 way to secure as a friend a young man who will have influence with 
 other young man , heir to something well than Rood Hall . you may 
 succeed in public life . a man in public life may attain to the knowledge 
 of State secret that be very profitable to one who dabble a little 
 in the Funds . we can perhaps hereafter do business together that may put 
 yourself in a way of clear off all mortgage on these estate , - on 
 the encumbered possession of which I shall soon congratulate you . you 
 see I be frank ; ' t be the only way of come to the point with so clever 
 a fellow as you . and now , since the less we rake up the mud in a pond 
 from which we have resolve to drink the well , let we dismiss all 
 other thought but that of secure our end . will you tell Peschiera 
 where the young lady be , or shall I ? well do it yourself ; reason 
 enough for it , that he have confide to you his hope , and ask you 
 to help he ; why should not you ? not a word to he about our little 
 arrangement ; he need never know it . you need never be trouble . " Levy 
 ring the bell : " order my carriage round . " 

 Randal make no objection . he be deathlike pale , but there be a 
 sinister expression of firmness on his thin , bloodless lip . 

 " the next point , " Levy resume , " be to hasten the match between Frank 
 and the fair widow . how do that stand ? " 

 " she will not see I , nor receive he . " 

 " oh , learn why ! and if you find on either side there be a hitch , just 
 let I know ; I will soon remove it . " 

 " have Hazeldean consent to the post - obit ? " 

 " not yet ; I have not press it ; I wait the right moment , if necessary . " 

 " it will be necessary . " 

 " ah , you wish it . it shall be so . " 

 Randal Leslie again pace the room , and after a silent self - commune come 
 up close to the baron , and say , 

 " look you , sir , I be poor and ambitious ; you have tempt I at the 
 right moment , and with the right inducement . I succumb . but what 
 guarantee have I that this money will be pay , these estate make mine 
 upon the condition stipulate ? "

 shall have the money , ' a man have it ; if I say , ' I renew your bill for

 " yes , " say Randal , " I will at all event take the day to consider .

 the baron sit thoughtful . " it be true , " say he to himself , " this young

 CHAPTER XXI . 

 Randal 's mind be make up . all he have learn in regard to Levy have 
 confirm his resolve or dissipate his scruple . he have start from 
 the improbability that Pesehiera would offer , and the still great 
 improbability that Peschiera would pay he , L10,000 for such information 
 or aid as he could bestow in further the count 's object . but when 
 Levy take such proposal entirely on himself , the main question to 
 Randal become this , - could it be Levy 's interest to make so considerable 
 a sacrifice ? have the baron imply only friendly sentiment as his 
 motif , Randal would have feel sure he be to be take in ; but the 
 usurer 's frank assurance that it would answer to he in the long - run to 
 concede to randal term so advantageous , alter the case , and lead our 
 young philosopher to look at the affair with calm , contemplative eye . 
 be it sufficiently obvious that Levy count on an adequate return ? 
 might he calculate on reap help by the bushel if he sow it by the 
 handful ? the result of Randal 's cogitation be that the baron might 
 fairly deem himself no wasteful sower . in the first place , it be clear 
 that Levy , not without reasonable ground , believe that he could soon 
 replace , with exceed good interest , any sum he might advance to 
 Randal , out of the wealth which Randal 's prompt information might bestow 
 on Levy 's client , the count ; and secondly , Randal 's self - esteem be 
 immense , and could he but succeed in secure a pecuniary independence 
 on the instant , to free he from the slow drudgery of the Bar , or from a 
 precarious reliance on Audley Egerton , as a politician out of power , 
 his conviction of rapid triumph in public life be as strong as if 
 whisper by an angel or promise by a fiend . on such triumph , with 
 all the social position they would secure , Levy might well calculate for 
 repayment by a thousand indirect channel . Randal 's sagacity detect 
 that , through all the good - natured or liberal action ascribe to the 
 usurer , Levy have steadily pursue his own interest , he see that Levy 
 mean to get he into his power , and use his ability as instrument 
 for dig new mine , in which Baron Levy would claim the right 
 of large royalty . but at that thought Randal 's pale lip curl 
 disdainfully ; he confide too much in his own power not to think that 
 he could elude the grasp of the usurer , whenever it suit he to do 
 so . thus , on a survey , all conscience hush itself ; his mind rush 
 buoyantly on to anticipation of the future . he see the hereditary 
 estate regain , - no matter how mortgage , - for the moment still his 
 own , legally his own , yield for the present what would suffice for 
 competence to one of few want , and free his name from that title of 
 Adventurer , which be so prodigally give in rich old country to those 
 who have no estate but their brain . he think of Violante but as the 
 civilized trader think of a trifling coin , of a glass bead , which 
 he exchange with some barbarian for gold dust ; he think of Frank 
 Hazeldean marry to the foreign woman of beggar mean , and repute 
 that have know the breath of scandal , - marry , and live on post - obit 
 instalment of the casino property ; he think of the poor squire 's 
 resentment ; his avarice sweep from the land annex to Rood on to

 parliament ; with one hand he grasp fortune , with the next power . " and 
 yet I enter on life with no patrimony   save a ruin hall and a barren 
 waste , - no patrimony but knowledge . I have but turn knowledge from 
 book to man ; for book may give fame after death , but man give we power

 have suspect Violante to be there : he resolve to ascertain . randal

 " I have call to see the young lady stay here , - the foreign young 
 lady . "

 CHAPTER XXII . 

 Harley have make one notable oversight in that appeal to Beatrice 's 
 well and gentler nature , which he entrust to the advocacy of 
 Leonard , - a scheme in itself very characteristic of Harley 's romantic 
 temper , and either wise or foolish , accord as his indulgent theory 
 of human idiosyncrasy in general , and of those peculiar to Beatrice 
 di Negra in especial , be the dream of an enthusiast , or the inductive 
 conclusion of a sound philosopher . 

 Harley have warn Leonard not to fall in love with the italian , - he have 
 forget to warn the italian not to fall in love with Leonard ; nor have 
 he ever anticipate the probability of that event . this be not to 
 be very much wonder at ; for if there be anything on which the 
 most sensible man be dull - eye , where those eye be not light by 
 jealousy , it be as to the probability of another male creature be 
 beloved . all , the least vain of the whisker gender , think it prudent 
 to guard themselves against be too irresistible to the fair sex ; and 
 each say of his friend , " good fellow enough , but the last man for that 
 woman to fall in love with ! " 

 but certainly there appear on the surface more than ordinary cause for 
 Harley 's blindness in the special instance of Leonard . 

 whatever Beatrice 's well quality , she be generally esteem worldly 
 and ambitious . she be pinch in circumstance , she be luxuriant and 
 extravagant ; how be it likely that she could distinguish any aspirant 
 of the humble birth and fortune of the young peasant - author ? as a 
 coquette , she might try to win his admiration and attract his fancy ; 
 but her own heart would surely be guard in the triple mail of pride , 
 poverty , and the conventional opinion of the world in which she live . 
 have Harley think it possible that Madame di Negra could stoop below 
 her station , and love , not wisely , but too well , he would rather have 
 think that the object would be some brilliant adventurer of fashion , 
 some one who could turn against herself all the art of deliberate 
 fascination , and all the experience bestow by frequent conquest . one 
 so simple as Leonard , so young and so new ! Harley L'Estrange would have 
 smile at himself , if the idea of that image subjugate the ambitious 
 woman to the disinterested love of a village maid have once cross his 
 mind . nevertheless , so it be , and precisely from those cause which 
 would have seem to Harley to forbid the weakness . 

 it be that fresh , pure heart , it be that simple , earnest sweetness , it 
 be that contrast in look , in tone , in sentiment , and in reasoning , to 
 all that have jade and disgust she in the circle of her admirer , - it 
 be all this that captivate Beatrice at the first interview with 
 Leonard . here be what she have confess to the sceptical Randal she 
 have dream and sigh for . her early youth have pass into abhorrent 
 marriage , without the soft , innocent crisis of human life , - virgin love . 
 many a wooer might have touch her vanity , please her fancy , excited 
 her ambition - her heart have never be awaken ; it wake now . the world , 
 and the year that the world have waste , seem to fleet away as a 
 cloud . she be as if restore to the blush and the sigh of youth , - the 
 youth of the italian maid . as in the restoration of our golden age 
 be the spell of poetry with we all , so such be the spell of the poet 
 himself on she . 

 oh , how exquisite be that brief episode in the life of the woman pal 
 with the " hack sight and sound " of worldly life ! how strangely happy 
 be those hour , when , lure on by her silent sympathy , the young 
 scholar speak of his early struggle between circumstance and impulse , 
 musing amidst the flower , and hearken to the fountain ; or of his 
 wandering in the desolate , lamp - light street , while the vision of 
 Chatterton 's glitter eye shone dread through the friendless shadow . 
 and as he speak , whether of his hope or his fear , her look dwelt 
 fondly on the young face , that vary between pride and sadness , - pride 
 ever so gentle , and sad ness ever so nobly touching . she be never 
 weary of gaze on that brow , with its quiet power ; but her lid drop 
 before those eye , with their serene , unfathomable passion . she feel , as 
 they haunt she , what a deep and holy thing love in such soul must 
 be . Leonard never speak to she of Helen - that reserve every reader can 
 comprehend . to nature like his , first love be a mystery ; to confide it 
 be to profane . but he fulfil his commission of interesting she in the 
 exile and his daughter , and his description of they bring tear to her 
 eye . she inly resolve not to aid Peschiera in his design on Violante . 
 she forget for the moment that her own fortune be to depend on the 
 success of those design . Levy have arrange so that she be not remind 
 of her poverty by creditor , - she know not how . she know nothing of 
 business . she give herself up to the delight of the present hour , and to 
 vague prospect of a future associate with that young image , - with that 
 face of a guardian angel that she see before she , fairest in the moment 
 of absence ; for in those moment come the life of fairy - land , when we 
 shut our eye on the world , and see through the haze of golden revery . 
 dangerous , indeed , to Leonard would have be the soft society of 
 Beatrice di Negra , have not his heart be wholly devoted to one object , 
 and have not his ideal of woman be from that object one sole and 
 indivisible reflection . but Beatrice guess not this barrier between 
 herself and he . amidst the shadow that he conjure up from his past 
 life , she behold no rival form . she see he lonely in the world , as 
 she be herself . and in his lowly birth , his youth , in the freedom from 
 presumption which characterize he in all thing   save that confidence 
 in his intellectual destiny which be the essential attribute of 
 genius , she but grow the bolder by the belief that , even if he love 
 she , he would not dare to hazard the avowal . 

 and thus , one day , yield , as she have ever be will not to yield , to the 
 impulse of her quick italian heart - how she never remember , in 
 what word she could never recall - she speak , she own her love , she 
 plead , with tear and blush , for love in return . all that pass be 
 to she as a dream , - a dream from which she wake with a fierce sense 
 of agony , of humiliation , - wake as the woman ' scorn . " no matter how 
 gratefully , how tenderly Leonard have reply , the reply be refusal . 

 for the first time she learn she have a rival ; that all he could give 
 of love be long since , from his boyhood , give to another . for the 
 first time in her life , that ardent nature know jealousy , its torturing 
 sting , its thirst for vengeance , its tempest of love hate . but , to 
 outward appearance , silent and cold she stand as marble . word that 
 seek to soothe fall on her ear unheeded : they be drown by the 
 storm within . Pride be the first feeling which dominate the war 
 element that rag in her soul . she tear her hand from that which 
 clasp her with so loyal a respect . she could have spurn the form 
 that knelt at her foot , not for love , but for pardon . she point to the 
 door with the gesture of an insult queen . she know no more till she 
 be alone . then come that rapid flash of conjecture peculiar to the 
 storm of jealousy ; that which seem to single from all nature the 
 one object to dread and to destroy ; the conjecture so often false , yet 
 receive at once by our conviction as the revelation of instinctive 
 truth . he to whom she have humble herself love another ; whom but 
 Violante , - whom else , young and beautiful , have he name in the record of 
 his life ? - none ! and he have seek to interest she , Beatrice di Negra , 
 in the object of his love ; hint at danger which Beatrice know too 
 well ; imply trust in Beatrice 's will to protect . blind fool that she 
 have be ! this , then , be the reason why he have come , day after day , 
 to Beatrice 's house ; this be the charm that have draw he thither ; 
 this - she press her hand to her burn temple , as if to stop the 
 torture of thought . suddenly a voice be hear below , the door open , 
 and randal Leslie enter . 




 CHAPTER XXIII . 

 punctually at eight o'clock that evening , Baron Levy welcome the new

 " well ! "

 CHAPTER XXIV . 

 the next morning Frank Hazeldean be sit over his solitary

 habit of breakfast late . one 's appetite do not come early when one 
 live in London , and never go to bed before daybreak . 

 there be nothing very luxurious or effeminate about Frank 's room , 
 though they be in a very dear street , and he pay a monstrous high

 can get through his money , and make very little show for it . the

 poor dear Frank Hazeldean ! - true type of many a poor dear fellow who 
 have long since go to the dog . and if , in this road to ruin , there 
 have be the least thing to do the traveller any credit by the way ! one 
 feel a respect for the ruin of a man like Audley Egerton . he be ruin 
 en roi ! from the wreck of his fortune he can look down and see stately 
 monument build from the stone of that dismantle edifice . in every

 which the proverbial sinew of war be necessary , in those reward for

 parliament , in those day when talent be bring forward through 
 the aid of wealth and rank , owe his career to the seat which Audley 
 Egerton 's large subscription have secure to he ; many an obscure 
 supporter in letter and the Press look back to the day when he have 
 be free from the jail by the gratitude of the patron . the city he 
 represent be embellish at his cost ; through the shire that hold his 
 mortgage land , which he have rarely ever visit , his gold have flow

 its civilization , claim kindre with his munificence , and never have a

 large retinue and superb hospitality , there be something worthy of a 
 representative of that time - honour portion of our true nobility , 
 the untitled gentleman of the land . the Great Commoner have , indeed ,

 yet Frank Hazeldean have stuff in he , - a good heart , and strict honour .

 " I can not   bear this , " say Frank , suddenly , and spring to his foot .

 here Frank stop in his soliloquy , and do make his first desperate 
 effort to - think ! 

 now , o dear reader , I assume , of course , that thou art one of the class 
 to which thought be familiar ; and , perhaps , thou hast smile in disdain 
 or incredulity at that remark on the difficulty of thinking which 
 precede Frank Hazeldean 's discourse to himself . but art thou quite sure 
 that when thou hast try to think thou hast always succeed ? hast thou 
 not often be dupe by that pale visionary simulacrum of thought which 
 go by the name of revery ? honest old Montaigne confess that he do 
 not understand that process of sit down to think , on which some 
 folk express themselves so glibly . he could not think unless he have 
 a pen in his hand and a sheet of paper before he ; and so , by a manual 
 operation , seize and connect the link of ratiocination . very often 
 have it happen to myself when I have say to Thought peremptorily , 
 " Bestir thyself : a serious matter be before thee , ponder it well , think

 rebellious manner conceivable ; and instead of concentrate its ray

 discover that I might as well have go comfortably to sleep - I have

 CHAPTER XXV .

 the Italian be deeply move . nor be her nature that of the mere sordid

   at that date the law of mesne process exist still .

 " if a man love , " answer Frank , simply , " he feel it most when the

 " on the casino property . " 

 Frank draw back . 

 " no other way ? " 

 " of course not . but I know your scruple ; let we see if they can be 
 conciliated . you would marry Madame di Negra ; she will have L20,000

 anticipative charge on the casino property be pay at once ? thus , in

 know ledge , nor wound his feeling . and when you marry   if you will but 
 be prudent in the mean while , you will not owe a debt in the world . " 

 here the count suddenly start up . 

 " Mr. Hazeldean , I ask you to stay and aid we by your counsel ; I see 
 now that counsel be unavaile . this blow on our House must fall ! I 
 thank you , Sir , - I thank you . farewell . Levy , come with I to my poor 
 sister , and prepare she for the bad . " 

 " count , " say Frank , " hear I . my acquaintance with you be but slight ,

 " no , no ! " exclaim Peschiera . " how can you suppose that I will hear of 
 such a proposition ? your youth and benevolence mislead and blind you . 
 Impossible , sir , - impossible ! why , even if I have no pride , no delicacy 
 of my own , my sister 's fair fame - " 

 " would suffer indeed , " interrupt Levy , " if she be under such 
 obligation to any one but her affiance husband . nor , whatever my regard 
 for you , Count , could I suffer my client , Mr. Hazeldean , to make this

 Madame di Negra be entitle . " 

 " ha ! - be this indeed so ? you be a suitor for my sister 's hand , Mr. 
 Hazeldean ? " 

 " but not at this moment , - not to owe her hand to the compulsion of

 Frank do not listen ; he could only listen to his love , to his heart

 follow mechanically by Frank , the count ascend the stair , and throw

 then the marchesa , as if by a violent effort , so sudden be her 
 movement , and so wild her look , turn her face to her wooer , and come

 " oh , " she say , clasp her hand , " be this true ? you would save I

 Frank 's room with the necessary deed ; and , above all , to say nothing

 that evening Levy come to he , and account be go into , and paper

 " you accept - you accept I - and of your own free will and choice ? "

 Frank hear , and his face be radiant . by degree Madame di Negra

 Westminster , but not the Abbey ! " and Randal Leslie feel that , within the
