  thus run the world away ! " 




 BOOK ELEVENTH . 




 INITIAL chapter . 

 on the importance of HATE as an agent in civilized life . 

 it be not an uncommon crotchet amongst benevolent man to maintain that 
 wickedness be necessarily a sort of insanity , and that nobody would make 
 a violent start out of the straight path unless sting to such disorder 
 by a bee in his bonnet . certainly when some very clever , well - educate 
 person like our friend , Randal Leslie , act upon the fallacious 
 principle that " roguery be the good policy , " it be curious to see how 
 many point he have in common with the insane : what over - cunning , what 
 irritable restlessness , what suspicious belief that the rest of the 
 world be in a conspiracy against he , which it require all his wit 
 to baffle and turn to his own proper aggrandizement and profit . perhaps 
 some of my reader may have think that I have represent Randal as 
 unnaturally far - fetched in his scheme , too wire - draw and subtle in 
 his speculation ; yet that be commonly the case with very refining 
 intellect , when they choose to play the knave ; it help to disguise 
 from themselves the ugliness of their ambition , just as a philosopher 
 delight in the ingenuity of some metaphysical process , which end 
 in what plain man call " atheism , " who would be infinitely shock and 
 offend if he be call an atheist . 

 have premise thus much on behalf of the " Natural " in Randal Leslie 's 
 character , I must here fly off to say a word or two on the agency in 
 human life exercise by a passion rarely see without a mask in our 
 debonair and civilized age , - I mean Hate . 

 in the good old day of our forefather , when plain speak and hard 
 blow be in fashion , when a man have his heart at the tip of his 
 tongue , and four foot of sharp iron dangle at his side , Hate play 
 an honest , open part in the theatre of the world . in fact , when we read 
 history , Hate seem to have ' star it " on the stage . but now , where 
 be Hate ? who ever see its face ? be it that smile , good - temper 
 creature , that press you by the hand so cordially , or that dignified 
 figure of state that call you its " right honourable friend " ? be it that 
 bow , grateful dependent ; be it that soft - eyed Amaryllis ? ask not , 
 guess not : you will only know it to be hate when the poison be in your 
 cup , or the poniard in your breast . in the Gothic age , grim Humour 
 paint " the Dance of Death ; " in our polished century , some sardonic wit 
 should give we " the Masquerade of Hate . " 

 certainly , the counter - passion betray itself with ease to our gaze . 
 Love be rarely a hypocrite . but hate - how detect , and how guard against 
 it ? it lurk where you least suspect it ; it be create by cause that 
 you can the least foresee ; and Civilization multiply its variety , 
 whilst it favour its disguise : for Civilization increase the number 
 of contend interest , and Refinement render more susceptible to the 
 least irritation the cuticle of Self - love . but Hate come covertly 
 forth from some self - interest we have cross , or some self - love we 
 have wound ; and , dullard that we be , how seldom we be aware of our 
 offence ! you may be hat by a man you have never see in your life : you 
 may be hat as often by one you have load with benefit ; you may so 
 walk as not to tread on a worm ; but you must sit fast on your easy - chair 
 till you be carry out to your bi , if you would be sure not to tread 
 on some snake of a foe . but , then , what harm do the hate do we ? very 
 often the harm be as unseen by the world as the hate be unrecognize 
 by we . it may come on we , unawares , in some solitary byway of our life ; 
 strike we in our unsuspecting privacy ; thwart as in some bless hope 
 we have never tell to another ; for the moment the world see that it be 
 hate that strike we , its bad power of mischief be go . 

 we have a great many name for the same passion , - Envy , Jealousy , 
 Spite , Prejudice , Rivalry ; but they be so many synonym for the one old 
 heathen demon . when the death - give shaft of Apollo send the plague to 
 some unhappy Achaean , it do not much matter to the victim whether the 
 god be call Helios or Smintheus . 

 no man you ever meet in the world seem more raise above the malice 
 of Hate than Audley Egerton : even in the hot war of politic he have 
 scarcely a personal foe ; and in private life he keep himself so aloof 
 and apart from other that he be little know , save by the benefit the 
 waste of his wealth confer . that the hate of any one could reach the 
 austere statesman on his high pinnacle of esteem , - you would have smile 
 at the idea ! but Hate be now , as it ever have be , an actual power 
 amidst " the Varieties of life ; " and , in spite of bar to the door , and 
 policeman in the street , no one can be say to sleep in safety while 
 there wake the eye of a single foe . 




 CHAPTER II . 

 the glory of Bond Street be no more . the title of Bond Street Lounger 
 have fade from our lip . in vain the crowd of equipage and the blaze of 
 shop : the renown of Bond Street be in its pavement , its pedestrian . 
 art thou old enough , o reader ! to remember the Bond Street Lounger and 
 his incomparable generation ? for my part , I can just recall the decline 
 of the grand era . it be on its wane when , in the ambition of boyhood , I 
 first begin to muse upon high neck cloth and Wellington boot . but the 
 ancient habitue - the magni nomini umbrae , contemporary of Brummell 
 in his zenith , boon companion of George IV . in his regency - still 
 haunt the spot . from four to six in the hot month of June , they 
 saunter stately to and fro , look somewhat mournful even then , 
 forebode the extinction of their race . the Bond Street Lounger be 
 rarely see alone : he be a social animal , and walk arm in arm with 
 his fellow - man . he do not seem bear for the care of these ruder 
 time ; not make be he for an age in which Finsbury return member to 
 parliament . he love his small talk ; and never since then have talk be 
 so pleasingly small . your true Bond Street Lounger have a very dissipate 
 look . his youth have be spend with hero who love their bottle . 
 he himself have perhaps sup with Sheridan . he be by nature a 
 spendthrift : you see it in the roll of his walk . man who make money 
 rarely saunter ; man who save money rarely swagger . but saunter and 
 swagger both unite to stamp PRODIGAL on the Bond Street Lounger . and so 
 familiar as he be with his own set , and so amusingly supercilious with 
 the vulgar residue of mortal whose face be strange to Bond Street ! 
 but he be go . the world , though sad for his loss , still strive to 
 do its good without he ; and our young man , nowadays , attend to 
 model cottage , and incline to Tractarianism . still the place , to 
 an unreflecte eye , have its brilliancy and bustle ; but it be a 
 thoroughfare , not a lounge . and adown the thoroughfare , somewhat 
 before the hour when the throng be thick , pass two gentleman of an 
 appearance exceedingly out of keep with the place . - yet both have 
 the air of man pretend to aristocracy , - an old - world air of 
 respectability and stake in the country , and Church - and - stateism . the 
 burlier of the two be even rather a beau in his way . he have first 
 learn to dress , indeed , when Bond Street be at its acme , and Brummell 
 in his pride . he still retain in his garb the fashion of his youth ; 
 only what then have speak of the town , now betray the life of the 
 country . his neckcloth ample and high , and of snowy whiteness , set off 
 to comely advantage a face smooth - shaven , and of clear florid hue ; his 
 coat of royal blue , with button in which you might have see yourself 
 " veluti in speculum " , be rather jauntily button across a waist that 
 speak of lusty middle age , free from the ambition , the avarice , and the 
 anxiety that fret londoner into thread - paper ; his small - clothe , 
 of grayish drab , loose at the thigh and tight at the knee , be make by 
 Brummell 's own breech - maker , and the gaiter to match   thrust half - way 
 down the calf , have a manly dandyism that would have do honour to 
 the beau - ideal of a county member . the profession of this gentleman 's 
 companion be unmistakable , - the shovel - hat , the clerical cut of the 
 coat , the neckcloth without collar , that seem make for its accessory 
 the band , and something very decorous , yet very mild , in the whole mien 
 of this personage , all speak of one who be every inch the gentleman and 
 the parson .

 CHAPTER III . 

 whatever the disposition towards merriment at his cousin 's expense

 CHAPTER IV . 

 the parson start , and so do Leonard .

 " tablespoonful ? " 

 " tablespoonful . "

 CHAPTER V. 

 Audley Egerton stand on his hearth alone . during the short interval 
 that have elapse since we last see he , event have occur memorable 
 in english history , wherewith we have nought to do in a narrative 
 studiously avoid all party politic even when treat of 
 politician . the new minister have state the general programme of their 
 policy , and introduce one measure in especial that have lift they at 
 once to the dizzy height of popular power . but it become clear that this 
 measure could not be carry without a fresh appeal to the people . 
 a dissolution of parliament , as Audley 's sagacious experience have 
 foresee , be inevitable . and Audley Egerton have no chance of return for 
 his own seat , for the great commercial city identify with his name . 
 oh , sad , but not rare , instance of the mutability of that same popular 
 favour now enjoy by his successor ! the great commoner , the weighty 
 speaker , the expert man of business , the statesman who have seem a 
 type of the practical steady sense for which our middle class be 
 renowne , - he who , not three year since , might have have his honour 
 choice of the large popular constituency in the kingdom , - he , Audley 
 Egerton , know not one single town   free from the influence of private 
 property or interest   in which the obscure candidate , who bawl out 
 for the new liberal measure , would not have beat he hollow . where one 
 popular husting , on which that grave sonorous voice , that have still 
 so often the roar of faction , would not be drown amidst the hoot of 
 the scornful mob ? 

 true , what be call the close borough still exist ; true , many a 
 chief of his party would have be too proud of the honour of claim 
 Andley Egerton for his nominee . but the ex - minister 's haughty soul 
 shrink from this contrast to his past position . and to fight against 
 the popular measure , as member of one of the seat most denounce by the 
 people , - he feel it be a post in the grand army of party below his 
 dignity to occupy , and foreign to his peculiar mind , which require the 
 sense of consequence and station . and if , in a few month , those seat 
 be sweep away - be annihilate from the roll of parliament - where 
 be he ? moreover , Egerton , emancipate from the trammel that have bind 
 his will while his party be in office , desire , in the turn of event , 
 to be nominee of no man , - desire to stand at least freely and singly on 
 the ground of his own service , be guide by his own penetration ; no law 
 for action but his strong sense and his stout english heart . therefore 
 he have decline all offer from those who could still bestow seat in 
 parliament . seat that he could purchase with hard gold be yet open to 
 he . and the L5,000 he have borrow from Levy be yet untouched . 

 to this lone public man , public life , as we have see , be the all in 
 all . but now more than ever it be vital to his very want . around 
 he yawn ruin . he know that it be in Levy 's power at any moment to 
 foreclose on his mortgaged land ; to pour in the bond and the bill 
 which lie within those rosewood receptacle that line the fatal lair of 
 the sleek usurer ; to seize on the very house in which now move all the 
 pomp of a retinue that vie with the valetaille of duke ; to advertise 
 for public auction , under execution , " the costly effect of the right 
 Hon . Audley Egerton . " but , consummate in his knowledge of the world , 
 Egerton feel assure that Levy would not adopt these measure against 
 he while he could still tower in the van of political war , - while he 
 could still see before he the full chance of restoration to power , 
 perhaps to power still high than before , perhaps to power the high 
 of all beneath the throne . that Levy , whose hate he divine , though he 
 do not conjecture all its cause , have hitherto delay even a visit , 
 even a menace , seem to he to show that Levy still think he one 
 " to be help , " or , at least , one too powerful to crush . to secure 
 his position in parliament unshackle , unfallen , if but for another 
 year , - new combination of party might arise , new reaction take place , 
 in public opinion ! and , with his hand press to his heart , the stern 
 firm man mutter , " if not , I ask but to die in my harness , and that man 
 may not know that I be a pauper until all that I need from my country be 
 a grave . " 

 Scarce have these word die upon his lip ere two quick knock in 
 succession resound at the street door . in another moment Harley 
 enter , and , at the same time , the servant in attendance approach 
 Audley , and announce Baron Levy . 

 " beg the baron to wait , unless he would prefer to name his own hour to 
 call again , " answer Egerton , with the slight possible change of 
 colour . " you can say I be now with Lord L'Estrange . " 

 " I have hop you have do forever with that deluder of youth , " say 
 Harley , as soon as the groom of the chamber have withdraw . " I remember 
 that you see too much of he in the gay time , ere wild oat be sown ; 
 but now surely you can never need a loan ; and if so be not Harley 
 L'Estrange by your side ? " 

 EGERTON . - " my dear Harley ! doubtless he but come to talk to I of some 
 borough . he have much to do with those delicate negotiation . " 

 HARLEY . - " and I have come on the same business . I claim the priority . 
 I not only hear in the world , but I see by the paper , that Josiah 
 Jenkins , Esq . , know to fame as an orator who leave out his h 's , 
 and young Lord Willoughby Whiggolin , who be just make a Lord of the 
 Admiralty , because his health be too delicate for the army , be certain 
 to come in for the city which you and your present colleague will as 
 certainly vacate . that be true , be it not ? " 

 EGERTON . - " my old Committee now vote for Jenkins and Whiggolin ; and I 
 suppose there will not be even a contest . go on . " 

 " so my father and I be agree that you must condescend , for the sake of 
 old friendship , to be once more member for Lansmere . " 

 " Harley , " exclaim Egerton , change countenance far more than he have 
 do at the announcement of Levy 's portentous visit , " Harley , no , no ! " 

 " no ! but why ? wherefore such emotion ? " ask L'Estrauge , in surprise . 

 Audley be silent . 

 HARLEY . - " I suggest the idea to two or three of the late minister ; 
 they all concur in advise you to accede . in the first place , if 
 decline to stand for the place which tempt you from Lansmere , 
 what more natural than that you should fall back on that early 
 representation ? in the second place , Lansmere be neither a rotten 
 borough to be buy , nor a close borough , under one man 's nomination . 
 it be a tolerably large constituency . my father , it be true , have 
 considerable interest in it , but only what be call the legitimate 
 influence of property . at all event , it be more secure than a contest 
 for a large town , more dignified than a seat for a small . hesitate 
 still ? even my mother entreat I to say how she desire you to renew 
 that connection . " 

 " Harley , " again exclaim Egerton ; and fix upon his friend 's earnest 
 face eye which , when soften by emotion , be strangely beautiful 
 in their expression , - " Harley , if you could but read my heart at this 
 moment , you would - you would - " his voice falter , and he fairly bend 
 his proud head upon Harley 's shoulder ; grasp the hand he have catch 
 nervously , clingingly , " oh , Harley , if I ever lose your love , your 
 friendship , nothing else be leave to I in the world . " 

 " Audley , my dear , dear Audley , be it you who speak to I thus ? you , my 
 school friend , my life 's confidant , - you ? " 

 " I be grow very weak and foolish , " say Egerton , try to smile . " I 
 do not know myself . I , too , whom you have so often call ' stoic , ' 
 and liken to the Iron Man in the poem which you use to read by the 
 riverside at Eton . " 

 " but even then , my Audley , I know that a warm human heart   do what you 
 would to keep it down   beat strong under the iron rib . and I often 
 marvel now , to think you have go through life so free from the wilder 
 passion . Happier so ! " 

 Egerton , who have turn his face from his friend 's gaze , remain silent 
 for a few moment ; and he then seek to divert the conversation , and 
 rouse himself to ask Harley how he have succeed in his view upon 
 Beatrice , and his watch on the count . 

 " with regard to Peschiera , " answer Harley , " I think we must have 
 overrate the danger we apprehend , and that his wager be but an 
 idle boast . he have remain quiet enough , and seem devoted to play . his 
 sister have shut her door both on myself and my young associate during 
 the last few day . I almost fear that in spite of very sage warning of 
 mine , she must have turn his poet 's head , and that either he have meet 
 with some scornful rebuff to incautious admiration or that , he himself 
 have grow aware of peril , and decline to face it ; for he be very much 
 embarrassed when I speak to he respect she . but if the count be not 
 formidable , why , his sister be not need ; and I hope yet to get justice 
 for my italian friend through the ordinary channel . I have secure 
 an ally in a young austrian prince , who be now in London , and who have 
 promise to back , with all his influence , a memorial I shall transmit 
 to Vienna . - a propos , my dear Audley , now that you have a little 
 breathing - time , you must fix an hour for I to present to you my young 
 poet , the son of her sister . at moment the expression of his face be so 
 like her . " 

 " ay , ay , " answer Egerton , quickly , " I will see he as you wish , but 
 later . I have not yet that breathing - time you speak of ; but you say he 
 have prosper ; and , with your friendship , he be secure from fortune . I 
 rejoice to think so . " 

 " and your own protege , this Vandal Leslie , whom you forbid I to 
 dislike - hard task ! - what have he decide ? " 

 " to adhere to my fate . Harley , if it please Heaven that I do not live 
 to return to power , and provide adequately for that young man , do not 
 forget that he cling to I in my fall . " 

 " if he still cle to you faithfully , I will never forget it . I will 
 forget only all that now make I doubt he . but you talk of not live , 
 Audley ! Pooh ! your frame be that of a predestined octogenarian . " 

 " Nay , " answer Audley , " I be but utter one of those vague 
 generality which be common upon all mortal lip . and now farewell , - I 
 must see this baron . " 

 " not yet , until you have promise to consent to my proposal , and be 
 once more member for Lansmere . tut ! do not   shake your head . I can not be 
 deny . I claim your promise in right of our friendship , and shall be 
 seriously hurt if you even pause to reflect on it . " 

 " well , well , I know not how to refuse you , Harley ; but you have not be 
 to Lansmere yourself since - since that sad event . you must not revive

 CHAPTER VI . 

 when Harley be go , Egerton sink back on his chair , as if in extreme

 " to go back to that place - there - there - where - courage , courage ! what

 CHAPTER VII . 

 Peschiera have not be so inactive as he have appear to Harley and 
 the reader . on the contrary , he have prepare the way for his ultimate 
 design , with all the craft and the unscrupulous resolution which 
 belong to his nature . his object be to compel Riccabocca into 
 assenting to the count 's marriage with Violante , or , fail that , to 
 ruin all chance of his kinsman 's restoration . quietly and secretly 
 he have seek out , amongst the most needy and unprincipled of his 
 own countryman , those whom he could suborn to depose to Riccabocca 's 
 participation in plot and conspiracy against the austrian dominion . 
 these his former connection with the Carbonari enable he to track to 
 their refuge in London ; and his knowledge of the character he have to 
 deal with fit he well for the villanous task he undertake . he have , 
 therefore , already select out of these desperado a sufficient number 
 either to serve as witness against his kinsman , or to aid he in any 
 more audacious scheme which circumstance might suggest to his adoption . 
 meanwhile , he have   as Harley have suspect he would   set spy upon 
 Randal 's movement ; and the day before that young traitor confide to 
 he Violante 's retreat , he have at least get scent of her father 's . 

 the discovery that Violante be under a roof so honour , and seemingly 
 so safe , as Lord Lansmere 's , do not discourage this bold and desperate 
 adventurer . we have see he set forth to reconnoitre the house at 
 Knightsbridge . he have examine it well , and discover the quarter which 
 he judge favourable to a coup - de - main , should that become necessary . 

 Lord Lansmere 's house and ground be surround by a wall , the 
 entrance be to the high - road , and by a porter 's lodge . at the rear 
 there lie field cross by a lane or byroad . to these field a small 
 door in the wall , which be use by the gardener in pass to and from 
 their work , give communication . this door be usually keep lock ; 
 but the lock be of the rude and simple description common to such 
 entrance , and easily open by a skeleton key . so far there be no

 violent mean in the first instance . he have a confidence in his personal 
 gift , in his address , in his previous triumph over the sex , which make 
 he naturally desire to hazard the effect of a personal interview ; and 
 on this he resolve with his wonted audacity . Randal 's description of 
 Violante 's personal appearance , and such suggestion as to her character

 lynx - eyed observer could bestow , be all that the count require of 
 present aid from his accomplice . 

 meanwhile we return to Violante herself . we see she now seat in 
 the garden at Knightsbridge , side by side with Helen . the place be 
 retire , and out of sight from the window of the house . 

 VIOLANTE . - " but why will you not tell I more of that early time ? you 
 be less communicative even than Leonard . "

 so change now . " 

 the tone of the last word be mournful , and the word end with a

 HELEN   sadly and humbly . - " a part ! oh , no ! a part ? I do not   understand

 thing in life and in Nature influence genius ; but what influence it

 violante   suddenly . - " yes , Helen , yes , - I know by my own heart how 
 to read your . such memory be ineffaceable . few guess what

 and this warm , young , southern nature but a moment before be so full of

 would not be disperse ; or heave a deep sigh , as if to throw off some 
 load that no time henceforth could remove . there be certain moment 
 in life in which we say to ourselves , " all be over ; no matter what else 
 change , that which I have make my all be go evermore - evermore ! " and 
 our own thought ring back in our ear , " evermore - evermore ! " 




 CHAPTER VIII . 

 as Violante thus sit , a stranger , pass stealthily through the tree ,

 at the word " father " Violante rouse herself , and all her love for that

 she see before she a man of mild aspect and princely form . peschiera

 PESCHIERA . - " no , not yet . I would not see he till I have see yourself ;

 peschiera   with a look of compassionate admiration , and in a tone yet 
 more emphatically parental . - " how lovely be that innocent joy ! but 
 do not indulge it yet . perhaps it be a sacrifice which be ask from

 participator in that scheme . in a sudden moment I discover that some 
 of its more active projector have couple with a patriotic enterprise 
 plot of a dark nature , and that the conspiracy itself be about to be 
 betray to the government . I wish to consult with your father ; but

 hour be to be lose . I take a bold resolve , that have expose I to his

 my early friend , from death , and my country from fruitless massacre . 

 " I withdraw from the intend revolt . I seek at once the head of the 
 austrian government in Italy , and make term for the life of Alphonso 
 and of the other more illustrious chief , which otherwise would have 
 be forfeit . I obtain permission to undertake myself the charge of 
 secure my kinsman in order to place he in safety , and to conduct 
 he to a foreign land , in an exile that would cease when the danger be 
 dispel . but unhappily he deem that I only seek to destroy he . he 
 flee from my friendly pursuit . the soldier with I be attack by an 
 intermeddle Englishman ; your father escape from Italy , conceal 
 his retreat ; and the character of his flight counteract my effort 
 to obtain his pardon . the government confer on I half his revenue , 
 hold the other half at its pleasure . I accept the offer in order to 
 save his whole heritage from confiscation . that I do not convey to he 
 what I pin to do , - namely , the information that I hold but in trust 
 what be bestow by the government , and the full explanation of what 
 seem blamable in my conduct , - be necessarily owe to the secrecy he 
 maintain . I could not discover his refuge ; but I never cease to plead 
 for his recall . this year only I have partially succeed . he can be 
 restore to his heritage and rank , on one proviso , - a guarantee for his 
 loyalty . that guarantee the government have name : it be the alliance 
 of his only child with one whom the government can trust . it be the 
 interest of all the italian nobility that the representation of a House 
 so great fall to a female should not pass away wholly from the direct 
 line , - in a word , that you should ally yourself with a kinsman . but one 
 kinsman , and he the next in blood , present himself . in short , Alphonso 
 regain all that he lose on the day in which his daughter give she 
 hand to Giulio Franzini , Count di Peschiera . ah , " continue the count , 
 mournfully , " you shriek , you recoil . he thus submit to your choice be 
 indeed unworthy of you . you be scarce in the spring of life , he be in 
 its wan autumn . youth love youth . he do not aspire to your love . 
 all that he can say be , love be not the only joy of the heart , - it be 
 joy to raise from ruin a beloved father ; joy to restore , to a land poor 
 in all but memory , a chief in whom it reverence a line of hero . 
 these be the joy I offer to you , - you , a daughter , and an italian 
 maid . still silent ? oh , speak to I ! " 

 certainly this Count Peschiera know well how woman be to be woo and 
 win ; and never be woman more sensitive to those high appeal which most 
 move all true earnest womanhood than be the young Violante . Fortune 
 favour he in the moment choose . Harley be wrench away from her

 dream of Harley as that father 's friend ! she could restore to he all

 ever in itself such a temptation to the noble ! still , in the midst of

 another seem so terrible and revolting , that she could not at once

 again the count besought she to speak , and with an effort she say , 
 irresolutely , 

 " if it be as you say , it be not for I to answer you ; it be for my 
 father . " 

 " Nay , " reply Peschiera . " Pardon , if I contradict you . do you know so 
 little of your father as to suppose that he will suffer his interest 
 to dictate to his pride ? he would refuse , perhaps , even to receive my 
 visit , to hear my explanation ; but certainly he would refuse to buy 
 back his inheritance by the sacrifice of his daughter to one whom he have

 success ; and Italy would pardon my error , and bless your name . 
 ah , Signorina , do not think of I save as an instrument towards the 
 fulfilment of duty so high and sacred ! think but of your ancestor ,

 Violante 's heart be touch at the right chord . her head rise , the

 he vanish amidst the tree , and be go , - noiselessly , mysteriously ,

 CHAPTER IX . 

 the last word of Peschiera be still ring in Violante 's ear when

 " your father be well , believe I , and please that you be here . no

 Harley muse irresolute . he incline to confide to she the danger which

 " give I , " he say , " time to reflect , and to obtain permission to

 Violante start .

 Violante turn her eye for one instant towards the melancholy speaker .

 an hour afterwards he re - enter the house , and again seek to see

 he approach she with a grave and serious gentleness . " my dear Helen , "

 " I owe you too much , " answer Helen , look down , " to have any will 
 but your . but your mother , " she add , perhaps cling to the idea of

 " my mother - true . I will speak first to she . you shall receive from my 
 family all honour due to your gentle virtue . Helen , by the way , have 
 you mention to Violante the bond between we ? " 

 " No ; that is , I fear I may have unguardedly betray it , against Lady 
 Lansmere 's command too - but - but - " 

 " so , Lady Lansmere forbid you to name it to Violante ? this should not 
 be . I will answer for her permission to revoke that interdict . it be due 
 to Violante and to you . tell your young friend all . ah , Helen , if I be 
 at time cold or wayward , bear with I - bear with I ; for you love I , 
 do you not ? " 




 CHAPTER X. 

 that same evening Randal hear from Levy   at whose house he stay late 
 of that self - introduction to Violante which   thank to his skeleton 
 key   Peschiera have contrive to effect ; and the count seem more than 
 sanguine , - he seem assure as to the full and speedy success of his 
 matrimonial enterprise . " therefore , " say Levy , " I trust I may very soon 
 congratulate you on the acquisition of your family estate . " 

 " strange ! " answer Randal , ' strange that my fortune seem so bind up 
 with the fate of a foreigner like Beatrice di Negra and her connection 
 with Frank Hazeldean . " he look up at the clock as he speak , and add , 

 " Frank by this time have tell his father of his engagement . " 

 " and you feel sure that the squire can not be coax into consent ? " 

 " no ; but I feel sure that the squire will be so choleric at the first 
 intelligence , that Frank will not have the self - control necessary for 
 coax ; and , perhaps , before the squire can relent upon this point , 
 he may , by some accident , learn his grievance on another , which would 
 exasperate he still more . " 

 " ay , I understand , - the post - obit ? " Randal nod . 

 " and what then ? " ask Levy . 

 " the next of kin to the land of Hazeldean may have his day . " 

 the baron smile . 

 " you have good prospect in that direction , Leslie ; look now to another . 
 I speak to you of the borough of Lansmere . your patron , Audley Egerton , 
 intend to stand for it . " 

 Randal 's heart have of late be so set upon other and more avaricious 
 scheme , that a seat in parliament have sink into a secondary object ; 
 nevertheless his ambitious and all - grasp nature feel a bitter pang , 
 when he hear that Egerton thus interpose between himself and any 
 chance of advancement . 

 " so , " he mutter sullenly , - ' so this man , who pretend to be my 
 benefactor , squander away the wealth of my forefather , throw I 
 penniless on the world ; and , while still encourage I to exertion and 
 public life , rob I himself of - " 

 " no ! " interrupt Levy , " not rob you ; we may prevent that . the Lansmere 
 interest be not so strong in the borough as Dick Avenel 's . " 

 " but I can not stand against Egerton . " 

 " assuredly not , - you may stand with he . " 

 " how ? " 

 " Dick Avenel will never suffer Egerton to come in ; and though he can not , 
 perhaps , carry two of his own politic , he can split his vote upon 
 you . " 

 Randal 's eye flash . he see at a glance that if Avenel do not 
 overrate the relative strength of party , his seat could be secure . 

 " but , " he say , " Egerton have not speak to I on such a subject ; nor can 
 you expect that he would propose to I to stand with he , if he foresee 
 the chance of be oust by the very candidate he himself introduce . " 

 " neither he nor his party will anticipate that possibility . if he ask 
 you , agree to stand , - leave the rest to I . " 

 " you must hate Egerton bitterly , " say randal ; " for I be not vain enough 
 to think that you thus scheme but from pure love to I . " 

 " the motif of man be intricate and complicated , " answer Levy , with 
 unusual seriousness . " it suffice to the wise to profit by the action , 
 and leave the motif in shade . " 

 there be silence for some minute . then the two draw close towards 
 each other , and begin to discuss detail in their joint design . 

 Randal walk home slowly . it be a cold moonlit night . young idler 
 of his own year and rank pass he by on their way from the haunt of 
 social pleasure . they be yet in the first fair holiday of life . Life 's 
 holiday have go from he forever . graver man , in the various calling 
 of masculine labour - profession , trade , the state - pass he also . 
 their step might be sober , and their face careworn ; but no step have 
 the furtive stealth of his , no face the same contract , sinister , 
 suspicious gloom . only once , in a lonely thoroughfare , and on the 
 opposite side of the way , fall a footfall , and glance an eye , that 
 seem to betray a soul in sympathy with Randal Leslie 's . 

 and Randal , who have heed none of the other passenger by the way , 
 as if instinctively , take note of this one . his nerve crisp at 
 the noiseless slide of that form , as it stalk on from lamp to lamp , 
 keep pace with his own . he feel a sort of awe , as if he have behold 
 the wraith of himself ; and even as he glance suspiciously at the 
 stranger , the stranger glance at he . he be inexpressibly relieve 
 when the figure turn down another street and vanish . 

 that man be a felon , as yet undetected . between he and his kind there 
 stand but a thought , - a veil air - spin , but impassable , as the veil of 
 the image at Sais . 

 and thus move and thus look randal Leslie , a thing of dark and secret 
 mischief , within the pale of the law , but equally remove from man by 
 the vague consciousness that at his heart lie that which the eye of man 
 would abhor and loathe . Solitary amidst the vast city , and on through 
 the machinery of Civilization , go the still spirit of Intellectual 
 Evil . 




 CHAPTER XI . 

 early the next morning Randal receive two note , one from Frank , 
 write in great agitation , beg Randal to see and propitiate his 
 father , whom he fear he have grievously offend ; and then run off , 
 rather incoherently , into protestation that his honour as well as his 
 affection be engage irrevocably to Beatrice , and that she , at least , 
 he could never abandon . 

 and the second note be from the squire himself - short , and far less 
 cordial than usual - request Mr. Leslie to call on he . 

 randal dress in haste , and go first to Limmer 's hotel . he find the 
 parson with Mr. Hazeldean , and endeavour in vain to soothe he . the 
 squire have not sleep all night , and his appearance be almost haggard . 

 " Oho ! Mr. young Leslie , " say he , throw himself back in his chair 
 as Randal enter , " I think you be a friend , - I think you be 
 Frank 's adviser . explain , sir ! explain ! " 

 " gently , my dear Mr. Hazeldean , " say the parson . " you do but surprise 
 and alarm Mr. Leslie . tell he more distinctly what he have to explain . " 

 SQUIRE . - " do you or do you not tell I or Mrs. Hazeldean that Frank 
 be in love with Violante Rickeybockey ? " 

 RANDAL   as in amaze . - " I ! never , sir ! I fear , on the contrary , that 
 he be somewhat enamoured of a very different person . I hint at that

 and a roman Catholic , do not appear to consider such objection 
 insuperable , if Frank 's happiness be really at stake . " 

 here the poor squire give way to a burst of passion , that involve

 foreigner , Roman Catholics , and woman . while the squire be still 
 incapable of hear reason , the parson , take aside Randal , convince

 origin in a very natural mistake ; and that while that young gentleman 
 have be hint at Beatrice , Mrs. Hazeldean have be think of 
 Violante . with considerable difficulty he succeed in convey this 
 explanation to the squire , and somewhat appease his wrath against 
 Randal . and the Dissimulator , seize his occasion , then express so 
 much grief and astonishment at learn that matter have go as far as 
 the parson inform he , - that Frank have actually propose to Beatrice , 
 be accept , and engage himself , before even communicate with his 
 father ; he declare so earnestly , that he could never conjecture such 
 evil , that he have have Frank 's positive promise to take no step without 
 the sanction of his parent ; he profess such sympathy with the 
 squire 's wound feeling , and such regret at Frank 's involvement , that 
 Mr. Hazeldean at last yield up his honest heart to his consoler , 
 and grip Randal 's hand , say , " well , well , I wrong you ; beg your 
 pardon . what now be to be do ? " 

 " why , you can not consent to this marriage , - impossible ! " reply Randal ; 
 " and we must hope , therefore , to influence Frank by his sense of duty . " 

 " that be it , " say the squire ; " for I will not give way . pretty pass thing 
 have come to , indeed ! a widow , too , I hear . artful jade ! thought , no 
 doubt , to catch a Hazeldean of Hazeldean . my estate go to an outlandish 
 papistical set of mongrel brat ! no , no , never ! " 

 " but , " say the parson , mildly , " perhaps we may be unjustly prejudice 
 against this lady . we should have consent to Violante ; why not to she ? 
 she be of good family ? " 

 " certainly , " say randal . 

 " and good character ? " 

 Randal shake his head , and sigh . the squire catch he roughly by the 
 arm - " answer the parson ! " cry he , vehemently . 

 " indeed , sir , I can not speak disrespectfully of the character of a 
 woman , - who may , too , become Frank 's wife ; and the world be ill - natured 
 and not to be believe . but you can judge for yourself , my dear Mr. 
 Hazeldean . ask your brother whether Madame di Negra be one whom he would 
 advise his nephew to marry . " 

 " my brother ! " exclaim the squire , furiously . " consult my distant 
 brother on the affair of my own son ? " 

 " he be a man of the world , " put in Randal . 

 " and of feeling and honour , " say the parson ; " and , perhaps , through 
 he , we may be enable to enlighten Frank , and save he from what 
 appear to be the snare of an artful woman . " 

 " meanwhile , " say Randal , " I will seek Frank , and do my good with he . 
 let I go now , - I will return in an hour or so . " 

 " I will accompany you , " say the parson . 

 " Nay , pardon I , but I think we two young man can talk more openly 
 without a third person , even so wise and kind as you . " 

 " let randal go , " growl the squire . and Randal go . he spend some 
 time with Frank , and the reader will easily divine how that time be 
 employ . as he leave Frank 's lodging , he find himself suddenly seize 
 by the squire himself . 

 " I be too impatient to stay at home and listen to the parson 's 
 prosing , " say Mr. Hazeldean , nervously . " I have shake Dale off . tell 
 I what have pass . oh , do not   fear , - I be a man , and can bear the bad . " 

 Randal draw the squire 's arm within his , and lead he into the adjacent 
 park . 

 " my dear sir , " say he , sorrowfully , " this be very confidential what 
 I be about to say . I must repeat it to you , because , without such 
 confidence , I see not how to advise you on the proper course to take . 
 but if I betray Frank , it be for his good , and to his own father ; - only 
 do not tell he . he would never forgive I ; it would forever destroy my 
 influence over he . " 

 " go on , go on , " gasp the squire ; ' speak out . I will never tell the 
 ungrateful boy that I learn his secret from another . " 

 " then , " say Randal , " the secret of his entanglement with Madame di 
 Negra be simply this : he find she in debt - nay , on the point of be 
 arrest - " 

 " debt ! arrest ! Jezebel ! " 

 " and in pay the debt himself , and save she from arrest , he 
 confer on she the obligation which no woman of honour could accept 
 save from an affiance husband . Poor Frank ! - if sadly take in , still we 
 must pity and forgive he ! " 

 suddenly , to Randal 's great surprise , the squire 's whole face brighten 
 up . 

 " I see , I see ! " he exclaim , slap his thigh . " I have it , I have 
 it ! ' t be an affair of money ! I can buy she off . if she take money from 
 he - the mercenary , paint baggage i - why , then , she will take it from 
 I . I do not   care what if cost - half my fortune - all ! I would be content 
 never to see Hazeldean Hall again , if I could save my son , my own son , 
 from disgrace and misery ; for miserable he will be , when he know he have 
 break my heart and his mother 's . and for a creature like that ! my boy , 
 a thousand hearty thank to you . where do the wench live ? I will go 
 to she at once . " and as he speak , the squire actually pull out his 
 pocketbook , and begin turn over and count the bank - note in it . 

 randal at first try to combat this bold resolution on the part of the 
 squire ; but Mr. Hazeldean have seize on it with all the obstinacy of his 
 straightforward english mind . he cut Randal 's persuasive eloquence off 
 in the midst . 

 " do not   waste your breath ! I have   settle it ; and if you do not   tell I 
 where she live , ' t be easily find out , I suppose . " 

 Randal muse a moment . " after all , " think he , " why not ? he will be 
 sure so to speak as to enlist her pride against himself , and to irritate

 accordingly he give the information require ; and , insist with great 
 earnestness on the squire 's promise not to mention to Madame di Negra 
 his knowledge of Frank 's pecuniary aid   for that would betray randal 
 as the informant ; and satisfy himself as he well might with the 
 squire 's prompt assurance , " that he know how to settle matter , without 
 say why or wherefore , as long as he open his purse wide enough , "

 he , - fix an hour in the evening for an interview at Limmer 's , 
 and hint that it would be good to have that interview without the 
 presence of the parson . 

 " excellent good man , " say Randal , " but not with sufficient knowledge of 
 the world for affair of this kind , which you understand so well . " 

 " I should think so , " quoth the squire , who have quite recover his 
 good - humour . " and the parson be as soft as buttermilk . we must be firm 
 here , - firm , sir . " and the squire strike the end of his stick on the 
 pavement , nod to Randal , and go on to May Fair as sturdily and as 
 confidently as if to purchase a prize cow at a cattle - show . 




 CHAPTER XII . 

 " bring the light nearer , " say John Burley , - " nearer still . " 

 Leonard obey , and place the candle on a little table by the sick 
 man 's bedside . 

 Burley 's mind be partially wandering ; but there be method in his 
 madness . Horace Walpole say that " his stomach would survive all the 
 rest of he . " that which in Burley survive the last be his quaint , 
 wild genius . he look wistfully at the still flame of the candle : " it 
 live ever in the air ! " say he . 

 " what live ever ? " 

 Burley 's voice swell , " Light ! " he turn from Leonard , and 
 again contemplate the little flame . " in the fix star , in the 
 Will - o'-the - wisp , in the great sun that illumine half a world , or the 
 farthing rushlight by which the ragged student strain his eye , - still 
 the same flower of the element ! light in the universe , think in 
 the soul - ay , ay , go on with the simile . my head swim . extinguish the 
 light ! you can not ; fool , it vanish from your eye , but it be still in 
 the space . Worlds must perish , sun shrivel up , matter and spirit both 
 fall into nothingness , before the combination whose union make that 
 little flame which the breath of a babe can restore to darkness , 
 shall lose the power to form themselves into light once more . lose the 
 power ! - no , the necessity : it be the one must in creation . ay , ay , very 
 dark riddle grow clear now , - now when I could not cast up an addition 
 sum in the baker 's bill ! what wise man deny that two and two make 
 four ? do they not make four ? I can not   answer he . but I could answer a 
 question that some wise man have contrive to make much knottier . " he 
 smile softly , and turn his face for some minute to the wall . 

 this be the second night on which Leonard have watch by his bedside , 
 and Burley 's state have grow rapidly bad . he could not last many day , 
 perhaps many hour . but he have evince an emotion beyond mere delight 
 at see Leonard again . he have since then be calm , more himself . " I 
 fear I might have ruin you by my bad example , " he say , with a touch 
 of humour that become pathos as he add , " that idea prey on I . " 

 " no , no ; you do I great good . " 

 " say that , - say it often , " say Burley , earnestly ; " it make my heart 
 feel so light . " 

 he have listen to Leonard 's story with deep interest , and be fond 
 of talk to he of little Helen . he detect the secret at the young 
 man 's heart , and cheer the hope that lie there , amidst fear and 
 sorrow . Burley never talk seriously of his repentance ; it be not in 
 his nature to talk seriously of the thing which he feel solemnly . but 
 his high animal spirit be quench with the animal power that feed 
 they . now , we go out of our sensual existence only when we be no long 
 enthral by the Present , in which the sens have their realm . the 
 sensual being vanishe when Ave be in the past or the Future . the 
 Present be go from Burley ; he could no more be its slave and its 
 king . 

 it be most touching to see how the inner character of this man unfold 
 itself , as the leaf of the outer character fall off and wither , - a 
 character no one would have guess in he , an inherent refinement that 
 be almost womanly ; and he have all a woman 's abnegation of self . he 
 take the care lavish on he so meekly . as the feature of the old 
 man return in the stillness of death to the aspect of youth , - the line 
 efface , the wrinkle go , - so , in see Burley now , you see what he 
 have be in his spring of promise . but he himself see only what he have 
 fail to be , - power squander , life waste . " I once behold , " he say , 
 " a ship in a storm . it be a cloudy , fitful day , and I could see the 
 ship with all its mast fight bard for life and for death . then come 
 night , dark as pitch , and I could only guess that the ship fight on . 
 towards the dawn the star grow visible , and once more I see the ship : 
 it be a wreck , - it go down just as the star shine forth . " 

 when he have make that allusion to himself , he sit very still for some 
 time , then he spread out his waste hand , and gaze on they , and on 
 his shrunken limb . " good , " say he , laugh low ; " these hand be too 
 large and rude for handle the delicate webs of my own mechanism , 
 and these strong limb run away with I . if I have be a sickly , puny 
 fellow , perhaps my mind would have have fair play . there be too much of 
 brute body here ! look at this hand now ! you can see the light through 
 it ! good , good ! " 

 now , that evening , until he have retire to bed , Burley have be 
 unusually cheerful , and have talk with much of his old eloquence , if 
 with little of his old humour . amongst other matter , he have speak with 
 considerable interest of some poem and other paper in manuscript which 
 have be leave in the house by a former lodger , and which , the reader may 
 remember , Mrs. Goodyer have urge he in vain to read , in his last visit 
 to her cottage . but then he have her husband Jacob to chat with , and the 
 spirit bottle to finish , and the wild craving for excitement pluck his 
 thought back to his London revel . now poor Jacob be dead , and it be 
 not brandy that the sick man drink from the widow 's cruse ; and London 
 lie afar amidst its fog , like a world resolve back into nebula . so , 
 to please his hostess and distract his own solitary thought , he have 
 condescend   just before Leonard find he out   to peruse the memorial 
 of a life obscure to the world , and new to his own experience of coarse 
 joy and woe . " I have be make a romance , to amuse myself , from 
 their content , " say he . " they maybe of use to you , brother author . I 
 have tell Mrs. Goodyer to place they in your room . amongst those paper 
 be a sort of journal , - a woman 's journal ; it move I greatly . a man 
 get into another world , strange to he as the orb of Sirius , if he can 
 transport himself into the centre of a woman 's heart , and see the 
 life there , so wholly unlike our own . thing of moment to we , to it so 
 trivial ; thing trifle to we , to it so vast . there be this journal , 
 in its date remind I of stormy event in my own existence , and 
 grand doing in the world 's . and those date there , chronicle but the 
 mysterious , unrevealed record of some obscure , love heart ! and in 
 that chronicle , o Sir Poet , there be as much genius , vigour of thought , 
 vitality of be , pour and waste , as ever kind friend will say be 
 lavish on the rude outer world by big John Burley ! Genius , genius ! be 
 we all alike , then , save when we leash ourselves to some matter - of - fact 
 material , and float over the roar sea on a wooden plank or a herring 
 tub ? " and after he have utter that cry of a secret anguish , John Burley 
 have begin to show symptom of grow fever and disturb brain ; and 
 when they have get he into bed , he lie there mutter to himself , 
 until , towards midnight , he have ask Leonard to bring the light nearer 
 to he . 

 so now he again be quiet , with his face turn towards the wall ; and 
 Leonard stand by the bedside sorrowfully , and Mrs. Goodyer , who do not 
 heed Burley 's talk , and think only of his physical state , be dip 
 cloth into iced water to apply to his forehead . but as she approach 
 with these , and address he soothingly , Burley raise himself on his 
 arm , and wave aside the bandage . " I do not need they , " say he , in a 
 collect voice . " I be well now . I and that pleasant light understand 
 one another , and I believe all it tell I . Pooh , pooh , I do not rave . " 
 he look so smilingly and so kindly into her face , that the poor woman , 
 who love he as her own son , fairly burst into tear . he draw she 
 towards he , and kiss her forehead . 

 " peace , old fool , " say he , fondly . " you shall tell angler hereafter 
 how John Burley come to fish for the one - eyed perch which he never 
 catch ; and how , when he give it up at the last , his bait all gone , and 
 the line break amongst the weed , you comfort the baffled man . there 
 be many good fellow yet in the world who will like to know that poor 
 Burley do not die on a dunghill . kiss I . come , boy , you too . now , God 
 bless you , I should like to sleep . " his cheek be wet with the tear 
 of both his listener , and there be a moisture in his own eye , which , 
 nevertheless , beam bright through the moisture . 

 he lay himself down again , and the old woman would have withdraw the 
 light . he move uneasily . " not that , " he murmur , - " light to the last ! " 
 and put forth his wan hand , he draw aside the curtain so that the 
 light might fall full on his face . 

   every one remember that Goethe 's last word be say to have be , 
  " More light ; " and perhaps what have occur in the text may be 
  suppose a plagiarism from those word . but , in fact , nothing be 
  more common than the craving and demand for light a little before 
  death . let any consult his own sad experience in the last moment 
  of those whose gradual close he have watch and tend . what more 
  frequent than a prayer to open the shutter and let in the sun ? 
  what complaint more repeat and more touching than " that it be 
  grow dark " ? I once know a sufferer , who do not then seem in 
  immediate danger , suddenly order the sick room to be light up as if 
  for a gala . when this be tell to the physician , he say gravely , 
  " no bad sign . " 

 in a few minute he be asleep , breathe calmly and regularly as an 
 infant . 

 the old woman wipe her eye , and draw Leonard softly into the adjoining 
 room , in which a bed have be make up for he . he have not leave the house 
 since he have enter it with Dr. Morgan . " you be young , sir , " say she , 
 with kindness , " and the young want sleep . lie down a bit : I will call 
 you when he wake . " 

 " no , I could not sleep , " say Leonard . " I will watch for you . " 

 the old woman shake her head . " I must see the last of he , sir ; but I 
 know he will be angry when his eye open on I , for he have grow very 
 thoughtful of other . " 

 " ah , if he have but be , as thoughtful of himself ! " murmur Leonard ; 
 and he seat himself by the table , on which , as he lean his elbow , he 
 dislodge some paper place there . they fall to the ground with a dumb , 
 moaning , sigh sound . - 

 " what be that ? " say he , start . 

 the old woman pick up the manuscript and smooth they carefully . 

 " ah , sir , he bid I place these paper here . he think they might keep 
 you from fret about he , in case you would sit up and wake . and he 
 have a thought of I , too ; for I have so pin to find out the poor young 
 lady , who leave they year ago . she be almost as dear to I as he be ; 
 dearer perhaps until now - when - when I be about to lose he ! " 

 Leonard turn from the paper , without a glance at their content : they 
 have no interest for he at such a moment . the hostess go on , 

 " perhaps she be go to heaven before he ; she do not look like one 
 long for this world . she leave we so suddenly . many thing of her 
 besides these paper be still , here ; but I keep they air and dust , 
 and strew lavender over they , in case she ever come for they again . 
 you never hear tell of she , do you , sir ? " she add , with great 
 simplicity , and drop a half courtesy . 

 " of she - of whom ? " 

 " do not Mr. John tell you her name - dear , dear ; Mrs. Bertram . " 

 Leonard start ; the very name so impressed upon his memory by Harley 
 L'Estrange ! 

 " Bertram ! " he repeat . " be you sure ? " 

 " oh , yes , sir ! and many year after she have leave we , and we have hear no 
 more of she , there come a packet address to she here , from over sea , 
 sir . we take it in , and keep it , and John would break the seal , to know 
 if it would tell we anything about she ; but it be all in a foreign 
 language like , - we could not read a word . " 

 " have you the packet ? pray show it to I . it may be of the great 
 value . to - morrow will do - I can not think of that just now . poor Burley ! " 

 Leonard 's manner indicate that he wish to talk no more , and to be 
 alone . so Mrs. Goodyer leave he , and steal back to Burley 's room on 
 tiptoe : 

 the young man remain in deep revery for some moment . " Light , " he 
 murmur . " how often ' Light ' be the last word of those round whom the 
 shade be gather ! " he move , and straight on his view through the 
 cottage lattice there stream light indeed , - not the miserable ray light 
 by a human hand , but the still and holy effulgence of a moonlit heaven . 
 it lie broad upon the humble floor , pierce across the threshold of the 
 death chamber , and halt clear amidst its shadow . 

 Leonard stand motionless , his eye follow the silvery silent 
 splendour . 

 " and , " he say inly - " and do this large err nature , mar by its 
 genial fault , this soul which should have fill a land , as yon orb the 
 room , with a light that link earth to heaven - do it pass away into 
 the dark , and leave not a ray behind ? Nay , if the element of light be 
 ever in the space , and when the flame go out , return to the vital air , 
 so think once kindle life forever around and about we , a part of our 
 breathing atmosphere . many a thinker , many a poet , may yet illumine the 
 world , from the thought which yon genius , that will have no name , give 
 forth to wander through air , and recombine again in some new form of 
 light . " 

 thus he go on in vague speculation , seek , as youth enamour 
 of fame seek too fondly , to prove that mind never work , however 
 erratically , in vain , and to retain yet , as an influence upon earth , 
 the soul about to soar far beyond the atmosphere where the element that 
 make fame abide . not thus have the die man interpret the endurance of 
 light and think . 

 suddenly , in the midst of his revery , a loud cry break on his ear . he 
 shudder as he hear , and hasten forebodingly into the adjoining 
 room . the old woman be kneel by the bedside , and chafe Burley 's 
 hand , eagerly look into his face . a glance suffice to Leonard . all 
 be over . Burley have die in sleep , - calmly , and without a groan . 

 the eye be half open , with that look of inexpressible softness which 
 death sometimes leave ; and still they be turn towards the light ; 
 and the light burn clear . 

 Leonard close tenderly the heavy lid ; and as he cover the face , the 
 lip smile a serene farewell . 




 CHAPTER XIII . 

 we have see Squire Hazeldean   proud of the content of his pocketbook , 
 and his knowledge of the mercenary nature of foreign woman   set off on 
 his visit to Beatrice di Negra . randal thus left , muse lone in the 
 crowded street , resolve with astute complacency the probable result 
 of Mr. Hazeldean 's bluff negotiation ; and convince himself that one of 
 his vista towards Fortune be become more clear and clear , he turn , 
 with the restless activity of some founder of destine city in a new 
 settlement , to lop the bough that cumber and obscure the other . for 
 truly , like a man in a vast columbian forest , open entangled space , 
 now with the ready axe , now with the patient train that kindle the 
 slow fire , this child of civilized life go toil on against 
 surround obstacle , resolute to destroy , but ever scheme to 
 construct . and now Randal have reach Levy 's dainty business - room , and 
 be bury deep in discussion how to secure to himself , at the expense 
 of his patron , the representation of Lansmere , and how to complete the 
 contract which shall reannex to his forlorn inheritance some fragment 
 of its ancient wealth . 

 meanwhile , Chance fight on his side in the boudoir of May Fair . the 
 squire have find the marchesa at home , briefly introduce himself and 
 his business , tell she she be mistaken if she have fancy she have 
 take in a rich heir in his son ; that , thank Heaven , he could leave his 
 estate to his ploughman , should he so please , but that he be willing 
 to do thing liberally ; and whatever she think Frank be worth , he be 
 very ready to pay for . 

 at another time Beatrice would perhaps have laugh at this strange 
 address ; or she might , in some prouder moment , have fire up with all

 speechless ; and the poor squire think he be quietly advance 
 towards a favourable result , when she suddenly burst into a passion

 filial duty give way . he be madden by irritation , by the insult 
 offer to the woman he love , which a few trembling word from she 
 explain to he , - madden yet more by the fear that the insult have 
 lose she to he ; warm word ensue between son and father , to close with 
 the peremptory command and vehement threat of the last . 

 " come away this instant , sir ! come with I , or before the day be over , I 
 strike you out of my will ! " 

 the son 's answer be not to his father ; he throw himself at Beatrice 's 
 foot . 

 " forgive he ; forgive we both - " 

 " what ! you prefer that strange to I , - to the inheritance of 
 Hazeldean ! " cry the squire , stamp his foot . 

 " leave your estate to whom you will ; all that I care for in life be 
 here ! " 

 the squire stand still a moment or so , gaze on his son with a strange 
 bewildered marvel at the strength of that mystic passion , which none

 sudden idol that no reason justifie , and sacrifice to its fatal shrine 
 alike the Past and the future . not trust himself to speak , the father

 and leave the house . 

 he walk through the street , bear his head very erect , as a proud

 that he deem a weakness ; and his tremble nervous finger fumble at

 as if to confirm a resolution that still seek to struggle out of the 
 revolting heart . 

 thus he go on , and the reader , perhaps , will wonder whither ; and the

 at the squire 's brief inquiry whether Mr. Egerton be at home , the

 Audley be seat at his desk , the grim iron box still at his foot ,

 the two brother stand on the great man 's lonely hearth , face each

 the squire , with his portly size , his hardy sunburned cheek , the

 man of city retain youth long than those of the country , - a remark

 CHAPTER XIV . 

 on enter the drawing - room of Madame di Negra , the peculiar charm 
 which the severe Audley Egerton have be ever repute to possess with 
 woman would have sensibly strike one who have hitherto see he chiefly 
 in his relation with man in the business - like affair of life . it be 
 a charm in strong contrast to the ordinary manner of those who be 
 emphatically call " lady ' man . " no artificial smile , no conventional , 
 hollow blandness , no frivolous gossip , no varnish either of ungenial 
 gayety or affected grace . the charm be in a simplicity that unbent more 
 into kindness than it do with man . Audley 's nature , whatever its 
 fault and defect , be essentially masculine ; and it be the sense 
 of masculine power that give to his voice a music when address the 
 gentler sex , and to his manner a sort of indulgent tenderness that 
 appear equally void of insincerity and presumption . 

 Frank have be go about half - an - hour , and Madame di Negra be scarcely 
 recover from the agitation into which she have be throw by the 
 affront from the father and the pleading of the son . 

 Egerton take her passive hand cordially , and seat himself by her side . 

 " my dear marchesa , " I say he , " be we then likely to be near 
 connection ? and can you seriously contemplate marriage with my young 
 nephew , Frank Hazeldean ? you turn away . ah , my fair friend , there be 
 but two inducement to a free woman to sign away her liberty at the 
 altar . I say a free woman , for widow be free , and girl be not . these 
 inducement be , first , worldly position ; secondly , love . which of these 
 motif can urge Madame di Negra to marry Mr. Frank Hazeldeani ? " 

 " there be other motif than those you speak of , - the need of 
 protection , the sense of solitude , the curse of dependence , gratitude 
 for honourable affection . but you man never know woman ! " 

 " I grant that you be right there , - we never do ; neither do woman ever 
 know man . and yet each sex contrive to dupe and to fool the other ! 
 listen to I . I have little acquaintance with my nephew , but I allow he 
 be a handsome young gentleman , with whom a handsome young lady in her 
 teen might fall in love in a ball - room . but you , who have know the 
 high order of our specie , you who have receive the homage of man , 
 whose thought and mind leave the small talk of drawing - room trifler 
 so poor and bald , you can not look I in the face and say that it be 
 any passion resemble love which you feel for my nephew . and as to 
 position , it be right that I should inform you that if he marry you 
 he will have none . he may risk his inheritance . you will receive no 
 countenance from his parent . you will be poor , but not free . you 
 will not gain the independence you seek for . the sight of a vacant , 
 discontent face in that opposite chair will be bad than solitude . 
 and as to grateful affection , " add the man of the world , " it be a 
 polite synonym for tranquil indifference . " 

 " Mr. Egerton , " say Beatrice , " people say you be make of bronze . do 
 you ever feel the want of a home ? " 

 " I answer you frankly , " reply the statesman , " if I have not feel it , 
 do you think I should have be , and that I should be to the last , the 
 joyless drudge of public life ? bronze though you call my nature , it 
 would have melt away long since like wax in the fire , if I have sit 
 idly down and dream of a home ! " 

 " but we woman , " answer Beatrice , with pathos , " have no public life , 
 and we do idly sit down and dream . oh , " she continue , after a short 
 pause , and clasp her hand firmly together , " you think I worldly , 
 grasping , ambitious ; how different my fate have be have I know a 
 home ! - know one whom I could love and venerate ; know one whose smile 
 would have develop the good that be once within I , and the fear of 
 whose rebuking or sorrowful eye would have correct what be evil . " 

 " yet , " answer Audley , " nearly all woman in the great world have have 
 that choice once in their life , and nearly all have throw it away . how 
 few of your rank really think of home when they marry ! how few ask to 
 venerate as well as to love ! and how many , of every rank , when the 
 home have be really gain , have wilfully lose its shelter , - some in 
 neglectful weariness , some from a momentary doubt , distrust , caprice , 
 a wild fancy , a passionate fit , a trifle , a straw , a dream ! true , you 
 woman be ever dreamer . Commonsense , common earth , be above or below 
 your comprehension . " 

 both now be silent . Audley first rouse himself with a quick , writhe 
 movement . " we two , " say he , smile half sadly , half cynically , - " we 
 two must not long waste time in talk sentiment . we know both

 need must sacrifice rank and fortune , you , with your beauty and your 
 romantic heart , will see one who , at least for a fair holiday season 
  if human love allow no more , can repay you for the sacrifice . Frank 
 Hazeldean never can . " 

 Beatrice turn away to conceal the tear that rush to her eye . 

 " think over this well , " say Audley , in the soft tone of his mellow 
 voice . " do you remember that when you first come to England , I tell you 
 that neither wedlock nor love have any lur for I ? we grow friend upon 
 that rude avowal , and therefore I now speak to you like some sage of 
 old , wise because stand apart and aloof from all the affection and 
 tie that mislead our wisdom . nothing but real love - how rare it be ; have 
 one human heart in a million ever know it ? - nothing but real love can 
 repay we for the loss of freedom , the care and fear of poverty , the 
 cold pity of the world that we both despise and respect . and all these , 
 and much more , follow the step you would inconsiderately take , an 
 imprudent marriage . " 

 " Audley Egerton , " say Beatrice , lift her dark , moistened eye , " you 
 grant that real love do compensate for an imprudent marriage . you 
 speak as if you have know such love - you ! can it be possible ? " 

 " real love - I think that I know it once . look back with remorse , 
 I should doubt it now but for one curse that only real love , when lose , 
 have the power to leave evermore behind it . " 

 " what be that ? " 

 " a void here , " answer Egerton , strike his heart . 
 " Desolation ! - Adieu ! " 

 he rise and leave the room . 

 " be it , " murmur Egerton , as he pursue his way through the 
 street - " be it that , as we approach death , all the first fair feeling 
 of young life come back to we mysteriously ? thus I have hear , or read , 
 that in some country of old , child scatter flower precede a 
 funeral bier . " 




 CHAPTER XV . 

 and so Leonard stand beside his friend 's mortal clay , and watch , in 
 the ineffable smile of death , the last gleam which the soul have leave 
 there ; and so , after a time , he creep back to the adjoining room with a 
 step as noiseless as if he have fear to disturb the dead . weary as 
 he be with watch , he have no thought of sleep . he sit himself down by 
 the little table , and lean his face on his hand , muse sorrowfully . 
 thus time pass . he hear the clock from below strike the hour . in the 
 house of death the sound of a clock become so solemn . the soul that we 
 miss have go so far beyond the reach of time ! a cold , superstitious

 near the extinguished fire , Leonard see the solitary woman , weep low ; 
 and watch still . he return to say a word of comfort ; she press

 truthful than bust or portrait , it bid we see the tear flow , and the

 of the dead ? 

 the bulk of the paper have be once lightly sew to each other ; they

 journal , - not , indeed , a regular diary , nor always relate to the

 who question , it be she who answer ; and soon there steal upon you ,

 youth , already recognize the rude imperfect scholar , the village bard , 
 Mark Fairfield . then there be a gap in description ; but there be short 
 weighty sentence , which show deepen thought , increase year , in

 now , insensibly , Leonard find that there be a new phase in the writer 's

 here Leonard 's interest grow intense . be there touch by which

 and now these sentence grow brief still , as with the decision of a

 a few word tell the final parting , - word that be a picture . the long

 the writer be in London , in the house of some high - bear

 after this the narrative , to judge by the date , pause for day and

 a personage not see till then appear on the page . and ever

 CHAPTER XVI . 

 Nora Avenel have flee from the boyish love of Harley L'Estrange ,

 possible that the lowborn girl could long sustain her generous pride ,

 and wholly of the same opinion as Lady Lansmere , and sincerely anxious

 " but why ? " 

 " the girl do not love you . cease then to think of she . "

 abandon prudence in this arch affair of life , Audley still

 learn already how to avenge himself on Egerton . Audley could not apply

 have a great number . and no man in town know well that he should lose

 of his paternal estate , on which there be a gloomy manor - house , long

 and now - to condense as much as possible a record that run darkly on

 image at war with love , and with the poetry of life , he disattune it 
  so to speak   for the reception of Nora 's letter , all musical as they 
 be with such thought as the most delicate fancy inspire to the 
 most earnest love . Egerton be one of those man who never confide their 
 affair frankly to woman . Nora , when she thus write , be wholly in 
 the dark as to the extent of his stern prosaic distress . and so - and 
 so - Levy always near - type of the prose of life in its most cynic 
 form - so by degree all that redundant affluence of affection , with its 
 gush of grief for his absence , prayer for his return , sweet reproach 
 if a post fail to bring back an answer to the woman 's yearning 
 sigh , - all this grow , to the sensible , positive man of real life , 
 like sickly romantic exaggeration . the bright arrow shoot too high into 
 heaven to hit the mark set so near to the earth . ah , common fate of all 
 superior nature ! what treasure , and how wildly waste ! " by - the - by , " 
 say Levy , one morning , as he be about to take leave of Audley 
 and return to town , - " by - the - by , I shall be this evening in the 
 neighbourhood of Mrs. Egerton . " 

 EGERTON . - " say Mrs. Bertram ! " 

 LEVY . - " Ay ; will she not be in want of some pecuniary supply ? " 

 EGERTON . " my wife ! - not yet . I must first be wholly ruin before she 
 can want ; and if I be so , do you think I should not be by her side ? " 

 LEVY . - " I beg pardon , my dear fellow ; your pride of gentleman be so 
 susceptible that it be hard for a lawyer not to wound it unawares . your 
 wife , then , do not know the exact state of your affair ? " 

 EGERTON . - " of course not . who would confide to a woman thing in which 
 she could do nothing , except to tease one the more ? " 

 LEVY . - " true , and a poetess too ! I have prevent your finish your 
 answer to Mrs. Bertram 's last letter . can I take it - it may save a day 's 
 delay - that be , if you do not object to my calling on she this evening . " 

 egerton   sit down to his unfinished letter . - " object ! no . " 

 LEVY   look at his watch . - " be quick , or I shall lose the coach . " 

 egepton   seal the letter . - " there . and I should be oblige to you if 
 you would call ; and without alarm she as to my circumstance , you 
 can just say that you know I be much harass about important affair at 
 present , and so soothe the effect of my very short answer - " 

 LEVY . - " to those doubly - cross , very long letter , - I will . " 

 " Poor Nora , " say Egerton , sighing , ' she will think this answer brief 
 and churlish enough . explain my excuse kindly , so that they will serve 
 for the future . I really have no time and no heart for sentiment . the 
 little I ever have be well - nigh worry out of I . still I love she 
 fondly and deeply . " 

 LEVY . - " you must have do so . I never think it in you to sacrifice 
 the world to a woman . " 

 EGERTON . - " nor I either ; but , " add the strong man , conscious of that 
 power which rule the world infinitely more than knowledge , conscious of 
 tranquil courage , " but I have not sacrifice the world yet . this right 
 arm shall bear up she and myself too . " 

 LEVY . - " well say ! but in the mean while , for heaven 's sake , do not 
 attempt to go to London , nor to leave this place ; for , in that case , 
 I know you will be arrest , and then adieu to all hope of 
 parliament , - of a career . " 

 Audley 's haughty countenance darken ; as the dog , in his brave mode ,

 then Nora 's anger burst forth . she believe such a stain on Audley 's

 " but where be the honour when he betray his friend ? do you not know

 Levy resume . he dwell fully on the trust and the breach of it , and then

 " I will go to he , " cry Nora , impetuously . 

 " go to he ! - in his own house ! what a scene , what a scandal ! could he 
 ever forgive you ? " 

 " at least , then , I will implore he to come here . I can not write such 
 horrible word ; I can not ! I can not ! go , go ! " Levy leave she , and hasten

 and Nora 's letter come ; and never be Audley 's grave brow more dark than 
 when he read it . still , with his usual decision , he resolve to obey

 Levy then take he aside , and lead he to the window . " look under yon

 " Sir ! " exclaim Egerton , turn white . 

 " do not   be angry , - all be fair in love as in war ; and L'Estrange will live

 with some difficulty and much fawning , the usurer appease the storm

 " you be quite right ; but let we see if we can not reconcile matter

 " why say six month ? " 

 " Parliament must be dissolve , and there must be a general election

 " or what ? " 

 " I shall renounce ambition altogether , ask my brother to assist I

 " sink into a country parson ! " 

 " and learn content . I have taste it already . she be then by my side . 
 explain all to she . this letter , I fear , be too unkind - but to doubt I

 Levy hastily place the letter in his pocketbook ; and , for fear it

 and of that letter he make such use , that the day after he have give

 " and a challenge from Lord L'Estrange . "

 meanwhile , the miserable Nora - deceive by the art and representation

 to this italian lady she now go , and the offer be renew with the

 all this time the poor girl be under a moral delirium , a confused

 physiologist agree that madness be rarest amongst person of the fine 
 imagination . but those person be , of all other , liable to a temporary

 and one morning this paragraph meet her eye : - 

  the Earl and Countess of Lansmere be receive a distinguished 
  party at their country seat . among the guest be Miss Leslie , whose

 again the anchor snap , again the storm descend , again the star
