 CHAPTER XVII . 

 when the scene in some long diorama pass solemnly before we , there be 
 sometimes one solitary object , contrasting , perhaps , the view of stately 
 city or the march of a mighty river , that halt on the eye for 
 a moment , and then glide away , leave on the mind a strange , 
 comfortless , undefined impression . 

 why be the object present to we ? in itself it seem comparatively 
 insignificant . it may have be but a broken column , a lonely pool with 
 a star - beam on its quiet surface , - yet it awe we . we remember it when 
 phantasmal picture of bright Damascus , or of colossal pyramid , of 
 bazaar in Stamboul , or lengthen caravan that defile slow amidst the 
 sand of Araby , have sate the wonder gaze . why be we detain in 
 the shadowy procession by a thing that would have be so commonplace 
 have it not be so lone ? some latent interest must attach to it . be it 
 there that a vision of woe have lift the wild hair of a Prophet ; there 
 where some Hagar have still the wail of her child on her indignant 
 breast ? we would fain call back the pageantry procession , fain see again 
 the solitary thing that seem so little worth the hand of the artist , 
 and ask , " why art thou here , and wherefore dost thou haunt we ? " 

 rise up , - rise up once more , by the broad great thoroughfare that 
 stretch onward and onward to the remorseless London ! rise up , rise up , 
 o solitary tree with the green leaf on thy bough , and the deep rent 
 in thy heart ; and the raven , dark bird of oman and sorrow , that build 
 their nest amidst the leaf of the bough , and drop with noiseless 
 plume down through the hollow rent of the heart , or be hear , it may 
 be in the grow shadow of twilight , call out to their young . 

 under the old pollard - tree , by the side of John Avenel 's house , there 
 cower , breathless and listening , John Avenel 's daughter Nora . now , 
 when that fatal newspaper paragraph , which lie so like truth , meet her 
 eye , she obey the first impulse of her passionate heart , - she tear 
 the wedding ring from her finger , she enclose it , with the paragraph 
 itself , in a letter to Audley , - a letter that she design to convey 
 scorn and pride - alas ! it express only jealousy and love . she could 
 not rest till she have put this letter into the post with her own hand , 
 address to , Audley at Lord Lansmere 's . Scarce have it leave she ere she 
 repent . what have she do , - resign the birth - right of the child 
 she be so soon to bring into the world , resign her last hope in her 
 lover 's honour , give up her life of life - and from belief in what ? - a 
 report in a newspaper ! no , no ; she would go herself to Lansmere ; to 
 her father 's home , - she could contrive to see Audley before that letter 
 reach his hand . the thought be scarcely conceive before obey . 
 she find a vacant place in a coach that start from London some hour 
 before the mail , and go within a few mile of Lansmere ; those last 
 mile she travel on foot . exhaust , fainting , she gain at last the 
 sight of home , and there halt , for in the little garden in front 
 she see her parent seat . she hear the murmur of their voice , and 
 suddenly she remember her altered shape , her terrible secret . how 
 answer the question , 

 " Daughter , where and who be thy husband ? " her heart fail she ; she 
 creep under the old pollard - tree , to gather up resolve , to watch , and to 
 listen . she see the rigid face of the thrifty , prudent mother , with the 
 deep line that tell of the care of an anxious life , and the chafe of 
 excitable temper and warm affection against the restraint of decorous 
 sanctimony and resolute pride . the dear stern face never seem to 
 she more dear and more stern . she see the comely , easy , indolent , 
 good - humour father ; not then the poor , paralytic sufferer , who could 
 yet recognize Nora 's eye under the lid of Leonard , but stalwart and 
 jovial , - first bat in the Cricket Club , first voice in the Glee Society , 
 the most popular canvasser of the Lansmere Constitutional True Blue 
 Party , and the pride and idol of the calvinistical prim wife ; never from 
 those pinch lip of hers have come forth even one pious rebuke to 
 the careless , social man . as he sit , one hand in his vest , his profile 
 turn to the road , the light smoke curl playfully up from the pipe , 
 over which lip , accustom to bland smile and hearty laughter , close 
 as if reluctant to be close at all , he be the very model of the 
 respectable retire trader in easy circumstance , and release from the 
 toil of make money while life could yet enjoy the delight of spend 
 it . 

 " well , old woman , " say John Avenel , " I must be off presently to see to 
 those three shaky voter in Fish Lane ; they will have do their work 
 soon , and I shall catch them at home . they do say as how we may have an 
 opposition ; and I know that old Smikes have go to Lonnon in search of 
 a candidate . we can not   have the Lansmere Constitutional Blues beat by a 
 Lonnoner ! ha , ha , ha ! " 

 " but you will be home before Jane and her husband Mark come ? how ever 
 she could marry a common carpenter ! " 

 " yes , " say John , " he be a carpenter ; but he have a vote , and that 
 strengthen the family interest . if Dick be not go to Amerikay , there 
 would be three on we . but Mark be a real good Blue ! a Lonnoner , indeed ! 
 a Yellow from Lonnon beat my Lord and the Blues ! ha , ha ! " 

 " but , John , this Mr. Egerton be a Lonnoner ! " 

 " you do not   understand thing , talk such nonsense . Mr. Egerton be the 
 blue candidate , and the blue be the Country Party ; therefore how can 
 he be a Lonnoner ? an uncommon clever , well - grow , handsome young man , 
 eh ! and my young Lord 's particular friend . " 

 Mrs. Avenel sigh . 

 " what be you sigh and shake your head for ? " 

 " I be think of our poor , dear , dear Nora ! " 

 " God bless she ! " cry John , heartily . 

 there be a rustle under the bough of the old hollow - hearte 
 pollard - tree . 

 " ha , ha ! Hark ! I say that so loud that I have startle the raven ! " 

 " how he do love she ! " say Mrs. Avenel , thoughtfully . " I be sure he 
 do ; and no wonder , for she look every inch a lady ; and why should not

 " he ? who ? oh , that foolish fancy of yours about my young Lord ? a prudent 
 woman like you ! - stuff ! I be glad my little beauty be go to Lonnon , 
 out of harm 's way . " 

 " John , John , John ! no harm could ever come to my Nora . she be too pure 
 and too good , and have too proper a pride in she , to - "

 election ; and just as I be go , she whisper , ' do not   let my wild boy

 pollard - tree - no house near it then ! "

 " oh , they say as soon as the election be over . a fine wedding we shall 
 have of it ! I dare say my young Lord will be bridesman . we will send for

 out from the bough of the old tree come the shriek of a lost 
 spirit , - one of those strange , appalling sound of human agony which , 
 once hear , be never forget . it be as the wail of Hope , when SHE , 
 too , rush forth from the Coffer of Woes , and vanish into viewless

 that would wrench itself from life ! for a moment all be still - and then 
 a dull , dumb , heavy fall ! 

 the parent gaze on each other , speechless : they steal close to the 
 pale , and look over . under the bough , at the gnarl root of the

 gate , and go round ; the mother creep to the road - side , and there stand 
 still . 

 " oh , wife , wife ! " cry John Avenel , from under the green bough , " it be 
 our child Nora ! our child ! our child ! " 

 and , as he speak , out from the green bough start the dark raven ,

 and when they have lay she on the bed , Mrs. Avenel whisper John to 
 withdraw for a moment ; and with set lip but tremble hand begin

 " John , run for Mr. Morgan , - make haste . but mind - do not   speak to any one 
 on the way . quick , quick ! " 

 " be she die ? " 

 " I do not   know . why not die before ? " say Mrs. Avenel , between her tooth ; 
 " but Mr. Morgan be a discreet , friendly man . "

 an hour or two later , a little , cover , tax cart stop at Mr. 
 Avenel 's cottage , out of which step a young man with pale face and 
 spare form , dress in the Sunday suit of a rustic craftsman ; then a

 and home - make bread . 

 " do not   forget the flower that the squire 's gardener give we , " say Mark 
 the Poet .

 can not   ha ' go to bed yet . " 

 Mark knock at the door - no answer . a light pass rapidly across the 
 window on the upper floor , but still no one come to his summon . Mark

 Lansinere Park , on the opposite side of the road , pause at the sound of 
 Mark 's second and more impatient knock , and say civilly ,

 he expect to visit he ? " 

 " yes , please , Mr. Dale , " say Mrs. Fairfield , drop her courtesy . 
 " you remember I ! and this be my dear good man ! " 

 " what ! mark the Poet ? " say the curate of Lansmere , with a smile . " come 
 to write squib for the election ? " 

 " Squibs , sir ! " cry Mark , indignantly . 

 " Burns write squibs , " say the curate , mildly .

 this time , a man , whose face , even see by the starlight , be much 
 flushed , present himself at the threshold . 

 " Mr. Morgan ! " exclaim the curate , in benevolent alarm ; " no illness 
 here , I hope ? " 

 " Cott ! it be you , Mr. Dale ! - come in , come in ; I want a word with you . 
 but who the teuce be these people ? " 

 " Sir , " say Mark , push through the doorway , " my name be Fairfield , 
 and my wife be Mr. Avenel 's daughter ! " 

 " oh , Jane - and her baby too ! - Cood ! cood ! come in ; but be quiet , can not 
 you ? still , still - still as death ! " 

 the party enter , the door close ; the moon rise , and shine calmly on 
 the pale silent house , on the sleep flower of the little garden , on 
 the old pollard with its hollow core . the horse in the tax cart doze 
 unheeded ; the light still at time flit across the upper window . 
 these be the only sign of life , except when a bat , now and then 
 attract by the light that pass across the window , brush against

 round the raven 's nest in the old pollard . 




 CHAPTER XVIII . 

 all that day Harley L'Estrange have be more than usually mournful and 
 deject . indeed , the return to scene associate with Nora 's presence 
 increase the gloom that have settle on his mind since he have lose sight 
 and trace of she . Audley , in the remorseful tenderness he feel for his 
 injure friend , have induce L'Estrange towards evening to leave the 
 Park , and go into a district some mile off , on pretence that he 
 require Harley 's aid there to canvass certain important outvoter : the 
 change of scene might rouse he from his revery . Harley himself be 
 glad to escape from the guest at Lansmere . he readily consent to go . 
 he would not return that night . the outvoter lie remote and scatter , 
 he might be absent for a day or two . when Harley be go , Egerton 
 himself sink into deep thought . there be rumour of some unexpected 
 opposition . his partisan be alarmed and anxious . it be clear that 
 the Lansmere interest , if attack , be weak than the earl would 
 believe ; Egerton might lose his election . if so , what would become of 
 he ? how support his wife , whose return to he he always count on , and 
 whom it would then become he at all hazard to acknowledge ? it be 
 that day that he have speak to William Hazeldean as to the family 
 living . - " Peace , at least , " think the ambitious man , - " I shall have 
 peace ! " and the squire have promise he the rectory if need ; not 
 without a secret pang , for his Harry be already use her conjugal 
 influence in favour of her old school - friend 's husband , Mr. Dale ; 
 and the squire think Audley would be but a poor country parson , and 
 Dale - if he would only grow a little plumper than his curacy would 
 permit he to be - would be a parson in ten thousand . but while Audley 
 thus prepare for the bad , he still bring his energy to bear on 
 the more brilliant option ; and sit with his committee , look into 
 canvass - book , and discuss the character , politic , and local 
 interest of every elector , until the night be well - nigh go . when he 
 gain his room ; the shutter be unclosed , and he stand a few moment 
 at the window , gaze on the moon . at that sight , the thought of Nora , 
 lose and afar , steal over he . the man , as we know , have in his nature 
 little of romance and sentiment . seldom be it his will not to gaze upon 
 moon or star . but whenever some whisper of romance do soften his hard , 
 strong mind , or whenever moon or star do charm his gaze from earth , 
 Nora 's bright muse - like face , Nora 's sweet love eye , be see in 
 moon and star - beam , Nora 's low tender voice hear in the whisper of 
 that which we call romance , and which be but the sound of the mysterious 
 poetry that be ever in the air , would we but deign to hear it ! he turn 
 with a sigh , undressed , throw himself on his bed , and extinguish his 
 light . but the light of the moon would fill the room . it keep he awake 
 for a little time ; he turn his face from the calm , heavenly beam 
 resolutely towards the dull blind wall , and fall asleep . and , in the 
 sleep , he be with Nora , - again in the humble bridal - home . never in 
 his dream have she seem to he so distinct and life - like , - her 
 eye upturned to his , her hand clasp together , and rest on his 
 shoulder , as have be her graceful will not , her voice murmur meekly , 
 " have it , then , be my fault that we part ? forgive , forgive I ! " 
 and the sleeper imagine that he answer , " never part from I 
 again , - never , never ! " and that he bend down to kiss the chaste lip 
 that so tenderly seek his own . and suddenly he hear a knock 
 sound , as of a hammer , - regular , but soft , low , subdue . do you ever , 
 o reader , hear the sound of the hammer on the lid of a coffin in a house 
 of woe , - when the undertaker 's decorous hireling fear that the living 
 may hear how he part they from the dead ? such seem the sound to 
 Audley . the dream vanish abruptly . 

 he wake , and again hear the knock ; it be at his door . he sit up 
 wistfully ; the moon be go , it be morning . " who be there ? " he cry 
 peevishly . 

 a low voice from without answer , " hush , it be I ; dress quick ; let I 
 see you . " 

 Egerton recognize Lady Lansmere 's voice . alarmed and surprised , he 
 rise , dress in haste , and go to the door . Lady Lansmere be stand 
 without , extremely pale . she put her finger to her lip , and beckon he 
 to follow she . he obey mechanically . they enter her dressing - room , a 
 few door from his own chamber , and the countess close the door . 

 then lay her slight firm hand on his shoulder , she say , in 
 suppress and passionate excitement , 

 " oh , Mr. Egerton , you must serve I , and at once . Harley ! Harley ! save 
 my Harley ! go to he , prevent his come back here , stay with he ; give 
 up the election , - it be but a year or two lose in your life , you will 
 have other opportunity ; make that sacrifice to your friend . " 

 " speak - what be the matter ? I can make no sacrifice too great for 
 Harley ! " 

 " thank , I be sure of it . go then , I say , at once to Harley ; keep he 
 away from Lansmere on any excuse you can invent , until you can break the 
 sad news to he , - gently , gently . oh , how will he bear it ; how recover 
 the shock ? my boy , my boy ! " 

 " calm yourself ! explain ! break what news ; recover what shock ? " 

 " true ; you do not know , you have not hear . Nora Avenel lie yonder , in 
 her father 's house , - dead , dead ! " 

 Audley stagger back , clap his hand to his heart , and then drop 
 on his knee as if bow down by the stroke of heaven . 

 " my bride , my wife ! " he mutter . " Dead - it can not be ! " 

 Lady Lansmere be so startled at this exclamation , so stunned by a 
 confession wholly unexpected , that she remain unable to soothe , to 
 explain , and utterly unprepared for the fierce agony that burst from the 
 man she have ever see so dignified and cold , when he spring to his foot , 
 and all the sense of his eternal loss rush upon his heart . 

 at length he crush back his emotion , and listen in apparent 
 calm , and in a silence break but by quick gasp for breath , to Lady 
 Lansmere 's account . 

 one of the guest in the house , a female relation of Lady Lansmere 's ,

 be disturb , the countess herself arouse , and Mr. Morgan summon 
 as the family medical practitioner . from he she have learn that Nora

 Audley listen , and turn to the door , still in silence . Lady Lansmere 
 catch he by the arm . " where be you go ? ah , can I now ask you

 Egerton go forth , stunned and dizzy . mechanically he take his way 
 across the park to John Avenel 's house . he have be force to enter that

 too late , too late ! the dew be glisten in the sun , the bird be

 like a charnel - house . nothing but death and the dead there , - nothing ! he

 seem in a heavy sleep . in fact , paralysis have smite he ; but he know

 himself , by the invisible icy hand ! Audley steal to the bedside ; he

 youth , all the sweet hope and joy of the household human life , for

 he return to Lady Lansmere , who await his come with the most

 " now , " say he , dryly , " I will go to Harley , and I will prevent his

 " you have see the parent . good heaven ! do they know of your

 " no ; to Harley I must own it first . meanwhile , silence ! "

 Audley 's , and Audley 's hand be as ice . 

 in another hour Egerton have leave the house , and before noon he be with 
 Harley . 

 it be necessary now to explain the absence of all the Avenel family , 
 except the poor stricken father . 

 Nora have die in give birth to a child , - die delirious . in her 
 delirium she have speak of shame , of disgrace ; there be no holy nuptial 
 ring on her finger . through all her grief , the first thought of Mrs. 
 Avenel be to save the good name of her lose daughter , the unblemished 
 honour of all the live Avenels . no matron long descend from 
 knight or king have keener pride in name and character than the poor , 
 punctilious calvinistic trader 's wife . " sorrow later , honour now ! " with 
 hard dry eye she muse and muse , and make out her plan . Jane Fairfield 
 should take away the infant at once , before the day dawn , and nurse 
 it with her own . Mark should go with she , for Mrs. Avenel dread the 
 indiscretion of his wild grief . she would go with they herself part of

 they could not go back to Hazeldean with another infant ; Jane must 
 go where none know she ; the two infant might pass as twin . and Mrs. 
 Avenel , though naturally a humane , kindly woman , and with a mother 's 
 heart to infant , look with almost a glad sternness at Jane 's puny 
 babe , and think to herself , " all difficulty would be over should there 
 be only one ! Nora 's child could thus pass throughout life for Jane 's ! " 

 fortunately for the preservation of the secret , the Avenels keep no 
 servant , - only an occasional drudge , who come a few hour in the day , 
 and go home to sleep . Mrs. Avenel could count on Mr. Morgan 's silence 
 as to the true cause of Nora 's death . and Mr. Dale , why should be reveal 
 the dishonour of a family ? that very day , or the next at furth , she 
 could induce her husband to absent himself , lest he should blab out the 
 tale while his sorrow be great than his pride . she alone would then 
 stay in the house of death until she could feel assured that all else 
 be hush into prudence . ay , she feel , that with due precaution , the 
 name be still safe . and so she awe and hurry Mark and his wife away ,

 charge , with many an admonition , to which he nod his head , and which

 have Nora look from heaven into her mother 's heart Nora would not have 
 think so . a good name when the burial stone close over dust be still 
 a possession upon the earth ; on earth it be indeed our only one ! well

 over perishable clay . and weep ! - oh , stern mother , long year be leave 
 to thee for weeping ! no tear shed for Nora make such deep furrow on

 Harley be in great surprise to see Egerton ; more surprised when Egerton

 election be wondrous thing ! no man who have not see can guess how the

 there be forward to Audley , from Lansmere Park , Nora 's last letter .

 " Sir ! "

 and excitement through which she have go dry the source of nutriment 
 in her breast . she put Nora 's child out to nurse at the house of a small 
 farmer , at a little distance from the village , and move from her first 
 lodge to be near to the infant . her own child be so sickly and 
 ailing , that she could not bear to intrust it to the care of an other . 
 she try to bring it up by hand ; and the poor child soon pin away 
 and die . she and Mark could not endure the sight of their baby 's grave ; 
 they hasten to return to Hazeldean , and take Leonard with they . from 
 that time Leonard pass for the son they have lose . 

 when Egerton arrive at the village , and inquire for the person whose 
 address have be give to he , he be refer to the cottage in which 
 she have last lodge , and be tell that she have be go some day , - the 
 day after her child be bury . her child bury ! Egerton stay to 
 inquire no more ; thus he hear nothing of the infant that have be put 
 out to nurse . he walk slowly into the churchyard , and stand for some 
 minute gaze on the small new mound ; then , press his hand on the 
 heart to which all emotion have be forbid , he re - enter his chaise 
 and return to London . the sole reason for acknowledge his marriage 
 seem to he now remove . Nora 's name have escape reproach . even 
 have his painful position with regard to Harley not constrain he to 
 preserve his secret , there be every motive to the world 's wise and 
 haughty son not to acknowledge a derogatory and foolish marriage , now 
 that none live whom concealment could wrong . 

 Audley mechanically resume his former life , - seek to resettle his 
 thought on the grand object of ambitious man . his poverty still 
 press on he ; his pecuniary debt to Harley sting and gall his 
 peculiar sense of honour . he see no way to clear his estate , to repay 
 his friend , but by some rich alliance . dead to love , he face this 
 prospect first with repugnance , then with apathetic indifference . Levy , 
 of whose treachery towards himself and Nora he be unaware , still hold 
 over he the power that the money - lender never lose over the man that 
 have owe , owe , or may owe again . Levy be ever urge he to propose , 
 to the rich Miss Leslie ; Lady Lansmere , willing to atone , as she 
 think , for his domestic loss , urge the same ; Harley , influence by 
 his mother , write from the continent to the same effect . 

 " manage it as you will , " at last say Egerton to Levy , ' so that I be not 
 a wife 's pensioner . " 

 " propose for I , if you will , " he say to Lady Lansmere , - " I can not 
 woo , - I can not talk of love . " 

 somehow or other the marriage , with all its rich advantage to the 
 ruin gentleman , be thus make up . and Egerton , as we have see , be 
 the polite and dignified husband before the world , - marry to a woman 
 who adore he . it be the common fate of man like he to be love too 
 well ! 

 on her death - bed his heart be touch by his wife 's melancholy 
 reproach , - " nothing I could do have ever make you love I ! " 

 " it be true , " answer Audley , with tear in his voice and eye ; " nature 
 give I but a small fund of what woman like you call ' love , ' and I 
 lavish it all away . " and he then tell she , though with reserve , some 
 portion of his former history ; and that soothe she ; for when she see 
 that he have love , and could grieve , she catch a glimpse of the human 
 heart she have not see before . she die , forgive he , and blessing . 

 Audley 's spirit be much affect by this new loss . he inly resolve 
 never to marry again . he have a vague thought at first of retrench his 
 expenditure , and make young Randal Leslie his heir . but when he first 
 see the clever Eton boy , his feeling do not warm to he , though his 
 intellect appreciate Randal 's quick , keen talent . he content himself 
 with resolve to push the boy , - to do what be merely just to the 
 distant kinsman of his late wife . always careless and lavish in money 
 matter , generous and princely , not from the delight of serve other , 
 but from a grand seigneur 's sentiment of what be due to himself and his 
 station , Audley have a mournful excuse for the lordly waste of the large 
 fortune at his control . the morbid function of the heart have become 
 organic disease . true , he might live many year , and die at last of some 
 other complaint in the course of nature ; but the progress of the disease 
 would quicken with all emotional excitement ; he might die suddenly - any 
 day - in the very prime , and , seemingly , in the full vigour , of his 
 life . and the only physician in whom he confide what he wish to 
 keep conceal from the world   for ambitious man would fain be think 
 immortal   tell he frankly that it be improbable that , with the wear 
 and tear of political strife and action , he could advance far into 
 middle age . therefore , no son of his succeed - his near relation 
 all wealthy - Egerton resign himself to his constitutional disdain 
 of money ; he could look into no affair , provide the balance in his 
 banker 's hand be such as become the munificent commoner . all else 
 he leave to his steward and to Levy . Levy grow rapidly rich , - very , very 
 rich , - and the steward thrive . 

 the usurer continue to possess a determined hold over the imperious 
 great man . he know Audley 's secret ; he could reveal that secret to 
 Harley . and the one soft and tender side of the statesman 's nature - the 
 sole part of he not dip in the ninefold Styx of practical prosaic 
 life , which render man so invulnerable to affection - be his remorseful 
 love for the school friend whom he still deceive . 

 here then you have the key to the locked chamber of Audley Egerton 's 
 character , the fortified castle of his mind . the envy minister , the 
 joyless man ; the oracle on the economy of an empire , the prodigal in 
 a usurer 's hand ; the august , high - crest gentleman , to whom prince 
 would refer for the casuistry of honour , the culprit tremble lest the 
 friend he well love on earth should detect his lie ! wrap thyself in the 
 decent veil that the Arts or the Graces weave for thee , o Human Nature ! 
 it be only the statue of marble whose nakedness the eye can behold 
 without shame and offence !

 of the narrative just place before the reader , it be clear that

 CHAPTER XX . 

 Harley L'Estrange be seat alone in his apartment . he have just put 
 down a volume of some favourite classic author , and he be rest 
 his hand firmly clench upon the book . ever since Harley 's return to 
 England , there have be a perceptible change in the expression of his 
 countenance , even in the very bearing and attitude of his elastic 
 youthful figure . but this change have be more marked since that last 
 interview with Helen which have be record . there be a compress , 
 resolute firmness in the lip , a decide character in the brow . to 
 the indolent , careless grace of his movement have succeed a certain 
 indescribable energy , as quiet and self - collect as that which 
 distinguish the determined air of Audley Egerton himself . in fact , if 
 you could have look into his heart , you would have see that Harley 
 be , for the first time , make a strong effort over his passion and 
 his humour ; that the whole man be nerve himself to a sense of duty . 
 " no , " he mutter , - " no ! I will think only of Helen ; I will think 
 only of real life ! and what   be I not engage to another   would that 
 dark - eyed italian girl be to I ? - what a mere fool 's fancy be this ! I 
 love again , - I , who through all the fair spring of my life have clung 
 with such faith to a memory and a grave ! come , come , come , Harley 
 L'Estrange , act thy part as man amongst man , at last ! accept regard ; 
 dream no more of passion . abandon false ideal . thou art no poet - why 
 deem that life itself can be a poem ? " 

 the door open , and the austrian prince , whom Harley have interest 
 in the cause of Violante 's father , enter , with the familiar step of a 
 friend . 

 " have you discover those document yet ? " say the prince . " I must now 
 return to Vienna within a few day ; and unless you can arm I with 
 some tangible proof of Peschiera 's ancient treachery , or some more 
 unanswerable excuse for his noble kinsman , I fear that there be no other 
 hope for the exile 's recall to his country than what lie in the hateful 
 option of give his daughter to his perfidious foe . " 

 " alas ! " say Harley , " as yet all research have be in vain ; and 
 I know not what other step to take , without arouse Peschiera 's 
 vigilance , and set his crafty brain at work to counteract we . my 
 poor friend , then , must rest content with exile . to give Violante to 
 the count be dishonour . but I shall soon be marry ; soon have a home , 
 not quite unworthy of their due rank , to offer both to father and to 
 child . " 

 " would the future Lady L'Estrange feel no jealousy of a guest so fair 
 as you tell I this young signorina be ? and would you be in no danger 
 yourself , my poor friend ? " 

 " Pooh ! " say Harley , colour . " my fair guest would have two father ; 
 that be all . pray do not jest on a thing so grave as honour . " 

 again the door open , and Leonard appear .

 prince 's penetrate eye , - " welcome . this be the noble friend who share

 design ; ' his entreaty to she to induce her husband to share they .

 woo ; look how artfully he combat her objection ; see how reluctant 
 our friend be to stir , till wife and kinsman both unite to urge he ! " 

 " it be enough,-quite enough , " exclaim the prince , look at the 
 passage in Peschiera 's letter which Harley point out to he . 

 " no , it be not enough , " shout Harley , as he continue to read the 
 letter with his rapid sparkling eye . " more still ! o villain , doubly

 scorn he before she die ! she be innocent ! I say so . Violante 's

 " I know enough of our emperor , " answer the prince , warmly , " to know

 whom you would have give a child 's place at your hearth , the wealthy 
 heiress of Italy , - the bride of some noble lover , with rank only below

 he take forth Nora 's manuscript as he speak .

 BOOK TWELFTH . 




 INITIAL chapter . 

 wherein the CAXTON FAMILY reappear . 

 " again , " quoth my father , - " again behold we ! we who greet the 
 commencement of your narrative , who absent ourselves in the midcourse 
 when we could but obstruct the current of event , and jostle personage 
 more important , - we now gather round the close . still , as the chorus to 
 the drama , we circle round the altar with the solemn but dubious 
 chant which prepare the audience for the completion of the appoint 
 destiny ; though still , ourselves , unaware how the skein be to be 
 unravel , and where the shear be to descend . " 

 so there they stand , the Family of Caxton , - all group round I , 
 all eager officiously to question , some over - anxious prematurely to 
 criticise . 

 " Violante can not   have voluntarily go off with that horrid count , " say 
 my mother ; " but perhaps she be deceive , like Eugenia by Mr. Bellamy , 
 in the novel of ' CAMILLA ' . " 

 " ha ! " say my father , " and in that case it be time yet to steal a hint 
 from Clarissa Harlowe , and make Violante die less of a broken heart than 
 a sullied honour . she be one of those girl who ought to be kill ! all 
 thing about her forebode an early tomb ! " 

 " dear , dear ! " cry Mrs. Caxton , " I hope not ! " 

 " Pooh , brother , " say the captain , " we have have enough of the tomb in 
 the history of poor Nora . the whole story grow out of a grave , and if 
 to a grave it must return - if , Pisistratus , you must kill somebody - kill 
 Levy . " 

 " or the count , " say my mother , with unusual truculence . " or randal 
 Leslie , " say Squills . " I should like to have a post - mortem cast of his 
 head , - it would be an instructive study . " 

 here there be a general confusion of tongue , all present conspiring 
 to bewilder the unfortunate author with their various and discordant 
 counsel how to wind up his story and dispose of his character . 

 " Silence ! " cry Pisistratus , clap his hand to both ear . " I can no 
 more alter the fate allot to each of the personage whom you honour 
 with your interest than I can change your own ; like you , they must 
 go where event lead there , urge on by their own character and the 
 agency of other . Providence so pervadingly govern the universe , 
 that you can not strike it even out of a book . the author may beget a 
 character , but the moment the character come into action , it escape 
 from his hand , - play its own part , and fulfil its own inevitable 
 doom . " 

 " besides , " say Squills , " it be easy to see , from the phrenological 
 development of the organ in those several head which Pisistratus have 
 allow we to examine , that we have see no creation of mere fiction , 
 but live person , whose true history have set in movement their various 
 bump of Amativeness , Constructiveness , Acquisitiveness , Idealty , 
 Wonder , Comparison , etc . they must act , and they must end , accord 
 to the influence of their crania . thus we find in Randal Leslie the 
 predominant organ of Constructiveness , Secretiveness , Comparison , and 
 Eventuality , while Benevolence , Conscientiousness , Adhesiveness , be 
 utterly nil . now , to divine how such a man must end , we must first see 
 what be the general composition of the society in which he move , in 
 short , what other gas be bring into contact with his phlogiston . 
 as to Leonard , and Harley , and Audley Egerton , survey they 
 phrenologically , I should say that - " 

 " hush ! " say my father , " Pisistratus have dip his pen in the ink , and 
 it seem to I easy for the wise man that ever live to account for 
 what other have do than to predict what they should do . phrenologist 
 discover that Mr. Thurtell have a very fine organ of Conscientiousness ; 
 yet , somehow or other , that err personage contrive to knock the 
 brain out of his friend 's organ of Individuality . therefore I rise to 
 propose a Resolution , - that this meeting be adjourn till Pisistratus 
 have complete his narrative ; and we shall then have the satisfaction of 
 know that it ought , accord to every principle of nature , science , 
 and art , to have be complete differently . why should we deprive 
 ourselves of that pleasure ? " 

 " I second the motion , " say the captain ; " but if Levy be not hang , I 
 shall say that there be an end of all poetical justice . " 

 " take care of poor Helen , " say Blanche , tenderly : " nor , that I would 
 have you forget Violante . " 

 " Pish ! and sit down , or they shall both die old maid . " frighten at 
 that threat , Blanche , with a deprecating look , draw her stool quietly 
 near I , as if to place she two protege in an atmosphere mesmerize to 
 matrimonial attraction ; and my mother set hard to work - at a new frock 
 for the baby . unsoftene by these undue female influence , Pisistratus 
 write on at the dictation of the relentless Fates . his pen be of iron , 
 and his heart be of granite . he be as insensible to the existence of 
 wife and baby as if he have never pay a house bill , nor rush from 
 a nursery at the sound of an infant squall . o blessed privilege of 
 Authorship ! 

  " o testudinis aureae 
  Dulcem quae strepitum , Pieri , tempera ! 
  o mutis quoque piscibus 
  Donatura cyeni , si libeat , sonum ! " 

   " o Muse , who dost temper the sweet sound of the golden shell of the 
  tortoise , and couldst also give , be it need , to silent fish 
  the song of the swan . " 




 CHAPTER II . 

 it be necessary to go somewhat back in the course of this narrative , and 
 account to the reader for the disappearance of Violante . 

 it may be remember that Peschiera , scar by the sudden approach of 
 Lord L'Estrange , have little time for further word to the young Italian , 
 than those which express his intention to renew the conference , and 
 press for her decision . but the next day , when he re - enter the garden , 
 secretly and stealthily , as before , Violante do not appear . and after 
 watch round the precinct till dusk , the count retreat , with an 
 indignant conviction that his art have fail to enlist on his side 
 either the heart or the imagination of his intended victim . he begin now 
 to revolve and to discuss with Levy the possibility of one of those 
 bold and violent measure , which be favour by his reckless daring 
 and desperate condition . but Levy treat with such just ridicule any 
 suggestion to abstract Violante by force from Lord Lansmere 's house , so 
 scout the notion of nocturnal assault , with the device of scale 
 window and rope - ladder , that the count reluctantly abandon that 
 romance of villany so unsuited to our sober capital , and which would no 
 doubt have terminate in his capture by the police , with the prospect of 
 committal to the House of Correction . 

 Levy himself find his invention at fault , and Randal Leslie be call 
 into consultation . the usurer have contrive that Randal 's scheme of 
 fortune and advancement be so base upon Levy 's aid and connivance , 
 that the young man , with all his desire rather to make instrument 
 of other man , than to be himself their instrument , find his superior 
 intellect as completely a slave to Levy 's more experienced craft , as 
 ever subtle Genius of air be subject to the vulgar Sorcerer of earth . 

 his acquisition of the ancestral acre , his anticipated seat in 
 parliament , his chance of oust Frank from the heritage of Hazeldean , 
 be all as string that pull he to and fro , like a puppet in the 
 sleek , filbert - nail finger of the smile showman , who could exhibit 
 he to the admiration of a crowd , or cast he away into dust and lumber . 

 Randal gnaw his lip in the sullen wrath of a man who bid his hour 
 of future emancipation , and lend his brain to the hire of the present 
 servitude , in mechanical acquiescence . the inherent superiority of the 
 profound young schemer become instantly apparent over the courage of 
 Peschiera and the practised wit of the baron . 

 " your sister , " say Randal , to the former , " must be the active agent in 
 the first and most difficult part of your enterprise . Violante can not 
 be take by force from Lord Lansmere 's , - she must be induce to leave 
 it with her own consent . a female be need here . woman can good decoy 
 woman . " 

 " admirably say , " quoth the count ; " but Beatrice have grow restive , and 
 though her dowry , and therefore her very marriage with that excellent 
 young Hazeldean , depend on my own alliance with my fair kinswoman , she 
 have grow so indifferent to my success that I dare not reckon on her 
 aid . between you and I , though she be once very eager to be marry , 
 she now seem to shrink from the notion ; and I have no other hold over 
 she . " 

 " have she not see some one , and lately , whom she prefer to poor Frank ? " 

 " I suspect that she have ; but I know not whom , unless it be that detest 
 L'Estrange . " 

 " ah , well , well . interfere with she no far yourself , but have all 
 in readiness to quit England , as you have before propose , as soon as 
 Violante be in your power . " 

 " all be in readiness , " say the count . " Levy have agree to purchase a 
 famous sailing - vessel of one of his client . I have engage a score or 
 so of determined outcast , accustom to the sea , - Genoese , Corsicans , 
 Sardinians , ex - Carbonari of the good sort , - no silly patriot , but 
 liberal cosmopolitan , who have iron at the disposal of any man 's gold . 
 I have a priest to perform the nuptial service , and deaf to any fair 
 lady 's ' no . ' once at sea , and wherever I land , Violante will lean on my 
 arm as Countess of Peschiera . " 

 " but Violante , " say Randal , doggedly , determine not to yield to the 
 disgust with which the count 's audacious cynicism fill even he - " but 
 Violante can not be remove in broad daylight at once to such a vessel , 
 nor from a quarter so populous as that in which your sister reside . " 

 " I have think of that too , " say the count ; " my emissary have find 
 I a house close by the river , and safe for our purpose as the dungeon 
 of Venice . " 

 " I wish not to know all this , " answer Randal , quickly ; " you will 
 instruct Madame di Negra where to take Violante . - my task limit itself 
 to the fair invention that belong to intellect ; what belong to force 
 be not in my province . I will go at once to your sister , whom I think I 
 can influence more effectually than you can ; though later I may give 
 you a hint to guard against the chance of her remorse . meanwhile as , the 
 moment Violante disappear , suspicion would fall upon you , show yourself 
 constantly in public surround by your friend . be able to account for 
 every hour of your time - " 

 " an alibi ? " interrupt the ci - devant solicitor . 

 " exactly so , Baron . complete the purchase of the vessel , and let the 
 count man it as he propose . I will communicate with you both as soon 
 as I can put you into action . to - day I shall have much to do ; it will be 
 do . " 

 as Randal leave the room , Levy follow he . 

 " what you propose to do will be well do , no doubt , " quoth the usurer , 
 link his arm in Randal 's ; " but take care that you do not   get yourself 
 into a scrape , so as to damage your character . I have great hope of you 
 in public life ; and in public life character be necessary , - that be , so 
 far as honour be concern . " 

 " I damage my character ! - and for a Count Peschiera ! " say Randal , 
 open his eye . " I ! what do you take I for ? " 

 the baron let go his hold . 

 " this boy ought to rise very high , " say he to himself , as he turn 
 back to the count . 




 CHAPTER III . 

 Randal 's acute faculty of comprehension have long since surmise the 
 truth that Beatrice 's view and temper of mind have be strangely and 
 suddenly alter by some such revolution as passion only can effect ; 
 that pique or disappointment have mingle with the motive which have 
 induce she to accept the hand of his rash young kinsman ; and that , 
 instead of the resigned indifference with which she might at one time 
 have contemplate any marriage that could free she from a position that 
 perpetually gall her pride , it be now with a repugnance , visible to 
 Randal 's keen eye , that she shrink from the performance of that pledge 
 which Frank have so dearly buy . the temptation which the count could 
 hold out to she to become his accomplice in design of which the fraud 
 and perfidy would revolt her well nature have cease to be of avail . a 
 dowry have grow valueless , since it would but hasten the nuptial from 
 which she recoil . Randal feel that he could not secure her aid , except 
 by work on a passion so turbulent as to confound her judgment . such 
 a passion he recognize in jealousy . he have once doubt if Harley be 
 the object of her love ; yet , after all , be it not probable ? he know , 
 at least , of no one else to suspect . if so , he have but to whisper , 
 " Violante be your rival . Violante remove , your beauty may find its 
 natural effect ; if not , you be an italian , and you will be at least 
 avenge . " he see still more reason to suppose that Lord L'Estrange be 
 indeed the one by whom he could rule Beatrice , since , the last time 
 he have see she , she have question he with much eagerness as to the 
 family of Lord Lansmere , especially as to the female part of it . randal 
 have then judge it prudent to avoid speak of Violante , and feign 
 ignorance ; but promise to ascertain all particular by the time he next 
 see the marchesa . it be the warmth with which she have thank he that 
 have set his busy mind at work to conjecture the cause of her curiosity 
 so earnestly arouse , and to ascribe that cause to jealousy . if Harley 
 love Violante   as Randal himself have before suppose , the little of 
 passion that the young man admit to himself be enlist in aid 
 of Peschiera 's scheme . for though Randal do not love Violante , he 
 cordially dislike L'Estrange , and would have go as far to render that 
 dislike vindictive , as a cold reasoner , intent upon worldly fortune , 
 will ever suffer mere hate to influence he . 

 " at the bad , " think Randal , " if it be not Harley , touch the chord of 
 jealousy , and its vibration will direct I right . " 

 thus soliloquizing , he arrive at Madame di Negra 's . 

 now , in reality the marchesa 's inquiry as to Lord Lansmere 's family 
 have their source in the misguided , restless , despairing interest with 
 which she still cling to the image of the young poet , whom Randal have 
 no reason to suspect . that interest have become yet more keen from the 
 impatient misery she have feel ever since she have plight herself 
 to another . a wild hope that she might yet escape , a vague regretful 
 thought that she have be too hasty in dismiss Leonard from her 
 presence , - that she ought rather to have court his friendship , and 
 contend against her unknown rival , - at time draw her wayward mind 
 wholly from the future to which she have consign herself . and , to 
 do her justice , though her sense of duty be so defective , and the 
 principle which should have guide her conduct be so lose to she 
 sight , still her feeling towards the generous Hazeldean be not so 
 hard and blunt but what her own ingratitude add to her torment ; and 
 it seem as if the sole atonement she could make to he be to find 
 an excuse to withdraw her promise , and save he from herself . she have 
 cause Leonard 's step to be watch ; she have find that he visit at 
 Lord Lansmere 's ; that he have go there often , and stay there long . 
 she have learn in the neighbourhood that Lady Lansmere have one or 
 two young female guest stay with she . surely this be the 
 attraction - here be the rival ! 

 Randal find Beatrice in a state of mind that answer his purpose ; 
 and first turn his conversation on Harley , and note that her 
 countenance do not change , by little and little he draw forth her 
 secret . 

 then say Randal , gravely , " if one whom you honour with a tender thought 
 visit at Lord Lansmere 's house , you have , indeed , cause to fear for 
 yourself , to hope for your brother 's success in the object which have 
 bring he to England ; for a girl of surpass beauty be a guest in 
 Lord Lansmere 's house , and I will now tell you that that girl be she 
 whom Count Peschiera would make his bride . " 

 as Randal thus speak , and see how his listener 's brow darken and her 
 eye flash , he feel that his accomplice be secure . Violante ! have not 
 Leonard speak of Violante , and with such praise ? have not his boyhood 
 be pass under her eye ? who but Violante could be the rival ? 
 Beatrice 's abrupt exclamation , after a moment 's pause , reveal to 
 randal the advantage he have gain . and partly by rouse her jealousy 
 into revenge , partly by flatter her love with assurance that , 
 if Violante be fairly remove from England , be the wife of Count 
 Peschiera , it would be impossible that Leonard could remain insensible 
 to her own attraction ; that he , Randal , would undertake to free she 
 honourably from her engagement to Frank Hazeldean , and obtain from her 
 brother the acquittal of the debt which have first fetter her hand to 
 that confide suitor , - he do not quit the marchesa until she have not 
 only promise to do all that randal might suggest , but impetuously urge 
 he to mature his plan , and hasten the hour to accomplish they . randal 
 then walk some minute musing and slow along the street , revolve 
 the next mesh in his elaborate and most subtle web . and here his craft 
 luminously devise its masterpiece . 

 it be necessary , during any interval that might elapse between 
 Violante 's disappearance and her departure from England , in order to 
 divert suspicion from Peschiera   who might otherwise be detain , that 
 some cause for her voluntary absence from Lord Lansmere 's should be at 
 least assignable ; it be still more necessary that randal himself should 
 stand wholly clear from any surmise that he could have connive at the 
 count 's design , even should their actual perpetrator be discover or 
 conjecture . to effect these object , Randal hasten to Norwood , and 
 obtain an interview with Riccabocca . in seem agitation and alarm , 
 he inform the exile that he have reason to know that Peschiera have 
 succeed in obtain a secret interview with Violante , and he fear 
 have make a certain favourable impression on her mind ; and speak as 
 if with the jealousy of a lover , he entreat Riccabocca to authorize 
 Randal 's direct proposal to Violante , and to require her consent to 
 their immediate nuptial . 

 the poor Italian be confound with the intelligence convey to he ; 
 and his almost superstitious fear of his brilliant enemy , conjoin 
 with his opinion of the susceptibility to outward attraction common 
 to all the female sex , make he not only implicitly credit , but 
 even exaggerate , the danger that Randal intimate . the idea of his 
 daughter 's marriage with Randal , towards which he have lately cool , he 
 now gratefully welcome . 

 but his first natural suggestion be to go , or send , for Violante , and 
 bring she to his own house . this , however , Randal artfully opposed . 

 " alas ! I know , " say he , " that Peschiera have discover your retreat , 
 and surely she would be far less safe here than where she be now ! " 

 " but , diavolo ! you say the man have see she where she be now , in spite 
 of all Lady Lansmere 's promise and Harley 's precaution . " 

 " true . of this Peschiera boast to I . he effect it not , of course , 
 openly , but in some disguise . I be sufficiently , however , in his 
 confidence - any man may be that with so audacious a braggart - to deter 
 he from renew his attempt for some day . meanwhile , I or yourself 
 will leave discover some surer home than this , to which you can 
 remove , and then will be the proper time to take back your daughter . 
 and for the present , if you will send by I a letter to enjoin she to 
 receive I as her future bridegroom , it will necessarily divert all 
 think at once from the count ; I shall be able to detect by the manner 
 in which she receive I , how far the count have overstate the effect 
 he pretend to have produce . you can give I also a letter to Lady 
 Lansmere , to prevent your daughter come hither . oh , sir , do not reason 
 with I . have indulgence for my lover 's fear . believe that I advise for 
 the good . have I not the keen interest to do so ? " 

 like many a man who be wise enough with pen and paper before he , and 
 plenty of time wherewith to get up his wisdom , Riccabocca be flurry , 
 nervous , and confuse when that wisdom be call upon for any ready 
 exertion . from the tree of knowledge he have take graft enough to 
 serve for a forest ; but the whole forest could not spare he a handy 
 walking - stick . the great folio of the dead Machiavelli lie useless 
 before he , - the live Machiavelli of daily life stand all puissant by 
 his side . the Sage be as supple to the Schemer as the Clairvoyant be to 
 the Mesmerist ; and the lean slight finger of Randal actually dictate 
 almost the very word that Riccabocca write to his child and her 
 hostess . 

 the philosopher would have like to consult his wife ; but he be ashamed 
 to confess that weakness . suddenly he remember Harley , and say , as 
 Randal take up the letter which Riccabocca have indite , 

 " there , that will give we time ; and I will send to Lord L'Estrange and 
 talk to he . " 

 " my noble friend , " reply Randal , mournfully , " may I entreat you not 
 to see Lord L'Estrange until at least I have plead my cause to your 
 daughter , - until , indeed , she be no long under his father 's roof ? " 

 " and why ? " 

 " because I presume that you be sincere when you deign to receive I as 
 a son - in - law , and because I be sure that Lord L'Estrange would hear with 
 distaste of your disposition in my favour . be I not right ? " 

 Riccabocca be silent . 

 " and though his argument would fail with a man of your honour and 
 discernment , they might have more effect on the young mind of your 
 child . think , I beseech you , the more she be set against I , the more 
 accessible she may be to the art of Peschiera . speak not , therefore , I 
 implore you , to Lord L'Estrange till Violante have accept my hand , or 
 at least until she be again under your charge ; otherwise take back your 
 letter , - it would be of no avail . " 

 " perhaps you be right . certainly Lord L'Estrange be prejudiced against 
 you ; or rather , he think too much of what I have be , too little of 
 what I be . " 

 " who can see you , and not do so ? I pardon he . " after kiss the hand 
 which the exile modestly seek to withdraw from that act of homage , 
 randal pocket the letter ; and , as if struggle with emotion , rush 
 from the house . 

 now , o curious reader , if thou wilt heedfully observe to what use 
 Randal Leslie put those letter , - what speedy and direct result he draw 
 forth from device which would seem to an honest simple understanding 
 the most roundabout , wire - draw waste of invention , - I almost fear 
 that in thine admiration for his cleverness , thou mayest half forget thy 
 contempt for his knavery . 

 but when the head be very full , it do not do to have the heart very 
 empty ; there be such a thing as be top - heavy ! 




 CHAPTER IV . 

 Helen and Violante have be converse together , and Helen have obey 
 her guardian 's injunction , and speak , though briefly , of her positive 
 engagement to Harley . however much Violante have be prepare for the 
 confidence , however clearly she have divine that engagement , however 
 before persuade that the dream of her childhood be flee forever , still 
 the positive truth , come from Helen 's own lip , be attend with that 
 anguish which prove how impossible it be to prepare the human heart for 
 the final verdict which slay its future . she do not , however , betray 
 her emotion to Helen 's artless eye ; sorrow , deep - seat , be seldom 
 self - betray . but , after a little while , she creep away ; and , forgetful 
 of Peschiera , of all thing that could threaten danger   what danger 
 could harm she more !   she glide from the house , and go her desolate 
 way under the leafless wintry tree . ever and anon she pause , ever 
 and anon she murmur the same word : " if she love he , I could be 
 console ; but she do not ! or how could she have speak to I 
 so calmly ! how could she very look have be so sad ! heartless ! 
 heartless ! " 

 then there come on she a vehement resentment against poor Helen , 
 that almost take the character of scorn or hate , - its excess startle 
 herself . " be I grow so mean ? " she say ; and tear that humble she 
 rush to her eye . " can so short a time alter one thus ? impossible ! " 

 randal Leslie ring at the front gate , inquire for Violante , and , 
 catch sight of her form as he walk towards the house , advance 
 boldly and openly . his voice startle she as she lean against one of 
 the dreary tree , still mutter to herself , - forlorn . " I have a letter 
 to you from your father , Signorina , " say randal ; " but before I give it 
 to your hand , some explanation be necessary . condescend , then , to hear 
 I . " Violante shake her head impatiently , and stretch forth her hand 
 for the letter . randal observe her countenance with his keen , cold , 
 search eye ; but he still withhold the letter , and continue , after a 
 pause , 

 " I know that you be bear to princely fortune ; and the excuse for my 
 address you now be , that your birthright be lose to you , at least 
 unless you can consent to a union with the man who have despoil you 
 of your heritage , - a union which your father would deem dishonour to 
 yourself and he . Signorina , I might have presume to love you , but I 
 should not have name that love , have your father not encourage I by 
 his assent to my suit . " 

 Violante turn to the speaker , her face eloquent with haughty surprise . 
 Randal meet the gaze unmove . he continue , without warmth , and in the 
 tone of one who reason calmly , rather than of one who feel acutely , 

 " the man of whom I speak be in pursuit of you . I have cause to believe 
 that this person have already intrude himself upon you . ah , your 
 countenance own it ; you have see Peschiera ? this house be , then , 
 less safe than your father deem it . no house be safe for you but a 
 husband 's . I offer to you my name , - it be a gentleman 's ; my fortune , 
 which be small ; the participation in my hope of the future , which be 
 large . I place now your father 's letter in your hand , and await your 
 answer . " randal bow slightly , give the letter to Violante , and retire 
 a few pace . 

 it be not his object to conciliate Violante 's affection , but rather to 
 excite her repugnance , or at least her terror , - we must wait to 
 discover why ; so he stand apart , seemingly in a kind of self - confident 
 indifference , while the girl read the following letter : 

  " my child , receive with favour Mr. Leslie . he have my consent to 
  address you as a suitor . circumstance of which it be needless now 
  to inform you render it essential to my very peace and happiness 
  that your marriage should be immediate . in a word , I have give my 
  promise to Mr. Leslie , and I confidently leave it to the daughter of 
  my House to redeem the pledge of her anxious and tender father . " 

 the letter drop from Violante 's hand . randal approach , and restore 
 it to she . their eye meet . Violante recoil . 

 " I can not marry you , " say she , passionately . 

 " indeed ? " answer Randal , dryly . " be it because you can not love I ? " 

 " yes . " 

 " I do not expect that you would as yet , and I still persist in my suit . 
 I have promise to your father that I would not recede before your first 
 unconsidered refusal . " 

 " I will go to my father at once . " 

 " do he request you to do so in his letter ? look again . pardon I , but 
 he foresee your impetuosity ; and I have another note for Lady Lansmere , 
 in which he beg her ladyship not to sanction your return to he   should 
 you so wish   until he come or send for you himself . he will do so 
 whenever your word have redeem his own . " 

 " and do you dare to talk to I thus , and yet pretend to love I ? " 

 Randal smile ironically . 

 " I pretend but to we d you . love be a subject on which I might have 
 speak formerly , or may speak hereafter . I give you some little time to 
 consider . when I next call , let I hope that we may fix the day for our 
 wedding . " 

 " never ! " 

 " you will be , then , the first daughter of your House who disobey a 
 father ; and you will have this additional crime ; that you disobey he 
 in his sorrow , his exile , and his fall . " 

 Violante wring her hand . 

 " be there no choice , no escape ? " 

 " I see none for either . listen to I . I love you , it be true ; but it be 
 not for my happiness to marry one who dislike I , nor for my ambition 
 to connect myself with one whose poverty be great than my own . I marry 
 but to keep my plighted faith with your father , and to save you from a 
 villain you would hate more than myself , and from whom no wall be 
 a barrier , no law a defence . one person , indeed , might perhaps have 
 preserve you from the misery you seem to anticipate with I ; that 
 person might defeat the plan of your father 's foe , - effect , it might 
 be , term which could revoke his banishment and restore his honour ; 
 that person be - " 

 " Lord L'Estrange ? " 

 " Lord L'Estrange ! " repeat Randal , sharply , and watch her pale 
 part lip and her change colour ; " Lord L'Estrange ! what could he do ? 
 why do you name he ? " 

 Violante turn aside . " he save my father once , " say she , feelingly . 

 " and have interfere , and trifle , and promise , Heaven know what , ever 
 since : yet to what end ? Pooh ! the person I speak of your father 
 would not consent to see , would not believe if he see she ; yet she be 
 generous , noble , could sympathize with you both . she be the sister of 
 your father 's enemy , the Marchesa di Negra . I be convinced that she 
 have great influence with her brother , - that she have know enough of his 
 secret to awe he into renounce all design on yourself ; but it be 
 idle now to speak of she . " 

 " no , no , " exclaim Violante . " tell I where she live - I will see she . " 

 " Pardon I , I can not obey you ; and , indeed , her own pride be now arouse 
 by your father 's unfortunate prejudice against she . it be too late to 
 count upon her aid . you turn from I , - my presence be unwelcome . I rid 
 you of it now . but welcome or unwelcome , later you must endure it - and 
 for life . " 

 Randal again bow with formal ceremony , walk towards the house , and 
 ask for Lady Lansmere . the countess be at home . randal deliver 
 Riccabocca 's note , which be very short , imply that he fear 
 Peschiera have discover his retreat , and request Lady Lansmere to 
 retain Violante , whatever her own desire , till her ladyship hear from 
 he again . 

 the countess read , and her lip curl in disdain . " strange ! " say she , 
 half to herself . 

 " strange ! " say Randal , " that a man like your correspondent should fear 
 one like the Count di Peschiera . be that it ? " 

 " Sir , " say the countess , a little surprised , ' strange that any man 
 should fear another in a country like ours ! " 

 " I do not   know , " say Randal , with his low soft laugh ; " I fear many man , 
 and I know many who ought to fear I ; yet at every turn of the street 
 one meet a policeman ! " 

 " yes , " say Lady Lansmere . " but to suppose that this profligate 
 foreigner could carry away a girl like Violante against her will , - a man 
 she have never see , and whom she must have be teach to hate ! " 

 " be on your guard , nevertheless , I pray you , madam ; ' where there be a 
 will there be a way ' ! " 

 Randal take his leave , and return to Madame di Negra 's . he stay with 
 she an hour , revisit the count , and then stroll to Limmer 's . 

 " randal , " say the squire , who look pale and worn , but who scorn 
 to confess the weakness with which he still grieve and yearn for his 
 rebellious son , " randal , you have nothing now to do in London ; can you 
 come and stay with I , and take to farming ? I remember that you show a 
 good deal of sound knowledge about thin sowing . " 

 " my dear sir , I will come to you as soon as the general election be 
 over . " 

 " what the deuce have you get to do with the general election ? " 

 " Mr. Egerton have some wish that I should enter parliament ; indeed , 
 negotiation for that purpose be now on foot . " 

 the squire shake his head . " I do not   like my half - brother 's politic . " 

 " I shall be quite independent of they , " cry randal , loftily ; " that 
 independence be the condition for which I stipulate . " 

 " glad to hear it ; and if you do come into parliament , I hope you will not 
 turn your back on the land ? " 

 " turn my back on the land ! " cry Randal , with devout horror . " oh , sir , 
 I be not so unnatural ! " 

 " that be the right way to put it , " quoth the credulous squire ; " it be 
 unnatural ! it be turn one 's back on one 's own mother . the land be a 
 mother - " 

 " to those who live by she , certainly , - a mother , " say Randal , gravely . 
 " and though , indeed , my father starve by she rather than life , and 
 Rood Hall be not like Hazeldean , still - i - " 

 " hold your tongue , " interrupt the squire ; " I want to talk to you . your 
 grandmother be a Hazeldean . " 

 " her picture be in the drawing - room at Rood . People think I very like 
 she . " 

 " indeed ! " say the squire . " the Hazeldeans be generally inclined to be 
 stout and rosy , which you be certainly not . but no fault of your . we 
 be all as Heaven make we . however , to the point . I be go to alter 
 my will , " -   say with a choke gulp . " this be the rough draft for the 
 lawyer to work upon . " 

 " pray , pray , sir , do not speak to I on such a subject . I can not bear to 
 contemplate even the possibility of - of - " 

 " my death ? ha , ha ! nonsense . my own son calculate on the date of it by 
 the insurance - table . ha , ha , ha ! a very fashionable son , eh ! ha , ha ! " 

 " Poor Frank ! do not let he suffer for a momentary forgetfulness of 
 right feeling . when he come to be married to that foreign lady , and be 
 a father himself , he - " 

 " Father himself ! " burst forth the squire . " Father to a swarm of 
 sallow - faced Popish tadpole ! no foreign frog shall hop about my 
 grave in Hazeldean churchyard . no , no . but you need not look so 
 reproachful , - I ' m not go to disinherit Frank . " 

 " of course not , " say Randal , with a bitter curve in the lip that 
 rebel against the joyous smile which he seek to impose on it . 

 " no ; I shall leave he the life - interest in the great part of 
 the property ; but if he marry a foreigner , her child will not 
 succeed , - you will stand after he in that case . but - now do not 
 interrupt I - but Frank look as if he would live long than you , so 
 small thank to I for my good intention , you may say . I mean to do 
 more for you than a mere barren place in the entail . what do you say to 
 marry ? " 

 " just as you please , " say randal , meekly . 

 " good . there be Miss Sticktorights disengage , - great heiress . her land 
 run onto Rood . at one time I think of she for that graceless puppy of 
 mine . but I can manage more easily to make up the match for you . there be 
 a mortgage on the property ; old Sticktorights would be very glad to pay 
 it off . I will pay it out of the hazeldean estate , and give up the right 
 of way into the bargain . you understand ? 

 " so come down as soon as you can , and court the young lady yourself . " 

 Randal express his thank with much grateful eloquence ; and he then 
 delicately insinuate , that if the squire ever do mean to bestow upon 
 he any pecuniary favour   always without injury to Frank , it would 
 gratify he more to win back some portion of the old estate of Rood , 
 than to have all the acre of the Sticktorights , however free from any 
 other incumbrance than the amiable heiress . 

 the squire listen to Randal with benignant attention . this wish the 
 country gentleman could well understand and sympathize with . he promise 
 to inquire into the matter , and to see what could be do with old 
 Thornhill . 

 Randal here let out that Mr. Thornhill be about to dispose of a large 
 slice of the ancient Leslie estate through Levy , and that he , Randal , 
 could thus get it at a more moderate price than would be natural , if Mr. 
 Thornhill know that his neighbour the squire would bid for the purchase . 

 " well say nothing about it either to Levy or Thornhill . " 

 " right , " say the squire . " no proprietor like to sell to another 
 proprietor , in the same shire , as largely acre as himself : it spoil 
 the balance of power . see to the business yourself ; and if I can help 
 you with the purchase   after that boy be marry , - I can attend to 
 nothing before , why , I will . " 

 Randal now go to Egerton 's . the statesman be in his library , settle 
 the account of his house - steward , and give brief order for the 
 reduction of his establishment to that of an ordinary private gentleman . 

 " I may go abroad if I lose my election , " say Egerton , condescend to 
 assign to his servant a reason for his economy ; " and if I do not lose 
 it , still , now I be out of office , I shall live much in private . " 

 " do I disturb you , sir ? " say Randal , enter . 

 " no ; I have just do . " 

 the house - steward withdraw , much surprised and disgusted , and meditate 
 the resignation of his own office , - in order , not like Egerton , to save , 
 but to spend . the house steward have private dealing with Baron Levy , 
 and be in fact the veritable X. Y. of the " Times , " for whom Dick Avenel 
 have be mistake . he invest his wage and perquisite in the discount 
 of bill ; and it be part of his own money that have   though unknown to 
 himself   swell the last l5,000 which Egerton have borrow from Levy . 

 " I have settle with our committee ; and , with Lord Lansmere 's consent , " 
 say Egerton , briefly , " you will stand for the borough , as we propose , 
 in conjunction with myself . and should any accident happen to I , - that 
 be , should I vacate this seat from any cause , - you may succeed to it , 
 very shortly perhaps . ingratiate yourself with the elector , and speak 
 at the public - house for both of we . I shall stand on my dignity , and 
 leave the work of the election to you . no thank , - you know how I hate 
 thank . good - night . " 

 " I never stand so near to fortune and to power , " say Randal , as he 
 slowly undressed . " and I owe it but to knowledge , - knowledge of man , 
 life , of all that book can teach we . " 

 so his slight thin finger drop the extinguisher on the candle , and 
 the prosperous Schemer lay himself down to rest in the dark . shutter 
 close , curtain draw - never be rest more quiet , never be room more 
 dark ! 

 that evening , Harley have din at his father 's . he speak much to Helen , 
 scarcely at all to Violante . but it so happen that when later , and 
 a little while before he take his leave , Helen , at his request , be 
 play a favourite air of his , Lady Lausmere , who have be seat 
 between he and Violante , leave the room , and Violante turn quickly 
 towards Harley . 

 " do you know the Marchesa di Negra ? " she ask , in a hurried voice . 

 " a little . why do you ask ? " 

 " that be my secret , " answer Violante , try to smile with her old 
 frank , childlike archness . " but , tell I , do you think well of she 
 than of her brother ? " 

 " certainly . I believe her heart to be good , and that she be not without 
 generous quality . " 

 " can you not induce my father to see she ? would you not counsel he to 
 do so ? " 

 " any wish of yours be a law to I , " answer Harley , gallantly . " you 
 wish your father to see she ? I will try and persuade he to do so . now , 
 in return , confide to I your secret . what be your object ? " 

 " leave to return to my Italy . I care not for honour , for rank ; and 
 even my father have cease to regret their loss . but the land , the native 
 land - oh , to see it once more ! oh , to die there ! " 

 " die ! you child have so lately leave heaven , that ye talk as if 
 ye could return there , without pass through the gate of sorrow , 
 infirmity , and age ! but I think you be content with England . why 
 so eager to leave it ? Violante , you be unkind to we , - to Helen , who 
 already love you so well . " as Harley speak , Helen rise from the piano ,

 and Helen follow she . Harley 's eye be very grave and thoughtful . 

 " be she not change - your friend ? " say he , look down . 

 " yes , lately ; much change . I fear there be something on her mind , - I 
 know not what . " 

 " ah , " mutter Harley , " it may be so ; but at your age and her , nothing 
 rest on the mind long . Observe , I say the mind , - the heart be more 
 tenacious . " 

 Helen sigh softly , but deeply . 

 " and therefore , " continue Harley , half to himself , " we can detect when 
 something be on the mind , - some care , some fear , some trouble . but when 
 the heart close over its own more passionate sorrow , who can discover , 
 who conjecture ? yet you at least , my pure , candid Helen , - you might 
 subject mind and heart alike to the fabled window of glass . " 

 " oh , no ! " cry Helen , involuntarily . 

 " oh , yes ! do not let I think that you have one secret I may not know , 
 or one sorrow I may not share . for , in our relationship , that would be 
 deceit . " 

 he press her hand with more than usual tenderness as he speak , and 
 shortly afterwards leave the house . 

 and all that night Helen feel like a guilty thing , - more wretched even 
 than Violante . 




 CHAPTER V. 

 early the next morning , while Violante be still in her room , a letter 
 address to she come by the post . the direction be in a strange hand . 
 she open it , and read , in Italian , what be thus translate : - 

  I would gladly see you , but I can not call openly at the house in 
  which you live . perhaps I may have it in my power to arrange family 
  dissension , - to repair any wrong your father may have sustain . 
  perhaps I may be enable to render yourself an essential service . 
  but for all this it be necessary that we should meet and confer 
  frankly . meanwhile time press , delay be forbid . will you meet 
  I , an hour after noon , in the lane , just outside the private gate 
  of your garden ? I shall be alone , and you can not fear to meet one 
  of your own sex , and a kinswoman . ah , I so desire to see you ! 
  come , I beseech you . 

  BEATRICE . 

 Violante read , and her decision be take . she be naturally fearless ,

 quality ; and who but Madame di Negra would write herself a kinswoman ,

 a little before the appoint hour , she steal unobserved through the

 the voice drop mournfully ; and Violante , surprise by the tone , and

 some hesitation , 

 " you be , I presume , the Marchesa di Negra ? and I have hear of you

 " of I ! from whom ? " ask Beatrice , almost fiercely . " from Mr Leslie , 
 and - and - " 

 " go on ; why falter ? " 

 " from Lord L'Estrange . " 

 " from no one else ? " 

 " not that I remember . "

 " we can not talk here , " say Beatrice , impatiently ; " and I have so much 
 to say , so much to know . trust I yet more ; it be for yourself I speak .

 this proposition startle Violante . she retreat towards the gate with

 " I , too , would once have recoil from one step beyond the formal line

 the questioner it rebuke .

 Violante approach , but still hesitatingly . " not by union with your

 she say this as of some one absent , and again sink into silence ; but

 " still , then , as woman to woman - both , as you say , akin , and spring from

 meanwhile the carriage have enter London ; it pass the quarter in

 arrive . "

 CHAPTER VI . 

 it be , as we have see , without take counsel of the faithful Jemima

 CHAPTER VII . 

 Harley go straight to Peschiera 's hotel . he be tell that the count

 Dutchman , ' - a fine vessel , and carry gun . " 

 " Enough . now , heed I . there can be no immediate danger to Violante , so

 " seem ! Nay , I be so ! if I once pause to despond - even to doubt - I
