 that make the angl fix it on the hook , and observe with complacency 
 that the vivacity of its wriggle will attract the bite . if the worm 
 could but make the angler respect , or even fear it , the barb would find 
 some other bait . few angler would impale an estimable silkworm , and 
 still few the angler who would finger into service a formidable 
 hornet . 

 " Pooh , my - dear Frank , " say randal ; " I have give you my advice ; you 
 reject it . well , what then will you do ? " 

 " I shall ask for leave of absence , and run away some where , " say Frank , 
 dry his tear . " I can not   face London ; I can not   mix with other . I want 
 to be by myself , and wrestle with all that I feel here - in my heart . 
 then I shall write to my mother , say the plain truth , and leave she to 
 judge as kindly of I as she can . " 

 " you be quite right . yes , leave town ! why not go abroad ? you have never 
 be abroad . new scene will distract your mind . run over to Paris . " 

 " not to Paris - I do not   want gayetie ; but I do intend to go abroad 
 somewhere , - any dull dismal hole of a place . good - by ! do not   think of I 
 any more for the present . " 

 " but let I know where you go ; and meanwhile I will see the squire . " 

 " say as little of I as you can to he . I know you mean most kindly , but 
 oh , how I wish there never have be any third person between I and my 
 father ! there : you may well snatch away your hand . what an ungrateful 
 wretch to you I be . I do believe I be the wicked fellow . what ! you 
 shake hand with I still ! my dear Randal , you have the good heart - God 
 bless you ! " Frank turn away , and disappear within his dressing - room . 

 " they must be reconcile now , soon or later , - squire and son , " say 
 randal to himself , as he leave the lodging . " I do not   see how I can 
 prevent that , - the marchesa be withdraw , - unless Frank do it for 
 I . but it be well he should be abroad , - something maybe make out 
 of that ; meanwhile I may yet do all that I could reasonably hope 
 to do , - even if Frank have marry Beatrice , - since he be not to be 
 disinherit . get the squire to advance the money for the thornhill 
 purchase , complete the affair ; this marriage with Violante will help ; 
 Levy must know that ; secure the borough ; - well thought of . I will go to 
 Avenel 's . by - the - by , by - the - by , the squire might as well keep I still 
 in the entail after Frank , suppose Frank die childless . this love 
 affair may keep he long from marry . his hand be very hot , - a hectic 
 colour ; those strong - look fellow often go off in rapid decline , 
 especially if anything prey on their mind , - their mind be so very 
 small . 

 " ah , the Hazeldean parson , - and with Avenel ! that young man , too , who 
 be he ? I have see he before some where . - my dear Mr. Dale , this be 
 a pleasant surprise . I think you have return to Hazeldean with our 
 friend the squire ? " 

 MR . DALE . - " the squire ! have he leave town , and without tell I ? " 

 RANDAL   take aside the parson . - " he be anxious to get back to Mrs. 
 Hazeldean , who be naturally very uneasy about her son and this foolish 
 marriage ; but I be happy to tell you that that marriage be effectually 
 and permanently break off . " 

 MR . DALE . - " how , how ? my poor friend tell I he have wholly fail to 
 make any impression on Frank , - forbid I to mention the subject . I be 
 just go to see Frank myself . I always have some influence with he . 
 but , Mr. Leslie , explain this very sudden and happy event . the marriage 
 break off ! " 

 RANDAL . - " it be a long story , and I dare not tell you my humble share in 
 it . Nay , I must keep that secret . Frank might not forgive I . Suffice 
 it that you have my word that the fair Italian have leave England , and 
 decidedly refuse Frank 's address . but stay , take my advice , do not 
 go to he ; you see it be not only the marriage that have offend the 
 squire , but some pecuniary transaction , - an unfortunate post - obit bond 
 on the casino property . Frank ought to be leave to his own repentant 
 reflection . they will be most salutary ; you know his temper , - he do not 
 bear reproof ; and yet it be well , on the other hand , not to let he 
 treat too lightly what have pass . let we leave he to himself for a few 
 day he be in an excellent frame of mind . " 

 MR . DALE   shake Randal 's hand warmly . - " you speak admirably - a 
 post - obit ! - so often as he have hear his father 's opinion on such 
 transaction . no , I will not see he ; I should be too angry - " 

 randal   lead the parson back , resume , after an exchange of 
 salutation with Avenel , who , meanwhile , have be confer with his 
 nephew . - " you should not be so long away from your rectory , Mr. Dale . 
 what will your parish do without you ? " 

 MR . DALE . - " the old fable of the wheel and the fly . I be afraid the 
 wheel roll on the same . but if I be absent from my parish , I be still 
 in the company of one who do I honour as an old parishioner . you 
 remember Leonard Fairfield , your antagonist in the Battle of the 
 stock ? " 

 MR . AVENEL . - " my nephew , I be proud to say , sir . " randal bow with 
 mark civility , Leonard with a reserve no less marked . 

 MR . AVENEL   ascribe his nephew 's reserve to shyness . - " you should be 
 friend , you two youngster . who know but you may run together in the 
 same harness ? ah , that remind I , Leslie , I have a word or two to say 
 to you . your servant , Mr. Dale . shall be happy to present you to 
 Mrs. Avenel . my card , - Eaton Square , number - . you will call on I 
 to - morrow , Leonard . and mind , I shall be very angry if you persist in 
 your refusal . such an opening ! " Avenel take Randal 's arm , while the 
 parson and Leonard walk on . 

 " any fresh hint as to Lansmere ? " ask Randal . 

 " yes ; I have now decide on the plan of contest . you must fight two and 
 two , - you and Egerton against I and   if I can get he to stand , as I 
 hope   my nephew , Leonard . " 

 " what ! " say Randal , alarmed ; " then , after all , I can hope for no 
 support from you ? " 

 " I do not   say that ; but I have reason to think Lord L'Estrange will 
 bestir himself actively in favour of Egerton . if so , it will be a very 
 sharp contest ; and I must manage the whole election on our side , and 
 unite all our shaky vote , which I can well do by stand myself in 
 the first instance , reserve it to after consideration whether I shall 
 throw up at the last ; for I do not   particularly want to come in , as I 
 do a little time ago , before I have find out my nephew . wonderful young 
 man ! with such a head , - will do I credit in the rotten old House ; and 
 I think I have good leave London , go to Screwstown , and look to my 
 business . no , if Leonard stand , I roust first see to get he in ; and 
 next , to keep Egerton out . it will probably , therefore , end in the 
 return of one and one or either side , as we think of before , - Leonard 
 on our side ; and Egerton sha'n't   be the man on the other . you 
 understand ? " 

 " I do , my dear Avenel . of course , as I before say , I can not   dictate to 
 your party whom they should prefer , - Egerton or myself . and it will be 
 obvious to the public that your party would rather defeat so eminent an 
 adversary as Mr. Egerton than a tyro in politic like I . of course I 
 can not scheme for such a result ; it would be misconstrue , and damage my 
 character . but I rely equally on your friendly promise . " 

 " promise ! no , I do not   promise . I must first see how the cat jump ; and 
 I do not   know yet how our friend may like you , nor how they can be 
 manage . all I can say be , that Audley Egerton sha'n't   be M.P. for 
 Lansmere . meanwhile , you will take care not to commit yourself in 
 speak so that our party can not   vote for you consistently ; they must 
 count on have you - when you get into the House . " 

 " I be not a violent party - man at present , " answer Randal , prudently . 
 " and if public opinion prove on your side , it be the duty of a statesman 
 to go with the time . " 

 " very sensibly say ; and I have a private bill or two , and some other 
 little job , I want to get through the House , which we can discuss 
 later , should it come to a frank understanding between we . we must 
 arrange how to meet privately at Lansmere , if necessary . I will see to 
 that . I shall go down this week . I think of take a hint from the free 
 and glorious land of America , and establish secret caucus . nothing 
 like them . " 

 " caucus ? " 

 " small sub - committee that spy on their man night and day , and do not 
 suffer they to be intimidate to vote the other way . " 

 " you have an extraordinary head for public affair , Avenel . you should 
 come into parliament yourself ; your nephew be so very young . " 

 " so be you . " 

 " yes ; but I know the world . do he ? " 

 " the world know he , though not by name , and he have be the making of 
 I . " 

 " how ? you surprise I . " 

 Avenel first explain about the patent which Leonard have secure 
 to he ; and next confide , upon honour , Leonard 's identity with the 
 anonymous author whom the parson have suppose to be Professor Moss . 

 Randal Leslie feel a jealous pang . what ! then - have this village boy , 
 this associate of John Burley   literary vagabond , whom he suppose 
 have long since go to the dog , and be bury at the expense of the 
 parish   - have this boy so triumph over birth , rearing , circumstance , 
 that , if Randal and Leonard have meet together in any public place , and 
 Leonard 's identity with the rise author have be reveal , every eye 
 would have turn from Randal to gaze on Leonard ? the common consent of 
 mankind would have acknowledge the supreme royalty of genius when it 
 once leave its solitude , and stride into the world . what ! be 
 this rude villager the child of Fame , who , without an effort , and 
 unconsciously , have inspire in the wearied heart of Beatrice di Negra 
 a love that randal know , by an instinct , no art , no craft , could ever 
 create for he in the heart of woman ? and now , do this same youth stand 
 on the same level in the ascent to power as he , the well - bear randal 
 Leslie , the accomplished protege of the superb Audley Egerton ? be they 
 to be rival in the same arena of practical busy life ? Randal gnaw his 
 quivering lip . 

 all the while , however , the young man whom he so envy be a prey to 
 sorrow deeply far than could ever find room or foot in the narrow 
 and stony heart of the unloving schemer . 

 as Leonard walk through the crowded street with the friend and 
 monitor of his childhood , confide the simple tale of his early 
 trial , - when , amidst the wreck of fortune and in despair of fame , the 
 child - angel smile by his side , like Hope , - all renown seem to he so 
 barren , all the future so dark ! his voice tremble , and his countenance 
 become so sad , that his benignant listener , divine that around the 
 image of Helen there clung some passionate grief that overshadow all 
 worldly success , draw Leonard gently and gently on , till the young man , 
 long yearn for some confidant , tell he all , - how , faithful through 
 long year to one pure and ardent memory , Helen have be see once more , 
 the child ripen to woman , and the memory reveal itself as love . 

 the parson listen with a mild and thoughtful brow , which expand into 
 a more cheerful expression as Leonard close his story . 

 " I see no reason to despond , " say Mr. Dale . " you fear that Miss 
 Digby do not return your attachment ; you dwell upon her reserve , her 
 distant , though kindly manner . cheer up ! all young lady be under the 
 influence of what phrenologist call the organ of Secretiveness , when 
 they be in the society of the object of their preference . just as you 
 describe Miss Digby 's manner to you , be my Carry 's manner to myself . " 

 the parson here indulge in a very appropriate digression upon female 
 modesty , which he wind up by assert that that estimable virtue 
 become more and more influence by the secretive organ , in proportion as 
 the favour suitor approach near and near to a definite proposal . 
 it be the duty of a gallant and honourable lover to make that proposal 
 in distinct and orthodox form , before it could be expect that a young 
 lady should commit herself and the dignity of her sex by the slight 
 hint as to her own inclination . 

 " next , " continue the parson , " you choose to torment yourself by 
 contrast your own origin and fortune with the altered circumstance 
 of Miss Digby , - the ward of Lord L'Estrange , the guest of Lady Lansmere . 
 you say that if Lord L'Estrange could have countenance such a union , 
 he would have adopt a different tone with you , - sound your heart , 
 encourage your hope , and so forth . I view thing differently . I 
 have reason to do so ; and from all you have tell I of this nobleman 's 
 interest in your fate , I venture to make you this promise , that if Miss 
 Digby would accept your hand , Lord L'Estrange shall ratify her choice . " 

 " my dear Mr. Dale , " cry Leonard , transport , " you make I that 
 promise ? " 

 " I do , - from what you have say , and from what I myself know of Lord 
 L'Estrange . go , then , at once to Knightsbridge , see Miss Digby , show 
 she your heart , explain to she , if you will , your prospect , ask she 
 permission to apply to Lord L'Estrange   since he have constitute 
 himself her guardian ; and if Lord L'Estrange hesitate , - which , if your 
 happiness be set on this union , I think he will not , - let I know , and 
 leave the rest to I . " 

 Leonard yield himself to the parson 's persuasive eloquence . indeed , 
 when he recall to mind those passage in the manuscript of the 
 ill - fat Nora , which refer to the love that Harley have once bear 
 to she , - for he feel convinced that Harley and the boy suitor of Nora 's 
 narrative be one and the same ; and when all the interest that Harley 
 have take in his own fortune be explain by his relationship to she 
  even when Lord L'Estrange have suppose it less close than he would now 
 discover it to be , the young man , reason by his own heart , could not 
 but suppose that the noble Harley would rejoice to confer happiness upon 
 the son of she , so beloved by his boyhood . 

 " and to thee , perhaps , o my mother ! " think Leonard , with swimming 
 eye - " to thee , perhaps , even in thy grave , I shall owe the partner 
 of my life , as to the mystic breath of thy genius I owe the first pure 
 aspiration of my soul . " 

 it will be see that Leonard have not confide to the parson his 
 discovery of Nora 's manuscript , nor even his knowledge of his real 
 birth ; for the proud son naturally shrink from any confidence that 
 implicate Nora 's fair name , until at least Harley , who , it be clear 
 from those paper , must have intimately know his father , should 
 perhaps decide the question which the paper themselves leave so terribly 
 vague , - namely , whether he be the offspring of a legal marriage , or 
 Nora have be the victim of some unholy fraud . 

 while the parson still talk , and while Leonard still muse and 
 listen , their step almost mechanically take the direction towards 
 Knightsbridge , and pause at the gate of Lord Lansmere 's house . 

 " go in , my young friend ; I will wait without to know the issue , " say 
 the parson , cheeringly . " go , and with gratitude to Heaven , learn how to 
 bear the most precious joy that can befall mortal man ; or how to submit 
 to youth 's sharp sorrow , with the humble belief that even sorrow be 
 but some mercy conceal . " 




 CHAPTER xiii . 

 Leonard be show into the drawing - room , and it so chance that Helen 
 be there alone . the girl 's soft face be sadly change , even 
 since Leonard have see it last ; for the grief of nature mild and 
 undemonstrative as her , gnaw with quick ravage ; but at Leonard 's 
 unexpected entrance , the colour rush so vividly to the pale cheek 
 that its hectic might be take for the lustre of bloom and health . she 
 rise hurriedly , and in great confusion falter out , " that she believe 
 Lady Lansmere be in her room , - she would go for she , " and move towards 
 the door , without seem to notice the hand tremulously hold forth to 
 she ; when Leonard exclaim in uncontrollable emotion which pierce to 
 her very heart , in the keen accent of reproach , - 

 " oh , Miss Digby - oh , Helen - be it thus that you greet I , - rather thus 
 that you shun I ? could I have foresee this when we two orphan stand 
 by the mournful bridge , - so friendless , so desolate , and so cling 
 each to each ? happy time ! " he seize her hand suddenly as he speak the 
 last word , and bow his face over it . 

 " I must not hear you . do not talk so , Leonard , you break my heart . let 
 I go , let I go ! " 

 " be it that I be grow hateful to you ; be it merely that you see my love 
 and would discourage it ? Helen , speak to I , - speak ! " 

 he draw she with tender force towards he ; and , hold she firmly 
 by both hand , seek to gaze upon the face that she turn from 
 he , - turn in such despair . 

 " you do not know , " she say at last , struggle for composure , - " you do 
 not know the new claim on I , my altered position , how I be bind , or 
 you would be the last to speak thus to I , the first to give I courage , 
 and bid I - bid I - " 

 " bid you what ? " 

 " feel nothing here but duty ! " cry Helen , draw from his clasp both 
 her hand , and place they firmly on her breast . 

 " Miss Digby , " say Leonard , after a short pause of bitter reflection , in 
 which he wrong , while he think to divine , her meaning , " you speak of 
 new claim on you , your altered position - I comprehend . you may retain 
 some tender remembrance of the past ; but your duty now be to rebuke my 
 presumption . it be as I think and fear . this vain reputation which 
 I have make be but a hollow sound , - it give I no rank , assure I no 
 fortune . I have no right to look for the Helen of old in the Helen of 
 to - day . be it so - forget what I have say , and forgive I . " 

 this reproach sting to the quick the heart to which it appeal . a flash 
 brighten the meek , tearful eye , almost like the flash of resentment ; 
 her lip writhe in torture , and she feel as if all other pain be 
 light compare with the anguish that Leonard could impute to her motif 
 which to her simple nature seem so unworthy of she , and so gall to 
 himself . 

 a word rush as by inspiration to her lip , and that word calm and 
 soothe she . 

 " Brother ! " she say touchingly , " brother ! " 

 the word have a contrary effect on Leonard . sweet as it be , tender as 
 the voice that speak it , it impose a boundary to affection , it come as 
 a knell to hope . he recoil , shake his head mournfully : " too late to 
 accept that tie , - too late even for friendship . henceforth - for long 
 year to come - henceforth , till this heart have cease to beat at your 
 name to thrill at your presence , we two - be stranger . " 

 " stranger ! well - yes , it be right - it must be so ; we must not meet . oh , 
 Leonard Fairfield , who be it that in those day that you recall to I , 
 who be it that find you destitute and obscure ; who , not degrade you 
 by charity , place you in your right career ; open to you , amidst the 
 labyrinth in which you be well - nigh lose , the broad road to knowledge , 
 independence , fame ? answer I , - answer ! be it not the same who rear , 
 shelter your sister orphan ? if I could forget what I have owe to 
 he , should I not remember what he have do for you ? can I hear of your 
 distinction , and not remember it ? can I think how proud she may be who 
 will one day lean on your arm , and bear the name you have already raise 
 beyond all the title of an hour , - can I think of this , and not remember 
 our common friend , benefactor , guardian ? would you forgive I , if I 
 fail to do so ? " 

 " but , " falter Leonard , fear mingle with the conjecture these word 
 call forth - " but be it that Lord L'Estrange would not consent to our 
 union ? or of what do you speak ? you bewilder I . " 

 Helen feel for some moment as if it be impossible to reply ; and the 
 word at length be drag forth as if from the depth of her very 
 soul . 

 " he come to I , our noble friend . I never dream of it . he do not tell 
 I that he love I . he tell I that he be unhappy , alone ; that in I , 
 and only in I , he could find a comforter , a soother - he , he ! and I have 
 just arrive in England , be under his mother 's roof , have not then once 
 more seen you ; and - and - what could I answer ? strengthen I , strengthen 
 I , you whom I look up to and revere . yes , yes , you be right . we must 
 see each other no more . I be betroth to another , - to he ! strengthen 
 I ! " 

 all the inherent nobleness of the poet 's nature rise at once at this 
 appeal . 

 " oh , Helen - sister - Miss Digby , forgive I . you need no strength from 
 I ; I borrow it from you . I comprehend you , I respect . banish all 
 thought of I . repay our common benefactor . be what he ask of you , - his 
 comforter , his soother ; be more , - his pride and his joy . happiness will 
 come to you , as it come to those who confer happiness and forget self . 
 God comfort you in the pass struggle ; God bless you , in the long 
 year to come . Sister , I accept the holy name now , and will claim it 
 hereafter , when I too can think more of other than myself . " 

 Helen have cover her face with her hand , sob ; but with that soft , 
 womanly constraint which press woe back into the heart . a strange 
 sense of utter solitude suddenly pervade her whole being , and by that 
 sense of solitude she know that he be go . 




 CHAPTER XIV . 

 in another room in that same house sit , solitary as Helen , a stern , 
 gloomy , brood man , in whom they who have well know he from his 
 childhood could scarcely have recognize a trace of the humane , 
 benignant , trustful , but wayward and vary Harley , Lord L'Estrange . 

 he have read that fragment of a memoir , in which , out of all the chasm 
 of his barren and melancholy past , there rise two malignant truth that 
 seem literally to glare upon he with mocking and demon eye . the 
 woman whose remembrance have darken all the sunshine of his life have 
 love another ; the friend in whom he have confide his whole affectionate 
 loyal soul have be his perfidious rival . he have read from the first 
 word to the last , as if under a spell that hold he breathless ; and when 
 he close the manuscript , it be without a groan or sigh ; but over his 
 pale lip there pass that wither smile , which be as sure an index 
 of a heart overcharge with dire and fearful passion , as the arrowy 
 flash of the lightning be of the tempest that be gather within the 
 cloud . 

 he then thrust the paper into his bosom , and , keep his hand over 
 they , firmly clench , he leave the room , and walk slowly on towards 
 his father 's house . with every step by the way , his nature , in the war 
 of its element , seem to change and harden into form of granite . 
 love , humanity , trust , vanish away . hate , revenge , misanthropy , 
 suspicion , and scorn of all that could wear the eye of affection , 
 or speak with the voice of honour , come fast through the gloom of his 
 thought , settle down in the wilderness , grim and menacing as the 
 harpy of ancient song - 

  " uncaeque manus , et pallida semper Ora . " 

  " hand arm with fang , and lip forever pale . " 

 thus the gloomy man have cross the threshold of his father 's house , and 
 silently enter the apartment still set apart for he . he have arrive 
 about an hour before Leonard ; and as he stand by the hearth , with his 
 arm fold on his breast , and his eye fix lead - like on the ground , 
 his mother come in to welcome and embrace he . he check her eager 
 inquiry after Violante , he recoil from the touch of her hand . 

 " hold , madam , " say he , startle her ear with the cold austerity of his 
 tone . " I can not heed your question , - I be fill with the question I 
 must put to yourself . you oppose my boyish love for Leonora Avenel . I 
 do not blame you , - all mother of equal rank would have do the same . 
 yet , have you not frustrate all frank intercourse with she , I might have 
 take refusal from her own lip , - survive that grief , and now be a 
 happy man . year since then have roll away , - roll over her quiet 
 slumber , and my restless wake life . all this time be you aware that 
 Audley Egerton have be the lover of Leonora Avenel ? " 

 " Harley , Harley ! do not speak to I in that cruel voice , do not look at 
 I with those hard eye ! " 

 " you know it , then , - you , my mother ! " continue Harley , unmove by her 
 rebuke ; " and why do you never say , ' Son , you be waste the bloom 
 and us of your life in sorrowful fidelity to a lie ! you be lavish 
 trust and friendship on a perfidious hypocrite . ' " 

 " how could I speak to you thus ; how could I dare to do so , see you 
 still so cherish the memory of that unhappy girl , still believe that 
 she have return your affection ? have I say to you what I know   but not 
 till after her death , as to her relation with Audley Egerton - " 

 " well ? you falter ; go on ; have you do so ? " 

 " would you have feel no desire for revenge ? might there not have be 
 strife between you , danger , bloodshe ? Harley , Harley ! be not such 
 silence pardonable in a mother ? and why deprive you too of the only 
 friend you seem to prize ; who alone have some influence over you ; who 
 concur with I in the prayer and hope , that some day you would find 
 a live partner worthy to replace this lose delusion , arouse your 
 faculty , - be the ornament your youth promise to your country ? for you 
 wrong Audley , - indeed you do ! " 

 " wrong he ! ah , let I not do that . proceed . " 

 " I do not excuse he his rivalship , nor his first concealment of it . but 
 believe I , since then , his genuine remorse , his anxious tenderness for 
 your welfare , his dread of lose your friendship - " 

 " stop ! it be doubtless Audley Egerton who induce you yourself to 
 conceal what you call his ' relation ' with she whom I can now so calmly 
 name , - Leonora Avenel ? " 

 " it be so , in truth ; and from motif that - " 

 " enough ! let I hear no more . " 

 " but you will not think too sternly of what be past ? you be about to 
 form new tie . you can not be wild and wicked enough to meditate what 
 your brow seem to threaten . you can not dream of revenge , - risk Audley 's 
 life or your own ? " 

 " tut , tut , tut ! what cause here for duel ? single combat be out of 
 date ; civilized man do not slay each other with sword and pistol . tut ! 
 revenge ! do it look like revenge , that one object which bring I 
 hither be to request my father 's permission to charge myself with the 
 care of Audley Egerton 's election ? what he value most in the world be 
 his political position ; and here his political existence be at stake . 
 you know that I have have through life the character of a weak , easy , 
 somewhat over - generous man . such man be not revengeful . hold ! you lay 
 your hand on my arm , - I know the magic of that light touch , Mother ; 
 but its power over I be go . Countess of Lansmere , hear I ! ever from 
 infancy   save in that frantic passion for which I now despise myself , I 
 have obey you , I trust , as a duteous son . now , our relative position 
 be somewhat altered . I have the right to exact - I will not say to 
 command - the right which wrong and injury bestow upon all man . Madam , 
 the injured man have prerogative that rival those of king . I now 
 call upon you to question I no more ; not again to breathe the name of 
 Leonora Avenel , unless I invite the subject ; and not to inform Audley 
 Egerton by a hint , by a breath , that I have discover - what shall I 
 call it ? - his ' pardonable deceit . ' promise I this , by your affection 
 as mother , and on your faith as gentlewoman ; or I declare solemnly , that 
 never in life will you look upon my face again . " 

 Haughty and imperious though the countess be , her spirit quail before 
 Harley 's brow and voice . 

 " be this my son , - this my gentle Harley ? " she say falteringly . " oh , put 
 your arm round my neck ; let I feel that I have not lose my child ! " 

 Harley look soften , but he do not obey the pathetic prayer ; 
 nevertheless , he hold out his hand , and turn away his face , say , in 
 a milder voice , " have I your promise ? " 

 " you have , you have ; but on condition that there pass no word between 
 you and Audley that can end but in the strife which - " 

 " strife ! " interrupt Harley . " I repeat that the idea of challenge and 
 duel between I and my friend from our school day , and on a quarrel 
 that we could explain to no second , would be a burlesque upon all that 
 be grave in the reality of life and feeling . I accept your promise and 
 seal it thus - " 

 he press his lip to his mother 's forehead , and passively receive she 
 embrace . 

 " hush , " he say , withdraw from her arm , " I hear my father 's voice . " 

 Lord Lansmere throw open the door widely , and with a certain 
 consciousness that a door by which an Earl of Lansmere enter ought to 
 be throw open widely . it could not have be open with more majesty 
 if a huissier or officer of the Household have stand on either side . the 
 countess pass by her lord with a light step , and escape . 

 " I be occupy with my architect in design for the new infirmary , of 
 which I shall make a present to our county . I have only just hear that 
 you be here , Harley . what be all this about our fair italian guest ? 
 be she not come back to we ? your mother refer I to you for 
 explanation . " 

 " you shall have they later , my dear father ; at present I can think only 
 of public affair . " 

 " public affair ! they be indeed alarming . I be rejoice to hear you 
 express yourself so worthily . an awful crisis , Harley ! and , gracious 
 Heaven ! I have hear that a low man , who be bear in Lansmere , but make 
 a fortune in America , be about to contest the borough . they tell I he 
 be one of the Avenels , - a bear Blue ; be it possible ? " 

 " I have come here on that business . as a peer you can not , of course , 
 interfere ; but I propose , with your leave , to go down myself to 
 Lansmere , and undertake the superintendence of , the election . it would 
 be well , perhaps , if you be not present ; it would give we more 
 liberty of action . " 

 " my dear Harley , shake hand ; anything you please . you know how I have 
 wish to see you come forward , and take that part in life which become 
 your birth . " 

 " ah , you think I have sadly waste my existence hitherto . " 

 " to be frank with you , yes , Harley , " say the earl , with a pride that 
 be noble in its nature , and not without dignity in its expression . " the 
 more we take from our country , the more we owe to she . from the moment 
 you come into the world as the inheritor of land and honour , you be 
 charge with a trust for the benefit of other , that it degrade one of 
 our order of gentleman not to discharge . " 

 Harley listen with a sombre brow , and make no direct reply . 

 " indeed , " resume the earl , " I would rather you be about to canvass 
 for yourself than for your friend Egerton . but I grant he be an example 
 that it be never too late to follow . why , who that have see you both 
 as youth , notwithstanding Audley have the advantage of be some 
 year your senior - who could have think that he be the one to become 
 distinguished and eminent , and you to degenerate into the luxurious 
 idler , averse to all trouble and careless of all fame ? you , with such 
 advantage , not only of high fortune , but , as every one say , of 
 superior talent ; you , who have then so much ambition , so keen a desire 
 for glory , sleep with Plutarch 's life under your pillow , and only , 
 my wild son , only too much energy . but you be a young man still ; it be 
 not too late to redeem the year you have throw away . " 

 " the year be nothing , - mere date in an almanac ; but the feeling , 
 what can give I back those ? - the hope , the enthusiasm , the - no matter ! 
 feeling do not , help man to rise in the world . Egerton 's feeling be 
 not too lively . what I might have be , leave it to I to remember ; let 
 we talk of the example you set before I , - of Audley Egerton . " 

 " we must get he in , " say the earl , sink his voice into a whisper . 
 " it be of more importance to he than I even think for . but you know 
 his secret . why do you not confide to I frankly the state of his 
 affair ? " 

 " his affair ? do you mean that they be seriously embarrassed ? this 
 interest I much . pray speak ; what do you know ? " 

 " he have discharge the great part of his establishment . that in itself 
 be natural on quit office ; but still it set people talk ; and it 
 have get wind that his estate be not only mortgage for more than they 
 be worth , but that he have be live upon the discount of bill ; in 
 short , he have be too intimate with a man whom we all know by sight , - a 
 man who drive the fine horse in London , and they tell I   but that 
 I can not believe   life in the familiar society of the young puppy 
 he snare to perdition . what be the man 's name ? Levy , be it not ? - yes , 
 Levy . " 

 " I have see Levy with he , " say Harley ; and a sinister joy light up 
 his falcon eye . " Levy - Levy - it be well . " 

 " I hear but the gossip of the club , " resume the earl ; " but they do say 
 that Levy make little disguise of his power over our very distinguished 
 friend , and rather parade it as a merit with our party   and , indeed , 
 with all man - for Egerton have personal friend in every party   that he 
 keep sundry bill lock up in his desk until Egerton be once more safe 
 in parliament . nevertheless if , after all , our friend be to lose his 
 election , and Levy be then to seize on his effect , and proclaim his 
 ruin , it would seriously damage , perhaps altogether destroy , Audley 's 
 political career . " 

 " so I conclude , " say Harley . " a Charles Fox might be a gamester , and 
 a William Pitt be a pauper . but Audley Egerton be not of their giant 
 stature ; he stand so high because he stand upon heap of respectable 
 gold . Audley Egerton , needy and impoverished , out of parliament , and , as 
 the vulgar slang have it , out at elbow , skulk from dun , perhaps in 
 the Bench - " 

 " no , no ; our party would never allow that ; we would subscribe - " 

 " bad than all , live as the pensioner of the party he aspire to 
 lead ! you say truly , his political prospect would be blast . a man 
 whose reputation lie in his outward respectability ! why , people would 
 say that Audley Egerton have be - a solemn lie ; eh , my father ? " 

 " how can you talk with such coolness of your friend ? you need say 
 nothing to interest I in his election - if you mean that . once in 
 parliament , he must soon again be in office , - and learn to live on 
 his salary . you must get he to submit to I the schedule of his 
 liability . I have a head for business , as you know . I will arrange his 
 affair for he . and I will yet bet five to one , though I hate wager , 
 that he will be prime minister in three year . he be not brilliant , it 
 be true ; but just at this crisis we want a safe , moderate , judicious , 
 conciliatory man ; and Audley have so much tact , such experience of the 
 House , such knowledge of the world , and , " add the earl , emphatically 
 sum up his eulogy , " he be so thorough a gentleman ! " 

 " a thorough gentleman , as you say , - the soul of honour ! but , my dear 
 father , it be your hour for riding ; let I not detain you . it be 
 settle , then ; you do not come yourself to Lansmere . you put the house 
 at my disposal , and allow I to invite Egerton , of course , and what 
 other guest I may please ; in short , you leave all to I ? " 

 " certainly ; and if you can not get in your friend , who can ? that borough , 
 it be an awkward , ungrateful place , and have be the plague of my life . 
 so much as I have spend there , too , - so much as I have do to its 
 trade ! " and the earl , with an indignant sigh , leave the room . 

 Harley seat himself deliberately at his writing - table , lean his 
 face on his hand , and look abstractedly into space from under knit 
 and lower brow . 

 Harley L'Estrange be , as we have see , a man singularly tenacious 
 of affection and impression . he be a man , too , whose nature be 
 eminently bold , loyal , and candid ; even the apparent whim and levity 
 which mislead the world , both as to his disposition and his power , 
 might be half ascribe to that open temper which , in its over - contempt 
 for all that seem to savour of hypocrisy , sport with form and 
 ceremonial , and extract humour , sometimes extravagant , sometimes 
 profound , from " the solemn plausibility of the world . " the shock 
 he have now receive smote the very foundation of his mind , and , 
 overthrow all the airier structure which fancy and wit have build 
 upon its surface , leave it clear as a new world for the operation of the 
 dark and more fearful passion . when a man of a heart so loving and 
 a nature so irregularly powerful as Harley 's suddenly and abruptly 
 discover deceit where he have most confide , it be not   as with the 
 calm pupil of that harsh teacher , experience   the mere withdrawal 
 of esteem and affection from the one offender ; it be , that trust in 
 everything seem go ; it be , that the injure spirit look back to the 
 Past , and condemn all its kindly virtue as folly that conduce 
 to its own woe ; and look on to the Future as to a journey beset with 
 smile traitor , whom it must meet with an equal simulation , or 
 crush with a superior force . the guilt of treason to man like these 
 be incalculable , - it rob the world of all the benefit they would 
 otherwise have lavish as they pass ; it be responsible for all the 
 ill that spring from the corruption of nature whose very luxuriance , 
 when the atmosphere be once taint , do but diffuse disease , - even as 
 the malaria settle not over thin and barren soil , not over waste that 
 have be from all time desolate , but over the place in which southern 
 sun have once ripen delightful garden , or the site of city , in 
 which the pomp of palace have pass away . 

 it be not enough that the friend of his youth , the confidant of his 
 love , have betray his trust , - be the secret and successful rival ; not 
 enough that the woman his boyhood have madly idolize , and all the while 
 he have seek her trace with pining , remorseful heart - believe she 
 but elude his suit from the emulation of a kindre generosity , desire 
 rather to sacrifice her own love than to cost to his the sacrifice of 
 all which youth rashly scorn and the world so highly estimate , - not 
 enough that all this while her refuge have be the bosom of another . 
 this be not enough of injury . his whole life have be waste on a 
 delusion ; his faculty and aim , the wholesome ambition of lofty 
 mind , have be arrest at the very onset of fair existence ; his 
 heart corrode by a regret for which there be no cause ; his conscience 
 charge with the terror that his wild chase have urge a too tender 
 victim to the grave , over which he have mourn . what year that might 
 otherwise have be to himself so serene , to the world so useful , have 
 be consume in objectless , barren , melancholy dream ! and all this 
 while to whom have his complaint be utter ? - to the man who know 
 that his remorse be an idle spectre and his faithful sorrow a mocking 
 self - deceit . every thought that could gall man 's natural pride , every 
 remembrance that could ste into revenge a heart that have love too 
 deeply not to be accessible to hate , conspire to goad those madden 
 Furies who come into every temple which be once desecrate by the 
 presence of the evil passion . in that sullen silence of the soul , 
 vengeance take the form of justice . change though his feeling towards 
 Leonora Avenel be , the story of her grief and her wrong embitter 
 still more his wrath against his rival . the fragment of her memoir leave 
 naturally on Harley 's mind the conviction that she have be the victim 
 of an infamous fraud , the dupe of a false marriage . his idol have not 
 only be steal from the altar , - it have be sully by the sacrifice ; 
 break with remorseless hand , and thrust into dishonour clay ; 
 mutilate , defame ; its very memory a thing of contempt to he who have 
 ravish it from worship . the live Harley and the dead Nora - both 
 call aloud to their joint despoiler , " restore what thou hast take 
 from we , or pay the forfeit ! " 

 thus , then , during the interview between Helen and Leonard , thus Harley 
 L'Estrange sit alone ! and as a rude irregular lump of steel , when 
 wheel round into rapid motion , assume the form of the circle it 
 describe , so his iron purpose , hurry on by his relentless passion , 
 fill the space into which he gaze with optical delusion , scheme 
 after scheme revolve and consummate the circle that clasp a foe . 




 CHAPTER XV . 

 the entrance of a servant , announce a name which Harley , in the 
 absorption of his gloomy revery , do not hear , be follow by that of a 
 person on whom he lift his eye in the cold and haughty surprise 
 with which a man much occupy greet and rebuke the intrusion of an 
 unwelcome stranger . 

 " it be so long since your Lordship have see I , " say the visitor , with 
 mild dignity , " that I can not wonder you do not recognize my person , and 
 have forget my name . " 

 " Sir , " answer Harley , with an impatient rudeness , ill in harmony with 
 the urbanity for which he be usually distinguish , - ' sir , your person 
 be strange to I , and your name I do not hear ; but , at all event , I be 
 not now at leisure to attend to you . excuse my plainness . " 

 " yet pardon I if I still linger . my name be Dale . I be formerly curate 
 at Lansmere ; and I would speak to your Lordship in the name and the 
 memory of one once dear to you , - Leonora Avenel . " 

 HARLEY   after a short pause . - " Sir , I can not conjecture your business . 
 but be seat . I remember you now , though year have alter both , and 
 I have since hear much in your favour from Leonard Fairfield . still let 
 I pray , that you will be brief . " 

 MR . DALE . - " may I assume at once that you have divine the parentage 
 of the young man you call Fairfield ? when I listen to his grateful 
 praise of your beneficence , and mark with melancholy pleasure 
 the reverence in which he hold you , my heart swell within I . I 
 acknowledge the mysterious force of nature . " 

 HARLEY . - " Force of nature ! you talk in riddle . " 

 MR . DALE   indignantly . - " oh , my Lord , how can you so disguise your 
 well self ? surely in Leonard Fairfield you have long since recognize 
 the son of Nora Avenel ? " 

 Harley pass his hand over his face . " ah , " think he , ' she live to 
 bear a son then , - a son to Egerton ! Leonard be that son . I should have 
 know it by the likeness , by the fond foolish impulse that move I to 
 he . this be why he confide to I these fearful memoir . he seek his 
 father , - he shall find he . " 

 MR . DALE   mistake the cause of Harley 's silence . - " I honour your 
 compunction , my Lord . oh , let your heart and your conscience continue to 
 speak to your worldly pride . " 

 HARLEY . - " my compunction , heart , conscience ! Mr. Dale , you insult I ! " 

 MR . DALE   sternly . - " not so ; I be fulfil my mission , which bid I 
 rebuke the sinner . Leonora Avenel speak in I , and command the guilty 
 father to acknowledge the innocent child ! " 

 Harley half rise , and his eye literally flash fire ; but he calm his 
 anger into irony . " ha ! " say he , with a sarcastic smile , ' so you suppose 
 that I be the perfidious seducer of Nora Avenel , - that I be the callous 
 father of the child who come into the world without a name . very well , 
 sir , take these assumption for grant , what be it you demand from I 
 on behalf of this young man ? " 

 " I ask from you his happiness , " reply Mr. Dale , imploringly ; and 
 yield to the compassion with which Leonard inspire he , and 
 persuade that Lord L'Estrange feel a father 's love for the boy whom 
 he have save from the whirlpool of London , and guide to safety and 
 honourable independence , he here , with simple eloquence , narrate all 
 Leonard 's feeling for Helen , - his silent fidelity to her image , though 
 a child 's , his love when he again behold she as a woman , the modest 
 fear which the parson himself have combat , the recommendation that Mr. 
 Dale have force upon he , to confess his affection to Helen , and plead 
 his cause . " anxious , as you may believe , for his success , " continue 
 the parson , " I wait without your gate till he come from Miss Digby 's 
 presence . and oh , my Lord , have you but see his face ! - such emotion and 
 such despair ! I could not learn from he what have pass . he escape 
 from I and rush away . all that I could gather be from a few 
 break word , and from those word I form the conjecture   it may be 
 erroneous   that the obstacle to his happiness be not in Helen 's heart , 
 my Lord , but seem to I as if it be in yourself . therefore , when he 
 have vanish from my sight , I take courage , and come at , once to you . 
 if he be your son , and Helen Digby be your ward , - she herself an orphan , 
 dependent on your bounty , - why should they be sever ? equal in year , 
 unite by early circumstance , congenial , it seem , in simple habit and 
 refined taste , - what should hinder their union , unless it be the want 
 of fortune ? and all man know your wealth , none ever question your 
 generosity . my Lord , my Lord , your look freeze I . if I have offend , 
 do not visit my offence on he , - on Leonard ! " 

 " and so , " say Harley , still control his rage , ' so this boy - whom , 
 as you say , I save from that pitiless world which have engulf many 
 a nobler genius - so , in return for all , he have seek to rob I of the 
 last affection , poor and lukewarm though it be , that remain to I 
 in life ? he presume to lift his eye to my affianced bride ! he ! and for 
 aught I know , steal from I her live heart , and leave to I her icy 
 hand ! " 

 " oh , my Lord , your affianced bride ! I never dream of this . I implore 
 your pardon . the very thought be so terrible , so unnatural ! the son to 
 woo the father 's ! oh , what sin have I fall into ! the sin be mine , - I 
 urge and persuade he to it . he be ignorant as myself . forgive he , 
 forgive he ! " 

 " Mr. Dale , " say Harley , rise , and extend his hand , which the 
 poor parson feel himself - unworthy to take , - " Mr. Dale , you be a good 
 man , - if , indeed , this universe of liar contain some man who do not 
 cheat our judgment when we deem he honest . allow I only to ask why you 
 consider Leonard Fairfield to be my son . " 

 " be not your youthful admiration for poor Nora evident to I ? remember 
 I be a frequent guest at Lansmere Park ; and it be so natural that you , 
 with all your brilliant gift , should captivate her refined fancy , her 
 affectionate heart . " 

 " natural - you think so , - go on . " 

 " your mother , as become she , separate you . it be not unknown to I 
 that you still cherish a passion which your rank forbid to be lawful . 
 poor girl ! she leave the roof of her protectress , Lady Jane . nothing 
 be know of she till she come to her father 's house to give birth to a 
 child , and die . and the same day that dawn on her corpse , you hurry 
 from the place . ah , no doubt your conscience smote you ; you have never 
 return to Lansmere since . " 

 Harley 's breast heave , he wave his hand ; the parson resume , 

 " whom could I suspect but you ? I make inquiry : they confirm my 
 suspicion . " 

 " perhaps you inquire of my friend , Mr. Egerton ? he be with I 
 when - when - as you say , I hurry from the place . " 

 " I do , my Lord . " 

 " and he ? " 

 " deny your guilt ; but still , a man of honour so nice , of heart so 
 feeling , could not feign readily . his denial do not deceive I . " 

 " honest man ! " say Harley ; and his hand grip at the breast over which 
 still rustle , as if with a ghostly sigh , the record of the dead . " he 
 know she have leave a son , too ? " 

 " he do , my Lord ; of course , I tell he that . " 

 " the son whom I find starve in the street of London ! Mr. Dale , 
 as you see , your word move I very much . I can not deny that he who 
 wrong , it may be with no common treachery , that young mother - for Nora 
 Avenel be not one to be lightly seduce into error - " 

 " indeed , no ! " 

 " and who then think no more of the offspring of her anguish and 
 his own crime - I can not deny that that man deserve some 
 chastisement , - should render some atonement . be I not right here ? answer 
 with the plain speech which become your sacred calling . " 

 " I can not say otherwise , my Lord , " reply the parson , pity what 
 appear to he such remorse . " but if he repent - " 

 " enough , " interrupt Harley . " I now invite you to visit I at Lansmere ; 
 give I your address , and I will apprise you of the day on which I will 
 request your presence . Leonard Fairfield shall find a father - I be 
 about to say , worthy of himself . for the rest - stay ; reseat yourself . 
 for the rest " - and again the sinister smile break from Harley 's eye and 
 lip - " I will not yet say whether I can , or ought to , resign to a young 
 and fair suitor the lady who have accept my own hand . I have 
 no reason yet to believe that she prefer he . but what think you , 
 meanwhile , of this proposal ? Mr. Avenel wish his nephew to contest 
 the borough of Lansmere , have urge I to obtain the young man 's consent . 
 true , that he may thus endanger the seat of Mr. Audley Egerton . what 
 then ? Mr. Audley Egerton be a great man , and may find another seat ; that 
 should not stand in the way . let Leonard obey his uncle . if he win the 
 election , why , he will be a more equal match , in the world 's eye , for 
 Miss Digby , that is , should she prefer he to myself ; and if she do not , 
 still , in public life , there be a cure for all private sorrow . that be 
 a maxim of Mr. Audley Egerton 's ; and he , you know , be a man not only 
 of the nice honour , but the deep worldly wisdom . do you like my 
 proposition ? " 

 " it seem to I most considerate , most generous . " 

 " then you shall take to Leonard the line I be about to write . " 

  LORD L'ESTRANGE to LEONARD FAIRFIELD . 

  I have read the memoir you intrust to I . I will follow up all 
  the clew that it give I . meanwhile I request you to suspend all 
  question ; forbear all reference to a subject which , as you may well 
  conjecture , be fraught with painful recollection to myself . at 
  this moment , too , I be compel to concentre my thought upon 
  affair of a public nature , and yet which may sensibly affect 
  yourself . there be reason why I urge you to comply with your 
  uncle 's wish , and stand for the borough of Lansmere at the 
  approach election . if the exquisite gratitude of your nature so 
  overrate what I may have do for you that you think you owe I 
  some obligation , you will richly repay they on the day in which I 
  bear you hail as member for Lansmere . rely on that generous 
  principle of self - sacrifice , which actuate all your conduct , 
  I shall count upon your surrender your preference to private 
  life , and enter the arena of that noble ambition which have 
  confer such dignity on the name of my friend Audley Egerton . he , 
  it be true , will be your opponent ; but he be too generous not to 
  pardon my zeal for the interest of a youth whose career I be vain 
  enough to think that I have aid . and as Mr. Randal Leslie stand 
  in coalition with Egerton , and Mr. Avenel believe that two 
  candidate of the same party can not both succeed , the result may be 
  to the satisfaction of all the feeling which I entertain for Audley 
  Egerton , and for you , who , I have reason to think , will emulate his 
  title to my esteem . 

  your , L'ESTRANGE . 

 " there , Mr. Dale , " say Harley , seal his letter , and give it into 
 the parson 's hand , - " there , you shall deliver this note to your friend . 
 but no ; upon second thought , since he do not yet know of your visit 
 to I , it be good that he should be still in ignorance of it . for should 
 Miss Digby resolve to abide by her present engagement , it be surely 
 kind to save Leonard the pain of learn that you have communicate 
 to I that rivalry he himself have conceal . let all that have pass 
 between we be keep in strict confidence . " 

 " I will obey you , my Lord , " answer the parson , meekly , startle to 
 find that he who have come to arrogate authority be now submit to 
 command ; and all at fault what judgment he could venture to pass upon 
 the man whom he have regard as a criminal , who have not even deny the 
 crime impute to he , yet who now impress the accuse priest with 
 something of that respect which Mr. Dale have never before concede but 
 to Virtue . could he have then but look into the dark and stormy heart , 
 which he twice misread ! 

 " it be well , - very well , " mutter Harley , when the door have close upon 
 the parson . " the viper and the viper 's brood ! so it be this man 's son 
 that I lead from the dire Slough of Despond ; and the son unconsciously 
 imitate the father 's gratitude and honour - ha , ha ! " suddenly the bitter 
 laugh be arrest ; a flash of almost celestial joy dart through the 
 war element of storm and darkness . if Helen return Leonard 's 
 affection , Harley L'Estrange be free ! and through that flash the face 
 of Violante shine upon he as an angel 's . but the heavenly light and 
 the angel face vanish abruptly , swallow up in the black abyss of the 
 rent and tortured soul . 

 " Fool ! " say the unhappy man , aloud , in his anguish - " fool ! what then ? 
 be I free , would it be to trust my fate again to falsehood ? if , in all 
 the bloom and glory of my youth , I fail to win the heart of a village 
 girl ; if , once more deluding myself , it be in vain that I have tend , 
 rear , cherish , some germ of woman 's human affection in the orphan 
 I save from penury , - how look for love in the brilliant princess , whom 
 all the sleek Lotharios of our gaudy world will surround with their 
 homage when once she alight on their sphere ! if perfidy be my 
 fate - what hell of hell , in the thought ! - that a wife might lay she 
 head in my bosom , and - oh , horror ! horror ! no ! I would not accept she 
 hand be it offer , nor believe in her love be it pledge to I . 
 stern soul of mine , wise at last , love never more , - never more believe 
 in truth ! " 




 CHAPTER XVI . 

 as Harley quit the room , Helen 's pale sweet face look forth from a 
 door in the same corridor . she advance towards he timidly . 

 " may I speak with you ? " she say , in almost inaudible accent ; " I have 
 be listen for your footstep . " 

 Harley look at she steadfastly . then , without a word , he follow she 
 into the room she have leave , and close the door . 

 " I , too , " say he , " mean to seek an interview with yourself - but later . 
 you would speak to I , Helen , - say on . ah , child , what mean you ? why 
 this ? " - for Helen be kneel at his foot . 

 " let I kneel , " she say , resist the hand that seek to raise she . 
 " let I kneel till I have explain all , and perhaps win your pardon . 
 you say something the other evening . it have weigh on my heart and my 
 conscience ever since . you say ' that I should have no secret from you ; 
 for that , in our relation to each other , would be deceit . ' I have have 
 a secret ; but oh , believe I ! it be long ere it be clearly visible to 
 myself . you honour I with a suit so far beyond my birth , my merit . 
 you say that I might console and comfort you . at those word , what 
 answer could I give , - I , who owe you so much more than a daughter 's 
 duty ? and I think that my affection be free , - that they would 
 obey that duty . but - but - but - " continue Helen , bow her head still 
 lowlier , and in a voice far fainter - " I deceive myself . I again see he 
 who have be all in the world to I , when the world be so terrible , and 
 then - and then - I tremble . I be terrify at my own memory , my own 
 thought . still I struggle to banish the past , resolutely , firmly . oh , 
 you believe I , do you not ? and I hop to conquer . yet ever since those 
 word of your , I feel that I ought to tell you even of the struggle . 
 this be the first time we have meet since you speak they . and now - now - I 
 have see he again , and - and - though not by a word could she you 
 have deign to woo as your bride encourage hope in another ; though 
 there - there where you now stand - he bid I farewell , and we part 
 as if forever , - yet - yet o Lord L'Estrange ! in return for your 
 rank , wealth , your still nobler gift of nature , what should I 
 bring ? - something more than gratitude , esteem ; reverence , - at least an 
 undivided heart , fill with your image , and your alone . and this I 
 can not give . pardon I , - not for what I say now , but for not say it 
 before . pardon I , o my benefactor , pardon I ! " 

 " Rise , Helen , " say Harley , with relax brow , though still unwilling 
 to yield to one soft and holier emotion . " rise ! " and he lift she up , 
 and draw she towards the light . " let I look at your face . there seem 
 no guile here . these tear be surely honest . if I can not be love , 
 it be my fate , and not your crime . now , listen to I . if you grant I 
 nothing else , will you give I the obedience which the ward owe to the 
 guardian , the child to the parent ? " 

 " yes , oh , yes ! " murmur Helen . 

 " then while I release you from all troth to I , I claim the right to 
 refuse , if I so please it , my assent to the suit of - of the person you 
 prefer . I acquit you of deceit , but I reserve to myself the judgment I 
 shall pass on he . until I myself sanction that suit , will you promise 
 not to recall in any way the rejection which , if I understand you 
 rightly , you have give to it ? " 

 " I promise . " 

 " and if I say to you , ' Helen , this man be not worthy of you ' " 

 " no , no ! do not say that , - I could not believe you . " Harley frown , but 
 resume calmly , " if , then , I say , ' ask I not wherefore , but I forbid 
 you to be the wife of Leonard Fairfield , I what would be your answer ? ' " 

 " ah , my Lord , if you can but comfort he , do with I as you will ! but do 
 not command I to break his heart . " 

 " oh , silly child , " cry Harley , laugh scornfully , " heart be not 
 find in the race from which that man spring . but I take your promise , 
 with its credulous condition . Helen , I pity you . I have be as weak as 
 you , bearded man though I be . some day or other , you and I may live 
 to laugh at the folly at which you weep now . I can give you no other 
 comfort , for I know of none . " 

 he move to the door , and pause at the threshold : " I shall not see you 
 again for some day , Helen . perhaps I may request my mother to join I 
 at Lansmere ; if so , I shall pray you to accompany she . for the present , 
 let all believe that our position be unchanged . the time will soon come 
 when I may - " 

 Helen look up wistfully through her tear . 

 " I may release you from all duty to I , " continue Harley , with 
 grave and severe coldness ; " or I may claim your promise in spite of the 
 condition ; for your lover 's heart will not be break . adieu ! " 




 CHAPTER XVII . 

 as Harley enter London , he come suddenly upon Randal Leslie , who be 
 hurry from Eaton Square , have not only accompany Mr. Avenel 
 in his walk , but go home with he , and spend half the day in that 
 gentleman 's society . he be now on his way to the House of Commons , at 
 which some disclosure as to the day for the dissolution of parliament 
 be expect . 

 " Lord L'Estrange , " say Randal , " I must stop you . I have be to 
 Norwood , and see our noble friend . he have confide to I , of course , 
 all that pass . how can I express my gratitude to you ! by what rare 
 talent , with what signal courage , you have save the happiness - perhaps 
 even the honour - of my plighted bride ! " 

 " your bride ! the duke , then , still hold to the promise you be 
 fortunate enough to obtain from Dr. Riccabocca ? "

 surprised at his magnanimity . " 

 " no ; he be a philosopher , - nothing in he can surprise I . but he seem 
 to think , when I see he , that there be circumstance you might find 
 it hard to explain . " 

 " hard ! nothing so easy . allow I to tender to you the same explanation 
 which satisfy one whom philosophy itself have make as open to truth as 
 he be clear - sighted to imposture . "

 " you be most kind , " answer randal , greatly surprised .

 " my dear Lord L'Estrange , let I shake you by the hand ; for Heaven know 
 when I may see you again , and you have suffer I to assist in one good 
 action . " 

 " Frank Hazeldean , I be pleased indeed to meet you . why do you indulge in 
 that melancholy doubt as to the time when I may see you again ? " 

 " I have just get leave of absence . I be not well , and I be rather 
 hippe , so I shall go abroad for a few week . " 

 in spite of himself , the sombre , brood man feel interest and sympathy 
 in the dejection that be evident in Frank 's voice and countenance .

 himself , - " of course , a dupe ; he be honest and artless - at present . " he 
 press kindly on the arm which he have involuntarily twin within his 
 own . " I conceive how you now grieve , my young friend , " say he ; " but you 
 will congratulate yourself hereafter on what this day seem to you an 
 affliction . " 

 " my dear Lord - " 

 " I be much old than you , but not old enough for such formal ceremony . 
 pray call I L'Estrange . " 

 " thank you ; and I should indeed like to speak to you as a friend . 
 there be a thought on my mind which haunt I . I dare say it be foolish 
 enough , but I be sure you will not laugh at I . you hear what Madame di 
 Negra say to I last night . I have be trifle with and mislead , but I 
 can not forget so soon how dear to I that woman be . I be not go to 
 bore you with such nonsense ; but from what I can understand , her 
 brother be likely to lose all his fortune ; and , even if not , he be a sad 
 scoundrel . I can not bear the thought that she should be so dependent 
 on he , that she may come to want . after all , there must be good in 
 she , - good in she to refuse my hand if she do not love I . a mercenary 
 woman so circumstanced would not have do that . " 

 " you be quite right . but do not torment yourself with such generous 
 fear . Madame di Negra shall not come to want , shall not be dependent on 
 her infamous brother . the first act of the Duke of Serrano , on regain 
 his estate , will be a suitable provision for his kinswoman . I will 
 answer for this . " 

 " you take a load off my mind . I do mean to ask you to intercede with 
 Riccabocca , - that is , the duke   it be so hard to think he can be a 
 duke !   - I , alas ! have nothing in my power to bestow upon Madame di 
 Negra . I may , indeed , sell my commission ; but then I have a debt which 
 I long to pay off , and the sale of the commission would not suffice even 
 for that ; and perhaps my father might be still more angry if I do sell 
 it . well , good - by . I shall now go away happy , - that be , comparatively . 
 one must bear thing like - a man ! " 

 " I should like , however , to see you again before you go abroad . I will 
 call on you . meanwhile , can you tell I the number of one Baron Levy ? he 
 live in this street , I know . " 

 " Levy ! oh , have no dealing with he , I advise , I entreat you ! he be the 
 most plausible , dangerous rascal ; and , for Heaven 's sake ! pray be warn 
 by I , and let nothing entangle you into - a post - OBIT ! " 

 " be re - assured , I be more accustomed to lend money than borrow it ; 
 and as to a post - obit , I have a foolish prejudice against such 
 transaction . " 

 " do not   call it foolish , L'Estrange ; I honour you for it . how I wish I 
 have know you early - so few man of the world be like you . even randal 
 Leslie , who be so faultless in most thing , and never get into a scrape 
 himself , call my own scruple foolish . however - " 

 " stay - randal Leslie ! what ! he advise you to borrow on a post - obit , and 
 probably share the loan with you ? " 

 " oh , no ; not a shilling . " 

 " tell I all about it , Frank . perhaps , as I see that Levy be mix up in 
 the affair , your information may be useful to myself , and put I on my 
 guard in deal with that popular gentleman . " 

 Frank , who somehow or other feel himself quite at home with Harley , 
 and who , with all his respect for Randal Leslie 's talent , have a vague 
 notion that Lord L'Estrange be quite as clever , and , from his year 
 and experience , likely to be a safe and more judicious counsellor , be 
 noway loath to impart the confidence thus press for . 

 he tell Harley of his debt , his first dealing with Levy , the unhappy 
 post - obit into which he have be hurry by the distress of Madame di 
 Negra ; his father 's anger , his mother 's letter , his own feeling of 
 mingle shame and pride , which make he fear that repentance would but 
 seem self - interest , his desire to sell his commission , and let its sale 
 redeem in part the post - obit ; in short , he make what be call a clean 
 breast of it . randal Leslie be necessarily mix up with this recital ; 
 and the subtle cross - questioning of Harley extract far more as to 
 that young diplomatist 's agency in all these melancholy concern than 
 the ingenuous narrator himself be aware of . 

 " so then , " say Harley , " Mr. Leslie assure you of Madame di Negra 's 
 affection , when you yourself doubt of it ? " 

 " yes ; she take he in , even more than she do I . " 

 " simple Mr. Leslie ! and the same kind friend ? - who be relate to you , 
 do you say ? " 

 " his grandmother be a Hazeldean . " 

 " Humph . the same kind relation lead you to believe that you could pay 
 off this bond with the marchesa 's portion , and that he could obtain the 
 consent of your parent to your marriage with that lady ? " 

 " I ought to have know well ; my father 's prejudice against foreigner 
 and Papists be so strong . " 

 " and now Mr. Leslie concur with you , that it be good for you to 
 go abroad , and trust to his intercession with your father . he have 
 evidently , then , gain a great influence over Mr. Hazeldean . " 

 " my father naturally compare I with he , - he so clever , so promising , 
 so regular in his habit , and I such a reckless scapegrace . " 

 " and the bulk of your father 's property be unentaile ; Mr. Hazeldean 
 might disinherit you ? " 

 " I deserve it . I hope he will . " 

 " you have no brother nor sister , - no relation , perhaps , after your 
 parent , near to you than your excellent friend Mr. Randal Leslie ? " 

 " No ; that be the reason he be so kind to I , otherwise I be the last 
 person to suit he . you have no idea how well - inform and clever he 
 be , " add Frank , in a tone between admiration and awe . 

 " my dear Hazeldean , you will take my advice , will you not ? " 

 " certainly . you be too good . " 

 " let all your family , Mr. Leslie include , suppose you to be go 
 abroad ; but stay quietly in England , and within a day 's journey of 
 Lansmere Park . I be oblige to go thither for the approach election . 
 I may ask you to come over . I think I see a way to serve you ; and if so , 
 you will soon hear from I . now , Baron Levy 's number ? " 

 " that be the house with the cabriolet at the door . how such a fellow can 
 have such a horse ! - ' t be out of all keep ! " 

 " not at all ; horse be high - spirit , generous , unsuspicious animal . 
 they never know if it be a rogue who drive they . I have your promise , 
 then , and you will send I your address ? " 

 " I will . strange that I feel more confidence in you than I do even in 
 Randal . do take care of Levy . " 

 Lord L'Estrange and Frank here shake hand , and Frank , with an anxious 
 groan , see L'Estrange disappear within the portal of the sleek 
 destroyer . 




 CHAPTER XVII . 

 Lord L'Estrange follow the spruce servant into Baron Levy 's luxurious 
 study . 

 the baron look greatly amazed at his unexpected visitor ; but he get 
 up , hand a chair to my Lord with a low bow . " this be an honour , " say 
 he . 

 " you have a charming abode here , " say Lord L'Estrange , look round . 
 " very fine bronze , - excellent taste . your reception - room above be , 
 doubtless , a model to all decorator ? " 

 " would your Lordship condescend to see they ? " say Levy , wonder , but 
 flatter . 

 " with the great pleasure . " 

 " light ! " cry Levy , to the servant who answer his bell . " light in 
 the drawing - room , - it be grow dark . " Lord L'Estrange follow the 
 usurer upstairs ; admire everything , - picture , drapery , Sevres china , 
 to the very shape of the downy fauteuil , to the very pattern of the 
 Tournay carpet . recline then on one of the voluptuous sofas , Lord 
 L'Estrange say smilingly , " you be a wise man : there be no advantage in 
 be rich , unless one enjoy one 's rich . " 

 " my own maxim , Lord L'Estrange . " 

 " and it be something , too , to have a taste for good society . small pride 
 would you have , my dear baron , in these room , luxurious though they 
 be , if fill with guest of vulgar exterior and plebeian manner . 
 it be only in the world in which we move that we find person who 
 harmonize , as it be , with the porcelain of Sevres , and these sofa 
 that might have come from Versailles . " 

 " I own , " say Levy , " that I have what some may call a weakness in a 
 parvenu like myself . I have a love for the beau monde . it be indeed a 
 pleasure to I when I receive man like your Lordship . " 

 " but why call yourself a parvenu ? though you be content to honour 
 the name of Levy , we , in society , all know that you be the son of a 
 long - descend english peer . child of love , it be true ; but the Graces 
 smile on those over whose birth Venus preside . Pardon my old - fashion 
 mythological simile , - they go so well with these room - Louis Quinze . " 

 " since you have touch on my birth , " say Levy , his colour rather 
 heightening , not with shame , but with pride , " I do not   deny that it have 
 have some effect on my habit and taste in life . in fact - " 

 " in fact , own that you would be a miserable man , in spite of all your 
 wealth , if the young dandy , who throng to your banquet , be to cut 
 you dead in the street ; if , when your high - step horse stop at 
 your club , the porter shut the door in your face ; if , when you lounge 
 into the opera - pit , handsome dog that you be , each spendthrift rake 
 in ' Fop 's Alley , ' who now wait but the scratch of your pen to endorse 
 billet doux with the charm that can chain to himself for a month some 
 nymph of the Ballet , spin round in a whirlwind of tulle , would 
 shrink from the touch of your condescending forefinger with more dread

 could inspire ; if , reduce to the company of city clerk , parasite 
 lead - captain - " 

 " oh , do not   go on , my dear Lord , " cry Levy , laugh affectedly . 
 " impossible though the picture be , it be really appalling . cut I off 
 from May Fair and St. James 's , and I should go into my strong closet and 
 hang myself . " 

 " and yet , my dear baron , all this may happen if I have the whim just to 
 try ; all this will happen , unless , ere I leave your house , you concede 
 the condition I come here to impose . " 

 " my Lord ! " exclaim Levy , start up , and pull down his waistcoat 
 with nervous passionate finger , " if you be not under my own roof , I 
 would - " 

 " truce with mock heroic . sit down , sir , sit down . I will briefly state 
 my threat , more briefly my condition . you will be scarcely more prolix 
 in your reply . your fortune I can not touch , your enjoyment of it I 
 can destroy . refuse my condition , make I your enemy , - and war to the 
 knife ! I will interrogate all the young dupe you have ruin . I will 
 learn the history of all the transaction by which you have gain the 
 wealth that it please you to spend in court the society and share 
 the vice of man who - go with these room , Louis Quinze . not a roguery 
 of yours shall escape I , down even to your last notable connivance with 
 an italian reprobate for the criminal abduction of an heiress . all 
 these particular I will proclaim in the club to which you have gain 
 admittance , in every club in London which you yet hope to creep into ; 
 all these I will impart to some such authority in the Press as Mr. Henry 
 Norreys ; all these I will , upon the voucher of my own name , have so 
 publish in some journal of repute , that you must either tacitly 
 submit to the revelation that blast you , or bring before a court of 
 law action that will convert accusation into evidence . it be but by 
 sufferance that you be now in society ; you be exclude when one man 
 like I come forth to denounce you . you try in vain to sneer at my 
 menace - your white lip show your terror . I have rarely in life draw 
 any advantage from my rank and position ; but I be thankful that they 
 give I the power to make my voice respect and my exposure triumphant . 
 now , Baron Levy , will you go into your strong closet and hang yourself , 
 or will you grant I my very moderate condition ? you be silent . I will 
 relieve you , and state those condition . until the general election , 
 about to take place , be conclude , you will obey I to the letter in 
 all that I enjoin , - no demur and no scruple . and the first proof 
 of obedience I demand be , your candid disclosure of all Mr. Audley 
 Egerton 's pecuniary affair . " 

 " have my client , Mr. Egerton , authorize you to request of I that 
 disclosure ? " 

 " on the contrary , all that pass between we you will conceal from your 
 client . " 

 " you would save he from ruin ? your trusty friend , Mr. Egerton ! " say 
 the baron , with a livid sneer . 

 " wrong again , Baron Levy . if I would save he from ruin , you be 
 scarcely the man I should ask to assist I . " 

 " ah , I guess . you have learn how he - " 

 " guess nothing , but obey in all thing . let we descend to your business 
 room . " 

 Levy say not a word until he have reconducte his visitor into his den 
 of destruction , all gleam with spoliaria in rosewood . then he say 
 this : " if , Lord L'Estrange , you seek but revenge on Audley Egerton , you 
 need not have utter those threat . I too - hate the man . " 

 Harley look at he wistfully , and the nobleman feel a pang that he 
 have debase himself into a single feeling which the usurer could 
 share . nevertheless , the interview appear to close with satisfactory 
 arrangement , and to produce amicable understanding . for as the baron 
 ceremoniously follow Lord L'Estrange through the hall , his noble 
 visitor say , with marked affability , 

 " then I shall see you at Lansmere with Mr. Egerton , to assist in 
 conduct his election . it be a sacrifice of your time worthy of your 
 friendship ; not a step far , I beg . Baron , I have the honour to wish 
 you good - evening . " 

 as the street door open on Lord L'Estrange he again find himself 
 face to face with Randal Leslie , whose hand be already lift to the 
 knocker . 

 " ha , Mr. Leslie ! - you too a client of Baron Levy 's , - a very useful , 
 accommodate man . " 

 randal star and stammer . " I come in haste from the House of Commons 
 on Mr. Egerton 's business . do not   you hear the newspaper vendor cry

 " we be prepared . Levy himself consent to give we the aid of his

 indifferent to Randal Leslie . 

 " how be this ? " cry Randal . " I come to tell you first of Peschiera 's 
 utter failure , the ridiculous coxcomb , and I meet at your door the 
 last man I think to find there , - the man who foil we all , Lord 
 L'Estrange . what bring he to you ? ah , perhaps his interest in 
 Egerton 's election ? " 

 " yes , " say Levy , sulkily . " I know all about Peschiera . I can not talk to 
 you now ; I must make arrangement for go to Lansmere . " 

 " but do not   forget my purchase from Thornhill . I shall have the money 
 shortly from a surer source than Peschiera . " 

 " the squire ? " 

 " or a rich father - in - law . " 

 in the mean while , as Lord L'Estrange enter Bond Street , his ear be 
 stun by vociferous cry from the Stentors employ by " Standard , " 
 " Sun , " and " Globe , " 

  - " Great News ! Dissolution of Parliament - Great News ! " the gas - lamp 
 be light ; a brown fog be gather over the street , blend

 lounger usually careless as to politic be talk eagerly and

 " I have constitute myself a fate , " he say inly ; " let the god be but

 lonely stride that none may follow , - 

  " ' oh , for a lodge in some vast wilderness . ' 

 " how weary I be of this world of man ! " and again the cry " Great 
 News - National Crisis - Dissolution of Parliament - Great News ! " ring 
 through the jostle throng . three man , arm - in - arm , brush by Harley ,

 office , and vain of the power to make and unmake government . 

 " you be the only man to lead we , Egerton , " say this last personage . 
 " do but secure your seat , and as soon as this popular fever have pass 
 away , you must be something more than the leader of Opposition , - you 
 must be the first man in England . " 

 " not a doubt of that , " chime in the fellow ex - minister , a worthy man ,

 L'Estrange 's friendship . "

 lay it upon Nora 's memoir . 

 " what could we do in parliament without you ? " say the great proprietor , 
 almost piteously . 

 " rather what could I do without parliament ? public life be the only 
 existence I own . Parliament be all in all to I . but we may cross now . " 

 Harley 's eye glitter cold as it follow the tall form of the 
 statesman , tower high above all other passer - by . " ay , " he mutter ,
