 CHAPTER XIX . 

 the scene be at Lansmere Park , - a spacious pile , commence in the reign 
 of Charles II . ; enlarge and alter in the reign of Anne . Brilliant 
 interval in the history of our National Manners , when even the courtier 
 dread to be dull , and Sir Fopling raise himself on tiptoe to catch 
 the ear of a wit ; when the name of Devonshire and Dorset , Halifax and 
 Carteret , Oxford and Bolingbroke , unite themselves , brotherlike , with 
 those of Hobbes and of Dryden , of Prior and Bentley , of Arbuthnot , Gay , 
 Pope , and Swift ; and still , wherever we turn , to recognize some ideal of 
 great Lord or fine Gentleman , the Immortals of Literature stand by his 
 side . 

 the wall of the room at Lansmere be cover with the portrait of 
 those who illustrate that time which Europe call the Age of Louis XIV . 
 a L'Estrange , who have live through the reign of four english prince 
  and with no mean importance through all   have collect those likeness 
 of noble contemporary . as you pass through the chamber - open one 
 on the other in that pomp of parade introduce with Charles II . from 
 the palace of France , and retain its mode till Versailles and the 
 Trianon pass , themselves , out of date - you feel you be in excellent 
 company . what saloon of our day , demean to tail coat and white 
 waistcoat , have that charm of high breeding which speak out from 
 the canvas of Kneller and Jervis , Vivien and Rigaud ? and withal , 
 notwithstanding lace and brocade - the frippery of artificial 
 costume - still those who give interest or charm to that day look from 
 their portrait like man , - rake or debonair , if you will , never 
 mince nor feminine . can we say as much of the portrait of Lawrence ? 
 gaze there on fair Marlborough ; what delicate perfection of feature , 
 yet how easy in boldness , how serene in the conviction of power ! so 
 fair and so tranquil he might have look through the cannon reek at 
 Ramillies and Blenheim , suggest to Addison the image of an angel 
 of war . ah , there , Sir Charles Sedley , the Lovelace of wit ! note 
 that strong jaw and marked brow ; do you not recognize the courtier who 
 scorn to ask one favour of the king with whom he live as an equal , 
 and who stretch forth the right hand of man to hurl from a throne the 
 king who have make his daughter - a countess ? 

   Sedley be so tenacious of his independence that when his affair 
  be most embarrassed , he refuse all pecuniary aid from Charles II . 
  his bitter sarcasm , in vindication of the part he take in the 
  deposition of James II . , who have corrupt his daughter , and make 
  her Countess of Dorchester , be well know . " as the king have make my 
  daughter a countess , the least I can do , in common gratitude , be to 
  assist in make his Majesty 's daughter - a queen ! " 

 perhaps , from his childhood thus surround by the haunt face - that 
 speak of their age as they look from the wall - that age and those 
 portrait be not without influence on the character of Harley 
 L'Estrange . the whim and the daring , the passion for letter and 
 reverence for genius , the mixture of levity and strength , the polish 
 saunter indolence , or the elastic readiness of energy once call 
 into action , - all might have find their prototype in the life which 
 those portrait rekindle . the deep sentiment , the more earnest 
 nature , which in Harley L'Estrange be commingle with the attribute 
 common to a former age , - these , indeed , be of his own . our age so 
 little comprehend , while it colour we from its atmosphere ! so full of 
 mysterious and profound emotion , which our ancestor never know ! - will 
 those emotion be understand by our descendant ? 

 in this stately house be now assemble , as Harley 's guest , many of 
 the more important personage whom the slow length of this story have 
 make familiar to the reader . the two candidate for the borough in the 
 true blue interest , - Audley Egerton and Randal Leslie ; and Levy , - chief 
 among the baron to whom modern society grant a seignorie of pillage , 
 which , have a baron of old ever venture to arrogate , burgess and 
 citizen , socman and bocman , villein and churl , would have burn he 
 alive in his castle ; the Duke di Serrano , still fondly cling to his 
 title of Doctor and pet name of Riccabocca ; Jemima , not yet with the 
 air of a duchess , but rob in very thick silk , as the chrysalis state 
 of a duchess ; Violante , too , be there , sadly against her will , and 
 shrink as much as possible into the retirement of her own chamber . 
 the Countess of Lansmere have desert her lord , in order to receive the 
 guest of her son ; my lord himself , ever bent on be of use in some 
 part of his country , and strive hard to distract his interest from 
 his plague of a borough , have go down into Cornwall to inquire into the 
 social condition of certain troglodyte who work in some mine which 
 the earl have lately have the misfortune to wring from the Court of 
 Chancery , after a lawsuit commence by his grandfather ; and a Blue Book , 
 issue in the past session by order of parliament , have especially 
 quote the troglodyte thus devolve on the earl as biped who be 
 in considerable ignorance of the sun , and have never be know to wash 
 their foot since the day when they come into the world , - their world 
 underground , chip off from the Bottomless Pit ! 

 with the countess come Helen Digby , of course ; and Lady Lansmere , who 
 have hitherto be so civilly cold to the wife elect of her son , have , 
 ever since her interview with Harley at Knightsbridge , clung to Helen 
 with almost a caress fondness . the stern countess be tame by fear ; 
 she feel that her own influence over Harley be go ; she trust to 
 the influence of Helen - in case of what ? - ay , what ? it be because 
 the danger be not clear to she that her bold spirit tremble : 
 superstition , like suspicion , be " as bat among bird , and fly by 
 twilight . " Harley have ridicule the idea of challenge and strife between 
 Audley and himself ; but still Lady Lansmere dread the fiery emotion 
 of the last , and the high spirit and austere self - respect which be 
 proverbial to the first . involuntarily she strengthen her intimacy 
 with Helen . in case her alarm should appear justify , what mediator 
 could be so persuasive in appease the angry passion , as one whom 
 courtship and betrothal sanctify to the gentle ? 

 on arrive at Lansmere , the countess , however , feel somewhat relieved . 
 Harley have receive she , if with a manner less cordial and tender 
 than have hitherto distinguish it , still with easy kindness and calm 
 self - possession . his bearing towards Audley Egerton still more reassure 
 she : it be not mark by an exaggeration of familiarity or friendship , 
 which would at once have excite her apprehension of some sinister 
 design , - nor ; on the other hand , do it betray , by covert sarcasm , an 
 ill - suppress resentment . it be exactly what , under the circumstance , 
 would have be natural to a man who have receive an injury from an 
 intimate friend , which , in generosity or discretion , he resolve to 
 overlook , but which those aware of it could just perceive have cool or 
 alienate the former affection . indefatigably occupy himself with 
 all the detail of the election , Harley have fair pretext for absent 
 himself from Audley , who , really look very ill , and almost wear out , 
 plead indisposition as an excuse for dispense with the fatigue of 
 a personal canvass , and , pass much of his time in his own apartment , 
 leave all the preparation for contest to his more active friend . it 
 be not till he have actually arrive at Lansmere that Audley become 
 acquaint with the name of his principal opponent . Richard Avenel ! the 
 brother of Nora ! rise up from obscurity , thus to stand front to front 
 against he in a contest on which all his fate be cast . Egerton 
 quail as before an appoint avenger . he would fain have retire from 
 the field ; he speak to Harley . 

 " how can you support all the painful remembrance which the very name of 
 my antagonist must conjure up ? " 

 " do you not tell I , " answer Harley , " to strive against such 
 remembrance , - to look on they as sickly dream ? I be prepared to brave 
 they . can you be more sensitive than I ? " 

 Egerton durst not say more . he avoid all further reference to the 
 subject . the strife rag around he , and he shut himself out from 
 it , - shut himself up in solitude with his own heart . strife enough 
 there ! once , late at night , he steal forth and repair to Nora 's grave . 
 he stand there , amidst the rank grass and under the frosty starlight , 
 long , and in profound silence . his whole past life seem to rise before 
 he ; and , when he regain his lonely room , and strove to survey the 
 future , still he could behold only that past and that grave . 

 in thus decline all active care for an election , to his prospect so 
 important , Audley Egerton be consider to have excuse , not only in 
 the state of his health , but in his sense of dignity . a statesman so 
 eminent , of opinion so well know , of public service so incontestable , 
 might well be spar the personal trouble that fall upon obscurer 
 candidate . and besides , accord to current report , and the judgment 
 of the Blue Committee , the return of Mr. Egerton be secure . but 
 though Audley himself be thus indulgently treat , Harley and the Blue 
 Committee take care to inflict double work upon Randal . that active 
 young spirit find ample material for all its restless energy . randal 
 Leslie be keep on his leg from sunrise to starlight . there do not 
 exist in the Three Kingdoms a constituency more fatiguing to a candidate 
 than that borough of Lansmere . as soon as you leave the High Street , 
 wherein , accord to immemorial usage , the blue canvasser be first 
 lead , in order to put he into spirit for the toil that await he 
  delectable , propitious , constitutional High Street , in which at least 
 two - third of the elector , opulent tradesman employ at the Park , 
 always vote for " my lord 's man , " and hospitably prepare wine and cake 
 in their tidy back - parlours !   - as soon as you quit this stronghold 
 of the party , labyrinth of lane and defile stretch away into the 
 farth horizon ; level ground be find nowhere ; it be all up hill and 
 down hill , - now rough , craggy pavement that blister the foot , and at 
 the very first tread upon which all latent corn shake prophetically ; 
 now deep , muddy rut , into which you sink ankle - deep , ooze slush 
 creep into the pore , and moisten the way for catarrh , rheum , 
 cough , sore throat , bronchitis , and phthisis ; black sewer and drain 
 Acherontian , run before the threshold , and so fill the home 
 behind with effluvia , that , while one hand clasp the grimy paw of 
 the voter , the other instinctively guard from typhus and cholera your 
 abhorrent nose . not in those day have mankind ever hear of a sanitary 
 reform ! and , to judge of the slow progress which that reform seem 
 to make , sewer and drain would have be much the same if they have . 
 scot - and - lot voter be the independent elector of Lansmere , with the 
 additional franchise of Freemen . universal suffrage could scarcely more 
 efficiently swamp the franchise of man who care a straw what become of 
 Great Britain ! with all Randal Leslie 's profound diplomacy , all his 
 art in talk over , deceive , and   to borrow Dick Avenel 's vernacular 
 phrase   " humbug " educate man , his eloquence fall flat upon mind 
 invulnerable to appeal , whether to State or to Church , to Reform or 
 to Freedom . to catch a Scot - and - lot voter by such frivolous 
 argument - randal Leslie might as well have try to bring down a 
 rhinoceros by a pop - gun charge with split pea ! the young man who so 
 firmly believe that " knowledge be power " be greatly disgusted . it be 
 here the ignorance that foil he . when he get hold of a man with some 
 knowledge , Randal be pretty sure to trick he out of a vote . 

 nevertheless , Randal Leslie walk and talk on , with most creditable 
 perseverance . the Blue Committee allow that he be an excellent 
 canvasser . they conceive a liking for he , mingle with pity . for , 
 though sure of Egerton 's return , they regard Randal 's as out of the 
 question . he be merely there to keep split vote from go to the 
 opposite side ; to serve his patron , the ex - minister ; shake the paw , and 
 smell the smell which the ex - minister be too great a man to shake 
 and to smell . but , in point of fact , none of that Blue Committee know 
 anything of the prospect of the election . Harley receive all the 
 report of each canvass - day . Harley keep the canvass - book lock up from 
 all eye but his own , or it might be Baron Levy 's , as Audley Egerton 's 
 confidential , if not strictly professional adviser , Baron Levy , the 
 millionaire , have long since retire from all acknowledge profession . 
 randal , however - close , observant , shrewd - perceive that he himself 
 be much strong than the Blue Committee believe ; and , to his infinite 
 surprise , he owe that strength to Lord L'Estrange 's exertion on 
 his behalf . for though Harley , after the first day , on which he 
 ostentatiously show himself in the High Street , do not openly canvass 
 with Randal , yet when the report be bring in to he , and he see the 
 name of the voter who give one vote to Audley , and withhold the other 
 from Randal , he would say to randal , dead beat as that young gentleman 
 be , " slip out with I , the moment dinner be over , and before you go the 
 round of the public - house ; there be some voter we must get for you 
 to - night . " and sure enough a few kindly word from the popular heir 
 of the Lansmere barony usually gain over the elector , from whom , 
 though Randal have prove that all England depend on their vote in his 
 favour , Randal would never have extract more than a " Wu'll , I shall 
 waute gin the Dauy coome ! " nor be this all that Harley do for the 
 young candidate . if it be quite clear that only one vote could be win 
 for the Blues , and the other be pledge to the Yellows , Harley would 
 say , " then put it down to Mr. Leslie , " - a request the more readily 
 concede , since Audley Egerton be consider so safe by the Blues , and 
 alone worth a fear by the Yellows . 

 thus Randal , who keep a snug little canvass - book of his own , become 
 more and more convinced that he have a well chance than Egerton , even 
 without the furtive aid he expect from Avenel ; and he could only 
 account for Harley 's peculiar exertion in his favour by suppose that 
 Harley , unpractise in election , and deceive by the Blue Committee , 
 believe Egerton to be perfectly safe , and seek , for the honour of the 
 family interest , to secure both the seat . 

 Randal 's public care thus deprive he of all opportunity of press 
 his courtship on Violante ; and , indeed , if ever he do find a moment 
 in which he could steal to her reluctant side , Harley be sure to seize 
 that very moment to send he off to canvass an hesitate freeman , or 
 harangue in some public - house . 

 Leslie be too acute not to detect some motive hostile to his wooing , 
 however plausibly veil in the guise of zeal for his election , in this 
 officiousness of Harley 's . but Lord L'Estrange 's manner to Violante be 
 so little like that of a jealous lover , and he be so well aware of her 
 engagement to Randal , that the latter abandon the suspicion he have 
 before conceive , that Harley be his rival . and he be soon lead to 
 believe that Lord L'Estrange have another , more disinterested , and 
 less formidable motive for thus stint his opportunity to woo the 
 heiress . 

 " Mr. Leslie , " say Lord L'Estrange , one day , " the duke have confide to 
 I his regret at his daughter 's reluctance to ratify his own promise ; 
 and know the warm interest I take in her welfare , for his sake and 
 her own ; believe , also , that some service to herself , as well as 
 to the father she so love , give I a certain influence over her 
 inexperienced judgment , he have even request I to speak a word to she 
 in your behalf ! " 

 " ah , if you would ! " say Randal , surprised . 

 " you must give I the power to do so . you be oblige enough to 
 volunteer to I the same explanation which you give to the duke , his 
 satisfaction with which induce he to renew or confirm the promise of 
 his daughter 's hand . should those explanation content I , as they do 
 he , I hold the duke bind to fulfil his engagement , and I be convince 
 that his daughter would , in that case , not be inflexible to your suit . 
 but , till such explanation be give , my friendship for the father , 
 and my interest in the child , do not allow I to assist a cause which , 
 however , at present suffer little by delay . " 

 " pray , listen at once to those explanation . " 

 " Nay , Mr. Leslie , I can now only think of the election . as soon as that 
 be over , rely on it you shall have the ample opportunity to dispel any 
 doubt which your intimacy with Count di Peschiera and Madame di Negra 
 may have suggest . - a propos of the election , here be a list of voter 
 you must see at once in Fish Lane . do not   lose a moment . " 

 in the mean while , Richard Avenel and Leonard have take up their 
 quarter in the hotel appropriate to the candidate for the yellow ; 
 and the canvass on that side be prosecute with all the vigour which 
 might be expect from operation conduct by Richard Avenel , and 
 back by the popular feeling . 

 the rival party meet from time to time in the street and lane , in 
 all the pomp of war , - banner streaming , fife resound   for band and 
 colour be essential proof of public spirit , and indispensable 
 item in a candidate 's bill , in those good old day . when they thus 
 encounter , very distant bow be exchange between the respective 
 chief ; but randal , contrive ever to pass close to Avenel , have ever 
 the satisfaction of perceive that gentleman 's countenance contract 
 into a knowing wink , as much as to say , " all right , in spite of this 
 tarnation humbug . " 

 but now that both party be fairly in the field , to the private art 
 of canvassing be add the public art of oratory . the candidate have 
 to speak , at the close of each day 's canvass , out from wooden box , 
 suspend from the window of their respective hotel , and which look 
 like den for the exhibition of wild beast . they have to speak at 
 meeting of Committees , meeting of elector , go the nightly round of 
 enthusiastic public - house , and appeal to the sense of an enlighten 
 people through wreath of smoke and odour of beer . 

 the alleged indisposition of Audley Egerton have spar he the 
 excitement of oratory , as well as the fatigue of canvassing . the 
 practise debater have limit the display of his talent to a concise , 
 but clear and masterly exposition of his own view on the lead public 
 question of the day , and the state of party , which , on the day after 
 his arrival at Lansmere , be deliver at a meeting of his general 
 Committee , in the great room of their hotel , and which be then print 
 and circulate amongst the voter . 

 randal , though he express himself with more fluency and 
 self - possession than be usually find in the first attempt of a public 
 speaker , be not effective in address an unlettered crowd ; for a 
 crowd of this kind be all heart - and we know that Randal Leslie 's heart 
 be as small as heart could be . if he attempt to speak at his 
 own intellectual level , he be so subtle and refining as to be 
 incomprehensible ; if he fall into the fatal error - not uncommon to 
 inexperienced orator - of try to lower himself to the intellectual 
 level of his audience , he be only elaborately stupid . no man can speak 
 too well for a crowd , - as no man can write too well for the stage ; but 
 in neither case should he be rhetorical , or case in period the dry 
 bone of reasoning . it be to the emotion or to the humour that the 
 speaker of a crowd must address himself ; his eye must brighten with 
 generous sentiment , or his lip must expand in the play of animate fancy 
 or genial wit . Randal 's voice , too , though pliant and persuasive in 
 private conversation , be thin and poor when strain to catch the ear 
 of a numerous assembly . the falsehood of his nature seem to come out 
 when he raise the tone which have be drill into deceit . man like 
 Randal Leslie may become sharp debater , admirable special pleader ; 
 they can no more become orator than they can become poet . educate 
 audience be essential to they , and the small the audience   that be , 
 the more the brain supersede the action of the heart   the well they 
 can speak . 

 Dick Avenel be generally very short and very pithy in his address . 
 he have two or three favourite topic , which always tell . he be a 
 fellow - townsman , - a man who have make his own way in life ; he want to 
 free his native place from aristocratic usurpation ; it be the battle 
 of the elector , not his private cause , etc . he say little against 
 Randal , - " Pity a clever young man should pin his future to two yard of 
 wear - out red tape ; " " he have well lay hold of the strong rope , which 
 the People , in compassion to his youth , be willing yet to throw out to 
 save he from sink , " etc . but as for Audley Egerton , " the gentleman 
 who would not show , who be afraid to meet the elector , who could only 
 find his voice in a hole - and - corner meeting , accustom all his venal 
 life to dark and nefarious job " - Dick , upon that subject , deliver 
 philippic truly Demosthenian . Leonard , on the contrary , never attack 
 Harley 's friend , Mr. Egerton ; but he be merciless against the youth who 
 have filch reputation from John Burley , and whom he know that Harley 
 despise as heartily as himself . and Randal do not dare to retaliate 
  though boil over with indignant rage , for fear of offend 
 Leonard 's uncle . Leonard be unquestionably the popular speaker of the 
 three . though his temperament be a writer 's , not an orator 's ; though 
 he abhor what he consider the theatrical exhibition of self , which 
 make what be call " delivery " more effective than idea ; though he have 
 little interest at any time in party politic ; though at this time 
 his heart be far away from the Blues and yellow of Lansmere , sad and 
 forlorn , - yet , force into action , the eloquence that be natural to his 
 conversation pour itself forth . he have warm blood in his vein ; 
 and his dislike to randal give poignancy to his wit , and barb his 
 argument with impassioned invective . in fact , Leonard could conceive no 
 other motive for Lord L'Estrange 's request to take part in the election 
 than that nobleman 's desire to defeat the man whom they both regard 
 as an impostor ; and this notion be confirm by some inadvertent 
 expression which Avenel let fall , and which make Leonard suspect that , 
 if he be not in the field , Avenel would have exert all his interest 
 to return Randal instead of Egerton . with Dick 's dislike to that 
 statesman Leonard find it impossible to reason ; nor , on the other hand , 
 could all Dick 's scolding or coaxing induce Leonard to divert his 
 siege on Randal to an assault upon the man who , Harley have often say , 
 be dear to he as a brother . 

 in the mean while , Dick keep the canvass - book of the yellow as closely 
 as Harley keep that of the Blues ; and in despite of many pouting fit 
 and gust of displeasure , take precisely the same pain for Leonard as 
 Harley take for Randal . there remain , however , apparently unshaken by 
 the effort on either side , a compact body of about a hundred and fifty 
 voter , chiefly freeman . would they vote Yellow ? would they vote Blue ? 
 no one could venture to decide ; but they declare that they would all 
 vote the same way . Dick keep his secret " caucus , " as he call they , 
 constantly nibble at this phalanx . a hundred and fifty voter ! - they 
 have the election in their hand ! never be hand so cordially shake , 
 so caressingly clung to , so fondly linger upon ! but the vote 
 still stick as firm to the hand as if a part of the skin , or of the 
 dirt , - which be much the same thing ! 




 CHAPTER XX . 

 whenever Audley join the other guest of an evening - while Harley be 
 perhaps closet with Levy and committeeman , and Randal be go the 
 round of the public - house - the one with whom he chiefly converse be 
 Violante . he have be strike at first , despite his gloom , less perhaps 
 by her extraordinary beauty than by something in the expression of 
 her countenance which , despite difference in feature and complexion , 
 remind he of Nora ; and when , by his praise of Harley , he draw she 
 attention , and win into her liking , he discover , perhaps , that the 
 likeness which have thus impress he come from some similarity 
 in character between the living and the lose one , - the same charming 
 combination of lofty thought and childlike innocence , the same 
 enthusiasm , the same rich exuberance of imagination and feeling . two 
 soul that resemble each other will give their likeness to the look 
 from which they beam . on the other hand , the person with whom Harley 
 most familiarly associate , in his rare interval of leisure , be Helen 
 Digby . one day , Audley Egerton , stand mournfully by the window of 
 the sit - room appropriate to his private use , see the two , whom he 
 believe still betroth , take their way across the park , side by side . 
 " pray Heaven , that she may atone to he for all ! " murmur Audley . " but 
 ah , that it have be Violante ! then I might have feel assure that the 
 Future would efface the Past , - and find the courage to tell he all . 
 and when last night I speak of what Harley ought to be to England , how 
 like be Violante 's eye and smile to Nora 's , when Nora listen in 
 delighted sympathy to the hope of my own young ambition . " with a 
 sigh he turn away , and resolutely sit down to read and reply to the 
 voluminous correspondence which cover the table of the busy public 
 man . for Audley 's return to parliament be consider by his political 
 party as secure , to he be transmit all the hope and fear of the 
 large and influential section of it whose member look up to he as 
 their future chief , and who in that general election   unprecedented for 
 the number of eminent man it be fat to expel from parliament , and the 
 number of new politician it be fat to send into it   draw their only 
 hope of regain their lose power from Audley 's sanguine confidence in 
 the reaction of that Public Opinion which he have hitherto so profoundly 
 comprehend ; and it be too clearly see , that the seasonable adoption 
 of his counsel would have save the existence and popularity of the 
 late Administration , whose most distinguished member could now scarcely 
 show themselves on the husting . 

 meanwhile Lord L'Estrange lead his young companion towards a green 
 hill in the centre of the park , on which stand a circular temple ; that 
 command a view of the country round for mile . they have walk in 
 silence till they gain the summit of the sloped and gradual ascent ; 
 and then , as they stand still , side by side , Harley thus speak , 

 " Helen , you know that Leonard be in the town , though I can not receive 
 he at the Park , since he be stand in opposition to my guest , 
 Egerton and Leslie . " 

 HELEN . - " but that seem to I so strange . how - how could Leonard do 
 anything that seem hostile to you ? " 

 HARLEY . - " would his hostility to I lower he in your opinion ? if he 
 know that I be his rival , do not rivalry include hate ? " 

 HELEN . - " oh , Lord L'Estrange , how can you speak thus ; how so wrong 
 yourself ? hate - hate to you ! and from Leonard Fairfield ! " 

 HARLEY . - " you evade my question . would his hate or hostility to I 
 affect your sentiment towards he ? " 

 HELEN   look down . - " I could not force myself to believe in it . " 

 HARLEY . - " why ? " 

 HELEN . - " because it would be so unworthy of he . " 

 HARLEY . - " poor child ! you have the delusion of your year . you deck a 
 cloud in the hue of the rainbow , and will not believe that its glory be 
 borrow from the sun of your own fancy . but here , at least , you be not 
 deceive . Leonard obey but my wish , and , I believe , against his own 
 will . he have none of man 's noble attribute , Ambition . " 

 HELEN . - " no ambition ! " 

 HARLEY . - " it be vanity that stir the poet to toil , - if toil the wayward 
 chase of his own chimera can be call . ambition be a more masculine 
 passion . " 

 Helen shake her head gently , but make no answer . 

 HARLEY . - " if I utter a word that profane one of your delusion , you 
 shake your head and be incredulous . pause : listen one moment to my 
 counsel , - perhaps the last I may ever obtrude upon you . lift your eye ; 
 look around . far as your eye can reach , nay , far beyond the line which 
 the horizon form in the landscape , stretch the land of my inheritance . 
 yonder you see the home in which my forefather for many generation 
 live with honour , and die lament . all these , in the course of 
 nature , might one day have be your own , have you not reject my 
 proposal . I offer you , it be true , not what be commonly call love ; 
 I offer you sincere esteem , and affection the more durable for their 
 calm . you have not be rear by the world in the low idolatry of rank 
 and wealth ; but even romance can not despise the power of serve other , 
 which rank and wealth bestow . for myself , hitherto indolence , and lately 
 disdain , rob fortune of these nobler attribute . but she who will share 
 my fortune may dispense it so as to atone for my sin of omission . 
 on the other side , grant that there be no bar to your preference for 
 Leonard Fairfield , what do your choice present to you ? those of his 
 kindre with whom you will associate be unrefined and mean . his sole 
 income be derive from precarious labour ; the most vulgar of all 
 anxiety - the fear of bread itself for the morrow - must mingle with 
 all your romance , and soon steal from love all its poetry . you think his 
 affection will console you for every sacrifice . folly ! the love of poet 
 be for a mist , a moonbeam , a denizen of air , a phantom that they call 
 an Ideal . they suppose for a moment that they have find that Ideal in 
 Chloe or Phyllis , Helen or a milkmaid . Bah ! the first time you come to 
 the poet with the baker 's bill , where fly the Ideal ? I know one more 
 brilliant than Leonard , more exquisitely gift by nature ; that one 
 be a woman ; she see a man hard and cold as that stone at your foot , - a 
 false , hollow , sordid worldling ; she make he her idol , beheld in he 
 all that history would not recognize in a Caesar , that mythology would 
 scarcely grant to an Apollo : to he she be the plaything of an hour ; 
 she die , and before the year be out he have marry for money ! I know 
 another instance , - I speak of myself . I love before I be your age . have 
 an angel warn I then , I would have be incredulous as you . how 
 that end , no matter : but have it not be for that dream of 
 maudlin delirium , I have live and act as other of my kind and my 
 sphere , - marry from reason and judgment , be now a useful and happy 
 man . pause , then . will you still reject I for Leonard Fairfield ? for 
 the last time you have the option , - I and all the substance of wake 
 life , Leonard Fairfield and the shadow of a fleeting dream . speak ! you 
 hesitate . Nay , take time to decide . " 

 HELEN . - " ah , Lord L'Estrange , you who have feel what it be to love , 
 how can you doubt my answer ; how think that I could be so base , so 
 ungrateful as take from yourself what you call the substance of wake 
 life , while my heart be far away , faithful to what you call a dream ? " 

 HARLEY . - " but can you not dispel the dream ? " 

 HELEN   her whole face one flush . - " it be wrong to call it dream ! it be 
 the reality of life to I . all thing else be as dream . " 

 HARLEY   take her hand and kiss it with respect . - " Helen , you have 
 a noble heart , and I have tempt you in vain . I regret your choice , 
 though I will no more oppose it . I regret it , though I shall never 
 witness your disappointment . as the wife of that man , I shall see and 
 know you no more . " 

 HELEN . - " oh , no ! do not say that . why ? wherefore ? " 

 HARLEY   his brow meeting . - " he be the child of fraud and of shame . 
 his father be my foe , and my hate descend to the son . he , too , the son , 
 filch from I - but complaint be idle . when the next few day be 
 over , think of I but as one who abandon all right over your action , 
 and be a stranger to your future fate . Pooh ! dry your tear : so long as 
 you love Leonard or esteem I , rejoice that our path do not cross . " 

 he walk on impatiently ; but Helen , alarmed and wonder , follow 
 close , take his arm timidly , and seek to soothe he . she feel that he 
 wrong Leonard , - that he know not how Leonard have yield all hope when 
 he learn to whom she be affiance . for Leonard 's sake she conquer 
 her bashfulness , and seek to explain . but at her first hesitating , 
 falter word , Harley , who with great effort suppress the emotion 
 which swell within he , abruptly leave her side , and plunge into the 
 recess of thick , farspreade grove , that soon wrap he from her 
 eye . 

 while this conversation occur between Lord L'Estrange and his ward , 
 the soi - disant Riccabocca and Violante be walk slowly through the 
 garden . the philosopher , unchanged by his brightening prospect , - so 
 far as the outer man be concern , - still characterize by the red 
 umbrella and the accustomed pipe , - take the way mechanically towards 
 the sunniest quarter of the ground , now and then glance tenderly at 
 Violante 's downcast , melancholy face , but not speak ; only , at each 
 glance , there come a brisker cloud from the pipe , as if obedient to a 
 full heave of the heart . 

 at length , in a spot which lie open towards the south , and seem to 
 collect all the gentle beam of the November sun , screen from the 
 pierce east by dense evergreen , and flank from the bleak north by 
 lofty wall , Riccabocca pause and seat himself . flower still bloom 
 on the sward in front , over which still flutter the wing of those 
 later and more brilliant butterfly that , unseen in the genial day 
 of our english summer , come with autumnal sky , and sport round the 
 mournful step of the come winter , - type of those thought which 
 visit and delight the contemplation of age , while the current yet 
 glide free from the iron ice , and the leaf yet linger on the bough ; 
 thought that associate the memory of the depart summer with 
 message from sun that shall succeed the winter , and expand colour the 
 most steeped in light and glory , just as the sky through which they 
 gleam be darken , and the flower on which they hover fade from the 
 surface of the earth , drop still seed , that sink deep out of sight 
 below . 

 " Daughter , " say Riccabocca , draw Violante to his side with caressing 
 arm , - " Daughter ! Mark how they who turn towards the south can still find 
 the sunny side of the land scape ! in all the season of life , how much 
 of chill or of warmth depend on our choice of the aspect ! sit down : let 
 we reason . " 

 Violante sit down passively , clasp her father 's hand in both her 
 own . reason ! harsh word to the ear of feeling ! " you shrink , " resume 
 Riccabocca , " from even the courtship , even the presence of the suitor in 
 whom my honour bind I to recognize your future bridegroom . " 

 Violante draw away her hand , and place they before her eye 
 shudderingly . 

 " but " continue Riccabocca , rather peevishly , " this be not listen to 
 reason . I may object to Mr. Leslie , because he have not an adequate rank 
 or fortune to pretend to a daughter of my house ; that would be what 
 every one would allow to be reasonable in a father ; except , indeed , " 
 add the poor sage , try hard to be sprightly , and catch hold of 
 a proverb to help he - " except , indeed , those wise enough to recollect 
 that admonitory say , ' Casa il figlio quando vuoi , e la figlia quando 
 puoi , ' -   marry your son when you will , your daughter when you can . 
 seriously , if I overlook those objection to Mr. Leslie , it be not 
 natural for a young girl to enforce they . what be reason in you be quite 
 another thing from reason in I . Mr. Leslie be young , not ill - look , 
 have the air of a gentleman , be passionately enamoured of you , and 
 have prove his affection by risk his life against that villanous 
 Peschiera , - that is , he would have risk it have Peschiera not be 
 ship out of the way . if , then , you will listen to reason , pray what 
 can reason say against Mr. Leslie ? " 

 " Father , I detest he ! " 

 " Cospetto ! " persist Riccabocca , testily , " you have no reason to detest 
 he . if you have any reason , child , I be sure that I should be the last 
 person to dispute it . how can you know your own mind in such a matter ? 
 it be not as if you have see anyone else you could prefer . not another 
 man of your own year do you even know , - except , indeed , Leonard 
 Fairfield , whom , though I grant he be handsomer , and with more 
 imagination and genius than Mr. Leslie , you still must remember as the 
 boy who work in my garden . ah , to be sure , there be Frank Hazeldean ; 
 fine lad , but his affection be pre - engaged . in short , " continue 
 the sage , dogmatically , " there be no one else you can , by any possible 
 caprice , prefer to Mr. Leslie ; and for a girl who have no one else in 
 her head to talk of detest a well - look , well - dress , clever young 
 man , be - a nonsense - ' Chi lascia il poco per haver l'assai ne l'uno , ne 
 l'altro avera mai ' - which may be thus paraphrase , - the young lady who 
 refuse a mortal in the hope of obtain an angel , lose the one , and 
 will never fall in with the other . so now , have thus show that the 
 dark side of the question be contrary to reason , let we look to the 
 brighter . in the first place - " 

 " oh , Father , Father ! " cry Violante , passionately , " you to whom I once 
 come for comfort in every childish sorrow do not talk to I with this 
 cutting levity . see , I lay my head upon your breast , I put my arm 
 around you ; and now , can you reason I into misery ? " 

 " child , child , do not be so wayward . strive , at least , against a 
 prejudice that you can not defend . my Violante , my darling , this be no 
 trifle . here I must cease to be the fond , foolish father , whom you can 
 do what you will with . here I be Alphonso , Duke di Serrano ; for here my 
 honour as noble and my word as man be involve . I , then , but a helpless 
 exile , no hope of fair prospect before I , tremble like a coward 
 at the wile of my unscrupulous kinsman , grasp at all chance to save 
 you from his snare , - self offer your hand to Randal Leslie , - offer , 
 promise , pledge it ; and now that my fortune seem assure , my rank in 
 all likelihood restore , my foe crush , my fear at rest , now , do it 
 become I to retract what I myself have urge ? it be not the noble , it 
 be the parvenu , who have only to grow rich , in order to forget those 
 whom in poverty he hail as his friend . be it for I to make the poor 
 excuse , never hear on the lip of an italian prince , ' that I can not 
 command the obedience of my child ; ' subject myself to the galling 
 answer , ' Duke of Serrano , you could once command that obedience , when , 
 in exile , penury , and terror you offer I a bride without a dower ' ? 
 Child , Violante , daughter of ancestor on whose honour never slander set 
 a stain , I call on you to redeem your father 's plighted word . " 

 " Father , must it be so ? be not even the convent open to I ? Nay , look 
 not so coldly on I . if you could but read my heart ! and oh ! I feel so 
 assured of your own repentance hereafter , - so assure that this man 
 be not what you believe he . I so suspect that he have be play 
 throughout some secret and perfidious part . " 

 " ha ! " interrupt Riccabocca , " Harley have perhaps infect you with that 
 notion . " 

 " no , no ! but be not Harley , be not Lord L'Estrange one whose opinion you 
 have cause to esteem ? and if he distrust Mr. Leslie - " 

 " let he make good his distrust by such proof as will absolve my word , 
 and I shall share your own joy . I have tell he this . I have invite 
 he to make good his suspicion , he put I off . he can not do so , " add 
 Riccabocca , in a dejected tone ; " randal have already so well explain 
 all that Harley deem equivocal . Violante , my name and my honour rest 
 in your hand . cast they away if you will ; I can not constrain you , and 
 I can not stoop to implore . Noblesse oblige ! with your birth you take 
 its duty . let they decide between your vain caprice and your father 's 
 solemn remonstrance . " 

 assume a sternness that he be far from feeling , and put aside 
 his daughter 's arm , the exile walk away . Violante pause a moment ,

 about an hour afterwards , as the short day close in , Harley , 
 return from his solitary wandering , after he have part from Helen ,

 the drop stand on Audley 's brow ! he fix his dark handsome eye , in

 HARLEY . - " perhaps the marriage may not take place . perhaps I have a 
 rival ; not an open one , - a secret , stealthy wooer , in one , too , whom

 egerton   with a languid smile . - " yes , you see I do not flatter . but he 
 be bear and rear a gentleman ; as such he would scarcely do anything

 Audley turn kindly to Randal . 

 " well , Leslie , what report of the canvass ? " 

 " Levy have the book , sir . I think we have gain ten fresh vote for you ,

 alarm at this threat , Randal lift his accuse eye to heaven and

 meanwhile Audley have draw close to Lady Lansmere , who be lean , in

 LADY LANSMERE . - " ah , that it be so ! Helen , I own , be charming ;

 engage to your young friend Mr. Leslie ? " 

 AUDLEY . - " Randal tell I so ; but I can not believe it . in fact , I have

 Harley , meanwhile , have linger with Riccabocca and Violante , speak

 Harley do not seem peculiarly flatter by that last assurance ; but

 " you have be quick ; I do not expect you so soon . you discover the 
 trace ? you give my letter - " 

 " and have bring back the answer , my Lord , " reply the man , take the 
 letter from a leathern pouch at his side . Harley hastily break open 
 the seal , and glance over the content , which be comprise in a few 
 line . 

 " good . say not whence you come . do not wait here ; return at once to 
 London . " 

 Harley 's face seem so unusually cheerful as he rejoin the Italians , 
 that the duke exclaim , - 

 " a despatch from Vienna ? my recall ! " 

 " from Vienna , my dear friend ! not possible yet . I can not calculate 
 on hear from the prince till a day or two before the close of this 
 election . but you wish I to speak to Violante . join my mother yonder . 
 what can she be say to Mr. Egerton ? I will address a few word apart 
 to your fair daughter , that may at least prove the interest in her fate 
 take by - her second father . " 

 " Kindest of friend ! " say the unsuspecting pupil of Machiavelli , and 
 he walk towards the terrace . Violante be about to follow . Harley 
 detain she . 

 " do not go till you have thank I ; for you be not the noble Violante 
 for whom I take you , unless you acknowledge gratitude to any one who 
 deliver you from the presence of an admirer in Mr. Randal Leslie . " 

 VIOLANTE . - " ought I to hear this of one whom - whom - " 

 HARLEY . - " one whom your father obstinately persist in obtrude on your 
 repugnance ? yet , o dear child , you who , when almost an infant , ere yet 
 you know what snare and pitfall , for all who trust to another , lie 
 under the sward at our foot , even when deck the fairest with the 
 flower of spring ; you who put your small hand around my neck , and 
 murmur in your musical voice , ' save we , - save my father , ' - you at 
 least I will not forsake , in a peril bad than that which menace you 
 then , - a peril which affright you more than that which threaten 
 you in the snare of Peschiera . randal Leslie may thrive in his meaner 
 object of ambition ; those I fle to he in scorn : but you ! 
 the presume varlet ! " Harley pause a moment , half stifle with 
 indignation . he then resume , calmly , " trust to I , and fear not . I will 
 rescue this hand from the profanation of Randal Leslie 's touch ; and then 
 farewell , for life , to every soft emotion . before I expand the 
 welcome solitude . the innocent save , the honest right , the perfidious 
 stricken by a just retribution , - and then - what then ? why , at least I 
 shall have study Machiavelli with more effect than your wise father ; 
 and I shall lay he aside , need no philosophy to teach I never again 
 to be deceive . " his brow darken ; he turn abruptly away , leave 
 Violante lose in amaze , fear , and a delight , vague , yet more vividly 
 feel than all . 




 CHAPTER XXI . 

 that night , after the labour of the day , Randal have gain the 
 sanctuary of his own room , and seat himself at his table , to prepare 
 the head of the critical speech he would have now very soon to deliver 
 on the day of nomination , - critical speech when , in the presence of foe 
 and friend , reporter from London , and amidst all the jarring interest 
 that he seek to weave into the sole self - interest of Randal Leslie , 
 he would be call upon to make the formal exposition of his political 
 opinion . randal Leslie , indeed , be not one of those speaker whom 
 either modesty , fastidiousness , or conscientious desire of truth 
 predispose towards the labour of write composition . he have too much 
 cleverness to be in want of fluent period or ready commonplace , - the 
 ordinary material of oratorical impromptu ; too little taste for the 
 Beautiful to study what grace of diction will well adorn a noble 
 sentiment ; too obtuse a conscience to care if the popular argument 
 be purify from the dross which the careless flow of a speech wholly 
 extemporaneous rarely fail to leave around it . but this be no ordinary 
 occasion . elaborate study here be requisite , not for the orator , 
 but the hypocrite . hard task , to please the Blues , and not offend the 
 yellow ; appear to side with Audley Egerton , yet insinuate sympathy with 
 Dick Avenel ; confront , with polite smile , the young opponent whose 
 word have lodge arrow in his vanity , which rankle the more gallingly 
 because they have raise the skin of his conscience . 

 he have dip his pen into the ink and smooth the paper before he , 
 when a knock be hear at the door . 

 " come in , " say he , impatiently . Levy enter saunteringly . 

 " I be come to talk over matter with you , mon cher , " say the baron , 
 throw himself on the sofa . " and , first , I wish you joy of your 
 prospect of success . " 

 Randal postpone his meditated composition with a quick sigh , draw his 
 chair towards the sofa , and lower his voice into a whisper . " you think 
 with I , that the chance of my success - be good ? " 

 " Chance ! why , it be a rubber of whist , in which your partner give you 
 all the winning , and in which the adversary be almost sure to revoke . 
 either Avenel or his nephew , it be true , must come in ; but not both . two 
 parvenus aspiring to make a family seat of an earl 's borough ! Bah ! too 
 absurd ! " 

 " I hear from Riccabocca   or rather the Duke di Serrano   that this same 
 young Fairfield be greatly indebted to the kindness of Lord L'Estrange . 
 very odd that he should stand against the Lansmere interest . " 

 " Ambition , mon cher . you yourself be under some obligation to Mr. 
 Egerton . yet , in reality , he have more to apprehend from you than from 
 Mr. Fairfield . " 

 " I disown obligation to Mr. Egerton . and if the elector prefer I to 
 he   whom , by - the - by , they once burn in effigy , it be no fault 
 of mine : the fault , if any , will rest with his own dearest friend , 
 L'Estrange . I do not understand how a man of such clear sense as 
 L'Estrange undoubtedly possess , should be risk Egerton 's election 
 in his zeal for mine . nor do his formal courtesy to myself deceive I . 
 he have even imply that he suspect I of connivance with Peschiera 's 
 scheme on Violante . but those suspicion he can not support . for of 
 course , Levy , you would not betray I - " 

 " I ! what possible interest could I serve in that ? " 

 " none that I can discover , certainly , " say Randal , relax into a 
 smile . " and when I get into parliament , aid by the social position 
 which my marriage will give I , I shall have so many way to serve you . 
 no , it be certainly your interest not to betray I ; and I shall count on 
 you as a witness , if a witness can be require . " 

 " count on I , certainly , my dear fellow , " say the baron . " and I suppose 
 there will be no witness the other way . do for eternally be my poor 
 dear friend Peschiera , whose cigar , by - the - by , be matchless ; - I 
 wonder if there will be any for sale . and if he be not so do for , it 
 be not you , it be L'Estrange , that he would be tempt to do for ! " 

 " we may blot Peschiera out of the map of the future , " rejoin Randal . 
 " man from whom henceforth we have nothing to hope or to fear be to we 
 as the race before the deluge . " 

 " fine remark , " quoth the baron , admiringly . " Peschiera , though not 
 without brain , be a complete failure . and when the failure of one I 
 have try to serve be complete , the rule I have adopt through life be 
 to give he up altogether . " 

 " of course , " say randal . 

 " of course , " echo the baron . " on the other hand , you know that I like 
 push forward young man of mark and promise . you really be amazingly 
 clever ; but how come it you do not   speak well ? do you know , I doubt 
 whether you will do in the House of Commons all that I expect from 
 your address and readiness in private life . " 

 " because I can not talk trash vulgar enough for a mob ? Pooh ! I shall 
 succeed wherever knowledge be really power . besides , you must allow for 
 my infernal position . you know , after all , that avenel , if he can only 
 return himself or his nephew , still hold in his hand the choice of 
 the candidate upon our side . I can not attack he ; I can not attack his 
 insolent nephew - " 

 " Insolent ! - not that , but bitterly eloquent . he hit you hard . you be 
 no match for he , Randal , before a popular audience ; though , en petit 
 comite , the devil himself be hardly a match for you . but now to a 
 somewhat more serious point . your election you will win , your bride be 
 promise to you ; but the old Leslie land , in the present possession of 
 Squire Thornhill , you have not gain , - and your chance of gain they 
 be in great jeopardy . I do not like to tell you this morning , - it would 
 have spoil your temper for canvass ; but I have receive a letter 
 from Thornhill himself . he have have an offer for the property , which be 
 only l1000 short of what he ask . a city alderman , call Jobson , be the 
 bidder ; a man , it seem , of large mean and few word . the alderman have 
 fix the date on which he must have a definite answer ; and that date 
 fall on the - th , two day after that fix for the poll at Lansmere . 
 the brute declare he will close with another investment , if Thornhill 
 do not then come in to his term . now , as Thornhill will accept these 
 term unless I can positively promise he well , and as those fund on 
 which you calculate   have the marriage of Peschiera with Violante , and 
 Frank Hazeldean with Madame di Negra , take place   fail you , I see no 
 hope for your be in time with the money , - and the old land of the 
 Leslies must yield their rent to a jobson . " 

 " I care for nothing on earth like those old land of my forefather , " 
 say Randal , with unusual vehemence ; " I reverence so little amongst the 
 living , and I do reverence the dead . and my marriage will take place so 
 soon ; and the dower would so amply cover the paltry advance require . " 

 " yes ; but the mere prospect of a marriage to the daughter of a man whose 
 land be still sequester would be no security to a money - lender . " 

 " surely , " say Randal , " you , who once offer to assist I when my 
 fortune be more precarious , might now accommodate I with this loan , 
 as a friend , and keep the title - deed of the estate as - " 

 " as a money - lender , " add the baron , laugh pleasantly . " no , mon 
 cher , I will still lend you half the sum require in advance , but 
 the other half be more than I can afford as friend , or hazard as 
 money - lender ; and it would damage my character , - be out of all 
 rule , - if , the estate fall by your default of payment into my own 
 hand , I should appear to be the real purchaser of the property of my 
 own distressed client . but , now I think of it , do not Squire Hazeldean 
 promise you his assistance in this matter ? " 

 " he do so , " answer Randal , " as soon as the marriage between Frank 
 and Madame di Negra be off his mind . I mean to cross over to Hazeldean 
 immediately after the election . how can I leave the place till then ? " 

 " if you do , your election be lose . but why not write to the squire ? " 

 " it be against my maxim to write where I can speak . however , there be 
 no option ; I will write at once . meanwhile , communicate with Thornhill ; 
 keep up his hope ; and be sure , at least , that he do not close with 
 this greedy alderman before the day fix for decision . " 

 " I have do all that already , and my letter be go . now , do your part : 
 and if you write as cleverly as you talk , you would coax the money 
 out from a stonier heart than poor Mr. Hazeldean 's . I leave you now ; 
 good - night . " 

 Levy take up his candlestick , nod , yawn , and go . randal still 
 suspend the completion of his speech , and indite the follow 
 epistle : - 

  MY DEAR MR . HAZELDEAN , - I write to you a few hasty line on leave 
  town , to inform you that the match you so dreaded be break off , 
  and propose to defer particular till I could visit your kind and 
  hospitable roof , which I trust to do for a few hour during my 
  stay at Lansmere , since it be not a day 's journey hence to 
  Hazeldean . but I do not calculate on find so sharp a contest . 
  in no election throughout the kingdom do I believe that a more 
  notable triumph , or a more stunning defeat , for the great landed 
  interest can occur . for in this town - so dependent on agriculture - 
  we be oppose by a low and sordid manufacturer , of the most 
  revolutionary notion , who have , moreover , the audacity to force his 
  own nephew - that very boy whom I chastise for impertinence on your 
  village green , son of a common carpenter - actually the audacity , I 
  say , to attempt to force this peasant of a nephew , as well as 
  himself , into the representation of Lansmere , against the earl 's 
  interest , against your distinguished brother , - of myself I say 
  nothing . you should hear the language in which these two man 
  indulge against all your family ! if we be beat by such person 
  in a borough suppose to be so loyal as Lansmere , every one with a 
  stake in the country may tremble at such a prognostic of the ruin 
  that must await not only our old English Constitution , but the 
  existence of property itself . I need not say that on such an 
  occasion I can not spare myself . Mr. Egerton be ill too . all the 
  fatigue of the canvass devolve on I . I feel , my dear and revere 
  friend , that I be a genuine Hazeldean , fight your battle ; and 
  that think carry I through all . I can not , therefore , come to 
  you till the election be over ; and meanwhile you , and my dear Mrs. 
  Hazeldean , must be anxious to know more about the affair that so 
  preyed on both your heart than I have yet inform you , or can well 
  trust to a letter . be assure , however , that the bad be over ; the 
  lady have go abroad . I earnestly entreat Frank   who show I 
  Mrs. Hazeldean 's most pathetic letter to he   to hasten at once to 
  the Hall and relieve your mind . unfortunately he would not be 
  rule by I , but talk of go abroad too - not , I trust   nay , I 
  feel assured , in pursuit of Madame di Negra ; but still - in short , I 
  should be so glad to see you , and talk over the whole . could you 
  not come hither - I pray do . and now , at the risk of your thinking 
  that in this I be only consult my own interest   but no - your 
  noble english heart will never so misiudge I ! , I will add with 
  homely frankness , that if you could accommodate I immediately with 
  the loan you not long since so generously offer , you would save 
  those land once in my family from pass away from we forever . a 
  city alderman - one Jobson - be meanly take advantage of Thornhill 's 
  necessity , and drive a hard bargain for those land . he have 
  fix the - th inst . for Thornhill 's answer , and Levy   who be here 
  assist Mr. Egerton 's election   inform I that Thornhill will 
  accept his offer , unless I be provide with L10,000 beforehand ; the 
  other L10,000 , to complete the advance require , Levy will lend I . 
  do not be surprised at the usurer 's liberality ; he know that I be 
  about shortly to marry a very great heiress   you will be please 
  when you learn whom , and will then be able to account for my 
  indifference to Miss Sticktorights , and her dower will amply serve 
  to repay his loan and your own , if I may trust to your generous 
  affection for the grandson of a Hazeldean ! I have the less scruple 
  in this appeal to you , for I know bow it would grieve you that a 
  Jobson , who perhaps never know a grandmother , should foist your own 
  kinsman from the land of his father . of one thing I be 
  convince , - we squire and son of squire must make common cause 
  against those great moneyed capitalist , or they will buy we all out 
  in a few generation . the old race of country gentleman be already 
  much diminish by the grasp cupidity of such leviathan ; and if 
  the race be once extinct , what will become of the boast and strength 
  of England ? 

  your , my dear Mr. Hazeldean , with most affectionate and grateful 
  respect , 

  RANDAL leslie . 




 CHAPTER XXII . 

 nothing to Leonard could as yet be more distasteful or oppressive than 
 his share in this memorable election . in the first place , it chafe 
 the secret sore of his heart to be compel to resume the name of 
 Fairfeld , which be a tacit disavowal of his birth . it have be such 
 delight to he that the same letter which form the name of Nora 
 should weave also that name of Oran , to which he have give distinction , 
 which he have associate with all his nobler toil , and all his hope of 
 endure fame , - a mystic link between his own career and his mother 's 
 obscurer genius . it seem to he as if it be render to she 
 the honour accord to himself , - subtle and delicate fancy of the 
 affection , of which only poet would be capable , but which other 
 than poet may perhaps comprehend ! that early name of Fairfield be 
 connect in his memory with all the ruder employment , the meaner 
 trial of his boyhood ; the name of Oran , with poetry and fame . it be 
 his title in the ideal world , amongst all fair shape and spirit . in 
 receive the old appellation , the practical world , with its bitterness 
 and strife , return to he as at the utterance of a spell . but in 
 come to Lansmere he have no choice . to say nothing of Dick , and Dick 's 
 parent with whom his secret would not be safe , Randal Leslie know that 
 he have go by the name of Fairfield , - know his suppose parentage , and 
 would be sure to proclaim they . how account for the latter name without 
 set curiosity to decipher the anagram it involve , and perhaps 
 guide suspicion to his birth from Nora , to the injury of her memory , 
 yet preserve from stain ? 

 his feeling as connect with Nora - sharpen and deepen as they all 
 have be by his discovery of her painful narrative - be embitter still 
 more by come in contact with her parent . Old John be in the same 
 helpless state of mind and body as before , - neither bad nor well ; 
 but wake up at interval with vivid gleam of interest in the election 
 at the wave of a blue banner , at the cry of " Blue forever ! " it be the 
 old break - clown charger , who , doze in the meadow , start at the roll 
 of the drum . no persuasion Dick could employ would induce his father to 
 promise to vote even one Yellow . you might as well have expect the 
 old Roman , with his monomaniac cry against Carthage , to have vote for 
 choose Carthaginians for consul . but poor John , nevertheless , be 
 not only very civil , but very humble to Dick , - " very happy to oblige the 
 gentleman . " 

 " your own son ! " bawl Dick ; " and here be your own grandson . " 

 " very happy to serve you both ; but you see you be the wrong colour . " 

 then as he gaze at Leonard , the old man approach he with trembling 
 knee , stroke his hair , look into his face , piteously . " be thee my 
 grandson ? " he falter . " Wife , wife , Nora have no son , have she ? my memory 
 begin to fail I , sir ; pray excuse it ; but you have a look about the 
 eye that - " Old John begin to weep , and his wife lead he away . 

 " do not   come again , " she say to Leonard , harshly , when she return . 
 " he will not sleep all night now . " and then , observe that the tear 
 stand in Leonard 's eye , she add , in soften tone , " I be glad to see 
 you well and thriving , and to hear that you have be of great service 
 to my son Richard , who be a credit and an honour to the family , though 
 poor John can not vote for he or for you against his conscience ; and he 
 should not be ask , " she add , fire up ; " and it be a sin to ask it , 
 and he so old , and no one to defend he but I . but defend he I will 
 while I have life ! " 

 the poet recognize woman 's brave , loving , wife - like heart here , and 
 would have embrace the stern grandmother , if she have not draw back 
 from he ; and , as she turn towards the room to which she have lead she 
 husband , she say over her shoulder , - 

 " I be not so unkind as I seem , boy ; but it be well for you , and for 
 all , that you should not come to this house again , - well that you have 
 not come into the town . " 

 " Fie , Mother ! " say Dick , see that Leonard , bend his head , 
 silently walk from the room . " you should be prouder of your grandson 
 than you be of I . " 

 " prouder of he who may shame we all yet ? " 

 " what do you mean ? " 

 but Mrs. Avenel shake her head and vanish . 

 " never mind she , poor old soul , " say Dick , as he join Leonard at the 
 threshold ; ' she always have her temper . and since there be no vote to be 
 get in this house , and one can not   set a caucus on one 's own father , - at 
 least in this extraordinary rotten and prejudiced old country , which be 
 quite in its dotage , - we will not come here to be snub any more . bless 
 their old heart , nevertheless ! " 

 Leonard 's acute sensibility in all that concern his birth , deeply 
 wound by Mrs. Avenel 's allusion , which he comprehend well than 
 his uncle do , be also keep on the edge by the suspense to which he 
 be condemn by Harley 's continued silence as to the paper confide 
 to that nobleman . it seem to leonard almost unaccountable that Harley 
 should have read those paper , be in the same town with himself , and 
 yet volunteer no communication . at length he write a few line to Lord 
 L'Estrange , bring the matter that concern he so deeply before 
 Harley 's recollection , and suggest his own earnest interest in any 
 information that could supply the gap and omission of the desultory 
 fragment . Harley , in reply to this note , say , with apparent reason , 
 " that it would require a long personal interview to discuss the subject 
 refer to , and that such an interview , in the thick of the contest 
 between himself and a candidate oppose to the Lansmere party , would 
 be sure to get wind , be ascribe to political intrigue , be impossible 
 otherwise to explain , and embarrass all the interest confide to their 
 respective charge . that for the rest , he have not be unmindful of 
 Leonard 's anxiety , which must now mainly be to see justice do to the 
 dead parent , and learn the name , station , and character of the parent 
 yet survive . and in this Harley trust to assist he as soon as the 
 close of the poll would present a suitable occasion . " the letter be 
 unlike Harley 's former cordial tone : it be hard and dry . Leonard 
 respect L'Estrange too much to own to himself that it be unfeele . 
 with all his rich generosity of nature , he seek excuse for what he 
 decline to blame . perhaps something in Helen 's manner or word have lead 
 Harley to suspect that she still cherish too tender an interest in the 
 companion of her childhood ; perhaps under this coldness of expression 
 there lurk the burn anguish of jealousy . and , oh , Leonard so well 
 understand , and could so nobly compassionate even in his prosperous 
 rival , that torture of the most agonizing of human passion , in which 
 all our reasoning follow the distorted writhing of our pain . 

 and Leonard himself , amidst his other cause of disquiet , be at once 
 so gnaw and so humble by his own jealousy . Helen , he know , be still 
 under the same roof as Harley . they , the betrothed , could see each other 
 daily , hourly . he would soon hear of their marriage . she would be bear 
 afar from the very sphere of his existence , - carry into a lofty 
 region , accessible only to his dream . and yet to be jealous of one to 
 whom both Helen and himself be under such obligation debase he in 
 his own esteem , - jealousy here be so like ingratitude . but for Harley , 
 what could have become of Helen , leave to his boyish charge , - he who have 
 himself be compel , in despair , to think of send she from his 
 side , to be rear into smileless youth in his mother 's humble cottage , 
 while he face famine alone , gaze on the terrible river , from the 
 bridge by which he have once beg for very alm , - beg of that Audley 
 Egerton to whom he be now oppose as an equal ; or fly from the 
 fiend that glare at he under the lid of the haunt Chatterton ? no , 
 jealousy here be more than agony , - it be degradation , it be crime ! 
 but , all ! if Helen be happy in these splendid nuptial ! be he sure 
 even of that consolation ? Bitter be the thought either way , - that she 
 should wholly forget he , in happiness from which he stand exclude as a 
 thing of sin ; or sinfully herself remember , and be wretche ! 

 with that healthful strength of will which be more often proportion to 
 the susceptibility of feeling than the world suppose , the young man at 
 last wrench himself for awhile from the iron that have enter into his 
 soul , and force his thought to seek relief in the very object from 
 which they otherwise would have the most loathingly recoil . he arouse 
 his imagination to befriend his reason ; he strove to divine some motive 
 not explain by Harley , not to be refer to the mere defeat , by 
 counter - scheme , of the scheming Randal , nor even to be solve by 
 any service to Audley Egerton , which Harley might evolve from the 
 complicated mesh of the election , - some motive that could more 
 interest his own heart in the contest , and connect itself with Harley 's 
 promise aid in clear up the mystery of his parentage . Nora 's memoir 
 have clearly hint that his father be of rank and station far beyond 
 her own . she have throw the glow of her glorious fancy over the 
 ambition and the destine career of the lover in whom she have merge her 
 ambition as poetess , and her career as woman . possibly the father might 
 be more disposed to own and to welcome the son , if the son could achieve 
 an opening , and give promise of worth , in that grand world of public 
 life in which alone reputation take precedence of rank . possibly , too , 
 if the son thus succeed , and become one whom a proud father could with 
 pride acknowledge , possibly he might not only secure a father 's welcome , 
 but vindicate a mother 's name . this marriage , which Nora darkly hint 
 she have be lead to believe be fraudulent , might , after all , have 
 be legal , - the ceremony conceal , even till now , by worldly shame at 
 disparity of rank . but if the son could make good his own footing - there 
 where rank itself own its chief in talent - that shame might vanish . 
 these supposition be not improbable ; nor be they uncongenial to 
 Leonard 's experience of Harley 's delicate benignity of purpose . here , 
 too , the image of Helen ally itself with those of his parent , to 
 support his courage and influence his new ambition . true , that she be 
 lose to he forever . no worldly success , no political honour , could now 
 restore she to his side . but she might hear he name with respect in 
 those circle in which alone she would hereafter move , and in which 
 parliamentary reputation rank high than literary fame . and perhaps in 
 future year , when love , retain its tenderness , be purify from its 
 passion , they might thus meet as friend . he might without a pang take 
 her child on his knee , and say , perhaps in their old age , when he 
 have climb to a social equality even with her high - bear lord , " it 
 be the hope to regain the privilege bestow on our childhood , that 
 strengthen I to seek distinction when you and happiness forsook my 
 youth . " thus regard , the election , which have before seem to he so 
 poor and vulgar an exhibition of vehement passion for petty object , 
 with its trumpery of banner and its discord of trumpet , suddenly grow 
 into vivid interest , and assume dignity and importance . it be ever thus 
 with all mortal strife . in proportion as it possess , or be void of , 
 the diviner something that quicken the pulse of the heart , and elevate 
 the wing of the imagination , it present a mockery to the philosopher , 
 or an inspiration to the bard . feel that something , and no contest be 
 mean ! feel it not , and , like Byron , you may class with the slaughter of 
 Cannae that field which , at Waterloo , restore the landmark of nation ; 
 or may jeer with Juvenal at the dust of Hannibal , because he seek to 
 deliver Carthage from ruin , and free a world from Rome . 




 CHAPTER XXIII . 

 once then , grapple manfully with the task he have undertake , and 
 constrain himself to look on what Riccabocca would have call " the 
 southern side of thing , " whatever there be really great in principle 
 or honourable to human nature , deep below the sordid detail and pitiful 
 interest apparent on the face of the agitated current , come clear to 
 his vision . the ardour of those around he begin to be contagious : 
 the generous devotion to some cause apart from self , which pervade an 
 election , and to which the poor voter will often render sacrifice 
 that may be call sublime ; the warm personal affection which community 
 of zeal create for the defender of beloved opinion , - all concur to 
 dispel that indifference to party politic , and counteract that disgust 
 of their baser leaven , which the young poet have first conceive . he 
 even begin to look with complacency , for itself , on a career of toil and 
 honour strange to his habitual labour and intellectual ambition . he 
 throw the poetry of idea within he   as poet ever do   into the prose of 
 action to which he be hurry forward . he no long oppose Dick Avenel 
 when that gentleman represent how detrimental it would be to his 
 business at Screwstown if he devote to his country the time and the 
 acuman require by his mill and its steamengine ; and how desirable it 
 would be , on all account , that Leonard Fairfield should become the 
 parliamentary representative of the Avenels . " if , therefore , " say 
 Dick , " two of we can not come in , and one must retire , leave it to I 
 to arrange with the Committee that you shall be the one to persist . oh , 
 never fear but what all scruple of honour shall be satisfied . I 
 would not for the sake of the Avenels have a word say against their 
 representative . " 

 " but , " answer Leonard , " if I grant this , I fear that you have some 
 intention of suffer the vote that your resignation would release to 
 favour Leslie at the expense of Egerton . " 

 " what the deuce be Egerton to you ? " 

 " nothing , except through my gratitude to his friend Lord L'Estrange . " 

 " Pooh ! I will tell you a secret . Levy inform I privately that 
 L'Estrange will be well satisfied if the choice of Lansmere fall upon 
 Leslie instead of Egerton ; and I think I convince my Lord - for I see 
 he in London - that Egerton would have no chance , though Leslie might . " 

 " I must think that Lord L'Estrange would resist to the utmost any 
 attempt to prefer Leslie - whom he despise - to Egerton , whom he honour . 
 and , so thinking , I too would resist it , as you may judge by the 
 speech which have so provoke your displeasure . " 

 " let we cut short a yarn of talk which , when it come to liking and 
 disliking , might last to almighty crack : I will ask you to do nothing 
 that Lord L'Estrange do not sanction . will that satisfy you ? " 

 " certainly , provide I be assure of the sanction . " 

 and now , the important day precede the poll , the day in which the 
 candidate be to be formally nominate , and meet each other in all the 
 ceremony of declare rivalship , dawn at last . the town - hall be the 
 place select for the occasion ; and before sunrise , all the street 
 be resonant with music , and gay with banner . 

 Audley Egerton feel that he could not - without incur some 
 just sarcasm on his dread to face the constituency he have formerly 
 represent , and by the malcontent of which he have be burn in

 present contest with the anguish that recall the first , - still the 
 thing must be do ; and it be the english habit of his life to face 
 with courage whatever he have to do . 




 CHAPTER XXIV . 

 the chief of the Blue party go in state from Lansmere Park ; the

 " from the squire ? " say he . " ah , he have write at last ! what make he

 CHAPTER XXV . 

 in the centre of the raise platform in the town - hall sit the mayor . 
 on either hand of that dignitary now appear the candidate of the 
 respective party , - to his right , Audley Egerton and Leslie ; to his 
 left , Dick Avenel and Leonard . 

 the place be as full as it could hold . row of grimy face peep in , 
 even from the upper window outside the building . the contest be one 
 that create intense interest , not only from public principle , but 
 local passion . Dick Avenel , the son of a small tradesman , stand 
 against the Right Honourable Audley Egerton , the choice of the powerful 
 Lansmere aristocratic party , - stand , too , with his nephew by his 
 side ; take , as he himself be will not to say , " the tarnation Blue Bull 
 by both its oligarchical horn ! " - there be a pluck and gallantry in the 
 very impudence of the attempt to convert the important borough - for one 
 member of which a great earl have hitherto strive , " with labour dire and 
 weary woe " into two family seat for the House of Avenel and the triumph 
 of the Capelocracy . 

 this alone would have excite all the spare passion of a country 
 borough ; but , besides this , there be the curiosity that attach to

 ex - minister , - a man whose career have commence with his success at 
 Lansmere , and who now , amidst the popular tempest that scatter his 
 colleague , seek to refit his vessel in the same harbour from which 
 it have first put forth . new generation have grow up since the name of 
 Audley Egerton have first flutter the dovecote in that Corioli . the

 Audley be propose , of course , by the crack man of the party , - a

 aristide ; and sit down , after an oration esteem classical by the

 necessity of be represent by gentleman of wealth and rank , and

 he   the maltster   have nothing to say against he . - he be , no doubt ,

 hear of Mr. Fairfield 's work . then it be reply Mr. Fairfield have 
 write under another name . what would that prove ? either that he be 
 ashamed of his name , or that the work do he no credit . for his 
 part , he   the maltster   be an Englishman ; he do not like anonymous 
 scribbler ; there be something not right in whatever be conceal . a 
 man should never be afraid to put his name to what he write . but grant 
 that Mr. Fairfield be a great author and a great poet , what the borough 
 of Lansinere want be , not a member who would pass his time in 
 writing sonnet to Peggy or Moggy , but a practical man of business , - a 
 statesman , - such a man as Mr. Audley Egerton , a gentleman of ancient 
 birth , high standing , and princely fortune . the member for such a place 
 as Lansmere should have a proper degree of wealth . "   " hear , hear ! " from 
 the Hundred and Fifty Hesitators , who all stand in a row at the 
 bottom of the hall ; and " Gammon ! " " Stuff ! " from some revolutionary but 
 incorruptible yellow .   still the allusion to Egerton 's private fortune 
 have considerable effect with the bulk of the audience , and the maltster 
 be much cheer on conclude . Mr. Avenel 's proposer and seconder - the 
 one a large grocer , the other the proprietor of a new shop for ticketed 
 print , shawl , blanket , and counterpane , - a man , who , as he boast , 
 deal with the People for ready money , and no mistake , at least none 
 that he ever rectify - next follow . both say much the same

 stranger , upright public principle , never fawn on government , would

 other job of a corrupt aristocracy , etc . Randal Leslie 's proposer , a
