 CHAPTER XXXIV . 

 audely Egerton be alone in his apartment . a heavy sleep have come over 
 he , shortly after Harley and Randal have leave the house in the early 
 morning ; and that sleep continue till late in the day . all the while 
 the town of Lansmere have be distract in his cause , all the while so 
 many tumultuous passion have run riot in the contest that be to close 
 or re - open for the statesman 's ambition the Janus gate of political 
 war , the object of so many fear and hope , scheme and counter - scheme , 
 have slumber quietly as an infant in the cradle . he wake but in time to 
 receive Harley 's despatch , announce the success of his election ; and 
 add , " before the night you shall embrace your son . do not join we 
 below when I return . keep calm , - we will come to you . " 

 in fact , though not aware of the dread nature of Audley 's complaint , 
 with its warning symptom , Lord L'Estrange wish to spare to his friend 
 the scene of Randal 's exposure . 

 on the receipt of that letter Egerton rise . at the prospect of see 
 his son - Nora 's son - the very memory of his disease vanish . the poor , 
 weary , over - labour heart indeed beat loud , and with many a jerk and 
 spasm . he heed it not . the victory , that restore he to the sole life 
 for which he have hitherto care to live , be clean forget . nature 
 claim her own , - claim it in scorn of death , and in oblivion of 
 renown . 

 there sit the man , dress with his habitual precision , - the black 
 coat , button across the broad breast ; his countenance , so mechanically 
 habituate to self - control , still reveal little of emotion , though 
 the sickly flush come and go on the bronzed cheek , and the eye watch 
 the hand of the clock , and the ear hunger for a foot - tread along the 
 corridor . at length the sound be hear , - step , many step . he spring 
 to his foot , he stand on the hearth . be the hearth to be solitary no 
 more ? Harley enter first . Egerton 's eye rest on he eagerly for 
 a moment , and strain onward across the threshold . Leonard come 
 next , - Leonard Fairfield , whom he have see as his opponent ! he begin 
 to suspect , to conjecture , to see the mother 's tender eye in the son 's 
 manly face . involuntarily he open his arm ; but , Leonard remain 
 still , let they fall with a deep sigh , and fancy himself deceive . 

 " Friend , " say Harley , " I give to you a son prove in adversity , and who 
 have fight his own way to fame . Leonard , in the man to whom I pray you 
 to sacrifice your own ambition , of whom you have speak with such 
 worthy praise , whose career of honour you have promote , and whose life , 
 unsatisfie by those honour , you will soothe with your filial love , 
 behold the husband of Nora Avenel ! kneel to your father ! o Audley , 
 embrace your son ! " 

 " here , here ! " exclaim Egerton , as Leonard bend his knee , - " here to my 
 heart ! look at I with those eye ! - kindly , forgivingly : they be your 
 mother 's ! " his proud head sink on his son 's shoulder . 

 " but this be not enough , " say Harley , lead Helen , and place she by 
 Leonard 's side . " you must open your heart for more . take into its fold 
 my sweet ward and daughter . what be a home without the smile of woman ? 
 they have love each other from child . Audley , yours be the hand to 
 join , - yours be the lip to bless . " 

 Leonard start anxiously . " oh , sir ! - oh , my father ! - this generous 
 sacrifice may not be ; for he - he who have save I for this surpass 
 joy - he too love she ! " 

 " Nay , Leonard , " say Harley , smile , " I be not so neglectful of myself . 
 another home woo you , Audley . he whom you long so vainly seek to 
 reconcile to life , exchange mournful dream for happy duty , - he , 
 too , present you to his bride . love she for my sake , - for your own . she 
 it be , not I , who preside over this hallowed reunion . but for she , I 
 should have be a blinded , vindictive , guilty , repentant man ; and - " 
 Violante 's soft hand be on his lip . " thus , " say the parson , with mild 
 solemnity , " man find that the Saviour 's precept , ' let not the sun go 
 down upon thy wrath , ' and ' love one another , ' be clew that conduct we 
 through the labyrinth of human life , when the scheme of fraud and hate 
 snap asunder , and leave we lose amidst the maze . " 

 Egerton rear his head , as if to answer ; and all present be strike 
 and appal by the sudden change that have come over his countenance . 
 there be a film upon the eye , a shadow on the aspect ; the word 
 fail his lip ; he sink on the seat beside he . the left hand rest 
 droopingly upon the pile of public paper and official document , and 
 the finger play with they , as the bedridden die sufferer play with 
 the coverlid he will soon exchange for the winding - sheet . but his right 
 hand seem to feel , as through the dark , for the recovered son ; and 
 have touch what it seek , feebly draw Leonard near and nearer . 
 alas ! that blissful private life - that close centre round the core of 
 be in the individual man - so long miss and pin for , slip from 
 he , as it be , the moment it reappear ; hurry away , as the circle 
 on the ocean , which be scarce see ere it vanish amidst infinity . 
 suddenly both hand be still ; the head fall back . Joy have burst 
 asunder the last ligament , so fret away in unrevealing sorrow . afar , 
 their sound bear into that room , the joy - bell be peal triumph ; 
 mob roar out huzzas ; the weak cry of John Avenel might be blent in 
 those shout , as the drunken zealot reel by his cottage door , and 
 startle the screaming raven that wheel round the hollow oak . the 
 boom which be send from the wave on the surface of life , while the 
 deep be so noiseless in their march , be waft on the wintry air into 
 the chamber of the statesman it honour , and over the grass sigh low 
 upon Nora 's grave . but there be one in the chamber , as in the grave , 
 for whom the boom on the wave have no sound , and the march of the deep 
 have no tide . amidst promise of home , and union , and peace , and fame , 
 death stride into the household ring , and , seat itself , calm and 
 still , look life - like , - warm heart throb round it ; lofty hope 
 flutter upward ; love kneel at its foot ; Religion , with lift 
 finger , stand by its side . 




 FINAL CHAPTER . 

  SCENE - the Hall in the Old Tower of CAPTAIN ROLAND DE CAXTON . 

 " but you have not do ? " say Augustine Caxton . 

 PISISTRATUS . - " what remain to do ? " 

 MR . CAXTON . - " what ! why , the final chapter ! - the last news you can give 
 we of those whom you have introduce to our liking or dislike . " 

 PISISTRATUS . - " surely it be more dramatic to close the work with a 
 scene that complete the main design of the plot , and leave it to the 
 prophetic imagination of all whose flattering curiosity be still not 
 wholly satisfied , to trace the stream of each several existence , when 
 they branch off again from the lake in which their water converge , and 
 by which the sibyl have confirm and make clear the decree that ' conduct 
 be Fate . ' " 

 MR . CAXTON . - " more dramatic , I grant ; but you have not write a drama . 
 a novelist should be a comfortable , garrulous , communicative , gossip 
 fortune - teller ; not a grim , laconical , oracular sibyl . I like a novel 
 that adopt all the old - fashioned custom prescribe to its art by 
 the rule of the Masters , - more especially a novel which you style ' my 
 Novel ' par emphasis . " 

 CAPTAIN ROLAND . - " a most vague and impracticable title ' my novel ' ! it 
 must really be change before the work go in due form to the public . " 

 MR . SQUILLS . - " certainly the present title can not be even pronounce by 
 many without inflict a shock upon their nervous system . do you think , 
 for instance , that my friend , Lady Priscilla Graves - who be a great 
 novel - reader indeed , but hold all female writer unfeminine deserter 
 to the standard of Man - could ever come out with , ' pray , sir , have you 
 have time to look at - MY novel ? ' - she would rather die first . and yet 
 to be silent altogether on the late acquisition to the circulate 
 library would bring on a functional derangement of her ladyship 's 
 organ of speech . or how could pretty Miss Dulcet - all sentiment , it 
 be true , but all bashful timidity - appall Captain Smirke from propose 
 with , ' do not you think the parson 's sermon a little too dry in - MY 
 Novel ' ? it will require a face of brass , or at least a long course 
 of citrate of iron , before a respectable lady or unassuming young 
 gentleman , with a proper dread of be take for scribbler , could 
 electrify a social circle with ' the reviewer do not   do justice to the 
 excellent thing in - my Novel . ' " 

 CAPTAIN ROLAND . - " awful consequence , indeed , may arise from the 
 mistake such a title give rise to . Counsellor Digwell , for instance , a 
 lawyer of literary taste , but whose career at the Bar be long delay 
 by an unjust suspicion amongst the attorney that he have write a 
 ' Philosophical Essay ' - imagine such a man excuse himself for be 
 late at a dinner of bigwig , with ' I could not get away from - my novel ! ' 
 it would be his professional ruin ! I be not fond of lawyer in general , 
 but still I would not be a party to take the bread out of the mouth 
 of those with a family ; and Digwell have child , - the tenth an innocent 
 baby in arm . " 

 MR . CAXTON . - " as to Digwell in particular , and lawyer in general , they 
 be too accustomed to circumlocution to expose themselves to the danger 
 your kind heart apprehend ; but I allow that a shy scholar like myself , 
 or a grave college tutor , might be a little put to the blush , if he be 
 to blurt forth inadvertently with , ' do not   waste your time over trash 
 like - MY Novel . ' and that think present to we another and more 
 pleasing view of this critical question . the title you condemn place 
 the work under universal protection . live there a man or a woman so 
 dead to self - love as to say , ' what contemptible stuff be - MY Novel ' ? 
 would he or she not rather be impel by that strong impulse of an 
 honourable and virtuous heart , which move we to stand as well as we can 
 with our friend , to say , ' allow that there be really a good thing now 
 and then in - my novel . ' moreover , as a novel aspire to embrace most of 
 the interest or the passion that agitate mankind , - to generalize , as 
 it be , the detail of life that come home to we all , - so , in reality , 
 the title denote that if it be such as the author may not unworthily 
 call his novel , it must also be such as the reader , whoever he be , may 
 appropriate in part to himself , represent his own idea , express 
 his own experience , reflect , if not in full , at least in profile , 
 his own personal identity . thus , when we glance at the look - glass in 
 another man 's room , our likeness for the moment appropriate the mirror ; 
 and accord to the humour in which we be , or the state of our spirit 
 and health , we say to ourselves , ' bilious and yellow ! - I might as well 
 take care of my diet ! ' or , ' well , I ' ve   half a mind to propose to dear 
 Jane ; I be not such an ill - look dog as I think for ! ' still , whatever 
 result from that glance at the mirror , we never doubt that ' t be our 
 likeness we see ; and each say to the phantom reflection , ' thou art 
 myself , ' though the mere article of furniture that give the reflection 
 belong to another . it be my likeness if it be his glass . and a 
 narrative that be true to the Varieties of Life be every man 's novel , 
 no matter from what shore , by what river , by what bay , in what pit , 
 be extract the sand and the silex , the pearlash , the nitre , and 
 quicksilver which form its material ; no matter who the craftsman who 
 fashion its form ; no matter who the vendor that sell , or the customer 
 who buy : still , if I but recognize some trait of myself , ' t be my 
 likeness that make it ' my novel . ' " 

 MR . SQUILLS   puzzle , and therefore admire . - " subtle , sir , - very 
 subtle . fine organ of Comparison in Mr. Caxton 's head , and much call 
 into play this evening ! " 

 MR . CAXTON   benignly . - " finally , the author by this most admirable and 
 much signifying title dispense with all necessity of preface . he need 
 insinuate no merit , he need extenuate no fault ; for , by call his 
 work thus curtly ' MY Novel , ' he doth delicately imply that it be no use 
 wasting talk about fault or merit . " 

 PISISTRATUS   amazed . - " how be that , sir ? " 

 MR . CAXTON . - " what so clear ? you imply that , though a well novel may 
 be write by other , you do not expect to write a novel to which , 
 take as a novel , you would more decisively and unblushingly prefix that 
 voucher of personal authorship and identity convey in the monosyllable 
 ' my . ' and if you have write your good , let it be ever so bad , what can 
 any man of candour and integrity require more from you ? perhaps you will 
 say that , if you have live two thousand year ago , you might have call 
 it ' the novel , ' or the ' Golden Novel , ' as Lucius call his story ' the 
 Ass ; ' and Apuleius , to distinguish his own more elaborate Ass from all 
 ass precede it , call his tale ' the Golden Ass . ' but live in the 
 present day , such a designation - imply a merit in general , not 
 the partial and limited merit correspond only with your individual 
 ability - would be presumptuous and offensive . true , I here anticipate 
 the observation I see Squills be about to make - " 

 squill . - " I , Sir ? " 

 MR . CAXTON . - " you would say that , as Scarron call his work of fiction 
 ' the Comic Novel , ' so Pisistratus might have call his ' the serious 
 novel , ' or ' the Tragic Novel . ' but , Squills , that title would not have 
 be invite nor appropriate , and would have be expose to comparison 
 with Scarron , who be dead be inimitable . wherefore - to put the 
 question on the irrefragable basis of mathematic - wherefore as a b ' my 
 Novel ' be not equal to b C ' the Golden Novel , ' nor to D E ' the serious 
 or Tragic Novel , ' it follow that a b ' my novel ' be equal to P C 
 ' Pisistratus Caxton , ' and p C ' Pisistratus Caxton ' must therefore be 
 just equal , neither more nor less , to a b ' my novel , ' - which be to be 
 demonstrate . " my father look round triumphantly , and observe that 
 Squills be dumfounde , and the rest of his audience pose , he add 
 mildly , 

 " and so now , ' non quieta movere , ' proceed with the Final chapter , and 
 tell we first what become of that youthful Giles Overreach , who be 
 himself his own Marrall ? " 

 " ay , " say the captain , " what become of randal Leslie ? do he repent and 
 reform ? " 

 " Nay , " quoth my father , with a mournful shake of the head , " you can 
 regulate the warm tide of wild passion , you can light into virtue the 
 dark error of ignorance ; but where the force of the brain do but clog 
 the free action of the heart , where you have to deal , not with ignorance 
 mislead , but intelligence corrupt , small hope of reform ; for reform 
 here will need re - organization . I have somewhere read   perhaps in Hebrew 
 tradition   that of the two order of fall spirit , - the Angels of Love 
 and the Angels of Knowledge , - the first miss the star they have lose , 
 and wander back through the darkness , one by one , into heaven ; but the 
 last , light on by their own lurid splendour , say , ' wherever we go , 
 there be heaven ! ' and deep and low descending , lose their shape 
 and their nature , till , deform and obscene , the bottomless pit close 
 around they . " 

 MR . SQUILLS . - " I should not have think , Mr. Caxton , that a book - man 
 like you would be thus severe upon Knowledge . " 

 MR . CAXTON   in wrath . - " severe upon knowledge ! oh , Squills , Squills , 
 Squills ! Knowledge pervert be knowledge no long . vinegar , which , 
 expose to the sun , breed small serpent , or at good slimy eel , not 
 comestible , once be wine . if I say to my grandchild , ' do not   drink 
 that sour stuff , which the sun itself fill with reptile , ' do that 
 prove I a foe to sound sherry ? Squills , if you have but receive a 
 scholastic education , you would know the wise maxim that saith , ' all 
 thing the bad be corruption from thing originally design as the 
 good . ' have not freedom breed anarchy , and religion fanaticism ? and if I 
 blame Marat call for blood , or Dominic rack a heretic , be I severe

 immortalize Thrasybulus ? " 

 Mr. Squills , dread a catalogue of all the saint in the calendar , 
 and an epitome of Ancient history , exclaim eagerly , " enough , sir ; I be 
 convince ! " 

 MR . CAXTON . - " moreover , I have think it a natural stroke of art in 
 Pisistratus to keep Randal Leslie , in his progress towards the rot 
 of the intellect unwholesomely refine , free from all the salutary 
 influence that deter ambition from settle into egotism . neither in 
 his slovenly home , nor from his classic tutor at his preparatory school , 
 do he seem to have learn any truth , religious or moral , that might 
 give sap to fresh shoot , when the first rank growth be cut down by the 
 knife ; and I especially note , as illustrative of Egerton , no less than 
 of Randal , that though the statesman 's occasional hint of advice to 
 his protege be worldly wise in their way , and suggestive of honour as 
 befit the creed of a gentleman , they be not such as much influence

 Eton have not serve to correct of the arid self - seeking , which look

 Audley , or seduce into fraud by a cold , subtle spirit like Leslie , 
 will find poor defence in the elegant precept , ' remember to act as 
 a gentleman . ' such moral embroidery add a beautiful scarf to one 's 
 armour ; but it be not the armour itself ! ten o'clock , as I live ! push 
 on , Pisistratus ! and finish the chapter . " 

 MRS . CAXTON   benevolently . - " do not   hurry . begin with that odious randal 
 Leslie , to oblige your father ; but there be other whom Blanche and I 
 care much more to hear about . " 

 Pisistratus , since there be no help for it , produce a supplementary 
 manuscript , which prove that , whatever his doubt as to the artistic

 be content without one . 

 Randal Leslie , late at noon the day after he quit Lansmere Park , 
 arrive on foot at his father 's house . he have walk all the way , and 
 through the solitude of the winter night ; but he be not sensible of 
 fatigue till the dismal home close round he , with its air of hopeless 
 ignoble poverty ; and then he sink upon the floor feel himself a 
 ruin amidst the ruin . he make no disclosure of what have pass to his 
 relation . miserable man , there be not one to whom he could confide , 
 or from whom he might hear the truth that connect repentance with 
 consolation ! after some week pass in sullen and almost unbroken 
 silence , be leave as abruptly as he have appear , and return to London . 
 the sudden death of a man like Egerton have even in those excited 
 time create intense , though brief sensation . the particular of the 
 election , that have be give in detail in the provincial paper , be 
 copy into the London journal , among those detail , Randal Leslie 's 
 conduct in the Committee - room , with many an indignant comment on 
 selfishness and ingratitude . the political world of all party form 
 one of those judgment on the great man 's poor dependant , which fix a 
 stain upon the character and place a barrier in the career of ambitious 
 youth . the important personage who have once notice Randal for Audley 's 
 sake , and who , on their subsequent and not long - defer restoration to 
 power , could have make his fortune , pass he in the street without 
 a nod . he do not venture to remind Avenel of the promise to aid he in 
 another election for Lansmere , nor dream of fill up the vacancy which 
 Egerton 's death have create . he be too shrewd not to see that all hope 
 of that borough be over , - he would have be hoot in the street and 
 pelt from the husting . forlorn in the vast metropolis as Leonard 
 have once be , in his turn he loiter on the bridge , and gaze on the 
 remorseless river . he have neither money nor connection , - nothing save 
 talent and knowledge to force his way back into the lofty world in 
 which all have smile on he before ; and talent and knowledge , that have 
 be exert to injure a benefactor , make he but the more despised . 
 but even now , Fortune , that have bestow on the pauper heir of Rood 
 advantage so numerous and so dazzling , out of which he have cheat 
 himself , give he a chance , at least , of present independence , by which , 
 with patient toil , he might have win , if not to the high place , at 
 least to a position in which he could have force the world to listen 
 to his explanation ; and perhaps receive his excuse . the L5,000 
 that Audley design for he , and which , in a private memorandum , the 
 statesman have entreat Harley to see safely rescue from the fang of 
 the law , be make over to Randal by Lord L'Estrange 's solicitor ; but 
 this sum seem to he so small after the loss of such gorgeous hope , 
 and the up - hill path seem so slow after such short cut to power , 
 that Randal look upon the unexpected bequest simply as an apology for 
 adopt no profession . Stung to the quick by the contrast between his 
 past and his present place in the english world , he hasten abroad . 
 there , whether in distraction from thought , or from the curiosity of a 
 restless intellect to explore the worth of thing yet untried , randal 
 Leslie , who have hitherto be so dead to the ordinary amusement of 
 youth , plunge into the society of damage gamester and third - rate 
 roue . in this companionship his very talent gradually degenerate , and 
 their exercise upon low intrigue and miserable project but abase his 
 social character , till , sink step after step as his fund decay , 
 he finally vanish out of the sphere in which even profligate still 
 retain the habit , and cle to the caste of gentleman . his father die ; 
 the neglect property of Rood devolve on Randal , but out of its scanty 
 proceed he have to pay the portion of his brother and sister , and 
 his mother 's jointure ; the surplus leave be scarcely visible in the 
 executor 's account . the hope of restore the home and fortune of his 
 forefather have long cease . what be the ruin hall and its bleak 
 waste , without that hope which have once dignify the wreck and the 
 desert ? he write from St. Petersburg , order the sale of the property . 
 no one great proprietor be a candidate for the unpromising investment ; 
 it be sell in lot among small freeholder and retired trader . a 
 builder buy the hall for its material . Hall , land , and name be 
 blot out of the map and the history of the county . 

 the widow , Oliver , and Juliet remove to a provincial town in another 
 shire . Juliet marry an ensign in a march regiment ; and die of 
 neglect after childbirth . Mrs. Leslie do not long survive she . Oliver 
 add to his little fortune by marriage with the daughter of a retail 
 tradesman , who have amass a few thousand pound . he set up a brewery , 
 and contrive to live without debt , though a large family and his own 
 constitutional inertness extract from his business small profit and 
 no saving . nothing of Randal have be hear of for year after the sale 
 of Rood , except that he have take up his residence either in Australia 
 or the United States ; it be not know which , but presume to be the 
 latter . still , Oliver have be bring up with so high a veneration of 
 his brother 's talent , that he cherish the sanguine belief that Randal 
 would some day appear , wealthy and potent , like the uncle in a 
 comedy ; lift rip the sunken family , and rear into graceful lady and 
 accomplished gentleman the clumsy little boy and the vulgar little 
 girl who now crowd round Oliver 's dinner - table , with appetite 
 altogether disproportione to the size of the joint . 

 one winter day , when from the say dinner - table wife and child have 
 retire , and Oliver sit sip his half - pint of bad port , and look 
 over unsatisfactory account , a thin terrier , lie on the threadbare 
 rug by the niggard fire , spring up and bark fiercely . Oliver lift 
 his dull blue eye , and see opposite to he , at the window , a human 
 face . the face be press close to the pane , and be obscure by the 
 haze which the breath of its lip draw forth from the frosty rime that 
 have gather on the glass . 

 Oliver , alarmed and indignant , suppose this intrusive spectator of his 
 privacy to be some bold and lawless tramper , step out of the room , 
 open the front door , and bade the stranger go about his business ; 
 while the terri still more inhospitably yelp and snap at the 
 stranger 's heel . then a hoarse voice say , " do not   you know I , Oliver ? 
 I be your brother randal ! call away your dog and let I in . " Oliver 
 star aghast ; he could not believe his slow sens , he could not 
 recognize his brother in the gaunt grim apparition before he ; but at 
 length he come forward , gaze into Randal 's face , and , grasp his hand 
 in amazed silence , lead he into the little parlour . not a trace of 
 the well - breed refinement which have once characterize Randal 's air and 
 person be visible . his dress bespoke the last stage of that terrible 
 decay which be significantly call the ' shabby genteel . " his mien be 
 that of the skulking , timorous , famished vagabond . as he take off his

 gloomy , menacing . 

 Randal stop short all questioning . he seize the small modicum of 
 wine on the table , and drain it at a draught . " Poole , " say he , " have 
 you nothing that warm a man well than this ? " Oliver , who feel as if

 and put his lip to the mouth of the bottle . " ah , " say he , after a

 he devour the cold meat set before he with terrible voracity , and 
 nearly finish the spirit leave in the bottle ; but the last have no

 " you would know my history ? " at length say Randal , bluntly . " it be 
 short . I have try for fortune and fail , I be without a penny and 
 without a hope . you seem poor , - 

 " I suppose you can not much help I . let I at least stay with you for a

 Mrs. Oliver Leslie , at first rather awed and taciturn , grow cold and

 could offer you something , only it seem , at first glance , so beneath - " 

 " beneath what ? " interrupt Randal , witheringly . " what I be - or what I 
 be ? speak out ! " 

 " to be sure you be a scholar ; and I have hear you say fine thing 
 about knowledge and so forth ; and you will have plenty of book at your 
 disposal , no doubt ; and you be still young , and may rise - and - " 

 " Hell and torment ! be quick , - say the bad or the good ! " cry Randal , 
 fiercely . 

 " well , then , " say poor Oliver , still try to soften the intended 
 proposal , " you must know that our poor sister 's husband be nephew

 himself , and attend chiefly to arithmetic and book - keeping , and such

 forget that I once be a Leslie . our tie of brotherhood cease when I

 not many day afterwards , the protege of Audley Egerton enter on his 
 duty as usher in one of those large , cheap school , which comprise a 
 sprinkling of the son of gentry and clergyman design for the learn 
 profession , with a far large proportion of the son of trader , 
 intend , some for the counting - house , some for the shop and the till . 
 there , to this day , under the name of John Smith , live Randal Leslie . 

 it be probably not pride alone that induce he to persist in that 
 change of name , and make he regard as perpetual the abandonment of 
 the one that he take from his forefather , and with which he have once 
 identify his vault ambition ; for shortly after he have quit 
 his brother 's house , Oliver read in the weekly newspaper , to which he 
 bound his lore of the time in which he live , an extract from 
 an american journal , wherein certain mention be make of an english 
 adventurer who , amongst other alias , have assume the name of 
 Leslie , - that extract cause Oliver to start , turn pale , look round , 
 and thrust the paper into the fire . from that time he never attempt to 
 violate the condition Randal have impose on he , never seek to renew 
 their intercourse , nor to claim a brother . Doubtless , if the adventurer 
 thus signalize be the man Oliver suspect , whatever might be impute 
 to Randal 's charge that could have pal a brother 's cheek , it be none 
 of the more violent crime to which law be inexorable , but rather   in 
 that progress make by ingratitude and duplicity , with Need and necessity 
 urge they on   some act of dishonesty which may just escape from the 
 law , to sink , without redemption , the name . however this be , there be 
 nothing in Randal 's present course of life which forbode any deeper 
 fall . he have know what it be to want bread , and his former restlessness 
 subside into cynic apathy . 

 he lodge in the town near the school , and thus the debasing habit of 
 unsocial besotment be not bring under the eye of his superior . the 
 drain be his sole luxury ; if it be suspect , it be think to be his

 fear that he may be discover to be - a Leslie ! on such day , when his 
 taskwork be over , he shut himself up in his room , lock the door , and 
 drug himself into insensibility . 

 once he find a well - wear volume run the round of delighted 
 schoolboy , take it up , and recognize Leonard 's early popular work , 
 which have , many year before , seduce himself into pleasant thought and 
 gentle emotion . he carry the book to his own lodging , read it again ; 
 and when he return it to its young owner , some of the leaf be 
 stain with tear . alas ! perhaps but the maudlin tear of broken 
 nerve , not of the awakened soul , - for the leaf smell strongly of 
 whiskey . yet , after that re - perusal , Randal Leslie turn suddenly to

 work . after each irregular and spasmodic effort , the pen drop from his

 " I can never now raise a name . why give reputation to - John Smith ? " 

 thus he drag on his life ; and perhaps , when he die , the fragment of 
 his learn work may be discover in the desk of the usher , and serve 
 as hint to some crafty student , who may filch idea and repute from the 
 dead Leslie , as Leslie have filch they from the live Burley . 

 while what may be call poetical justice have thus evolve itself from 
 the scheme in which Randal Leslie have waste rare intellect in baffle 
 his own fortune , no outward sign of adversity evince the punishment 
 of Providence on the head of the more powerful offender , Baron Levy . no 
 fall in the Funds have shake the sumptuous fabric , build from the ruin 
 house of other man . Baron Levy be still Baron Levy the millionaire ; but 
 I doubt if at heart he be not more acutely miserable than Randal Leslie 
 the usher . for Levy be a man who have admit the fiercer passion into 
 his philosophy of life ; he have not the pale blood and torpid heart which 
 allow the scotch adder to dose away its sense of pain . just as old age 
 begin to creep upon the fashionable usurer , he fall in love with a young 
 opera - dancer , whose light heel have turn the light head of half the 
 eligans of Paris and London . the craft of the dancer be proof against 
 all less bribe than that of marriage ; and Levy marry she . from 
 that moment his house , Louis Quinze , be more crowded than ever by the 
 high - bear dandy whose society he have long so eagerly court . that 
 society become his curse . the baroness be an accomplished coquette ; and 
 Levy   with whom , as we have see , jealousy be the predominant passion 
 be stretch on an eternal rack . his low estimate of human nature , his 
 disbelief in the possibility of virtue , add strength to the agony 
 of his suspicion , and provoke the very danger he dread . his 
 self - torture task be that of the spy upon his own hearth . his 
 banquet be haunt by a spectre ; the attribute of his wealth be as 
 the goad and the scourge of Nemesis . his gay cynic smile change into 
 a sullen scowl , his hair blanch into white , his eye be hollow with 
 one consume care . suddenly he leave his costly house , - leave London ; 
 abjure all the society which it have be the joy of his wealth 
 to purchase ; bury himself and his wife in a remote corner of the 
 province ; and there he still live . he seek in vain to occupy his day 
 with rural pursuit , - he to whom the excitement of a metropolis , with 
 all its corruption and its vice , be the sole source of the torpid 
 stream that he call " pleasure . " there , too , the fiend of jealousy 
 still pursue he : he prowl round his demesne with the haggard eye 
 and furtive step of a thief ; he guard his wife as a prisoner , for she 
 threaten every day to escape . the life of the man who have open the 
 prison to so many be the life of a jailer . his wife abhor he , and do 
 not conceal it ; and still slavishly he dot on she . accustomed to the 
 free liberty , demand applause and admiration as her right ; wholly 
 uneducated , vulgar in mind , coarse in language , violent in temper , the 
 beautiful Fury he have bring to his home make that home a hell . thus , 
 what might seem to the superficial most enviable , be to their possessor 
 most hateful . he dare not ask a soul to see how he spend his gold ; 
 he have shrink into a mean and niggardly expenditure , and complain 
 of reverse and poverty , in order to excuse himself to his wife for 
 debar she the enjoyment which she anticipate from the Money Bags 
 she have marry . a vague consciousness of retribution have awaken 
 remorse , to add to his other sting . and the remorse come from 
 superstition , not religion   send from below , not descend from above , 
 bring with it none of the consolation of a genuine repentance . he 
 never seek to atone , never dream of some redeem good action . his 
 riches flow around he , spread wide and wide - out of his own reach . 

 the Count di Peschiera be not deceive in the calculation which 
 have induce he to affect repentance , and establish a claim upon his 
 kinsman . he receive from the generosity of the Duke di Serrano an 
 annuity not disproportione to his rank , and no order from his court 
 forbid his return to Vienna . but , in the very summer that follow his 
 visit to Lansmere , his career come to an abrupt close . at Baden - Baden 
 he pay court to a wealthy and accomplished polish widow ; and his fine 
 person and terrible repute awe away all rival , save a young Frenchman , 
 as dare as himself , and much more in love . a challenge be give and 
 accept . Peschiera appear on the fatal ground , with his customary

 his opponent 's nerve be affect in spite of his courage ; and the 
 Frenchman 's trigger go off before he have even take aim , to his own 
 ineffable astonishment , he shoot the count through the heart , dead . 

 Beatrice di Negra live for some year after her brother 's death in 
 strict seclusion , lodge within a convent , though she do not take 
 the veil , as she at first propose . in fact , the more she see of the 
 sisterhood , the more she find that human regret and human passion 
  save in some rarely gifted nature   find their way through the bar 
 gate and over the lofty wall . finally , she take up her abode in Rome , 
 where she be esteem for a life not only mark by strict propriety , 
 but active benevolence . she can not be prevail on to accept from the 
 duke more than a fourth of the annuity that have be bestow on her 
 brother ; but she have few want , save those of charity ; and when charity 
 be really active , it can do so much with so little gold ! she be not 
 know in the gayer circle of the city ; but she gather round her a 
 small society compose chiefly of artist and scholar , and be never so 
 happy as when she can aid some child of genius , - more especially if his 
 country be England . 

 the squire and his wife still flourish at Hazeldean , where Captain 
 Barnabas Higginbotham have take up his permanent abode . the captain be a 
 confirm hypochondriac ; but he brighten up now and then when he hear

 be hear to murmur , " if those seven sickly child should go off , I

 in at the table , - in the person of that old enemy and neighbour , Mr.

 of the parson 's life . we must now explain how Mr. Sticktorights come 
 to cut in at the hazeldean whist - table . Frank have settle at the Casino

 it be two year before Frank recover the disappointment with which

 awake his reflection . an affection , however misplaced and ill - requite ,

 person be instantly attract towards each other , perhaps by the

 waste land would still remain in the same state of delectable dispute 
 in which it then stand . there seem , however , little chance of a lawsuit

 Jackeymo , though his master have assess the long arrear of his wage 
 at a sum which would enable he to have orange - grove and servant

 of the male sex by her reverence for the duke , who continue to satirize 
 woman and wedlock , and deem himself - thank to his profound experience

 husband in the world . long still be it before the sage , who have be

 eye , - not , indeed , till he seriously betook himself to the task of 
 educate the son with whom , accord to his scientific prognostic ,

 the sage begin betime with his italian proverb , full of hardhearte 
 worldly wisdom , and the boy be scarce out of the hornbook before he be 
 introduce to Machiavelli . but somehow or other the simple goodness

 lesson , that the Heir of Serrano be little likely to be make more wise

 have practically make the progenitor , whose opinion his countryman 
 still shame with the title of " Alphonso the Good . " 

 the duke long cherish a strong curiosity to know what have become of 
 Randal . he never trace the adventurer to his closing scene . but once

 Dr. Morgan continue to prescribe globule for grief , and to administer 
 infinitesimally to a mind disease . practise what he prescribe , 
 he swallow a globule of caustic whenever the sight of a distressed 
 fellow - creature move he to compassion , - a constitutional tendency 
 which , he be at last convince , admit of no radical cure . for the rest , 
 his range of patient have notably expand ; and under his sage care 
 his patient unquestionably live as long - as Providence please . no 
 allopathist can say more . 

 the death of poor John Burley find due place in the obituary of 
 " literary man . " admirer , unknown before , come forward and subscribe

 subscribe for the relief of his widow and child , if he have leave

 writing which find place in standard library , though their subject

 Thought , but they be hoard by collector as rare curiosity . alas , 
 poor Burley !

 the vacancy create in the borough of Lansmere by Audley Egerton 's

 Dick Avenel do not retire from parliament so soon as he have 
 anticipate . he be not able to persuade Leonard , whose brief fever of 
 political ambition be now quench in the calm fountain of the Muse , 
 to supply his place in the senate , and he feel that the House of Avenel 
 need one representative . he contrive , however , to devote , for the 
 first year or two , much more of his time to his interest at Screwstown 
 than to the affair of his country , and succeed in baffle the 
 over - competition to which he have be subject by take the competitor 
 into partnership . have thus secure a monopoly at Screwstown , Dick , of 
 course , return with great ardour to his former enlightened opinion in 
 favour of free trade . he remain some year in parliament ; and though 
 far too shrewd to venture out of his depth as an orator , distinguish 
 himself so much by his exposure of " humbug " on an important Committee , 
 that he acquire a very high reputation as a man of business , and 
 gradually become so in request amongst all the member who move for 
 " Select Committees , " that he rise into consequence ; and Mrs. Avenel , 
 court for his sake , more than her own , obtain the wish of her 
 heart , and be receive as an acknowledge habituee into the circle 
 of fashion . amidst these circle , however , Dick find that his home 
 entirely vanish ; and when he come home from the House of Commons , 
 tired to death , at two in the morning , disgust at always hear that 
 Mrs. Avenel be not yet return from some fine lady 's ball , he form a 
 sudden resolution of cut Parliament , Fashion , and London altogether ; 
 withdraw his capital , now very large , from his business ; buy the 
 remain estate of Squire Thornhill ; and his chief object of ambition 
 be in endeavour to coax or bully out of their holding all the small 
 freeholder round , who have subdivide amongst they , into pole and 
 furlong , the fat inheritance of randal Leslie . an excellent justice 
 of the peace , though more severe than your old family proprietor 
 generally be ; a spirited landlord , as to encourage and make , at 
 a proper percentage , all permanent improvement on the soil , but 
 formidable to meet if the rent be not pay to the day , or the least 
 breach of covenant be heedlessly incur on a farm that he could let 
 for more money ; employ a great many hand in productive labour , but 
 exact rigorously from all the utmost degree of work at the small 
 rate of wage which competition and the poor - rate permit ; the young 
 and robust in his neighbourhood never stint in work , and the aged 
 and infirm , as lumber wear out , stow away in the workhouse , - Richard 
 Avenel hold himself an example to the old race of landlord ; and , take 
 altogether , he be no very bad speciman of the rural civilizer whom the 
 application of spirit and capital raise up in the new . 

 from the wreck of Egerton 's fortune , Harley , with the aid of his

 expose the false wile of the schemer be fulfil , to afford some

 Leonard 's birth and identity be easily prove , and no one appear to 
 dispute they . the balance due to he as his father 's heir , together with 
 the sum Avenel ultimately pay to he for the patent of his invention , 
 and the dowry which Harley insist upon bestow on Helen , amount to 
 that happy competence which escape alike the anxiety of poverty , and 
  what to one of contemplative taste and retired habit be often more 
 irksome to bear   the show and responsibility of wealth . his father 's 
 death make a deep impression upon Leonard 's mind ; but the discovery that 
 he owe his birth to a statesman of so great a repute , and occupy a 
 position in society so conspicuous , contribute not to confirm , but to 
 still , the ambition which have for a short time divert he from his 
 more serene aspiration . he have no long to win a rank which might 
 equal Helen 's . he have no long a parent , whose affection might be good 
 win through pride . the memory of his early peasant life , and his 
 love for retirement , - in which habit confirm the constitutional 
 tendency , - make he shrink from what a more worldly nature would have 
 consider the enviable advantage of a name that secure the entrance 
 into the lofty sphere of our social world . he want not that name to 
 assist his own path to a rank far more durable than that which king can 
 confer . and still he retain in the work he have publish , and still 
 he propose to bestow on the work more ambitious that he have , in 
 leisure and competence , the facility to design with care , and complete 
 with patience , the name he have himself invent , and link with the 
 memory of the low - bear mother . therefore , though there be some 
 wonder , in drawing - room and club , at the news of Egerton 's first 
 unacknowledged marriage , and some curiosity express as to what the son 
 of that marriage might do , - and great man be prepared to welcome , and 
 fine lady to invite and bring out , the heir to the statesman 's grave 
 repute , - yet wonder and curiosity soon die away ; the repute soon pass 
 out of date , and its heir be soon forget . politician who fall short 
 of the high renown be like actor ; no applause be so vivid while 
 they be on the stage , no oblivion so complete when the curtain fall on 
 the last farewell . 

 Leonard see a fair tomb rise above Nora 's grave , and on the tomb be 
 engrave the word of WIFE , which vindicate her beloved memory . he 
 feel the warm embrace of Nora 's mother , no long ashame to own she 
 grandchild ; and even old John be make sensible that a secret weight 
 of sorrow be take from his wife 's stern silent heart . lean 
 on Leonard 's arm , the old man gaze wistfully on Nora 's tomb , and 
 muttering , " Egerton ! Egerton ! ' Leonora , the first wife of the right 
 Honourable Audley Egerton ! ' ha ! I vote for he . she marry the right 
 colour . be that the date ? be it so long since she die ? well , well ! I 
 miss she sadly . but wife say we shall both now see she soon ; and wife 
 once think we should never see she again , - never ; but I always know 
 well . thank you , sir . I be a poor creature , but these tear do not   pain 
 I , - quite otherwise . I do not   know why , but I be very happy . where be my 
 old woman ? she do not mind how much I talk about Nora now . oh , 
 there she be ! thank you , sir , humbly ; but I would rather lean on my old 
 woman , - I be more used to it ; and - wife , when shall we go to Nora ? " 

 Leonard have bring Mrs. Fairfield to see her parent , and Mrs. Avenel 
 welcome she with unlooked - for kindness . the name inscribe upon Nora 's 
 tomb soften the mother 's heart to her survive daughter . as poor John 
 have say , " she could now talk about Nora ; " and in that talk , she and the 
 child she have so long neglect discover how much they have in common . 
 so when , shortly after his marriage with Helen , Leonard go abroad , 
 Jane Fairfield remain with the old couple . after their death , which 
 be within a day of each other , she refuse , perhaps from pride , to take 
 up her residence with Leonard ; but she settle near the home which he 
 subsequently find in England . Leonard remain abroad for some year . 
 a quiet observer of the various manner and intellectual development of 
 living race , a rapt and musing student of the monument that revive 
 the dead , his experience of mankind grow large in silence , and his 
 perception of the Sublime and Beautiful brighten into tranquil art 
 under their native sky . 

 on his return to England he purchase a small house amidst the most 
 beautiful scene of Devonshire , and there patiently commence a work 
 in which he design to bequeath to his country his noble thought 
 in their fair form . some man well develop their idea by constant 
 exercise ; their thought spring from their brain ready - armed , and seek , 
 like the fabled goddess , to take constant part in the war of man . and 
 such be , perhaps , on the whole , the most vigorous and lofty writer ; 
 but Leonard do not belong to this class . sweetness and serenity be 
 the main characteristic of his genius ; and these be deepen by his 
 profound sense of his domestic happiness . to wander alone with Helen 
 by the bank of the murmurous river ; to gaze with she on the deep still 
 sea ; to feel that his thought , even when most silent , be comprehend 
 by the intuition of love , and reflect on that translucent sympathy so 
 yearn for and so rarely find by poet , - these be the Sabbaths of 
 his soul , necessary to fit he for its labour : for the Writer have this 
 advantage over other man , that his repose be not indolence . his duty , 
 rightly fulfil , be discharge to earth and man in other capacity 
 than those of action . if he be not see among those who act , he be 
 all the while mature some noiseless influence , which will guide or 
 illumine , civilize or elevate , the restless man whose noble action 
 be but the obedient agency of the thought of writer . call not , 
 then , the poet whom we place amidst the Varieties of Life , the sybarite 
 of literary ease , if , return on Summer eve , Helen 's light footstep 
 by his musing side , he greet his sequester home , with its trellis 
 flower smile out from amidst the lonely cliff in which it be 
 embed ; while lover still , though wed long , they turn to each 
 other , with such deep joy in their speak eye , grateful that the 
 world , with its various distraction and noisy conflict , lie so far 
 from their actual existence , - only unite to they by the happy link that 
 the writer weave invisibly with the heart that he move and the soul 
 that he inspire . no ! character and circumstance alike unfit Leonard 
 for the strife of the thronged literary democracy ; they lead towards the 
 development of the gentle and purer portion of his nature , - to the 
 gradual suppression of the more combative and turbulent . the influence 
 of the happy light under which his genius so silently and calmly grow , 
 be see in the exquisite harmony of its colour , rather than the 
 gorgeous diversity of their glow . his contemplation , intent upon 
 object of peaceful beauty , and undisturbe by rude anxiety and 
 vehement passion , suggest only kindre reproduction to the creative 
 faculty by which it be vivified ; so that the whole man be not only 
 a poet , but , as it be , a poem , - a live idyl , call into pastoral 
 music every reed that sigh and tremble along the stream of life . and 
 Helen be so suited to a nature of this kind , she so guard the ideal 
 existence in which it breathe ! all the little care and trouble of 
 the common practical life she appropriate so quietly to herself , - the 
 strong of the two , as should be a poet 's wife , in the necessary 
 household virtue of prudence and forethought . thus if the man 's genius 
 make the home a temple , the woman 's wisdom give to the temple the 
 security of the fortress . they have only one child , - a girl ; they call 
 her Nora . she have the father 's soul - light eye , and the mother 's warm 
 human smile . she assist Helen in the morning 's noiseless domestic 
 duty ; she sit in the evening at Leonard 's foot , while he read or 
 write . in each light grief of childhood she steal to the mother 's 
 knee ; but in each young impulse of delight , or each bright flash of 
 progressive reason , she spring to the father 's breast . Sweet Helen , 
 thou hast teach she this , take to thyself the shadow even of thine 
 infant 's life , and leave to thy partner 's eye only its rosy light ! 

 but not here shall this picture of Helen close . even the Ideal can only 
 complete its purpose by connection with the real ; even in solitude the 
 writer must depend upon mankind . 

 Leonard at last have complete the work , which have be the joy and the 
 labour of so many year , - the work which he regard as the flower of all 
 his spiritual being , and to which he have commit all the hope that 
 unite the creature of today with the generation of the future . the work 
 have go through the press , each line linger over with the elaborate 
 patience of the artist , loath to part with the thought he have sculpture 
 into form , while an improve touch can be impart by the chisel . he 
 have accept an invitation from Norreys . in the restless excitement 
  strange to he since his first happy maiden effort   he have go to 
 London . Unrecognized in the huge metropolis , he have watch to see if 
 the world acknowledge the new tie he have weave between its busy life and 
 his seclude toil . and the work come out in an unpropitious hour ; other 
 thing be occupy the public ; the world be not at leisure to heed 
 he , and the book do not penetrate into the great circle of reader . 
 but a savage critic have seize on it , and mangle , distort , deform 
 it , confound together defect and beauty in one mocking ridicule ; and 
 the beauty have not yet find an exponent , nor the defect a defender ; 
 and the publisher shake his head , point to groan shelf , and 
 delicately hint that the work which be to be the epitome of the sacred 
 life within life do not hit the taste of the day . Leonard think over 
 the year that his still labour have cost he , and know that he have 
 exhaust the rich mine of his intellect , and that long year will 
 elapse before he can recruit that capital of idea which be necessary to 
 sink new shaft and bring to light fresh ore ; and the deep despondency 
 of intellect , frustrate in its high aim , have seize he , and all 
 he have before do be involve in failure by the defeat of the crowning 
 effort . failure , and irrecoverable , seem his whole ambition as writer ; 
 his whole existence in the fair Ideal seem to have be a profitless 
 dream , and the face of the Ideal itself be obscure . and even Norreys 
 frankly , though kindly , intimate that the life of a metropolis be 
 essential to the healthful intuition of a writer in the intellectual 
 want of his age , since every great writer supply a want in his own 
 generation , for some feeling to be announce , some truth to be reveal . 
 and as this maxim be generally sound , as most great writer have live 
 in city , Leonard dare not dwell on the exception ; it be only success 
 that justify the attempt to be an exception to the common rule ; and 
 with the blunt manhood of his nature , which be not a poet 's , Norreys 
 sum up with , " what then ? one experiment have fail ; fit your life to 
 your genius , and try again . " try again ! easy counsel enough to the man 
 of ready resource and quick combative mind ; but to Leonard , how hard 
 and how harsh ! " fit his life to his genius ! " - renounce contemplation and 
 Nature for the jostle of Oxford Street ! would that life not scare away 
 the genius forever ? perplexed and despondent , though still struggle 
 for fortitude , he return to his home ; and there at his hearth await 
 the Soother , and there be the voice that repeat the passage most 
 beloved , and prophesy so confidently of future fame ; and gradually all 
 around smile from the smile of Helen . and the profound conviction that 
 Heaven place human happiness beyond the reach of the world 's contempt 
 or praise , circulate through his system and restore its serene calm . 
 and he feel that the duty of the intellect be to accomplish and perfect 
 itself , - to harmonize its sound into music that may be hear in heaven , 
 though it wake not an echo on the earth . if this be do , as with some 
 man , well amidst the din and the discord , be it so ; if , as with he , 
 well in silence , be it so too . and the next day he recline with Helen 
 by the seashore , gaze calmly as before on the measureless sunlit 
 ocean ; and Helen , look into his face , see that it be sunlit as the 
 deep . his hand steal within her own , in the gratitude that endear 
 beyond the power of passion , and he murmur gently , " blessed be the 
 woman who console . " 

 the work find its way at length into fame , and the fame send its voice 
 loud to the poet 's home . but the applause of the world have not a sound 
 so sweet to his ear , as , when , in doubt , humiliation , and sadness , the 
 lip of his Helen have whisper " Hope ! and believe ! " 

 side by side with this picture of Woman the Consoler , let I place the 
 companion sketch . Harley L'Estrange , shortly after his marriage with 
 Violante , have be induce , whether at his bride 's persuasion , or to 
 dissipate the shadow with which Egerton 's death still cloud his wed 
 felicity , to accept a temporary mission , half military , half civil , to 
 one of our colony . on this mission he have evince so much ability 
 and achieve so signal a success , that on his return to England he be 
 raise to the peerage , while his father yet live to rejoice that the 
 son who would succeed to his honour have achieve the nobler dignity 
 of honour not inherit , but win . high expectation be form of 
 Harley 's parliamentary success ; but he see that such success , to be 
 durable , must find itself on the knowledge of wearisome detail , and 
 the study of that practical business which jar on his taste , though 
 it suit his talent . Harley have be indolent for so many year , - and 
 there be so much to make indolence captivate to a man whose rank be 
 secure , who have nothing to ask from fortune , and who find at his home 
 no care from which he seek a distraction ; so he laugh at ambition in 
 the whim of his delightful humour , and the expectation form from 
 his diplomatic triumph die away . but then come one of those political 
 crisis , in which man ordinarily indifferent to politic rouse themselves 
 to the recollection that the experiment of legislation be not make upon 
 dead matter , but on the live form of a noble country ; and in both 
 Houses of Parliament the strength of party be put forth . 

 it be a lovely day in spring , and Harley be seat by the window of 
 his old room at Knightsbridge , - now glance to the lively green of 
 the bud tree ; now idle with Nero , who , though in canine old age , 
 enjoy the sun like his master ; now repeat to himself , as he turn 
 over the leaf of his favourite horace , some of those line that make 
 the shortness of life the excuse for seize its pleasure and 
 elude its fatigue , which form the staple morality of the polished 
 epicurean ; and Violante   into what glorious beauty her maiden bloom 
 have mature !   come softly into the room , seat herself on a low stool 
 beside he , lean her face on her hand , and look up at he 
 through her dark , clear , spiritual eye ; and as she continue to speak , 
 gradually a change come over Harley 's aspect , gradually the brow grow 
 thoughtful , and the lip lose their playful smile . there be no hateful 
 assumption of the would - be ' superior woman , " no formal remonstrance , no 
 lecture , no homily which grate upon masculine pride ; but the high theme 
 and the eloquent word elevate unconsciously of themselves , and the 
 Horace be lay aside , - a Parliamentary Blue Book have be , by some 
 marvel or other , conjure there in its stead ; and Violante now move 
 away as softly as she enter . Harley 's hand detain she . 

 " not so . share the task , or I quit it . here be an extract I condemn you 
 to copy . do you think I would go through this labour if you be not to 
 halve the success ? - halve the labour as well ! " 

 and Violante , overjoyed , kiss away the imply rebuke , and sit down 
 to work , so demure and so proud , by his side . I do not know if Harley 
 make much way in the Blue Book that morning ; but a little time after he 
 speak in the Lords , and surpass all that the most sanguine have hop 
 from his talent . the sweetness of fame and the consciousness of utility 
 once fully taste , Harley 's consummation of his proper destiny 
 be secure . a year later , and his voice be one of the influence of 
 England . his boyish love of glory revive , - no long vague and dreamy , 
 but ennoble into patriotism , and strengthen into purpose . one night , 
 after a signal triumph , he return home , with his father , who have 
 witness it , and Violante - who all lovely , all brilliant , though she 
 be , never go forth in her lord 's absence , to lower among fop and 
 flatterer the dignity of the name she so aspired to raise - sprang to 
 meet he . Harley 's eld son - a boy yet in the nursery - have be keep 
 up later than usual ; perhaps Violante have anticipate her husband 's 
 triumph , and wish the son to share it . the old earl beckon the 
 child to he , and lay his hand on the infant 's curly lock , say with 
 unusual seriousness , 

 " my boy , you may see troubled time in England before these hair be 
 as gray as mine ; and your stake in England 's honour and peace will be 
 great . Heed this hint from an old man who have no talent to make a 
 noise in the world , but who yet have be of some use in his generation . 
 neither sound title , nor wide land , nor fine ability , will give 
 you real joy , unless you hold yourself responsible for all to your God 
 and to your country ; and when you be tempt to believe that the gift 
 you may inherit from both entail no duty , or that duty be at war 
 with true pleasure , remember how I place you in your father 's arm , 
 and say , ' let he be as proud of you some day as I at this hour be of 
 he . ' " 

 the boy clung to his father 's breast , and say manfully , " I will try ! " 
 Harley bend his fair smooth brow over the young earnest face , and say 
 softly , " your mother speak in you ! " 

 then the old countess , who have remain silent and listen on her 
 elbow - chair , rise and kiss the earl 's hand reverently . perhaps in that 
 kiss there be the repentant consciousness how far the active goodness 
 she have often secretly undervalue have exceed , in its fruit , her own 
 cold unproductive power of will and mind . then pass on to Harley , 
 her brow grow elate , and the pride return to her eye . 

 " at last , " she say , lay on his shoulder that light firm hand , from 
 which he no long shrunk , - " at last , o my noble son , you have fulfil 
 all the promise of your youth ! " 

 " if so , " answer Harley , " it be because I have find what I then seek 
 in vain . " he draw his arm around Violante , and add , with half tender , 
 half solemn smile , " Blessed be the woman who exalt ! " 

 so , symbol forth in these twin and fair flower which Eve save for 
 Earth out of Paradise , each with the virtue to heal or to strengthen , 
 store under the leaf that give sweet to the air ; here , soothe the 
 heart when the world bring the trouble ; here , recruit the soul which 
 our sloth or our sens enervate , leave we Woman , at least in the place 
 Heaven assign to she amidst the multiform " Varieties of Life . " 

 farewell to thee , gentle reader ; and go forth to the world , o MY novel ! 


 the END .
